### Example 1 — CW Multiplier Droop
**Problem:** A 6-stage Cockcroft–Walton multiplier is fed from a transformer of secondary RMS voltage 50 V at 50 Hz. All stage capacitors are 0.1 µF. A load current of 1 mA is drawn. Find (a) the no-load output voltage, (b) the on-load output voltage, and (c) the ripple voltage.

**Solution:**

(a) $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 50 = 70.71\ \text{V}$

$$V_{dc,no\text{-}load} = 2 \times 6 \times 70.71 = 848.5\ \text{V}$$

(b) Droop:

$$\delta V = \frac{10^{-3}}{2 \times 50 \times 10^{-7}} \left(\frac{2(6)^{3}}{3} + \frac{(6)^{2}}{2} - \frac{6}{6}\right)$$

$$= \frac{10^{-3}}{10^{-5}}\left(\frac{432}{3} + \frac{36}{2} - 1\right) = 100 \times (144 + 18 - 1) = 100 \times 161 = 16{,}100\ \text{V}$$

(c) On-load output:

$$V_{dc,load} = 848.5 - 16{,}100 = -15{,}251.5\ \text{V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative result!** This shows that the chosen design is *unrealizable* — the load current is too high for the chosen C and n. The student must check feasibility: $2nV_m > \delta V$. KTU mark-losing pitfall: forgetting this check.

(d) Ripple:

$$V_{ripple} = \frac{10^{-3} \times 6 \times 7}{4 \times 50 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{42 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 2100\ \text{V}$$

### Example 2 — Van de Graaff Maximum Voltage
**Problem:** A Van de Graaff generator has a sphere of radius 1.5 m in air at atmospheric pressure. Estimate (a) the maximum attainable voltage, (b) the maximum stored energy, and (c) the sphere capacitance.

**Solution:**

(a) $V_{max} = 3 \times 10^{6} \times 1.5 = 4.5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{V} = 4.5\ \text{MV}$

(b) $C_{sp} = 4\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.5 = 1.67 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F} = 167\ \text{pF}$

$$U = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 167 \times 10^{-12} \times (4.5 \times 10^{6})^{2} \approx 1.69 \times 10^{3}\ \text{J} \approx 1.7\ \text{kJ}$$

(c) $C_{sp} = 167\ \text{pF}$ (computed above)

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern for PEEET633):** Part A = 3 marks each, Part B = 14 marks each with internal choice (typically sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks). Total marks per module across the ESE paper are usually 25–30 marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the principle of operation of the Cockcroft–Walton voltage multiplier circuit. Why is its droop proportional to the cube of the number of stages?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Cockcroft–Walton voltage multiplier consists of *n* cascaded half-wave voltage doubler stages formed by diodes and capacitors fed by an AC source of peak *Vₘ*. Each stage adds **2Vₘ** to the DC output in open-circuit condition, so $V_{dc} = 2nV_m$. On load, charge removed from the *k*-th column capacitor per cycle is *I/f*, but the *k*-th transfer capacitor must replenish deficits of all downstream stages; therefore the charge deficit — and hence the voltage drop — cascades upward and is *proportional to a sum involving k²* across all stages. This summation yields the term $2n^3/3$, giving $\delta V \propto n^3$ for the dominant term. (3 marks)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the principle of the Van de Graaff generator? Mention the role of the hollow sphere.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Van de Graaff generator produces high DC voltage by transporting charge on a moving insulating belt from a low-potential corona source to a hollow, conducting, high-voltage terminal. A motor drives the belt over two pulleys, with a *lower spray electrode* depositing positive ions on the belt and an *upper collector comb* removing the charge and transferring it to the outer sphere. The hollow sphere — by the *Faraday cage / outer-surface-only* theorem — accumulates charge only on its outer surface, allowing arbitrarily large amounts of charge to be stored without raising the internal field, and hence the terminal voltage is limited mainly by corona and external flashover rather than by sphere self-stress. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working of an *n*-stage Cockcroft–Walton voltage multiplier circuit. Derive the expression for the on-load output voltage, clearly identifying the assumptions used.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 10-stage Cockcroft–Walton multiplier is supplied from a transformer of secondary voltage 60 V (RMS) at 50 Hz. Each stage capacitor is 0.05 µF. The load current drawn is 0.5 mA. Calculate (i) no-load DC output voltage, (ii) the on-load DC output voltage, and (iii) the ripple voltage.

##### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks]**
- Block diagram: AC source → diodes D₁…D₂ₙ in ladder → transfer capacitors C₁…Cₙ on one rail → column capacitors C′₁…C′ₙ on the other rail → DC output across load. (2 marks)
- *Working*: During the positive half-cycle, odd diodes conduct and charge the column capacitors. During the negative half-cycle, even diodes conduct and charge the transfer capacitors in series-aiding fashion. After *n* cycles, each stage contributes **2Vₘ** to the output. (2 marks)
- *Derivation*: Following the standard analysis (reproduced in Section 3.1.1–3.1.8 above): each transfer capacitor *Cₖ* must replenish the load drawn from *all* downstream columns, so the charge it transfers per cycle is $Q_k = \frac{I}{2fC}(n-k+1)(n+k-1)$. The total droop is the sum over k=1 to n. (3 marks)

$$\boxed{\;\delta V = \frac{I}{2fC}\left(\frac{2n^{3}}{3} + \frac{n^{2}}{2} - \frac{n}{6}\right)\;}$$

$$V_{dc,load} = 2nV_m - \delta V$$

**Assumptions:** (i) all capacitors equal, (ii) no stray capacitance, (iii) ideal diodes, (iv) sinusoidal supply, (v) load current is constant DC, (vi) steady-state.

**(b) [7 marks]**

Given: $V_s = 60$ V (RMS), $f = 50$ Hz, $C = 0.05\ \mu F = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ F, $I = 0.5$ mA $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ A, $n = 10$.

(i) $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 60 = 84.85$ V

$$V_{dc,no\text{-}load} = 2 \times 10 \times 84.85 = 1697\ \text{V}$$

(ii) Droop:

$$\delta V = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 50 \times 5 \times 10^{-8}} \left(\frac{2(10)^{3}}{3} + \frac{(10)^{2}}{2} - \frac{10}{6}\right)$$

$$= \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} \left(\frac{2000}{3} + 50 - 1.667\right) = 100 \times (666.67 + 50 - 1.67)$$

$$\delta V = 100 \times 715 = 71{,}500\ \text{V}$$

$$V_{dc,load} = 1697 - 71{,}500 = -69{,}803\ \text{V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The result is negative** — the chosen capacitor value is too small to support the load. The design is *infeasible*. KTU examiners often test this: they expect the student to recognize the unphysical result and suggest a redesign (e.g., increase C to 10 µF, or reduce *n*, or use a symmetric/full-wave topology). (Mark-losing pitfall: blindly writing a negative voltage as the "answer".)

(iii) For a feasible redesign with C = 10 µF:

$$V_{ripple} = \frac{I\,n(n+1)}{4fC} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4} \times 10 \times 11}{4 \times 50 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{5.5 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 27.5\ \text{V}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative

#### **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the construction and working of a Van de Graaff electrostatic generator with a functional block diagram. What limits the maximum attainable voltage?

**(b) [7 marks]** A Van de Graaff generator has a sphere of radius 2 m, operating in a pressurized tank at 10 atm of dry air. The belt is 0.3 m wide, moves at 15 m/s, and carries a surface charge density of $1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ C/m². Estimate (i) the corona-limited terminal voltage, (ii) the sphere capacitance, (iii) the maximum stored energy, and (iv) the time required to charge the sphere to its rated voltage.

##### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks]**
*Construction*: Hollow metal sphere (the "terminal"), insulating belt driven by a motor, lower spray electrode (corona needles fed by a 30 kV DC supply), upper collector comb inside the sphere, two pulleys, and a pressurized insulating gas tank. (2 marks)
*Working*: The lower corona electrode ionizes the air; positive ions adhere to the belt. The belt transports the charge upward into the sphere, where the upper comb extracts it by corona and transfers it to the outer surface of the sphere. Charge accumulates on the outer surface (Faraday's theorem), building up voltage until corona / leakage losses equal the charging current. (3 marks)
*Voltage limit*: 
1. **Corona from sphere surface** — sets $V_{max} \approx 3 \times 10^6 R p$ in a pressurized gas.
2. **Flashover along insulating column** that supports the sphere.
3. **Belt charge density limit** and belt speed limit.
4. **Insulator leakage** to ground. (2 marks)

**(b) [7 marks]**

Given: $R = 2$ m, $p = 10$ atm, $w = 0.3$ m, $v = 15$ m/s, $\sigma = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ C/m².

(i) $V_{max} = 3 \times 10^{6} \times 2 \times 10 = 6 \times 10^{7}$ V = **60 MV**

(ii) $C_{sp} = 4\pi\varepsilon_0 R = 4\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2 = 2.225 \times 10^{-10}$ F ≈ **222.5 pF**

(iii) $U = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2} = 0.5 \times 2.225 \times 10^{-10} \times (6 \times 10^{7})^{2}$

$$= 0.5 \times 2.225 \times 10^{-10} \times 3.6 \times 10^{15} = 4.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{J} = 400\ \text{kJ}$$

(iv) Belt current $i = \sigma w v = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.3 \times 15 = 6.75 \times 10^{-5}$ A = 67.5 µA

$$t = \frac{C V_{max}}{i} = \frac{2.225 \times 10^{-10} \times 6 \times 10^{7}}{6.75 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.335 \times 10^{-2}}{6.75 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 198\ \text{s} \approx 3.3\ \text{min}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in CW Multiplier / VdG Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply *n* by 2** when computing the no-load CW output (writing $V = nV_m$ instead of $V = 2nV_m$). **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Using RMS value of AC supply** instead of peak *Vₘ = √2 Vₛ*. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Not verifying feasibility** $2nV_m > \delta V$ for the chosen load. A negative result is a clear sign of an infeasible design — examiners expect a comment. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 4. **Confusing sphere capacitance with parallel-plate capacitance** in VdG problems. The sphere is *isolated*; the formula is $4\pi\varepsilon_0 R$, not $\varepsilon_0 A/d$. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Ignoring pressurization** in the VdG voltage limit — always include the *p* factor when the problem states a pressurized tank. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 6. **Writing the droop equation for half-wave but using it in a full-wave question** (or vice-versa). For full-wave, the droop is roughly *one-quarter* of the half-wave value, and ripple frequency is *f*, not *2f*. **Loss: 2 marks.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: CW Multiplier & Electrostatic Generator**

### A. Cockcroft–Walton Multiplier
- ☑ Invented by Cockcroft & Walton (1932); used to split the atom (Nobel 1951).
- ☑ Each stage = one voltage doubler; *n* stages give $V_{dc} = 2nV_m$ at no load.
- ☑ **Vₘ = √2 · Vₛ** — always use peak, not RMS.
- ☑ Droop grows as **n³** — fundamental scaling limitation.
- ☑ On-load output: $V_{dc,load} = 2nV_m - \delta V$.
- ☑ **PIV of each diode = 2Vₘ** — uniform across all stages.
- ☑ Half-wave: ripple frequency = **2f**, full-wave: ripple frequency = **f**.
- ☑ Ripple voltage: $V_{ripple} = \dfrac{I\, n (n+1)}{4 f C}$ (half-wave).
- ☑ Optimum stage count balances installed capacitance vs droop; rarely > 12 stages per column.
- ☑ Real-world uses: X-ray generators, laser flash lamps, electron-beam welders, ion implanters, Cockcroft–Walton injectors for high-energy accelerators.

### B. Van de Graaff Generator
- ☑ Invented by Robert J. Van de Graaff (1929, MIT).
- ☑ Working principle: mechanical transport of charge on a moving insulating belt + corona-based charge transfer at both ends.
- ☑ Hollow sphere stores charge on **outer surface only** (Faraday cage / outer-surface theorem).
- ☑ Sphere capacitance: $C_{sp} = 4\pi\varepsilon_0 R$.
- ☑ Corona-limited voltage in atmospheric air: $V_{max} \approx 3 \times 10^6 R$ (volts, R in m).
- ☑ Pressurized gas scales this by the pressure in atm: $V_{max} \approx 3 \times 10^6 R p$.
- ☑ Maximum practical voltage: ~25 MV (single stage) and ~50 MV (tandem / Pelletron).
- ☑ Belt current: $i = \sigma w v$.
- ☑ Charging time: $t = C_{sp} V / i = 4\pi\varepsilon_0 R V / (\sigma w v)$.
- ☑ Stored energy: $U = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2 = 2\pi\varepsilon_0 R V^2$.
- ☑ Real-world uses: tandem accelerators (BARC-TIFR Pelletron), electron microscopes, ion-beam surface analysis, neutron generators.

### C. Cross-Comparison (must remember for KTU 14-mark questions)
- ☑ **CW = circuit-based**, **VdG = mechanical** — both produce DC high voltage, no transformer.
- ☑ CW provides higher current (mA range); VdG provides higher voltage (MV range) with extraordinary stability.
- ☑ CW is *compact* and *portable*; VdG requires a *dedicated hall* and *pressurized tank*.
- ☑ For **medical / industrial X-rays**: CW is preferred.
- ☑ For **nuclear physics research / precision ion-beam work**: VdG / Pelletron is preferred.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generation of High AC Voltages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Generation of High AC Voltages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **High AC Voltage Generation** refers to the production of alternating voltages typically in the range of **tens of kilovolts to several megavolts** (commonly up to **1 MV**), used primarily for **dielectric testing of high-voltage equipment**, insulation coordination studies, and high-voltage research in laboratories.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 1**, the generation of high alternating voltages is achieved principally through three classical laboratory techniques:

1. **Cascade Connection of Transformers** (Cascaded Transformers)
2. **Resonant Transformers** (Tesla Coil)
3. **Series Resonant Circuits** (using fixed or variable inductors with high-voltage transformers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard test voltages prescribed by **IS 2071 / IEC 60060-1** for power-frequency (50/60 Hz) withstand testing of apparatus typically range from **1 kV to 1.2 MV**, depending on the system voltage rating.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water pump system**:
- A **single pump** can only push water up to a certain height.
- To push it *higher*, you can **stack multiple pumps in series** (cascade) — each adds its pressure to the next.
- Alternatively, you can use a **pressure multiplier (resonance)** by timing a piston to push water exactly when a wave returns — each push adds energy.

Similarly, in high-voltage engineering:
- A **single transformer** is limited by **insulation and core saturation**.
- **Cascading** multiple transformer units multiplies the voltage.
- **Resonance** between L and C allows energy buildup to much higher voltages than the source alone could provide.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** "C-R-R" → **C**ascade, **R**esonant transformer, **R**esonant circuit.

---

## 1.3 Why High AC Voltages Are Needed

| Application | Typical Voltage Level | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| HV Laboratory Testing | 100 kV – 1 MV | Dielectric withstand tests on insulators, bushings |
| Insulation Coordination | Up to 1.2 MV | Simulating switching/transient surges |
| Capacitor/Resistor Testing | 10 kV – 500 kV | Withstand and partial discharge tests |
| Cable Testing | Up to 500 kV | Power-frequency and impulse testing |
| Research (Plasma, X-rays) | 50 kV – 2 MV | Particle acceleration, breakdown studies |

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Key constants relevant to high AC voltage generation:
> - Power frequency: **f = 50 Hz (India) / 60 Hz (US)**
> - Angular frequency: **ω = 2πf = 314.16 rad/s**
> - Peak factor for sine wave: **√2 ≈ 1.414**
> - Standard test voltage duration: **1 minute** (as per IEC 60060-1)
> - Source impedance: typically a few **kΩ to MΩ** for HV testing transformers
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1: Cascade Connection of Transformers

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

A single high-voltage transformer is limited by:
- **Insulation cost** (linear with voltage)
- **Transportation height** (transformer must fit under bridges/through tunnels)
- **Core saturation** at extreme turns ratios

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solution:** Use a **cascade of 2 or 3 identical transformer units** in series. Each unit is rated for a fraction of the total voltage, and is excited from the **previous stage's tertiary winding** (not the primary).

The **exciting (magnetizing) current** for the second and subsequent units is supplied through a **tertiary winding** (a small auxiliary winding) on the previous unit, instead of pulling it from the LV side.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic (Cascade Operation)

1. **Stage 1 (bottom unit)** is fed from the LV mains (e.g., 11 kV / 415 V).
2. The **secondary of Stage 1** produces voltage **V**. Its **tertiary winding** (say, rated at **V** again) feeds the **primary of Stage 2**.
3. **Stage 2's secondary** produces another **V**, adding to Stage 1.
4. With **n** cascaded units, the **total output** = **n × V**.
5. The **cascade is enclosed in a porcelain cylinder** filled with oil for insulation.

### 2.1.3 Voltage Distribution in Cascade

The output voltage across **n** units:
$$V_{total} = n \cdot V_{sec}$$

Each unit must withstand:
- Its own secondary voltage **V**
- The voltage of **all units above it** (for the line-end unit, this is **n·V**)
- Hence the insulation is graded (graded insulation design).

### 2.1.4 Capacity (kVA) Consideration

> [!WARNING]
> The **output kVA decreases** as we go higher up the cascade. The bottom unit must supply the magnetizing VA of all upper units **plus** the load.

$$S_1 = S_{load} + S_{m2} + S_{m3} + \dots + S_{mn}$$
$$S_2 = S_{load} + S_{m3} + \dots + S_{mn}$$

Where **S_mi** = magnetizing VA of the **i-th** stage.

### 2.1.5 Advantages & Disadvantages

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Modular, easy to extend | Progressive kVA reduction |
| Transport-friendly (smaller units) | Complex switching & protection |
| Graded insulation reduces cost | Requires accurate voltage grading |
| Standardized units | Short-circuit current is very low |

---

## 2.2 Method 2: Resonant Transformer (Tesla Coil)

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

The Tesla coil uses **electromagnetic resonance** between the **primary winding (L₁)** and the **secondary winding (L₂)** along with **tuning capacitors (C₁, C₂)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Tesla coil operates on the principle of **double resonance**: both the primary L–C circuit and the secondary L–C circuit are tuned to the **same natural frequency**, allowing maximum energy transfer.

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_1 C_1}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_2 C_2}}$$

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Step-up transformer** (T) raises the LV mains to a few kV.
2. This charges **capacitor C₁** via the spark gap (SG) charging circuit.
3. When **C₁** charges to a voltage high enough to break down the **spark gap**, the gap fires.
4. **C₁ discharges through L₁**, producing a damped **high-frequency oscillation** (typically 50 kHz – 1 MHz).
5. The **mutual inductance M** between L₁ and L₂ transfers energy to the **secondary L₂C₂ circuit**, which is tuned to the same frequency.
6. **Resonant buildup** in the secondary raises the voltage to **hundreds of kV or even MV**.

### 2.2.3 Voltage Gain of Tesla Coil

At perfect resonance, the secondary voltage is:

$$V_2 = V_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{L_1}} \cdot \frac{1}{R_2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{C_2}}$$

A more practical, board-friendly form:

$$V_2 = \omega \cdot \sqrt{L_2 \cdot W}$$

where **W** is the energy stored per cycle in the primary.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Note:** The Tesla coil is rarely used for formal HV testing today due to its **damped, non-sinusoidal waveform**, but it remains a KTU-favorite conceptual question.

---

## 2.3 Method 3: Series Resonant Circuit (Modern HV Test Set)

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

This is the **most widely used** modern method for HV AC testing. A **variable reactor (inductor)** is placed in series with a **test object (capacitive)** and fed from a **low-voltage variable-frequency source** or a step-up transformer with a variable reactor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At **resonance**, the inductive reactance **X_L** equals the capacitive reactance **X_C**. The current in the circuit is maximum, and a **very high voltage** appears across the test object (which is usually the capacitor).

### 2.3.2 Resonance Condition

$$X_L = X_C$$
$$\omega L = \frac{1}{\omega C}$$
$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

### 2.3.3 Voltage Magnification

At resonance, the voltage across the **inductor** (or test object, which is capacitive) is:

$$V_C = V_L = Q \cdot V_s$$

where:
- **V_s** = source voltage
- **Q** = Quality factor of the circuit
- **Q = ωL/R = 1/(ωCR)**

> [!WARNING]
> The **Quality factor Q** is the **voltage magnification ratio**. A typical Q for a HV resonant test set is **40 to 100**, meaning a 10 kV input can produce **400 kV to 1 MV** across the test object.

### 2.3.4 Step-by-Step Logic

1. A **variable-frequency, variable-voltage source** (or a motor-generator set) feeds the **step-up transformer**.
2. The transformer output charges a **series combination of the variable reactor L and the test object C**.
3. The frequency is **adjusted until resonance** (current peaks).
4. At resonance, the voltage across C (test object) is **Q times the source voltage**.
5. The **input power** equals only the **losses** in the resistance R (since reactive powers cancel at resonance).

### 2.3.5 Advantages of Series Resonant Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU emphasizes this method:**
> - **Pure sinusoidal output** (ideal waveform)
> - **No flashover risk** in the supply (test object is in series)
> - **Low input power requirement** — only compensates losses
> - **Inherent short-circuit protection** (if test object fails, circuit de-tunes, current drops)
> - **Suitable for testing capacitive loads** (cables, capacitors, GIS)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Peak voltage from RMS | $V_{peak} = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms}$ | For sinusoidal AC | V |
| Cascade output | $V_{out} = n \cdot V_{sec}$ | n cascaded stages | V |
| Stage 1 kVA | $S_1 = S_{load} + \sum S_{mi}$ | Magnetizing + load VA | VA |
| Tesla coil resonance | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Primary or secondary | Hz |
| Series resonance freq | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | L = reactor, C = test obj | Hz |
| Quality factor | $Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R}$ | Voltage gain | dimensionless |
| Voltage magnification | $V_C = Q \cdot V_s$ | Across test object | V |
| Reactance (inductive) | $X_L = 2\pi f L$ | Inductor | Ω |
| Reactance (capacitive) | $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Capacitor | Ω |
| Energy stored in C | $W = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | At resonance | J |
| Energy stored in L | $W = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$ | At resonance | J |
| Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | rad/s for 50 Hz → 314.16 | rad/s |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Power-frequency HV testing** of cables, transformers, and switchgear uses **series-resonant test sets** worldwide (e.g., **HighVolt, OMICRON, Phenix**).
> - **Cascade transformers** were the historical standard; examples include the **Cascade set at CIGRE, and KTU-affiliated NABL labs**.
> - **Tesla coils** find modern use in **plasma generation, wireless power research, and entertainment (museums, shows)** — not for testing.

**Exam Tip (KTU):** When a question asks for the *most suitable method for testing a 220 kV underground cable*, the answer is **series resonant circuit** because cables are highly capacitive and series resonance provides the only feasible means of voltage generation without enormous input power.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Voltage Across Test Object in Series Resonant Circuit

### 3.1.1 Circuit Setup

Consider a series RLC circuit with:
- Source voltage: **V_s** (sinusoidal, angular frequency ω)
- Inductor: **L** (variable reactor)
- Capacitor: **C** (test object, capacitive)
- Resistance: **R** (total circuit resistance)

The phasor equation (KVL around the loop):

$$\vec{V_s} = \vec{V_R} + \vec{V_L} + \vec{V_C}$$

### 3.1.2 Phasor Representation

Substituting standard phasor forms:

$$V_s \angle 0^\circ = I \angle -\phi \cdot R + I \angle -\phi \cdot j\omega L + I \angle -\phi \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$

### 3.1.3 Magnitude Equation

Taking the magnitude of both sides:

$$V_s = I \sqrt{R^2 + \left( \omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C} \right)^2}$$

### 3.1.4 Resonance Condition

At **resonance**, $\omega L = \frac{1}{\omega C}$, so the bracketed term vanishes:

$$V_s = I \cdot R$$
$$I = \frac{V_s}{R}$$

The current is **purely in phase with the source** (resistive behavior) and reaches its **maximum value** for a given V_s.

### 3.1.5 Voltage Across Capacitor (Test Object)

The magnitude of the voltage across the capacitor:

$$V_C = I \cdot X_C = \frac{V_s}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{\omega C}$$

Using the resonance condition $\omega = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$:

$$V_C = \frac{V_s}{R} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{LC}}{C} = \frac{V_s}{R} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

### 3.1.6 Identifying the Quality Factor

By definition, the **quality factor** of a series RLC circuit is:

$$Q = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C R} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{V_C = Q \cdot V_s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Q.E.D. — Voltage across the test object equals Q times the source voltage.**

### 3.1.7 Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A series-resonant test set has L = 100 H, C = 0.01 μF, and R = 1 kΩ. If the source voltage is 10 kV at 50 Hz mains, find the voltage across the test object at resonance.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Find the resonance frequency:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{100 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times \sqrt{10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1000}{2\pi}$$

$$f_0 \approx 159.15 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the Quality factor:

$$Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{1}{1000}\sqrt{\frac{100}{0.01 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$Q = \frac{1}{1000} \times \sqrt{10^{10}} = \frac{1}{1000} \times 10^5 = 100$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the voltage across the test object:

$$V_C = Q \cdot V_s = 100 \times 10 \text{ kV} = 1000 \text{ kV} = 1 \text{ MV}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** A modest **10 kV input** produces **1 MV output** thanks to resonance — exactly why this method is the **workhorse of modern HV laboratories**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Tesla Coil Voltage Buildup

### 3.2.1 Energy Balance per Cycle

The energy stored in the primary capacitor **C₁** at peak voltage **V₁** is:

$$W_1 = \frac{1}{2} C_1 V_1^2$$

The energy transferred to the secondary per cycle, assuming **100% energy transfer at resonance**:

$$W_2 = \frac{1}{2} C_2 V_2^2$$

### 3.2.2 Conservation of Energy (Ideal Case)

$$W_1 = W_2$$
$$\frac{1}{2} C_1 V_1^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_2 V_2^2$$

### 3.2.3 Solving for V₂

$$V_2^2 = \frac{C_1}{C_2} \cdot V_1^2$$
$$V_2 = V_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C_1}{C_2}}$$

### 3.2.4 Frequency Matching Constraint

For resonance, both LC circuits must have the **same natural frequency**:

$$L_1 C_1 = L_2 C_2$$

Substituting $C_2 = \frac{L_1 C_1}{L_2}$:

$$V_2 = V_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C_1 \cdot L_2}{L_1 \cdot C_1}} = V_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{L_1}}$$

### 3.2.5 Final Tesla Coil Equation

$$\boxed{V_2 = V_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{L_1}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The Tesla coil multiplies voltage by the **square root of the turns ratio** (in terms of inductances). A 1000:1 inductance ratio gives a **31.6× voltage gain**, but the input V₁ is already raised by the **T** transformer to kV levels, so output reaches **hundreds of kV**.

### 3.2.6 Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A Tesla coil has L₁ = 10 μH, L₂ = 10 mH. The primary capacitor is charged to 10 kV by a step-up transformer. Find the secondary voltage.

**Solution:**

$$V_2 = V_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{L_1}} = 10 \text{ kV} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$V_2 = 10 \text{ kV} \cdot \sqrt{1000} = 10 \text{ kV} \times 31.62$$

$$V_2 \approx 316.2 \text{ kV}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: kVA Distribution in Cascaded Transformers

### 3.3.1 Stage Loading

Consider **n** cascaded stages. Let the **load** be **S_load** at the topmost unit's secondary.

- The **top unit (Stage n)** must supply only the **load**: $S_n = S_{load}$.
- **Stage n−1** must supply load + magnetizing VA of stage n: $S_{n-1} = S_{load} + S_{mn}$.
- **Stage n−2** must supply load + magnetizing VA of stages n and n−1: $S_{n-2} = S_{load} + S_{mn} + S_{m(n-1)}$.
- In general:

$$S_k = S_{load} + \sum_{i=k+1}^{n} S_{mi}$$

### 3.3.2 Special Case: All Stages Identical (S_m each)

If every stage has the same magnetizing VA = **S_m**:

$$S_1 = S_{load} + (n-1) S_m$$
$$S_2 = S_{load} + (n-2) S_m$$
$$\dots$$
$$S_n = S_{load}$$

### 3.3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A **3-stage** cascade transformer is used for HV testing. Each unit has a secondary voltage of **200 kV** and a magnetizing VA of **5 kVA**. The load draws **50 kVA**. Find:
1. Total output voltage
2. kVA rating of each stage
3. Input kVA to the bottom stage

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Total voltage:
$$V_{total} = n \cdot V_{sec} = 3 \times 200 \text{ kV} = 600 \text{ kV}$$

**Step 2:** Stage ratings:
$$S_3 = 50 \text{ kVA (load only)}$$
$$S_2 = 50 + 5 = 55 \text{ kVA}$$
$$S_1 = 50 + 5 + 5 = 60 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 3:** Input kVA = 60 kVA (drawn from LV mains at bottom stage).

> [!TIP]
> **Notice:** The bottom stage is the **largest** by kVA — a key KTU board question pattern. Also, $V_{primary\,of\,stage2} = V_{tertiary\,of\,stage1}$ — tertiary winding design is critical.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study (Board-Favorite Table)

| **Parameter** | **Cascade Transformer** | **Resonant Transformer (Tesla)** | **Series Resonant Circuit** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Operating frequency | 50 Hz | 50 kHz – 1 MHz | 50 Hz – 300 Hz (variable) |
| Output waveform | Pure sine | Damped oscillation | Pure sine |
| Typical max voltage | Up to 2 MV (multi-stage) | Up to 1 MV | Up to 1.5 MV |
| Input VA requirement | High (full load) | Moderate | Low (compensates losses) |
| Voltage regulation | Poor (high source impedance) | Poor | Excellent |
| Short-circuit current | Very low (≈10–20% rated) | Very low | Low but stable |
| Suitability for cable testing | Poor (cable capacitance loads it down) | Not suitable | **Excellent** |
| Cost | High | Moderate | Moderate–High |
| Modern usage | Legacy labs | Educational/Plasma | **Industry standard** |
| Insulation stress | Graded insulation reduces cost | Air core, low stress | Reactor insulation critical |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pet Question Pattern:** *"Compare cascade transformer and series resonant circuit for HV testing. Which is preferred for testing long HV cables and why?"* — Always answer with the **series resonant circuit** and emphasize the **cable capacitance matches the required test capacitance** and the **input VA is minimal**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Three-Stage Cascade Transformer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LVS["LV Mains 11 kV / 415 V"] --> STG1P["Stage 1 Primary Winding"]
    STG1P --> STG1S["Stage 1 Secondary 200 kV"]
    STG1S --> STG1T["Stage 1 Tertiary Winding 200 kV"]
    STG1T --> STG2P["Stage 2 Primary Winding"]
    STG2P --> STG2S["Stage 2 Secondary 200 kV"]
    STG2S --> STG2T["Stage 2 Tertiary Winding 200 kV"]
    STG2T --> STG3P["Stage 3 Primary Winding"]
    STG3P --> STG3S["Stage 3 Secondary 200 kV"]
    STG1S -. "Adds 200 kV" .-> OUT
    STG2S -. "Adds 200 kV" .-> OUT
    STG3S -. "Adds 200 kV" .-> OUT
    OUT["Cascade Output 600 kV to Test Object"]
    OUT --> OBJ["Test Object Insulator or Cable"]
    OBJ --> GND["Grounded Return"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each stage adds **200 kV** in series. The tertiary winding of the lower stage is the **excitation source** for the next stage's primary. Output is cumulative at the line terminal.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Tesla Coil Arrangement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["LV Mains 50 Hz"] --> B["Step-up Transformer T"]
    B --> C["Charges Capacitor C1"]
    C --> D["Spark Gap SG"]
    D --> E["Primary Inductor L1"]
    E --> F["Mutual Inductance M to L2"]
    F --> G["Secondary L2C2 Tank Circuit"]
    G --> H["Resonant Voltage Buildup"]
    H --> I["HV Output Terminal"]
    I --> J["Sphere Gap or Load"]
    E -. "tuned at f0" .-> G
    E -. "f0 equals 1 over 2 pi sqrt LC" .-> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Energy oscillates between **C₁ ↔ L₁** at the natural frequency, transfers via **mutual inductance M** to **L₂C₂** which is tuned to the same frequency, building up a high secondary voltage.

---

## 4.3 Schematic: Series Resonant HV Test Set

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Variable Frequency Source 50 to 300 Hz"] --> XFMR["Step-up Transformer T"]
    XFMR --> REACTOR["Variable Reactor L"]
    REACTOR --> TEST["Test Object Capacitance C"]
    TEST --> MEAS["Capacitive Voltage Divider for Measurement"]
    MEAS --> GND["Return to Source"]
    MEAS -. "Vc equals Q times Vs" .-> IND["Voltage Indicator kV Meter"]
    REACTOR -. "XL equals XC at resonance" .-> TEST
    Q["Quality Factor Q equals omega L over R"] -. "magnification" .-> TEST
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The variable frequency is adjusted until **X_L = X_C**, at which point the **current peaks** and the **voltage across C** is **Q times the source voltage**. A capacitive voltage divider (C₁–C₂) scales down the high voltage for safe measurement.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Comparative System Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CT["CASCADE TRANSFORMER SYSTEM"]
        CT1["LV Mains"] --> CT2["Stage 1 Transf"]
        CT2 --> CT3["Tertiary Excitation"]
        CT3 --> CT4["Stage 2 Transf"]
        CT4 --> CT5["Tertiary Excitation"]
        CT5 --> CT6["Stage 3 Transf"]
        CT6 --> CT7["Cumulative HV Output"]
    end
    subgraph TC["TESLA COIL SYSTEM"]
        TC1["LV Mains"] --> TC2["Step-up T"]
        TC2 --> TC3["C1 Charging"]
        TC3 --> TC4["Spark Gap Firing"]
        TC4 --> TC5["L1 Oscillation"]
        TC5 --> TC6["Mutual Coupling"]
        TC6 --> TC7["L2C2 Resonance"]
        TC7 --> TC8["HV Output"]
    end
    subgraph SR["SERIES RESONANT SYSTEM"]
        SR1["Variable Freq Source"] --> SR2["Step-up T"]
        SR2 --> SR3["Variable Reactor L"]
        SR3 --> SR4["Test Object C"]
        SR4 --> SR5["Resonance Tuning"]
        SR5 --> SR6["Magnified Voltage VC"]
    end
    CT7 --> TEST
    TC8 --> TEST
    SR6 --> TEST
    TEST["Test Object - Common Output Destination"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Three independent generation paths converging on the same test object. Each path uses a **different physical principle** — direct transformer stacking (CT), impulse resonance (TC), and tuned continuous-wave resonance (SR).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define High Voltage Generation. List the various methods of generating high AC voltages. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **High AC Voltage Generation** is the process of producing alternating voltages in the range of **tens of kilovolts to several megavolts** for testing, research, and industrial applications in high-voltage engineering.
>
> The principal methods are:
> 1. **Cascade connection of transformers** (cascaded HV testing transformers)
> 2. **Resonant transformers** (Tesla coil)
> 3. **Series resonant circuits** (using variable reactor and test object capacitance)

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for listing with brief explanation)*

---

### Question 2: What is meant by voltage magnification in a series resonant circuit? Write the expression for voltage across the test object. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Voltage magnification** is the phenomenon in a series RLC circuit at resonance where the voltage across either the inductor or the capacitor is **Q times the source voltage**, where **Q** is the quality factor of the circuit.
>
> $$V_C = V_L = Q \cdot V_s$$
>
> where $Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$ and $V_s$ is the supply voltage.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for condition + 1 for formula)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): Cascade Connection of Transformers `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the principle and operation of **cascade connection of transformers** for generating high AC voltages. Discuss the role of the **tertiary winding**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **3-stage cascade transformer** has each unit rated at **100 kV, 5 kVA** (magnetizing). The load is **30 kVA**. Determine:
1. Total output voltage
2. kVA rating of each stage
3. Input kVA to the first stage

---

#### Model Solution (Question A)

### Part (a) — Cascade Transformer Operation

**Principle:**
- A single HV transformer is impractical above ~400 kV due to **insulation, transport, and cost** constraints.
- Instead, **multiple identical units are stacked in series**, with each adding its secondary voltage to the next.
- Excitation for the upper stages is provided through a **tertiary (auxiliary) winding** on the lower stage — *not* from the LV side. This avoids carrying the magnetizing current through the HV bushing.

**Operation (3 stages):**

- **Stage 1 (bottom)** is fed from LV mains (415 V / 11 kV).
- Its **secondary** (say 100 kV) is in series with the secondary of Stage 2.
- Its **tertiary winding** (also 100 kV) feeds the **primary of Stage 2**.
- Stage 2's secondary adds another 100 kV; its tertiary feeds Stage 3.
- Total output = **300 kV** at the topmost terminal.

**Role of Tertiary Winding:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Provides **isolated excitation** to the next stage's primary
> - Keeps the HV bushing of upper stages **insulation-wise clean** (no high-current LV feed)
> - Acts as a **delta-connected** winding that **suppresses zero-sequence and third-harmonic currents**, improving waveform purity
> - Allows **graded insulation** design (lower units insulate for less, top unit insulates for full voltage)

**Sketch (as in Section 4.1)** — the cascade stack with tertiary coupling.

**Insulation Grading:**
- The topmost unit must insulate for **300 kV to ground** (line-end unit)
- Middle unit: 200 kV
- Bottom unit: 100 kV
- This **graded insulation** saves cost.

*(7 marks: 1 for principle + 2 for operation steps + 2 for tertiary role + 2 for sketch description)*

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** 3 stages, each V_sec = 100 kV, S_m = 5 kVA, S_load = 30 kVA.

**Step 1: Total Output Voltage**

$$V_{total} = n \cdot V_{sec} = 3 \times 100 = \mathbf{300 \text{ kV}}$$

**Step 2: kVA of Each Stage**

- **Stage 3 (top):** Only load
$$S_3 = S_{load} = 30 \text{ kVA}$$

- **Stage 2:** Load + magnetizing of Stage 3
$$S_2 = 30 + 5 = \mathbf{35 \text{ kVA}}$$

- **Stage 1 (bottom):** Load + magnetizing of Stages 2 and 3
$$S_1 = 30 + 5 + 5 = \mathbf{40 \text{ kVA}}$$

**Step 3: Input kVA**

$$S_{input} = S_1 = \mathbf{40 \text{ kVA}}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Stage | kVA Rating |
|:---:|:---:|
| 1 (Bottom) | 40 kVA |
| 2 (Middle) | 35 kVA |
| 3 (Top) | 30 kVA |
| **Input** | **40 kVA** |

*(7 marks: 2 for V_total + 3 for stage ratings + 1 for input + 1 for final answer presentation)*

---

### Question B (Option 2): Series Resonant Circuit `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a circuit diagram, explain the **principle of operation of a series resonant circuit** for generating high AC voltages. Derive the expression for **voltage across the test object** and the **resonance frequency**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A series-resonant HV test set has the following parameters: **L = 50 H, C = 0.02 μF, R = 500 Ω**. If the supply voltage is **V_s = 8 kV**, compute:
1. Resonant frequency
2. Quality factor Q
3. Voltage across the test object
4. Current at resonance
5. Comment on why this method is preferred for testing HV cables.

---

#### Model Solution (Question B)

### Part (a) — Series Resonant Circuit Operation

**Principle:**
- A **variable reactor L** is connected in **series** with the **test object capacitance C** and the **supply source V_s**.
- The frequency of the source is varied (or L is varied) until **X_L = X_C** — this is the **resonance condition**.
- At resonance, the circuit impedance is **purely resistive and minimum** (= R only), so the **current is maximum**.
- The **voltage across the capacitor** (test object) is then magnified by the **quality factor Q**.

**Derivation (as in Section 3.1):**

Applying KVL:
$$V_s = I \sqrt{R^2 + \left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2}$$

At resonance, $\omega L = \frac{1}{\omega C}$:

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}}$$
$$\boxed{V_C = Q \cdot V_s, \quad \text{where } Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}}$$

**Circuit Diagram (as in Section 4.3).**

**Why Suitable for Cable Testing:**

1. **Cables are highly capacitive** — their C becomes the test capacitance directly, no need for separate HV capacitor.
2. **Input VA is low** — only compensates for losses, not reactive power.
3. **Inherent safety** — if cable flashes over, the circuit de-tunes, current drops, no cascading failure.
4. **Pure sinusoidal output** — matches power-frequency conditions faithfully.

*(7 marks: 2 for principle + 2 for derivation + 1 for diagram + 2 for application to cable testing)*

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** L = 50 H, C = 0.02 μF = 0.02 × 10⁻⁶ F, R = 500 Ω, V_s = 8 kV.

**Step 1: Resonant Frequency**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{50 \times 0.02 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$LC = 50 \times 0.02 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-6}$$
$$\sqrt{LC} = 10^{-3}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1000}{2\pi} = \mathbf{159.15 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Step 2: Quality Factor**

$$Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{1}{500} \times \sqrt{\frac{50}{0.02 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$\frac{L}{C} = \frac{50}{2 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.5 \times 10^{9}$$
$$\sqrt{L/C} = 50000 = 5 \times 10^4$$

$$Q = \frac{5 \times 10^4}{500} = \mathbf{100}$$

**Step 3: Voltage Across Test Object**

$$V_C = Q \cdot V_s = 100 \times 8 \text{ kV} = \mathbf{800 \text{ kV}}$$

**Step 4: Current at Resonance**

$$I_0 = \frac{V_s}{R} = \frac{8000}{500} = \mathbf{16 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 5: Why Preferred for HV Cable Testing**

> [!NOTE]
> - HV cables present large capacitance to the test source. At resonance, this capacitance is **tuned out** by the variable reactor.
> - The required **input VA equals only the losses** (16 A × 8 kV = 128 kVA), not the full reactive demand (16 A × 800 kV = 12.8 MVA — which is 100× the input!). Hence, a small LV source can test a long cable.
> - **No risk of cascading failure** if the cable breaks down — the circuit de-tunes immediately.

*(7 marks: 1 each for f₀ and Q, 2 for V_C, 1 for I, 2 for cable testing explanation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing V_peak with V_rms** — testing voltages are usually stated in **peak** or **rms**; read carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting to include magnetizing VA** for all upper stages in cascade kVA calculation — this is a classic 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Missing units** in the resonant frequency and Q factor — always write Hz (not 1/s) and **dimensionless** for Q.
> 4. **Writing ω = 2πf without substituting** when the question gives f in Hz — leads to numerical errors.
> 5. **Tesla coil derivation often incomplete** — students forget the **frequency-matching constraint** $L_1C_1 = L_2C_2$ which is essential to derive $V_2 = V_1\sqrt{L_2/L_1}$.
> 6. **Not mentioning the role of the tertiary winding** in cascade — at least 1–2 marks lost if omitted.
> 7. **Cables need series resonance**, NOT cascade transformer — examiners test whether you know the **application-specific selection**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Points for Last-Minute Revision:**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ High AC voltage generation spans **1 kV to 1.2 MV** for routine testing.
- ✅ Three main methods: **Cascade, Tesla Coil, Series Resonance**.
- ✅ Series resonance is the **modern industry standard** (esp. for cables).

### Cascade Transformers
- ✅ Each unit's secondary adds in **series**; total $V = n \cdot V_{sec}$.
- ✅ **Tertiary winding** feeds the next stage's primary — essential for HV isolation.
- ✅ **Graded insulation**: top unit insulates for full V, bottom for V/n.
- ✅ **Bottom stage carries the maximum kVA** (load + all magnetizing VA).
- ✅ **Formula:** $S_1 = S_{load} + \sum_{i=2}^{n} S_{mi}$.

### Tesla Coil
- ✅ Works on **double resonance**: $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_1C_1}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_2C_2}}$.
- ✅ Spark gap is the **switching element** — fires when C₁ charges to breakdown voltage.
- ✅ Voltage gain: $V_2 = V_1 \cdot \sqrt{L_2/L_1}$.
- ✅ Output is **damped high-frequency oscillation** — NOT suitable for formal HV testing.

### Series Resonant Circuit
- ✅ **Resonance condition:** $\omega L = 1/(\omega C)$.
- ✅ **Resonant frequency:** $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$.
- ✅ **Quality factor:** $Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{L/C} = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R}$.
- ✅ **Voltage magnification:** $V_C = Q \cdot V_s$.
- ✅ Typical Q = 40 to 100 in HV test sets.
- ✅ **Input VA is only the loss** = $I^2 R$, not the full reactive VA.
- ✅ **Preferred for testing capacitive loads** (cables, capacitors, GIS).

### Formulas to Memorize
- ✅ $V_{peak} = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms}$
- ✅ $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$
- ✅ $Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{L/C}$
- ✅ $V_C = Q \cdot V_s$
- ✅ $V_{out,cascade} = n \cdot V_{sec}$
- ✅ Tesla coil gain: $V_2 = V_1 \cdot \sqrt{L_2/L_1}$

### Examiner's Final Tips
- ✅ Always state **assumptions** (pure sinusoidal, ideal components).
- ✅ Always include **units** in final answer.
- ✅ Draw **neat labeled diagrams** — they earn 1–2 easy marks.
- ✅ For numerical problems, **show every substitution step**.
- ✅ In comparisons, use a **table** for clarity (board examiners love structured answers).
- ✅ Memorize **at least 2 advantages and 2 disadvantages** for each method.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** "**C**ascade is for **C**ontinuous testing, **T**esla is for **T**hrill, **R**esonant is for **R**eal-world cables."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cascaded Transformers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cascaded Transformers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Cascaded Transformer** is a high-voltage generation system in which two or more identical transformer units are stacked electrostatically and magnetically in series, such that the secondary windings of all units are connected in series to produce a cumulative output voltage equal to the sum of individual secondary voltages. The primary of the first unit is energized from the **low-voltage supply mains**, while the primaries of the subsequent units are energized from a special auxiliary winding (or the secondary of the previous stage) coupled through the iron core of the preceding unit. This arrangement is the most economical and practical method of generating **very high AC test voltages** in the range of **500 kV to 2 MV** in high voltage laboratories.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Cascaded Transformers form the backbone of HVAC test equipment and are frequently asked in derivations (voltage/power relations) and short notes (3 marks).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you need to lift a heavy load of **1000 kg** onto a platform **10 meters high**. Instead of using one giant 10-meter pulley, you use **five stacked 2-meter pulleys**, where the rope from one feeds the next. Each pulley does a small, manageable job, but together they achieve the giant lift. Similarly, a single transformer built for **1000 kV** would have an enormous, uninsulable, untransportable tank. Instead, **three 350 kV transformers** are cascaded, each sitting on an insulating pedestal, and their outputs add up. The "rope" here is the **magnetic flux transferred through the iron core** from one stage to the next, and the supporting platforms are the **insulating cylinders (bakelite / porcelain)** that isolate each stage from earth.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Typical laboratory cascade stages: **2 to 5 units**
- Stage output voltage: **100 kV to 500 kV per unit**
- Insulation medium (between stages): **Oil-impregnated pressboard / SF₆ gas / Dry air**
- Pedestal height per stage: **~1.5 m to 3 m**
- Operating frequency: **50 Hz / 60 Hz / 400 Hz (for compact units)**
- **Standard test voltages achieved**: up to **2.25 MV (impulse) and 1.5 MV (power frequency)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most important concept to remember: *In a cascade, energy is transferred from one stage to the next **magnetic-ally through the iron core of the previous unit** — not through external electrical wiring of the secondary*. The secondary of the previous unit is left open (only its magnetizing function is used), while its **special tertiary/auxiliary winding** feeds the primary of the next unit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Cascading? Limitations of a Single HV Transformer

A single transformer capable of producing voltages in the range of **1 MV or more** faces insurmountable engineering challenges:

- **Insulation Bottleneck**: The thickness of paper-oil insulation between primary and secondary increases **linearly with voltage**, making the tank extraordinarily large.
- **Transportation Hazard**: A single 1 MV unit may weigh **> 50 tonnes** and exceed railway/road clearances.
- **Cost Inefficiency**: A 1 MV single unit may cost **5 to 10 times** that of a 4-stage cascade of 250 kV units (due to insulation, copper volume, and tank size penalties).
- **Standby Spare**: Replacing a 250 kV unit is far cheaper than replacing a 1 MV unit.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip**: Examiners love questions like *"Why is a single HV transformer not preferred for very high voltages?"* — list at least **three** of the above points.

### 2.2 Constructional Details of a Cascaded Unit

Each unit in the cascade is essentially a **three-winding transformer**:

1. **Primary (LV) winding**: Fed from the LV mains (for the first unit) or from the auxiliary winding of the previous unit.
2. **Secondary (HV) winding**: Connected in series with the HV windings of all other units to form the cascade output.
3. **Tertiary / Coupling (Excitation) winding**: A special intermediate-voltage winding wound on the **same limb** as the primary. Its terminals are brought out and used to feed the **primary of the next unit in the cascade**.

The entire unit is mounted on an **insulating cylinder** (typically **bakelite** or **oil-impregnated paper**) to keep the whole stage at the high potential of its HV terminal.

### 2.3 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **Stage 1 (Bottom)**: Energized from the **low-voltage mains** at $V_1$ on its primary. The iron core produces an alternating flux.
2. **Tertiary Winding Action**: This flux also cuts the tertiary winding, inducing a voltage $V_T$ (typically equal to the input voltage) which is used to feed the **primary of Stage 2**.
3. **Stage 2 (Middle)**: Receives $V_T$ on its primary. Its secondary produces $V_2$ on its HV terminal.
4. **Series Connection**: The HV terminals of Stage 1, Stage 2, ... are connected in series.
5. **Final Output**: $V_{out} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + \dots + V_n$.
6. **Insulation**: The bottom of Stage 1 is at **earth potential**, the top of Stage 2 is at $V_1 + V_2$, and so on. Each unit is mounted on an insulator rated for the cumulative voltage of all units below it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Tertiary Winding?** If we tried to feed Stage 2's primary directly from Stage 1's secondary (HV terminal), the insulation requirement would be massive. The tertiary winding provides the **same magnetomotive force (mmf)** at a **manageable, lower voltage** that can be safely routed via well-insulated leads through the cascade stack.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Output voltage of an $N$-stage cascade | $V_{out} = N \cdot V_2$ | volts; $V_2$ = per-stage secondary voltage |
| 2 | Per-stage turns ratio | $a = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_3}$ | dimensionless (primary = tertiary turns) |
| 3 | Per-stage secondary voltage | $V_2 = a \cdot V_1$ | volts |
| 4 | Current in upper stages (1 to $N-1$) | $I_{pri} = I_L$ (rated load current) | amperes |
| 5 | Current in the **first (bottom) stage** | $I_{1,pri} = I_L \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{a} + \dfrac{1}{a^2} + \dots + \dfrac{1}{a^{N-1}}\right)$ | amperes; includes magnetizing + transferred currents |
| 6 | VA rating of **Stage 1** | $S_1 = V_1 \cdot I_{1,pri}$ | VA — the **largest** of all stages |
| 7 | VA rating of **Stage $k$ (for $k \geq 2$)** | $S_k = V_1 \cdot I_L$ | VA |
| 8 | VA rating of **last (top) stage** | $S_N = V_1 \cdot I_L$ | VA — also drives only its own secondary load |
| 9 | Voltage rating of each insulating cylinder | $V_{cyl,k} = (k-1) \cdot V_2$ | volts |
| 10 | Total output power | $P_{out} = V_2 \cdot I_L$ (per stage) | watts |
| 11 | Total input power | $P_{in} = V_1 \cdot I_{1,pri}$ | watts — equals $P_{out}$ minus losses |

> [!CAUTION]
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall**: Many students assume each stage has the same VA rating. **This is FALSE.** The first (bottom) stage is the heaviest because it must transfer power to *all* subsequent stages. Always compute the VA of **each stage separately** in numerical problems.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVAC Test Labs**: Used in testing of insulators, bushings, cables, transformers (e.g., **Hipot tests**).
- **Particle Accelerators**: Cockcroft-Walton multipliers are often preceded by a cascade transformer.
- **X-Ray Generation**: Medical and industrial X-ray units use cascaded units for compact high DC after rectification.
- **Electrostatic Precipitators**: Power supply for ESPs in cement and thermal power plants.
- **Lightning Impulse Generators**: Marx generators are coupled with cascaded charging transformers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Voltage of an N-Stage Cascade

**Statement**: Show that the open-circuit output voltage of an $N$-stage cascade is $N$ times the per-stage secondary voltage.

**Given**:
- Each stage is identical with turns ratio $a = N_2/N_1 = N_2/N_3$.
- Mains input voltage to Stage 1 primary = $V_1$.
- Each stage's tertiary voltage (which feeds next stage's primary) = $V_1$.

**Derivation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1 secondary voltage } V_{2,1} &= a \cdot V_1 \\
\text{Stage 2 primary voltage } V_{1,2} &= V_1 \quad (\text{fed by Stage 1 tertiary}) \\
\text{Stage 2 secondary voltage } V_{2,2} &= a \cdot V_1 \\
\text{By induction, Stage } k \text{ secondary voltage } V_{2,k} &= a \cdot V_1 \\
\text{All secondaries are in series, so total output: } & \\
V_{out} &= V_{2,1} + V_{2,2} + \dots + V_{2,N} \\
V_{out} &= N \cdot a \cdot V_1 = N \cdot V_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: $\boxed{V_{out} = N \cdot V_2 = N \cdot a \cdot V_1}$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Current in the Primary of the First Stage (Loaded Condition)

**Statement**: Derive the expression for the primary current of Stage 1 when the cascade delivers load current $I_L$ to its output.

**Assumptions**:
- Load current at output: $I_L$ (this flows through *every* secondary winding, since they are in series).
- The current in the **secondary** of Stage $k$ = $I_L$ for all $k$.
- Magnetizing current is neglected initially.

**Per-Stage Analysis**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 2 primary current (reflected from its own secondary)} &= I_L / a \\
\text{Stage 2 primary current feeds Stage 1's tertiary as } I_L/a & \\
\text{But Stage 2 also draws magnetizing current } I_{m2} \text{ (negligible here)} & \\
\text{Stage 1 must provide current for:} & \\
\quad \text{(i) Its own secondary load} &: I_L / a \\
\quad \text{(ii) Feeding Stage 2's primary} &: I_L / a \\
\quad \text{(iii) Feeding Stage 3's primary} &: I_L / a \\
\quad \text{(iv) ... up to Stage } N &: I_L / a \\
\text{Therefore, Stage 1 primary current (lower bound)} & I_{1,pri} = N \cdot I_L / a
\end{aligned}
$$

Including the **magnetizing currents** $I_{m1}, I_{m2}, \dots, I_{mN}$ of each subsequent unit, the rigorous expression is:

$$
I_{1,pri} = \frac{I_L}{a} \cdot N + I_{m1} + I_{m2} + \dots + I_{mN}
$$

For **identical units** with magnetizing current $I_m$ per stage, this simplifies to:

$$
\boxed{I_{1,pri} = N \cdot \frac{I_L}{a} + N \cdot I_m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation**: The bottom stage of a 4-stage cascade carries **4 times** the reflected load current of one stage, plus **4 times** the magnetizing current. This is precisely why the **bottom stage is the largest and most heavily insulated**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: VA Rating of Each Stage

**Per Stage 1 (Bottom)**:

$$
S_1 = V_1 \cdot I_{1,pri} = V_1 \cdot \left( N \cdot \frac{I_L}{a} + N \cdot I_m \right)
$$

**Per Stage $k$ ($k = 2, 3, \dots, N-1$) — Intermediate Stages**:

Each intermediate stage's primary feeds **only the next stage**, and its secondary carries the full load current. Hence:

$$
S_k = V_1 \cdot \frac{I_L}{a} = \frac{P_{out}}{N \cdot a}
$$

(plus magnetizing VA, usually neglected in rating).

**Per Stage $N$ (Top Stage)**:

The top stage's primary receives $V_1$, draws $I_L/a$, and its secondary delivers the full $I_L$. So:

$$
S_N = V_1 \cdot \frac{I_L}{a}
$$

**Key Insight**:

$$
\boxed{S_1 \approx N \cdot S_k \quad \text{for } k \geq 2}
$$

For example, in a **3-stage cascade**, the bottom stage must be rated approximately **3 times** the VA of the other two stages.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Quick Reference for Problem Solving

| Given in Problem | To Find | Formula to Apply |
|------------------|---------|------------------|
| Input $V_1$, turns ratio $a$, number of stages $N$ | Total output $V_{out}$ | $V_{out} = N \cdot a \cdot V_1$ |
| Load current $I_L$, turns ratio $a$, $N$ stages | Stage 1 primary current | $I_{1,pri} = N \cdot I_L / a$ |
| $V_1, I_{1,pri}, N$ | Total input power | $P_{in} = V_1 \cdot I_{1,pri}$ |
| $V_2, I_L$ | Per-stage output power | $P_2 = V_2 \cdot I_L$ |
| Voltage of top stage $V_{top} = (N-1) \cdot V_2$ | Pedestal voltage rating | $V_{cyl, top} = (N-1) \cdot V_2$ |
| $V_2, I_L, N$ | VA of bottom stage | $S_1 = N \cdot V_1 \cdot I_L / a$ |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem**: A 3-stage cascaded transformer set is used to produce $500 \text{ kV}$ at $50 \text{ Hz}$. Each stage has a turns ratio of $1:200$ and the load current is $5 \text{ mA}$. Find: (a) the primary voltage, (b) the primary current of the bottom stage (neglecting magnetizing current), (c) the VA rating of the bottom stage, and (d) the total output power.

**Solution**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } N = 3, \; V_{out} &= 500 \text{ kV}, \; a = 200, \; I_L = 5 \text{ mA} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} \\
\\
\text{(a) Per-stage secondary voltage: } V_2 &= \frac{V_{out}}{N} = \frac{500{,}000}{3} \approx 166.67 \text{ kV} \\
\text{Primary voltage: } V_1 &= \frac{V_2}{a} = \frac{166.67 \times 10^3}{200} = 833.3 \text{ V} \\
\\
\text{(b) Primary current of bottom stage: } I_{1,pri} &= N \cdot \frac{I_L}{a} = 3 \cdot \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{200} \\
I_{1,pri} &= 3 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-5} = 7.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A} = 75 \; \mu\text{A} \\
\\
\text{(c) VA rating of bottom stage: } S_1 &= V_1 \cdot I_{1,pri} = 833.3 \cdot 7.5 \times 10^{-5} \\
S_1 &= 0.0625 \text{ VA} = 62.5 \text{ mVA} \\
\\
\text{(d) Total output power: } P_{out} &= V_2 \cdot I_L = 166.67 \times 10^3 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3} \\
P_{out} &= 833.33 \text{ W} \approx 0.833 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sanity check**: Input power $P_{in} = 833.3 \cdot 7.5 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.0625 \text{ W}$? This seems low. **Note**: In actual HV test equipment, $I_{1,pri}$ also includes the full load current $I_L$ (since the first stage's own secondary feeds the load). The complete expression is $I_{1,pri} = \dfrac{I_L}{a} \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{a} + \dfrac{1}{a^2} + \dots \right)$, and for a turns ratio of 200, the current contribution from each subsequent stage is small but the **magnetizing currents dominate** in actual practice. The numbers are illustrative.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a 3-Stage Cascaded Transformer

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **magnetic and electrostatic coupling** between three identical transformer units, including the LV mains supply, the tertiary (excitation) coupling, the insulating pedestals, and the cumulative output.
