### 4.3 Schematic Annotation Table

| Symbol in Diagram | Physical Component | Function |
|-------------------|-------------------|----------|
| LV Mains | Single-phase AC source, 230 V to 11 kV | Energizes the bottom stage primary |
| Stage 1 Primary (P1) | LV winding | Receives mains voltage |
| Stage 1 Tertiary (T1) | Intermediate-voltage coupling winding | Feeds Stage 2 primary magnetically |
| Stage 1 Secondary (S1) | HV winding | Adds $V_2$ to the series string |
| Iron Core | Common magnetic circuit | Transfers flux from primary to secondary and tertiary |
| Insulating Pedestal | Bakelite / porcelain cylinder | Isolates the stage electrically from earth |
| Series Connection | External HV link between stages | Stacks voltages additively |
| Top HV Terminal | Final output node | At potential $N \cdot V_2$ above earth |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define a cascaded transformer. Why is it preferred over a single high-voltage transformer for generating very high AC test voltages?*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

A **cascaded transformer** is an arrangement of two or more identical transformer units connected in series such that the secondary windings add their voltages cumulatively, and the primary of each subsequent unit is energized through a tertiary (coupling) winding of the previous unit.

It is preferred over a single HV transformer for the following reasons:
1. **Insulation Simplicity**: A 1 MV single unit requires enormous oil-paper insulation. Cascading splits the stress across stages.
2. **Transportation**: A 1 MV unit may weigh 50+ tonnes and exceed transport limits; 250 kV units are easily moved.
3. **Cost-Effectiveness**: Cascaded units cost significantly less than an equivalent single unit due to economy of repeated identical construction.
4. **Modularity & Maintenance**: Faulty units can be replaced individually; spares are cheaper.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any two reasons.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the function of the tertiary (coupling) winding in a cascaded transformer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

The tertiary (coupling) winding performs the following functions in a cascaded transformer:
1. **Energy Transfer Medium**: It magnetically couples the iron core of one unit to the primary of the next unit, transferring energy between stages without requiring a high-voltage electrical connection.
2. **Voltage Step-Down for Insulation**: It provides a manageable intermediate voltage (typically equal to the input mains voltage) to feed the next stage's primary, avoiding the need to route the HV terminal through thick insulation.
3. **mmf Balancing**: It provides the magnetomotive force required by the next stage without drawing the magnetizing current from the high-voltage side.

*(Examiner's note: 2 functions × 1.5 marks each, or all 3 at 1 mark each.)*

---

### Part B Question (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Model]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a **3-stage cascaded transformer** used for HVAC testing. Clearly identify the function of the primary, secondary, and tertiary windings and the role of the insulating pedestals. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 3-stage cascaded transformer has a per-stage turns ratio of $1:250$ and is energized from a $1 \text{ kV}$, $50 \text{ Hz}$ mains. If the cascade delivers a load current of $10 \text{ mA}$, calculate:
   (i) the total output voltage, *(2 marks)*
   (ii) the primary current of the bottom stage, neglecting magnetizing current, *(2 marks)*
   (iii) the VA rating of the bottom stage, and *(1 mark)*
   (iv) the total input power. *(2 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Question A**:

**Part (a) — Schematic and Working (7 marks)**:

Refer to **Section 4 (Diagrams)** above for the schematic. The construction and working explanation:

- **Construction**: Three identical transformer units, each consisting of a common **iron core** carrying three windings:
  1. **Primary (LV)**: Low-voltage winding rated for the mains input.
  2. **Tertiary (Coupling)**: Intermediate-voltage winding, equal turns to the primary (1:1 with primary).
  3. **Secondary (HV)**: High-voltage winding with turns ratio $a$ relative to the primary.
  Each unit is mounted on an **insulating cylinder (bakelite/porcelain)** of height 1.5–3 m, capable of withstanding the cumulative voltage of all units below it.

- **Working**:
  1. Stage 1 primary is energized from the LV mains.
  2. The resulting AC flux in Core I induces voltages in the tertiary and secondary of Stage 1.
  3. The tertiary feeds Stage 2's primary via an insulated lead passing through Pedestal 1.
  4. The HV secondary of Stage 1 is connected in series with Stage 2's secondary, and so on.
  5. The total output at the top of the cascade is $V_{out} = 3 \cdot V_2$, with the bottom of Stage 1 at earth potential.
  6. The insulating pedestals support the **electrostatic potential gradient** and prevent flashover to earth.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks schematic, 2 marks winding identification, 3 marks working explanation.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**:

**Given**:
- Number of stages: $N = 3$
- Turns ratio per stage: $a = 250$
- Mains input voltage: $V_1 = 1 \text{ kV} = 1000 \text{ V}$
- Load current: $I_L = 10 \text{ mA} = 0.01 \text{ A}$

**(i) Total Output Voltage**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2 &= a \cdot V_1 = 250 \times 1000 = 250{,}000 \text{ V} = 250 \text{ kV} \\
V_{out} &= N \cdot V_2 = 3 \times 250 \text{ kV} = 750 \text{ kV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Primary Current of Bottom Stage** (neglecting magnetizing current):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{1,pri} &= N \cdot \frac{I_L}{a} = 3 \times \frac{0.01}{250} \\
I_{1,pri} &= 3 \times 4 \times 10^{-5} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A} = 0.12 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) VA Rating of Bottom Stage**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= V_1 \cdot I_{1,pri} = 1000 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \\
S_1 &= 0.12 \text{ VA} = 120 \text{ mVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Total Input Power**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= V_1 \cdot I_{1,pri} = 1000 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4} = 0.12 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Confusing the rating**: Many students compute $S_1 = N \cdot V_2 \cdot I_L$ instead of $V_1 \cdot I_{1,pri}$. **The correct definition of VA rating uses primary-side voltage and primary-side current** of *that* stage.
> 2. **Forgetting the turns ratio**: A common mistake is to write $I_{1,pri} = N \cdot I_L$ without dividing by $a$. Always remember: load current is reflected through $a$.
> 3. **Magnetizing current**: In real problems, $I_m$ is often given (e.g., 5% of full-load current). Don't forget to add it: $I_{1,pri} = N \cdot I_L/a + N \cdot I_m$.
> 4. **Units**: Mark deduction if kV and V are mixed without conversion. Always show unit conversion in the first step.
> 5. **Power loss**: If the problem mentions efficiency $\eta$, multiply $P_{in}$ by $1/\eta$ for the *actual* required input power.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **output voltage** and **primary current of the bottom stage** of an $N$-stage cascaded transformer. State clearly the assumptions made. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 4-stage cascaded transformer set is used to produce $1 \text{ MV}$, $50 \text{ Hz}$ AC. The turns ratio of each stage is $1:240$. Calculate:
   (i) the per-stage secondary voltage, *(1 mark)*
   (ii) the input mains voltage, *(1 mark)*
   (iii) the rated current in each HV secondary winding if the load current is $2 \text{ mA}$, *(1 mark)*
   (iv) the VA rating of the bottom stage, *(2 marks)*
   (v) the voltage that the **top-most insulating pedestal** must withstand. *(2 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Question B**:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**:

*(Refer to Section 3.1 and 3.2 above for the full derivation. The student should:)*

1. Define the turns ratio $a = N_2/N_1$.
2. State the assumption: all stages identical, magnetizing current neglected for the current derivation.
3. Show $V_{out} = N \cdot a \cdot V_1$.
4. Use current reflection $I_{pri} = I_L/a$ per stage.
5. Sum contributions: $I_{1,pri} = N \cdot I_L/a$ + magnetizing terms.
6. Mention that the bottom stage carries the largest current, hence has the highest VA rating.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks assumptions, 3 marks derivation of voltage, 2 marks derivation of current.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**:

**Given**: $N = 4$, $V_{out} = 1 \text{ MV} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ V}$, $a = 240$, $I_L = 2 \text{ mA} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}$.

**(i) Per-stage secondary voltage**:

$$
V_2 = \frac{V_{out}}{N} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{4} = 250{,}000 \text{ V} = 250 \text{ kV}
$$

**(ii) Input mains voltage**:

$$
V_1 = \frac{V_2}{a} = \frac{250{,}000}{240} \approx 1041.67 \text{ V} \approx 1.04 \text{ kV}
$$

**(iii) Rated HV secondary current**:

The current in each HV secondary winding equals the load current (since they are in series):

$$
I_{HV} = I_L = 2 \text{ mA}
$$

**(iv) VA rating of bottom stage**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{1,pri} &= N \cdot \frac{I_L}{a} = 4 \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{240} \\
I_{1,pri} &= 4 \cdot 8.333 \times 10^{-6} = 3.333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A} \\
S_1 &= V_1 \cdot I_{1,pri} = 1041.67 \times 3.333 \times 10^{-5} \\
S_1 &\approx 0.0347 \text{ VA} = 34.7 \text{ mVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) Voltage on top-most pedestal**:

The top pedestal separates the **top of Stage 3** from the **bottom of Stage 4**. The potential difference is the sum of all secondary voltages below it, i.e., $3 \cdot V_2$:

$$
V_{ped, top} = (N-1) \cdot V_2 = 3 \times 250 \text{ kV} = 750 \text{ kV}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Part (b)**:
> 1. **Pedestal Voltage Error**: Some students use $N \cdot V_2$ instead of $(N-1) \cdot V_2$. The top pedestal separates the **third** and **fourth** stages, not the fourth from earth.
> 2. **Misidentifying which pedestal**: The bottom pedestal of a 4-stage cascade supports only $V_2$ (i.e., 250 kV), while the top pedestal supports $3 V_2$ (750 kV). All pedestals have **different** voltage ratings.
> 3. **Off-by-one in series connection**: Count the number of secondary windings **in series between** the two terminals of the pedestal.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Print This Section!**

- **Cascaded Transformer Definition**: Multiple identical transformer units with secondaries in **series** and primaries fed through **tertiary/coupling** windings of preceding units.
- **Output Voltage**: $V_{out} = N \cdot a \cdot V_1$ (per stage adds $V_2$).
- **First-Stage Current**: $I_{1,pri} = N \cdot (I_L / a) + N \cdot I_m$ (magnetizing included).
- **Bottom-Stage VA Rating**: $S_1 = V_1 \cdot I_{1,pri}$ — the **largest** among all stages.
- **Other Stages VA Rating**: $S_k = V_1 \cdot (I_L/a) + I_m \cdot V_1$ for $k = 2, \dots, N$.
- **Insulating Pedestal Voltage**: $V_{ped, k} = (k-1) \cdot V_2$ for the pedestal beneath Stage $k$.
- **Why cascade?**: Insulation feasibility, transport, cost, modular maintenance.
- **Tertiary function**: Magnetic energy transfer between stages at a manageable voltage level.
- **Operating frequency**: Usually **50 Hz** (India). For compact units, **400 Hz** is used (smaller iron core).
- **Typical applications**: HV testing of insulators, cables, bushings; Cockcroft-Walton multipliers; X-ray units; ESPs.
- **Most asked question type**: Derive output voltage expression + numerical on VA rating of bottom stage.
- **Common pitfall**: Treating all stages as equal VA — **WRONG**. Always highlight the **asymmetric** VA distribution.
- **Examiner's favourite catch**: If efficiency $\eta$ is given, ask for *input* kVA, not just apparent VA.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series Resonant Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Series Resonant Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Series Resonant Circuit** (also called a *Series RLC Circuit under Resonance* or *Variable Inductance Resonant Test System*) is a single-loop electrical network comprising a resistance (R), an inductance (L), and a capacitance (C) connected in **series** with a sinusoidal voltage source, in which the inductive reactance (X_L) and the capacitive reactance (X_C) become **equal in magnitude but opposite in phase** at a specific frequency called the **resonant frequency (f₀ or ω₀)**.

Mathematically, the condition of series resonance is:

$$X_L = X_C \implies \omega_0 L = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 1):** Series resonant circuits are studied in HV Engineering primarily in the context of **AC High-Voltage Testing**. The capacitive test object (e.g., a power cable, transformer winding, or insulator) is used as the capacitor `C` of the resonant tank, while a **variable reactor (inductor)** is tuned to achieve resonance with the supply frequency (50/60 Hz). This dramatically reduces the input power demand from the mains.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Swing and the Pusher":**
> Imagine pushing a child on a swing (the swing = the circuit, the child = oscillating energy between L and C). If you push at *exactly* the swing's natural rhythm, very small pushes produce large swinging amplitudes. If you push at the wrong rhythm, the swing barely moves and the energy is dissipated.
> In the **series resonant circuit**, the inductor "pushes" current one way, while the capacitor "pushes" it the opposite way. At resonance, they **cancel each other's opposition perfectly**, leaving only the small resistance `R` to limit the current. The result: a *huge* current circulates inside the loop, producing a *huge* voltage across L and C — even though the source supplies only a small voltage. This is called **voltage magnification** (factor = Q).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Resonant Angular Frequency:** $\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ (in **rad/s**)
- **Resonant Frequency:** $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ (in **Hz**)
- **Quality Factor (Q):** A dimensionless number; for HV resonant test sets, **Q is typically 10 to 100**.
- **Power Frequency:** **50 Hz (India) / 60 Hz (US)** — the supply frequency for which the inductor is tuned.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this important for KTU exams?**
> Most numerical problems in Module 1 ask you to compute the inductance required to resonate with a *given* test-object capacitance at *power frequency*, or to find the voltage magnification and the input VA demand. Memorize the four key formulas: $f_0$, $Q$, $V_L = V_C = QV$, and $P_{in} = P_{loss}/Q$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Series RLC Circuit

Consider a series loop of R, L, and C supplied by a sinusoidal source $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)**:

$$v(t) = v_R(t) + v_L(t) + v_C(t)$$

$$V_m \sin(\omega t) = R i(t) + L\frac{di(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{C}\int i(t)\,dt$$

In phasor form (using $V = V_m/\sqrt{2}$ as the RMS value):

$$\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I}\left[R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)\right] = \mathbf{I}\cdot \mathbf{Z}$$

where the **complex impedance** is:

$$\mathbf{Z} = R + jX \quad \text{with} \quad X = \omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}$$

### 2.1.1 The Three Operating Regimes

| Regime | Frequency Range | Net Reactance X | Circuit Behavior | Current |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Below Resonance** | $\omega < \omega_0$ | $X < 0$ (Capacitive) | Capacitor dominates | Leads voltage |
| **At Resonance** | $\omega = \omega_0$ | $X = 0$ | Purely resistive; Z = R (minimum) | In-phase with V; **maximum** |
| **Above Resonance** | $\omega > \omega_0$ | $X > 0$ (Inductive) | Inductor dominates | Lags voltage |

## 2.2 Why Does Resonance Matter in HV Engineering?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Engineering "Why" — Voltage Magnification:**
> In HV laboratories, generating high AC voltages (e.g., 500 kV to 5 MV) directly from the mains is impractical. A step-up transformer rated for such voltages would require a **massive kVA rating** (often 10–100 MVA). However, a **series resonant circuit** exploits the fact that at resonance:
>
> 1. The **input current** from the source is **minimum** (limited only by small R).
> 2. The **voltage developed across L and across C** is **Q times the source voltage**.
> 3. Therefore, a **low-voltage, low-kVA source** can produce a **very high voltage** across the test object (which is the capacitor C).
> 4. **Power saving factor = Q** (typically 20–50×).
>
> This is the principle behind **Resonant Test Sets (RTS)**, **AC Resonant Test Systems**, and **inductively-tuned cable testing rigs**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Golden Four" Formulas — memorize these for KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resonant Angular Frequency | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | rad/s | Independent of R |
| 2 | Resonant Frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz | The tuning target |
| 3 | Quality Factor (Series) | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 R C}$ | dimensionless | $Q \geq 10$ for HV use |
| 4 | Voltage Magnification | $V_L = V_C = Q \cdot V_S$ | V | Across L or C individually |
| 5 | Input Impedance at Resonance | $Z_0 = R$ (minimum) | Ω | Purely resistive |
| 6 | Current at Resonance | $I_0 = \dfrac{V_S}{R}$ (maximum) | A | RMS value |
| 7 | Half-Power Bandwidth | $\Delta f = \dfrac{f_0}{Q}$ | Hz | Sharpness of tuning |
| 8 | Lower & Upper Cutoff Frequencies | $f_1 = f_0 - \dfrac{\Delta f}{2}$,  $f_2 = f_0 + \dfrac{\Delta f}{2}$ | Hz | 3-dB points |
| 9 | Inductance for Tuning | $L = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0^{2} C} = \dfrac{1}{(2\pi f_0)^{2} C}$ | H | The variable reactor value |
| 10 | Power Supplied by Source | $P_{in} = \dfrac{P_{loss}}{Q} = I^{2} R$ | W | Reduced by factor Q |
| 11 | Power Dissipated in R | $P_{loss} = I^{2} R$ | W | Heat loss in circuit |
| 12 | Reactive Power Circulating | $Q_{reactive} = I^{2} X_L = I^{2} X_C$ | VAR | Swaps between L and C |

> [!TIP]
> **Units Tip (KTU exam-friendly):** When capacitance is given in **μF or nF** and you want L in **H or mH**, remember: $1\,\mu F = 10^{-6}\,F$ and $(2\pi \cdot 50)^{2} = 98696 \approx 9.87 \times 10^{4}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HV Cable Testing:** XLPE cables have very high capacitance (≈ 0.2–0.5 μF/km). Series resonance enables 50 Hz testing at cable-rated voltage with low input kVA.
- **GIS (Gas-Insulated Switchgear) Testing:** Each phase section is capacitive (~ nF range).
- **Transformer Induced Voltage Testing:** Used as a *resonant load* on the LV side.
- **Lightning Impulse & Switching Impulse Calibrations** (oscillator circuits).
- **Damped Oscillatory Wave Generators** for steep-front testing.
- **Wireless Power Transfer** and **RF filters** (non-HV applications, same math).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resonant Frequency

**Step 1 — Setup the KVL equation** for the series RLC loop with phasor impedance:

$$\mathbf{Z}(\omega) = R + j\omega L - j\frac{1}{\omega C} = R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Define resonance** as the condition where the **net reactive part is zero** (the circuit is purely resistive):

$$\text{Im}\{\mathbf{Z}\} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 L - \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for ω₀** by isolating $\omega_0$:

$$\omega_0 L = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C}$$

$$\omega_0^2 \, L \, C = 1$$

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to frequency in Hz** using $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$:

$$2\pi f_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The resonant frequency depends *only* on L and C, not on R. Doubling L (or C) reduces $f_0$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$. This is why a *variable inductor* is the preferred tuning element in HV resonant test sets — the test object's C is fixed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quality Factor (Q)

**Step 1 — Define Q** as the ratio of the **reactive power** oscillating in the inductor to the **real power** dissipated in the resistance, evaluated at resonance:

$$Q = \frac{\text{Reactive Power}}{\text{Real Power}} = \frac{I^2 \cdot X_L}{I^2 \cdot R} = \frac{X_L}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** $X_L = \omega_0 L$ and $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$:

$$Q = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \cdot \frac{L}{R} = \frac{L}{R\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{\sqrt{L}}{R\sqrt{C}} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

**Step 3 — Alternate form** using $X_C = 1/(\omega_0 C)$:

$$Q = \frac{X_C}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega_0 R C}$$

**Step 4 — Boxed result (three equivalent forms):**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega_0 R C} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Voltage Magnification Across L and C

**Step 1 — Compute the current** at resonance (R is the only impedance):

$$I_0 = \frac{V_S}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the voltage across the inductor** (magnitude):

$$V_L = I_0 \cdot X_L = \frac{V_S}{R} \cdot \omega_0 L = V_S \cdot \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = V_S \cdot Q$$

**Step 3 — Compute the voltage across the capacitor** (magnitude):

$$V_C = I_0 \cdot X_C = \frac{V_S}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} = V_S \cdot \frac{1}{\omega_0 R C} = V_S \cdot Q$$

**Step 4 — Boxed result:**

$$\boxed{V_L = V_C = Q \cdot V_S}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial KTU takeaway:** The two voltages are **180° out of phase** with each other, so they **cancel in the KVL sense** (around the loop, $V_L$ and $V_C$ sum to zero), but each is **Q times larger** than the source voltage in magnitude. This is the heart of HV resonant testing — a 230 V mains input can produce tens of kV across the test object.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bandwidth and Selectivity

**Step 1 — Define bandwidth** as the frequency range over which the current is at least $1/\sqrt{2}$ (i.e., -3 dB) of its maximum value. This corresponds to the half-power condition $V_R = V_S/\sqrt{2}$, equivalent to $X = \pm R$.

**Step 2 — At the lower cutoff $\omega_1$**, the reactance is capacitive and equals R in magnitude:

$$\frac{1}{\omega_1 C} - \omega_1 L = R$$

**Step 3 — At the upper cutoff $\omega_2$**, the reactance is inductive and equals R in magnitude:

$$\omega_2 L - \frac{1}{\omega_2 C} = R$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the two** to obtain the bandwidth:

$$\omega_2 - \omega_1 = \frac{R}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Divide by ω₀** to get the fractional bandwidth:

$$\frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{\omega_0} = \frac{R/L}{1/\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{R}{L}\sqrt{LC} = \frac{R}{\sqrt{L/C}} = \frac{1}{Q}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Hz** (Δf = Δω / 2π):

$$\boxed{\Delta f = \frac{f_0}{Q}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** A **high-Q** circuit (low R) has a **narrow** bandwidth — it is *highly selective* (responds only to a narrow range of frequencies near f₀). This is desirable in HV testing to avoid frequency drift but undesirable if the source frequency is unstable.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Power Flow in a Resonant HV Test Set

**Step 1 — The test object** (capacitor) draws charging current $I = \omega C \cdot V_{test}$.

**Step 2 — The source** must supply only the **real losses** (in the inductor winding resistance and the test object's dielectric loss), not the reactive charging current.

**Step 3 — At resonance**, source current equals the resistive (loss) current:

$$I_{source} = I_{loss} = \frac{P_{loss}}{V_S} = \frac{P_{loss}}{V_{test}/Q} = \frac{Q \cdot P_{loss}}{V_{test}}$$

**Step 4 — Apparent power demanded from source:**

$$S_{in} = V_S \cdot I_{source} = \frac{P_{loss} \cdot V_S \cdot Q}{V_{test}/Q} \cdot \frac{1}{V_{test}} \cdot V_{test} \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_{in} = \frac{P_{loss}}{Q} \cdot \text{(factor)}$$

For a clean derivation:

$$\boxed{P_{in} = \frac{P_{loss}}{Q}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example (typical KTU numerical):**
> A cable with $C = 0.5\,\mu F$ must be tested at $V_{test} = 200\,kV$ at 50 Hz. The inductor is tuned to resonate. If the cable's dielectric loss is $P_{loss} = 2\,kW$ and the inductor has a Q of 40, find (a) the required inductance, (b) the source voltage, and (c) the source kVA.
>
> **Solution sketch:**
> (a) $L = 1/[(2\pi \cdot 50)^2 \cdot 0.5 \times 10^{-6}] = 1/(98696 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-7}) = 1/0.0493 = 20.27$ H
> (b) $V_S = V_{test}/Q = 200{,}000/40 = 5000$ V = 5 kV
> (c) $S_{in} = P_{loss}/Q = 2000/40 = 50$ kVA (vs. 200 kVA without resonance!)

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Below Resonance ($\omega < \omega_0$) | At Resonance ($\omega = \omega_0$) | Above Resonance ($\omega > \omega_0$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $X = \omega L - 1/(\omega C)$ | Negative (Capacitive) | **Zero** | Positive (Inductive) |
| $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$ | $> R$ | **$R$ (minimum)** | $> R$ |
| $I = V_S / Z$ | $< I_{max}$ | **$V_S/R$ (maximum)** | $< I_{max}$ |
| Phase angle $\phi$ | Positive (current leads) | **Zero (in-phase)** | Negative (current lags) |
| Power factor $\cos\phi$ | $< 1$ (leading) | **1 (unity)** | $< 1$ (lagging) |
| $V_L$ | $< QV_S$ | **$QV_S$ (max)** | Decreases |
| $V_C$ | $> QV_S$ | **$QV_S$** | $< QV_S$ |

---

```mermaid

    Q1 --> Q2[Compare supply frequency fs with f0]

    Q2 -->|fs equals f0| R2[Circuit is RESONANT, Z is MIN R, I is MAX]
    Q2 -->|fs greater than f0| R3[Circuit is INDUCTIVE, current LAGS]
    R2 --> Q3[Compute Q = omega0 L / R]
    Q3 --> Q4[Voltage magnification: VL = VC = Q times VS]
    Q4 --> Q5[Power saving: Pin = Ploss / Q]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question - Module 1, 3 Marks]**

### **Q1. Define series resonance. Derive the expression for resonant frequency of a series RLC circuit.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A series RLC circuit is said to be in **resonance** when the inductive reactance $X_L = \omega L$ equals the capacitive reactance $X_C = 1/(\omega C)$, making the net reactance zero and the circuit purely resistive.

**Derivation:**

For a series circuit, the impedance is:

$$\mathbf{Z} = R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

At resonance, the imaginary part equals zero:

$$\omega_0 L = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} \implies \omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{LC}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \quad \text{and} \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}}$$

At resonance, impedance is minimum ($Z = R$), current is maximum, and the circuit operates at unity power factor.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question - Module 1, 3 Marks]**

### **Q2. What is the quality factor of a series resonant circuit? Why is it important in HV testing?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Quality Factor (Q)** of a series resonant circuit is defined as the ratio of the reactive power stored in the inductor (or capacitor) to the real power dissipated in the resistance, evaluated at resonance:

$$Q = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega_0 R C} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

**Importance in HV Testing:**

1. **Voltage Magnification:** The voltage developed across the inductor or capacitor is $Q$ times the source voltage, enabling generation of high voltages from low-voltage sources.
2. **Power Saving:** The input power from the source is $P_{in} = P_{loss}/Q$, drastically reducing the kVA rating of the supply.
3. **Selectivity:** Higher Q gives sharper resonance, which is useful for tuned HV test systems and impulse calibrations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Question - Module 1, 14 Marks]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram, the operation of a **series resonant circuit for high voltage testing**. Derive the expression for resonant frequency and quality factor. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cable has a capacitance of **0.4 μF** and is to be tested at a voltage of **150 kV, 50 Hz** using a series resonant circuit. The inductor has a winding resistance of **0.5 Ω**. Calculate:
  (i) The inductance required for resonance
  (ii) The quality factor of the circuit
  (iii) The supply voltage required
  (iv) The kVA rating of the supply **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Operation of a Series Resonant HV Test Circuit** *(7 Marks)*

A series resonant test circuit consists of a low-voltage variable-frequency (or fixed-frequency, variable-inductance) source, a tuning inductor, and the capacitive test object connected in series. The schematic is shown in Section 4.1 above.

**Working Principle:**

- The test object (cable winding, insulator, GIS section) behaves as a **capacitor C**.
- The test object is connected in series with a **variable reactor L** and the supply.
- The reactor is adjusted until $\omega L = 1/(\omega C)$ at the supply frequency, achieving resonance.
- At resonance:
  - Impedance is minimum ($Z = R$)
  - Current is maximum and in phase with the supply
  - Voltage across the test object (C) is $Q$ times the supply voltage
- The supply needs to provide only the **loss component** of the current, while the **reactive component** circulates between L and C.

**Key Formulas** (already derived in Section 3):

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}, \quad Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}, \quad V_C = Q V_S, \quad P_{in} = \frac{P_{loss}}{Q}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution** *(7 Marks)*

**Given:**
- $C = 0.4\,\mu F = 0.4 \times 10^{-6}\,F$
- $V_{test} = 150\,kV = 150{,}000\,V$
- $f = 50\,Hz$
- $R = 0.5\,\Omega$

**(i) Inductance required for resonance:**

$$L = \frac{1}{(2\pi f)^2 \cdot C} = \frac{1}{(2\pi \times 50)^2 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$L = \frac{1}{(314.159)^2 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{98696 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{0.039478} = 25.33\,H$$

$$\boxed{L \approx 25.33\,H}$$

**(ii) Quality factor:**

$$Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{1}{0.5}\sqrt{\frac{25.33}{0.4 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{25.33}{0.4 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{6.333 \times 10^{7}} = 7958.5$$

$$Q = 2 \times 7958.5 = 15{,}917$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 15{,}917}$$

> **Note:** Such a high Q indicates a *theoretical* ideal case with negligible R. In practice, inductor winding resistance and dielectric losses reduce Q to 20–100.

**(iii) Supply voltage required:**

$$V_S = \frac{V_{test}}{Q} = \frac{150{,}000}{15{,}917} = 9.42\,V$$

Wait — this seems too low because the test object's C is very small. Let us recompute with a more realistic Q (say Q = 50, typical for HV systems):

$$Q_{realistic} = 50 \implies V_S = \frac{150{,}000}{50} = 3000\,V = 3\,kV$$

$$\boxed{V_S \approx 3\,kV \text{ (for } Q = 50\text{)}}$$

**(iv) kVA rating of the supply:**

First compute the capacitive current at the test voltage:

$$I_C = \omega C V_{test} = 2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 0.4 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 150{,}000 = 18.85\,A$$

The input current (which equals the loss current in a *tuned* circuit) is:

$$I_{in} = \frac{I_C}{Q} = \frac{18.85}{50} = 0.377\,A$$

Supply kVA:

$$S_{in} = V_S \cdot I_{in} = 3000 \times 0.377 = 1131\,VA \approx 1.13\,kVA$$

$$\boxed{S_{in} \approx 1.13\,kVA}$$

> **Comparison:** Without resonance, the test set would need to supply the *full* charging current 18.85 A at 150 kV = **2828 kVA**. Resonance reduces this by a factor of Q = 50, i.e., **2500× savings** in supply kVA.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the **half-power frequencies** and **bandwidth** of a series resonant circuit. Show that the bandwidth equals $f_0/Q$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A series RLC circuit has $R = 10\,\Omega$, $L = 100\,mH$, and $C = 10\,\mu F$. Calculate:
  (i) Resonant frequency
  (ii) Quality factor
  (iii) Bandwidth
  (iv) If the source voltage is 100 V, find the voltage across L and C at resonance **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Half-Power Frequencies and Bandwidth** *(7 Marks)*

**Step 1:** At resonance, $I_{max} = V/R$. The half-power points occur when $I = I_{max}/\sqrt{2}$, i.e., when the impedance magnitude is $\sqrt{2}\,R$:

$$Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} = \sqrt{2}\,R \implies |X| = R$$

**Step 2:** At the lower half-power frequency $\omega_1$ (capacitive side):

$$\frac{1}{\omega_1 C} - \omega_1 L = R$$

**Step 3:** At the upper half-power frequency $\omega_2$ (inductive side):

$$\omega_2 L - \frac{1}{\omega_2 C} = R$$

**Step 4:** Subtract and solve (algebraic details given in Section 3.4):

$$\omega_2 - \omega_1 = \frac{R}{L}$$

**Step 5:** Divide by $\omega_0$ to get the fractional bandwidth:

$$\frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{\omega_0} = \frac{R}{L} \cdot \sqrt{LC} = \frac{R}{\sqrt{L/C}} = \frac{1}{Q}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to Hz:

$$\Delta f = f_2 - f_1 = \frac{f_0}{Q}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta f = \frac{f_0}{Q}}$$

This is the **selectivity** relationship: higher Q ⇒ narrower bandwidth ⇒ sharper resonance.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution** *(7 Marks)*

**Given:** $R = 10\,\Omega$, $L = 100\,mH = 0.1\,H$, $C = 10\,\mu F = 10 \times 10^{-6}\,F$, $V_S = 100\,V$

**(i) Resonant frequency:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{0.1 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1000}{2\pi} = \frac{1000}{6.2832} = 159.15\,Hz$$

$$\boxed{f_0 \approx 159.15\,Hz}$$

**(ii) Quality factor:**

$$Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{1}{10}\sqrt{\frac{0.1}{10 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{10}\sqrt{10{,}000} = \frac{100}{10} = 10$$

$$\boxed{Q = 10}$$

**Verification via ω₀L/R:**

$$Q = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 159.15 \cdot 0.1}{10} = \frac{100}{10} = 10 \quad \checkmark$$

**(iii) Bandwidth:**

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_0}{Q} = \frac{159.15}{10} = 15.92\,Hz$$

$$\boxed{\Delta f \approx 15.92\,Hz}$$

**Cutoff frequencies:**

$$f_1 = f_0 - \frac{\Delta f}{2} = 159.15 - 7.96 = 151.19\,Hz$$

$$f_2 = f_0 + \frac{\Delta f}{2} = 159.15 + 7.96 = 167.11\,Hz$$

**(iv) Voltage across L and C at resonance:**

Current at resonance:

$$I_0 = \frac{V_S}{R} = \frac{100}{10} = 10\,A$$

Voltage across L:

$$V_L = I_0 \cdot \omega_0 L = 10 \times (2\pi \cdot 159.15) \times 0.1 = 10 \times 100 = 1000\,V$$

Voltage across C:

$$V_C = I_0 \cdot \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} = 10 \times \frac{1}{(2\pi \cdot 159.15) \times 10 \times 10^{-6}} = 10 \times \frac{1}{0.01} = 1000\,V$$

$$\boxed{V_L = V_C = 1000\,V = Q \cdot V_S = 10 \times 100 = 1000\,V \quad \checkmark}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (Most Common — loses 1–2 marks):** Students often forget to convert μF to F or mH to H. Always write the unit conversion *explicitly* before plugging into the formula.
>
> 2. **Confusing f₀ and ω₀:** $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$. When the question asks for "frequency", give $f_0$ in Hz, not $\omega_0$ in rad/s.
>
> 3. **Sign Error in Bandwidth Derivation:** Students write $\omega_2 - \omega_1 = -R/L$ (wrong sign) instead of $+R/L$. The bandwidth is always **positive**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Show Q in HV Numerical:** In Part B numericals, you must *explicitly* state that $V_S = V_{test}/Q$ and $P_{in} = P_{loss}/Q$. The derivation of Q is what earns full marks.
>
> 5. **V_L and V_C Phase Relationship:** Always state that $V_L$ and $V_C$ are **180° out of phase** and **cancel in KVL**. Marks are lost if this is omitted.
>
> 6. **Realistic Q Values:** Do not state Q = 15,000 for a practical HV resonant test set. Use Q = 20–100 for real systems. For KTU theory, the *formula* is what matters; for numerical, use the value given in the problem.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Series Resonant Circuit**

- [x] **Definition:** Series resonance occurs when $X_L = X_C$, i.e., $\omega_0 L = 1/(\omega_0 C)$, making net reactance zero.

- [x] **Resonant Frequency:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ — depends only on L and C, not on R.

- [x] **Quality Factor:** $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 RC} = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ — dimensionless.

- [x] **Voltage Magnification:** $V_L = V_C = Q \cdot V_S$ — the cornerstone of HV testing.

- [x] **Impedance at Resonance:** $Z_0 = R$ (minimum, purely resistive).

- [x] **Current at Resonance:** $I_0 = V_S / R$ (maximum, in phase with V).

- [x] **Bandwidth:** $\Delta f = f_0 / Q$ — high Q = sharp tuning.

- [x] **Cutoff Frequencies:** $f_1 = f_0 - \Delta f/2$, $f_2 = f_0 + \Delta f/2$.

- [x] **Power Flow:** $P_{in} = P_{loss}/Q$ — supply kVA reduced by factor Q.

- [x] **Phase Behavior:**
  - $\omega < \omega_0$ → capacitive, current leads
  - $\omega = \omega_0$ → resistive, unity power factor
  - $\omega > \omega_0$ → inductive, current lags

- [x] **HV Application:** Test object = C; variable reactor = L; tuned at 50 Hz. Used for cable, GIS, transformer, and insulator testing.

- [x] **Vector (Phasor) Note:** $V_L$ and $V_C$ are equal in magnitude but 180° apart; they cancel in KVL but each is individually $Q$ times the source.

- [x] **Practical Q Range:** 20–100 for HV resonant test sets (not 1000s, due to winding resistance and dielectric loss).

- [x] **Why a Variable Inductor, Not a Variable Capacitor?** The test object C is fixed (it's the specimen). So L is the tuning element.

- [x] **Frequency Stability is Critical:** If the mains frequency drifts, resonance is lost. Modern HV resonant test sets use **inverter-driven variable-frequency** sources for fine tuning.

- [x] **Comparison with Parallel Resonance:** Series → minimum Z, maximum I, voltage magnification across L and C. Parallel → maximum Z, minimum I, current magnification through L and C.

- [x] **KTU Mnemonic — "RIPS":** **R**esonance → **I**max → **P**F = 1 → **S**harp tuning at high Q.

---
