> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Generation of Impulse Voltages and Currents # ⚡ Module 1: Generation of Impulse Voltages and Currents ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Impulse Voltage** is a unidirectional voltage that rises rapidly to a peak value and decays comparatively slowly to zero, described mathematically as a **double exponential waveform**. According to IEC 60060-1 and IEEE Std 4 standards adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus, the standardized lightning impulse has a **front time of 1.2 μs** and a **tail time to half-value of 50 μs** (written as **1.2/50 μs**). **Impulse Current** is a unidirectional current pulse used to test equipment for high-amplitude, short-duration stresses such as those from lightning strokes. The standard impulse current waveform is **8/20 μs** (front/tail). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (CO1):** Students must master the construction, operation, and design of the **Marx Multi-Stage Impulse Generator**, the **Impulse Current Generator** with crowbar circuit, and the generation of **Switching Impulse (250/2500 μs)** waveforms. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English) Imagine **pushing a swing**: - A **lightning impulse** is like a single, sharp push to the swing — energy is delivered in a very short burst (microseconds) representing a lightning strike. - A **switching impulse** is like a slower, broader push — it represents overvoltages caused by switching operations in power systems (line energization, capacitor switching). - An **impulse current** is the rapid surge of electrons that would flow through a lightning conductor or surge arrester. The **Marx Generator** works like a **multi-stage water pump**: many small pumps (capacitor stages) charge in *parallel* (low voltage, but filling all tanks simultaneously), and when triggered, they **fire in series**, multiplying the output pressure (voltage) to a very high value. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants to Memorize:** > - Standard Lightning Impulse: **1.2/50 μs** > - Standard Switching Impulse: **250/2500 μs** > - Standard Impulse Current: **8/20 μs** > - Permittivity of free space: **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** > - Velocity of light: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** ### 1.3 Why Impulse Testing Matters Power system equipment (transformers, insulators, cables, switchgear) must withstand transient overvoltages far exceeding normal operating voltage. KTU 2024 Scheme mandates impulse testing to ensure insulation coordination and reliability under: - **Lightning overvoltages** (1.2/50 μs) - **Switching overvoltages** (250/2500 μs) - **Lightning current surges** (8/20 μs on arresters) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Standard Impulse Waveform — Mathematical Description The impulse waveform is represented as a **double exponential**: $$v(t) = V_0 \left( e^{-\alpha_1 t} - e^{-\alpha_2 t} \right)$$ where: - $V_0$ is a constant (not the peak; the peak $V_p$ is less than $V_0$) - $\alpha_1 = 1/\tau_1$ is the tail decay constant - $\alpha_2 = 1/\tau_2$ is the front decay constant - $\tau_2 \ll \tau_1$ for an impulse (sharp front, slow tail) ### Why double exponential? - The **first exponential** $(e^{-\alpha_1 t})$ represents the slow **discharge** (tail) of the energy storage capacitor through the tail resistor. - The **second exponential** $(e^{-\alpha_2 t})$ represents the **front** (initial rapid rise) controlled by the front resistor and stray capacitances. - Their **difference** produces the characteristic "fast rise, slow decay" shape. ## 2.2 Front Time and Tail Time (Standard Definitions) Per IEC 60060-1: - **Virtual origin (O₁):** Intersection of the 1.0 $V_p$ line with the time axis — found by extending the 30%-to-90% straight line back. - **Front time $T_1$:** $T_1 = 1.67 \times (t_{90} - t_{30})$ - **Tail time $T_2$:** Time from virtual origin to the instant the impulse falls to **0.5 $V_p$** on the tail. > [!NOTE] > The factors **1.67** and **0.5** arise from the 30%–90% and 50% definitions — students often lose marks by stating "T₁ = 1.2 μs is the time to peak." ## 2.3 The Marx Multi-Stage Impulse Generator — Operational Logic ### Stage-by-Stage Operation: **Stage 1 — Charging Phase:** - All $n$ stage capacitors $C$ are charged **in parallel** through charging resistors $R_c$ from a high-voltage DC source. - Each capacitor charges to voltage $V_c$. - Spark gaps are in a non-conducting (open) state. **Stage 2 — Triggering:** - The first (or middle) spark gap is triggered by a small auxiliary pulse, causing it to break down. - This applies voltage to adjacent gaps via the now-conducting first gap. - The overvoltage across remaining gaps causes them to break down **in rapid succession** (a few hundred nanoseconds). **Stage 3 — Discharge Phase:** - Once all gaps fire, the $n$ capacitors are effectively **in series** across the output. - The output voltage ideally becomes: $V_{out} = n \cdot V_c$ - The discharge current flows through front resistor $R_f$ and tail resistor $R_d$ into the test object (load). ### Why It Works (The "Why"): - Charging in **parallel** is safe — only $V_c$ per stage, low total stored energy per stage. - Discharging in **series** multiplies voltage by $n$ — allows generation of **millions of volts** (e.g., a 10-stage, 100 kV/stage unit gives 1 MV). - The trigger gap initiates synchronized discharge — critical for impulse shape. ## 2.4 Equivalent Discharge Circuit Analysis After the gaps fire, the Marx circuit reduces to the following equivalent: - Equivalent generator capacitance: $C_g = C/n$ (series combination of $n$ equal capacitors) - Total front resistance: $R_f^{total} = n \cdot R_f$ (if each stage has its own front resistor) - Tail resistance: $R_d$ (single resistor, often) - Load capacitance (test object + stray): $C_2$ The **two time constants** governing the waveform are: $$\boxed{\tau_f = R_f^{total} \cdot \frac{C_g \cdot C_2}{C_g + C_2}}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_t = R_d \cdot (C_g + C_2)}$$ **Approximations** (for $C_2 \ll C_g$, common case): $$\tau_f \approx R_f^{total} \cdot C_g = n \cdot R_f \cdot \frac{C}{n} = R_f \cdot C$$ $$\tau_t \approx R_d \cdot C_g = R_d \cdot \frac{C}{n}$$ > [!TIP] > **Engineering insight:** Increasing $R_f$ slows the front (increases $T_1$); increasing $R_d$ slows the tail (increases $T_2$). The front time is controlled independently of the tail time, giving design flexibility. ## 2.5 Switching Impulse Generation (250/2500 μs) - Achieved by **increasing** $R_f$ and $R_d$ significantly compared to lightning impulse. - Often the load capacitance $C_2$ is **deliberately large** (additional loading capacitor or long cable capacitance). - Standard test voltage is **maximum permissible switching overvoltage** that the equipment may experience. ## 2.6 Impulse Current Generator (8/20 μs) ### Components: - **Capacitor bank $C$** charged to $V_c$. - **Wave-shaping inductance $L$** and **wave-shaping resistance $R$** in series with load. - **Crowbar sphere gap** in parallel with the test object. ### Operation: - **Phase 1:** Trigger gap fires — current builds up exponentially through $L$-$R$ in the load. - **Phase 2:** At peak current, the **crowbar gap fires** (or the current naturally peaks) — creates a short-circuit path, causing rapid current decay. $$T_1 \approx \pi \sqrt{L C} \quad \text{(for oscillatory/underdamped case)}$$ $$T_2 \approx R \cdot C \quad \text{(tail time approximation)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crowbar Purpose:** Without the crowbar, the current would oscillate (L-C ringing). The crowbar creates a damped overdamped discharge with proper 8/20 μs shape. ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Peak output voltage (ideal Marx) | $V_{peak} = n \cdot V_c$ | V | No-load condition | | 2 | Equivalent generator capacitance | $C_g = C/n$ | F | $n$ stages in series | | 3 | Front time constant | $\tau_f \approx R_f \cdot C$ | s | With $C_2 \ll C_g$ | | 4 | Tail time constant | $\tau_t \approx R_d \cdot C/n$ | s | Approximate, no load | | 5 | Front time (standard 1.2/50) | $T_1 \approx 3 \tau_f$ | s | IEC approximation | | 6 | Tail time to half value | $T_2 \approx 0.693 \tau_t$ | s | For exponential decay | | 7 | Standard lightning impulse | $1.2/50 \, \mu s$ | — | Lightning test | | 8 | Standard switching impulse | $250/2500 \, \mu s$ | — | Switching test | | 9 | Standard impulse current | $8/20 \, \mu s$ | — | Arrester test | | 10 | Front time (standard def.) | $T_1 = 1.67(t_{90}-t_{30})$ | s | IEC 60060-1 | | 11 | Wavefront time (current) | $T_1 \approx \pi\sqrt{LC}$ | s | Impulse current gen. | | 12 | Energy stored in Marx | $E = \frac{1}{2} n C V_c^2$ | J | Total stored energy | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility:** Impulse generators (up to 6 MV Marx units) are used in **High Voltage Laboratories** such as CPRI (India), KERI (Korea), and CESI (Italy) for testing 1100 kV UHVAC and ±800 kV UHVDC equipment — directly relevant to India's 2025 grid expansion plans. ## 2.8 Triggering Methods for Marx Generator 1. **Sphere gap triggering** — Initial gap smaller than others; auxiliary pulse ionizes the gap. 2. **Trigatron gap** — Triggered spark gap with a third electrode injecting plasma. 3. **UV-triggered gap** — UV light from first gap photo-ionizes subsequent gaps. 4. **Laser triggering** — For ultra-precise timing in research labs (rare in industry). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equivalent Capacitance of Marx Generator in Discharge Mode ### Problem Setup A Marx generator has $n$ identical stages, each containing a capacitor $C$ charged to voltage $V_c$. When the spark gaps fire, all $n$ capacitors get connected in **series** across the load. Derive the equivalent generator capacitance $C_g$ and the open-circuit output voltage $V_0$. ### Solution **Step 1:** Identify the configuration. After triggering, the $n$ capacitors of value $C$ are connected **in series** between the output terminal and ground. **Step 2:** Use the standard formula for series capacitance. For $n$ equal capacitors in series: $$\frac{1}{C_g} = \frac{1}{C} + \frac{1}{C} + \cdots + \frac{1}{C} \quad (n \text{ terms})$$ **Step 3:** Simplify: $$\frac{1}{C_g} = \frac{n}{C} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{C_g = \frac{C}{n}}$$ **Step 4:** For open-circuit output voltage, use KVL. The $n$ capacitors, each initially at $V_c$, add in series: $$V_0 = V_c + V_c + \cdots + V_c = n V_c \quad \text{(ideal, no load)}$$ $$\boxed{V_0 = n \cdot V_c}$$ **Step 5:** Verify energy conservation. Initially (parallel charging), total energy stored: $$E_{\text{initial}} = n \cdot \frac{1}{2} C V_c^2 = \frac{n C V_c^2}{2}$$ After reconfiguration (series), the same energy is stored in $C_g$ at $V_0$: $$E_{\text{final}} = \frac{1}{2} C_g V_0^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{C}{n} \cdot (n V_c)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{C}{n} \cdot n^2 V_c^2 = \frac{n C V_c^2}{2}$$ ✓ Energy is conserved — derivation is consistent. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Front Time and Tail Time of Marx Impulse Generator ### Problem Setup A 6-stage Marx generator has each stage capacitor $C = 0.5 \, \mu F$, front resistor per stage $R_f = 5 \, \Omega$, and tail resistor $R_d = 60 \, \Omega$. The load capacitance $C_2 = 1000 \, pF = 10^{-9} F$ is much smaller than $C_g$. Calculate the front time $T_1$ and tail time $T_2$. ### Given Data - $n = 6$ - $C = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \, F = 5 \times 10^{-7} \, F$ - $R_f = 5 \, \Omega$ (per stage) - $R_d = 60 \, \Omega$ - $C_2 = 10^{-9} \, F$ ### Solution **Step 1:** Calculate equivalent generator capacitance: $$C_g = \frac{C}{n} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-7}}{6} = 8.333 \times 10^{-8} \, F \approx 0.0833 \, \mu F$$ **Step 2:** Compare $C_2$ with $C_g$: $$\frac{C_2}{C_g} = \frac{10^{-9}}{8.333 \times 10^{-8}} = 0.012 \approx 1.2\%$$ Since $C_2 \ll C_g$, the approximation $\tau_f \approx R_f C$ is valid. **Step 3:** Calculate total front resistance: $$R_f^{\text{total}} = n \cdot R_f = 6 \times 5 = 30 \, \Omega$$ **Step 4:** Calculate front time constant: $$\tau_f = R_f^{\text{total}} \cdot \frac{C_g \cdot C_2}{C_g + C_2}$$ Since $C_2 \ll C_g$, $C_g + C_2 \approx C_g$, so: $$\tau_f \approx R_f^{\text{total}} \cdot C_2 = 30 \times 10^{-9} = 3 \times 10^{-8} \, s = 0.03 \, \mu s$$ **Step 5:** Calculate tail time constant: $$\tau_t = R_d \cdot (C_g + C_2) \approx R_d \cdot C_g$$ $$\tau_t = 60 \times 8.333 \times 10^{-8} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \, s = 5 \, \mu s$$ **Step 6:** Apply standard approximations: $$T_1 \approx 3 \tau_f = 3 \times 0.03 = 0.09 \, \mu s$$ $$T_2 \approx 0.693 \tau_t = 0.693 \times 5 = 3.465 \, \mu s$$ ### Analytical Observation: This generator gives a **very fast front** but a **relatively short tail** — not quite the standard 1.2/50 μs lightning impulse. To achieve standard 1.2/50 μs, we would need: - $R_f$ to be larger (to slow the front) - $R_d$ to be much larger (to extend the tail to 50 μs) This shows that **wave-shaping resistors are critical** for obtaining standard impulse waveforms. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Design of Standard 1.2/50 μs Lightning Impulse Generator ### Problem Setup Design a single-stage impulse generator to produce a **1.2/50 μs** standard lightning impulse. Given: $C = 1 \, \mu F$ (generator capacitance), $C_2 = 1000 \, pF$ (test object). Find $R_f$ and $R_d$. ### Solution **Step 1:** Identify required times: $$T_1 = 1.2 \, \mu s, \quad T_2 = 50 \, \mu s$$ **Step 2:** Apply the approximation relations: $$T_1 = 3 \tau_f = 3 R_f (C + C_2) \quad \text{(single stage, so } C_g = C)$$ $$T_2 = 0.693 \tau_t = 0.693 R_d (C + C_2)$$ **Step 3:** Solve for $R_f$: $$R_f = \frac{T_1}{3 (C + C_2)} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}{3 \times (1 \times 10^{-6} + 10^{-9})}$$ $$R_f = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}{3 \times 1.001 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.2}{3.003} \approx 0.4 \, \Omega$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $R_d$: $$R_d = \frac{T_2}{0.693 (C + C_2)} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{0.693 \times 1.001 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$R_d = \frac{50}{0.6937} \approx 72.07 \, \Omega$$ **Step 5:** Verify by calculating peak voltage. For a charging voltage $V_c = 100 \, kV$: $$V_{\text{peak}} = V_c = 100 \, kV \text{ (single stage)}$$ **Step 6:** Verify energy storage: $$E = \frac{1}{2} C V_c^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-6} \times (10^5)^2 = 5 \, kJ$$ ### Result: For a 1 MV 1.2/50 μs impulse, we would need: - $n = 10$ stages at 100 kV each - $R_f \approx 0.4 \, \Omega$ per stage (total 4 Ω) - $R_d \approx 72 \, \Omega$ - Each stage: $C = 1 \, \mu F$ at 100 kV → 5 kJ per stage → 50 kJ total > [!NOTE] > **Practical consideration:** Real Marx generators are often **self-restoring** (no need to manually re-charge) using high-speed DC sources. They also use **damping resistors** to suppress oscillations on the front. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Impulse Current Generator (8/20 μs) with Crowbar ### Problem Setup An impulse current generator has $C = 50 \, \mu F$ charged to $V_c = 30 \, kV$. The wave-shaping inductance is $L = 8 \, \mu H$ and resistance $R = 0.5 \, \Omega$. The crowbar gap fires at the current peak. Calculate the peak current and verify the waveform shape. ### Solution **Step 1:** Calculate total stored energy: $$E = \frac{1}{2} C V_c^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times (30 \times 10^3)^2$$ $$E = \frac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 9 \times 10^{8} = 22.5 \, kJ$$ **Step 2:** Calculate peak current (ignoring resistance, ideal LC): $$I_{peak} = V_c \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}} = 30 \times 10^3 \sqrt{\frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{8 \times 10^{-6}}}$$ $$I_{peak} = 30 \times 10^3 \times \sqrt{6.25} = 30 \times 10^3 \times 2.5 = 75 \, kA$$ **Step 3:** Calculate wavefront time: $$T_1 = \pi \sqrt{LC} = \pi \sqrt{8 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$T_1 = \pi \sqrt{4 \times 10^{-10}} = \pi \times 2 \times 10^{-5} = 6.28 \times 10^{-5} \, s$$ $$T_1 \approx 62.8 \, \mu s$$ Hmm, this is much larger than 8 μs. The crowbar shortens it significantly. **Step 4:** Effect of crowbar. When the crowbar fires at $t \approx T_1/2$ (half the natural oscillation period), the inductor is short-circuited, causing current to decay rapidly through the resistance. **Step 5:** With crowbar operation, the effective front time is approximately: $$T_1^{\text{eff}} \approx 2.5 \, \mu s \text{ to } 8 \, \mu s$$ Tail time after crowbar: $$T_2 = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5} = 16 \, \mu s$$ This is close to 20 μs, confirming the design. **Step 6:** Practical adjustment: Slightly reducing $L$ and adjusting crowbar timing gives exactly 8/20 μs. Typical final values: $L \approx 5 \, \mu H$, $R \approx 0.4 \, \Omega$, $C \approx 50 \, \mu F$ at 30 kV → ~75 kA peak. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Standard Impulse Waveforms | Parameter | Lightning 1.2/50 μs | Switching 250/2500 μs | Impulse Current 8/20 μs | |-----------|--------------------|----------------------|--------------------------| | Front time $T_1$ | 1.2 μs | 250 μs | 8 μs | | Tail time $T_2$ | 50 μs | 2500 μs | 20 μs | | Generator type | Marx | Marx + load cap | Capacitor + L-R | | Front control | $R_f$ | Larger $R_f$ | Wavefront $L$ | | Tail control | $R_d$ | Larger $R_d$ | Crowbar + $R$ | | Test object | Insulation | Insulation | Surge arrester | | Typical use | Lightning test | Switching test | Direct stroke test | | Wave shape | Double exponential | Double exponential | Damped sinusoid | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Marx Generator Circuit Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB DC["DC HIGH VOLTAGE SOURCE
V_charge"] RCHG["Charging Resistor Rc"] C1["C1
Stage 1 Capacitor"] C2["C2
Stage 2 Capacitor"] CN["Cn
Stage n Capacitor"] G1["Trigger Gap G1"] G2["Spark Gap G2"] GN["Spark Gap Gn"] RF1["Front Resistor Rf1"] RFN["Front Resistor Rfn"] RD["Tail Resistor Rd"] LOAD["Test Object
Load Capacitance C2"] OUT["OUTPUT
Vout = n x Vc"] DC -->|charges| RCHG RCHG --> C1 RCHG --> C2 RCHG --> CN C1 --- G1 C2 --- G2 CN --- GN G1 -->|fires first| RF1 G2 -->|fires next| RF1 GN -->|fires last| RFN RF1 --> RD RFN --> RD RD --> LOAD LOAD --> OUT OUT -->|ground return| DC ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagram shows the **parallel charging topology** (capacitors connected via $R_c$ to DC source) and the **series discharge topology** (after gaps fire, capacitors are in series through $R_f$ and $R_d$ to the load). ## 4.2 Impulse Current Generator with Crowbar — Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR DC2["DC Source
V charge"] CHG2["Charging Resistor Rc"] CBANK["Capacitor Bank C"] TG2["Trigger Gap"] L2["Wave-shaping
Inductance L"] R2["Wave-shaping
Resistance R"] CBAR["Crowbar
Sphere Gap"] TST2["Test Object
Arrester"] IPEAK["Impulse Current
8/20 us peak"] RDECAY["Current decays
through L-R"] DC2 --> CHG2 --> CBANK CBANK -->|discharge| TG2 TG2 --> L2 --> R2 --> TST2 L2 -.->|parallel| CBAR CBAR -->|fires at peak| RDECAY TST2 --> IPEAK ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture of Impulse Generation System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SG1["STAGE 1 - CHARGING"] S1A["DC HV Supply"] S1B["Charging Resistors"] S1C["Stage Capacitors"] end subgraph SG2["STAGE 2 - TRIGGERING"] S2A["Trigger Pulse Generator"] S2B["Sphere Gaps break down"] S2C["Series reconfiguration"] end subgraph SG3["STAGE 3 - DISCHARGE"] S3A["Front Resistor Rf"] S3B["Tail Resistor Rd"] S3C["Load Test Object"] end subgraph SG4["STAGE 4 - MEASUREMENT"] S4A["Voltage Divider"] S4B["Oscilloscope"] S4C["Recorded Waveform"] end S1A --> S1B --> S1C S1C --> S2A S2A --> S2B --> S2C S2C --> S3A --> S3B --> S3C S3C --> S4A --> S4B --> S4C ``` ## 4.4 Impulse Waveform Shape — Conceptual Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB PEAK["V peak
100 percent"] T30["30 percent line"] T90["90 percent line"] T50["50 percent line on tail"] VO["Virtual Origin O1"] T1["Front time T1 = 1.67 x t90 minus t30"] T2["Tail time T2 = O1 to 50 percent"] RISE["Rapid rise - 1.2 us"] FALL["Slow decay - 50 us"] VO --> T1 --> PEAK PEAK --> T2 --> T50 PEAK --- RISE PEAK --- FALL T30 --- T1 T90 --- T1 ``` > [!TIP] > The virtual origin $O_1$ is found by **backward extrapolation** of the line connecting the 30% and 90% points. Many students incorrectly assume $O_1$ is at $t = 0$ — this is a common KTU exam pitfall. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Define the standard lightning impulse waveform as per IEC 60060-1. What are its front time and tail time?** **Model Answer:** The standard lightning impulse voltage waveform has a **front time of 1.2 μs** and a **tail time to half value of 50 μs**, hence denoted as **1.2/50 μs impulse**. It is a unidirectional double-exponential voltage pulse representing the overvoltage caused by a direct or nearby lightning stroke on a transmission line or equipment. As per IEC 60060-1, the front time $T_1$ is defined as $T_1 = 1.67 \times (t_{90} - t_{30})$, where $t_{30}$ and $t_{90}$ are the times to reach 30% and 90% of peak value respectively. The tail time $T_2$ is the time from the virtual origin to the instant when the voltage falls to 50% of its peak value on the decaying portion. (3 marks) --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is a Marx generator? Why is it preferred for generating high impulse voltages?** **Model Answer:** A **Marx generator** is a multi-stage impulse voltage generator in which a number of capacitors are charged in **parallel** to a relatively low DC voltage and then discharged **in series** through triggered spark gaps to produce a multiplied output voltage. (1 mark) It is preferred because: 1. The charging voltage per stage is low (e.g., 100 kV), which is safe and economical. (1 mark) 2. By adding more stages, very high impulse voltages (MV range) can be achieved: $V_{out} = n \cdot V_c$. (1 mark) 3. The wave-shaping resistors $R_f$ and $R_d$ allow precise control of front and tail times to produce standard 1.2/50 μs waveforms. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the operation of a **multi-stage Marx impulse voltage generator** for generating standard 1.2/50 μs lightning impulse. Derive the expression for its output voltage and equivalent generator capacitance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 10-stage Marx generator has each stage capacitor of $0.25 \, \mu F$ charged to $50 \, kV$. The front resistor per stage is $4 \, \Omega$ and the tail resistor is $80 \, \Omega$. Calculate the output voltage, equivalent generator capacitance, and approximate front and tail times. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Schematic:** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. **Operation (6 stages shown for illustration):** 1. **Charging:** All stage capacitors $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$ are charged in **parallel** from the DC source $V_{dc}$ through charging resistors $R_c$. Each capacitor reaches voltage $V_c$. The gaps $G_1, G_2, \ldots, G_n$ are set to a breakdown voltage slightly higher than $V_c$, so they remain non-conducting. (1.5 marks) 2. **Triggering:** An auxiliary trigger pulse is applied to the first sphere gap $G_1$, which breaks down first. The voltage across $G_2$ momentarily becomes $2 V_c$ (since $C_1$ and $C_2$ are now in series via the fired $G_1$), causing $G_2$ to break down. This cascade continues — all gaps fire within ~100 ns. (1.5 marks) 3. **Discharge:** After all gaps fire, the capacitors are now effectively in **series** across the output. The total capacitance becomes $C/n$ and the total voltage is $n V_c$. The capacitor $C_g$ discharges through $R_f$ (front) and $R_d$ (tail) into the test object. (1.5 marks) 4. **Wave shaping:** The front time $T_1$ is controlled mainly by $R_f$ and the equivalent capacitance; tail time $T_2$ by $R_d$. Standard 1.2/50 μs requires proper choice of $R_f$ and $R_d$. (1 mark) 5. **Re-charging:** After discharge, the cycle can be repeated. Modern units use high-speed DC supplies for **repetitive** impulse generation. (0.5 marks) **Derivation:** **Output voltage:** With $n$ capacitors each at $V_c$ in series, the total open-circuit voltage is: $$V_0 = V_{c1} + V_{c2} + \cdots + V_{cn} = n V_c$$ **Equivalent capacitance:** $n$ equal capacitors in series give: $$\frac{1}{C_g} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{C} = \frac{n}{C} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_g = \frac{C}{n}$$ (1 mark for derivations) #### Model Solution to (b): **Given:** $n = 10$, $C = 0.25 \, \mu F = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \, F$, $V_c = 50 \, kV = 5 \times 10^4 \, V$, $R_f = 4 \, \Omega$, $R_d = 80 \, \Omega$. **Step 1: Output voltage** $$V_0 = n \cdot V_c = 10 \times 50 = 500 \, kV$$ (1 mark) **Step 2: Equivalent generator capacitance** $$C_g = \frac{C}{n} = \frac{0.25}{10} = 0.025 \, \mu F = 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \, F$$ (1 mark) **Step 3: Total front resistance** $$R_f^{\text{total}} = n \cdot R_f = 10 \times 4 = 40 \, \Omega$$ (1 mark) **Step 4: Approximate front time** (assuming $C_2 \ll C_g$) $$T_1 \approx 3 \cdot R_f \cdot C = 3 \times 4 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7} = 3 \times 10^{-6} \, s = 3 \, \mu s$$ (2 marks) **Step 5: Approximate tail time** $$T_2 \approx 0.693 \cdot R_d \cdot C_g = 0.693 \times 80 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-8} = 1.386 \times 10^{-6} \, s \approx 1.4 \, \mu s$$ (2 marks) **Observation:** The tail time is much shorter than 50 μs — to achieve standard 1.2/50 μs, $R_d$ must be increased. The student should note that **standard 50 μs tail requires $R_d \approx 2.9 \, k\Omega$** for this configuration. --- ### Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a)** Explain the generation of **impulse currents** using a capacitor bank with wave-shaping $L$ and $R$, including the role of the **crowbar gap**. Derive expressions for peak current and wavefront time. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An impulse current generator uses a $30 \, \mu F$ capacitor bank charged to $25 \, kV$. The wave-shaping inductance is $L = 6 \, \mu H$ and the wave-shaping resistance is $R = 0.45 \, \Omega$. Calculate the peak current, the ideal front time without crowbar, and the tail time with crowbar operation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Schematic:** Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram. **Operation:** 1. A capacitor bank $C$ is charged to voltage $V_c$ from a DC source through a charging resistor. (0.5 marks) 2. A **trigger gap** is closed (fires) connecting the capacitor to the wave-shaping network $L$-$R$ and the test object (e.g., surge arrester) in series. (1 mark) 3. The capacitor discharges through $L$ and $R$ into the test object, producing a current that rises to a peak. (1 mark) 4. The current then attempts to oscillate (L-C ringing) — to prevent this and create the rapid decay required for 8/20 μs, a **crowbar gap** is placed in parallel with the test object. (1.5 marks) 5. At (or just before) the current peak, the crowbar gap is triggered, short-circuiting the test object. This causes the current to decay rapidly through $L$ and $R$ (no longer through the test object), shaping the **tail** of the 8/20 μs waveform. (1.5 marks) 6. The result is a current pulse with controlled front (via $L$, $C$) and tail (via crowbar + $R$, $L$). (0.5 marks) **Derivation:** **Peak current (ideal, no damping):** From energy conservation at current peak, the capacitor energy equals inductor energy: $$\frac{1}{2} C V_c^2 = \frac{1}{2} L I_{\text{peak}}^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{\text{peak}} = V_c \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}$$ (1.5 marks) **Wavefront time (no crowbar):** For an L-C discharge starting from $V_c$, the current is: $$i(t) = \frac{V_c}{\omega L} \sin(\omega t), \quad \omega = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$ The first peak occurs at $t = \pi/(2\omega) = (\pi/2)\sqrt{LC}$. Using the 10%–90% definition: $$T_1 \approx \pi \sqrt{LC} \quad \text{(oscillatory case, no crowbar)}$$ (1 mark) #### Model Solution to (b): **Given:** $C = 30 \, \mu F = 3 \times 10^{-5} \, F$, $V_c = 25 \, kV = 2.5 \times 10^4 \, V$, $L = 6 \, \mu H = 6 \times 10^{-6} \, H$, $R = 0.45 \, \Omega$. **Step 1: Peak current** $$I_{\text{peak}} = V_c \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}} = 2.5 \times 10^4 \times \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 10^{-5}}{6 \times 10^{-6}}}$$ $$I_{\text{peak}} = 2.5 \times 10^4 \times \sqrt{5} = 2.5 \times 10^4 \times 2.236 = 55.9 \, kA$$ (2 marks) **Step 2: Ideal front time (no crowbar)** $$T_1 = \pi \sqrt{LC} = \pi \sqrt{6 \times 10^{-6} \times 3 \times 10^{-5}} = \pi \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-10}}$$ $$T_1 = \pi \times 1.342 \times 10^{-5} = 4.21 \times 10^{-5} \, s = 42.1 \, \mu s$$ (2 marks) This is much larger than 8 μs — confirms the need for crowbar to shorten the front. **Step 3: Tail time with crowbar** When crowbar fires at peak, the inductor discharges through $R$: $$T_2 \approx \frac{L}{R} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-6}}{0.45} = 13.3 \, \mu s$$ (2 marks) Closer to the standard 20 μs; to achieve exactly 20 μs, $R$ would need to be reduced to ~0.3 Ω. **Observation:** The crowbar operation shortens the front from 42 μs to ~8 μs and shapes the tail to ~13–20 μs range. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Virtual Origin Confusion (2-3 marks):** Students often state $T_1$ as the time from $t=0$ to peak — WRONG. It is $1.67 \times (t_{90} - t_{30})$ from the **virtual origin** (backward-extrapolated). > 2. **Wrong Capacitance Formula:** For $n$ Marx stages in series during discharge, $C_g = C/n$ (NOT $nC$). Many students write $nC$ and lose 2 marks. > 3. **Forgetting "1.67" factor:** When asked for $T_1$ given front time constant $\tau_f$, write $T_1 \approx 3 \tau_f$ or use the 1.67 factor explicitly. > 4. **Crowbar Function:** Writing that crowbar "increases current" — WRONG. It **shortens the tail** by providing a low-impedance short-circuit path after the peak. > 5. **Energy Units:** Impulse generator energy is in **Joules (J)** or **kJ**, not volts. Always show units explicitly. > 6. **Standard Waveform Names:** Switching impulse is 250/2500 μs, NOT 250/250 μs (common typo). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Rapid Revision Checklist: - [x] **Standard Lightning Impulse:** 1.2/50 μs — defined per IEC 60060-1 - [x] **Standard Switching Impulse:** 250/2500 μs — for switching overvoltages - [x] **Standard Impulse Current:** 8/20 μs — for surge arrester testing - [x] **Marx Generator:** $n$ capacitors charged in **parallel** at $V_c$, discharged in **series** giving $V_0 = n V_c$ - [x] **Equivalent Capacitance:** $C_g = C/n$ (series during discharge) - [x] **Front time:** $T_1 = 1.67(t_{90} - t_{30}) \approx 3 \tau_f$ - [x] **Tail time:** $T_2 \approx 0.693 \tau_t$ (from virtual origin to 50%) - [x] **Wave-shaping resistors:** $R_f$ controls front, $R_d$ controls tail - [x] **Energy stored:** $E = \frac{1}{2} n C V_c^2$ (in Joules) - [x] **Impulse current generator:** Capacitor + L-R + crowbar gap - [x] **Peak current:** $I_{\text{peak}} = V_c \sqrt{C/L}$ - [x] **Crowbar role:** Shortens tail by shorting the test object at current peak - [x] **Virtual Origin:** Backward-extrapolated point of intersection of 30%–90% line with time axis ### Key Numerical Values to Memorize: - $\pi \approx 3.1416$ - $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$ - $1.67 \times \ln(3) \approx 1.835$ (theoretical $T_1/\tau_f$) - $e \approx 2.718$ ### Conceptual Map: - **Lightning overvoltage** → 1.2/50 μs voltage test → **Marx Generator** - **Switching overvoltage** → 250/2500 μs voltage test → **Marx with larger $R_f$, $R_d$** - **Direct stroke current** → 8/20 μs current test → **Capacitor + L-R + Crowbar**

topics.title: Impulse Voltage - Impulse Generator Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Impulse Voltage - Impulse Generator Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Impulse Voltage?

An **impulse voltage** is a unidirectional voltage that rises rapidly to a peak value and then decays slowly to zero, characterized by its **front time** and **tail time**. It is used in high voltage laboratories to simulate transient overvoltages that occur in power systems due to lightning strikes and switching operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Impulse Waveforms (As Per IEC 60060-1:2010)**
> - **Standard Lightning Impulse:** $\mathbf{1.2/50\ \mu s}$ — front time $T_1 = 1.2\ \mu s$, time to half value $T_2 = 50\ \mu s$
> - **Standard Switching Impulse:** $\mathbf{250/2500\ \mu s}$ — front time $T_1 = 250\ \mu s$, time to half value $T_2 = 2500\ \mu s$
> - Tolerances: $T_1 \pm 30\%$, $T_2 \pm 20\%$

The shape is **NOT** exponential — it is a **damped double-exponential wave**:
$$V(t) = V_p \left[ e^{-t/\tau_1} - e^{-t/\tau_2} \right] \quad \text{where } \tau_2 \gg \tau_1$$

## 1.2 Why Do We Need Impulse Voltages?

Power system insulation is subjected to transient overvoltages of two principal types:

| Source | Cause | Waveform Simulated |
|--------|-------|--------------------|
| **Lightning** | Direct strike or induced coupling | 1.2/50 μs impulse |
| **Switching Surge** | Line energization, load rejection, fault clearing | 250/2500 μs impulse |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Transmission line insulators, transformer windings, and bushings are routinely impulse-tested at **2–3×** the normal system voltage to verify their lightning/switching withstand capability.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> **Real-World Analogy: "The Pressure Hammer Test"**
> Imagine testing a water pipe. Just as continuous water pressure is one test, you also test the pipe by giving it a sudden "hammer blow" — a sharp pressure spike that rises in 1 millisecond and decays over 50 milliseconds. This hammer blow simulates a **water hammer effect** (analogous to a lightning surge). The pipe that survives this "impulse pressure" is safe for service.
>
> An **impulse generator** in the HV lab is precisely this "pressure hammer" — it creates a brief, intense electrical hammer blow to test insulation against real-world transient stresses.

## 1.4 Impulse Generator — Basic Definition

An **impulse generator** is a circuit that stores electrical energy in a capacitor bank at DC voltage and then releases this energy in a controlled, very short duration into a test object through wave-shaping resistors, producing a standardized impulse waveform.

**Core components of any impulse generator:**
1. **DC charging source** (HVDC rectifier) — to charge the capacitor bank
2. **Charging resistors ($R_c$)** — limit charging current
3. **Discharge/Generator capacitors ($C_1$)** — store the energy
4. **Sphere/Trigger gaps ($G$)** — initiate the discharge
5. **Wavefront resistance ($R_1$ or $R_f$)** — controls the rise time
6. **Wavetail resistance ($R_2$ or $R_t$)** — controls the decay time
7. **Load capacitance ($C_2$)** — represents the test object
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Single-Stage Impulse Generator — Circuit Description

A **single-stage impulse generator** is the fundamental building block. It uses **one capacitor** charged in parallel and discharged through wave-shaping resistors.

### 2.1.1 Charging Phase (DC Mode)

- The DC source $V_s$ charges $C_1$ through the **charging resistor** $R_c$.
- The sphere gap $G$ is open (non-conducting).
- Time to fully charge: $T_{charge} \approx (3 \text{ to } 5) \times R_c \cdot C_1$

> [!NOTE]
> $R_c$ is typically **kΩ to MΩ** range — it must be large enough to limit the charging current but small enough to allow recharging between successive shots.

### 2.1.2 Discharge Phase (Impulse Mode)

When the sphere gap is triggered, $C_1$ discharges through the wave-shaping network $R_1, R_2$ into the load $C_2$.

### 2.1.3 Component Functions

| Component | Symbol | Function | Typical Range |
|-----------|--------|----------|---------------|
| Charging Resistor | $R_c$ | Limits charging current, isolates DC source from discharge | $10\ \text{k}\Omega \text{ to } 1\ \text{M}\Omega$ |
| Generator Capacitor | $C_1$ | Stores energy for the impulse | $0.1 \text{ to } 10\ \mu F$ |
| Sphere Gap | $G$ | Switches from charge to discharge mode | — |
| Front Resistor (Damping) | $R_1$ | Controls wavefront (rise time) | $10\ \Omega \text{ to } 500\ \Omega$ |
| Tail Resistor (Discharge) | $R_2$ | Controls wavetail (decay time) | $1\ \text{k}\Omega \text{ to } 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ |
| Load Capacitance | $C_2$ | Represents test object (transformer/insulator) | $100\ \text{pF to } 10\ \text{nF}$ |

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Charging:** DC source $V_s$ charges $C_1$ to voltage $V_0$ through $R_c$ (which is **much larger** than $R_1$ and $R_2$).

**Step 2 — Triggering:** An external trigger pulse is applied to the **trigatron** (three-electrode sphere gap). This ionizes the gap and causes $G$ to break down at a lower voltage than its self-breakdown value.

**Step 3 — Rapid Voltage Rise:** Once $G$ fires, $C_1$ rapidly transfers charge to $C_2$ through $R_1$. Because $C_1 \gg C_2$, the transfer is fast → this defines the **wavefront** (rise).

**Step 4 — Slow Decay:** After the peak, $C_1$ and $C_2$ together discharge through $R_2$ (and $R_1$ in series) → this defines the **wavetail** (decay).

**Step 5 — Reset:** Gap $G$ de-ionizes (extinguishes arc), circuit returns to charging mode.

## 2.3 Multi-Stage (Marx) Impulse Generator

For voltages above ~200 kV, single-stage circuits become impractical (insulation of DC source, switch ratings). **Marx generator** solves this by charging many capacitors in **parallel** and discharging them in **series**.

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

- **N stages**, each containing capacitor $C_1$ and gap $G_i$ ($i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$).
- **Charging:** All $C_1$'s charge in **parallel** to $V_0$ through resistors $R_c$.
- **Triggering:** First gap $G_1$ is triggered → its breakdown raises the potential of the next stage's lower electrode, causing $G_2$ to over-voltage and fire. **Cascade firing** occurs in microseconds.
- **Discharge:** All $C_1$'s now effectively in **series** → Output voltage = $N \times V_0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The brilliance of the Marx circuit: **Charge at low voltage (V₀), Discharge at high voltage (N·V₀).**

### 2.3.2 Efficiency of Marx Generator

Because of the load capacitance $C_2$, not all the stored voltage appears at the output:

$$\eta = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + N \cdot C_2}$$

This **efficiency drops as N increases** — a fundamental limitation of multi-stage impulse generators.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Notes / Conditions |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Output Peak Voltage (Single Stage) | $V_p = V_0 \cdot \dfrac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2}$ | $C_1 \gg C_2$ ⇒ $V_p \approx V_0$ |
| 2 | Output Peak Voltage (Marx, N stages) | $V_p = N \cdot V_0 \cdot \dfrac{C_1}{C_1 + N C_2}$ | Series discharge |
| 3 | Wavefront Time | $T_1 \approx 3 R_1 C_2$ | For $C_1 \gg C_2$ |
| 4 | Wavetail Time | $T_2 \approx 0.7 (R_1 + R_2) C_1$ | Dominated by $R_2$ if $R_2 \gg R_1$ |
| 5 | Energy Stored | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C_1 V_0^2$ | Per stage |
| 6 | Marx Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{C_1}{C_1 + N C_2}$ | Decreases with N |
| 7 | Charging Time Constant | $\tau_c = R_c C_1$ | Recharge between shots |
| 8 | Equivalent Marx Capacitance | $C_{eq} = C_1 / N$ | Series of N capacitors |
| 9 | Equivalent Marx Front Resistance | $R_{1,eq} = N R_1$ | All in series |
| 10 | Equivalent Marx Tail Resistance | $R_{2,eq} = N R_2$ | All in series |
| 11 | Virtual Front Time (defined) | $T_1 = 1.67 (t_{0.9} - t_{0.3})$ | IEC 60060-1 definition |
| 12 | Time to Half Value (defined) | $T_2 = t_{0.5} - O_1$ | $O_1$ = virtual origin |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transformer Testing:** Every power transformer must undergo **BIL (Basic Insulation Level)** impulse testing before commissioning.
- **Insulator/Insulation Coordination:** Selection of surge arresters, line insulation, and clearance distances.
- **Research:** Aging studies of dielectric materials under impulse stress.
- **Qualification Testing of HV equipment** — bushings, cables, switchgear, instrument transformers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Waveform of Single-Stage Impulse Generator

### 3.1.1 Equivalent Discharge Circuit

When the sphere gap fires, the DC source and charging resistor are isolated (since $R_c \gg R_1, R_2$). The equivalent discharge circuit is:

$$C_1 \longrightarrow R_1 \longrightarrow (R_2 \parallel C_2) \longrightarrow \text{back to } C_1$$

Let:
- $V_1(t)$ = voltage across $C_1$
- $V_2(t)$ = voltage across $C_2$ (= output voltage $V_{out}$)
- Initial conditions: $V_1(0) = V_0$, $V_2(0) = 0$

### 3.1.2 Setting Up the Loop Equation

Let $i$ be the loop current. Applying KVL around the discharge loop:

$$V_1(t) = i \cdot R_1 + V_2(t)$$

Charge conservation (KCL at the output node):

$$i = C_2 \frac{dV_2}{dt} + \frac{V_2}{R_2}$$

For $C_1$:

$$i = -C_1 \frac{dV_1}{dt}$$

### 3.1.3 Differential Equation Setup

Substitute $i = -C_1 \frac{dV_1}{dt}$ into the KVL equation:

$$V_1 = -C_1 R_1 \frac{dV_1}{dt} + V_2$$

Differentiate and substitute. After algebraic manipulation (assuming $C_1 \gg C_2$, which is standard design), we obtain the **second-order linear ODE**:

$$\frac{d^2 V_2}{dt^2} + \left(\frac{1}{R_2 C_2} + \frac{1}{R_1 C_1}\right)\frac{dV_2}{dt} + \frac{V_2}{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2} = 0$$

### 3.1.4 Solution — Double Exponential

Defining the two time constants:
$$\tau_1 = R_1 \cdot \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2} \quad \text{(fast time constant — front)}$$
$$\tau_2 = R_2 (C_1 + C_2) \quad \text{(slow time constant — tail)}$$

The solution satisfying $V_2(0) = 0$ and $V_2'(0) = V_0/(R_1 C_2)$ is:

$$V_2(t) = \frac{V_0 \, C_1}{C_1 + C_2} \cdot \frac{\tau_2}{\tau_2 - \tau_1} \left[ e^{-t/\tau_2} - e^{-t/\tau_1} \right]$$

### 3.1.5 Simplification (For $C_1 \gg C_2$)

Since practical impulse generators satisfy $C_1 \gg C_2$:

$$C_1 + C_2 \approx C_1$$

So the two time constants simplify to:

$$\tau_1 \approx R_1 C_2 \quad \text{and} \quad \tau_2 \approx R_2 C_1$$

And since $\tau_2 \gg \tau_1$ (because $R_2 \gg R_1$ and $C_1 \gg C_2$), the peak voltage:

$$V_p = \frac{V_0 \, C_1}{C_1 + C_2} \cdot \frac{\tau_2}{\tau_2 - \tau_1} \approx V_0 \cdot \frac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2} \approx V_0$$

The simplified standard impulse waveform equation:

$$\boxed{V_2(t) \approx V_0 \left[ e^{-t/\tau_2} - e^{-t/\tau_1} \right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of simplification:** The peak occurs at time $t_p$ when $dV_2/dt = 0$. Setting the derivative to zero gives $t_p = \frac{\tau_1 \tau_2}{\tau_2 - \tau_1} \ln(\tau_2/\tau_1)$. For $\tau_2 \gg \tau_1$, $t_p \approx 2.3 \tau_1$ (front), and the peak is dominated by the slow decay $e^{-t/\tau_2}$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Front Time ($T_1$)

The **virtual front time** $T_1$ is defined by IEC 60060-1 as:

$$T_1 = 1.67 \times (t_{0.9} - t_{0.3})$$

where $t_{0.3}$ and $t_{0.9}$ are times when voltage reaches 30% and 90% of peak, respectively.

For the simplified equation $V_2(t) = V_0 [e^{-t/\tau_2} - e^{-t/\tau_1}]$:

At small $t$ (wavefront region), $e^{-t/\tau_2} \approx 1$, so:

$$V_2(t) \approx V_0 \left[ 1 - e^{-t/\tau_1} \right]$$

Solving for the times:

$$V_2(t_{0.3}) = 0.3 V_0 \Rightarrow t_{0.3} = -\tau_1 \ln(0.7) = 0.357 \tau_1$$

$$V_2(t_{0.9}) = 0.9 V_0 \Rightarrow t_{0.9} = -\tau_1 \ln(0.1) = 2.303 \tau_1$$

Therefore:

$$t_{0.9} - t_{0.3} = (2.303 - 0.357)\tau_1 = 1.946 \tau_1$$

$$T_1 = 1.67 \times 1.946 \tau_1 \approx 3.25 \tau_1$$

For engineering practice (and as per KTU textbook notation), the **approximate formula** used is:

$$\boxed{T_1 \approx 3 R_1 C_2}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Tail Time ($T_2$)

The **time to half value** $T_2$ is the time from virtual origin $O_1$ until the voltage decays to 50% of peak.

At large $t$ (tail region), $e^{-t/\tau_1} \to 0$, so:

$$V_2(t) \approx V_0 e^{-t/\tau_2}$$

Setting $V_2(T_2) = 0.5 V_0$:

$$0.5 V_0 = V_0 e^{-T_2/\tau_2}$$

$$T_2 = \tau_2 \ln 2 = 0.693 \tau_2$$

Substituting $\tau_2 = R_2 (C_1 + C_2) \approx (R_1 + R_2) C_1$ (when both resistors are in series in the discharge path):

$$\boxed{T_2 \approx 0.7 (R_1 + R_2) C_1 \approx 0.7 R_2 C_1 \quad (\text{since } R_2 \gg R_1)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Marx Generator Output

### 3.4.1 Initial Voltage

Each of the N stages has capacitor $C_1$ charged to $V_0$. The total stored voltage, when discharged in series = $N \cdot V_0$.

### 3.4.2 Equivalent Circuit After Triggering

After all gaps fire, the equivalent circuit is:
- One capacitor of value $C_1/N$ (series combination)
- Front resistance $N R_1$ (series)
- Tail resistance $N R_2$ (series or parallel depending on topology)
- Load $C_2$

### 3.4.3 Peak Voltage Across Load

Using the same charge-transfer principle:

$$V_p = (N V_0) \cdot \frac{C_1/N}{C_1/N + C_2} = N V_0 \cdot \frac{C_1}{C_1 + N C_2}$$

$$\boxed{V_p = \eta \cdot N V_0 \quad \text{where} \quad \eta = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + N C_2}}$$

### 3.4.4 Front and Tail Times for Marx

$$\boxed{T_1 = 3 R_1 C_2 \quad \text{(unchanged — only } R_1 \text{ is in series, but it's between stage and load)}}$$

$$\boxed{T_2 = 0.7 N R_2 \cdot \frac{C_1}{N} = 0.7 R_2 C_1 \quad \text{(front and tail per stage remain the same)}}$$

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

> **Problem:** A 6-stage Marx impulse generator has each stage capacitor $C_1 = 0.4\ \mu F$, charged to $V_0 = 100\ kV$. The front resistor per stage is $R_1 = 40\ \Omega$ and the tail resistor per stage is $R_2 = 4000\ \Omega$. The load capacitance is $C_2 = 1000\ pF$. Calculate the output peak voltage, wavefront time, and wavetail time.

**Step 1 — Equivalent circuit parameters:**

$$C_{eq} = C_1 / N = 0.4\ \mu F / 6 = 0.0667\ \mu F$$
$$R_{1,eq} = N R_1 = 6 \times 40 = 240\ \Omega$$
$$R_{2,eq} = N R_2 = 6 \times 4000 = 24000\ \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Peak Voltage:**

$$V_p = N V_0 \cdot \frac{C_1}{C_1 + N C_2}$$

$$V_p = 6 \times 100\ kV \times \frac{0.4 \times 10^{-6}}{0.4 \times 10^{-6} + 6 \times 1000 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$V_p = 600\ kV \times \frac{0.4 \times 10^{-6}}{0.4 \times 10^{-6} + 6 \times 10^{-9}} = 600\ kV \times \frac{0.4}{0.406}$$

$$V_p = 600 \times 0.9852 = 591.1\ kV$$

**Step 3 — Front Time:**

$$T_1 = 3 R_1 C_2 = 3 \times 40 \times 1000 \times 10^{-12} = 1.2 \times 10^{-7}\ s = 0.12\ \mu s$$

*(For exact 1.2 μs front, this needs adjustment — e.g., add external $R_1$ between the last stage and load.)*

**Step 4 — Tail Time:**

$$T_2 = 0.7 \cdot (R_{1,eq} + R_{2,eq}) \cdot C_{eq} = 0.7 \times (240 + 24000) \times 0.0667 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T_2 = 0.7 \times 24240 \times 0.0667 \times 10^{-6} = 1.13 \times 10^{-3}\ s = 1131\ \mu s$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + N C_2} = \frac{0.4 \times 10^{-6}}{0.406 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.9852 = 98.52\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** Always draw the equivalent single-stage circuit first, then identify $\tau_1 = R_1 C_2$ and $\tau_2 = R_2 C_1$ (or equivalents). Then plug into the standard formulas — this avoids re-deriving every time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single-Stage Impulse Generator — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    DC[DC HVDC Source] -->|Charges| RC[Charging Resistor Rc]
    RC -->|Charges| C1[Generator Capacitor C1]
    C1 --- GAP[Sphere Gap G]
    GAP -->|Discharges through| R1[Wavefront Resistor R1]
    R1 -->|Tail shaping| R2[Tail Resistor R2]
    R1 --> NODE
    NODE[Output Node] --> C2[Load Capacitor C2]
    NODE --> OUT[Output to Test Object]
    R2 -.->|Parallel with C2| NODE
    TRIG[Trigger Pulse] -.->|External ionization| GAP
    style DC fill:#fff4cc
    style C1 fill:#cce5ff
    style GAP fill:#ffcccc
    style R1 fill:#ccffcc
    style R2 fill:#ccffcc
    style C2 fill:#cce5ff
    style OUT fill:#ffccff
    style TRIG fill:#ffd9b3
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**
- **DC + Rc** block: Energy input and isolation
- **C1**: Energy storage
- **G**: Fast electronic switch (trigatron)
- **R1**: Front (rise) time control
- **R2**: Tail (decay) time control
- **C2**: Load / test object

## 4.2 Multi-Stage (Marx) Impulse Generator — Cascade Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1]
        C1a[C1] --- G1[G1]
        Rc1[Rc] --- C1a
    end
    subgraph Stage2[Stage 2]
        C1b[C1] --- G2[G2]
        Rc2[Rc] --- C1b
    end
    subgraph StageN[Stage N]
        C1n[C1] --- Gn[GN]
        Rcn[Rc] --- C1n
    end
    Stage1 -->|Series connection| Stage2
    Stage2 -->|Series connection| StageN
    DC[DC Source V0] -->|Parallel charging bus| Rc1
    DC --> Rc2
    DC --> Rcn
    StageN --> R1f[External Front Resistor R1]
    R1f --> C2L[Load Capacitor C2]
    C2L --> VOUT[Output Terminal]
    VOUT -->|Return| DC
    TRIGP[Trigger Pulse] -.-> G1
    G1 -.->|Overvoltages| G2
    G2 -.->|Cascade| Gn
    style DC fill:#fff4cc
    style C1a fill:#cce5ff
    style C1b fill:#cce5ff
    style C1n fill:#cce5ff
    style G1 fill:#ffcccc
    style G2 fill:#ffcccc
    style Gn fill:#ffcccc
    style VOUT fill:#ffccff
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**
- **DC bus** charges all $C_1$'s **in parallel** to $V_0$ through $R_c$'s
- **Trigger pulse** fires $G_1$ → **cascade over-voltage** causes $G_2, G_3, \ldots, G_N$ to break down
- All $C_1$'s now in **series** → output = $N \cdot V_0$

## 4.3 Standard Impulse Waveform — Parameter Identification

```mermaid
graph TB
    AX[Time axis] --> O1[Virtual Origin O1]
    O1 --> T030[30 percent Point]
    T030 --> T090[90 percent Point]
    T090 --> PEAK[Peak Value Vp]
    PEAK --> T050[50 percent Point]
    T050 --> TAIL[End of Wave]
    T030 -.->|t0.3| T090
    T090 -.->|T1 Rise| PEAK
    PEAK -.->|T2 Decay| T050
    style O1 fill:#ffd9b3
    style T030 fill:#cce5ff
    style T090 fill:#cce5ff
    style PEAK fill:#ffcccc
    style T050 fill:#ccffcc
```

**Waveform Parameter Definitions:**
- **T1 (Wavefront Time):** $1.67 \times (t_{0.9} - t_{0.3})$ — drawn from 30% and 90% intercepts
- **T2 (Time to Half Value):** Time from $O_1$ to 50% point on the tail
- **O1 (Virtual Origin):** Point on time axis where the line joining 30% and 90% points intersects

## 4.4 Triggering Methods — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TrigA[Trigatron Triggering]
        TA1[Trigger Electrode] --> TA2[Field Distortion]
        TA2 --> TA3[Lower breakdown voltage]
    end
    subgraph TrigB[UV Irradiation Triggering]
        TB1[UV Lamp] --> TB2[Pre-ionization of gap]
        TB2 --> TB3[Reduced statistical lag]
    end
    subgraph TrigC[Laser Triggering]
        TC1[Pulsed Laser] --> TC2[Multi-photon ionization]
        TC2 --> TC3[Sub-ns jitter]
    end
    TrigA --> APPL[Applied to Marx stage 1]
    TrigB --> APPL
    TrigC --> APPL
    style APPL fill:#ffccff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define impulse voltage. What are the standard impulse waveforms used in HV testing as per IEC standards?**

**Model Answer:**

> An **impulse voltage** is a unidirectional transient voltage that rises rapidly to a peak value and then decays to zero, used to simulate lightning and switching overvoltages in HV testing.
>
> **Standard waveforms (IEC 60060-1):**
> 1. **Standard Lightning Impulse:** $1.2/50\ \mu s$ — front time $T_1 = 1.2\ \mu s$, time to half value $T_2 = 50\ \mu s$. Used to simulate lightning overvoltages.
> 2. **Standard Switching Impulse:** $250/2500\ \mu s$ — front time $T_1 = 250\ \mu s$, time to half value $T_2 = 2500\ \mu s$. Used to simulate switching surges.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**State the functions of the front resistor and tail resistor in an impulse generator circuit.**

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Front (Damping) Resistor $R_1$:** Connected in series with the load. It **controls the rate of rise** of the output voltage. The wavefront time $T_1 \approx 3 R_1 C_2$. A small $R_1$ gives a fast (steep) front; a large $R_1$ gives a slower front. It also damps any oscillations on the wavefront.
>
> 2. **Tail (Discharge) Resistor $R_2$:** Connected in parallel with the load capacitor $C_2$. It **controls the rate of decay** of the output voltage. The wavetail time $T_2 \approx 0.7 R_2 C_1$. A large $R_2$ gives a slow tail; a small $R_2$ gives a fast decay.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Choice-Based Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ **Confusing** $C_1$ and $C_2$ in formulas — remember $C_1$ is the **generator (energy storage)** capacitor and $C_2$ is the **load**.
> - ❌ Forgetting that for **Marx**, the equivalent $C = C_1/N$ and the equivalent $R = N R$ — students often use $C_1$ and $R_1$ directly, giving wrong answers.
> - ❌ **Not calculating efficiency** — KTU expects the efficiency $\eta = C_1/(C_1 + N C_2)$ to be explicitly stated.
> - ❌ Drawing Marx circuit as a **single block** instead of showing charging (parallel) and discharging (series) modes.
> - ❌ Confusing **$T_1$** (front time) and **$T_2$** (tail time) when reading the problem.

---

### Choice A — Question (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a single-stage impulse generator. Derive the expressions for the front time and tail time of the generated impulse wave.**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Circuit, Working, and Wavefront Derivation**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram (as drawn in Section 4.1 above):**

Components: DC source $V_s$, charging resistor $R_c$, generator capacitor $C_1$, sphere gap $G$, front resistor $R_1$, tail resistor $R_2$, load capacitor $C_2$.

**Working — Two Modes:**

**(i) Charging Mode:** DC source $V_s$ charges $C_1$ through $R_c$ to voltage $V_0$. The gap $G$ is open. Charging time constant $\tau_c = R_c C_1$ (typically a few seconds).

**(ii) Discharge Mode:** An external trigger pulse is applied to the trigatron electrode. The gap $G$ ionizes and breaks down. $C_1$ now discharges through $R_1$ into the parallel combination of $R_2$ and $C_2$.

**Wavefront (Front) Derivation:**

The equivalent circuit during discharge has two time constants:
$$\tau_1 = R_1 \cdot \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}, \quad \tau_2 = (R_1 + R_2) \cdot (C_1 + C_2)$$

For $C_1 \gg C_2$:
$$\tau_1 \approx R_1 C_2, \quad \tau_2 \approx (R_1 + R_2) C_1$$

Output voltage (using initial condition $V_2(0) = 0$, $V_1(0) = V_0$):
$$V_2(t) = \frac{V_0 C_1}{C_1 + C_2} \cdot \frac{1}{\tau_2 - \tau_1} \left[ \tau_2 e^{-t/\tau_2} - \tau_1 e^{-t/\tau_1} \right]$$

Since $\tau_2 \gg \tau_1$, the **front** is dominated by the rapidly decaying $e^{-t/\tau_1}$ term. The front time is:

$$\boxed{T_1 = 3 R_1 C_2}$$

(2 marks for circuit diagram, 3 marks for working, 2 marks for front time derivation)

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Tail Time Derivation and Numerical Application**

**Tail Time Derivation:**

In the tail region ($t \to$ large), $e^{-t/\tau_1} \to 0$ rapidly, so:
$$V_2(t) \approx \frac{V_0 C_1}{C_1 + C_2} \cdot \frac{\tau_2}{\tau_2 - \tau_1} e^{-t/\tau_2}$$

For the half-value time $T_2$ when $V_2(T_2) = 0.5 V_0$:
$$0.5 = e^{-T_2/\tau_2} \Rightarrow T_2 = \tau_2 \ln 2 = 0.693 \tau_2$$

Since $\tau_2 \approx (R_1 + R_2) C_1$ and $R_2 \gg R_1$:

$$\boxed{T_2 \approx 0.7 (R_1 + R_2) C_1 \approx 0.7 R_2 C_1}$$

**Numerical:** A single-stage generator has $C_1 = 1\ \mu F$, $C_2 = 2000\ pF$, $R_1 = 50\ \Omega$, $R_2 = 5000\ \Omega$, $V_0 = 150\ kV$. Find $T_1$, $T_2$, and $V_p$.

$$T_1 = 3 \times 50 \times 2000 \times 10^{-12} = 0.3\ \mu s$$
$$T_2 = 0.7 \times 5050 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} = 3535\ \mu s \approx 3.5\ ms$$
$$V_p = 150\ kV \times \frac{1\ \mu F}{1\ \mu F + 2000\ pF} = 150 \times 0.9998 = 149.97\ kV$$

(3 marks for tail derivation, 2 marks for numerical, 2 marks for final result interpretation)

---

### Choice B — Question (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2022]`

**Explain the working principle of a multi-stage Marx impulse generator with a neat circuit diagram. Derive the expressions for output voltage, efficiency, front time, and tail time.**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Marx Circuit and Working**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram (as drawn in Section 4.2):** N stages, each with $C_1$, charging resistor $R_c$, and sphere gap $G_i$. Stages are connected through gaps and wave-shaping resistors.

**Working — Two Phases:**

**Phase 1: Charging (Parallel Mode)**

All capacitors $C_1$ are charged in **parallel** to voltage $V_0$ from the DC source through charging resistors $R_c$. The gaps $G_1, G_2, \ldots, G_N$ are not conducting. Charging time constant is $R_c C_1$.

**Phase 2: Discharging (Series Mode — Cascade Triggering)**

An external trigger pulse is applied to the first gap $G_1$. It breaks down. The voltage across $C_1$ of stage 1 now adds to the next stage's potential, causing $G_2$ to over-voltage and break down. This cascade continues through all stages in microseconds. The result: all $C_1$'s are now effectively in **series**, and the output voltage is $N \times V_0$.

> **Key Insight:** Charging at **low voltage $V_0$** for high **DC safety**, discharging at **high voltage $N V_0$** for the test object.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Derivation of Output Voltage, Efficiency, Front, and Tail**

**Output Voltage and Efficiency:**

The equivalent series capacitor is $C_{eq} = C_1/N$ and the total source voltage is $N V_0$. Applying the same charge-transfer principle as for the single stage:

$$V_p = N V_0 \cdot \frac{C_{eq}}{C_{eq} + C_2} = N V_0 \cdot \frac{C_1/N}{C_1/N + C_2} = N V_0 \cdot \frac{C_1}{C_1 + N C_2}$$

$$\boxed{V_p = \eta \cdot N V_0 \quad \text{where} \quad \eta = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + N C_2}}$$

**Efficiency analysis:** As $N$ increases, $\eta$ decreases. For $C_1 \gg N C_2$, $\eta \to 1$.

**Front Time:** With the equivalent front resistance $R_{1,eq} = N R_1$ in series and load $C_2$:
$$\tau_1 \approx N R_1 \cdot C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{T_1 = 3 N R_1 C_2}$$
(In practice, the last stage's $R_1$ is split — part inside the stage, part external to the load — so $T_1 \approx 3 R_1 C_2$ where $R_1$ is the *external* front resistor.)

**Tail Time:** With $R_{2,eq} = N R_2$ and $C_{eq} = C_1/N$:
$$\boxed{T_2 = 0.7 \cdot N R_2 \cdot \frac{C_1}{N} = 0.7 R_2 C_1}$$

(Tail time is **independent of the number of stages** for the same per-stage components — a useful design fact!)

**Numerical:** A 10-stage Marx generator has $C_1 = 0.5\ \mu F$, $R_1 = 75\ \Omega$, $R_2 = 5000\ \Omega$, $C_2 = 1500\ pF$, $V_0 = 100\ kV$.

$$V_p = 10 \times 100 \times \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5 \times 10^{-6} + 10 \times 1500 \times 10^{-12}} = 1000 \times \frac{0.5}{0.515} = 970.9\ kV$$
$$T_1 = 3 \times 75 \times 1500 \times 10^{-12} = 0.3375\ \mu s$$
$$T_2 = 0.7 \times 5000 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} = 1.75\ ms$$

(2 marks circuit + 2 marks working, 3 marks derivations, 2 marks numerical)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Impulse Generator Circuits**

### A. Conceptual Recap
- [x] **Impulse voltage** = brief unidirectional transient to simulate lightning/switching
- [x] **Standard Lightning Impulse:** 1.2/50 μs (most common)
- [x] **Standard Switching Impulse:** 250/2500 μs
- [x] **Generator operation:** charge in **parallel**, discharge in **series** (Marx)
- [x] **Standard waveform equation:** $V(t) = V_0 [e^{-t/\tau_2} - e^{-t/\tau_1}]$, $\tau_2 \gg \tau_1$

### B. Key Formulas to Memorize (No Pipe Symbols Used)
- [x] $V_p = V_0 \cdot \dfrac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2}$ (single stage)
- [x] $V_p = N V_0 \cdot \dfrac{C_1}{C_1 + N C_2}$ (Marx)
- [x] $T_1 \approx 3 R_1 C_2$ — **depends on load and front resistor only**
- [x] $T_2 \approx 0.7 R_2 C_1$ — **independent of N in Marx**
- [x] $\eta = \dfrac{C_1}{C_1 + N C_2}$
- [x] $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C_1 V_0^2$ (energy per stage)
- [x] $\tau_1 \approx R_1 C_2$, $\tau_2 \approx R_2 C_1$

### C. Component Functions (One-Liners)
- [x] $R_c$ → limits charging current
- [x] $C_1$ → energy storage
- [x] $G$ → fast switch (trigatron)
- [x] $R_1$ → controls front (rise) time
- [x] $R_2$ → controls tail (decay) time
- [x] $C_2$ → represents test object

### D. Critical Design Insights
- [x] **Design rule:** $C_1 \gg C_2$ (so that $V_p \approx V_0$ or $N V_0$)
- [x] **Tail time in Marx is independent of N** for the same per-stage $R_2$ and $C_1$
- [x] **Efficiency drops with N** — this is why impulse generators are designed with intermediate output stages
- [x] **Triggering** of multi-stage Marx is achieved by **cascade over-voltage** of sphere gaps
- [x] **Virtual front time** $T_1$ is defined using 30% and 90% points, not 0% and 100%
- [x] $T_1$ tolerance ±30%, $T_2$ tolerance ±20% as per IEC 60060-1

### E. Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- [x] ❌ Don't write $V_p = N V_0$ (you forgot the efficiency factor!)
- [x] ❌ Don't confuse $C_1$ (generator) and $C_2$ (load)
- [x] ❌ Don't forget to convert $C_2$ from pF to F (or μF) — units matter
- [x] ❌ In Marx, don't apply $T_1 = 3 R_1 C_2$ with $R_1$ being per-stage without checking if it's the external resistor
- [x] ❌ Don't draw the circuit and forget the trigger mechanism for the first gap
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multistage Impulse Generator Circuit - Construction of Impulse Generator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multistage Impulse Generator Circuit - Construction of Impulse Generator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Multistage Impulse Generator (MIG)** is a high-voltage impulse generator that produces a standard non-oscillatory unidirectional voltage impulse (typically **1.2 / 50 μs** lightning impulse or **250 / 2500 μs** switching impulse) by charging a bank of capacitors in **parallel** through high-value charging resistors and then discharging them in **series** through a set of triggered sphere gaps and wave-shaping resistors.

The circuit is the practical realization of the **Marx Multiplier Circuit** proposed by Erwin Otto Marx (1923), and remains the universally accepted standard apparatus for generating impulse voltages in **high-voltage testing laboratories** for verifying the dielectric strength of power system equipment (transformers, cables, insulators, bushings, surge arresters, etc.) as per **IS 2071 / IEC 60060-1** standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** Construction and operation of multistage impulse generator circuits form the foundation for understanding impulse voltage generation, impulse current generation, and standard impulse waveforms used in high-voltage engineering practice.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Water-Tower Cascade"

Imagine a row of **identical water tanks** mounted side by side at ground level. Each tank has the same capacity, and a thin pipe connects the bottom of one tank to the top of the next, with a **small one-way valve** (a "trigger gap") between them.

**Charging phase (parallel):** A low-pressure pump quietly fills **all the tanks simultaneously** from a common supply pipe — each tank now holds the same modest amount of water at low pressure.

**Triggering:** When you strike the first valve with a small hammer (the **trigger pulse**), the first valve opens. The water from the first tank now falls down its outlet pipe to the next tank's inlet — but wait, the inlet is at the *top* of the next tank, so the **pressure adds**: the second tank now sees double the original pressure. This over-pressurisation punches open the second valve, then the third, and so on in a **domino cascade**.

**Discharge (series):** In a fraction of a microsecond, **all valves are open**, and the tanks are effectively stacked one on top of the other. The water rushes out from the topmost tank through a shaped nozzle (the **wave-shaping resistors**), producing a single high-pressure squirt — the **impulse wave** — that hits the test target.

The slow pump = the **DC charging source**; the tanks = the **stage capacitors**; the valves = the **sphere gaps**; the shaped nozzle = the **front and tail resistors**; the cascade = the **multistage Marx action**.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Lightning Impulse (per IEC 60060-1):**
> - **Front time $T_1 = 1.2 \ \mu s$** (virtual front time, 30 % to 90 % rise)
> - **Time to half-value $T_2 = 50 \ \mu s$**
> - **Tolerance:** $\pm 30\%$ on $T_1$, $\pm 20\%$ on $T_2$
> - **Peak voltage range:** **200 kV to 3 MV** (typical laboratory ratings)
> - **Stage charging voltage:** typically **$\pm 50$ kV to $\pm 100$ kV DC**
> - **Number of stages:** typically **6 to 30** stages in cascade

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Switching Impulse:**
> - $T_p / T_2 = 250 / 2500 \ \mu s$
> - Generated by the same multistage generator with different wave-shaping component values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details of a Single Stage

A single stage of the multistage impulse generator contains the following components, arranged as shown in Section 4:

| S.No | Component | Symbol | Typical Range | Function |
|------|-----------|--------|---------------|----------|
| 1 | **Stage Capacitor** | $C$ | $0.1$ to $1.0 \ \mu F$ | Energy storage element; charged in parallel, discharged in series |
| 2 | **Charging Resistor** | $R_c$ | $10$ to $100 \ k\Omega$ | Allows slow DC charging; prevents short-circuit during cascade breakdown |
| 3 | **Front (Wave) Resistor** | $R_f$ | $10$ to $800 \ \Omega$ | Controls the **rise time** of the impulse wave |
| 4 | **Tail (Damping) Resistor** | $R_t$ | $1$ to $20 \ k\Omega$ | Controls the **decay time** of the impulse wave |
| 5 | **Sphere Gap** | $G_1, G_2, \dots, G_n$ | Copper / brass spheres | Triggered switching element; enables parallel-to-series transition |
| 6 | **Trigger Electrode** | $T$ | Central electrode in $G_1$ | Initiates the cascade by reducing breakdown voltage of $G_1$ |
| 7 | **DC Charging Source** | $V_{dc}$ | $\pm 50$ to $\pm 100 \ kV$ | High-voltage DC supply from rectifier + capacitor filter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $R_c$ must be very large (kΩ range):** During the cascade discharge, the lower plates of all capacitors become raised to high voltage through the series connection. If $R_c$ were small, it would short-circuit adjacent stages and dissipate the generated impulse. By keeping $R_c \gg R_f, R_t$, the charging resistors are effectively **open-circuited** during the brief discharge (μs timescale).

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism

### Stage A — Charging Mode (Steady State, Duration: seconds)
1. The DC source charges **all $n$ stage capacitors to the same DC voltage $V$** through the high-value charging resistors $R_c$.
2. The sphere gaps are set with a spacing such that their **self-breakdown voltage is slightly higher than $V$** — they hold off without firing.
3. Total stored energy: $E = \dfrac{1}{2} n C V^2$.
4. All capacitors are connected in **parallel** with respect to the DC bus.

### Stage B — Triggering Initiation (Duration: $\mu s$)
1. A small **trigger pulse** is applied to the central electrode of the first sphere gap $G_1$.
2. This distorts the field and causes $G_1$ to break down, becoming a virtual short-circuit.
3. The **lower plate of $C_1$** is now suddenly lifted from ground potential to nearly the full $V$ (the voltage of the upper plate of $C_1$).

### Stage C — Marx Cascade (Duration: $\le 0.1 \ \mu s$)
1. With $G_1$ shorted, the voltage across $G_2$ becomes **$2V$** — the original charging voltage of $C_2$ *plus* the voltage appearing across the now-firing $G_1$.
2. This **2× overvoltage** forces $G_2$ to break down immediately.
3. The cascade continues: $G_3$ sees $3V$, $G_4$ sees $4V$, and so on.
4. All $n$ gaps break down in rapid succession, and the capacitors find themselves effectively connected in **series**.

### Stage D — Discharge into Test Object (Duration: tens of μs)
1. The **series-stacked capacitors** (total capacitance $C_s = C / n$) now discharge through the wave-shaping network $R_f$, $R_t$ into the test object (capacitance $C_b$).
2. The output voltage rises rapidly to a peak value close to $nV$, then decays exponentially — producing the standard **double-exponential impulse waveform**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The transition from parallel charging to series discharge is the **core ingenuity** of the Marx circuit. It allows a relatively low-voltage DC supply ($\sim 100$ kV) to generate multi-megavolt impulses ($1$–$3$ MV) using a stack of identical, commercially available capacitors.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | $V_{out} = n \, V$ | Ideal peak output voltage ($n$ stages, each charged to $V$) | Volts (V) — *ideal, no losses* |
| 2 | $V_{out} = \eta \, n \, V$ | Actual output voltage, $\eta$ = generator efficiency | $\eta = 0.80$ to $0.95$ |
| 3 | $C_s = C / n$ | Equivalent series discharge capacitance | Farads (F) |
| 4 | $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_s \, C_b}{C_s + C_b}$ | Equivalent capacitance of $C_s$ in series with load $C_b$ | Farads (F) |
| 5 | $T_1 = 3 \, R_f \, C_{eq} = 3 R_f \dfrac{C_s C_b}{C_s + C_b}$ | **Virtual front time** (1.2 μs standard) | Seconds (s) |
| 6 | $T_2 = 0.7 \, (R_t + R_f) C_s \approx 0.7 \, R_t \, C_s$ | **Time to half-value / tail time** (50 μs standard) — valid when $R_t \gg R_f$ | Seconds (s) |
| 7 | $R_c \gg R_f, \ R_t$ | Design constraint to isolate charging and discharging paths | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | $E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} n C V^2$ | Total stored electrostatic energy | Joules (J) |
| 9 | $E_{delivered} = \dfrac{V_{out}^2}{R_t} \cdot T_2$ | Approximate energy delivered to load | Joules (J) |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{C_s}{C_s + C_b} \cdot \dfrac{R_t}{R_t + R_f} \cdot k_{gap}$ | Multi-factor efficiency (capacitive divider, resistive divider, gap non-simultaneity) | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used directly in the **design of impulse test labs** — selecting the capacitor rating, calculating the charging transformer secondary voltage, sizing the wave-shaping resistors for the required standard waveform, and estimating the test object voltage divider ratio for measurement.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Routine & Type Testing of Power Transformers** as per IS 2026 / IEC 60076.
- **Dielectric Testing of HV Cables, Bushings, Insulators** — both lightning impulse (1.2/50 μs) and switching impulse (250/2500 μs).
- **Calibration of Impulse Voltage Dividers** and peak voltmeters.
- **Surge Arrester Testing** — impulse current generators are a closely related Marx-derivative design.
- **Steep-Front Impulse Testing** for GIS (Gas-Insulated Switchgear) — uses specially designed low-inductance Marx stages.
- **Research Applications** — pulsed power, plasma physics, high-energy density physics, and electromagnetic launcher research all use Marx-derived circuits.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equivalent Series Capacitance $C_s$ of $n$ Stages

**Given:** $n$ identical stage capacitors, each of capacitance $C$, initially charged in parallel to voltage $V$, then reconnected in series during discharge.

When $n$ capacitors each of capacitance $C$ are connected in **series**, the equivalent capacitance is:

$$ C_s = \left[ \dfrac{1}{C} + \dfrac{1}{C} + \cdots + \dfrac{1}{C} \right]^{-1} = \left[ \dfrac{n}{C} \right]^{-1} = \dfrac{C}{n} $$

This is the **total discharge capacitance** seen by the load during the impulse.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ideal Output Voltage $V_{out} = nV$

**Given:** $n$ capacitors each charged to $V$ and reconnected in series.

By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) applied around the series loop during discharge, the total voltage is the sum of all individual capacitor voltages:

$$ V_{out} = V_{C_1} + V_{C_2} + \cdots + V_{C_n} $$

Since each capacitor is charged to the same voltage $V$:

$$ V_{C_1} = V_{C_2} = \cdots = V_{C_n} = V $$

Therefore:

$$ V_{out} = V + V + \cdots + V \ (n \ \text{times}) $$

$$ \boxed{\,V_{out} = n \, V\,} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **ideal (no-loss) peak output voltage**. The actual output is lower due to charging-resistor losses, load-capacitance sharing, and non-simultaneous gap firing (covered in the efficiency formula).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent Discharge Circuit

During discharge, the active circuit reduces to a single equivalent network:

- A charged capacitor $C_s = C / n$ at voltage $nV$.
- Connected through the **front resistor $R_f$** (in series) and the **tail resistor $R_t$** (in parallel branch) to the **load (test object) capacitance $C_b$**.

The standard equivalent circuit (used by KTU/Naidu textbook derivation) simplifies to:

- **Capacitor $C_s$** discharges through $R_f$ into the parallel combination of $C_b$ and $R_t$.
- The voltage across $C_b$ is the **impulse output voltage $v(t)$**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Front Time $T_1$ (Rise Time of the Impulse)

**Assumption (KTU standard):** $R_t \gg R_f$ (typical design: $R_t \sim 10 \ k\Omega$, $R_f \sim 50$–$800 \ \Omega$).

Under this condition, the tail resistor branch draws negligible current during the **short** front-time interval (μs scale). The circuit reduces to $C_s$ discharging through $R_f$ into $C_b$ — a classic **two-capacitor transfer problem**.

The equivalent capacitance seen by the discharge current is the **series combination of $C_s$ and $C_b$**:

$$ C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_s \cdot C_b}{C_s + C_b} $$

The time constant of the front (rise) portion is:

$$ \tau_f = R_f \cdot C_{eq} = R_f \cdot \dfrac{C_s C_b}{C_s + C_b} $$

By definition, the **virtual front time** $T_1$ is **3 times the time constant** (this is the IEC 60060-1 definition — front time is measured from the 30 % to 90 % points of the peak, and equals 3τ for a single-time-constant rise):

$$ \boxed{\,T_1 = 3 \, R_f \cdot \dfrac{C_s \, C_b}{C_s + C_b} = 3 R_f \cdot C_{eq}\,} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Set $T_1 = 1.2 \ \mu s$ for the standard lightning impulse. Given $C_s$ and $C_b$, solve for $R_f$:
> $$ R_f = \dfrac{T_1 \, (C_s + C_b)}{3 \, C_s \, C_b} $$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Tail Time $T_2$ (Time to Half-Value)

After the voltage reaches its peak (in a few microseconds), the slow discharge dominates. Now $C_b$ has charged up and behaves as an **open circuit** (no further current flows into it from $C_s$ because the voltages have equalized in the front-time scale).

The remaining slow discharge is governed by $C_s$ discharging through the **series combination** of $R_f$ and $R_t$:

$$ R_{total,tail} = R_f + R_t $$

The time constant for the tail decay is:

$$ \tau_t = (R_f + R_t) \cdot C_s $$

The voltage across the load follows an exponential decay:

$$ v(t) = V_{peak} \cdot e^{-t / \tau_t} $$

The **time to half-value $T_2$** is defined as the time at which $v(t)$ drops to $0.5 \cdot V_{peak}$:

$$ 0.5 = e^{-T_2 / \tau_t} $$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$ \ln(0.5) = -\dfrac{T_2}{\tau_t} $$

$$ -\ln(2) = -\dfrac{T_2}{\tau_t} $$

$$ \dfrac{T_2}{\tau_t} = \ln(2) \approx 0.693 $$

Therefore:

$$ T_2 = 0.693 \, \tau_t \approx 0.7 \, (R_f + R_t) \cdot C_s $$

Since in practice $R_t \gg R_f$, the formula is often simplified to:

$$ \boxed{\,T_2 \approx 0.7 \, R_t \, C_s\,} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Problem Hint:** Set $T_2 = 50 \ \mu s$ for the standard lightning impulse. Given $C_s$ and $T_2$, solve for $R_t$:
> $$ R_t = \dfrac{T_2}{0.7 \, C_s} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{0.7 \, C_s} $$

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Output Voltage Efficiency $\eta$

The **actual peak output voltage** is always lower than the ideal $nV$ due to three principal loss mechanisms.

### Loss Mechanism 1 — Capacitive Divider Effect
The load capacitance $C_b$ forms a capacitive divider with $C_s$. The fraction of voltage appearing across the load is:

$$ \dfrac{V_{load}}{V_{source}} = \dfrac{C_s}{C_s + C_b} $$

This is a loss factor $\eta_1 = \dfrac{C_s}{C_s + C_b}$.

### Loss Mechanism 2 — Resistive Divider Effect
The tail resistor $R_t$ draws current continuously, forming a resistive divider with the load. The voltage across the load during steady state is:

$$ \eta_2 = \dfrac{R_{load}}{R_t + R_{load}} $$

For a high-impedance load, $\eta_2 \to 1$; for low-impedance loads, $\eta_2$ is smaller.

### Loss Mechanism 3 — Sphere Gap Non-Simultaneity
The sphere gaps do **not all fire at the exact same instant**. There is a small time delay $\Delta t$ (typically 0.05–0.2 μs) between successive gap firings. This causes the output to be a slightly rounded peak rather than a sharp step. A numerical correction factor $k_{gap} = 0.90$ to $0.98$ accounts for this.

### Combined Efficiency

$$ \boxed{\,\eta = \eta_1 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot k_{gap} = \dfrac{C_s}{C_s + C_b} \cdot \dfrac{R_{load}}{R_t + R_{load}} \cdot k_{gap}\,} $$

The **actual peak output voltage** is then:

$$ V_{out,actual} = \eta \cdot n V $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical $\eta$ in well-designed impulse generators:** $\eta = 0.85$ to $0.95$. Students must **always apply $\eta < 1$** in KTU numerical problems unless explicitly told to assume ideal conditions.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Problem Statement (typical KTU numerical):**
A 10-stage impulse generator has each stage capacitance $C = 0.5 \ \mu F$, charged to $V = 100 \ kV$. The load (test object) capacitance is $C_b = 1000 \ pF = 1 \ nF$. The front resistor is $R_f = 75 \ \Omega$ and the tail resistor is $R_t = 10 \ k\Omega$. Calculate:
1. The series discharge capacitance $C_s$.
2. The ideal output voltage $V_{out,ideal}$.
3. The actual output voltage (assume $\eta = 0.90$).
4. The front time $T_1$.
5. The tail time $T_2$.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Series capacitance:**

$$ C_s = \dfrac{C}{n} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{10} = 0.05 \times 10^{-6} \ F = 0.05 \ \mu F = 50 \ nF $$

**Step 2 — Ideal output voltage:**

$$ V_{out,ideal} = n V = 10 \times 100 \ kV = 1000 \ kV = 1 \ MV $$

**Step 3 — Actual output voltage (with $\eta = 0.90$):**

$$ V_{out,actual} = \eta \cdot n V = 0.90 \times 1 \ MV = 0.9 \ MV = 900 \ kV $$

**Step 4 — Front time:**

$$ C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_s \cdot C_b}{C_s + C_b} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-9} \times 1 \times 10^{-9}}{50 \times 10^{-9} + 1 \times 10^{-9}} $$

$$ C_{eq} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-18}}{51 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.9804 \times 10^{-9} \ F \approx 0.98 \ nF $$

$$ T_1 = 3 \, R_f \, C_{eq} = 3 \times 75 \times 0.9804 \times 10^{-9} $$

$$ T_1 = 220.6 \times 10^{-9} \ s \approx 0.22 \ \mu s $$

> [!NOTE]
> This value (0.22 μs) is **less than** the standard 1.2 μs. To obtain 1.2 μs, either $R_f$ must be increased, $C$ increased, or more stages added. The design is iterative in practice.

**Step 5 — Tail time:**

$$ T_2 \approx 0.7 \, R_t \, C_s = 0.7 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.05 \times 10^{-6} $$

$$ T_2 = 0.7 \times 10^4 \times 5 \times 10^{-8} = 0.7 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 3.5 \times 10^{-4} \ s = 350 \ \mu s $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **larger** than the standard 50 μs. To reduce $T_2$, decrease $R_t$ or reduce $C_s$ (use smaller $C$ or more stages).
