## 4.3 Operating Mode State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CHARGING
    CHARGING : All capacitors charged in parallel through Rc to Vdc
    CHARGING : Duration seconds
    CHARGING --> TRIGGER : External trigger pulse applied to G1
    TRIGGER : G1 breaks down lower plate of C1 rises to V
    TRIGGER : Duration less than 0.1 us
    TRIGGER --> CASCADE : Overvoltage 2V on G2
    CASCADE : G2 G3 ... Gn fire in rapid succession
    CASCADE : All caps now in series
    CASCADE : Duration less than 0.1 us
    CASCADE --> DISCHARGE : Full series stack across load
    DISCHARGE : Voltage rises to peak nV over T1
    DISCHARGE : Voltage decays to half in T2
    DISCHARGE : Duration 50 to 100 us
    DISCHARGE --> RECOVERY : All gaps deionize
    RECOVERY : Stage capacitors partially discharged
    RECOVERY : System returns to charging state
    RECOVERY --> CHARGING
    CHARGING --> [*] : Power off
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"With a neat sketch, explain the principle of operation of a multistage impulse generator."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The multistage impulse generator (Marx circuit) consists of $n$ identical stages, each containing a capacitor $C$, charging resistor $R_c$, front resistor $R_f$, tail resistor $R_t$, and a sphere gap.

**Operation:**
1. **Charging:** All $n$ capacitors are charged in **parallel** from a common DC source $V$ through high-value charging resistors $R_c$. (1 mark)
2. **Triggering:** A trigger pulse initiates breakdown of the first sphere gap $G_1$. (0.5 mark)
3. **Cascade action:** With $G_1$ shorted, the voltage across $G_2$ becomes $2V$, causing it to break down. Successive gaps see overvoltages of $3V, 4V, \dots, nV$ and fire in rapid cascade. (1 mark)
4. **Discharge:** All capacitors are now effectively in **series**, producing an output voltage $V_{out} = nV$, which discharges through $R_f$ and $R_t$ to give the standard 1.2/50 μs impulse. (0.5 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention that capacitors charge in **parallel** and discharge in **series** — this dual-mode operation is the **key concept** and carries 1 mark by itself.

---

### Question A2 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Why are charging resistors $R_c$ in a multistage impulse generator chosen to have a value much larger than the wave-shaping resistors $R_f$ and $R_t$?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The charging resistors $R_c$ must be very large compared to the wave-shaping resistors ($R_c \gg R_f, R_t$) for the following reasons:

1. **Isolation of discharge path:** During the cascade discharge (μs timescale), the charging resistors must behave as **open circuits** to prevent them from short-circuiting adjacent stages. If $R_c$ were small, the parallel charging path would provide a low-impedance bypass around the wave-shaping network, dissipating the impulse and reducing output voltage. (1.5 marks)

2. **Independent charging in parallel:** During the charging phase (seconds timescale), the small current through large $R_c$ allows all capacitors to charge to nearly the same voltage $V$ independently. (1 mark)

3. **Protection of DC source:** The large $R_c$ limits the fault current if any capacitor fails (short-circuits), protecting the high-voltage DC rectifier. (0.5 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Merely stating "$R_c$ is large" is insufficient — the student must explain the **functional role** of $R_c$ in isolating the charging and discharging time constants.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**"With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the construction and working of a multistage impulse generator. Derive the expressions for the front time and tail time of the standard impulse waveform generated by it. A 12-stage impulse generator has each stage capacitance of $0.8 \ \mu F$ charged to $50 \ kV$. The load capacitance is $1500 \ pF$. Calculate the values of front and tail resistors required to produce a standard $1.2/50 \ \mu s$ lightning impulse wave. Assume the generator efficiency as 0.9."**

#### Part (a) — Construction, Working & Derivations (7 marks)

**Construction (2 marks):** A multistage impulse generator has $n$ identical stages. Each stage contains:
- A stage capacitor $C$ (energy storage).
- A charging resistor $R_c$ (large, kΩ range).
- A front (wave) resistor $R_f$ (small, Ω range).
- A tail (damping) resistor $R_t$ (medium, kΩ range).
- A sphere gap for triggered switching.
- A common DC high-voltage source for charging.

**Working (2 marks):** Capacitors charge in **parallel** through $R_c$ to voltage $V$. Upon triggering, sphere gaps break down in cascade, and capacitors discharge in **series** through $R_f$ and $R_t$ into the load, producing the impulse waveform of peak voltage $\eta n V$.

**Derivations (3 marks):** (See Section 3.4 and 3.5 for full derivations.)

$$ T_1 = 3 R_f \cdot \dfrac{C_s C_b}{C_s + C_b} \quad ; \quad T_2 = 0.7 \, R_t \cdot C_s $$

where $C_s = C / n$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Number of stages: $n = 12$
- Stage capacitance: $C = 0.8 \ \mu F = 0.8 \times 10^{-6} \ F$
- Charging voltage per stage: $V = 50 \ kV$
- Load capacitance: $C_b = 1500 \ pF = 1.5 \times 10^{-9} \ F$
- Efficiency: $\eta = 0.9$
- Required waveform: $T_1 = 1.2 \ \mu s$, $T_2 = 50 \ \mu s$

**Step 1 — Calculate series capacitance:**

$$ C_s = \dfrac{C}{n} = \dfrac{0.8 \times 10^{-6}}{12} = 0.0667 \times 10^{-6} \ F = 66.67 \ nF $$

**Step 2 — Calculate ideal and actual output voltage:**

$$ V_{out,ideal} = n V = 12 \times 50 = 600 \ kV $$

$$ V_{out,actual} = \eta \cdot nV = 0.9 \times 600 = 540 \ kV $$

**Step 3 — Calculate front resistor $R_f$:**

$$ C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_s \cdot C_b}{C_s + C_b} = \dfrac{66.67 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-9}}{66.67 \times 10^{-9} + 1.5 \times 10^{-9}} $$

$$ C_{eq} = \dfrac{100.0 \times 10^{-18}}{68.17 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.467 \times 10^{-9} \ F = 1.467 \ nF $$

From $T_1 = 3 R_f C_{eq}$:

$$ R_f = \dfrac{T_1}{3 C_{eq}} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}{3 \times 1.467 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}{4.401 \times 10^{-9}} $$

$$ \boxed{R_f \approx 272.7 \ \Omega} $$

**Step 4 — Calculate tail resistor $R_t$:**

From $T_2 \approx 0.7 R_t C_s$:

$$ R_t = \dfrac{T_2}{0.7 \cdot C_s} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{0.7 \times 66.67 \times 10^{-9}} $$

$$ R_t = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{46.67 \times 10^{-9}} = 1071.4 \ \Omega \approx 1.07 \ k\Omega $$

$$ \boxed{R_t \approx 1.07 \ k\Omega} $$

**Verification of design assumption $R_t \gg R_f$:** $1071 \ \Omega$ vs $272.7 \ \Omega$ — ratio $\approx 3.9$. The assumption is **not strongly satisfied** in this design, but acceptable since $T_2$ is dominated by the $R_t C_s$ product once the peak has been reached.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Students commonly forget to apply the **efficiency factor** $\eta$ and report the ideal $600$ kV instead of the actual $540$ kV. Marks are specifically allocated for correct application of $\eta$ in the output voltage calculation. Also, ensure that the **units of capacitance** are correctly converted ($pF \to F$, $\mu F \to F$) — a common source of off-by-1000 errors.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]

**"Describe the working of a multistage impulse generator with the help of a circuit diagram. Explain how the front time and tail time of the standard impulse wave are controlled. An impulse generator of 8 stages, each of $1 \ \mu F$, charged to $60 \ kV$ is available. Find the wave-shaping resistor values to produce a $1.2/50 \ \mu s$ impulse wave on a test object of capacitance $2000 \ pF$. Also calculate the peak output voltage assuming 90 % efficiency."**

#### Part (a) — Working, Front/Tail Control (7 marks)

**Working (3 marks):** As in Question B-A, capacitors charge in **parallel** through $R_c$ from the DC source. Upon triggering the first gap, the **Marx cascade** action occurs: successive gaps see overvoltages of $2V, 3V, \dots, nV$ and break down. All capacitors are now in **series**, and the equivalent series capacitance $C_s = C/n$ discharges through the wave-shaping network to the test object, producing the standard double-exponential impulse.

**Front time $T_1$ control (2 marks):** Controlled by $R_f$ and the equivalent capacitance $(C_s C_b)/(C_s + C_b)$. The relationship is $T_1 = 3 R_f C_{eq}$. Increasing $R_f$ → longer front time (slower rise).

**Tail time $T_2$ control (2 marks):** Controlled by $R_t$ and the series capacitance $C_s$. The relationship is $T_2 \approx 0.7 R_t C_s$ (when $R_t \gg R_f$). Increasing $R_t$ → longer tail time (slower decay).

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $n = 8$, $C = 1 \ \mu F = 10^{-6} \ F$, $V = 60 \ kV$, $C_b = 2000 \ pF = 2 \times 10^{-9} \ F$, $\eta = 0.9$
- Required: $T_1 = 1.2 \ \mu s$, $T_2 = 50 \ \mu s$

**Step 1 — Series capacitance:**

$$ C_s = \dfrac{C}{n} = \dfrac{10^{-6}}{8} = 0.125 \times 10^{-6} \ F = 125 \ nF $$

**Step 2 — Peak output voltage:**

$$ V_{peak,ideal} = nV = 8 \times 60 = 480 \ kV $$

$$ V_{peak,actual} = \eta \cdot nV = 0.9 \times 480 = 432 \ kV $$

$$ \boxed{V_{peak,actual} = 432 \ kV} $$

**Step 3 — Equivalent capacitance:**

$$ C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_s \cdot C_b}{C_s + C_b} = \dfrac{125 \times 10^{-9} \times 2 \times 10^{-9}}{125 \times 10^{-9} + 2 \times 10^{-9}} $$

$$ C_{eq} = \dfrac{250 \times 10^{-18}}{127 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.9685 \times 10^{-9} \ F \approx 1.97 \ nF $$

**Step 4 — Front resistor:**

$$ R_f = \dfrac{T_1}{3 C_{eq}} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}{3 \times 1.9685 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}{5.9055 \times 10^{-9}} $$

$$ \boxed{R_f \approx 203.2 \ \Omega} $$

**Step 5 — Tail resistor:**

$$ R_t = \dfrac{T_2}{0.7 \cdot C_s} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{0.7 \times 0.125 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{0.0875 \times 10^{-6}} $$

$$ \boxed{R_t \approx 571.4 \ \Omega} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** A common mistake is using the **ideal** $nV$ (480 kV) instead of the **actual** $\eta nV$ (432 kV) for the peak voltage. Both could be asked, but always state the **actual peak** as the answer unless asked for the ideal. Also, watch for **unit errors** in the time constants (μs vs s).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

- ⭐ The multistage impulse generator is based on **Marx's principle (1923)**: capacitors charge in **parallel**, discharge in **series**.
- ⭐ **Ideal output voltage:** $V_{out} = nV$ where $n$ is the number of stages and $V$ is the per-stage DC charging voltage.
- ⭐ **Actual output voltage:** $V_{out} = \eta \cdot nV$ with $\eta = 0.80$ to $0.95$ (efficiency accounts for capacitive divider, resistive divider, and gap non-simultaneity).
- ⭐ **Series discharge capacitance:** $C_s = C/n$.
- ⭐ **Front time (rise time):** $T_1 = 3 R_f \cdot \dfrac{C_s C_b}{C_s + C_b}$ — set to $1.2 \ \mu s$ for standard lightning impulse.
- ⭐ **Tail time (time to half-value):** $T_2 \approx 0.7 R_t C_s$ (valid when $R_t \gg R_f$) — set to $50 \ \mu s$ for standard lightning impulse.
- ⭐ **Design constraint:** $R_c \gg R_f, R_t$ (typically kΩ vs Ω) to isolate charging and discharging paths.
- ⭐ **Standard waveforms per IEC 60060-1:** Lightning impulse **1.2/50 μs**; Switching impulse **250/2500 μs**.
- ⭐ **Components of each stage:** Capacitor $C$, charging resistor $R_c$, front resistor $R_f$, tail resistor $R_t$, sphere gap.
- ⭐ **Triggering:** External trigger pulse initiates $G_1$; subsequent gaps fire by **overvoltage cascade** ($2V, 3V, \dots, nV$).
- ⭐ **Stored energy:** $E = \dfrac{1}{2} n C V^2$.
- ⭐ **Numerical problem-solving flow:** (i) compute $C_s = C/n$, (ii) compute $V_{peak} = \eta nV$, (iii) compute $C_{eq} = C_s C_b / (C_s + C_b)$, (iv) compute $R_f$ from $T_1$, (v) compute $R_t$ from $T_2$.
- ⭐ **Always convert units** to SI base: $pF \to 10^{-12} \ F$, $nF \to 10^{-9} \ F$, $\mu F \to 10^{-6} \ F$, $k\Omega \to 10^3 \ \Omega$, $M\Omega \to 10^6 \ \Omega$.
- ⭐ **Exam Tip:** In derivation questions, clearly state the **assumptions** ($R_t \gg R_f$, $C_b$ is purely capacitive) before deriving the front and tail time expressions.
- ⭐ **Real-world application:** This circuit is the workhorse of every HV laboratory — used for impulse testing of transformers, cables, insulators, GIS, and surge arresters as per IS 2071 / IEC 60060-1.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Topic Notes — Multistage Impulse Generator Circuit.** Students should be able to draw the multistage circuit, explain the Marx cascade, derive the front and tail time formulas, and solve numerical design problems for the standard 1.2/50 μs waveform.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Triggering of Impulse Generator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Triggering of Impulse Generator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triggering of an Impulse Generator** is the process of *initiating the discharge of the parallel-capacitor bank* of a Marx-type impulse generator at a precisely controlled instant of time, by forcing the inter-stage spark gaps (or a dedicated three-electrode gap) to break down in a synchronized manner. It converts the *stored DC electrostatic energy* into a *standardized transient impulse waveform* (1.2/50 µs lightning or 250/2500 µs switching impulse) applied to the test object.

In an impulse generator, the capacitors are charged in **parallel** from a high-voltage DC source, but the **discharge must be series-connected** to multiply the voltage. This switching from parallel-charging to series-discharging is achieved by triggering the sphere gaps.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Water Tower & Pistol Trigger:**
> Imagine a row of buckets (capacitors) being filled from a single tap (charging rectifier). Each bucket has a thin sheet of paper (spark gap) blocking it from a vertical pipe (discharge path). When the water is full, the pipes *do not yet* deliver water — they are waiting. Now you prick the paper in bucket #1 with a pin (trigger pulse). The water rushes down, hits the paper in bucket #2, breaks it, and so on — within microseconds, the whole stack discharges into the test bucket (test object). The "pin prick" is the **trigger pulse**, and the "pin" is the **trigger electrode**. Without it, you cannot control *when* the discharge happens; the system would either self-flash at random or never fire.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Lightning Impulse:** 1.2/50 µs (front time / tail time)
- **Standard Switching Impulse:** 250/2500 µs
- **Typical Trigger Pulse Amplitude:** **10 – 30 kV** (much smaller than the charging voltage of 100 kV – 1 MV)
- **Acceptable Jitter for KTU lab standards:** **≤ 0.1 µs**
- **Statistical time lag (tₛ):** typically 0.1 – 5 µs in atmospheric air
- **Formative time lag (t_f):** typically 0.01 – 0.1 µs

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes *controlled triggering* because impulse tests on power apparatus (transformers, insulators, cables) are **destructive** and the **time of breakdown must be captured on an oscilloscope**. Without precise triggering, the CRO sweep will not start at the right moment and the waveform will be missed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Triggering is Required in an Impulse Generator

1. **Synchronization with measuring instruments** (CRO, peak voltmeter, divider).
2. **Prevention of random self-breakdown** of the sphere gaps before the charging voltage is reached.
3. **Ensuring the standard wave-shape** is initiated from a known zero-crossing of the discharge.
4. **Application of the impulse at a precise phase angle** of the AC supply (for impulse testing of transformer windings under service-like conditions).
5. **Repeatability** of the test (IEEE Std 4 / IEC 60060-1 require consistent triggering with low jitter).

### 2.2 Methods of Triggering

| S.No. | Method | Principle | Typical Use |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | **Over-volting of sphere gaps** | Set the gap distance smaller than the self-breakdown value, so breakdown occurs near peak charging voltage. | Low-precision laboratory setups |
| 2 | **Three-Electrode Gap (Trigatron)** | A trigger pin is inserted in one of the main electrodes; a sharp trigger pulse ionizes the gap, forcing breakdown. | **Most widely used in modern impulse generators** |
| 3 | **Triggered Spark Gap with Auxiliary Gap** | A small auxiliary gap breaks down first and its UV radiation / over-voltage triggers the main gap. | Multi-stage Marx circuits |
| 4 | **Thyratron / Solid-state Switch Triggering** | Electronic switching of an auxiliary pulse to the trigger electrode. | High-frequency, low-jitter applications |
| 5 | **Laser Triggering** | Focused UV laser creates initial electrons in the gap via photo-ionization. | Research-grade, ultra-low jitter (< 1 ns) |

### 2.3 Three-Electrode Gap (Trigatron) — Working

- The **trigger pin** is placed in the **center electrode** (the one at lower potential) and protrudes into the gap.
- During charging, the gap holds the full DC voltage across it.
- When a **trigger pulse (10–30 kV)** is applied to the pin, the field distribution between the pin and the opposite electrode is suddenly distorted.
- A **corona discharge** initiates at the pin tip, supplying **seed electrons** and **UV photons**.
- The main gap breaks down within nanoseconds.
- The pulse propagates to the next stage in a Marx generator (domino effect).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | $t_{lag} = t_s + t_f$ | Total time lag = statistical + formative time lag | s |
| 2 | $\bar{t_s} = \dfrac{1}{P \cdot n_0}$ | Mean statistical time lag; P = probability of initiatory electron per unit time, n₀ = initial electron density | s |
| 3 | $t_f = \dfrac{\ln(N_c / N_0)}{\alpha \, v_d}$ | Formative time lag; N_c ≈ 10⁸ electrons (Raether criterion), α = Townsend's first ionization coefficient, v_d = electron drift velocity | s |
| 4 | $I(t) = I_0 \, e^{\alpha \, v_d \, t}$ | Townsend current growth in the gap | A |
| 5 | $V_{trigger} \approx (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \, V_{charging}$ | Empirical rule for trigger pulse amplitude | V |
| 6 | $\sigma_{jitter} = \sqrt{\sigma_s^2 + \sigma_f^2}$ | RMS jitter of the triggered breakdown | s |
| 7 | $v_{drift} = \mu_e \, E$ | Electron drift velocity; μ_e ≈ 0.04 m²/(V·s) in air at STP | m/s |
| 8 | $E_{crit} = \dfrac{V_{charging}}{d_{gap}}$ | Average electric field in the gap (typical: 3–5 kV/mm for sphere gaps) | V/m |
| 9 | $\eta_{trigger} = \dfrac{V_{trigger}}{V_{charging}} \times 100\%$ | Triggering efficiency | % |
| 10 | $\Delta t_{CRO} = R_{CRO} \, C_{CRO}$ | Time-base of CRO must be triggered simultaneously | s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** The trigatron is the heart of *every* commercial impulse generator made by Haefely, Tettex, or Hipotronics. The triggering circuit determines the **reliability** of the entire high-voltage laboratory. In power-system commissioning, the **jitter** dictates whether the test is "valid" per IEC 60060-1.

### 2.5 Design Considerations for the Trigger Circuit

- The **trigger pulse rise time** must be ≤ 0.5 µs to ensure the ionization front develops before the local field collapses.
- The **trigger pulse source** is usually a small Marx generator (5–30 kV) or a pulsed transformer fed by a capacitor discharged through a thyratron.
- The **isolation capacitor** (1–10 pF) couples the trigger pulse to the trigger pin while blocking the main DC voltage.
- The **damping resistor** (50–500 Ω) prevents ringing on the trigger pulse.
- **Opto-isolation** between the low-voltage control panel and the HV trigger circuit is mandatory for personnel safety.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Formative Time Lag (t_f)

We begin with **Townsend's electron-avalanche growth** in a uniform electric field between two parallel electrodes.

**Step 1 — Define the avalanche current growth.**

In a uniform field $E$ with Townsend's first ionization coefficient $\alpha$ (ionizations per unit length per electron), the number of electrons $N$ at a position $x$ grows as:

$$N(x) = N_0 \, e^{\alpha x}$$

Since each electron drifts with velocity $v_d$, the position evolves in time as $x = v_d t$. Therefore the current grows in time as:

$$I(t) = I_0 \, e^{\alpha v_d t}$$

**Step 2 — State the breakdown condition (Raether criterion).**

The avalanche transitions into a self-sustaining streamer when the electron population reaches the critical value:

$$N_c \approx 10^{8} \text{ electrons}$$

Substituting the breakdown condition $N(t_f) = N_c$:

$$N_c = N_0 \, e^{\alpha v_d t_f}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the formative time lag $t_f$.**

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$$\ln N_c = \ln N_0 + \alpha v_d t_f$$

Rearranging for $t_f$:

$$\boxed{\, t_f = \frac{\ln(N_c / N_0)}{\alpha \, v_d} \,}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of current (alternate form).**

Using the equivalent condition $I(t_f) = I_c$ (critical current ≈ 10⁻⁴ A in atmospheric air):

$$t_f = \frac{1}{\alpha v_d} \ln\!\left( \frac{I_c}{I_0} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (typical air gap, E = 4 kV/mm).**

Given:
- $\alpha \approx 8$ ionizations/cm at this field (in air) $\Rightarrow 800$ m⁻¹
- $v_d \approx 1.5 \times 10^5$ m/s
- $N_0 = 1$ initial electron, $N_c = 10^8$

$$t_f = \frac{\ln(10^8 / 1)}{800 \times 1.5 \times 10^5} = \frac{18.42}{1.2 \times 10^8} \approx 1.53 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{s}$$

So $t_f \approx 0.15\ \mu s$ — consistent with the empirical range quoted in KTU textbooks.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Mean Statistical Time Lag ($\bar{t_s}$)

**Step 1 — Define the initiatory process.**

The statistical time lag is governed by the random appearance of the **first initiatory electron** in the gap volume. Let $P$ be the probability (per unit time) that a *seed* electron appears in the critical gap region, and let $n_0$ be the rate of supply of initial electrons (cosmic-ray induced, background radiation, etc.).

**Step 2 — Poisson's probability model.**

The probability that the first electron appears within time $t$ is:

$$p(t) = 1 - e^{-P n_0 t}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mean value.**

The mean statistical time lag is the expected value:

$$\bar{t_s} = \int_0^\infty t \cdot \frac{dp}{dt} \, dt = \int_0^\infty t \, P n_0 \, e^{-P n_0 t} \, dt$$

Using the standard integral $\int_0^\infty t e^{-at} dt = 1/a^2$:

$$\boxed{\, \bar{t_s} = \frac{1}{P \, n_0} \,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The statistical time lag is the **dominant source of jitter** in triggered impulse generators. By increasing $n_0$ (e.g., with a UV lamp irradiating the gap) or by increasing $P$ (via a sharp trigger pulse), $\bar{t_s}$ is drastically reduced.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Triggering Parameters Summary

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | How Triggering Affects It |
|:--|:-:|:-:|:--|
| Statistical time lag | $t_s$ | 0.5 – 5 µs | **Drastically reduced** by UV / trigger pulse |
| Formative time lag | $t_f$ | 0.05 – 0.2 µs | Slightly reduced by over-voltage |
| Total time lag | $t_{lag}$ | 0.6 – 5 µs | Sum of $t_s$ and $t_f$ |
| RMS Jitter | $\sigma_{jitter}$ | 0.05 – 0.3 µs | Must be < 0.1 µs for IEC compliance |
| Trigger pulse amplitude | $V_{tr}$ | 10 – 30 kV | 5–10 % of charging voltage |
| Trigger pulse rise time | $t_{rise}$ | 50 – 200 ns | Faster rise → lower jitter |
| Gap spacing | $d$ | 10 – 50 mm | Trade-off between hold-off and breakdown |
| Charging voltage | $V_c$ | 100 kV – 2 MV | Set by test specification |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **Problem:** A 10-stage Marx impulse generator is charged to 100 kV per stage (i.e., $V_c = 1$ MV total). The first-stage sphere gap has a spacing such that the electric field is $E = 5$ kV/mm. The three-electrode trigger circuit applies a 15 kV pulse with a rise time of 100 ns. Estimate the formative time lag and the RMS jitter, given $\alpha = 1000$ m⁻¹ and $v_d = 2 \times 10^5$ m/s in air.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Formative time lag:

$$t_f = \frac{\ln(10^8)}{1000 \times 2 \times 10^5} = \frac{18.42}{2 \times 10^8} = 9.21 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s} \approx 0.092\ \mu s$$

Step 2 — Statistical time lag (with UV irradiation, $n_0 = 10^6$ s⁻¹, $P = 0.5$):

$$\bar{t_s} = \frac{1}{0.5 \times 10^6} = 2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 2\ \mu s$$

Step 3 — RMS Jitter (assuming $\sigma_s \approx \bar{t_s}/2 = 1\ \mu s$ and $\sigma_f \approx t_f/2 = 0.046\ \mu s$):

$$\sigma_{jitter} = \sqrt{(1\ \mu s)^2 + (0.046\ \mu s)^2} \approx 1.00\ \mu s$$

Step 4 — Verification with IEC 60060-1: This jitter is **borderline acceptable**. To reduce it, increase $n_0$ by stronger UV irradiation or use a steeper trigger pulse.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Three-Electrode Gap (Trigatron) — Detailed Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CHG["CHARGING STAGE"]
        REC["HV Rectifier 0 to 1 MV"]
        RC["Charging Resistor Rc"]
        CAPBANK["Parallel Capacitor Bank C1 to Cn"]
    end
    subgraph TRIG["TRIGGERING STAGE"]
        TPG["Trigger Pulse Generator 15 kV"]
        ISO["Isolation Capacitor 1 to 10 pF"]
        DMP["Damping Resistor 50 to 500 ohm"]
        TRGPIN["Trigger Pin inside Center Electrode"]
    end
    subgraph DISCH["DISCHARGE / OUTPUT STAGE"]
        SG1["Sphere Gap 1"]
        SG2["Sphere Gap 2"]
        SGN["Sphere Gap N"]
        LOAD["Test Object Load"]
        CRO["Oscilloscope with Voltage Divider"]
    end
    REC --> RC
    RC --> CAPBANK
    CAPBANK --> SG1
    CAPBANK --> SG2
    CAPBANK --> SGN
    TPG --> ISO
    ISO --> DMP
    DMP --> TRGPIN
    TRGPIN -. breaks down .-> SG1
    SG1 -. propagates .-> SG2
    SG2 -. propagates .-> SGN
    SGN --> LOAD
    LOAD --> CRO
```

### 4.2 Trigatron Physical Layout (Cross-Sectional Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GAP["THREE ELECTRODE GAP TRIGATRON"]
        HV["HV Electrode Anode"]
        TRG["Trigger Pin Hollow Center"]
        GND["Ground Electrode Cathode"]
    end
    HV --- TRG
    TRG --- GND
    TPGSRC["Trigger Pulse Source 15 kV"] --- TRG
    HV --- LOAD["Test Object Cable"]
    LOAD --- GND
    UV["Optional UV Lamp for Pre-ionization"] -. illuminates .-> GAP
```

### 4.3 Triggering Sequence (Timing Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["t = 0  Rectifier switched ON"] --> B["Capacitors charge in parallel Vc reaches set value"]
    B --> C{"Charging complete  Yes or No"}
    C -- "No" --> B
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Hold for stabilization 0.5 to 5 s"]
    D --> E["Trigger pulse fired 15 kV, 100 ns rise time"]
    E --> F["Gap A1 breaks down  t equals tlag"]
    F --> G["Voltage doubles across next stage"]
    G --> H["Gap A2 breaks down"]
    H --> I["Voltage quadruples"]
    I --> J["Subsequent gaps cascade"]
    J --> K["Full impulse applied to test object 1.2 by 50 us"]
    K --> L["CRO captures waveform through divider"]
```

### 4.4 Control Logic of a Modern Trigger Generator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CTRL["Microcontroller or PLC"] --> DLY["Programmable Time Delay 0 to 999 ms"]
    DLY --> TCHARGE["Initiate Charging Relay"]
    TCHARGE --> VSENSE["Voltage Sensor on Capacitor Bank"]
    VSENSE --> COMP{"Vc equals Vset"}
    COMP -- "Below Vset" --> TCHARGE
    COMP -- "Reached Vset" --> TRGEN["Enable Trigger Pulse Generator"]
    TRGEN --> TGAP["Fire Three Electrode Gap"]
    TGAP --> ACK["Acknowledge to Control Panel"]
    ACK --> LOG["Log timestamp, jitter, Vpeak in database"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
*Define "triggering" in the context of an impulse generator. Why is a three-electrode gap preferred over a simple sphere gap for triggering?*

**Model Answer:**

Triggering is the controlled initiation of the discharge of the capacitor bank of a Marx impulse generator by forcing the spark gaps to break down at a precisely defined instant, using a low-energy trigger pulse.

A **three-electrode gap (trigatron)** is preferred over a simple sphere gap because:
1. It allows **precise, externally-controlled timing** of the breakdown (a sphere gap is passive and self-breaks only at its own characteristic voltage).
2. It drastically **reduces the statistical time lag** by providing initial seed electrons at the trigger pin tip.
3. It enables **synchronization** with measuring instruments (CRO, peak voltmeter) and with the AC supply phase.
4. The **jitter** is reduced from several microseconds (sphere gap alone) to < 0.1 µs, meeting IEC 60060-1 standards.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`**
*Distinguish between "statistical time lag" and "formative time lag" in spark breakdown. Which one is more affected by the trigger pulse?*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Statistical Time Lag ($t_s$) | Formative Time Lag ($t_f$) |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Definition | Time required for the appearance of an *initiatory (seed) electron* in the gap volume. | Time required for the electron avalanche to grow to the critical size and form a conducting channel. |
| Magnitude | 0.5 – 5 µs (large variability) | 0.05 – 0.2 µs (more deterministic) |
| Governing equation | $\bar{t_s} = 1/(P \cdot n_0)$ | $t_f = \ln(N_c/N_0)/(\alpha v_d)$ |
| Affected by | Background radiation, UV illumination, cathode surface, trigger pulse | Over-voltage, gas pressure, gap geometry |

The **statistical time lag is far more affected by the trigger pulse**, because the trigger pulse injects seed electrons (raising $n_0$) and creates localized ionization (raising $P$), thereby shrinking $\bar{t_s}$ by orders of magnitude.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / ESE Module 1]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **three-electrode triggered spark gap (trigatron)** used in a Marx impulse generator. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **formative time lag** in a spark gap using Townsend's ionization mechanism, and compute its value for a gap with $\alpha = 800$ m⁻¹, $v_d = 1.5 \times 10^5$ m/s, and a critical electron count of $10^8$ starting from a single electron. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of the Trigatron — 7 Marks**

*Construction (3 marks):*
- The trigatron consists of **two main electrodes** (anode and cathode) of spherical or hemispherical shape, separated by a gap of 10–50 mm.
- A **trigger pin** (typically tungsten) is inserted coaxially through a hole in the **center electrode (cathode)** and protrudes a few millimetres into the gap.
- The trigger pin is electrically isolated from the cathode by a **ceramic bushing** and connected to the **trigger pulse generator** through an **isolation capacitor** (1–10 pF) and a **damping resistor** (50–500 Ω).
- Optionally, a **UV lamp** is mounted near the gap to provide continuous background ionization.

*Working (3 marks):*
- During the **charging phase**, the full DC voltage $V_c$ is applied across the gap; the trigger pin floats and the gap does **not** break down (gap distance is set slightly *larger* than the self-breakdown distance).
- When the **trigger pulse** (10–30 kV, < 200 ns rise time) is applied to the pin, a high field develops between the pin tip and the opposite (HV) electrode.
- A **corona discharge** initiates at the pin tip, providing a copious supply of seed electrons and UV photons.
- The seed electrons trigger an **electron avalanche** across the main gap within nanoseconds, the gap breaks down, and the capacitor discharges into the next stage.
- The **domino effect** continues through all stages of the Marx generator, producing the standard impulse waveform at the output.

*Diagram (1 mark):* See the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes.

---

**(b) Derivation of Formative Time Lag — 7 Marks**

*Derivation (5 marks):*

Starting from Townsend's first ionization coefficient $\alpha$, the number of electrons after drifting a distance $x$ is:

$$N(x) = N_0 \, e^{\alpha x}$$

Replacing $x = v_d t$ (electron drift velocity $\times$ time):

$$N(t) = N_0 \, e^{\alpha v_d t}$$

The breakdown occurs when the electron count reaches the **Raether critical value** $N_c \approx 10^8$:

$$N_c = N_0 \, e^{\alpha v_d t_f}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln N_c = \ln N_0 + \alpha v_d t_f$$

Solving for $t_f$:

$$\boxed{\, t_f = \frac{\ln(N_c / N_0)}{\alpha \, v_d} \,}$$

*Numerical Computation (2 marks):*

Given $\alpha = 800$ m⁻¹, $v_d = 1.5 \times 10^5$ m/s, $N_c = 10^8$, $N_0 = 1$:

$$t_f = \frac{\ln(10^8 / 1)}{800 \times 1.5 \times 10^5}$$

$$t_f = \frac{8 \times \ln 10}{1.2 \times 10^8} = \frac{8 \times 2.3026}{1.2 \times 10^8} = \frac{18.42}{1.2 \times 10^8}$$

$$\boxed{\, t_f \approx 1.535 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{s} \approx 0.154\ \mu s \,}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*
**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / ESE Module 1]`**

**(a)** Explain the **need for precise triggering** in an impulse generator. List any **four triggering methods** and discuss the merits of three-electrode triggering over the others. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A Marx generator is charged to $V_c = 200$ kV per stage across a 12 mm sphere gap. The trigger circuit delivers a 20 kV pulse. Calculate the **triggering efficiency** and the **average field** in the gap. If the formative time lag is 0.12 µs, determine the **first Townsend ionization coefficient** $\alpha$ (take $v_d = 1.8 \times 10^5$ m/s). (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Need for Precise Triggering & Triggering Methods — 7 Marks**

*Need (3 marks):*
1. **Synchronization with CRO / digital recorder** — the time base must start exactly when the impulse is applied; otherwise the front of the wave will be missed.
2. **Validity of the test waveform** — IEC 60060-1 demands that the standard 1.2/50 µs shape be initiated from a known instant; random self-breakdown would not yield a valid wave.
3. **Phase control of AC supply** — testing of transformer windings under switching-impulse conditions requires the impulse to be applied at a specific phase angle of the 50 Hz mains.
4. **Repeatability & personnel safety** — the operator must know exactly when the high voltage will appear at the test object; triggering gives a controlled "fire-on-command" sequence.

*Four Triggering Methods (2 marks):*
1. Over-volted sphere gap (self-breakdown)
2. Three-electrode triggered gap (trigatron)
3. UV-irradiated triggered gap
4. Thyratron-triggered pulse transformer

*Merits of Three-Electrode Triggering (2 marks):*
- Lowest jitter (< 0.1 µs achievable)
- Wide range of charging voltages can be triggered
- Compact, robust, no need for moving parts
- Easily synchronized to external control signals

---

**(b) Numerical Problem — 7 Marks**

*Given:* $V_c = 200$ kV per stage, $d = 12$ mm, $V_{trigger} = 20$ kV, $t_f = 0.12\ \mu s$, $v_d = 1.8 \times 10^5$ m/s.

*Step 1 — Triggering Efficiency (2 marks):*

$$\eta_{trigger} = \frac{V_{trigger}}{V_{charging}} \times 100\% = \frac{20}{200} \times 100\% = \boxed{10\%}$$

*Step 2 — Average Electric Field in the Gap (2 marks):*

$$E_{avg} = \frac{V_c}{d} = \frac{200 \times 10^3}{12 \times 10^{-3}} = \boxed{1.667 \times 10^7\ \text{V/m} \approx 16.67\ \text{kV/mm}}$$

*Step 3 — Determination of $\alpha$ (3 marks):*

Starting from the formative time lag formula, with $N_0 = 1$ and $N_c = 10^8$:

$$t_f = \frac{\ln(N_c / N_0)}{\alpha \, v_d}$$

Rearranging:

$$\alpha = \frac{\ln(N_c / N_0)}{t_f \cdot v_d}$$

Substituting values:

$$\alpha = \frac{18.42}{0.12 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.8 \times 10^5} = \frac{18.42}{21.6}$$

$$\boxed{\, \alpha \approx 0.853\ \text{m}^{-1} \,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Such a low value of $\alpha$ implies a low electric field — students must verify the field is below the Paschen minimum regime. If the field were too low, the discharge would not occur, signalling a numerical inconsistency to be flagged in the answer.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Triggering Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "statistical" and "formative" time lags.** Statistical = waiting for a seed electron; Formative = avalanche growth. Examiners frequently give 0 marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting the natural logarithm in $t_f$.** Many students write $t_f = N_c / (\alpha v_d)$ — this is dimensionally and mathematically wrong. Always show $\ln(N_c/N_0)$.
> 3. **Skipping the "critical" number of electrons.** The Raether criterion $N_c \approx 10^8$ must be stated; examiners look for it.
> 4. **Triggering efficiency:** Sometimes students compute $V_{trigger}/V_{output}$ instead of $V_{trigger}/V_{charging}$. KTU expects the per-stage charging voltage, not the multi-stage output.
> 5. **Jitter calculation:** Mixing standard deviation with peak-to-peak values; examiners expect RMS ($\sigma$) values.
> 6. **Unit errors:** Always use SI units (m, s, V) consistently; mixing mm and m inside a single calculation loses at least 1 mark.
> 7. **No diagram:** A 7-mark question on trigatron without a neat diagram loses a minimum of 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Triggering of Impulse Generator**

- **Triggering** = controlled initiation of capacitor-bank discharge in a Marx generator.
- **Why needed:** synchronization, IEC compliance, repeatability, personnel safety.
- **Most common method:** **Three-electrode gap (Trigatron)** with a 10–30 kV, < 200 ns rise-time trigger pulse.
- **Total time lag:** $t_{lag} = t_s + t_f$
- **Statistical lag:** $t_s = 1/(P \cdot n_0)$ — reduced by UV or trigger pulse.
- **Formative lag:** $t_f = \ln(N_c/N_0)/(\alpha v_d)$ — Raether criterion $N_c \approx 10^8$.
- **Jitter (RMS):** $\sigma_{jitter} = \sqrt{\sigma_s^2 + \sigma_f^2}$; must be < 0.1 µs.
- **Triggering efficiency:** $\eta = (V_{trigger}/V_{charging}) \times 100\%$, typically 5–10 %.
- **Trigger pin** is in the **cathode** (grounded) electrode, isolated by ceramic.
- **Isolation capacitor (1–10 pF)** blocks DC; couples the trigger pulse.
- **Damping resistor (50–500 Ω)** prevents trigger pulse ringing.
- **Standard impulse waves:** 1.2/50 µs (lightning), 250/2500 µs (switching).
- **IEC 60060-1** is the governing standard; KTU exam questions test its key clauses.
- **Equation to memorize verbatim:** $t_f = \ln(10^8)/(\alpha v_d) = 18.42/(\alpha v_d)$ in air.
- **Drift velocity in air:** $v_d \approx (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0) \times 10^5$ m/s at standard conditions.
- **Domain: Module 1** of PEEET633 (High Voltage Engineering), 2024 KTU Scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impulse Current Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Impulse Current Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Impulse Current** is a unidirectional, heavily damped, transient current pulse of short duration (typically a few microseconds to milliseconds) used to test the ability of high-voltage equipment, surge arresters, and insulation systems to withstand high-current surges such as those produced by **lightning discharges** or **switching transients**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Impulse Current Waveforms (per IEC 60060-1 / IEEE Std 4):**
> - **8/20 μs waveform** – Standard lightning impulse current. Front time = **8 μs**, Time to half-value (tail time) = **20 μs**.
> - **4/10 μs waveform** – High-impulse current waveform for steep testing. Front time = **4 μs**, Time to half-value = **10 μs**.
> - **Rectangular (long-duration) waveform** – 500 A to 1000 A peak, 1–4 ms duration, used for testing earth electrodes and surge arresters.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a giant water tank (the **capacitor bank**) placed at an elevation, connected to a narrow pipe (the **wave-shaping inductor/resistor**) leading to a target (the **test object**). When a valve (**sphere gap / trigatron**) is suddenly opened, the stored water rushes out as a single, intense, but brief surge — first rising sharply (the **wavefront**) and then gradually dying down (the **wavetail**). This is exactly how an **impulse current generator** works: it stores energy in a **capacitor**, then releases it through a controlled **switch** and **wave-shaping network** to produce a single, high-magnitude current pulse that simulates real-world surges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike impulse voltage (where the test object behaves as a capacitor), impulse current testing requires the test object to handle **huge instantaneous energy** (I²t effects) — so the generator must be an efficient, low-impedance energy source.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in **bold**)

- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
- **Dielectric strength of standard sphere gap air:** $\approx$ **30 kV/cm (peak)**
- **Standard test current amplitudes:** **5 kA to 200 kA** for lightning simulation; up to **1000 kA** for nuclear EMP simulation.
- **Damped oscillatory frequency** range in impulse current testing: **10 kHz to 1 MHz**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The generation of impulse current is performed in **four sequential stages**:

1. **Energy Storage Stage (Slow):**
   - A **high-voltage DC source** (typically a Marx-type or Cockcroft-Walton multiplier at 50–200 kV) charges a **bank of capacitors** $C$ through a high-value charging resistor $R_c$ and a rectifier. The capacitor stores energy:
   
   $$W = \frac{1}{2}CV_0^2$$

2. **Switching Stage (Triggered):**
   - A **trigatron** (three-electrode triggered spark gap) or **sphere gap** is used as the closing switch. When triggered, it closes in nanoseconds, connecting the charged capacitor to the wave-shaping network.
   - **Dumping gap (Crowbar gap)** is sometimes used in parallel to extinguish the arc after the first current peak (used in high-voltage impulse current generators).

3. **Wave-Shaping Stage (R–L–C Discharge):**
   - The capacitor discharges through a combination of:
     - **Series Inductance $L_s$** — controls the **front time** ($T_1$).
     - **Series Resistance $R_s$** and **damping resistance $R_d$** — controls the **damping** and the **tail time** ($T_2$).
   - The resulting current is a **damped unidirectional pulse**.

4. **Test Object Stage:**
   - The current is forced through the **test object** (surge arrester, fuse, conductor, etc.). Voltage across the test object is measured using a **voltage divider**, and current using a **Rogowski coil** or **shunt resistor**.

### 2.2 Why Overdamping is Required

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In impulse current generation, we deliberately operate in the **overdamped** regime so that the current is **unidirectional** (no zero-crossing). If the circuit oscillated (underdamped), the second half-cycle would be a **reverse** current, which is not representative of natural lightning (which is unidirectional).

The condition for overdamping is:

$$\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 > \frac{1}{LC}$$

Equivalently: $R > 2\sqrt{L/C}$ (a small resistance already gives overdamping because $L$ is small and $C$ is large).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|--------------|
| $V_0$ | Initial charging voltage | Set by DC source | kV |
| $C$ | Capacitance of bank | Sum of parallel capacitors | $\mu$F |
| $L$ | Total loop inductance | $L_s + L_{\text{loop}}$ | $\mu$H |
| $R$ | Total loop resistance | $R_s + R_d$ | $\Omega$ |
| $\alpha$ | Damping coefficient | $R/(2L)$ | s$^{-1}$ |
| $\omega_0$ | Natural (undamped) frequency | $1/\sqrt{LC}$ | rad/s |
| $\omega_d$ | Damped natural frequency | $\sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$ | rad/s |
| $I_p$ | Peak impulse current | Function of $V_0, L, C, R$ | kA |
| $T_1$ | Front time (8/20) | $1.67 \times (t_{90\%} - t_{30\%})$ | $\mu$s |
| $T_2$ | Tail time (8/20) | Time from virtual origin to 50% peak | $\mu$s |
| $W$ | Energy stored | $\frac{1}{2}CV_0^2$ | kJ |
| $\eta$ | Transfer efficiency | $W_{\text{test object}} / W$ | % |
| $Z_0$ | Surge impedance | $\sqrt{L/C}$ | $\Omega$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important relation:** For an **underdamped** LC discharge (R=0): $I_p = V_0 \sqrt{C/L} = V_0 / Z_0$. With damping, the peak is reduced.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Surge Arrester Testing (Lightning Current Withstand):**
  Class 1 and Class 2 SPDs (Surge Protective Devices) must withstand 8/20 μs current pulses of 10 kA, 20 kA, or even 100 kA. This is verified in impulse current labs.

- **Bonding & Earthing System Testing:**
  The 4/10 μs waveform simulates direct lightning strikes to test the **i²t let-through** capability of bonding straps and earth electrodes.

- **High-Energy Fuse & Conductor Testing:**
  To verify that fuses clear a short circuit before the conductor melts.

- **Aerospace & EMP Studies:**
  Nuclear electromagnetic pulse (NEMP) simulators use capacitive impulse current generators at the **mega-ampere** scale.

- **Railway & Traction System Surge Testing:**
  Overhead catenary systems are tested for lightning-induced impulse currents.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Impulse Current Waveform (R–L–C Discharge, Overdamped Case)

Consider a charged capacitor $C$ at voltage $V_0$, discharging through a series $R$–$L$–$C$ circuit into a test object. Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the loop:

$$V_C = V_R + V_L$$

Since the current $i$ is related to the capacitor voltage by $i = -C \dfrac{dV_C}{dt}$, the differential equation becomes:

$$\frac{1}{C}\int i \, dt + R i + L \frac{di}{dt} = 0$$

Differentiating once with respect to time to eliminate the integral:

$$L \frac{d^2i}{dt^2} + R \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{i}{C} = 0$$

This is a **second-order linear homogeneous ODE** with characteristic equation:

$$L s^2 + R s + \frac{1}{C} = 0$$

Solving for roots:

$$s_{1,2} = \frac{-R \pm \sqrt{R^2 - 4L/C}}{2L} = -\frac{R}{2L} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 - \frac{1}{LC}}$$

Let us define the **damping coefficient** $\alpha = \dfrac{R}{2L}$ and the **natural frequency** $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$. Then:

$$s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **overdamped** condition: $\alpha > \omega_0 \Rightarrow R > 2\sqrt{L/C}$. Both roots $s_1$ and $s_2$ are real, negative, and distinct.

The general solution is:

$$i(t) = A_1 e^{s_1 t} + A_2 e^{s_2 t}$$

Applying the **initial conditions**:
- At $t = 0^+$: $i(0) = 0$ (inductor opposes sudden change in current) ⇒ $A_1 + A_2 = 0 \Rightarrow A_2 = -A_1$
- At $t = 0^+$: $\dfrac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0} = \dfrac{V_0}{L}$ (since $V_L = L \dfrac{di}{dt} = V_0$ initially, as the capacitor holds the full voltage and the resistor carries zero current at $t=0$)

Differentiating the general solution:

$$\frac{di}{dt} = A_1 s_1 e^{s_1 t} + A_2 s_2 e^{s_2 t}$$

At $t = 0$:

$$\frac{V_0}{L} = A_1 s_1 + A_2 s_2 = A_1 (s_1 - s_2)$$

Therefore:

$$A_1 = \frac{V_0}{L(s_1 - s_2)}, \quad A_2 = -\frac{V_0}{L(s_1 - s_2)}$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{\,i(t) = \frac{V_0}{L(s_1 - s_2)} \left( e^{s_1 t} - e^{s_2 t} \right)\,}$$

With $s_1 - s_2 = 2\sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$:

$$i(t) = \frac{V_0}{2L\sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}} \left( e^{s_1 t} - e^{s_2 t} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical impulse current waveform** for the overdamped case. The first exponential term (with the larger root $s_1$, less negative) builds the current to its peak, and the second exponential term (with the more negative $s_2$) causes the decay.

### 3.2 Special Case: Critically Damped Condition

If $\alpha = \omega_0$, the two roots coincide at $s_1 = s_2 = -\alpha$. The general solution changes to:

$$i(t) = (A_1 + A_2 t) e^{-\alpha t}$$

Using the same initial conditions:

$$\boxed{\,i(t) = \frac{V_0}{L} \, t \, e^{-\alpha t}\,}$$

The peak occurs at $t_p = 1/\alpha = 2L/R$, with peak value:

$$I_p = \frac{2V_0}{e R} = \frac{0.736 \, V_0}{R}$$

### 3.3 Expression for Peak Current $I_p$ and Time to Peak $t_p$ (Overdamped)

The peak occurs when $\dfrac{di}{dt} = 0$:

$$\frac{di}{dt} = 0 \Rightarrow A_1 s_1 e^{s_1 t_p} + A_2 s_2 e^{s_2 t_p} = 0$$

$$e^{(s_1 - s_2)t_p} = -\frac{A_2 s_2}{A_1 s_1} = -\frac{s_2}{s_1}$$

$$t_p = \frac{1}{s_1 - s_2} \ln\left(-\frac{s_2}{s_1}\right) = \frac{\ln(-s_2/s_1)}{2\sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}}$$

Since both $s_1$ and $s_2$ are negative, the ratio $-s_2/s_1$ is positive. The peak current is then:

$$I_p = \frac{V_0}{L(s_1 - s_2)} \left( e^{s_1 t_p} - e^{s_2 t_p} \right) = \frac{V_0}{L(s_1 - s_2)} \left[ \left(-\frac{s_2}{s_1}\right)^{s_1/(s_1-s_2)} - \left(-\frac{s_2}{s_1}\right)^{s_2/(s_1-s_2)} \right]$$

### 3.4 Energy Transfer Efficiency

The total energy delivered to the test object equals the energy dissipated in the **test object resistance $R_t$**. If the test object is purely resistive:

$$\eta = \frac{R_t}{R_s + R_d + R_t}$$

For maximum efficiency, the test object resistance should dominate the circuit resistance — but this is not always practical for high-current testing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **surge arrester testing**, the test object is **non-linear** (zinc-oxide), and the voltage across it is nearly constant during conduction. Hence the efficiency is computed numerically from the integral $\eta = \int V i \, dt / (\frac{1}{2}CV_0^2)$.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A $C = 10 \mu F$ capacitor is charged to $V_0 = 50 kV$ and discharged through $L = 50 \mu H$ and $R = 0.5 \Omega$. Find the peak current and the nature of the discharge.

**Step 1 — Compute $\alpha$ and $\omega_0$:**

$$\alpha = \frac{R}{2L} = \frac{0.5}{2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}} = 5000 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5 \times 10^{-10}}} \approx 44721 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compare:**

$$\alpha = 5000 \;\;<\;\; \omega_0 = 44721 \implies \text{UNDERDAMPED (oscillatory)}$$

This is **not** an impulse current (it would have reversals). For impulse current, we need $\alpha > \omega_0$, i.e., $R > 2\sqrt{L/C} = 2\sqrt{50/10} = 2\sqrt{5} \approx 4.47\;\Omega$.

**Step 3 — Re-compute with $R = 5\;\Omega$:**

$$\alpha = \frac{5}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 50000 \text{ s}^{-1} > \omega_0 \quad \checkmark \text{ Overdamped}$$

$$s_1 = -50000 + \sqrt{50000^2 - 44721^2} = -50000 + 21804 \approx -28196 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$s_2 = -50000 - 21804 = -71804 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$I_p \approx \frac{50000}{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 43608} \times \text{(factor)} \approx 22.9 \text{ kA (factor of 1)}$$

> **Result:** $I_p \approx \mathbf{22.9\ kA}$ at $t_p \approx \mathbf{25\ \mu s}$ — a valid 8/20 μs-class impulse current.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Impulse Current Generator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AC["AC Mains Supply 230V 50Hz"] --> Rect["High Voltage Rectifier Unit"]
    Rect --> Rchrg["Charging Resistor Rchrg"]
    Rchrg --> Cbank["Capacitor Bank C 1 to 100 uF at 50 to 200 kV"]
    Cbank --> Trig["Trigatron Switch T closing in nanoseconds"]
    Trig --> Ls["Wave Shaping Inductance Ls 10 to 100 uH"]
    Ls --> Rs["Damping Resistor Rs 0.5 to 10 ohm"]
    Rs --> TObj["Test Object Surge Arrester or Fuse"]
    TObj --> GND1["Ground Return Path"]
    GND1 --> Cbank
    CB["Crowbar Gap CB parallel to capacitor for overvoltage protection"] -.-> Cbank

```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Impulse Current Waveform Generation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Stage 1 DC Charging 0.1 to 10 seconds"] --> S2["Stage 2 Trigger Trigatron closes in ns"]
    S2 --> S3["Stage 3 RLC Discharge produces 8 20 us current pulse"]
    S3 --> S4["Stage 4 Measurement Rogowski coil and shunt"]
    S4 --> S5["Stage 5 Oscilloscope Recording Ip T1 T2"]
    style S1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
```
