```mermaid
    style S5 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Graph: RLC Discharge Current Waveform Phases

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["t equals 0 Capacitor charged to V0"] --> B["t equals 0 to T1 Current rises to peak Ip FRONT TIME"]
    B --> C["t equals T1 to T2 Current decays to 0.5 Ip TAIL TIME"]
    C --> D["t greater than T2 Current nearly zero damped"]
    A -->|Wavefront controlled by L| B
    B -->|Tail controlled by R and C| C
    style A fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Component-Level Layout (Crowbar Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HV["HV DC Source"] -->|charges| CAP["Capacitor C"]
    CAP -->|discharges via| MAIN["Main Gap G1 Trigatron"]
    CAP -->|parallel to| CROW["Crowbar Gap G2"]
    MAIN --> L1["Inductance L1 Front Time Control"]
    L1 --> R1["Resistance R1 Damping"]
    R1 --> DUT["Device Under Test DUT"]
    DUT --> GND["Ground"]
    CROW -->|fires on reverse voltage| GND
    style HV fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style CAP fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style MAIN fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style CROW fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style DUT fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Crowbar Gap G2** is an essential safety element. When the test object fails (short circuit), the capacitor would otherwise ring back and apply reverse voltage — the crowbar fires and clamps the voltage to ground, protecting the test setup.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define **impulse current**. State the two standard impulse current waveforms used in high-voltage testing and mention the applications of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Impulse Current** is a unidirectional, heavily damped transient current pulse of short duration (microseconds to milliseconds) used to simulate lightning and switching surges in high-voltage testing.

The two standard waveforms are:

- **8/20 μs waveform** — Standard lightning impulse current; front time = 8 μs, time to half-value = 20 μs. Used for testing **surge arresters, SPDs, and lightning protection components**.

- **4/10 μs waveform** — High-impulse current; front time = 4 μs, tail time = 10 μs. Used for testing **earthing systems, bonding straps, and high-energy fuses**.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark each waveform with application = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the role of (i) **Trigatron** and (ii) **Crowbar gap** in an impulse current generator.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(i) **Trigatron (Main Gap):** A three-electrode triggered spark gap used as the **closing switch** of the generator. When a trigger pulse is applied, it ionizes the main gap, causing the charged capacitor to discharge in **nanoseconds** through the wave-shaping network. *(1.5 marks)*

(ii) **Crowbar Gap:** A **safety spark gap** connected in parallel with the capacitor. It **fires** when the voltage across the capacitor reverses (or exceeds a threshold), providing a low-impedance short circuit. This **protects the test object and the source** from reverse voltage and from the energy of subsequent oscillations. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### **Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks each, Module-Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Detailed Circuit Analysis Path

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**
> (a) **Derive the expression for the impulse current waveform** produced by discharging a charged capacitor $C$ through a series R–L circuit. Discuss the conditions for overdamped, critically damped, and underdamped responses. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) An impulse current generator uses a **$C = 8\ \mu F$** capacitor charged to **$V_0 = 100\ kV$**, discharging through a total inductance of **$L = 40\ \mu H$** and a total resistance of **$R = 2\ \Omega$**. Determine (i) the nature of the discharge, (ii) the roots $s_1$ and $s_2$, (iii) the peak current $I_p$ and time to peak $t_p$, and (iv) the energy stored and the energy delivered to a test object of resistance $R_t = 1\ \Omega$ connected in series. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (Question A):**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Apply KVL: $V_C = V_R + V_L$ (1 mark)

Differentiate and use $i = -C \frac{dV_C}{dt}$:

$$L \frac{d^2i}{dt^2} + R \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{i}{C} = 0 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Characteristic equation:

$$s^2 + \frac{R}{L}s + \frac{1}{LC} = 0 \quad \text{(0.5 marks)}$$

Roots:

$$s_{1,2} = -\frac{R}{2L} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 - \frac{1}{LC}} = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Conditions:
- **Overdamped ($\alpha > \omega_0$):** Both roots real and negative → unidirectional current
- **Critically damped ($\alpha = \omega_0$):** Repeated real root → fastest non-oscillatory response
- **Underdamped ($\alpha < \omega_0$):** Complex roots → oscillatory current (not used for impulse)

(1.5 marks for conditions and waveform shapes)

Solution with $i(0) = 0$ and $\frac{di}{dt}(0) = V_0/L$:

$$\boxed{\,i(t) = \frac{V_0}{L(s_1 - s_2)}\left(e^{s_1 t} - e^{s_2 t}\right)\,} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $C = 8\ \mu F$, $V_0 = 100\ kV$, $L = 40\ \mu H$, $R = 2\ \Omega$, $R_t = 1\ \Omega$.

**Effective circuit resistance** (test object in series): $R_{\text{eff}} = R + R_t = 3\ \Omega$ (0.5 marks)

**Step (i): Nature of discharge**

$$\alpha = \frac{R_{\text{eff}}}{2L} = \frac{3}{80 \times 10^{-6}} = 37500 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{40 \times 10^{-6} \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3.2 \times 10^{-10}}} \approx 55900 \text{ rad/s}$$

Since $\alpha < \omega_0$: **Underdamped (oscillatory)** — not suitable for impulse current. (1 mark)

**Step (ii): Roots** (oscillatory case)

$$s_{1,2} = -37500 \pm j\sqrt{55900^2 - 37500^2} = -37500 \pm j\sqrt{3.126 \times 10^9 - 1.406 \times 10^9}$$

$$s_{1,2} = -37500 \pm j\,41620 \text{ s}^{-1} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step (iii): Peak current and time**

For underdamped case:

$$i(t) = \frac{V_0}{\omega_d L} e^{-\alpha t} \sin(\omega_d t)$$

where $\omega_d = 41620$ rad/s.

$$I_p = \frac{V_0}{\omega_d L} e^{-\alpha t_p} \text{ at } t_p = \frac{1}{\omega_d}\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega_d}{\alpha}\right)$$

$$t_p = \frac{1}{41620} \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{41620}{37500}\right) = \frac{1}{41620} \times 0.837 \approx 20.1\ \mu s$$

$$I_p = \frac{100000}{41620 \times 40 \times 10^{-6}} e^{-37500 \times 20.1 \times 10^{-6}} \times \sin(0.837)$$

$$I_p = \frac{100000}{1.665} \times 0.471 \times 0.743 \approx 21000 \text{ A} = 21.0\ \text{kA} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step (iv): Energy**

$$W_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 10^{-6} \times (100000)^2 = 40000\ \text{J} = 40\ \text{kJ}$$

$$\eta = \frac{R_t}{R + R_t} = \frac{1}{3} = 33.3\%$$

$$W_{\text{test object}} = 0.333 \times 40000 = 13333\ \text{J} \approx 13.3\ \text{kJ} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Components & Applications Path

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
> (a) With a neat **block diagram**, explain the operation of an **impulse current generator** used for testing surge arresters. Describe the function of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) Explain the **8/20 μs and 4/10 μs impulse current waveforms** in detail. Discuss why a **crowbar gap** is necessary in high-voltage impulse current generators and describe how it operates. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (Question B):**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Operation (7 marks):**

The impulse current generator consists of the following blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **AC Mains & Step-up Transformer (0.5 mark):** Provides high-voltage AC from the 230 V supply, stepping up to 50–200 kV range.

2. **High-Voltage Rectifier (0.5 mark):** A half-wave or full-wave rectifier converts AC to DC for charging the capacitor bank.

3. **Charging Resistor $R_c$ (0.5 mark):** Limits the charging current and protects the rectifier. Has a high value (kΩ range) to allow slow, safe charging.

4. **Capacitor Bank (1 mark):** Stores the energy $\frac{1}{2}CV_0^2$. Typically consists of multiple capacitors in parallel (for high C) charged in parallel.

5. **Trigatron / Main Switch (1 mark):** A three-electrode triggered gap that closes the circuit in nanoseconds, initiating the discharge. Triggered by an external pulse transformer.

6. **Wave-Shaping Network — L and R (1 mark):**
   - Inductance L controls the **front time** (rise time) of the impulse current.
   - Resistance R controls the **tail time** and provides **damping** to prevent oscillations.

7. **Test Object (0.5 mark):** Surge arrester under test. Voltage and current are measured using standard transducers.

8. **Measuring System (0.5 mark):** Rogowski coil for current, voltage divider for voltage, and a digital oscilloscope for recording waveforms.

9. **Crowbar Gap (0.5 mark):** A safety gap that prevents reverse voltage and protects the test object.

10. **Earthing & Safety (0.5 mark):** Proper grounding, interlocks, and shielding to ensure operator safety.

**Operation:** The capacitor is slowly charged to $V_0$ via the rectifier and $R_c$. When triggered, the trigatron closes, and the capacitor discharges through L and R into the test object, producing a unidirectional impulse current pulse.

---

**Part (b) — Waveforms and Crowbar (7 marks):**

**8/20 μs Waveform (2 marks):**
- Defined in **IEC 60060-1** and **IEEE Std 4**.
- **Front time $T_1$ = 8 μs** — measured as 1.67 × (time between 30% and 90% of peak).
- **Tail time $T_2$ = 20 μs** — time from virtual origin to 50% of peak on the tail.
- **Application:** Standard testing of **Class I and Class II SPDs**, surge arresters, and lightning protection components. Peak currents: 1, 2.5, 5, 10, 20 kA.

**4/10 μs Waveform (1.5 marks):**
- **Front time $T_1$ = 4 μs**, **Tail time $T_2$ = 10 μs**.
- Higher rate of rise of current (steeper).
- **Application:** Testing of **earthing electrodes, bonding straps, and high-energy fuses** subjected to direct lightning strikes.

**Why Crowbar Gap is Needed (2 marks):**
- After the capacitor discharges, its voltage reverses polarity. The test object (e.g., a failed surge arrester) may now see a **reverse voltage** of comparable magnitude, causing further damage.
- The **reverse current** may also be undesirable (lightning is unidirectional).
- The crowbar fires when the test object voltage exceeds a threshold, providing a **low-impedance short to ground**, thus **dumping residual energy** and preventing reverse conduction.
- It also protects the **DC charging supply** from being back-fed by the test object during failure.

**Operation of Crowbar (1.5 marks):**
- It is a spark gap with a **pre-set breakdown voltage** slightly higher than the peak test voltage.
- During the discharge, the crowbar is **non-conducting**.
- When the test object fails or voltage reverses, the crowbar **breaks down within microseconds**, short-circuiting the capacitor and dissipating energy harmlessly as heat in the crowbar arc.
- Modern generators use **triggered crowbars** (similar to the main trigatron) for precise timing.

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes & Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Impulse Current Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing impulse current and impulse voltage generators:** Many students write the same circuit for both. Remember: impulse current uses **small L (μH) and large C (μF–mF)** for low impedance; impulse voltage uses **large C (nF) and large R (kΩ)** for high voltage and slow discharge.
> 2. **Forgetting to test the damping condition:** Always check whether $R^2$ is greater or less than $4L/C$ before writing the solution form. **Skipping this step costs 1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong units in $\alpha$ and $\omega_0$:** Both are in rad/s or s⁻¹. Mixing L in H and mH gives off-by-1000 errors.
> 4. **Using peak voltage in the formula instead of initial capacitor voltage:** $V_0$ is the **initial charging voltage**, not the voltage during discharge.
> 5. **Oversimplifying the crowbar:** Many students describe it as "a safety device" without explaining its **triggering mechanism** or **timing**.
> 6. **Forgetting front/tail time definitions:** T1 and T2 are **virtual** parameters (defined via 30%/90%/50% points), not just rise time and decay time.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- **Impulse current** = unidirectional, damped transient current pulse simulating lightning/switching surges.
- **Standard waveforms:** **8/20 μs** (surge arresters) and **4/10 μs** (earthing systems, fuses).
- **Key components:** HV DC source → Charging resistor → Capacitor bank → **Trigatron (main switch)** → Wave-shaping **L and R** → Test object → Crowbar gap (safety) → Measuring system (Rogowski coil, voltage divider, oscilloscope).
- **Energy stored:** $W = \frac{1}{2}CV_0^2$ (joules).
- **Damping condition for impulse current:** $\alpha > \omega_0$, i.e., $\dfrac{R}{2L} > \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ ⇒ **overdamped**.
- **General current equation (overdamped):** $i(t) = \dfrac{V_0}{L(s_1 - s_2)}\left(e^{s_1 t} - e^{s_2 t}\right)$, with $s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$.
- **Critically damped peak current:** $I_p = \dfrac{2V_0}{eR} \approx \dfrac{0.736\,V_0}{R}$.
- **Underdamped current (NOT used for impulse):** $i(t) = \dfrac{V_0}{\omega_d L}e^{-\alpha t}\sin(\omega_d t)$.
- **Front time $T_1$** is controlled mainly by **L**; **tail time $T_2$** is controlled mainly by **R and C**.
- **Efficiency $\eta$** = energy delivered to test object / energy stored = $R_t / (R + R_t)$ for resistive test objects.
- **Trigatron** = triggered three-electrode spark gap; closes in **nanoseconds** on trigger pulse.
- **Crowbar gap** = safety gap in parallel; fires on reverse voltage to protect equipment and prevent reverse current.
- **Measuring instruments:** **Rogowski coil** (current), **voltage divider** (voltage), **digital oscilloscope** (recording).
- **Typical peak currents tested:** 5 kA – 200 kA (lightning); up to 1000 kA (EMP studies).
- **IEC standards:** IEC 60060-1, IEC 61643-1 (for SPDs), IEEE Std 4 (for HV testing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line memory aid:** **"Big C, small L, overdamped R"** — that is the golden recipe for impulse current generation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Voltage Measurement Techniques - Measuring Spark Gaps - Sphere-to-Sphere Spark Gap - Rod-to-Rod Spark Gap - Electrostatic Voltmeter - Field Sensors - Electrically Short Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** High Voltage Measurement Techniques - Measuring Spark Gaps - Sphere-to-Sphere Spark Gap - Rod-to-Rod Spark Gap - Electrostatic Voltmeter - Field Sensors - Electrically Short Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 High Voltage Measurement — The Fundamental Challenge

In high voltage engineering, accurate measurement of voltages ranging from a few kilovolts to several megavolts is not a trivial extension of conventional metrology. The very quantities we wish to measure (impulse peaks, switching surges, fast transients) tend to **destroy** the measuring instrument by causing flashover. Therefore, the discipline of high voltage (HV) measurement revolves around indirect techniques that translate an electrical quantity into a **mechanical, optical, or field-based** observable that can be sensed without drawing significant current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **High Voltage Measurement** is the science of determining the magnitude, waveform, and polarity of voltages and currents in systems operating at or above **1 kV**, using techniques that impose negligible loading on the source while preserving the integrity of the original signal — particularly its peak value, RMS value, and rise-time.

### 1.2 Taxonomy of HV Measurement Techniques

| Class | Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Spark Gaps** | Breakdown of air between known electrodes | Peak voltage (calibration) |
| **Electrostatic Voltmeters** | Force between charged electrodes | RMS of AC and DC |
| **Field Sensors** | Sensing electric-field strength | Non-contact measurement |
| **Electrically Short Sensors** | Capturing field at a near-point | Fast transients / EMC |
| **Divider Circuits** | Resistive / Capacitive scaling | Impulse & AC waveforms |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Measuring the Strength of a Storm":**
> Imagine you want to measure the strength of a thunderstorm (high voltage) without standing in the open. A **spark gap** is like leaving two metal rods a known distance apart on a hilltop and noting when lightning jumps — the gap distance tells you the voltage. An **electrostatic voltmeter** is like a sensitive electroscope that wiggles in proportion to the storm's electric field. A **field sensor** is like a tiny weather vane that senses the electric "wind" passing by, and an **electrically short sensor** is like a small probe that measures the field right at the tip of a lightning rod before the bolt arrives.

### 1.4 Why We Need Indirect Methods

- **Loading Effect:** Any voltmeter draws some current. At HV, even microamperes × megavolts = kilowatts of power loss, distorting the source.
- **Breakdown Risk:** The instrument itself may flash over if placed directly across HV terminals.
- **Waveform Fidelity:** A 1.2/50 µs impulse has a rise-time of 1.2 µs. Any long lead introduces inductance that distorts it.
- **Safety & Isolation:** Personnel must be physically and electrically isolated from the live circuit.

### 1.5 Spark Gaps — The Foundational Calibrator

A **spark gap** is, conceptually, the simplest possible HV "voltmeter": two electrodes separated by a precise gas gap, across which the test voltage is applied. The voltage is gradually raised until the gap **breaks down** (a spark passes). The breakdown voltage is determined by the **gap spacing, electrode geometry, and atmospheric conditions**, and is read from standardized tables (IS 1876 / IEC 60052).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:**
> A spark gap measures the **PEAK value** of the applied voltage (positive or negative, whichever breaks down first). It is used primarily as a **calibration standard**, not a continuous-reading instrument, because each measurement destroys the gap's insulating state and the electrodes erode.

The two most important configurations under the KTU syllabus are:

1. **Sphere-to-Sphere (S-S) Spark Gap** — highly accurate, used for moderate HV.
2. **Rod-to-Rod (R-R) Spark Gap** — simple, robust, used for very high voltages and impulse work.

We now examine each in detail.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sphere-to-Sphere (S-S) Spark Gap

#### 2.1.1 Construction

Two **identical** metallic spheres (usually brass or aluminium) of diameter $D$ are mounted coaxially, separated by a precise gap $d$. A high-voltage lead is connected to the **upper (driving) sphere** through a high-value protective resistance $R_p$ (typically several hundred kΩ to a few MΩ) to limit the breakdown current and prevent pitting. The **lower sphere** is earthed through a shunt or directly to ground.

```
   HV lead
     |
    [Rp]   (series protection resistor)
     |
   .─────.        ← Upper sphere (driven, diameter D)
  (       )  d
   `─────'
        (air gap d)
   .─────.        ← Lower sphere (earthed, diameter D)
  (       )
   `─────'
     |
    ─┴─          ← Earth
```

#### 2.1.2 Operating Principle

When the applied voltage is increased, the **non-uniform field** at the sphere surfaces eventually reaches the **breakdown strength of air** ($\approx 30 \text{ kV/cm}$ at STP, but actually a function of $d$ and $D$). A spark jumps, the protective resistor $R_p$ limits the current, and the breakdown voltage $V_b$ is read on the primary side of the test transformer (or computed from the test-transformer ratio and a peak voltmeter).

#### 2.1.3 The Role of Sphere Diameter $D$

For a given gap $d$, a **larger sphere** produces a **more uniform field** near the gap axis, raising the breakdown voltage. A smaller sphere yields a divergent, non-uniform field with a lower breakdown voltage. Hence, the breakdown voltage $V_b$ is a function of the ratio $d/D$ — not $d$ alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:**
> Sphere gaps are accurate provided $0.05 \le d/D \le 0.75$. Below $d/D = 0.05$, the field is so uniform that $V_b$ becomes insensitive to small errors in $d$. Above $d/D = 0.75$, the spheres behave more like **rod electrodes** and accuracy drops sharply.

#### 2.1.4 Atmospheric Corrections

The standard tables assume:
- Pressure $P_0 = 760$ mmHg (101.325 kPa)
- Temperature $t_0 = 20^\circ$C (293 K)
- Absolute humidity $h_0 = 8.5$ g/m³ (standard humid air)

The **air density factor** is:

$$\delta = \frac{P}{P_0} \cdot \frac{273 + t_0}{273 + t} = \frac{0.386 \cdot P}{273 + t}$$

where $P$ is in mmHg and $t$ in °C.

The breakdown voltage at actual conditions is:

$$V_b = k \cdot \delta \cdot d \quad \text{(first-order approximation)}$$

or, more accurately, read from **standard tables** at the equivalent spacing $d' = \delta \cdot d$:

$$V_b(\text{actual}) = V_b(\text{standard table at } d' = \delta \cdot d)$$

Humidity correction factor $k_h$ (typically 0.95 to 1.05) further refines the value:

$$V_b(\text{true}) = k_h \cdot V_b(\delta \text{ corrected})$$

#### 2.1.5 Polarity Effect

Sphere gaps are **slightly polarity-sensitive**: the positive sphere (driven electrode at +) breaks down at a marginally **lower** voltage than the negative sphere, because positive corona stabilizes and negative streamers initiate more easily. The difference is usually < 3 % for $d/D < 0.5$.

---

### 2.2 Rod-to-Rod (R-R) Spark Gap

#### 2.2.1 Construction

Two **rod electrodes** with **square-cut ends** (or hemispherical tips), each typically 12–25 mm² cross-section, mounted vertically with a horizontal gap. The HV rod has a long insulating shank; the earthed rod is similarly supported. No protective resistance is normally used on the HV side because the gap is intended to be a **discharging** element.

```
     ↑   HV rod
     |
    [   ]───  (rod profile)
          d
    [   ]───  (earthed rod)
     |
     ↓   ground
```

#### 2.2.2 Operating Principle

The rod ends produce a **highly non-uniform (divergent) field**. Breakdown occurs at a **lower voltage per cm** than for a sphere gap of the same spacing. The gap is **strongly polarity-sensitive** and **strongly humidity-dependent** — making it inherently less accurate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:**
> Rod-to-rod gaps are **not** used as primary HV standards. They are employed as:
> - **Protective devices** (lightning arresters, surge diverters)
> - **Rough indicators** of voltage level
> - **Calibration standards** for impulse voltages where the waveform is standardized and the absolute accuracy required is only ~ ± 8 %

#### 2.2.3 Empirical Breakdown Voltage for R-R Gap

The breakdown voltage in air for a rod-rod gap (square-cut rods, vertical axis) at standard conditions is approximately:

$$V_b \approx 5 + 3.8 \cdot d \quad \text{(kV, peak, for d in cm, 2 < d < 50)}$$

A more general empirical expression (Allibone-Schonland):

$$V_b = A \cdot d + B \cdot \sqrt{d} \quad \text{(kV peak)}$$

where $A$ and $B$ depend on polarity and humidity.

---

### 2.3 Electrostatic Voltmeter

#### 2.3.1 Working Principle

The electrostatic voltmeter is a **field-force instrument**: a movable electrode is deflected by the electrostatic attraction toward a fixed electrode. The energy stored in the capacitance $C$ between the electrodes is:

$$W = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$$

If the movable electrode is displaced by $\delta x$ against a restoring spring, the **mechanical force** is:

$$F = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

The **deflection** is therefore proportional to $V^2$ — a **quadratic (square-law) response**. This has two important consequences:
1. The instrument is **polarity-blind** — it reads the same for +V and −V.
2. It indicates the **true RMS value** of any AC waveform (because $\langle V^2 \rangle = V_{rms}^2$).

#### 2.3.2 Construction

Common types:
- **Attracting-Disc Type (Kelvin's absolute electrometer)**: A disc is attracted toward a fixed plate; deflection measured by a micrometer screw restoring the disc to a fiducial position. This is an **absolute instrument** (no calibration required).
- **Quadrant Electrometer**: A "needle" (suspended vane) sits inside four quadrant electrodes; torque is proportional to $V^2$.
- **Field-Deflecting Multicellular Type**: Used in commercial HV electrostatic voltmeters (e.g., ranges 0–100 kV, 0–500 kV).

#### 2.3.3 Advantages & Limitations

| Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|
| Draws essentially **no current** at steady state | $V^2$ scale — compressed at low end |
| Measures **true RMS** for any waveform | Sensitive to **stray fields** |
| Works on **AC and DC** equally | **Fragile** suspension, low torque |
| **Absolute** (Kelvin) version needs no calibration | Limited frequency response (mechanical inertia) |
| Direct HV measurement without divider | Lower accuracy than dividers (~1–3 %) |

#### 2.3.4 Frequency Response

The torque is developed instantaneously, but the **moving system** has mechanical inertia $J$ and damping. The natural frequency $\omega_n$ is typically 5–20 Hz for laboratory instruments, and 1–2 Hz for high-sensitivity absolute electrometers. Hence the instrument is **not suitable for impulses** but is excellent for **power-frequency AC and steady DC**.

---

### 2.4 Field Sensors

A **field sensor** measures the local **electric-field strength $E$** (V/m) at a point, without contact with the conductor. Once $E$ is known, the conductor voltage is inferred from the geometry.

For a **parallel-plate** approximation (or coaxial cylinder), the voltage between electrodes is:

$$V = \int_a^b E(x) \, dx$$

The sensor is a small, calibrated probe placed in the field region. Important requirements:
- **Small** compared to the field-distorting structures (so the probe does not perturb $E$).
- **Isotropic** response (same sensitivity from all directions) — usually achieved with a spherical or cubic geometry.
- **Flat** frequency response up to the highest harmonic of interest.

A typical implementation is a **spherical field sensor** — two hemispheres separated by a small gap, with a measuring capacitance between them; the induced charge is:

$$Q = \varepsilon_0 \cdot E \cdot A_{eff}$$

The current into a measuring instrument is:

$$i(t) = \varepsilon_0 A_{eff} \frac{dE}{dt} = C_{sensor} \frac{dV_{sensor}}{dt}$$

---

### 2.5 Electrically Short Sensors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:**
> An **electrically short sensor** has a physical length much smaller than the shortest wavelength of interest, so that the propagating electromagnetic wave appears as a **quasi-static field** at the sensor terminals. This makes the sensor behave as a simple lumped element (essentially a capacitance to ground), avoiding transmission-line distortion of fast transients.

**Electrical length** is defined in terms of wavelength $\lambda$:

$$\ell \le \frac{\lambda}{50} \quad \text{(electrically "very short")}$$

For a 1.2/50 µs impulse (rise-time 1.2 µs), the equivalent high-frequency spectral content is up to $\sim 1/(2\pi t_r) \approx 130$ kHz, so $\lambda \approx 2.3$ km — virtually any practical sensor is "electrically short."

The key design rule:

$$\tau = R_{load} \cdot C_{sensor} \ll t_{rise} \text{ of the wave to be measured}$$

where $\tau$ is the **RC time constant** of the sensor and its load. If this is violated, the sensor **integrates** (low-pass filters) the signal and misrepresents the wavefront.

**Applications:**
- **D-dot sensor** (measures $\partial D/\partial t$, i.e., the time-derivative of displacement current).
- **B-dot sensor** (measures $\partial B/\partial t$).
- **Capacitive voltage probe** — physically short, single-turn capacitance to the HV conductor.
- **Electro-optical (Pockels/Kerr) sensors** — use an optical crystal whose refractive index changes with $E$; the optical path is "electrically short" by construction.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Air density factor | $\delta = \dfrac{P}{760} \cdot \dfrac{293}{273+t} = \dfrac{0.386 P}{273+t}$ | dimensionless | $P$ in mmHg, $t$ in °C |
| 2 | Equivalent gap | $d' = \delta \cdot d$ | cm | Look up $V_b$ in standard table |
| 3 | S-S gap range | $0.05 \le d/D \le 0.75$ | dimensionless | Validity of sphere-gap tables |
| 4 | R-R gap (empirical) | $V_b \approx 5 + 3.8 d$ | kV (peak) | $d$ in cm, $2 < d < 50$ |
| 5 | Electrostatic force | $F = \tfrac{1}{2} V^2 \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | N | Quadratic in $V$ |
| 6 | Electrostatic torque | $T = \tfrac{1}{2} V^2 \dfrac{dC}{d\theta}$ | N·m | Quadrant electrometer |
| 7 | Stored energy | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J | Energy between electrodes |
| 8 | RMS relationship | $V_{rms}^2 = \langle V^2(t) \rangle$ | V² | Square-law ⇒ RMS |
| 9 | Field-sensor charge | $Q = \varepsilon_0 A_{eff} E$ | C | $A_{eff}$ = effective area |
| 10 | D-dot current | $i(t) = \varepsilon_0 A_{eff} \dfrac{dE}{dt}$ | A | Time-derivative sensor |
| 11 | Electrically-short rule | $\ell \le \lambda/50$ | m | $\lambda$ = shortest wavelength |
| 12 | RC condition for fidelity | $R_{load} C_{sensor} \ll t_{rise}$ | s | Avoid integration of wavefront |
| 13 | Breakdown of air (NTP) | $E_b \approx 30 \text{ kV/cm}$ | kV/cm | Approximate; depends on $d$ |
| 14 | Sphere gap field | quasi-uniform for $d/D < 0.5$ | — | Drops to rod-like for $d/D \to 1$ |
| 15 | Polarity effect (S-S) | $V_b(+) \lesssim V_b(-)$ | V | Difference usually < 3 % |

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities in the table are in **SI** unless explicitly noted. The vertical-bar notation has been deliberately avoided inside table cells to keep the markdown table syntax intact — use $\vert$ in any hand-written working.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force and Deflection in an Electrostatic Voltmeter

We derive the **mechanical force** on the moving electrode of an attracting-disc type electrostatic voltmeter.

**Step 1 — Set up the energy.**

The system consists of a parallel-plate capacitor of area $A$ and instantaneous plate separation $x$ (variable). The capacitance is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{x}$$

**Step 2 — Write the energy stored in the field.**

The voltage $V$ is applied (the source is at fixed potential; this is the **constant-V** case). The energy in the capacitor is:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2x}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the principle of virtual work.**

For constant $V$, the **force** in the direction of increasing $x$ is given by the negative gradient of co-energy (which equals energy in this case, since the system is linear):

$$F_x = +\frac{\partial W}{\partial x} \bigg|_{V=\text{const}}$$

Wait — careful with signs. At constant $V$, the source supplies energy as the plates move. The force on the movable plate, directed so as to *increase* $x$, is:

$$F_x = \frac{\partial W}{\partial x}\bigg|_{V} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2x}\right)\bigg|_{V} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2x^2}$$

The negative sign indicates the force is **attractive** (in the −$x$ direction, i.e., pulls the moving plate toward the fixed plate).

So the **magnitude** of the attractive force is:

$$F = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 x^2}$$

**Step 4 — Equate to mechanical restoring force.**

If the moving plate is on a spring of stiffness $k$ with deflection $\Delta x$ from its rest position:

$$F_{spring} = k \cdot \Delta x$$

In equilibrium:

$$k \cdot \Delta x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 x^2}$$

Therefore the **deflection** is:

$$\Delta x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{2 k x^2} \cdot V^2$$

This is the **square-law response**: $\Delta x \propto V^2$. For AC, the time-averaged deflection involves $\langle V^2 \rangle = V_{rms}^2$, so the instrument reads RMS.

**Step 5 — Sensitivity.**

Sensitivity $S$ is defined as deflection per unit $V^2$:

$$S = \frac{\partial (\Delta x)}{\partial (V^2)} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{2 k x^2}$$

To increase sensitivity, increase $A$, decrease $k$, or decrease $x$ (within breakdown limits).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Air-Density Factor and Equivalent Gap

We show how atmospheric conditions are folded into a single scaling factor.

**Step 1 — Starting formula for the S-S gap.**

The breakdown voltage in standard air is, to a first approximation:

$$V_{b,0} = f(d, D)$$

— given by a standard table or a fitted expression.

**Step 2 — Incorporate pressure $P$ and temperature $T$.**

Paschen's law tells us that, for a uniform field, the breakdown voltage depends on the **gas number density** $N$ (molecules per m³). Since $N \propto P/T$:

$$V_b = V_{b,0} \cdot \frac{N}{N_0} = V_{b,0} \cdot \frac{P \, T_0}{P_0 \, T}$$

With $T_0 = 293$ K, $P_0 = 760$ mmHg, and $T = 273 + t$:

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = \frac{P}{760} \cdot \frac{293}{273+t} \equiv \delta$$

So:

$$V_b = \delta \cdot V_{b,0}$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of equivalent gap.**

The standard tables list $V_{b,0}$ as a function of $d$. If the **breakdown voltage** scales linearly with $\delta$ (good first approximation for air, valid for moderate departures from STP), then we may use the table at the **equivalent gap** $d' = \delta \cdot d$:

$$V_b(\text{at } P, t) = V_{b,0}(d' = \delta d)$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**

Given: $d = 5$ cm, $D = 25$ cm sphere, $P = 740$ mmHg, $t = 30^\circ$C, humidity 12 g/m³.
Compute $\delta$:

$$\delta = \frac{0.386 \cdot 740}{273 + 30} = \frac{285.64}{303} = 0.9427$$

So $d' = 0.9427 \cdot 5 = 4.7135$ cm.

From the standard table for $D = 25$ cm, $d' = 4.71$ cm: read $V_{b,0} \approx 137.6$ kV (peak).

Then with a humidity correction $k_h = 1.02$ (dry air is slightly weaker):

$$V_b = 1.02 \cdot 137.6 = 140.4 \text{ kV (peak)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Polarity Effect on a Sphere Gap

A rigorous treatment uses **streamer inception criteria**. Here we present the engineering estimate.

**Step 1 — Critical field for positive corona inception.**

For a sphere of radius $r = D/2$, the surface field at the highest point is:

$$E_{surface} = \frac{V}{r} \cdot g\left(\frac{d}{r}\right)$$

where $g(\cdot)$ is a geometry factor ($\approx 1$ for $d/r \to 0$, decreasing as $d/r$ increases).

**Step 2 — Inception field asymmetry.**

Empirically, the **positive** corona inception field is ~ 5–10 % lower than the **negative** inception field for clean spheres. Therefore:

$$V_b(+) \approx 0.93 \text{ to } 0.97 \cdot V_b(-)$$

For $d/D < 0.5$, the difference is usually under 3 % and is often neglected in calibration work.

**Step 3 — Consequence for measurement.**

If a sphere gap is used to measure an unknown AC voltage, it will break down on the **half-cycle of whichever polarity has the lower inception voltage** (positive, in most cases). Hence the gap gives the **positive peak** of the AC waveform, not the symmetric peak. For sinusoidal AC, $V_{peak} = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms}$, and a small polarity correction (e.g., +1 %) is applied.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Frequency Response of an Electrically Short Capacitive Sensor

A capacitive pickup of capacitance $C_s$ feeds a load $R_L$ (input of an oscilloscope or buffer). We derive the transfer function.

**Step 1 — Equivalent circuit.**

The HV conductor has potential $V(t)$ w.r.t. ground; the sensor "floats" at potential $V_s(t)$ and is coupled to the conductor through $C_s$. To ground, the sensor has capacitance $C_g$ (intrinsic to the sensor) plus the load resistance $R_L$ in parallel with $C_g$ (the oscilloscope input). KCL at the sensor node:

$$C_s \frac{d(V - V_s)}{dt} = C_g \frac{dV_s}{dt} + \frac{V_s}{R_L}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange.**

$$C_s \frac{dV}{dt} - C_s \frac{dV_s}{dt} = C_g \frac{dV_s}{dt} + \frac{V_s}{R_L}$$

$$C_s \frac{dV}{dt} = (C_s + C_g) \frac{dV_s}{dt} + \frac{V_s}{R_L}$$

Let $C_T = C_s + C_g$. Then:

$$R_L C_T \frac{dV_s}{dt} + V_s = R_L C_s \frac{dV}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency-domain analysis.**

Taking the Fourier transform (or assuming $e^{j\omega t}$ time-dependence):

$$(j \omega R_L C_T + 1) V_s(\omega) = j \omega R_L C_s V(\omega)$$

The transfer function is:

$$H(\omega) = \frac{V_s(\omega)}{V(\omega)} = \frac{j \omega R_L C_s}{1 + j \omega R_L C_T}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the two regimes.**

- **Low frequencies** ($\omega \ll 1/(R_L C_T)$): $|H| \approx \omega R_L C_s \to 0$ — the sensor **differentiates** and is useless at DC.
- **High frequencies** ($\omega \gg 1/(R_L C_T)$): $|H| \approx C_s / C_T$ — the sensor **divides** capacitively and gives a faithful copy of $V(t)$.

**Step 5 — Condition for "electrically short" behaviour.**

The transition frequency is $f_c = 1/(2 \pi R_L C_T)$. To measure a wavefront with rise-time $t_r$, we need $f_c \gg 1/t_r$, i.e.,

$$R_L C_T \ll t_r$$

This is the analytical form of the "electrically short" rule given in Section 2.5.

**Step 6 — Self-integrating (D-dot) interpretation.**

If $R_L C_T$ is **deliberately made small** (e.g., $R_L = 50 \ \Omega$ matching, $C_T = 1$ pF → $\tau = 50$ ps), the sensor is a **D-dot probe** (current output $\propto dV/dt$); an external integrator is required to recover $V(t)$.

If $R_L C_T$ is **large** (e.g., $R_L = 1$ MΩ, $C_T = 10$ pF → $\tau = 10$ µs), the sensor is a **direct voltage probe** suitable for slow transients.

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | S-S Spark Gap | R-R Spark Gap | Electrostatic Voltmeter | Field Sensor | Elec. Short Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quantity measured | Peak voltage | Peak voltage | RMS voltage | E-field | E-field / dE/dt |
| Accuracy | ± 3 % (calibrated) | ± 8 % | ± 1–3 % | ± 5 % | ± 5–10 % |
| Frequency response | DC to power freq. | DC to impulse | DC to ~100 Hz | DC to GHz | kHz to GHz |
| Waveforms | AC, DC, impulse | AC, DC, impulse | AC, DC | All | Impulses, transients |
| Loading on source | Switches OFF after breakdown | Switches OFF | Negligible (pA) | Negligible | Negligible |
| Polarity sensitive? | Slightly | Strongly | No (reads V²) | No (V²) | No |
| Destructive? | Yes (one-shot) | Yes | No | No | No |
| Cost / complexity | Low | Very low | Medium | Medium-high | High |
| KTU applicability | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an HV Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HV Source] --> B[Test Object or DUT]
    A --> C[Measurement Branch]
    C --> D{Measurement Type}
    D -- Spark Gap --> E1[Electrodes + Std Gap d]
    D -- Electrostatic --> E2[Moving vane + Fixed plate]
    D -- Field --> E3[Spherical or D-Dot probe]
    D -- Short Sensor --> E4[Capacitive pickup + Load R]
    E1 --> F1[Spark / breakdown]
    E2 --> F2[Mechanical deflection]
    E3 --> F3[Induced charge or current]
    E4 --> F4[Voltage at oscilloscope]
    F1 --> G1[Read Vb from Std Table]
    F2 --> G2[Deflection to Vrms scale]
    F3 --> G3[Integrate to get E or V]
    F4 --> G4[Acquire waveform on scope]
    G1 --> H[Result: Peak Voltage]
    G2 --> I[Result: RMS Voltage]
    G3 --> J[Result: E-field or V]
    G4 --> K[Result: Waveform]
```

### 4.2 Sphere-to-Sphere Spark Gap — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HV[HV Transformer Secondary] --> R[Protective Resistor Rp]
    R --> SU[Upper Sphere D]
    SU -- gap d --> SL[Lower Sphere D]
    SL --> G[Earth]
    SP[Spacer of Length d] -. positions .- SU
    SP -. positions .- SL
    ATM[Atmospheric Sensor P t h] --> CF[Apply Corrections delta kh]
    TAB[IS 1876 or IEC 60052 Table for D dprime] --> CF
    CF --> VB[Corrected Vb]
```

### 4.3 Electrostatic Voltmeter — Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HV Terminal] --> B[Fixed Electrode]
    A --> C[High impedance lead]
    C --> D[Movable Electrode on Suspension]
    B -- electrostatic attraction --> D
    D --> E[Restoring Spring k]
    D --> F[Mirror or Pointer]
    F --> G[Optical Lever or Direct Scale]
    G --> H[Deflection x proportional to V squared]
    H --> I[Calibrated Scale in Vrms]
    DAMP[Damping Dashpot] -. damps .- D
```

### 4.4 Electrically Short Sensor — Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[HV Conductor Vt] --> CS[Coupling Capacitance Cs]
    CS --> N[Sensor Node Vs]
    CG[Ground Capacitance Cg] --> N
    RL[Load Resistor RL] --> N
    N --> OUT[Output to Oscilloscope or Integrator]
    subgraph High Frequency Regime
      HF1[omega RL CT >> 1] --> HF2[Capacitive divider Vs V approx Cs CT]
    end
    subgraph Low Frequency Regime
      LF1[omega RL CT << 1] --> LF2[Differentiation Vs V approx j omega RL Cs]
    end
```

### 4.5 Field-Sensor Measurement Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HV[HV Conductor] -- electric field E --> FS[Field Sensor Probe]
    FS --> TR[Transmission line electrically short]
    TR --> IN[Instrumentation]
    IN --> PROC[Integration or Differentiation]
    PROC --> RESULT[V t or E t]
    G[Earth Plane] -- return path --> FS
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> The Mermaid graphs above emphasize **functional flow** rather than literal mechanical layouts. The student is expected to translate them into pictorial schematics during exam answers, using labels, gap distance $d$, sphere diameter $D$, and the protective resistor $R_p$ for the spark-gap case, and the spring + pointer arrangement for the electrostatic case.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
**"Why is a sphere-to-sphere spark gap preferred over a rod-to-rod gap for accurate measurement of high voltages?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Sphere gaps produce a **quasi-uniform electric field** between the electrodes (for $d/D < 0.5$), which makes the breakdown voltage depend only on the well-defined spacing $d$ and sphere diameter $D$.
- (1 mark) Rod gaps produce a **highly non-uniform divergent field**, making the breakdown voltage strongly dependent on electrode condition, humidity, and polarity, and hence inherently less reproducible.
- (1 mark) As a result, the sphere gap breakdown voltage can be **standardized in tables (IS 1876 / IEC 60052)** with corrections only for air density and humidity, yielding accuracy of ~ ± 3 %, whereas rod gaps have ~ ± 8 % accuracy and are not used as primary standards.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"An electrostatic voltmeter is said to have a 'square-law' response. Justify the statement and state one practical consequence."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1.5 marks) The mechanical force on the moving electrode is $F = \tfrac{1}{2} V^2 \, dC/dx$, and equilibrium deflection is proportional to $V^2$. For AC, the time-averaged deflection involves $\langle V^2 \rangle = V_{rms}^2$, so the scale is non-linear in $V$ but linear in $V^2$.
- (1.5 marks) **Consequence:** The instrument reads the **true RMS value** for *any* waveform (sinusoidal, distorted, square) without needing waveform correction, and it is **insensitive to polarity** — it gives the same deflection for +V and −V.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE of the following:**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 2]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **sphere-to-sphere spark gap** for the measurement of high voltages. Explain the role of the **series protective resistor** $R_p$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 25 cm diameter sphere gap is used to measure a 50 Hz AC voltage. The gap is set to $d = 4$ cm. The ambient conditions are $P = 750$ mmHg, $t = 27^\circ$C, and the humidity correction factor $k_h = 1.01$. Determine the **breakdown voltage** of the gap. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
- (2 marks) **Construction:** Two identical metallic spheres (diameter $D$, typically 5–100 cm) mounted coaxially. The upper sphere is connected to the HV source through a high-value protective resistance; the lower sphere is earthed. The gap $d$ is set with an accurately machined spacer.
- (2 marks) **Working:** Test voltage is increased gradually. At the **breakdown value**, a spark passes, the source impedance and $R_p$ limit the current, and the gap becomes conducting. The peak of the AC voltage (or the DC level) at breakdown is read from the primary side and divided by the transformer's turns ratio. The standard table for $(D, d)$ at standard atmospheric conditions gives the peak breakdown voltage; corrections $\delta$ and $k_h$ are applied.
- (2 marks) **Role of $R_p$:** (i) Limits the breakdown current to a few mA, preventing **pitting and erosion** of the sphere surfaces. (ii) Damps any high-frequency oscillation that would otherwise arise from the sudden collapse of voltage. (iii) Protects the test transformer and source from short-circuit-like currents. Typical values: 100 kΩ to 1 MΩ for power-frequency work; 5–25 kΩ for impulse work.
- (1 mark) **Validity range:** $0.05 \le d/D \le 0.75$ for accurate measurements.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compute the air-density factor.**

$$\delta = \frac{0.386 \cdot P}{273 + t} = \frac{0.386 \cdot 750}{273 + 27} = \frac{289.5}{300} = 0.965$$

**Step 2: Compute the equivalent gap.**

$$d' = \delta \cdot d = 0.965 \cdot 4 = 3.86 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3: Read the standard table.**
For $D = 25$ cm, $d' = 3.86$ cm, from IS 1876 / IEC 60052 (typical values):

| $d'$ (cm) | 3.5 | 3.86 | 4.0 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{b,0}$ (kV peak) | 113 | 122.5 | 127 |

Interpolating between 3.5 and 4.0:

$$V_{b,0}(3.86) \approx 113 + \frac{(122.5 - 113)(3.86 - 3.5)}{0.5}\cdot \text{scale adjustment}$$

A more practical linear interpolation between 3.5 cm (113 kV) and 4.0 cm (127 kV) gives, at 3.86 cm:

$$V_{b,0} \approx 113 + (127 - 113) \cdot \frac{3.86 - 3.5}{4.0 - 3.5} = 113 + 14 \cdot \frac{0.36}{0.5} = 113 + 10.08 = 123.08 \text{ kV}$$

**Step 4: Apply the humidity correction.**

$$V_b = k_h \cdot V_{b,0} = 1.01 \cdot 123.08 = 124.31 \text{ kV (peak)}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_b \approx 124.3$ kV (peak). The corresponding RMS value (for sinusoidal 50 Hz AC) is:

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{124.3}{1.414} = 87.9 \text{ kV (rms)}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2]

**(a)** Describe the **working principle and construction of an electrostatic voltmeter**. Derive an expression for the **deflecting torque** and show that the scale is a **square-law** scale. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of an **electrically short sensor** for impulse-voltage measurement. Derive the **transfer function** of a capacitive pickup feeding a load resistance, and state the condition for faithful reproduction of the wavefront. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

- (2 marks) **Principle:** The instrument relies on the **electrostatic attraction** between two charged conductors. The moving vane is pulled toward the fixed vane by a force proportional to $V^2$. The instrument is essentially a **variable capacitor** whose capacitance depends on the position of the moving vane.
- (2 marks) **Construction:** Quadrant type has a thin suspended vane (the "needle") placed inside a cylindrical box divided into four quadrants. HV is applied to opposite quadrants; the needle is at ground (or vice versa). Deflection is read by a mirror-and-lamp scale (or pointer). For HV, an attracting-disc type is used: a disc is held at HV, a fixed plate at ground, and the disc is restored to a fiducial position by a micrometer screw — the screw position is the **absolute** measure of $V$.
- (3 marks) **Derivation of deflecting torque:**

Energy stored in the capacitance $C$ at voltage $V$:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$

For a small angular displacement $d\theta$ of the moving vane, the work done by the electric field is $T \cdot d\theta = dW$, so:

$$T = \frac{\partial W}{\partial \theta}\bigg|_V = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{\partial C}{\partial \theta}$$

The restoring torque of the suspension is $T_r = K \theta$. At equilibrium:

$$K \theta = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{dC}{d\theta}$$

$$\theta = \frac{1}{2K} \frac{dC}{d\theta} V^2$$

Since $dC/d\theta$ is approximately constant for small deflections, $\theta \propto V^2$ — the **square-law scale**. For AC, $\theta$ is proportional to the **mean-square value** of $V$, i.e., $V_{rms}^2$, confirming that the instrument reads RMS.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

- (2 marks) **Concept:** An electrically short sensor has its **physical length** much smaller than the shortest wavelength of the signal to be measured (rule of thumb: $\ell \le \lambda/50$). The sensor thus presents only a **lumped capacitance** to the conductor, and the wavefront is not distorted by transmission-line effects. Such sensors include **D-dot probes**, **B-dot loops**, **capacitive voltage pick-ups**, and **electro-optical sensors**.
- (3 marks) **Derivation:** As derived in Section 3.4, with coupling capacitance $C_s$, sensor-to-ground capacitance $C_g$, and load resistance $R_L$, the transfer function is:

$$H(\omega) = \frac{V_s}{V} = \frac{j \omega R_L C_s}{1 + j \omega R_L (C_s + C_g)}$$

- (2 marks) **Interpretation & condition:** At high frequency ($\omega \gg 1/[R_L (C_s + C_g)]$), $H \to C_s / (C_s + C_g)$ — a **frequency-independent** capacitive divider. For a wavefront with rise-time $t_r$, the highest significant frequency is $f_h \approx 0.35 / t_r$ (the 10–90 % bandwidth). The condition for **faithful reproduction** is:

$$R_L (C_s + C_g) \ll t_r$$

If this is satisfied, the sensor behaves as a near-ideal **capacitive divider** and reproduces the wavefront correctly. Otherwise it integrates the signal and misrepresents the front (smearing it).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the humidity correction** in sphere-gap problems. Many students compute $\delta$ and apply it directly, but fail to multiply by $k_h$ — a full 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing peak and RMS** at the final step. The sphere gap gives the **peak**; the electrostatic voltmeter gives the **RMS**. Mixing them up is a 2-mark trap.
> 3. **Stating "the breakdown voltage is 30 kV/cm"** as if it were a universal constant. The field at breakdown depends on $d$ and $D$ — there is no single number.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the validity range** $0.05 \le d/D \le 0.75$ in sphere-gap answers. Examiners look for this explicitly.
> 5. **Confusing polarity effect** — claiming that a sphere gap breaks down symmetrically. It does not, and the small polarity effect should be acknowledged.
> 6. **D-dot vs V-dot confusion** — students often write "D-dot measures $V(t)$" when it actually measures $dD/dt$ and requires integration to recover $V$.

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Spark gap measures PEAK voltage**, not RMS.
- **Sphere gap validity range:** $0.05 \le d/D \le 0.75$. **Most accurate at low $d/D$**.
- **Air density factor** $\delta = 0.386 P / (273 + t)$ with $P$ in mmHg, $t$ in °C.
- **Humidity correction** $k_h$ (typically 0.95–1.05) is applied **after** the $\delta$ correction.
- **Equivalent gap** $d' = \delta \cdot d$ — use standard tables at $d'$.
- **Series protective resistor** $R_p$ in the sphere gap limits current and prevents pitting; do not omit it.
- **Rod gap** is polarity- and humidity-sensitive, accuracy ~ ± 8 %, used for protection and rough indication.
- **Electrostatic voltmeter** is governed by $F = \tfrac{1}{2} V^2 \, dC/dx$ — **square-law** ⇒ reads **true RMS** for any waveform.
- **Electrostatic voltmeters are polarity-blind** — they cannot distinguish +V from −V.
- **Field sensors** measure local $E$ (V/m); voltage is recovered by spatial integration along a known geometry.
- **Electrically short sensor** has $\ell \le \lambda/50$ and satisfies $R_L C_T \ll t_r$ — these are the two conditions to memorize.
- **D-dot sensor** measures $dD/dt$ and requires **integration** to recover $V(t)$.
- **Polarity effect on sphere gap** — positive half-cycle breaks down at ~ 1–3 % lower voltage than negative.
- **Breakdown strength of air** at NTP is ~ 30 kV/cm, but the field is not uniform, so the simple $\text{kV/cm}$ rule is only a rough guide.
- **KTU standard tables** are those of **IS 1876** or **IEC 60052** — quote them in your answer for full marks.
- **B-dot and D-dot** are used for **pulsed power** and **EMP/EMC** measurements; they are *not* for DC.
- **Kelvin absolute electrometer** — the disc is restored to a fiducial position by a calibrated micrometer, so it is an **absolute** instrument needing no prior calibration.
- **Spark gap is destructive (single shot)** — electrostatic voltmeter is non-destructive; field/short sensors are non-perturbative.
- For an impulse waveform, the sphere gap gives the **peak** of the impulse, not the time-integrated value.
- The **stray capacitance** of connecting leads in an electrostatic voltmeter can be comparable to the instrument capacitance at HV — use guarded, short, well-defined leads.
- **Safety rule:** Never assume a sphere gap will hold off the test voltage; never approach the gap while HV is on, even with a protective resistor.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Topic Module 2 — High Voltage Measurement Techniques**
> Sphere-sphere gap · Rod-rod gap · Electrostatic voltmeter · Field sensors · Electrically short sensors
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrically Long Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrically Long Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **electrically long sensor** is a high-voltage measurement transducer whose physical dimensions (length of the sensing element and/or the associated transmission path) are no longer negligible when compared to the wavelength of the highest frequency component present in the signal under measurement. In such a regime, the sensor cannot be represented by **lumped parameter** circuit elements (R, L, C) alone; instead, its response must be analysed using **distributed parameter** (transmission line) theory, since the time taken by an electromagnetic wave to traverse the sensor is comparable to, or greater than, the rise time of the measured transient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A sensor is *electrically long* when its physical length $L_s$ satisfies the condition $L_s \ge \dfrac{\lambda_{min}}{10}$ (sometimes quoted as $\dfrac{\lambda_{min}}{6}$), where $\lambda_{min}$ is the wavelength corresponding to the highest significant frequency component of the measured signal.

## 1.2 Wavelength and Frequency Relationship

For a signal propagating through a sensor/cable medium with velocity $v$, the wavelength is given by:

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f}$$

where:
- $\lambda$ = wavelength in metres (**m**)
- $v$ = velocity of propagation in the medium (typically $1.5 \times 10^8$ to $2 \times 10^8$ m/s for typical HV coaxial cables)
- $f$ = frequency of the signal in Hertz (**Hz**)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Whispering in a Long Corridor**
>
> Imagine shouting at one end of a long corridor and listening at the other end. If the corridor is short, the sound reaches you almost instantly and reflects back as a single coherent echo. However, if the corridor is *electrically long* (e.g., 100 m vs a sound wavelength of 1 m), multiple reflections, standing waves, and distortions occur before the sound fully dies down. Similarly, when a **lightning impulse** (with rise time of ~1 μs) travels through a sensor of 30 m length, the wave reflections from terminations distort the measured waveform, requiring transmission line analysis.

In high voltage labs, the **lightning impulse (1.2/50 μs)** and **switching impulse (250/2500 μs)** contain frequency components up to several **MHz**. A 10 m sensor cable at 5 MHz has $\lambda \approx 40$ m, making the sensor about $\lambda/4$ — already a quarter-wave resonator, and hence electrically long.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb Used by KTU Examiners:**
> A sensor/lead is electrically long when its one-way transit time $t_t = L_s/v$ exceeds 1/10 of the signal's **rise time** $t_r$.
> $$\text{Electrically Long Criterion:} \quad \frac{L_s}{v} \ge \frac{t_r}{10}$$

## 1.4 Why This Matters in High Voltage Engineering

| Scenario | Frequency / Rise Time | Wavelength $\lambda$ | Sensor Length | Status |
|----------|----------------------|----------------------|---------------|--------|
| 50 Hz Power Frequency | 50 Hz | 3000 km | a few metres | Electrically **short** |
| Switching Impulse (250 μs rise) | ~4 kHz equivalent | 50 km | 10–30 m | Electrically **short** |
| Lightning Impulse (1.2 μs rise) | ~0.8 MHz | 300 m | 10–30 m | Electrically **long** |
| Steep-front impulse (ns rise) | 100+ MHz | 2–3 m | 1 m | Electrically **very long** |

This means that for **fast transient measurements**, ordinary leads and dividers become transmission lines and must be treated as such.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Distributed vs Lumped Parameter Behaviour

A sensor is modelled as:
- **Lumped**: All elements (R, L, C) at a single point; valid when physical size $L_s \ll \lambda$.
- **Distributed**: Each infinitesimal section $dz$ has its own $R\,dz$, $L\,dz$, $C\,dz$, $G\,dz$; required when $L_s \ge \lambda/10$.

The transition between these two regimes is governed by the **electrical length** $\beta L_s$, where $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the phase constant.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Why and How

The key operational steps in dealing with electrically long sensors are:

1. **Identify the maximum usable frequency** of the measurement. For a step-like transient, this is approximately $f_{max} \approx 0.35 / t_r$ (the bandwidth required to resolve the rise time).
2. **Compute the shortest wavelength** $\lambda_{min} = v / f_{max}$ in the sensor medium.
3. **Compare** $L_s$ with $\lambda_{min}/10$ to determine if the sensor is electrically long.
4. **If electrically long**, model the sensor as a transmission line with characteristic impedance $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ and apply the **Telegrapher's Equations**.
5. **Terminate** the line in its characteristic impedance $Z_0$ to avoid reflections that distort the measured waveform.
6. **Use deconvolution** or **numerical compensation** if perfect matching is not possible.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the consolidated KTU-style cheat sheet for this topic. Use `\vert` for absolute values to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Where Used | Units |
|---|-------------------|-----------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Wavelength | $\lambda = v/f$ | Determining electrical length | m |
| 2 | Phase constant | $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$ | Transmission line analysis | rad/m |
| 3 | Electrical length | $\beta L_s = 2\pi L_s/\lambda$ | Lumped vs distributed check | rad |
| 4 | Electrically long criterion | $L_s \ge \lambda/10$ | Test for distributed behaviour | m |
| 5 | Rise-time–bandwidth | $t_r \approx 0.35/f_{max}$ | Equivalent bandwidth of step | s |
| 6 | One-way transit time | $t_t = L_s / v$ | Compare with rise time | s |
| 7 | Criterion in time domain | $t_t \ge t_r/10$ | Time-domain check | s |
| 8 | Characteristic impedance | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ | Matching transmission line | $\Omega$ |
| 9 | Reflection coefficient | $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$ | Termination analysis | dimensionless |
| 10 | Standing wave ratio | $SWR = (1 + \vert\Gamma\vert)/(1 - \vert\Gamma\vert)$ | Reflection severity | ratio |
| 11 | Velocity in cable | $v = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ | Wavelength in dielectric medium | m/s |
| 12 | Voltage divider ratio (lossless line) | $V_{out}/V_{in} = e^{-j\beta L_s}$ | Travelling wave amplitude | ratio |
| 13 | D-dot sensor output | $i(t) = \varepsilon_0 A\, dE/dt$ | E-field measurement | A |
| 14 | B-dot sensor output | $v(t) = \mu_0 A\, dH/dt$ | H-field measurement | V |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Electrically long sensor analysis is critical in:
- **HV impulse testing labs** (CPRI, ERDA, NABL-accredited labs in Kerala) for accurate recording of $1.2/50\,\mu s$ lightning impulse waveforms.
- **EHV/UHV substation transient monitoring** (e.g., 400 kV substations where steep-front surges need accurate recording).
- **Pulse power systems** such as Z-pinch, Z-machine, electromagnetic launchers, where sub-microsecond transients dominate.
- **Partial discharge detection**, where nanosecond PD pulses need to be captured without distortion from leads.
- **EMP/HEMP testing** for defence and aerospace, where sensors may be metres long and signals are nanoseconds wide.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rise-Time–Bandwidth Relationship

A step-like high-voltage transient with rise time $t_r$ is equivalent (in first-order approximation) to a low-pass filter with $-3$ dB cut-off frequency $f_{max}$. The exact relationship depends on the filter type; the most common is the RC-equivalent (single-pole), giving:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{0}\left(1 - e^{-2\pi f_{max} t}\right)$$

**Step (a)**: Define rise time as the time to go from 10% to 90% of final value.

$$t_r = t_{90\%} - t_{10\%}$$

**Step (b)**: At $t = t_{10\%}$: $0.1 = 1 - e^{-2\pi f_{max} t_{10\%}}$, so

$$e^{-2\pi f_{max} t_{10\%}} = 0.9 \;\Rightarrow\; t_{10\%} = \frac{-\ln 0.9}{2\pi f_{max}} = \frac{0.1054}{f_{max}}$$

**Step (c)**: At $t = t_{90\%}$: $0.9 = 1 - e^{-2\pi f_{max} t_{90\%}}$, so

$$e^{-2\pi f_{max} t_{90\%}} = 0.1 \;\Rightarrow\; t_{90\%} = \frac{-\ln 0.1}{2\pi f_{max}} = \frac{2.3026}{f_{max}}$$

**Step (d)**: Subtract:

$$t_r = t_{90\%} - t_{10\%} = \frac{2.3026 - 0.1054}{2\pi f_{max}}$$

$$t_r = \frac{2.1972}{2\pi f_{max}} = \frac{0.3499}{f_{max}}$$

**Step (e)**: Round off and rearrange:

$$\boxed{f_{max} \approx \frac{0.35}{t_r}}$$

This is the **bandwidth** that the sensor (including its leads) must have to faithfully reproduce a transient of rise time $t_r$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Electrically Long Criterion in Time Domain

Consider a transmission line of length $L_s$ with propagation velocity $v$. The one-way travel time is:

$$t_t = \frac{L_s}{v}$$

For the line to behave as a lumped element, the wave must traverse the structure in time much less than the rise time:

$$t_t \ll t_r \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{L_s}{v} \ll t_r$$

Taking the practical limit (one-tenth rule):

$$\frac{L_s}{v} \le \frac{t_r}{10}$$

Or equivalently in terms of wavelength (using $\lambda = v/f$ and $f_{max} \approx 0.35/t_r$):

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{v}{f_{max}} = \frac{v \cdot t_r}{0.35}$$

$$L_s \le \frac{\lambda_{min}}{10} \cdot \frac{0.35}{1} \cdot \frac{10}{1} \;\approx\; \frac{\lambda_{min}}{10}$$

Hence the well-known **"$\lambda/10$ rule"**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Reflection Coefficient and Standing Wave Ratio

When a transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$ is terminated in a load $Z_L$ (not equal to $Z_0$), a portion of the wave is reflected.

**Step (a)**: Define the forward voltage wave $V^+$ at the termination point. The boundary condition at $Z_L$ is:

$$V^+ + V^- = I^+ Z_0 - I^- Z_0$$
$$V^+ + V^- = Z_L \cdot (I^+ - I^-)$$

Wait — let me re-derive carefully. With conventional current direction:

$$V^+ + V^- = Z_L(I^+ - I^-)$$

**Step (b)**: Using $V^+ = I^+ Z_0$ and $V^- = -I^- Z_0$ (with proper sign convention), and solving:

$$V^- = V^+ \cdot \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

**Step (c)**: Define reflection coefficient:

$$\Gamma = \frac{V^-}{V^+} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

**Step (d)**: Voltage Standing Wave Ratio is defined as the ratio of maximum to minimum voltage on the line:

$$SWR = \frac{V_{max}}{V_{min}} = \frac{\vert V^+ \vert + \vert V^- \vert}{\vert V^+ \vert - \vert V^- \vert} = \frac{1 + \vert\Gamma\vert}{1 - \vert\Gamma\vert}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Sensor Types in HV Measurements

| Sensor Type | Typical Length | Equivalent Circuit | Electrically Long? (for 1 μs rise) | Mitigation |
|-------------|----------------|--------------------|------------------------------------|------------|
| Resistive Voltage Divider (low-voltage arm) | 1–5 m | Distributed R, C | **Yes** at $f > 6$ MHz | Compensated divider with parallel C |
| Capacitive Divider | 1–10 m | Distributed Z | **Yes** | Add damping resistor |
| Rogowski Coil | 0.5–2 m | Distributed L | Marginal | Shielded termination |
| Coaxial Current Shunt | 0.2–1 m | Lumped to ~100 MHz | **No** at moderate $f$ | Use low-inductance design |
| D-dot (electric field) sensor | 0.05–0.5 m | Small capacitance | Borderline | Integrator with matched cable |
| B-dot (magnetic field) sensor | 0.05–0.5 m | Small inductance | Borderline | Damping resistor |
| Optical Sensor (Pockels) | 1–50 m fibre | Distributed optical | **Yes** as cable | Use matched $50\,\Omega$ cables |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A $50\,\Omega$ coaxial cable of length 15 m is used to connect a high-voltage impulse divider to a digital recorder. The cable has dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r = 2.25$ and the impulse has a rise time of $0.5\,\mu s$. Determine whether the cable is electrically long. If the recorder has input impedance $1\,\mathrm{M}\Omega$, calculate the reflection coefficient and comment on waveform distortion.

**Given:**
- $L_s = 15$ m
- $Z_0 = 50\,\Omega$
- $\varepsilon_r = 2.25$
- $t_r = 0.5\,\mu s$
- $Z_L = 1\,\mathrm{M}\Omega$

**Step 1: Velocity of propagation**

$$v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2.25}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5} = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Maximum frequency**

$$f_{max} = \frac{0.35}{t_r} = \frac{0.35}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 7 \times 10^5 \text{ Hz} = 0.7 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 3: Minimum wavelength**

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{v}{f_{max}} = \frac{2 \times 10^8}{7 \times 10^5} = 285.7 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Check electrical length**

$$\frac{\lambda_{min}}{10} = 28.57 \text{ m}, \quad L_s = 15 \text{ m}$$

Since $L_s = 15 \text{ m} < 28.57 \text{ m}$, the cable is **electrically SHORT** in this case.

**Step 5: Reflection coefficient (still useful to compute)**

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = \frac{10^6 - 50}{10^6 + 50} \approx 0.9999$$

Although $\Gamma \approx 1$, because the cable is electrically short, the reflected wave reappears almost instantaneously and adds to the recorded waveform, producing an apparent doubling of voltage at the leading edge. This is a **classic high-impedance termination** problem.

**Step 6: Practical solution** — terminate the cable with $50\,\Omega$ at the recorder (using a feed-through terminator) and use a buffer amplifier with high input impedance but connected via a $50\,\Omega$ matched network.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]

> Define an **electrically long sensor**. Under what condition is a sensor classified as electrically long?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An electrically long sensor is a high-voltage measurement transducer whose physical length is comparable to the wavelength of the highest frequency component of the measured signal.

**Condition:** A sensor is classified as electrically long when

$$L_s \ge \frac{\lambda_{min}}{10}$$

where $L_s$ is the sensor's physical length and $\lambda_{min}$ is the wavelength corresponding to $f_{max} \approx 0.35/t_r$.

Equivalently, in the time domain, the one-way transit time $t_t = L_s/v$ should be at least $t_r/10$ for it to be considered electrically long.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for time-domain equivalent condition.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]

> What is the significance of the **characteristic impedance** $Z_0$ of a coaxial cable in the context of an electrically long sensor? What happens when $Z_L \neq Z_0$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The characteristic impedance $Z_0$ of a transmission line (typically $50\,\Omega$ or $75\,\Omega$ for HV measurement cables) represents the ratio of voltage to current for a single travelling wave. When a load $Z_L$ is connected at the far end of an electrically long sensor cable:

- If $Z_L = Z_0$ (matched condition), there is **no reflection**, and the measured waveform is a true representation of the input.
- If $Z_L \neq Z_0$ (mismatched), a **reflection** occurs with coefficient $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$. The reflected wave superposes on the incident wave, producing waveform distortion, overshoot, or ringing.

In high-impedance recorders (e.g., $1\,\mathrm{M}\Omega$ oscilloscope input), $\Gamma \approx 1$, causing an apparent doubling of the recorded voltage. Hence, matched terminations are essential.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

> **(a)** Derive the condition under which a high-voltage measurement sensor is classified as **electrically long**. Discuss the relevance of the $\lambda/10$ rule in HV impulse measurements. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **20 m** long coaxial cable with polyethylene insulation ($\varepsilon_r = 2.25$, $Z_0 = 50\,\Omega$) connects a compensated voltage divider to a digitizer of input impedance $1\,\mathrm{M}\Omega$. The measured lightning impulse has a rise time of $1.2\,\mu s$. Determine:
>    - (i) the maximum frequency and minimum wavelength,
>    - (ii) whether the cable is electrically long,
>    - (iii) the reflection coefficient at the digitizer end, and
>    - (iv) the practical steps to obtain an undistorted recording. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The condition for a sensor to be electrically long is derived from the requirement that the wave transit time through the sensor be a non-negligible fraction of the rise time of the measured signal.

The one-way transit time of an EM wave through a sensor of length $L_s$ with velocity $v$ is:

$$t_t = \frac{L_s}{v}$$

The maximum frequency content of a step-like transient with rise time $t_r$ is:

$$f_{max} = \frac{0.35}{t_r}$$

The minimum wavelength in the medium is:

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{v}{f_{max}} = \frac{v \cdot t_r}{0.35}$$

For the sensor to be considered electrically long, its length should be at least $\lambda_{min}/10$ (one-tenth rule), giving:

$$L_s \ge \frac{\lambda_{min}}{10} = \frac{v \cdot t_r}{3.5}$$

In time domain:

$$\frac{L_s}{v} \ge \frac{t_r}{10}$$

**Relevance to HV impulse measurements:**

- The standard **lightning impulse (1.2/50 μs)** contains frequency components up to ~0.3 MHz, giving $\lambda_{min} \approx 1$ km in air and ~700 m in polyethylene-insulated cable. Hence, even a 30 m divider cable is electrically long and must be treated as a transmission line.
- The **steep-front impulse** (rise time ~ ns) has frequency components in the 100 MHz range with $\lambda_{min}$ of a few metres, making almost any practical sensor electrically long.
- Ignoring this leads to **waveform distortion**, erroneous peak voltage readings, and non-compliance with IEC 60060-1/-2/-3 standards.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $L_s = 20$ m, $\varepsilon_r = 2.25$, $Z_0 = 50\,\Omega$, $t_r = 1.2\,\mu s$, $Z_L = 1\,\mathrm{M}\Omega$

**(i) Maximum frequency and minimum wavelength:**

$$v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2.25}} = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

$$f_{max} = \frac{0.35}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.917 \times 10^5 \text{ Hz} \approx 0.29 \text{ MHz}$$

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{v}{f_{max}} = \frac{2 \times 10^8}{2.917 \times 10^5} = 685.7 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Electrically long check:**

$$\frac{\lambda_{min}}{10} = 68.57 \text{ m}, \quad L_s = 20 \text{ m}$$

Since $20 \text{ m} < 68.57 \text{ m}$, the cable is **electrically SHORT** in this case (lumped behaviour). However, the *transit time* check:

$$t_t = \frac{L_s}{v} = \frac{20}{2 \times 10^8} = 100 \text{ ns} = 0.1 \,\mu s$$

$$t_r/10 = 0.12 \,\mu s$$

So $t_t \approx t_r/12$ — borderline, but still within lumped limits for IEC-standard $1.2/50\,\mu s$ impulse.

**(iii) Reflection coefficient:**

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = \frac{10^6 - 50}{10^6 + 50} = 0.9999 \approx 1$$

This is a near-open-circuit termination, producing almost full reflection.

**(iv) Practical steps for undistorted recording:**

1. Use a **feed-through $50\,\Omega$ terminator** at the digitizer input (with a $50\,\Omega$ buffer amplifier afterwards) so that $Z_L = Z_0$ and $\Gamma = 0$.
2. Keep cable length as short as possible to minimise $t_t$.
3. Use **dielectrics with low $\varepsilon_r$** (e.g., cellular polyethylene) to maximise $v$ and reduce transit time.
4. Use **compensated dividers** with carefully matched high- and low-voltage arms to flatten the frequency response up to the required $f_{max}$.
5. If long cables are unavoidable, perform **deconvolution** of the recorded waveform using the known cable impulse response.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the various functional blocks in the **measurement chain of an electrically long sensor** for HV impulse testing. Discuss the role of impedance matching in each stage. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the construction and working of a **D-dot sensor** and a **B-dot sensor** as electrically long sensors used for measuring fast transient electric and magnetic fields. Derive expressions for their outputs. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The measurement chain of an electrically long sensor in HV impulse testing consists of the following functional blocks (refer to the Mermaid diagrams in Section 4):

1. **HV Test Object / Source** — the device under test, e.g., a transformer, insulator, or surge generator.
2. **Sensor Head** — e.g., a compensated voltage divider, current shunt, Rogowski coil, or D-dot/B-dot probe. This is the primary transducer.
3. **Coaxial Lead / Cable** — transmits the low-voltage replica of the HV signal. Because of its distributed L and C, it is treated as a transmission line when the sensor is electrically long.
4. **Impedance Match Network** — ensures $Z_L = Z_0$ at the receiving end; may include a damping resistor for the divider.
5. **Termination** — usually a $50\,\Omega$ feed-through resistor to absorb reflections.
6. **Buffer Amplifier** — high-input-impedance device presenting $Z_0$ to the cable, so that the cable sees a matched load regardless of the recorder impedance.
7. **Digitizer / Oscilloscope** — records the waveform; must have a bandwidth $\ge f_{max}$.
8. **Display and Analysis** — software for waveform analysis, peak detection, time parameter measurement (per IEC 60060-1).

**Role of impedance matching in each stage:**

- **Sensor to cable:** The output impedance of the sensor must equal $Z_0$ of the cable for maximum power transfer and to prevent intra-cable reflections.
- **Cable to digitizer:** The digitizer input impedance must equal $Z_0$ (via a $50\,\Omega$ terminator) to prevent reflections from the far end.
- **Compensation:** The sensor's internal impedance is shaped (e.g., $R_{damping} \parallel C_{compensating}$) so that the divider ratio remains constant across the required frequency band.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**D-dot Sensor (Electric Field Sensor):**

A D-dot sensor is essentially a small capacitive electrode (typically a flat disc or hemisphere) connected to a coaxial cable. The displacement current through the sensor capacitance $C_s$ is:

$$i(t) = \frac{dQ}{dt} = C_s \frac{dV}{dt}$$

The voltage across the electrode in a uniform electric field $E$ (perpendicular to the disc) is:

$$V = E \cdot d$$

where $d$ is the effective spacing. For a flat disc of area $A$ in free space:

$$V = E \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{C_s + C_{cable}}$$

Defining the **equivalent area** $A_{eq}$ and noting that $C_s \ll C_{cable}$ (cable capacitance dominates), the output current becomes:

$$i(t) = \varepsilon_0 A_{eq} \frac{dE}{dt}$$

The output of a D-dot sensor is therefore **proportional to the time derivative of the electric field** — hence "D-dot". To recover $E(t)$, the signal must be **integrated** either via a passive RC integrator at the cable end or numerically in software.

> **Construction:** A flush-mounted disc electrode on a ground plane, with a $50\,\Omega$ coaxial feed-through, a $50\,\Omega$ matched terminator, and a passive or active integrator.

**B-dot Sensor (Magnetic Field Sensor):**

A B-dot sensor is a small loop antenna (typically a few cm diameter) that senses the rate of change of magnetic flux. The induced EMF in a loop of area $A$ in a magnetic field $B$ is:

$$v(t) = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -A \frac{dB}{dt}$$

For a multi-turn loop of $N$ turns:

$$v(t) = -N A \frac{dB}{dt}$$

> **Construction:** A small shielded loop (to avoid capacitive pickup) connected to a $50\,\Omega$ cable, with a $50\,\Omega$ termination. The loop is oriented perpendicular to the magnetic field direction of interest.

**Why both are "electrically long" sensors:** Even though the active element is small, the connecting cable may be several metres long. For fast transients (ns rise time), the cable's transit time becomes a significant fraction of the signal's rise time. The cable must therefore be treated as a transmission line and terminated in $Z_0$ to avoid distorting the derivative output. The D-dot/B-dot outputs are then integrated to recover the field waveform.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing transit time with rise time** — the criterion is $t_t \ge t_r/10$, NOT $t_t \ge t_r$.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by $\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$** — students often use $v = c$ instead of $v = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ when calculating wavelength in cables.
> 3. **Mixing up the reflection coefficient sign** — at an open circuit, $\Gamma = +1$ (voltage doubles), at a short circuit, $\Gamma = -1$ (voltage goes to zero). Examiners penalise confusion here.
> 4. **Not converting units** — $t_r$ must be in seconds and $L_s$ in metres; mark deductions for unit errors.
> 5. **Ignoring the $50\,\Omega$ termination practice** — when an oscilloscope is mentioned, students often assume $1\,\mathrm{M}\Omega$ input impedance and forget to specify matched termination.
> 6. **Not specifying which equivalent circuit model** — examiners expect explicit mention of "lumped" vs "distributed" model in your answer.
