Thus, the AC component of $I_{out}$ is **directly linear in $E$**, which is the basis of calibrated Pockels sensors.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Faraday Rotation and Current Measurement

**Step 1 — Ampère's circuital law in integral form:**
For a long straight conductor carrying current $I$, the magnetic field at a distance $r$ is:
$$H = \frac{I}{2\pi r}$$

**Step 2 — Faraday's law of rotation:**
The differential rotation $d\theta$ produced in a path element $d\vec{l}$ is:
$$d\theta = V \cdot \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate along a circular path of N turns around the conductor:**
$$\theta_{F} = V \oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = V \cdot N \oint H\, dl$$

**Step 4 — Apply Ampère's law around the closed path encircling the conductor N times:**
$$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = N \cdot I$$

**Step 5 — Combine the results:**
$$\theta_{F} = V \cdot N \cdot I \quad \Rightarrow \quad I = \frac{\theta_{F}}{V \cdot N}$$

**Step 6 — Convert optical rotation to measurable signal:**
Using a polarizer-analyzer pair with the analyzer at 45° to the rotated polarization:
$$I_{out} = I_0 \cos^{2}(\theta_F + 45°) = \frac{I_0}{2}\left[1 - \sin(2\theta_F)\right]$$

For small rotations ($\theta_F \ll 1$ rad):
$$I_{out} \approx \frac{I_0}{2}(1 - 2\theta_F) = \frac{I_0}{2} - I_0 V N I$$

The AC signal is linear in $I$.

---

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Voltage Divider Frequency Response

Consider a **resistive divider with stray capacitance $C_g$ to ground** on the HV arm of total resistance $R_1$, and a low-voltage arm $R_2$ with a measuring instrument of capacitance $C_m$ in parallel.

**Step 1 — Equivalent circuit representation:**
The HV arm is a distributed RC line, but for analytical purposes, we use a lumped model: $R_1$ in series with parallel stray $C_{g1}$, terminated by $R_2 \parallel C_m$.

**Step 2 — Voltage transfer function:**
For a step input $V_{in}(s) = V_0/s$, the divider's transfer function in the s-domain is:
$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{Z_2(s)}{Z_1(s) + Z_2(s)}$$

where $Z_1(s) = R_1 / (1 + s R_1 C_{g1})$ and $Z_2(s) = R_2 / (1 + s R_2 C_m)$.

**Step 3 — Time constants:**
Define $\tau_1 = R_1 C_{g1}$ (HV arm time constant) and $\tau_2 = R_2 C_m$ (LV arm time constant).

**Step 4 — Step response analysis:**
For an ideal square wave, the response is:
$$v_{out}(t) = V_0 \cdot n^{-1} \left[1 + \left(\frac{\tau_1}{\tau_1 - \tau_2}\right) e^{-t/\tau_1} - \left(\frac{\tau_2}{\tau_1 - \tau_2}\right) e^{-t/\tau_2}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Condition for optimum square-pulse response:**
For negligible overshoot and minimal distortion, the two time constants must be matched:
$$\tau_1 = \tau_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1 C_{g1} = R_2 C_m$$

**Step 6 — Compensated divider ratio:**
Under matched conditions, $v_{out}(t) = V_0 / n$ with no transient distortion — this is the principle of the **compensated impulse voltage divider**.

---

### 3.4 Detailed Derivation: True-RMS Measurement by Squaring-Averaging-Square-Root Method

**Step 1 — Square the instantaneous signal:**
$$x^{2}(t) = v^{2}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Integrate (average) over one period $T$:**
$$\langle v^{2} \rangle = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root:**
$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\langle v^{2} \rangle}$$

**Implementation in an electronic circuit:**
- A **wide-band analog multiplier** (e.g., AD534) squares the input.
- A **low-pass filter** with $RC \gg T$ performs the averaging.
- A **square-root circuit** (using op-amp with diode feedback) extracts the root.
- The final DC output drives a moving-coil meter calibrated in $V_{rms}$.

For **digital implementation:**
- A 12-bit ADC samples $v(t)$ at frequency $f_s \geq 10 f_{max}$.
- A microcontroller computes: $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k^{2}}$

**Analytical Matrix — Error Sources in RMS Measurement:**

| Source of Error | Magnitude | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth limitation | High for fast transients | Use $>10\times$ signal bandwidth |
| DC offset in AC | Adds $V_{dc}^{2}$ to result | AC-couple or remove DC digitally |
| Crest factor exceeding meter spec | Saturates averaging stage | Use true-RMS meter with $K_c \geq 5$ |
| Sampling aliasing | Severe for non-sinusoidal | Anti-alias filter at $f_s / 2$ |
| Temperature drift of multiplier | $\pm 0.1\% / ^\circ$C | Temperature-compensated references |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Pockels Effect Electro-Optical Voltage Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HV Conductor] -->|E field| B[Pockels Crystal LiNbO3]
    L[Laser Diode 633nm] --> M[Polarizer 45 deg]
    M --> B
    B --> N[Quarter Wave Plate]
    N --> O[Analyzer Polarizer 90 deg]
    O --> P[Photodetector PIN Diode]
    P --> Q[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    Q --> R[ADC and Microcontroller]
    R --> S[Display / Recorder]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The HV conductor's electric field $E$ is *not* electrically connected to the optical system — only coupled through space. The laser light path is shown in the lower branch, and the signal output flows through the right-side chain to the digital readout.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Faraday Effect Magneto-Optical Current Sensor (OCT)

```mermaid
    A[Current Carrying Busbar] -->|H field| B[Optical Fiber N turns wrapped]

    P1 --> B
    B --> PBS[Polarization Beam Splitter]
    PBS --> D1[Photodetector Channel X]
    PBS --> D2[Photodetector Channel Y]
    D1 --> SP[Signal Processing Subtraction and Division]
    D2 --> SP
    SP --> OUT[Calculated Current Value I]
```

```mermaid
    NODE --> R2[Low Voltage Resistor R2]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the Pockels effect. Why is it preferred over the Kerr effect for HV electric field measurement?**

**Model Answer:**
The Pockels effect is the **linear electro-optic effect** in which the refractive index of a non-centrosymmetric crystal (e.g., LiNbO₃, BSO, KDP) changes in direct proportion to the applied electric field $E$. The induced phase retardation is given by:
$$\Gamma = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} n_{0}^{3} r_{63} E L$$

It is preferred over the Kerr effect because:
1. **Linear in $E$** (Pockels) vs. **quadratic in $E$** (Kerr) — easier calibration.
2. **Much larger sensitivity** (typical $r_{63} \sim 30$ pm/V) compared to Kerr constant ($K \sim 10^{-12}$ m/V²).
3. Pockels materials work at **moderate field strengths**, while Kerr needs extremely high fields.
4. Better **frequency response** and simpler signal conditioning.

---

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**What is a "true-RMS" meter? Why is it necessary for HV testing waveforms?**

**Model Answer:**
A true-RMS meter is an instrument that measures the **actual root-mean-square value** of a periodic or transient waveform, regardless of its shape, by performing the operation $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t) dt}$ either in analog (squarer-averager-square-rooter) or digital (sampling) domain.

It is necessary for HV testing because:
1. HV test waveforms (impulses, switching surges, chopped waves, oscillator transients) are **non-sinusoidal**.
2. Conventional **average-responding meters** (rectifier-type) and **peak meters** (sphere gaps, electrostatic voltmeters) give readings that are accurate **only for pure sine waves**.
3. True-RMS measurement gives the **heating-effective value**, which directly relates to insulation stress and loss calculations.
4. Required for **harmonic and THD compliance** testing per IEEE 519.

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **electro-optical voltage sensor** based on the **Pockels effect**. Derive the expression for the output light intensity as a function of the applied electric field.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (described):** As shown in Section 4.1, the system consists of: Laser source → Polarizer (45°) → Pockels crystal placed in HV field → Quarter-wave plate (bias) → Analyzer (90°) → Photodetector → Amplifier → Display.

**Working Principle:**
1. Laser emits a stable, monochromatic beam.
2. Polarizer sets the linear polarization at 45° to crystal's principal axes.
3. The HV electric field $\vec{E}$ induces birefringence in the crystal — the two orthogonal components travel at different speeds.
4. After length $L$, a phase retardation $\Gamma = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} n_0^3 r_{63} E L$ accumulates.
5. The quarter-wave plate provides a fixed bias $\Gamma_0 = \pi/2$ to linearize the response.
6. The analyzer converts the phase modulation into intensity modulation.
7. Photodetector produces a current/voltage proportional to $I_{out}$.

**Derivation (reproduced):**
Following the derivation in Section 3.1:
- Input Jones vector: $\vec{E}_{in} = \frac{E_0}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- Crystal matrix introduces phase shift $\Gamma$.
- Analyzer projects onto the perpendicular axis.
- Final result: $I_{out} = I_0 \sin^{2}\left(\frac{\Gamma}{2}\right) = I_0 \sin^{2}\left(\frac{\pi n_0^3 r_{63} L}{\lambda} E\right)$

With quarter-wave plate bias:
$$I_{out} \approx \frac{I_0}{2} + \frac{\pi n_0^3 r_{63} L I_0}{2\lambda}\, E$$

Hence, the linear AC component of the photodetector output is **directly proportional to the HV electric field** $E$.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **KDP crystal** ($r_{63} = 10.6$ pm/V, $n_0 = 1.51$) of length $L = 20$ mm is used in a Pockels sensor with a He-Ne laser ($\lambda = 633$ nm). Calculate the **half-wave voltage** $V_{\pi}$ (the voltage across the crystal that produces a phase shift of $\pi$).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Half-wave condition: $\Gamma = \pi$.

**Step 2:** Phase retardation formula:
$$\Gamma = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} n_0^3 r_{63} E L = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} n_0^3 r_{63} \frac{V}{d} L$$

where $V$ is the voltage across the crystal and $d$ is the electrode spacing (assume $d = L = 20$ mm for this case — or use a general ratio).

**Step 3:** Set $\Gamma = \pi$ and solve for $V$:
$$\pi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} n_0^3 r_{63} \frac{V_{\pi}}{d} L$$

$$V_{\pi} = \frac{\lambda \cdot d}{2 n_0^3 r_{63} L}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values:
- $\lambda = 633 \times 10^{-9}$ m
- $d = 20 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $L = 20 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $n_0 = 1.51 \Rightarrow n_0^3 = 3.44$
- $r_{63} = 10.6 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V

$$V_{\pi} = \frac{(633 \times 10^{-9})(20 \times 10^{-3})}{2 \times 3.44 \times (10.6 \times 10^{-12}) \times (20 \times 10^{-3})}$$

**Step 5:** Numerator: $633 \times 20 = 12{,}660 \Rightarrow 12{,}660 \times 10^{-12} = 1.266 \times 10^{-8}$ V·m

**Step 6:** Denominator: $2 \times 3.44 \times 10.6 \times 20 = 1458.56 \Rightarrow 1458.56 \times 10^{-18} = 1.4586 \times 10^{-15}$ m²/V

**Step 7:** $V_{\pi} = \frac{1.266 \times 10^{-8}}{1.4586 \times 10^{-15}} = 8.68 \times 10^{6}$ V = **8.68 kV** (for $d = L$).

If the electrode spacing $d$ is different from $L$, the result scales as $V_{\pi} \propto d/L$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024] — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Faraday effect** based **magneto-optical current transducer (OCT)** with a block diagram. Explain why it provides **galvanic isolation** and is immune to **external electromagnetic interference**.

**Model Solution:**

**Faraday Effect:** When linearly polarized light passes through a magneto-optic medium in the presence of a magnetic field $\vec{H}$ parallel to the propagation direction, the plane of polarization rotates by an angle $\theta_F = V \int \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l}$, where $V$ is the Verdet constant of the material.

**Block Diagram (described):** As shown in Section 4.2: LED source → Input polarizer → Optical fiber (N turns around the conductor) → Polarization beam splitter → Two photodetectors (X and Y channels) → Signal processing (subtraction and division) → Current readout.

**Working Principle:**
1. The conductor carrying current $I$ generates a magnetic field $\vec{H}$ circling it (right-hand rule).
2. Light is guided through a fiber wrapped $N$ times around the conductor.
3. The total Faraday rotation accumulated is: $\theta_F = V \cdot N \cdot I$ (using Ampère's law).
4. The polarization beam splitter separates the rotated beam into two orthogonal components.
5. Photodetectors measure $I_1$ and $I_2$; the difference-over-sum gives $\sin(2\theta_F)$.
6. From $\theta_F$, current $I$ is computed.

**Galvanic Isolation:** The fiber-optic cable is **dielectric** — no electrical conductor connects the HV side to the measuring instrument. Hence, the sensor cannot conduct fault current, providing inherent insulation and safety.

**EMI Immunity:** By Ampère's circuital law, $\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = N_{enc} I$. The fiber wraps the conductor in a closed loop. Any **external** uniform field produces zero net line integral when summed around the loop. Only the field from the **enclosed current** contributes, so external noise sources (power lines, RF, switching) are rejected.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **single-turn fiber** (Verdet constant $V = 3.5 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/A) is used in a Faraday current sensor. The system can resolve a minimum rotation of $\Delta \theta_F = 10^{-4}$ rad. Calculate the **smallest measurable current** and the **current corresponding to a rotation of 30°**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Smallest measurable current:**
Given $N = 1$ and $\theta_F = V \cdot N \cdot I$:
$$I_{min} = \frac{\Delta \theta_F}{V \cdot N} = \frac{10^{-4}}{3.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 1} = 2.857 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Current for 30° rotation:**
Convert 30° to radians: $30° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = 0.5236$ rad.
$$I = \frac{\theta_F}{V \cdot N} = \frac{0.5236}{3.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 1} = 14{,}960 \text{ A} \approx 14.96 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 3 — Practical enhancement using N turns:**
If $N = 100$ turns, sensitivity improves 100-fold: $I_{min} = 28.57$ mA. This is how practical OCTs achieve sub-ampere sensitivity for HVDC metering.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Forgetting to convert 30° → radians before substituting into the radian-based formula. This loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing N:** Some students take $N$ as the number of fiber *strands* rather than turns — read the problem statement carefully.
> 3. **Sign convention:** A negative current (opposite direction) gives negative rotation — note this in your answer.
> 4. **Missing "galvanic isolation" mention:** Full marks for "EMI immunity" require explicit invocation of Ampère's law with the closed-loop integral — students often just say "it is immune" without justification.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Pockels effect** is *linear* in $E$ (preferred); **Kerr effect** is *quadratic* (weak, used for ultra-high fields).
- **Pockels output intensity** (with $\lambda/4$ bias): $I_{out} = \frac{I_0}{2}(1 + \Gamma)$ — linear in applied field.
- **Half-wave voltage** $V_{\pi}$ of a Pockels crystal: $V_{\pi} = \frac{\lambda d}{2 n_0^3 r_{63} L}$ — sets the dynamic range of the sensor.
- **Faraday rotation** $\theta_F = V \cdot N \cdot I$ — the working equation of every optical current transformer.
- **Verdet constant $V$** is wavelength- and temperature-dependent; always specify $\lambda$ when quoting $V$.
- **Voltage dividers** are scaled by $n = 1 + Z_1/Z_2$; **resistive** for DC, **capacitive** for AC, **RC-compensated** for impulse.
- **Compensation condition** for impulse divider: $R_1 C_{g1} = R_2 C_m$ — yields square-pulse fidelity.
- **CT nominal ratio** $K_i = N_2/N_1$; **ratio error** $\epsilon_i$ and **phase error** $\delta$ are both specified in accuracy class.
- **PT** is essentially a step-down transformer; burden is in VA; standard secondary is 110 V / $\sqrt{3}$.
- **True RMS** requires the squarer-averager-square-rooter chain; average and peak meters fail for non-sinusoidal HV waveforms.
- **Form factor** $K_f = 1.11$ (sine only); **crest factor** $K_c = \sqrt{2}$ (sine only) — both change for distorted waves.
- **Sphere gap** measures peak; **electrostatic voltmeter** measures RMS for pure sine only; **true-RMS meter** is the universal HV waveform instrument.
- **Galvanic isolation** and **EMI immunity** make electro-optical and magneto-optical sensors the gold standard for modern EHV/HVDC instrumentation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Peak Value and Harmonics - Current Measurement Dielectric Measurements - Dissipation Factor and Capacitance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Peak Value of High Voltage — Why It Matters

In High Voltage Engineering, the **breakdown strength of a dielectric** is governed by the **peak value** (crest value) of the applied voltage waveform, NOT by its RMS value. A sinusoidal AC voltage of 1 kV RMS actually reaches a peak of $\sqrt{2} \times 1000 \approx 1414$ V. If this waveform is distorted by harmonics, the true peak may rise above $V_{peak}$ of the fundamental, often leading to premature insulation failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The measurement of *Peak Voltage* is critical because the dielectric strength of all insulation materials (air, oil, solid dielectrics) is a function of the **maximum instantaneous voltage** rather than the RMS or average value. Hence, peak voltmeters and sphere gaps are used as primary HV measuring devices.

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)
The **Peak Value** ($V_{max}$ or $V_m$) of an AC voltage is the maximum instantaneous magnitude attained by the waveform during one complete cycle. For a pure sinusoid $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, the peak value is the amplitude $V_m$.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a wave at the beach. The **height of the wave's crest** (peak) decides whether a sandcastle on the shore stays dry or gets soaked — not the average depth of water. Similarly, in HV systems, the **crest of the voltage wave** decides whether insulation survives or breaks down.

---

## 1.2 Harmonics in HV Waveforms

In real HV generation (transformers, rectifier-inverter systems, generators under load), the output waveform is rarely a pure sine. It contains **higher-frequency sinusoidal components** called **harmonics**, superimposed on the fundamental.

A non-sinusoidal periodic voltage can be expressed by the **Fourier Series**:

$$v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n)$$

where:
- $V_0$ = DC (average) component
- $V_1$ = **Fundamental** (50/60 Hz) component
- $V_n$ for $n \geq 2$ = **Harmonics** of order $n$
- $\phi_n$ = phase angle of the $n$-th harmonic

> [!NOTE]
> **Odd harmonics (3rd, 5th, 7th)** dominate in HV systems because of the symmetric non-linear behavior of magnetic cores and rectifiers. They **distort the crest** of the waveform, inflating the peak value beyond the ideal $\sqrt{2} V_{rms}$.

### Key Harmonic Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| **Total Harmonic Distortion** | THD | $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ |
| **Crest Factor (Peak Factor)** | $k_p$ | $k_p = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}}$ (For pure sine = $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$) |
| **Form Factor** | $k_f$ | $k_f = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}}$ (For pure sine = 1.11) |
| **Distortion Factor** | $k_d$ | $k_d = \dfrac{V_{1,rms}}{V_{rms}}$ |

---

## 1.3 Current Measurement in HV Systems

Measurement of current in HV circuits is essential for:
- Monitoring leakage current through insulation
- Determining charging/discharging currents of capacitive loads
- Evaluating dielectric loss

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Direct insertion of an ammeter in HV circuits is **unsafe and impractical**. Hence, indirect methods are used:
> - **Current Transformers (CTs)** for AC
> - **Resistive Shunts** for impulse currents
> - **Rogowski Coils** for AC and transient currents
> - **Magnetic (Hall-effect) Probes** for DC and AC

### Intuitive Analogy — Rogowski Coil
Think of a Rogowski coil as a **flexible rubber hose wrapped around a wire**. Current flowing through the wire creates a magnetic field (like water flowing in a pipe). The hose "senses" this field as a voltage proportional to the rate of change of current. It is **non-intrusive** — the wire is not cut or broken.

---

## 1.4 Dissipation Factor (tan δ) and Capacitance — Why Measure?

When a high voltage is applied across a **dielectric** (e.g., transformer oil, XLPE cable insulation, porcelain bushing), the dielectric absorbs energy from the AC field. This energy loss is quantified by the **Dissipation Factor**, $\tan \delta$.

### Formal Definition
The **Dissipation Factor** is the tangent of the loss angle $\delta$, where $\delta$ is the angle by which the current through a capacitive dielectric **leads** the pure capacitive current of an ideal lossless capacitor.

$$\boxed{\tan \delta = \dfrac{I_R}{I_C} = \dfrac{1}{\omega R_p C_p} = \omega R_s C_s}$$

where:
- $I_R$ = resistive (loss) current
- $I_C$ = capacitive current
- $R_p, C_p$ = parallel equivalent circuit of dielectric
- $R_s, C_s$ = series equivalent circuit

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** $\tan \delta$ represents the **fraction of reactive power that gets dissipated as heat** in the dielectric per cycle. A low $\tan \delta$ (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$) means a **high-quality insulator**; a high $\tan \delta$ indicates **deteriorating or contaminated insulation**.

### Capacitance of HV Apparatus
The capacitance of HV equipment (bushings, cables, transformer windings, CTs) ranges from a few **picofarads** (for small bushings) to several **microfarads** (for long cables). It must be measured precisely because:
- It determines the **charging current** drawn at operating voltage
- It controls the **resonant frequency** when connected to system inductance
- It influences **switching transients**

---

## 1.5 Summary of Measurement Methods (Module 2 Scope)

| Quantity | Preferred KTU Method |
|----------|---------------------|
| **Peak Voltage** | Chubb-Fortescue circuit, Sphere gap |
| **Harmonic Content** | Crest factor measurement, Distortion analyzer |
| **HV Current** | Rogowski coil, CT with burden resistor |
| **Dissipation Factor ($\tan \delta$)** | Schering Bridge |
| **Capacitance (HV)** | Schering Bridge, Transformer ratio-arm bridge |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chubb-Fortescue Peak Voltmeter Circuit

The Chubb-Fortescue method is the **standard precision method** for measuring the peak value of an AC high voltage in the laboratory.

### Circuit Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

1. The HV AC source is connected to a **standard, lossless capacitor** $C_1$.
2. A **smoothing (reservoir) capacitor** $C_2$ (with $C_2 \gg C_1$) is connected across the output.
3. A **two-way rectifier** (or two diodes in opposition) charges $C_2$ to the **peak value** of the AC voltage on **both half-cycles**.
4. A high-resistance **voltmeter V** (electrostatic or high-impedance DC voltmeter) reads the DC voltage across $C_2$, which equals the true peak $V_m$ (minus small diode drops).
5. Because both halves charge $C_2$ to the peak, the circuit effectively captures the **highest crest** even if the waveform is distorted by harmonics.

### Why This Method Is Special
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Chubb-Fortescue circuit measures the **TRUE peak** of a waveform **regardless of its harmonic content or waveform shape**. It does NOT assume a sinusoidal waveform. This makes it superior to rectifier-ammeter voltmeters that assume form factor = 1.11.

### Charging Equation Derivation (Logical Flow)

During each half-cycle, $C_2$ is charged through the diode to the instantaneous peak voltage. Let the source voltage be $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 1:** Current through diode flows when $v(t) > V_C$ (voltage across $C_2$).

**Step 2:** The diode becomes reverse-biased when $v(t)$ falls below $V_C$.

**Step 3:** Between conduction intervals, $C_2$ discharges slowly through the voltmeter resistance $R_V$.

**Step 4:** At steady state, the voltage across $C_2$ settles at:

$$V_{C_2} = V_m - V_D - \Delta V_{discharge}$$

where $V_D$ is diode forward drop ($\approx 0.6$ V per diode, negligible for HV) and $\Delta V_{discharge}$ is the small ripple.

**Step 5:** Reading of voltmeter $V$ gives peak value: $V = V_m$.

---

## 2.2 Sphere Gap as Peak Voltage Indicator

The **sphere gap** is the **primary standard** for HV peak voltage measurement. Two metallic spheres of identical diameter $D$ are separated by a precisely set gap $S$.

### Operating Principle
- Air between the spheres breaks down when the **peak voltage** across the gap reaches the **breakdown strength** of air under the given gap/diameter ratio.
- The breakdown voltage depends on **$S/D$** ratio and is read from **IS 1876 / IEC 60052 standard tables**.
- Once calibrated against a sphere gap, secondary standards (like Chubb-Fortescue) can be used.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The sphere gap measures the **PEAK value** of AC voltage, not RMS. Students often incorrectly assume it measures RMS. This is a common valuation error.

### Empirical Formula for Sphere Gap (Up to 1000 kV peak, for $0.05 \leq S/D \leq 0.5$)

$$V_{bd} = \dfrac{27.2 \, \delta \, S}{(1 + 0.54/S)}$$

where:
- $V_{bd}$ = breakdown voltage in kV (peak)
- $S$ = gap spacing in cm
- $\delta$ = air density factor = $\dfrac{3.92 \, b}{273 + T}$ (b in cm Hg, T in °C)

---

## 2.3 Current Measurement Using Rogowski Coil

### Working Principle
A Rogowski coil is an **air-cored toroidal coil** uniformly wound on a non-magnetic former, encircling the current-carrying conductor.

$$v_{coil}(t) = -M \dfrac{di(t)}{dt}$$

where $M$ = mutual inductance between the conductor and the coil.

Integrating:

$$i(t) = -\dfrac{1}{M} \int v_{coil}(t) \, dt$$

In practice, an **integrator circuit** (active op-amp with feedback capacitor) is used to recover $i(t)$ from $v_{coil}(t)$.

### Why Rogowski Coil Excels for HV
- **Electrically isolated** from HV conductor
- **Linear response** (no core saturation)
- Measures **AC, impulse, and transient** currents
- **Wide bandwidth** (Hz to MHz)

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with CT:** A Current Transformer (CT) uses a magnetic core and is suitable for **50/60 Hz AC only**. A Rogowski coil works for both AC and high-frequency transients.

---

## 2.4 Dielectric Loss — Equivalent Circuits

A real dielectric with loss can be represented in two equivalent forms:

### Parallel Representation
- $C_p$ = capacitance of lossless dielectric
- $R_p$ = resistance representing losses
- $\tan \delta = \dfrac{1}{\omega R_p C_p}$

### Series Representation
- $C_s$ = equivalent series capacitance
- $R_s$ = equivalent series resistance
- $\tan \delta = \omega R_s C_s$

### Power Loss in Dielectric

$$\boxed{P_{loss} = V^2 \omega C_p \tan \delta = V^2 \omega C_s \tan \delta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Power loss is **proportional to $V^2$**, $\omega$, $C$, and $\tan \delta$. This is why HV equipment generates enormous heat in degraded insulation — doubling voltage **quadruples** the dielectric loss.

---

## 2.5 The Schering Bridge — KTU High-Yield Circuit

The **Schering Bridge** is the **standard instrument** for measuring capacitance and dissipation factor of HV dielectrics.

### Bridge Circuit Description

| Arm | Component | Description |
|-----|-----------|-------------|
| Arm 1 (Unknown) | $C_x$, $R_x$ in parallel | Unknown dielectric sample (HV side) |
| Arm 2 (Standard) | $C_2$ (lossless) | Standard high-voltage compressed-gas capacitor |
| Arm 3 | $R_3$ | Pure non-inductive resistor |
| Arm 4 | $R_4$ in series with $C_4$ | Variable resistor and variable capacitor |

A **detector D** (vibration galvanometer / tuned amplifier) is connected between opposite nodes; the bridge is balanced by adjusting $R_4$ and $C_4$.

### Balance Condition — Final Equations

At balance, the detector current is zero. Applying the standard bridge balance condition:

$$\boxed{C_x = C_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}}$$

$$\boxed{\tan \delta_x = \omega R_4 C_4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is beautiful:** The unknown capacitance depends ONLY on the **ratio $R_4/R_3$** (precision resistors), and $\tan \delta$ depends on **$R_4$ and $C_4$** which are low-voltage components. The HV capacitor $C_2$ only appears in a ratio. Thus, the **HV capacitor does not need to be calibrated** — only the ratio arms.

### Wagner Earth Connection
A **Wagner earth** (auxiliary arm) is used to eliminate the effect of **stray capacitances** to ground, ensuring accurate balance even at high voltages.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms}$ | Pure sine peak | Ideal AC |
| 2 | $k_p = V_m / V_{rms}$ | Crest factor | Waveform purity |
| 3 | $k_f = V_{rms} / V_{avg}$ | Form factor | Rectifier design |
| 4 | $\text{THD} = \sqrt{\sum V_n^2}/V_1$ | Harmonic distortion | Power quality |
| 5 | $V_{bd} = 27.2 \, \delta \, S / (1 + 0.54/S)$ | Sphere gap breakdown | HV calibration |
| 6 | $v_{coil} = -M \cdot di/dt$ | Rogowski coil output | Current sensing |
| 7 | $\tan \delta = 1/(\omega R_p C_p)$ | Dissipation factor | Dielectric quality |
| 8 | $P = V^2 \omega C \tan \delta$ | Dielectric loss | Heating in insulation |
| 9 | $C_x = C_2 (R_4 / R_3)$ | Schering bridge C | Capacitance measurement |
| 10 | $\tan \delta_x = \omega R_4 C_4$ | Schering bridge tan δ | Loss measurement |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Measurement Matters |
|-------------|------------------------|
| **Power Transformer Bushings** | Aging increases $\tan \delta$; preventive diagnosis |
| **XLPE Underground Cables** | Water trees raise $\tan \delta$; failure prediction |
| **Capacitor Voltage Transformers (CVT)** | Capacitance must remain stable for metering accuracy |
| **Generators (Stator Winding)** | $\tan \delta$ test reveals inter-turn insulation moisture |
| **Surge Arresters** | Capacitance affects reference voltage grading |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Schering Bridge Balance Equations

### Setup
Consider a Wheatstone-bridge-type network with four arms:
- Arm AB (unknown): $C_x \parallel R_x$
- Arm BC (standard HV capacitor): $C_2$ (lossless)
- Arm CD: $R_3$ (pure resistor)
- Arm DA: $R_4$ in series with $C_4$
- Detector D between B and D
- AC source between A and C

### Step-by-Step Algebra

**Step 1:** Convert the unknown arm to a complex admittance (parallel form is easier here):

$$Y_x = \dfrac{1}{R_x} + j\omega C_x$$

The series arm DA has impedance:

$$Z_4 = R_4 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_4} = R_4 - \dfrac{j}{\omega C_4}$$

The standard capacitor BC has impedance:

$$Z_2 = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_2} = -\dfrac{j}{\omega C_2}$$

Arm CD: $Z_3 = R_3$

**Step 2:** At balance, no current flows through the detector, so the potentials at B and D are equal. The bridge balance condition in complex form is:

$$Z_1 Z_3 = Z_2 Z_4$$

Substituting:

$$Z_1 R_3 = Z_2 Z_4$$

$$Z_1 = \dfrac{Z_2 Z_4}{R_3}$$

**Step 3:** The unknown arm impedance $Z_1$ in parallel form is:

$$Z_1 = \dfrac{1}{Y_x} = \dfrac{R_x}{1 + j\omega R_x C_x}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $Z_2$ and $Z_4$:

$$Z_1 = \dfrac{\left(-\dfrac{j}{\omega C_2}\right)\left(R_4 - \dfrac{j}{\omega C_4}\right)}{R_3}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply the numerator (using $j \cdot j = -1$):

$$Z_2 Z_4 = -\dfrac{j R_4}{\omega C_2} + \dfrac{j^2}{\omega^2 C_2 C_4} = -\dfrac{1}{\omega^2 C_2 C_4} - \dfrac{j R_4}{\omega C_2}$$

**Step 6:** Divide by $R_3$:

$$Z_1 = -\dfrac{1}{\omega^2 R_3 C_2 C_4} - \dfrac{j R_4}{\omega R_3 C_2}$$

**Step 7:** Equate real and imaginary parts with $Z_1$ in parallel form. The parallel $R_x \parallel C_x$ written in Cartesian form is:

$$Z_1 = \dfrac{R_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2} - j\dfrac{\omega R_x^2 C_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2}$$

**Step 8:** Compare real parts:

$$\dfrac{R_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2} = -\dfrac{1}{\omega^2 R_3 C_2 C_4}$$

For small loss ($\omega R_x C_x \gg 1$):

$$R_x \approx \dfrac{-1}{\omega^2 R_3 C_2 C_4 \cdot (\omega R_x C_x)^2}$$

This implicit form is messy — but using the **approximation for low-loss dielectrics** where $\tan \delta \ll 1$:

**Step 9 (KTU Exam Shortcut):** The classical balance equations obtained by direct inspection of the bridge for low-loss dielectrics are:

$$\boxed{C_x = C_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}}$$

$$\boxed{\tan \delta_x = \omega R_4 C_4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $R_x$ disappears:** For low-loss dielectrics, $\tan \delta \ll 1$, so the resistive component of $Z_1$ dominates over the reactive component. The standard derivation (above) reduces exactly to these neat equations used in all KTU problems.

### Step 10: Numerical Example

**Problem:** A Schering bridge has $C_2 = 100$ pF, $R_3 = 1000 \, \Omega$, $R_4 = 318.3 \, \Omega$, $C_4 = 0.01 \, \mu F$. Supply frequency = 50 Hz. Find $C_x$ and $\tan \delta_x$.

**Solution:**

$$C_x = C_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3} = 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot \dfrac{318.3}{1000}$$

$$C_x = 31.83 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F} = 31.83 \, \text{pF}$$

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.16 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$\tan \delta_x = \omega R_4 C_4 = 314.16 \times 318.3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\tan \delta_x = 314.16 \times 318.3 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\tan \delta_x = 1.000 \times 10^{-3} = 0.001$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** This numerical result ($\tan \delta = 0.001$) is typical for **good-quality transformer oil or XLPE insulation**. Anything above $\tan \delta = 0.01$ usually indicates **deteriorated insulation**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Crest Factor for a Waveform with Harmonics

Consider a waveform $v(t)$ with fundamental $V_1$ and one dominant harmonic $V_3$ (3rd harmonic):

$$v(t) = V_1 \sin(\omega t) + V_3 \sin(3\omega t + \phi)$$

**Step 1:** RMS value:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{V_1^2}{2} + \dfrac{V_3^2}{2}}$$

**Step 2:** Peak value: Found by setting $dv/dt = 0$ and solving for $t$. For $V_3 \ll V_1$, the peak occurs near $t \approx \pi/(2\omega)$. Using a Taylor expansion:

$$V_m \approx V_1 + V_3 \sin(3\pi/2 + \phi) = V_1 - V_3 \cos(\phi)$$

**Step 3:** Crest factor:

$$k_p = \dfrac{V_m}{V_{rms}} = \dfrac{V_1 - V_3 \cos\phi}{\sqrt{(V_1^2 + V_3^2)/2}}$$

For small $V_3/V_1$ ratio $\alpha = V_3/V_1$:

$$k_p \approx \sqrt{2}(1 - \alpha \cos\phi)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The presence of a **3rd harmonic reduces the peak value** when its phase $\phi = 0$ (in phase with fundamental at peak). But for $\phi = \pi$, it **increases** the peak. This explains why **harmonic phase matters** in HV breakdown testing — students often forget phase and only consider magnitude.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameters on $\tan \delta$

| Parameter Increased | Effect on $\tan \delta$ | Physical Reason |
|---------------------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| Voltage (in non-linear range) | Increases sharply | Partial discharge onset |
| Frequency | Usually decreases (for dipole loss) | Relaxation dispersion |
| Temperature | Increases then decreases (peak at $\omega \tau = 1$) | Debye relaxation |
| Moisture content | Increases significantly | Conductive paths in insulation |
| Aging | Increases | Polymer chain scission, voids |
| Contamination (particles) | Increases | Local field enhancement |

---

## 3.4 Detailed Worked Example — Peak Voltmeter Reading

**Problem:** A Chubb-Fortescue peak voltmeter reads 100 kV on the DC voltmeter across $C_2$. The HV source is 50 Hz with a 3rd harmonic content of 5% of fundamental. What is the true peak of the waveform?

**Given:**
- Reading: $V_m = 100$ kV (peak)
- $V_3 = 0.05 V_1$
- Assume worst-case phase alignment: $\phi = \pi$ (harmonic adds to peak)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** The voltmeter reads the **true peak** of the distorted waveform directly, NOT the fundamental peak.

**Step 2:** Let $V_{fundamental\_peak} = V_1$.

$$V_m = V_1 + V_3 = V_1 (1 + 0.05) = 1.05 \, V_1$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $V_1$:

$$V_1 = \dfrac{100}{1.05} = 95.24 \, \text{kV (peak)}$$

**Step 4:** RMS of fundamental:

$$V_{1,rms} = \dfrac{95.24}{\sqrt{2}} = 67.34 \, \text{kV}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Students who wrongly assume $V_m = \sqrt{2} V_{rms}$ without accounting for harmonics will get $V_{rms} = 70.7$ kV instead of 67.34 kV — a **4.7% error** which is significant in HV engineering.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] 
**Define Dissipation Factor (tan δ). Why is it considered a key indicator of insulation quality in high voltage equipment?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Dissipation Factor** ($\tan \delta$) of a dielectric is defined as the tangent of the loss angle $\delta$, which is the angle by which the current through a real (lossy) dielectric leads the pure capacitive current of an ideal lossless capacitor.

$$\tan \delta = \dfrac{I_R}{I_C} = \dfrac{1}{\omega R_p C_p}$$

It is a key indicator of insulation quality because:
1. **Direct measure of dielectric loss**: Higher $\tan \delta$ means more energy dissipated as heat per cycle.
2. **Sensitive to deterioration**: Moisture, contamination, voids, and aging cause $\tan \delta$ to rise sharply.
3. **Non-destructive**: The test is performed at low voltage on equipment, allowing condition assessment without damage.
4. **Typical benchmark**: $\tan \delta < 0.005$ indicates good insulation; $> 0.01$ indicates deterioration.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Explain why a sphere gap is used for measuring peak voltage in high voltage testing.**

**Model Answer:**

A **sphere gap** consists of two identical metallic spheres separated by a precise gap distance. It is used to measure **peak voltage** because:

1. **Breakdown depends on peak value**: Air breakdown between spheres occurs when the **instantaneous peak** of the applied voltage exceeds the dielectric strength of air at that gap spacing. Thus, the breakdown voltage corresponds to the **crest value**, not RMS.

2. **Primary standard**: The sphere gap serves as the **primary reference standard** for HV measurement. Standard tables (IS 1876 / IEC 60052) directly give peak voltage versus gap distance and sphere diameter.

3. **Independent of waveform**: Unlike rectifier-type voltmeters that assume a sinusoidal form factor, the sphere gap works correctly for distorted waveforms because breakdown is governed only by the **peak instantaneous voltage**.

4. **Wide range**: With appropriate sphere diameters, it can measure from a few kV up to several MV.

**Limitation**: It is **destructive** — each measurement consumes the test object (the gap breaks down), so it is mainly used for **calibration**, not routine testing.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **Chubb-Fortescue peak voltmeter**. Discuss how it provides the true peak value even for distorted waveforms containing harmonics. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **Schering Bridge** is used to measure the capacitance and dissipation factor of a HV bushing. The bridge arms are: Standard capacitor $C_2 = 110$ pF, ratio arm $R_3 = 1000 \, \Omega$, and at balance $R_4 = 252.6 \, \Omega$, $C_4 = 0.0085 \, \mu F$. Supply frequency = 50 Hz. Calculate **(i)** the capacitance $C_x$ of the bushing and **(ii)** the dissipation factor $\tan \delta_x$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Chubb-Fortescue peak voltmeter** is a precision instrument for measuring the **true peak value** of an AC high voltage, regardless of harmonic distortion.

**Circuit Description:**

The circuit consists of:
- A standard, lossless HV capacitor $C_1$ connected across the HV source.
- A smoothing (reservoir) capacitor $C_2$ connected across a high-impedance DC voltmeter V, with $C_2 \gg C_1$.
- A **double-diode rectifier** arrangement (two diodes in anti-parallel) connected between $C_1$ and $C_2$.

**Working:**

1. During the **positive half-cycle**, the upper diode conducts and charges $C_2$ to the instantaneous positive peak of the source voltage (minus a small diode drop).
2. During the **negative half-cycle**, the lower diode conducts and attempts to charge $C_2$ to the negative peak.
3. Because $C_2$ retains charge, only the **highest absolute peak** is registered on the DC voltmeter.
4. Between successive cycles, $C_2$ discharges very slowly through the high-impedance voltmeter, holding its charge near the peak value.

**Why it gives the true peak for distorted waveforms:**

- For a **distorted** waveform $v(t) = V_1 \sin\omega t + V_3 \sin 3\omega t + \ldots$, the maximum instantaneous value during a cycle is the **actual crest**, which may be higher or lower than $\sqrt{2} V_{rms}$.
- The Chubb-Fortescue circuit captures **whatever** value $C_2$ reaches, which is exactly this crest.
- Unlike RMS voltmeters (which use rectifier + form factor correction), it does **not assume sinusoidal shape**.
- Therefore, it is **directly traceable to the peak** and is the most accurate method for laboratory HV measurement.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $C_2 = 110$ pF $= 110 \times 10^{-12}$ F
- $R_3 = 1000 \, \Omega$
- $R_4 = 252.6 \, \Omega$
- $C_4 = 0.0085 \, \mu F = 8.5 \times 10^{-9}$ F
- $f = 50$ Hz $\Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.16$ rad/s

**(i) Capacitance $C_x$:**

Using the Schering bridge balance equation:

$$C_x = C_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}$$

$$C_x = 110 \times 10^{-12} \cdot \dfrac{252.6}{1000}$$

$$C_x = 110 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.2526$$

$$\boxed{C_x = 27.79 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F} = 27.79 \, \text{pF}}$$

**(ii) Dissipation factor $\tan \delta_x$:**

$$\tan \delta_x = \omega R_4 C_4$$

$$\tan \delta_x = 314.16 \times 252.6 \times 8.5 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\tan \delta_x = 314.16 \times 252.6 \times 8.5 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\tan \delta_x = 6.747 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\tan \delta_x \approx 6.75 \times 10^{-4} = 0.000675}$$

**Interpretation:** This very low $\tan \delta$ value indicates the bushing insulation is in **excellent condition** (typical for new porcelain bushings at 50 Hz).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the principle and operation of a **Schering Bridge** for measuring capacitance and dissipation factor of a high-voltage dielectric. Derive the balance equations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **Crest Factor** and **Form Factor**. A distorted voltage waveform has fundamental component of 70 kV (RMS) and a 3rd harmonic component of 14 kV (RMS) in phase opposition with the fundamental. Calculate **(i)** the RMS value of the distorted waveform, **(ii)** the peak value, and **(iii)** the crest factor. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Schering Bridge — Principle:**

The Schering Bridge is a four-arm AC bridge used for precise measurement of capacitance and dissipation factor of HV dielectrics. It is an **equal-arm-ratio** bridge in which the unknown dielectric forms one arm and a lossless standard capacitor forms the opposite arm.

**Circuit Diagram (textual description):**

```
        A ─────────────── C
        │                 │
       Cx||Rx            C2
        │                 │
        B ──[Detector]─── D
        │                 │
        │                R3
        │                 │
        D ─────────────── C
        R4 + C4 (series)
```

**Arms:**
- Arm AB (HV side): Unknown — $C_x$ in parallel with $R_x$
- Arm BC: Standard — Lossless capacitor $C_2$
- Arm CD: Pure resistor $R_3$
- Arm DA: Variable resistor $R_4$ in series with variable capacitor $C_4$
- Detector (vibration galvanometer): Between B and D
- AC source: Between A and C
- Wagner earth: Connected at B and D to ground via auxiliary arms

**Balance Condition:**

At balance, no current flows through the detector. The standard bridge condition is:

$$Z_1 Z_3 = Z_2 Z_4$$

With:
- $Z_1$ = parallel combination of $R_x$ and $C_x$
- $Z_2 = 1/(j\omega C_2)$
- $Z_3 = R_3$
- $Z_4 = R_4 + 1/(j\omega C_4)$

After algebraic manipulation (shown in Section 3.1) and applying the low-loss approximation ($\tan \delta \ll 1$):

$$\boxed{C_x = C_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}}$$

$$\boxed{\tan \delta_x = \omega R_4 C_4}$$

**Significance:** Note that the unknown $R_x$ does **not** appear in the final equations — only $R_4, R_3, C_4$, and $C_2$ do. Thus, calibration of the HV capacitor $C_2$ is not critical, making this bridge highly practical for HV measurements.

**Wagner Earth:** Eliminates stray capacitance errors at high voltages by ensuring both B and D nodes are at ground potential when balanced.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Definitions:**

- **Crest Factor (Peak Factor)** $k_p = V_m / V_{rms}$
- **Form Factor** $k_f = V_{rms} / V_{avg}$

**Given:**
- Fundamental RMS: $V_{1,rms} = 70$ kV
- 3rd harmonic RMS: $V_{3,rms} = 14$ kV
- Phase relation: 3rd harmonic in phase opposition with fundamental (relative phase = 180°)

**(i) RMS Value of Distorted Waveform:**

RMS values are added in quadrature (regardless of phase):

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_{1,rms}^2 + V_{3,rms}^2}$$

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{70^2 + 14^2} = \sqrt{4900 + 196} = \sqrt{5096}$$

$$V_{rms} = 71.39 \, \text{kV}$$

**(ii) Peak Value:**

Convert to peak amplitudes:

$$V_1 = \sqrt{2} \times 70 = 98.99 \, \text{kV}$$

$$V_3 = \sqrt{2} \times 14 = 19.80 \, \text{kV}$$

Since the 3rd harmonic is in phase **opposition** (180°), at the fundamental peak (which occurs at $\omega t = \pi/2$), the 3rd harmonic is at $3\omega t = 3\pi/2$, with value $V_3 \sin(3\pi/2 + \pi) = V_3 \sin(5\pi/2) = V_3 \sin(\pi/2) = +V_3$.

Wait, careful: if the 3rd harmonic is in **phase opposition**, we have $v_3(t) = -V_3 \sin(3\omega t)$.

At the peak of fundamental ($t = \pi/2\omega$):
$$v_1(\pi/2\omega) = V_1 = 98.99 \, \text{kV}$$
$$v_3(\pi/2\omega) = -V_3 \sin(3\pi/2) = -V_3 \times (-1) = +V_3 = +19.80 \, \text{kV}$$

So total peak:
$$V_m = V_1 + V_3 = 98.99 + 19.80 = 118.79 \, \text{kV}$$

**(iii) Crest Factor:**

$$k_p = \dfrac{V_m}{V_{rms}} = \dfrac{118.79}{71.39}$$

$$\boxed{k_p = 1.664}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** For a pure sine, $k_p = \sqrt{2} = 1.414$. Here $k_p = 1.664 > 1.414$, indicating significant harmonic distortion. This **higher crest factor** increases the stress on insulation during testing.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Sphere gap vs RMS voltmeter confusion:** Writing that sphere gap measures RMS — it measures PEAK. (Lose 2-3 marks)
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the diagonal layout of Schering bridge:** Drawing the wrong arms opposite each other. Detector must be across the diagonal of the bridge. (Lose 1 mark)
> 
> 3. **Missing Wagner Earth:** Schering Bridge problems without Wagner Earth → examiner deducts for stray capacitance not being accounted for. (Lose 1 mark)
> 
> 4. **Phase of harmonics ignored in peak calculation:** Computing $V_m$ as $V_1 + V_3$ regardless of phase. Must check whether harmonic **adds** or **subtracts** at the crest. (Lose 2 marks)
> 
> 5. **Unit errors:** Mixing pF with $\mu F$ or expressing $C_4$ in nF instead of F in $\tan \delta$ formula. (Lose 1-2 marks)
> 
> 6. **Crest factor assumed = $\sqrt{2}$ even for distorted waveforms:** This is only valid for pure sinusoids. Always compute $V_{rms}$ separately. (Lose 2 marks)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Peak Voltage & Harmonics
- ✅ **Peak voltage** governs insulation breakdown, NOT RMS.
- ✅ **Crest Factor** $k_p = V_m / V_{rms} = \sqrt{2}$ for pure sine only.
- ✅ **Form Factor** $k_f = V_{rms} / V_{avg} = 1.11$ for pure sine only.
- ✅ **THD** quantifies harmonic content; 3rd, 5th, 7th harmonics dominate in HV.
- ✅ RMS is **independent of phase** of harmonics; peak **depends on phase**.

### Chubb-Fortescue Peak Voltmeter
- ✅ Uses two-way rectifier + smoothing capacitor + DC voltmeter.
- ✅ Captures true peak irrespective of waveform shape.
- ✅ Standard HV capacitor $C_1$ must be **lossless**.

### Sphere Gap
- ✅ Measures **peak** voltage via breakdown of air.
- ✅ Standard reference per **IS 1876 / IEC 60052**.
- ✅ Destructive — used mainly for calibration.

### HV Current Measurement
- ✅ **Rogowski coil**: $v_{coil} = -M \cdot di/dt$; non-intrusive, wide bandwidth.
- ✅ **CT**: Magnetic-core based; only for power frequency AC.
- ✅ **Integrator**: Recovers $i(t)$ from $v_{coil}(t)$.

### Dissipation Factor ($\tan \delta$)
- ✅ $\tan \delta = I_R / I_C = 1/(\omega R_p C_p) = \omega R_s C_s$
- ✅ Typical good insulation: $\tan \delta \sim 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$.
- ✅ Deteriorated insulation: $\tan \delta > 10^{-2}$.
- ✅ Sensitive to moisture, contamination, aging, voids.

### Schering Bridge (KTU Favourite)
- ✅ Balance equations: $C_x = C_2 (R_4/R_3)$, $\tan \delta_x = \omega R_4 C_4$.
- ✅ Detector: vibration galvanometer or tuned amplifier.
- ✅ **Wagner Earth** eliminates stray capacitance to ground.
- ✅ Standard HV capacitor $C_2$: usually **compressed gas** (SF₆) or **vacuum**.
- ✅ Bridge operates at high voltage (5 kV to 100 kV typical).

### Engineering Significance
- ✅ Power loss in dielectric: $P = V^2 \omega C \tan \delta$.
- ✅ Used for **predictive maintenance** of transformers, cables, bushings, generators.
- ✅ Increasing $\tan \delta$ → increasing heat → thermal runaway → insulation failure.

### Quick Formula Memory Aid
- **C**apacitance $C_x$ depends on **C**apacitor $C_2$ and **R**esistor **R**atio ($R_4/R_3$).
- **T**angent delta depends on $\omega$, $R_4$, and **C**apacitor $C_4$ — "**T**an **ωRC**".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Insulation Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Insulation Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Insulation Resistance — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insulation Resistance (IR)** is the ohmic resistance offered by an electrical insulating material, when a small DC voltage is applied between its conducting parts and earth (or between two conductors), thereby limiting the leakage current through and over the surface of the dielectric.

In the strict KTU 2024 syllabus context, insulation resistance is **not** a single lumped property of the material — it is the *combined* opposition offered by **two distinct conduction paths** that exist simultaneously in any practical insulating system:

1. **Volume Resistance (Rᵥ)** — Resistance of the *bulk* dielectric material to current passing *through* its body.
2. **Surface Resistance (Rₛ)** — Resistance of the *outer skin* of the dielectric to leakage currents creeping *along* its surface.

The **Total Insulation Resistance** is therefore the **parallel combination** of these two paths:

$$R_t = \frac{R_v \cdot R_s}{R_v + R_s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Whenever the term *insulation resistance* appears in a University question, the student is *expected* to distinguish between volume and surface components and explain them with equations, not just quote a single numerical value.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **concrete water tank** with a steel inlet pipe at the top and an outlet at the bottom:

- **Volume resistance (Rᵥ)** is like the **water leaking through cracks *inside* the concrete wall**. The water travels straight through the bulk of the material.
- **Surface resistance (Rₛ)** is like the **water trickling down the *outer painted surface* of the tank**. It never enters the wall — it just slips along the outside, picking up dust and grime.
- The **total water lost** from the tank is the **sum (parallel equivalent) of both leaks**, because they occur independently and simultaneously.
- If you paint the outside (clean, dry surface), **Rₛ increases**, reducing surface leakage. If you increase wall thickness, **Rᵥ increases**, reducing volume leakage.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exam:** *"Through is Volume, Along is Surface"* — *T-V, A-S* (TV channel and *AS* surface).

## 1.3 Why Insulation Resistance Matters in High Voltage Engineering

| Application | Consequence of Low IR | Required Value |
|---|---|---|
| Power Cables | Leakage current → dielectric heating → failure | > 1000 MΩ |
| Transformer Windings | Earth fault, loss of life | > 100 MΩ |
| Bushings | Flashover, line-to-ground fault | > 500 MΩ (per kV) |
| Rotating Machines | Winding short to body | > 5 MΩ per kV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard rule of thumb followed by Indian electricity utilities (per **IS 732** and **CBIP manuals**) is:
> **IR (in MΩ) ≥ Rated Voltage (in kV) × 1000** — for most HV equipment before energising.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Volume Resistivity (ρᵥ)** — measured in **Ω·m** (SI) or **Ω·cm** (practical).
- **Surface Resistivity (ρₛ)** — measured in **Ω** (dimensionless when expressed per square).
- **Test DC Voltage** for IR measurement is typically **500 V / 1000 V / 2500 V / 5000 V DC** (per IS 1356 / IEEE 43).
- **Acceptance Criterion (IEEE 43 for rotating machines):** Minimum **5 MΩ per kV** of rated voltage at **40 °C**, corrected for temperature.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — The Two-Path Model

Any solid/liquid insulation sandwiched between two electrodes (a *test cell*) offers two parallel current paths when DC is applied:

### Path 1 — Volume Conduction (Through the Bulk)

A small current Iᵥ flows **straight through** the dielectric from the high-voltage electrode to the earth electrode.

$$I_v = \frac{V \cdot A}{\rho_v \cdot t}$$

where:
- V = applied DC voltage
- A = electrode cross-sectional area
- ρᵥ = volume resistivity of material
- t = thickness of insulation

### Path 2 — Surface Conduction (Along the Surface)

A small current Iₛ flows **along the dielectric surface**, primarily driven by:
- Surface moisture (humidity)
- Surface contamination (dust, salt, oil)
- Surface charge accumulation

$$I_s = \frac{V \cdot l}{\rho_s \cdot d}$$

where:
- l = length between electrodes along surface
- d = distance between electrodes across surface
- ρₛ = surface resistivity of material (in Ω per square)

> [!NOTE]
> **Surface resistivity** is independent of geometry — that is why it has units of **Ω** (or Ω/square). A 1 cm × 1 cm square has the same ρₛ as a 10 cm × 10 cm square if made of the same material.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

### Why DC and not AC?
- AC measurements include capacitive charging current, which masks the tiny leakage current.
- DC allows only true conduction current → true ohmic resistance can be extracted.

### Why is current measured after some time?
- The instantaneous current includes **capacitive (geometric)**, **absorption (polarisation)**, and **conduction (leakage)** components.
- After ~1 minute (for IR) or ~10 minutes (for PI test), only the **true leakage current** remains.

### Why parallel combination?
- The volume and surface paths are physically **separate conduction paths** between the same two terminals → they obey Kirchhoff's current law → resistances add in parallel.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volume Resistance | $R_v = \dfrac{\rho_v \cdot t}{A}$ | Ω (Ω) | t = thickness; A = electrode area |
| 2 | Surface Resistance | $R_s = \dfrac{\rho_s \cdot d}{l}$ | Ω (Ω) | d = gap; l = electrode length |
| 3 | Total Insulation Resistance | $R_t = \dfrac{R_v \cdot R_s}{R_v + R_s}$ | Ω (Ω) | Parallel combination |
| 4 | Volume Current | $I_v = \dfrac{V}{R_v}$ | A | Conduction component |
| 5 | Surface Current | $I_s = \dfrac{V}{R_s}$ | A | Surface leakage |
| 6 | Dielectric Absorption Ratio (DAR) | $DAR = \dfrac{R_{60s}}{R_{30s}}$ | — | Needs ≥ 1.0; healthy > 1.4 |
| 7 | Polarisation Index (PI) | $PI = \dfrac{R_{10min}}{R_{1min}}$ | — | Healthy ≥ 2.0; poor < 1.0 |
| 8 | Temperature Correction | $R_{T_2} = R_{T_1} \cdot k^{(T_2 - T_1)/10}$ | Ω (Ω) | k ≈ 0.5–0.8; doubles every ~10 °C drop |
| 9 | Geometric Capacitance (C₉) | $C_g = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$ | F | Sets initial charging current |
| 10 | Time Constant (τ) | $\tau = R_t \cdot C_g$ | s | Determines discharge behaviour |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion Note:** 1 MΩ = 10⁶ Ω. KTU often gives IR in **MΩ** — convert to **Ω** *before* plugging into time-constant formulas, or convert τ to **μs/ms** as appropriate.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of IR Test |
|---|---|
| **Power Cables** | Pre-energising check; identifies moisture ingress in XLPE/oil-paper cables |
| **Power Transformers** | Winding-to-earth IR; detects wet or contaminated oil |
| **Rotating Machines** | PI test predicts insulation dryness without dismantling |
| **Bushings** | Detects surface contamination pre-flashover |
| **Quality Control (Manufacturers)** | Incoming raw material acceptance for PVC, XLPE, epoxy resin |
| **Field Maintenance** | Routine condition monitoring per IS 1356 & IEEE 43 |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volume Resistance From First Principles

### Setup
Consider a slab of dielectric of thickness *t* and uniform cross-sectional area *A*, placed between two parallel-plate electrodes. Apply a DC voltage V across it. A uniform electric field E is established inside the bulk.

### Step 1 — Define Electric Field
For a parallel-plate geometry under DC:

$$E = \frac{V}{t}$$

The electric field is assumed uniform (no edge effects). This is the standard textbook simplification accepted by KTU examiners.

### Step 2 — Define Current Density
Ohm's law in point form inside a conductor/poor dielectric:

$$J = \sigma \cdot E$$

where σ is the volume conductivity of the dielectric. For a good insulator, σ is extremely small (≈ 10⁻¹⁴ S/m for polyethylene).

### Step 3 — Express σ in Terms of ρᵥ
By definition, volume resistivity and volume conductivity are reciprocals:

$$\sigma = \frac{1}{\rho_v}$$

Therefore:

$$J = \frac{E}{\rho_v} = \frac{V}{\rho_v \cdot t}$$

### Step 4 — Total Current Through Area A
Current is current density integrated over area (uniform here):

$$I_v = J \cdot A = \frac{V \cdot A}{\rho_v \cdot t}$$

### Step 5 — Apply Ohm's Law (Macro Form)
Voltage = Current × Resistance:

$$V = I_v \cdot R_v$$

Equate and solve for Rᵥ:

$$V = \frac{V \cdot A}{\rho_v \cdot t} \cdot R_v$$

Cancel V from both sides:

$$1 = \frac{A}{\rho_v \cdot t} \cdot R_v$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{R_v = \frac{\rho_v \cdot t}{A}}$$

> This is the **fundamental expression for volume resistance** in any KTU answer.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Surface Resistance From First Principles

### Setup
Two parallel strip electrodes of length *l* (into the page) are placed on a flat dielectric surface, separated by a gap *d*. Surface leakage current flows along the surface film.

### Step 1 — Surface Current Density
Define surface current density as current per unit width flowing along the surface. For a uniform film:

$$J_s = \frac{I_s}{l}$$

### Step 2 — Tangential Electric Field
The tangential field driving the surface current:

$$E_t = \frac{V}{d}$$

### Step 3 — Surface Ohm's Law
Surface current density is proportional to tangential electric field, with surface resistivity ρₛ as the proportionality:

$$J_s = \frac{E_t}{\rho_s} = \frac{V}{\rho_s \cdot d}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Surface Current

$$I_s = J_s \cdot l = \frac{V \cdot l}{\rho_s \cdot d}$$

### Step 5 — Apply Ohm's Law

$$V = I_s \cdot R_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_s = \frac{V}{I_s}$$

Substitute Iₛ:

$$R_s = \frac{V \cdot \rho_s \cdot d}{V \cdot l} = \frac{\rho_s \cdot d}{l}$$

$$\boxed{R_s = \frac{\rho_s \cdot d}{l}}$$

> This is the **fundamental expression for surface resistance** in any KTU answer.

### Physical Interpretation of ρₛ Units
Even though Rₛ has units of ohms, the formula shows:

$$[R_s] = \frac{[\rho_s] \cdot [d]}{[l]}$$

For ρₛ to keep units as **ohms**, d/l must be **dimensionless**. Therefore ρₛ is expressed in **Ω per square** (a geometric invariant). This is why surface resistivity is independent of electrode dimensions — only the *ratio* d/l matters.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Insulation Resistance as Parallel Combination

The volume path and surface path are **two distinct, independent current paths** between the same pair of terminals (high-voltage electrode and earth electrode). Therefore, by Kirchhoff's Current Law:

$$I_{total} = I_v + I_s$$

Apply Ohm's law to each path:

$$I_v = \frac{V}{R_v}, \quad I_s = \frac{V}{R_s}$$

Sum:

$$I_{total} = \frac{V}{R_v} + \frac{V}{R_s} = V \left( \frac{1}{R_v} + \frac{1}{R_s} \right)$$

But also by definition:

$$I_{total} = \frac{V}{R_t}$$

Equate:

$$\frac{V}{R_t} = V \left( \frac{1}{R_v} + \frac{1}{R_s} \right)$$

Cancel V:

$$\frac{1}{R_t} = \frac{1}{R_v} + \frac{1}{R_s}$$

Take reciprocal:

$$\boxed{R_t = \frac{R_v \cdot R_s}{R_v + R_s}}$$

### Limiting Case Analysis

| Condition | Result | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Rₛ ≫ Rᵥ (clean, dry surface) | Rₜ ≈ Rᵥ | Surface path negligible — only bulk conduction matters |
| Rᵥ ≫ Rₛ (wet, dirty surface) | Rₜ ≈ Rₛ | Surface leakage dominates — typical field condition |
| Rₛ = Rᵥ | Rₜ = Rᵥ/2 | Both paths equally contribute |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In field conditions, the *surface path almost always dominates* because humidity, salt, and pollution make Rₛ orders of magnitude smaller than Rᵥ. Hence **drying/cleaning** insulation restores IR far more effectively than replacing the bulk material.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Polarisation Index (PI) and Dielectric Absorption Ratio (DAR)

When a DC voltage is suddenly applied to an insulating material, the total current i(t) consists of three components:

$$i(t) = i_c(t) + i_a(t) + i_l$$

where:
- **i_c(t)** = capacitive (geometric) charging current — decays in milliseconds
- **i_a(t)** = absorption current (polarisation relaxation) — decays in seconds to minutes
- **i_l** = true leakage (conduction) current — constant in time

For a fixed applied voltage V, IR(t) = V / i(t). As time increases, i(t) decreases, so IR(t) increases and asymptotically approaches V/i_l.

### Step 1 — Define R at Standard Times

$$R_{1\,min} = \frac{V}{i(60s)}, \quad R_{10\,min} = \frac{V}{i(600s)}$$

### Step 2 — Polarisation Index

$$PI = \frac{R_{10\,min}}{R_{1\,min}}$$

### Step 3 — Dielectric Absorption Ratio (Quick PI)

$$DAR = \frac{R_{60s}}{R_{30s}}$$

### Interpretation Table

| PI Value | Condition | Action |
|---|---|---|
| < 1.0 | Dangerous (conduction dominates, no absorption) | Replace insulation |
| 1.0 – 2.0 | Questionable | Investigate further |
| ≥ 2.0 | Healthy (good absorption) | Energise |
| ≥ 4.0 | Excellent (very dry, good grade) | Excellent |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Test Setup with Guard Electrode (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The figure below shows the standard three-terminal electrode configuration used for measuring volume and surface resistance independently, conforming to **ASTM D257 / IEC 60093**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HV[HV Electrode]:::highV
    DUT[Dielectric Sample]:::sample
    GND[Earth Electrode]:::earth
    GUARD[Guard Electrode]:::guardNode
    MEGGER[Megger DC Source and Meter]:::meter
    IVOL[Volume Current Iv]:::volLine
    ISURF[Surface Current Is]:::surfLine

    HV -- Active face --> DUT
    DUT -- Opposite face --> GND
    HV --- MEGGER
    GND --- MEGGER
    GUARD ---|Routes Is away| MEGGER
    DUT -. Surface film .-> GUARD
    GUARD -. Eliminates Is from Iv measurement .-> IVOL

    classDef highV fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#7a0d0d,color:#ffffff
    classDef sample fill:#ffd166,stroke:#a05a00,color:#1a1a1a
    classDef earth fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#035d44,color:#ffffff
    classDef guardNode fill:#118ab2,stroke:#0a4a66,color:#ffffff
    classDef meter fill:#9b5de5,stroke:#3d1a78,color:#ffffff
    classDef volLine fill:none,stroke:#003049,stroke-width:2px
    classDef surfLine fill:none,stroke:#d62828,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray:5 5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Role of the Guard Electrode:** By connecting the guard ring to the **same potential as the HV electrode**, the surface current Iₛ is *bypassed directly* to the meter return path and does **not** flow through the measurement ammeter. The ammeter therefore reads **only** the volume current Iᵥ → Rᵥ alone can be measured. Conversely, to measure Rₛ alone, the guard is left disconnected and the meter reads Iᵥ + Iₛ combined.

## 4.2 Equivalent Circuit of Insulating Material

The figure below shows the canonical two-branch R-C equivalent circuit used by KTU to model insulation under DC testing.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TERM1((Terminal A - HV)):::term
    TERM2((Terminal B - Earth)):::term

    subgraph VOL_PATH[Volume Branch Path]
        RV[Volume Resistance Rv]:::rvStyle
        CV[Geometric Capacitance Cg]:::cvStyle
        RV --- CV
    end

    subgraph SURF_PATH[Surface Branch Path]
        RS[Surface Resistance Rs]:::rsStyle
        CS[Surface Capacitance Cs]:::csStyle
        RS --- CS
    end

    TERM1 --- VOL_PATH
    TERM1 --- SURF_PATH
    VOL_PATH --- TERM2
    SURF_PATH --- TERM2

    classDef term fill:#1d3557,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    classDef rvStyle fill:#e63946,stroke:#6a0f1a,color:#ffffff
    classDef cvStyle fill:#f1faee,stroke:#1d3557,color:#1d3557
    classDef rsStyle fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#1d3557,color:#1d3557
    classDef csStyle fill:#457b9d,stroke:#1d3557,color:#ffffff
```

> [!TIP]
> When DC is applied for a long time (≫ τ = RₜC), all capacitors charge fully → no current flows through them → only the resistors carry current → true ohmic Rₜ is measured. This is the basis of the **1-minute** and **10-minute** IR readings.

## 4.3 Decision Flow — How to Interpret an IR Test Result

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Perform DC IR Test]):::startNode
    READ60[Read IR at 60 seconds]:::readNode
    READ600[Read IR at 600 seconds]:::readNode
    PI{Calculate PI = R600 / R60}:::decision
    HIGH{IR meets\nIS 732 / IEEE 43 minimum?}:::decision
    PI_HIGH[PI greater than or equal 2.0]:::good
    PI_LOW[PI less than 1.0]:::bad
    CLEAN[Dry and Clean Surface]:::action
    RETEST[Retest after Cleaning]:::action
    PASS[Accept and Energise]:::success
    INVESTIGATE[Further Investigation: Oil Test, PD Test]:::warn
    REPLACE[Replace or Recondition Insulation]:::bad

    START --> READ60
    READ60 --> READ600
    READ600 --> PI
    PI -->|PI high| PI_HIGH
    PI -->|PI low| PI_LOW
    PI_HIGH --> HIGH
    HIGH -->|Yes| PASS
    HIGH -->|No| CLEAN
    CLEAN --> RETEST
    RETEST --> READ60
    PI_LOW --> INVESTIGATE
    INVESTIGATE --> REPLACE

    classDef startNode fill:#264653,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    classDef readNode fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#0a3a36,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#7a5d0e,color:#1a1a1a
    classDef good fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#035d44,color:#ffffff
    classDef bad fill:#ef476f,stroke:#7a1530,color:#ffffff
    classDef action fill:#118ab2,stroke:#0a4a66,color:#ffffff
    classDef success fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#035d44,color:#ffffff
    classDef warn fill:#f4a261,stroke:#7a3d0e,color:#1a1a1a
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Insulation Resistance. Distinguish between volume resistance and surface resistance.   [3 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]**

#### Model Answer:

**Insulation Resistance (IR)** is the ohmic resistance offered by an electrical insulating material when a small DC voltage is applied between its conducting parts, limiting the leakage current through and over the dielectric.

The two components are:

| Aspect | Volume Resistance (Rᵥ) | Surface Resistance (Rₛ) |
|---|---|---|
| Current path | Through the bulk of the material | Along the outer surface |
| Driving field | Internal E = V/t | Tangential Eₜ = V/d |
| Affected by | Material composition, temperature, voids | Moisture, dust, pollution on surface |
| Formula | Rᵥ = ρᵥ·t ⁄ A | Rₛ = ρₛ·d ⁄ l |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write *surface resistance = ρₛ × l ⁄ d* with dimensions inverted. **Remember:** Rₛ is *directly proportional to the gap d* (longer gap → more resistance) and *inversely proportional to the electrode length l* (longer electrode → more parallel paths → lower resistance).

---

### Q2. What is the Polarisation Index (PI)? State its significance in assessing insulation health.   [3 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

#### Model Answer:

**Polarisation Index (PI)** is defined as the ratio of the 10-minute insulation resistance to the 1-minute insulation resistance:

$$PI = \frac{R_{10\,min}}{R_{1\,min}}$$

**Significance:**

- It is **independent of temperature** to a large extent (since both R₁₀ and R₁ are affected similarly), making it more reliable than absolute IR for condition assessment.
- A **high PI (≥ 2.0)** indicates a dry, clean, well-aged insulation with healthy polarisation behaviour — safe to energise.
- A **low PI (< 1.0)** indicates conduction-dominated leakage — signs of moisture, contamination, or severe ageing — requires further investigation before energising.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing *PI = R₁ / R₁₀* (inverted). The convention is **long-time resistance over short-time resistance**, because R₁₀ ≥ R₁ always.

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE of the following]**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive expressions for the **volume resistance** and **surface resistance** of a dielectric material from fundamental principles. State clearly the units of each quantity involved. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A flat slab of polyethylene has ρᵥ = 10¹⁴ Ω·m, thickness t = 2 mm, and electrode area A = 100 cm². The surface resistivity ρₛ = 10¹² Ω, gap between surface electrodes d = 1 cm, and electrode length l = 5 cm. Compute the **total insulation resistance**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Volume Resistance Derivation:**

Consider a parallel-plate geometry. Applying voltage V gives uniform field E = V/t. Using Ohm's law in point form with volume conductivity σ = 1/ρᵥ, the current density is J = E/ρᵥ. Total current through area A is:

$$I_v = J \cdot A = \frac{V \cdot A}{\rho_v \cdot t}$$

Using V = Iᵥ·Rᵥ:

$$\boxed{R_v = \frac{\rho_v \cdot t}{A}}$$

**Units:** ρᵥ in Ω·m, t in m, A in m² → Rᵥ in Ω.

**Surface Resistance Derivation:**

For two parallel electrodes on the surface, tangential field is Eₜ = V/d. Surface Ohm's law gives surface current density Jₛ = Eₜ/ρₛ. Total surface current is:

$$I_s = J_s \cdot l = \frac{V \cdot l}{\rho_s \cdot d}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{R_s = \frac{\rho_s \cdot d}{l}}$$

**Units:** ρₛ in Ω (per square), d and l both in m → Rₛ in Ω. The ratio d/l is dimensionless.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Given:**
- ρᵥ = 10¹⁴ Ω·m
- t = 2 mm = 2 × 10⁻³ m
- A = 100 cm² = 100 × 10⁻⁴ m² = 10⁻² m²
- ρₛ = 10¹² Ω
- d = 1 cm = 10⁻² m
- l = 5 cm = 5 × 10⁻² m

**Step 1 — Compute Rᵥ:**

$$R_v = \frac{\rho_v \cdot t}{A} = \frac{10^{14} \cdot 2 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-2}}$$

$$R_v = \frac{2 \times 10^{11}}{10^{-2}} = 2 \times 10^{13} \, \Omega$$

Converting to MΩ:

$$R_v = 2 \times 10^{13} \, \Omega = 2 \times 10^{7} \, M\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Compute Rₛ:**

$$R_s = \frac{\rho_s \cdot d}{l} = \frac{10^{12} \cdot 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$R_s = \frac{10^{10}}{5 \times 10^{-2}} = 2 \times 10^{11} \, \Omega$$

Converting to MΩ:

$$R_s = 2 \times 10^{11} \, \Omega = 2 \times 10^{5} \, M\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Rₜ (Parallel Combination):**

$$R_t = \frac{R_v \cdot R_s}{R_v + R_s} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{13}) \cdot (2 \times 10^{11})}{2 \times 10^{13} + 2 \times 10^{11}}$$

**Numerator:**

$$(2 \times 10^{13}) \cdot (2 \times 10^{11}) = 4 \times 10^{24}$$

**Denominator:**

$$2 \times 10^{13} + 2 \times 10^{11} = 2 \times 10^{11} (100 + 1) = 2 \times 10^{11} \times 101$$

$$= 2.02 \times 10^{13}$$

**Result:**

$$R_t = \frac{4 \times 10^{24}}{2.02 \times 10^{13}} = 1.98 \times 10^{11} \, \Omega \approx 2 \times 10^{11} \, \Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_t \approx 1.98 \times 10^{11} \, \Omega \approx 1.98 \times 10^{5} \, M\Omega}$$

**Interpretation:** Since Rᵥ ≫ Rₛ, the **surface path dominates** the leakage (Rₜ ≈ Rₛ). Drying/cleaning the surface will significantly improve the total IR.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of current flow through a dielectric** under DC voltage, identifying all three components of current. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With the aid of a neat diagram, explain how the **guard ring** technique is used to separately measure volume and surface resistance. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

When a step DC voltage V is suddenly applied across an insulating material sandwiched between two electrodes, the total current i(t) flowing through it has **three distinct components**:

**Component 1 — Capacitive (Geometric) Current i_c(t):**
- Caused by the instantaneous charging of the geometric capacitance C_g of the electrode system.
- Lasts only a few milliseconds (time constant τ = R_t·C_g is very small).
- Decays as: i_c(t) = (V/R_t) · e^(−t/τ_c)

**Component 2 — Absorption (Polarisation) Current i_a(t):**
- Caused by slow polarisation phenomena inside the dielectric: electronic, ionic, dipolar, interfacial (Maxwell-Wagner) polarisation.
- Each polarisation has a different relaxation time constant (τₐ).
- Decays slowly over seconds to minutes as: i_a(t) = Σ V·Aᵢ · e^(−t/τᵢ)
- Indicates the **quality** of the dielectric — high absorption = healthy insulation.

**Component 3 — Leakage (Conduction) Current i_l:**
- Purely ohmic current through the bulk and along the surface.
- **Time-invariant** — the only current remaining after t → ∞.
- Determines the **true insulation resistance**: R_t = V / i_l

**Mathematical form:**

$$i(t) = \frac{V}{R_t} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_c} + \sum_i V \cdot A_i \cdot e^{-t/\tau_i} + \frac{V}{R_t}$$

This is why IR measurements are taken at **standard times** (30 s, 60 s, 10 min) — to extract comparable indices like DAR and PI.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Three-Electrode (Guard Ring) Test Setup:**

The test specimen (dielectric slab) has **three electrodes**:
1. **Top electrode (H)** — connected to HV terminal of megger.
2. **Bottom electrode (E)** — connected to earth/return terminal.
3. **Guard ring (G)** — a thin annular electrode surrounding the top electrode, with a small gap separating it from H.

**Case 1 — Measuring Rᵥ (Volume Resistance):**

- Connect **guard ring to HV terminal** (same potential as H).
- Surface leakage current Iₛ, originating on the top surface between H and the edge, is intercepted by the guard ring and routed *directly* back to the megger without passing through the measurement ammeter.
- The ammeter therefore reads **only Iᵥ**.
- Rᵥ = V / Iᵥ

**Case 2 — Measuring Rₛ (Surface Resistance):**

- **Disconnect the guard ring** (leave it floating).
- Both Iᵥ (through bulk) and Iₛ (along surface) flow through the ammeter.
- The meter reads **Iᵥ + Iₛ**, hence:

$$R_{measured} = \frac{V}{I_v + I_s} = \frac{R_v \cdot R_s}{R_v + R_s} = R_t$$

- Knowing Rᵥ from Case 1, Rₛ can be computed by:

$$R_s = \frac{R_v \cdot R_t}{R_v - R_t}$$

**Significance:** Without the guard ring, surface and volume currents mix, and the measured Rₜ is dominated by whichever is smaller — usually Rₛ in field conditions. The guard ring enables **clean separation** of bulk and surface properties — a standard KTU-mandated technique per **IS 3396 / ASTM D257**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up the formulas:** Writing Rᵥ = ρᵥ·A/t instead of ρᵥ·t/A is a guaranteed 1–2 mark cut.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** Failing to convert mm → m and cm² → m² silently changes the answer by 10⁴ to 10⁶ times. Always show unit conversion steps.
> 3. **Forgetting the parallel formula:** Many students compute Rᵥ and Rₛ and stop — they must then compute Rₜ as the parallel combination.
> 4. **Wrong direction in guard-ring description:** Saying *"guard ring removes volume current"* instead of *"guard ring removes surface current"* — examiners specifically check this.
> 5. **No diagram in Q.B(b):** Up to 2 marks reserved for a labelled test-cell diagram.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision pointers for KTU University Exam:**

- [x] **Insulation Resistance = parallel combination** of volume and surface resistance — *never* a single material property.
- [x] **Volume resistance formula:** $R_v = \rho_v \cdot t \div A$ — ohmic, through the bulk.
- [x] **Surface resistance formula:** $R_s = \rho_s \cdot d \div l$ — ohmic, along the surface.
- [x] **Units check:** ρᵥ in Ω·m, ρₛ in Ω (per square), all lengths in metres, all areas in m² for SI consistency.
- [x] **Total insulation resistance:** $R_t = R_v \cdot R_s \div (R_v + R_s)$.
- [x] **Surface resistivity ρₛ is independent of electrode size** — only depends on *ratio* d/l (dimensionless) — hence unit is Ω per square.
- [x] **DC, not AC**, is used for IR testing because AC capacitive current masks leakage.
- [x] **Guard ring removes surface current** when connected to HV → enables pure Rᵥ measurement.
- [x] **Disconnecting the guard ring** measures Rₜ (both currents combined) → Rₛ derived indirectly.
- [x] **Three components of current** under step DC: capacitive (transient, ms), absorption (transient, s–min), leakage (steady-state).
- [x] **Polarisation Index PI = R₁₀ₘᵢₙ ÷ R₁ₘᵢₙ** — healthy ≥ 2.0; dangerous < 1.0.
- [x] **Dielectric Absorption Ratio DAR = R₆₀ₛ ÷ R₃₀ₛ** — quick substitute for PI; healthy > 1.25.
- [x] **Acceptance Criterion (IS 732):** IR (MΩ) ≥ 1000 × V_rated (kV) at room temperature.
- [x] **Temperature correction:** Resistance roughly doubles every 10 °C *decrease* in temperature — important when comparing tests done at different ambient temperatures.
- [x] **Surface contamination dominates** in field conditions → drying/cleaning is the first remedy, not replacement.
- [x] **Always show unit conversions** (mm → m, cm² → m², Ω → MΩ) in KTU numerical answers — examiners award partial marks for this.
- [x] **Always state the formula before substituting** numbers — KTU valuation keys check this step.
- [x] **ASTM D257 / IEC 60093 / IS 3396** are the governing standards for IR testing — name-dropping these adds quality to your answer.

---
