topics.title: Conductivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Conductivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electrical Conductivity ($\sigma$)** of a dielectric material is defined as the ability of the material to conduct electric current under the influence of an applied electric field. It is the reciprocal of **resistivity ($\rho$)** and represents the **current density ($J$) per unit electric field ($E$)** applied across the material.

Mathematically, it is expressed by the point form of **Ohm's Law**:

$$J = \sigma E$$

where:
- $J$ = Current density in **A/m²**
- $\sigma$ = Electrical conductivity in **Siemens/meter (S/m)**
- $E$ = Applied electric field in **V/m**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In High Voltage Engineering, perfect dielectrics **do not exist**. All practical insulating materials possess a small but finite conductivity, typically in the range of **$10^{-16}$ to $10^{-10}$ S/m**. This "leakage" current is responsible for **dielectric losses**, **heating**, and eventual **insulation failure** under high voltage stress.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a water pipe system carrying pure, distilled water (the **dielectric**). Even though the water is "clean," it is not 100% pure H₂O. There are always a few stray ions, dust particles, and dissolved impurities floating around. 

If you apply a very high pressure (the **electric field**), these stray particles start moving through the pipe, creating a tiny trickle of current — this trickle is the **conductivity**. 

- **Low field** → very little movement (very high resistance).
- **High field** → particles get energized and move more freely (conductivity increases).
- **Heating the water** → ions move faster (conductivity increases with temperature).

In dielectrics, the "particles" moving are usually **electrons**, **ions**, or **holes**, and their movement under high voltage is what we study as **conduction in dielectrics**.

## 1.3 Types of Conduction in Dielectrics

| **Type** | **Charge Carrier** | **Typical Material** | **Key Feature** |
|:---------|:-------------------|:---------------------|:----------------|
| **Electronic** | Electrons & Holes | Crystals, Semiconductors | Temperature independent mobility |
| **Ionic** | Positive & Negative Ions | Ceramics, Glass, Polymers | Highly temperature dependent |
| **Molecular** | Polar Molecules | Liquid Dielectrics (Oil) | Increases with field strength |
| **Colloidal** | Charged Colloidal Particles | Contaminated Oil | Due to impurities |
| **Surface** | Surface Ions/Films | Solid Insulator Surfaces | Caused by moisture & dust |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept to Remember:** In a *perfect* dielectric, $\sigma = 0$. In a *practical* dielectric, $\sigma$ is **very small but non-zero**, and it is the **enemy of insulation design**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background — The Three Conduction Regions

The conduction current in a dielectric varies non-linearly with the applied electric field. Plotting $\log(J)$ vs $\log(E)$ reveals **three distinct regions**:

### Region 1: Low Field (Ohmic Region)
- At low fields, current obeys **Ohm's Law** strictly.
- $J \propto E$ → Linear relationship.
- Conductivity is **constant** and depends only on temperature.
- Predominant charge carriers: **thermally generated ions** in the bulk.

### Region 2: Intermediate Field (Saturation Region)
- All available bulk charge carriers are pulled toward electrodes.
- Current tends to become **saturated** and almost independent of field.
- Behavior approaches **space-charge limited current (SCLC)** or Schottky-type emission.

### Region 3: High Field (Non-Ohmic / Exponential Region)
- Current increases **exponentially** with field.
- New charge carriers are generated by:
  - **Schottky Injection** (electrons jump over electrode-dielectric barrier).
  - **Fowler-Nordheim Tunneling** (field-assisted tunneling).
  - **Impact Ionization** (avalanche multiplication).
- This region precedes **dielectric breakdown**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Applicable Region** |
|:------------|:----------------|:----------------------|
| $J = \sigma E$ | Point form of Ohm's Law | Low field (Ohmic) |
| $\sigma = nq\mu$ | Conductivity in terms of charge carrier density | Low field |
| $J = A E^n$ | Non-linear conduction (n = 1 to 2) | High field |
| $J_{Schottky} = A T^2 \exp\left(\dfrac{\phi - \beta_E E^{1/2}}{kT}\right)$ | Schottky emission current | High field, low temperature |
| $J = J_0 \exp(\alpha d)$ | Townsend-type avalanche (pre-breakdown) | Very high field |
| $\sigma = \sigma_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{W}{2kT}\right)$ | Ionic conductivity (Arrhenius-type) | Solid dielectrics |
| $J = n e v_d = n e \mu E$ | Drift current density | All regions |
| $\rho = \dfrac{1}{\sigma}$ | Resistivity in **Ω·m** | All regions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units to Memorize for KTU:**
> - Conductivity: **S/m** (Siemens per meter)
> - Resistivity: **Ω·m**
> - Current Density: **A/m²**
> - Field Strength: **V/m**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding conductivity in dielectrics is **critical** for:

1. **Transformer Design** — Minimizing leakage current in transformer oil (target: $\sigma < 10^{-12}$ S/m).
2. **Power Cable Insulation** — Selecting XLPE vs. EPR based on conductivity behavior under high stress.
3. **Capacitor Manufacturing** — High-$\sigma$ materials cause high $\tan \delta$ (loss tangent) and thermal runaway.
4. **Switchgear** — SF₆ gas has extremely low conductivity, making it an ideal gaseous dielectric.
5. **High-Voltage Bushings** — Surface conductivity (controlled by creepage distance) must be minimized.

## 2.4 Effect of Key Parameters on Conductivity

| **Parameter** | **Effect on $\sigma$** | **Mechanism** |
|:--------------|:-----------------------|:--------------|
| ↑ Temperature | ↑ Conductivity (Exponentially for ionic) | More thermal energy → more carriers |
| ↑ Field Strength | ↑ Conductivity (Exponentially at high E) | Schottky & Poole-Frenkel emission |
| ↑ Moisture | ↑ Conductivity (Drastically) | Dissolved water provides free ions |
| ↑ Impurities | ↑ Conductivity | Foreign ions act as charge carriers |
| ↑ Pressure (liquids) | ↓ Conductivity slightly | Reduced mobility of ions |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Conductivity, Carrier Density & Mobility

We derive the fundamental expression for conductivity starting from the drift velocity of charge carriers.

**Step 1 — Define Drift Velocity**
When an electric field $E$ is applied, charge carriers (electrons, ions) acquire a **drift velocity** $v_d$ given by:

$$v_d = \mu E$$

where $\mu$ is the **mobility** of the charge carrier (in m²/V·s).

**Step 2 — Define Current Density from Motion of Charges**
Consider a unit volume containing $n$ charge carriers, each of charge $q$. The total charge crossing a unit area per unit time is:

$$J = n q v_d$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Drift Velocity Expression**
Substituting $v_d = \mu E$ into the current density equation:

$$J = n q (\mu E)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into Ohm's Law Form**
Rearranging:

$$J = (n q \mu) E$$

**Step 5 — Identify Conductivity**
Comparing this with the fundamental Ohm's law in point form, $J = \sigma E$:

$$\sigma = n q \mu$$

This is the **fundamental conductivity equation**, valid for any material with charge carriers of density $n$ and mobility $\mu$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Temperature Dependence of Ionic Conductivity (Arrhenius Equation)

For ionic conduction in solid dielectrics, the number of free ions available for conduction depends on the **thermal activation energy** $W$.

**Step 1 — Number of Free Ions (Boltzmann Statistics)**
The probability of an ion gaining enough thermal energy to break free from its lattice site is given by the **Boltzmann factor**:

$$n \propto \exp\left(-\frac{W}{2kT}\right)$$

where:
- $W$ = Activation energy in **Joules (eV → J × 1.6×10⁻¹⁹)**
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant = **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature in **K**

**Step 2 — Substitute into Conductivity Equation**
Since $\sigma = nq\mu$, and $n$ follows the Boltzmann distribution:

$$\sigma = n_0 q \mu \exp\left(-\frac{W}{2kT}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Write the Final Arrhenius Form**
Defining the pre-exponential factor $\sigma_0 = n_0 q \mu$:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \sigma_0 \exp\left(-\frac{W}{2kT}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation**
- A plot of $\ln(\sigma)$ vs $1/T$ gives a **straight line** with slope $= -W/(2k)$.
- From the slope, the **activation energy $W$** can be experimentally determined.
- This is a **standard KTU numerical problem** — given $\sigma$ at two temperatures, find $W$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Schottky Effect (High-Field Conduction)

At high fields, electrons can be emitted from the cathode into the conduction band of the dielectric by overcoming a reduced potential barrier (image force lowering).

**Step 1 — Work Function Reduction**
The applied field $E$ reduces the cathode-dielectric interface barrier $\phi$ by an amount $\Delta\phi$:

$$\Delta \phi = \beta_S E^{1/2}$$

where $\beta_S$ is the **Schottky constant**.

**Step 2 — Effective Barrier Height**

$$\phi_{eff} = \phi - \beta_S E^{1/2}$$

**Step 3 — Schottky Emission Current Density (Richardson-Dushman Modified)**

$$J_{Schottky} = A T^2 \exp\left(\frac{\phi_{eff}}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Final Form of Schottky Equation**

$$\boxed{J_{Schottky} = A T^2 \exp\left(\frac{\phi - \beta_S E^{1/2}}{kT}\right)}$$

This shows that at **high field**, $J$ rises **exponentially** with $\sqrt{E}$, leading to rapid increase in conduction current — a precursor to **dielectric breakdown**.

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical: Finding Activation Energy

**Problem (Typical KTU 2-Mark Style):**
The conductivity of a dielectric material at 300 K is $10^{-14}$ S/m and at 400 K is $10^{-10}$ S/m. Calculate the **activation energy $W$** in eV.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write conductivity at both temperatures:**

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_0 \exp\left(-\frac{W}{2kT_1}\right) \quad \text{at } T_1 = 300\,\text{K}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \sigma_0 \exp\left(-\frac{W}{2kT_2}\right) \quad \text{at } T_2 = 400\,\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Take the ratio:**

$$\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1} = \exp\left[\frac{W}{2k}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$\frac{10^{-10}}{10^{-14}} = 10^4 = \exp\left[\frac{W}{2k}\left(\frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{400}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Compute the temperature term:**

$$\frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{400} = \frac{400 - 300}{300 \times 400} = \frac{100}{120000} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Take natural log on both sides:**

$$\ln(10^4) = 4 \ln(10) = 4 \times 2.3026 = 9.2103$$

$$9.2103 = \frac{W}{2k} \times 8.333 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for W:**

$$W = \frac{9.2103 \times 2 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23}}{8.333 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$W = \frac{2.542 \times 10^{-22}}{8.333 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.051 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to eV:**

$$W = \frac{3.051 \times 10^{-19}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.907\,\text{eV}$$

**Final Answer: $W \approx 1.91$ eV**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conduction Regions in Dielectrics — Schematic Flow

This diagram maps the **three conduction regions** in a dielectric as a function of applied electric field strength.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Electric Field E applied across dielectric] --> B{Field Magnitude Classification}
    
    B -->|Low Field E less than 10 to power 6 V per m| C[Region 1: Ohmic Conduction]
    C --> C1[J proportional to E linear relationship]
    C1 --> C2[sigma constant thermally generated ions dominate]
    C2 --> C3[Current density J equals sigma times E]
    
    B -->|Intermediate Field 10 to power 6 to 10 to power 8 V per m| D[Region 2: Saturation Region]
    D --> D1[All available bulk charge carriers drift to electrodes]
    D1 --> D2[Current saturates and becomes nearly field independent]
    D2 --> D3[Space charge limited current SCLC may appear]
    
    B -->|High Field E greater than 10 to power 8 V per m| E[Region 3: Non Ohmic Exponential Region]
    E --> E1[Schottky Emission]
    E --> E2[Fowler Nordheim Tunneling]
    E --> E3[Poole Frenkel Effect]
    E --> E4[Impact Ionization]
    E1 --> F[Pre Breakdown Avalanche]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Dielectric Breakdown Imminent]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Conductivity in High Voltage Insulation System

This functional block diagram shows the **input-process-output** architecture of conductivity phenomena in a high-voltage dielectric system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        P1[Applied Voltage V]
        P2[Frequency f]
        P3[Temperature T]
        P4[Moisture and Impurity Content]
        P5[Mechanical Stress]
    end
    
    subgraph PROCESSES[CONDUCTION MECHANISMS IN DIELECTRIC]
        M1[Electronic Conduction]
        M2[Ionic Conduction]
        M3[Colloidal Conduction]
        M4[Surface Conduction]
        M5[Space Charge Effects]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUTS[ENGINEERING OUTPUTS]
        O1[Leakage Current IL]
        O2[Dielectric Loss tan delta]
        O3[Heat Generation P equals V times IL]
        O4[Insulation Aging Rate]
        O5[Probability of Breakdown]
    end
    
    P1 --> M1
    P1 --> M2
    P2 --> M1
    P3 --> M2
    P3 --> M3
    P4 --> M3
    P4 --> M4
    P5 --> M5
    
    M1 --> O1
    M2 --> O1
    M3 --> O1
    M4 --> O1
    M5 --> O1
    M1 --> O2
    M2 --> O2
    M3 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
    O3 --> O4
    O4 --> O5

    M2 --> O5

```

## 4.3 Mechanism Decision Tree: How Current Flows in a Dielectric

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Dielectric Subjected to High Voltage] --> Q1{Type of Dielectric?}
    
    Q1 -->|Solid Crystal| S1[Intrinsic Conduction Electrons and Holes]
    Q1 -->|Solid Amorphous| S2[Ionic Conduction via hopping]
    Q1 -->|Liquid Oil| L1[Pure Liquid: Electronic Conduction]
    Q1 -->|Liquid Oil| L2[Commercial Oil: Ionic and Colloidal]
    Q1 -->|Gas| G1[Townsend Avalanche at high E]
    
    S1 --> S1A{Field Level?}
    S1A -->|Low| S1A1[Ohmic sigma constant]
    S1A -->|High| S1A2[Schottky and FN Tunneling]
    
    S2 --> S2A[Temperature dependent Arrhenius]
    
    L1 --> L1A[Field dependent: J equals A times E to power n]
    L2 --> L2A[Impurity ions act as charge carriers]
    L2 --> L2B[Colloidal particles bridge electrodes]
    
    G1 --> G1A[Electron multiplication via ionization]
    G1A --> G1B[Breakdown when alpha times d reaches critical value]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define electrical conductivity of a dielectric material. Why is it non-zero in practical insulators?**

**Model Answer:**
Electrical conductivity $\sigma$ of a dielectric is defined as the ratio of current density $J$ to the applied electric field $E$:

$$\sigma = \frac{J}{E} \quad \text{(S/m)}$$

In practical insulators, $\sigma \neq 0$ because:
1. **No perfect insulator exists** — all materials have thermally generated charge carriers.
2. **Impurities and defects** (vacancies, dislocations, grain boundaries) provide ions/electrons for conduction.
3. At high fields, **electrons are injected from electrodes** (Schottky/Fowler-Nordheim emission).
4. **Moisture and contaminants** on surfaces create ionic conduction paths.

> [!NOTE]
> Typical values: Polymers $\sigma \approx 10^{-16}$ to $10^{-12}$ S/m; Transformer oil $\sigma \approx 10^{-13}$ to $10^{-10}$ S/m.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]
**State and explain the Arrhenius equation for ionic conductivity. What does the activation energy $W$ signify?**

**Model Answer:**
The Arrhenius equation for ionic conductivity in solid dielectrics is:

$$\sigma = \sigma_0 \exp\left(-\frac{W}{2kT}\right)$$

where:
- $\sigma_0$ = pre-exponential constant (depends on ion density & mobility)
- $W$ = activation energy in **eV or Joules**
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant = $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $T$ = absolute temperature in **K**

**Significance of $W$:** The activation energy is the **minimum thermal energy** required for an ion to break free from its lattice site and become a mobile charge carrier. A **higher $W$** means a **better insulator** at low temperatures; a **lower $W$** means conduction rises sharply with temperature.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full Descriptive Question (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the **three regions of conduction** in a solid dielectric as a function of applied electric field. Discuss the **charge carrier mechanisms** dominating each region.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The conductivity of an insulating material at 350 K is $5 \times 10^{-14}$ S/m and at 500 K is $8 \times 10^{-11}$ S/m. Calculate the **activation energy** in eV.

#### Model Solution to (a):

The conduction current $J$ vs electric field $E$ in a solid dielectric shows three distinct regions:

**Region 1 — Low Field (Ohmic Region):**
- Current density obeys Ohm's law: $J = \sigma E$.
- $\sigma$ is **constant** and independent of $E$.
- Charge carriers are **thermally generated ions** in the bulk.
- Predominant mechanism: **ionic hopping** between lattice sites.

**Region 2 — Intermediate Field (Saturation):**
- All thermally generated carriers reach electrodes.
- $J$ becomes **nearly independent of $E$**.
- Space charge accumulation at electrodes gives **space-charge limited current (SCLC)** behavior.

**Region 3 — High Field (Non-Ohmic / Pre-breakdown):**
- $J$ increases **exponentially** with $E$.
- New mechanisms activate:
  - **Schottky emission** — electrons overcome reduced cathode barrier.
  - **Fowler-Nordheim tunneling** — electrons tunnel through thin barrier.
  - **Poole-Frenkel effect** — trapped electrons are field-emitted into conduction band.
  - **Impact ionization** — electrons gain kinetic energy and create new carriers.

These mechanisms lead to **avalanche multiplication** and eventual **dielectric breakdown**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often **draw the graph incorrectly** — log(J) vs log(E) is the correct plot, NOT J vs E linearly. The three regions are clearly visible ONLY on a log-log plot. **2 marks lost** for wrong graph.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 350$ K, $\sigma_1 = 5 \times 10^{-14}$ S/m
- $T_2 = 500$ K, $\sigma_2 = 8 \times 10^{-11}$ S/m

**Step 1 — Take ratio using Arrhenius equation:**

$$\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1} = \exp\left[\frac{W}{2k}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Compute ratio:**

$$\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-11}}{5 \times 10^{-14}} = 1600$$

**Step 3 — Temperature term:**

$$\frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{500} = \frac{500 - 350}{350 \times 500} = \frac{150}{175000} = 8.571 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Take natural log:**

$$\ln(1600) = 7.378$$

**Step 5 — Solve for W:**

$$W = \frac{2k \times \ln(\sigma_2/\sigma_1)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)}$$

$$W = \frac{2 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 7.378}{8.571 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$W = \frac{2.036 \times 10^{-22}}{8.571 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.376 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to eV:**

$$W = \frac{2.376 \times 10^{-19}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 1.485\,\text{eV}$$

**Final Answer: $W \approx 1.49$ eV**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Many students **forget to convert J to eV** at the end. **1 mark lost** for unit error. Also, ensure the temperature term is in $K^{-1}$ (NOT °C⁻¹).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Alternative for Internal Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Schottky emission mechanism** in solid dielectrics. Derive the expression for **Schottky current density** and discuss its significance in **pre-breakdown conduction**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **different types of conduction mechanisms** in **commercial liquid dielectrics** (transformer oil). How does the presence of **impurities and moisture** affect the conductivity?

#### Model Solution to (a) — Schottky Emission:

**Concept:**
At a metal-dielectric interface, an electron must overcome a **potential barrier** of height $\phi$ (work function) to enter the dielectric's conduction band. When a high electric field $E$ is applied at the cathode, the image-force potential is **lowered** by an amount $\Delta\phi$, allowing more electrons to escape.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Image force potential at distance $x$ from electrode:**

$$\Phi(x) = \phi - \frac{q^2}{16\pi\varepsilon x} - qEx$$

**Step 2 — Maximum of $\Phi(x)$ occurs at:**

$$x_m = \sqrt{\frac{q}{16\pi\varepsilon E}}$$

**Step 3 — Reduced barrier height:**

$$\phi_{eff} = \phi - \beta_S E^{1/2}$$

where $\beta_S = \sqrt{\dfrac{q^3}{4\pi\varepsilon}}$ is the **Schottky coefficient**.

**Step 4 — Modified Richardson-Dushman emission current:**

$$\boxed{J_{Schottky} = A T^2 \exp\left[-\frac{\phi - \beta_S E^{1/2}}{kT}\right]}$$

**Significance in Pre-Breakdown:**
- Predicts the **exponential rise** of current at high $E$.
- Provides a **theoretical basis** for designing electrodes that minimize emission.
- Used to determine the **cathode work function** experimentally from J-E plots.

#### Model Solution to (b) — Conduction in Liquid Dielectrics:

In commercial transformer oil, three conduction mechanisms coexist:

1. **Electronic Conduction:** Even in pure oil, a few free electrons exist due to cosmic radiation or thermal excitation. The current depends on field as $J = A E^n$ (where $n \approx 1$ to 2).

2. **Ionic Conduction:** Dissociation of impurities (water, acids, salts) produces free ions. Mobility $\mu \approx 10^{-7}$ to $10^{-4}$ m²/V·s. Strongly temperature dependent.

3. **Colloidal Conduction:** Charged colloidal particles (e.g., dirt, dust, paper fibers from windings) move under the field. This is the **dominant mechanism** in aged oils and is the primary cause of **partial discharge** activity.

**Effect of Impurities and Moisture:**
- **Moisture:** Even 50 ppm of water can **increase conductivity 10-100 times**. Water molecules dissociate into H⁺ and OH⁻ ions, providing abundant charge carriers.
- **Particulate contaminants:** Solid particles (cellulose fibers, rust, dust) act as **charge carriers** in colloidal conduction and can **bridge the electrodes**, initiating **dielectric breakdown**.
- **Dissolved gases:** O₂, CO₂, N₂ can ionize under high fields, contributing to conduction.

**KTU Practical Insight:** A **good transformer oil** must have:
- Moisture content: **< 30 ppm** (for HV transformers)
- Breakdown voltage: **> 30 kV** (per 2.5 mm gap, ASTM D877)
- Conductivity: **< $10^{-12}$ S/m**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** Students confuse **electronic conduction in liquids with that in metals**. In liquids, electrons are *not* the primary carriers in commercial oils — **ions and colloids** dominate. **2 marks lost** for stating "conduction is by free electrons like in metals."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Conductivity in Dielectrics**

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Definitions
- **Conductivity ($\sigma$):** $J/E$, units **S/m**; reciprocal of resistivity.
- **Resistivity ($\rho$):** $1/\sigma$, units **Ω·m**.
- **Mobility ($\mu$):** $v_d/E$, units **m²/V·s**.
- **Activation Energy ($W$):** Energy needed to free an ion from a lattice site.

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Formulas
- ✅ $J = \sigma E$ (Ohm's law in point form)
- ✅ $\sigma = nq\mu$ (carrier density × mobility)
- ✅ $\sigma = \sigma_0 \exp(-W/2kT)$ (Arrhenius for ionic conduction)
- ✅ $J_{Schottky} = AT^2 \exp[-(\phi - \beta_S E^{1/2})/kT]$ (high field)
- ✅ $\ln(\sigma_2/\sigma_1) = (W/2k)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$ (for 2-point numerical)

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Numerical Constants
- $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $1\,\text{eV} = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ J
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m

### 🔑 Three Conduction Regions (log-log plot)
1. **Ohmic** (low E) — linear, $\sigma$ = constant.
2. **Saturation** (mid E) — current saturates, SCLC may appear.
3. **Non-Ohmic** (high E) — exponential, Schottky/FN/PF mechanisms.

### 🔑 Key Factors Increasing $\sigma$
- ↑ Temperature (most powerful)
- ↑ Field strength
- ↑ Moisture (10–100× increase)
- ↑ Impurities (ionic & colloidal)

### 🔑 Typical Conductivity Values
| Material | $\sigma$ (S/m) |
|:---------|:--------------:|
| Polystyrene | $10^{-16}$ |
| Transformer Oil (good) | $10^{-13}$ |
| Glass | $10^{-12}$ |
| Porcelain | $10^{-10}$ |

### 🔑 KTU Frequently Asked Traps
- ❌ Do not confuse **surface conductivity** with **volume conductivity**.
- ❌ Do not use **°C** in Arrhenius equation — always use **Kelvin**.
- ❌ Do not forget the **factor of 2** in the denominator of the Arrhenius exponent.
- ❌ Do not assume $J \propto E$ at high fields — it becomes **exponential**.
- ❌ In liquid dielectrics, **conduction ≠ conduction in metals**.

### 🎯 Top 3 KTU-Preferred Topics for 14-Mark Questions
1. **Three regions of conduction** + Arrhenius numerical (combined).
2. **Schottky emission derivation** + significance in pre-breakdown.
3. **Conduction in liquid dielectrics** + effect of impurities/moisture.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dielectric System Response - Partial Discharge Measuring Technique - Requirements on a Partial Discharge Measuring System - Measuring Systems for Apparent Charge - Partial Discharge Measurements on High-Voltage Transformers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dielectric System Response - Partial Discharge Measuring Technique - Requirements on a Partial Discharge Measuring System - Measuring Systems for Apparent Charge - Partial Discharge Measurements on High-Voltage Transformers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Dielectric System Response — Formal Definition

The **Dielectric System Response** refers to the manner in which an insulating material (dielectric) reacts when subjected to an alternating or transient electric field. It encompasses three fundamental physical phenomena: **polarization**, **conduction (loss)**, and **breakdown (discharge)**. When the electric stress exceeds the local dielectric strength of a small region (e.g., a void, bubble, or interface), a localized ionization event occurs. This event is termed a **Partial Discharge (PD)** — a discharge that only partially bridges the dielectric between conductors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal IEC Definition (IEC 60270):** A *partial discharge* is a **localized electrical discharge that only partially bridges the insulation between conductors** and which may or may not occur adjacent to a conductor.

**Dielectric Response Model (Debye / Loss-Tangent Approach):**
The complex permittivity of the dielectric, $\varepsilon_r^* = \varepsilon_r' - j\varepsilon_r''$, governs the response.
- The **loss tangent** is $\tan \delta = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r''}{\varepsilon_r'}$.
- The **dissipation factor (DF)** is the same as $\tan\delta$ for low-loss dielectrics.
- A perfect vacuum has $\varepsilon_r' = 1$, $\varepsilon_r'' = 0$, $\tan\delta = 0$.

### 1.2 Partial Discharge Measuring Technique — Overview

A **PD Measuring System** is an instrument chain designed to **detect, quantify, and localize** these tiny, fast electrical pulses (rise times in the nanosecond range, magnitudes in picocoulombs) without being affected by the 50/60 Hz power frequency voltage. Modern systems are governed by the international standard **IEC 60270** (High-voltage test techniques – Partial discharge measurements).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Leaky Pipe Network**
> Imagine a large water pipeline (the conductor) connected to a smaller pipe (the conductor's insulation). Inside the insulation, a small air bubble (a **void**) exists. When water pressure (analogous to **voltage stress**) becomes very high, water seeps momentarily into the bubble through a tiny crack — a **partial discharge** occurs. The pressure in the main pipe does not drop to zero (the discharge is *partial*, not a full short circuit), but a small pressure pulse travels back through the pipe wall. The PD measuring system is the **pressure sensor** placed on the pipe, calibrated to ignore the steady-state water flow (50 Hz) but to record every micro-pulse (the PD event). The size of the pressure pulse is the **apparent charge**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light (propagation of EM waves in cables):** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \;\text{m/s}$
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \;\text{F/m}$
- **Standard PD measurement unit (apparent charge):** **picocoulomb (pC)** = $10^{-12} \;\text{C}$
- **Standard PD current pulse unit:** **nanoampere (nA)** or directly in pC per cycle
- **IEC 60270 lower limit of measurement:** **1 pC** (in well-shielded environments, 0.1 pC is achievable)
- **Recommended measuring frequencies (IEC 60270):** Low-frequency range **30 kHz – 100 kHz**; High-frequency range **100 kHz – 1 MHz** (sometimes extended to 30 MHz for UHF).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of Partial Discharges

A partial discharge originates from a **localized electrical breakdown** in a region of lower dielectric strength within the bulk insulation. The major sources are:

1. **Internal (Cavity / Void) Discharges** — gas-filled cavities in solid/liquid insulation.
2. **Corona Discharges** — ionization in gas surrounding sharp conductors.
3. **Surface Discharges** — discharges along the boundary of two dielectrics.
4. **Treeing Discharges** — electrical / water treeing in polymeric insulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why voids cause PD:** Gases (especially air, $\varepsilon_r \approx 1$) have a much lower dielectric strength than the surrounding solid insulation (e.g., epoxy, $\varepsilon_r \approx 3.5$, $E_{bd} \approx 100–200$ kV/mm). The voltage across a cavity is given by the capacitive divider formula.

Voltage across the cavity:
$$V_{\text{cavity}} = V_{\text{applied}} \cdot \dfrac{C_a}{C_a + C_c}$$
where $C_a$ is the capacitance of the dielectric in series with the cavity and $C_c$ is the cavity capacitance. When $V_{\text{cavity}} \geq V_{\text{breakdown, cavity}}$, a PD occurs.

### 2.2 The Apparent Charge (q_a) Concept

The **apparent charge** $q_a$ is defined as that charge which, if instantaneously injected between the terminals of the test object, would change the voltage across them by the same amount as the actual PD pulse.

- It is **NOT** equal to the real charge that actually flowed in the void.
- It is measured in **picocoulombs (pC)** at the terminals of the test object.
- It is the only PD quantity that can be unambiguously calibrated.

**Derivation of apparent charge:** When a PD of real charge $q$ occurs in a cavity, it redistributes charges on the electrodes. The change in voltage at the terminals, $\Delta V$, when multiplied by the total test-object capacitance $C_a$, gives:
$$q_a = C_a \cdot \Delta V$$

### 2.3 Requirements on a Partial Discharge Measuring System (per IEC 60270)

The IEC standard places stringent requirements on any PD measuring instrument:

| # | Requirement | Engineering Justification |
|---|-------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Wide dynamic range** (≥ 60 dB, i.e., 1 pC to 1000 pC) | PD magnitudes vary by orders of magnitude. |
| 2 | **Defined measuring bandwidth** (narrow-band or wide-band) | To reject 50 Hz mains and to give reproducible results. |
| 3 | **High sensitivity** — minimum detectable charge as low as 1 pC | Early-stage insulation defects must be detected. |
| 4 | **Short pulse resolution time** (typically < 100 μs) | Successive PDs must be resolvable. |
| 5 | **Calibration capability** using a calibrator of known charge $q_0$ (e.g., 50 pC) | To ensure traceability of $q_a$ to SI units. |
| 6 | **Effective noise suppression** — gating, balanced bridge, or windowed FFT | To distinguish PD from external interference. |
| 7 | **Linear amplitude response** across the entire range | Otherwise distortion of PD pattern. |
| 8 | **Adequate input impedance** to avoid loading the source | To preserve pulse shape. |

### 2.4 Classification of PD Measuring Systems (IEC 60270)

IEC 60270 classifies the systems into two categories:

- **Narrow-band (NB) systems** — Bandwidth typically **9 kHz to 30 kHz** (centre frequency 50–1000 kHz). Better noise rejection, lower amplitude accuracy.
- **Wide-band (WB) systems** — Bandwidth from **30 kHz to 1 MHz** (or more). Better resolution, captures fast pulses, better for digital processing.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes / Boundary |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Complex permittivity | $\varepsilon_r^* = \varepsilon_r' - j\varepsilon_r''$ | dimensionless | Real part $\varepsilon_r'$ = energy storage; Imag part $\varepsilon_r''$ = loss |
| 2 | Loss tangent | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r''}{\varepsilon_r'}$ | dimensionless | $\delta$ = loss angle |
| 3 | Voltage across cavity | $V_c = V \cdot \dfrac{C_a}{C_a + C_c}$ | V (volts) | $C_a$ = dielectric in series; $C_c$ = cavity capacitance |
| 4 | **Apparent charge** | $q_a = C_a \cdot \Delta V$ | **C (Coulombs)** | pC in practice: $1\;\text{pC} = 10^{-12}$ C |
| 5 | PD current | $i(t) = \dfrac{dq_a}{dt}$ | A | Pulse width $\sim$ ns to μs |
| 6 | Repetition rate (PD pulse rate) | $n$ = pulses/cycle or pulses/sec | Hz or 1/cycle | Affects ageing rate |
| 7 | Calibrator injection | $q_0 = C_0 \cdot U_0$ | C | $C_0$ = calibrator cap; $U_0$ = step voltage |
| 8 | Discharge magnitude relation | $q_a = C_b \cdot V_a$ | C | $C_b$ = test-object capacitance; $V_a$ = transient voltage |
| 9 | Inception voltage | $V_i$ : smallest $V$ at which PD $\geq$ specified level occurs | V or kV | Used for pass/fail |
| 10 | Extinction voltage | $V_e < V_i$ | V or kV | Hysteresis: $V_e < V_i$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Do not confuse **apparent charge $q_a$** (measured at the terminals) with **real charge $q$** (actually transferred in the cavity). They are different by a factor of $\dfrac{C_c}{C_a+C_c}$ (the capacitive divider). Only $q_a$ is reported per IEC 60270.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Transformer factories** use PD testing on every power transformer before dispatch (acceptance test).
- **GIS (Gas-Insulated Substations)** use UHF couplers for online PD monitoring.
- **Cable manufacturers** run PD tests on every reel of HV/EHV cable.
- **Aircraft wiring** uses PD detection to find insulation degradation.
- **Wind turbine generators** use online PD sensors to predict bearing / winding failure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Apparent Charge from First Principles

**Setup:** Consider a parallel-plate test object with capacitance $C_a$ (the "test object"). A coupling capacitor $C_k$ is connected in parallel. A measuring impedance $Z_m$ (or a quadrupole) is connected in series with $C_k$ to ground. A calibrator injects a known step charge $q_0$ across the test object for calibration.

When a real PD of charge $q$ occurs inside a cavity within $C_a$, charge redistribution occurs. From the perspective of the external terminals, the effect is the same as if a charge
$$q_a = \dfrac{C_b}{C_b + C_c} \cdot q \cdot \text{(transfer ratio)}$$
were instantaneously transferred at the terminals.

**Step 1 — Define test object terminal voltage change.** The voltage transient at the terminals of the test object caused by the PD is:
$$\Delta V = \dfrac{q}{C_a + \dfrac{C_a \cdot C_c}{C_c}}$$
For simplicity, with $C_a$ and $C_c$ in series, the equivalent capacitance seen is $C_s = \dfrac{C_a C_c}{C_a + C_c}$.

**Step 2 — Apply charge conservation.** The total charge appearing on the test-object terminals due to the cavity discharge is:
$$q_a = C_a \cdot \Delta V$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\Delta V$ from Step 1.** Expressing the change in voltage across $C_a$ when $q$ is transferred in the cavity:
$$\Delta V = q \cdot \dfrac{C_c}{C_a + C_c} \cdot \dfrac{1}{C_a}$$
Wait — let us redo this carefully. The voltage across the cavity *before* the discharge is:
$$V_c^{\text{before}} = V \cdot \dfrac{C_a}{C_a + C_c}$$
This is the **steady-state** voltage (Step 1). The *transient* drop after the discharge equals $V_c^{\text{before}} - V_c^{\text{after}}$, with $V_c^{\text{after}} \approx 0$ (cavity is now conductive for a brief instant).

**Step 4 — Compute the voltage change at the terminals.** The voltage transient at the terminals is the same as the cavity voltage change scaled by the capacitance ratio:
$$\Delta V = (V_c^{\text{before}} - V_c^{\text{after}}) \cdot \dfrac{C_c}{C_a + C_c}$$

**Step 5 — Express the apparent charge.**
$$q_a = C_a \cdot \Delta V = C_a \cdot (V_c^{\text{before}}) \cdot \dfrac{C_c}{C_a + C_c}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the cavity voltage.**
$$q_a = C_a \cdot \left( V \cdot \dfrac{C_a}{C_a + C_c} \right) \cdot \dfrac{C_c}{C_a + C_c}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify.**
$$q_a = V \cdot \dfrac{C_a^2 \cdot C_c}{(C_a + C_c)^2}$$

This is the **fundamental relation** between the applied voltage $V$, the test object capacitance $C_a$, the cavity capacitance $C_c$, and the measured apparent charge $q_a$.

For most practical cases, $C_c \ll C_a$ (small void in large insulation), so:
$$q_a \approx V \cdot \dfrac{C_c}{1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C_a}{C_a}\right)^2 \rightarrow \text{limit form needs care}$$

A more useful engineering approximation, valid when $C_c \ll C_a$:
$$q_a \approx \dfrac{C_c}{C_a} \cdot q$$
where $q = C_c \cdot V_c^{\text{before}}$ is the **real charge** that actually crossed the cavity.

### 3.2 Derivation: Calibration Using a Charge Calibrator

**Setup:** A charge calibrator (a small capacitor $C_0$ in series with a step voltage generator) is connected across the test object.

**Step 1 — Inject step voltage $U_0$ across $C_0$.** The calibrator's own step voltage produces a defined charge:
$$q_0 = C_0 \cdot U_0$$

**Step 2 — Measure the response at the PD detector.** The detector reads a peak voltage $V_m$ (or a digital count).

**Step 3 — Define the scale factor (K-factor).** The scale factor $K$ of the complete measuring system is:
$$K = \dfrac{q_0}{V_m} \quad \text{[pC/V]}$$

**Step 4 — Apply to unknown PD.** For any subsequent PD reading $V_x$:
$$q_{a,\text{measured}} = K \cdot V_x \quad \text{[pC]}$$

**Step 5 — Traceability.** Because $C_0$ and $U_0$ are traceable to national standards (NABL / NIST), the $q_a$ value is traceable to SI.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choice of System for Different Applications

| Application | Best PD Method | Typical Frequency / Bandwidth | Sensitivity |
|-------------|----------------|-------------------------------|-------------|
| Power transformers (factory test) | IEC 60270 conventional (electrical) | 30 kHz – 1 MHz | 5 – 10 pC |
| Power transformers (online) | UHF + acoustic | 300 MHz – 1.5 GHz (UHF); 20 – 300 kHz (AE) | 5 – 50 pC |
| GIS | UHF couplers (internal) | 300 MHz – 1.5 GHz | 1 – 5 pC |
| HV cables | IEC 60270 / HFCT | 30 kHz – 1 MHz (HFCT 1 – 30 MHz) | 1 – 5 pC |
| Rotating machines | Stator slot couplers + VHF | 30 – 300 MHz | 5 pC |
| Outdoor insulators (corona) | UV camera + IEC RF | 30 kHz – 1 MHz | 10 pC |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A test object has $C_a = 1.2$ nF and contains a small cavity of $C_c = 20$ pF. A calibrator of $C_0 = 10$ pF is charged to $U_0 = 50$ V. The detector reads a peak of $V_m = 25$ mV for the calibrator pulse, and $V_x = 80$ mV for the actual PD.

**Solution:**
1. Calibrator charge:
   $$q_0 = C_0 \cdot U_0 = 10 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 50 = 500 \;\text{pC}$$
2. Scale factor:
   $$K = \dfrac{q_0}{V_m} = \dfrac{500 \;\text{pC}}{25 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{V}} = 20{,}000 \;\text{pC/V}$$
3. Apparent charge of PD:
   $$q_a = K \cdot V_x = 20{,}000 \cdot 80 \times 10^{-3} = 1600 \;\text{pC} = 1.6 \;\text{nC}$$
4. Real charge in cavity (approx.):
   $$q \approx \dfrac{C_a}{C_c} \cdot q_a = \dfrac{1200}{20} \cdot 1600 = 96{,}000 \;\text{pC} = 96 \;\text{nC}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **apparent charge is 60× smaller** than the real cavity charge. This is why apparent charge is *apparent* — it understates the actual local discharge magnitude.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — IEC 60270 Conventional PD Measuring System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HV[HV AC Source 50 Hz] -->|High Voltage Cable| Z1[Impedance Z1 Filter]
    Z1 --> CX[Test Object Cx with cavity]
    CX --> GND1[Ground 1]
    CX --> CK[Coupling Capacitor Ck]
    CK --> ZM[Measuring Impedance Zm Quadrupole]
    ZM --> DET[PD Detector Display]
    DET --> METER[Oscilloscope or Digital PD Meter]
    CAL[Calibrator C0 U0] -.Charge Injection.-> CX
    NOISE[External Noise] -.Rejected by.-> DET
    HV --> NOISE
    subgraph Calib[Calibration Path]
      CAL
    end
    subgraph Meas[Measurement Path]
      CX
      CK
      ZM
      DET
      METER
    end
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — PD Measurement on a Power Transformer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Lab[HV Laboratory Setup]
      T1[HV Transformer 400V to 250kV]
      T1 --> REACTOR[Reactance for Filter]
      REACTOR --> BUSH[Transformer Bushing HV]
      BUSH --> TX[Power Transformer Under Test]
      TX --> NEUT[Neutral Grounded Through Shunt]
      NEUT --> GND2[Lab Ground]
      CK2[Coupling Capacitor 1000 pF] --- BUSH
      CK2 --> ZM2[Measuring Impedance Zm]
      ZM2 --> PDM[PD Meter IEC 60270]
      PDM --> COMP[Computer with Pattern Recorder]
      subgraph Optional[Optional Online Methods]
        UHF[UHF Coupler in Drain Valve]
        UHF --> PDM
        ACO[Acoustic AE Sensor on Tank]
        ACO --> PDM
      end
      CAL2[Calibrator Injecting q0] -.-> BUSH
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — PD Pulse Detection Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PD[PD Pulse Generation] --> AFE[Analog Front End: Impedance Matching]
    AFE --> AMP[Preamplifier LNA]
    AMP --> BW[Band Pass Filter 30 kHz to 1 MHz]
    BW --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter 100 MSps]
    ADC --> DSP[DSP FPGA: Pulse Detection]
    DSP --> PHA[Phase Resolver using 50 Hz Reference]
    PHA --> DISP[Display Phi q n Pattern]
    CAL3[Calibrator q0] -.Periodic Self Test.-> AFE
    NOISE2[Mains 50 Hz Interference] -.Notched at 50Hz.-> BW
```

### 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — PD Activity vs. Voltage

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NoPD: Voltage less than Vi
    NoPD --> PDOnset: Voltage reaches Vi
    PDOnset --> SteadyPD: Voltage above Vi
    SteadyPD --> NoPD: Voltage reduced below Ve
    SteadyPD --> Degradation: Sustained PD causes aging
    Degradation --> Breakdown: Complete insulation failure
    Breakdown --> [*]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparison of Apparent vs. Real Charge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Applied Voltage V] --> CAV[Cavity Cc]
    CAV --> QREAL[Real Charge q in cavity]
    QREAL --> QAPP[Apparent Charge qa at terminals]
    QAPP --> METER[PD Meter Reads qa]
    QREAL --> DAMAGE[Local Erosion Ageing]
    DAMAGE --> FAIL[Insulation Failure]
    METER --> ENG[Engineer Decides Pass Fail]
    ENG -.Repair Replace.-> TX[Transformer]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set A]`

**Define apparent charge as per IEC 60270. Why is it called "apparent" and not "real"?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The apparent charge $q_a$ of a partial discharge is defined as that charge which, if instantaneously injected between the terminals of the test object, would change the voltage across them by the same amount as the actual PD pulse.
- **Why "apparent" (1 mark):** It is called apparent because it is **not equal to the real charge that actually flows in the cavity**; it is measured at the external terminals. Due to the capacitive divider formed by the cavity ($C_c$) and the rest of the insulation ($C_a$), the charge observed at the terminals is only a small fraction of the real cavity charge.

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**List any four essential requirements of a PD measuring system as per IEC 60270.**

**Model Answer (1 mark each, any 4):**
1. Defined measuring bandwidth (narrow-band 9–30 kHz or wide-band 30 kHz – 1 MHz).
2. High sensitivity with minimum detectable charge ≤ 1 pC.
3. Wide dynamic range (≥ 60 dB, e.g., 1 pC to 1000 pC).
4. Capability of calibration using a known charge calibrator.
5. Adequate noise suppression (balanced bridge or windowed gating).
6. Short pulse resolution time (< 100 μs).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **conventional IEC 60270 partial discharge measuring system** for apparent charge. List the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relation between the **apparent charge $q_a$**, the real cavity charge $q$, and the capacitances $C_a$ and $C_c$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — (a):**
A block diagram of the system contains:
1. **HV source** with filter (Z1) to suppress noise from mains.
2. **Test object $C_a$** (sample under test) connected in parallel.
3. **Coupling capacitor $C_k$** providing a low-impedance path for the PD pulse to the measuring impedance.
4. **Measuring impedance (quadrupole) $Z_m$** — converts the PD current pulse into a voltage signal.
5. **PD detector / meter** — amplifies, filters, displays pulse magnitude in pC.
6. **Calibrator** — injects a known charge $q_0 = C_0 U_0$ for scale-factor (K-factor) calibration.
7. **Display / pattern recorder** — shows the $\phi$–$q$–$n$ pattern (phase, magnitude, repetition).

(Each block: 1 mark, total 7 marks.)

**Model Answer — (b):**
1. Voltage across cavity: $V_c = V \cdot \dfrac{C_a}{C_a + C_c}$. **(1 mark)**
2. Real charge in cavity when it discharges: $q = C_c \cdot V_c$. **(1 mark)**
3. Voltage transient at terminals: $\Delta V = V_c \cdot \dfrac{C_c}{C_a + C_c}$. **(1 mark)**
4. Apparent charge definition: $q_a = C_a \cdot \Delta V$. **(1 mark)**
5. Substitute $\Delta V$:
   $$q_a = C_a \cdot V \cdot \dfrac{C_a}{C_a + C_c} \cdot \dfrac{C_c}{C_a + C_c} = V \cdot \dfrac{C_a^2 C_c}{(C_a + C_c)^2}$$
   **(1.5 marks)**
6. Approximation for $C_c \ll C_a$:
   $$q_a \approx \dfrac{C_c}{C_a} \cdot q$$
   Hence $q = \dfrac{C_a}{C_c} \cdot q_a$. **(1.5 marks)**

#### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the procedure for **partial discharge measurement on a high-voltage power transformer** during a factory acceptance test. Mention the calibration method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **IEC 60270 electrical method**, the **UHF method**, and the **acoustic emission method** for PD detection in transformers. State one advantage and one limitation of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — (a):**
1. **Setup (2 marks):** The transformer is isolated and de-energised. All bushings except the HV bushing are shorted and grounded. The HV bushing tap is connected through a coupling capacitor $C_k$ (typically 1000 pF) to the measuring impedance.
2. **Pre-stress (1 mark):** The transformer is energized gradually to 1.5 × rated voltage and held for 30 s, then reduced to 1.1 × rated voltage for measurement.
3. **Measurement (2 marks):** PD pulses are recorded over 1 minute. The **inception voltage $V_i$** and **extinction voltage $V_e$** are noted, the maximum $q_a$ is recorded in pC, and the $\phi$–$q$–$n$ pattern is captured.
4. **Calibration (2 marks):** A square-wave calibrator of $C_0 = 10$ pF and $U_0 = 50$ V ($q_0 = 500$ pC) is connected at the HV terminal. The detector's K-factor is set. Calibration is performed at a voltage *below* $V_i$ to avoid interference with the test.

**Model Answer — (b):**

| Method | Operating Principle | Advantage | Limitation |
|--------|--------------------|-----------|-----------|
| IEC 60270 Electrical | Couples PD pulse through $C_k$ to $Z_m$ | Quantitative, traceable, well-defined | Cannot localize; sensitive to external noise; offline only |
| UHF | Couples EM waves via disc couplers in drain valve | Localizes defect; online; immune to electrical noise | Complex calibration; not traceable to pC |
| Acoustic Emission | Piezo sensors detect ultrasonic waves from discharge | Immune to EM noise; locates defect by time-of-flight | Heavy attenuation in oil; lower sensitivity; slow |

(1.5 marks per row × 3 methods = 4.5 marks; summary statement 2.5 marks.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** the *apparent charge* (in pC, at terminals) with the *real cavity charge*. Always mention "apparent" explicitly.
> 2. **Do NOT skip** the **calibration step** in transformer PD test answers. Marks are specifically reserved for it.
> 3. **Do NOT** call a "wide-band" system the same as a "broad-band" one with 1 GHz — IEC 60270 strictly defines the bandwidths. Use exact figures.
> 4. **Do NOT** omit the **K-factor calculation** when asked. Numerical problems always test it.
> 5. **Acoustic + UHF + electrical** is the modern trifecta for transformer PD. Examiners reward answers that mention all three.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PD = partial bridge of insulation**, never a full short circuit.
- **Apparent charge $q_a$ is the only quantity that is calibrated and traceable**; reported in **pC**.
- **Real charge $q$ in cavity** is $\dfrac{C_a}{C_c}$ times $q_a$, generally orders of magnitude larger.
- **IEC 60270** is the governing standard. Two bandwidths: **narrow-band (9–30 kHz)** and **wide-band (30 kHz – 1 MHz)**.
- **Six essential blocks** of a conventional PD system: HV source + filter, test object $C_a$, coupling capacitor $C_k$, measuring impedance $Z_m$, calibrator, detector/display.
- **Calibration**: $q_0 = C_0 U_0$; scale factor $K = q_0 / V_m$; apparent charge $q_a = K \cdot V_x$.
- **Inception voltage $V_i$ > extinction voltage $V_e$** (hysteresis is a key signature of PD).
- **Transformer factory test**: pre-stress at 1.5 × rated → measure at 1.1 × rated, accept if $q_a < 50$ pC (typical for power transformers > 66 kV class).
- **Three modern methods on transformers**: (i) **IEC electrical** (offline, quantitative), (ii) **UHF** (300 MHz – 1.5 GHz, online, locational), (iii) **Acoustic AE** (20 – 300 kHz, online, immune to EM noise).
- **Cavity voltage divider** $V_c = V \cdot \dfrac{C_a}{C_a + C_c}$ is the root cause of PD inception.
- **Loss tangent $\tan\delta$** characterises dielectric losses; rising $\tan\delta$ with voltage is an early indicator of PD activity.
- **Phase-resolved PD (PRPD) pattern** $\phi$–$q$–$n$ is the modern fingerprint used for defect classification (corona, void, surface, treeing).
- **Common KTU trick:** "Define $\tan\delta$" — remember it's the **ratio** of imaginary to real permittivity.
- **Common KTU trick:** "Why UHF for GIS?" — Because GIS acts as a **coaxial waveguide** above its cut-off frequency (~88 MHz for a typical 100 mm inner diameter at $\text{SF}_6$ 0.4 MPa), so PD-generated EM waves propagate with low attenuation and can be picked up by internal disc couplers.
- **Pass/fail typical limits:** Cables — 5 pC; Power transformers — 50 pC (class-dependent); Generators — 1000 pC; Bushings — 10 pC.
- **NEVER** use a "high-pass filter" alone in PD measurement — it must be **band-pass** to reject both 50 Hz mains and high-frequency radio noise.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High-Voltage Cables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** High-Voltage Cables # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of High-Voltage Cables A **High-Voltage (HV) Power Cable** is a specially designed insulated electrical conductor assembly used for the bulk transmission and distribution of electrical energy at voltages typically above **11 kV (low tension) up to 500 kV and beyond (extra high tension)**. The cable is engineered to confine the electric field strictly within its dielectric medium, preventing unwanted leakage, corona discharge, and external interference. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, HV cables are classified based on the constructional arrangement of their insulation and conducting layers, and graded based on the distribution of electric stress within the dielectric. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** A high-voltage cable is fundamentally a **coaxial cylindrical capacitor**. The core conductor (inner cylinder) and the metallic sheath (outer cylinder) form the two electrodes, separated by a dielectric insulator. The electric stress is purely **radial**, meaning the maximum stress always occurs at the surface of the conductor and the minimum at the sheath. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **pressurized water pipeline** carrying high-speed water through a city. - The **water flow** = electric current. - The **inner pipe wall** = the conductor (carrying the current). - The **thick insulated outer coating** = the dielectric (preventing water from leaking out and protecting the surroundings). - The **pressure** = voltage potential. - If the pressure (voltage) becomes too high at certain points inside the pipe, the pipe wall may rupture (dielectric breakdown). To prevent rupture, engineers either: 1. **Use a stronger, layered wall (Belted vs. Screened Cables)** — better materials. 2. **Add intermediate pressure-relief rings (Intersheath Grading)** — equalizing pressure. 3. **Use a wall whose density varies gradually (Capacitance Grading)** — distributing pressure evenly. This is exactly the philosophy behind **grading of cables** — to ensure uniform electric stress so that no single point of insulation is overstressed. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > - **Permittivity of free space**: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$ > - **Relative permittivity** (paper-oil dielectric): $\varepsilon_r \approx 2.5$ to **4.0** (typical for HV cables) > - **Operating voltage range (KTU focus)**: **11 kV, 33 kV, 66 kV, 132 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV** > - **Dielectric strength of impregnated paper**: ~**30 to 50 kV/mm** > - **Maximum allowable working stress**: typically limited to **~5 kV/mm** for long life ## 1.4 Classification of HV Cables (Based on Insulation Construction) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 2 Mandate:** Students must be able to **draw, label, and explain** the constructional differences between these four types. | Type | Construction Feature | Voltage Range | Stress Distribution | |---|---|---|---| | **Belted Cable** | All three cores wrapped in a common belt insulation | Up to **11 kV** | Non-uniform, inter-cores stresses exist | | **H-Type (Hochstadter) Cable** | Each core individually screened with metallic foil, common metallic sheath | **11 kV – 33 kV** | Radial, better than belted | | **S.L. Type (Separate Lead) Cable** | Each core has its own lead sheath, then belted together | **11 kV – 66 kV** | Radial, no inter-core stresses | | **H.S.L. Type (H-SL) Cable** | Combination of H-type and SL-type (screening + separate lead) | **Above 66 kV** | Most uniform radial stress |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Electric Stress in a Single-Core Cable (The Coaxial Capacitor Model) Consider a single-core cable with: - Inner conductor radius = $a$ - Outer sheath inner radius = $b$ - Applied voltage = $V$ (phase voltage, rms) By Gauss's law applied to a cylinder of radius $r$ (where $a \le r \le b$), the electric flux density is: $$D = \frac{Q}{2\pi r L}$$ where $Q$ is the charge per unit length (C/m) and $L$ is the cable length. The electric field intensity at radius $r$ is: $$E(r) = \frac{V}{r \ln(b/a)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** The electric field $E(r)$ is **inversely proportional to $r$**. Therefore: > - **Maximum stress** $E_{max}$ occurs at the **conductor surface** ($r = a$): > $$E_{max} = \frac{V}{a \ln(b/a)}$$ > - **Minimum stress** $E_{min}$ occurs at the **sheath surface** ($r = b$): > $$E_{min} = \frac{V}{b \ln(b/a)}$$ The **stress ratio** is therefore: $$\frac{E_{max}}{E_{min}} = \frac{b}{a}$$ For $b/a$ ratios typical in practice (around 3 to 5), the maximum stress is **3 to 5 times** the minimum stress, leading to inefficient use of insulation. ## 2.2 Capacitance of a Single-Core Cable The capacitance per unit length of a single-core cable is: $$C = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)} \quad \text{[F/m]}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the same equation as a coaxial capacitor. For a 3-phase belted cable, the working capacitance per phase (with all three cores in parallel) is approximately: > $$C_n = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)} + \frac{0.32 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\ln(d/a)}$$ > where $d$ is the spacing between core centers. ## 2.3 Dielectric Losses in Cables In an AC cable, the dielectric is subjected to an alternating field. The dielectric loss per unit length is: $$W_d = 2\pi f C V^2 \tan\delta \quad \text{[W/m]}$$ where: - $f$ = supply frequency (Hz) - $C$ = capacitance per unit length (F/m) - $V$ = phase voltage (V) - $\tan\delta$ = loss tangent of the dielectric > [!NOTE] > The loss tangent for impregnated paper is typically **0.01 to 0.025** at operating temperature. For XLPE, it is even lower (~0.001 to 0.005). ## 2.4 Grading of Cables — The Two Principal Methods ### Method 1: Capacitance Grading (Dielectric Grading) In capacitance grading, the dielectric is constructed in **multiple concentric layers** with progressively **decreasing permittivity** from the conductor outward. This equalizes the stress. For a two-layer dielectric with inner radius $a$, interface radius $c$, and outer radius $b$: - Layer 1 (near conductor): permittivity $\varepsilon_1$ (higher) - Layer 2 (near sheath): permittivity $\varepsilon_2$ (lower) The condition for **uniform stress** is: $$\varepsilon_1 \cdot a = \varepsilon_2 \cdot b = \varepsilon_3 \cdot c = \dots = \text{constant}$$ For $n$ layers, the optimum permittivity of the $k$-th layer (with inner radius $r_{k-1}$ and outer radius $r_k$) is: $$\varepsilon_k = \frac{V}{E_0 \cdot r_{k-1} \cdot \ln(r_k / r_{k-1})}$$ where $E_0$ is the desired uniform stress level. ### Method 2: Intersheath Grading In intersheath grading, **metallic intersheaths** (thin copper or aluminum layers) are placed at specific radii within a homogeneous dielectric. These intersheaths are maintained at fixed potentials by static screens or auxiliary windings, breaking the single thick dielectric into multiple thinner regions, each with a more uniform stress distribution. For a cable with $n$ intersheaths dividing the dielectric into $(n+1)$ regions, the total thickness is preserved, and the stress is given by: $$E_{max} = \frac{V}{a \cdot \left[\ln(b/a) - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln(b_i/b)\right] \cdot \left(\frac{\text{voltage distribution factor}}{1}\right)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Trick Question to Watch Out For:** Intersheath grading uses a **homogeneous** dielectric, while capacitance grading uses a **non-homogeneous** dielectric. Students often confuse these. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units | Key Condition | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Electric stress at radius $r$ | $E(r) = \dfrac{V}{r \ln(b/a)}$ | V/m | Single-core cable | | 2 | Max stress (at $r = a$) | $E_{max} = \dfrac{V}{a \ln(b/a)}$ | V/m | Occurs at conductor | | 3 | Min stress (at $r = b$) | $E_{min} = \dfrac{V}{b \ln(b/a)}$ | V/m | Occurs at sheath | | 4 | Stress ratio | $E_{max}/E_{min} = b/a$ | dimensionless | Always > 1 | | 5 | Capacitance per unit length | $C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)}$ | F/m | Single-core | | 6 | Charging current | $I_c = 2\pi f C V$ | A | Per phase | | 7 | Dielectric loss | $W_d = 2\pi f C V^2 \tan\delta$ | W/m | AC operation | | 8 | Capacitance grading optimum | $\varepsilon_k \cdot r_{k-1} = \text{constant}$ | — | For uniform stress | | 9 | Most economical conductor size | $a = b/e \approx b/2.718$ | m | Minimizes insulation for fixed max stress | | 10 | Heat dissipation per unit length | $W = \dfrac{T \cdot 2\pi L}{\rho \ln(b/a)}$ | W/m | $T$ = temperature rise, $\rho$ = thermal resistivity | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Most-Astute-Check:** The conductor radius that minimizes the overall insulation cost (for a given $E_{max}$) is $a = b/e$, where $e = 2.718$ is Euler's number. This is a **favourite 3-mark question** in KTU boards. ## 2.6 Real-World Utility - **Underground urban power distribution**: HV cables are essential in metropolitan areas where overhead lines are impractical. - **Offshore wind farms**: Submarine HV cables transmit power from offshore turbines to onshore grids. - **Hydroelectric plants**: Underground HV cables connect underground powerhouses to surface switchyards. - **Crossing rivers and highways**: Where overhead lines cannot be erected. - **HV DC transmission**: Modern ±500 kV and ±800 kV HVDC cables (e.g., XLPE-based) connect renewable energy zones to consumption centers.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Electric Field Expression in a Single-Core Cable **Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for the electric field intensity at a distance $r$ from the axis of a single-core cable, where the inner conductor has radius $a$, the inner surface of the sheath has radius $b$, and the applied phase voltage is $V$. **Step 1: Setup and Gaussian Surface** Consider a single-core cable of length $L$ carrying a uniform linear charge density $Q$ (in C/m) on the inner conductor. Apply Gauss's law to a coaxial cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $r$ and length $L$, where $a \le r \le b$. By symmetry, the electric field $E$ is purely radial and constant over the Gaussian surface. **Step 2: Apply Gauss's Law** The total electric flux crossing the Gaussian surface is: $$\Phi = E \cdot (2\pi r L)$$ By Gauss's law, this equals the enclosed charge $Q L$ divided by the permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$: $$E \cdot (2\pi r L) = \frac{Q L}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$$ **Step 3: Solve for the Field Magnitude** $$E(r) = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot r}$$ **Step 4: Express Charge in Terms of Voltage** The potential difference $V$ between the conductor (at $r = a$) and the sheath (at $r = b$) is found by integrating the field: $$V = -\int_{b}^{a} E(r) \, dr = \int_{a}^{b} \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot r} \, dr$$ $$V = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} \ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$ Solving for $Q$: $$Q = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot V}{\ln(b/a)}$$ **Step 5: Substitute Back into the Field Equation** Plugging this back into the expression for $E(r)$: $$E(r) = \frac{1}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot r} \cdot \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot V}{\ln(b/a)}$$ $$\boxed{E(r) = \frac{V}{r \cdot \ln(b/a)}}$$ **Step 6: Identify Maximum and Minimum** - At $r = a$ (conductor surface): $$E_{max} = \frac{V}{a \ln(b/a)}$$ - At $r = b$ (sheath inner surface): $$E_{min} = \frac{V}{b \ln(b/a)}$$ **Step 7: Derive the Stress Ratio** $$\frac{E_{max}}{E_{min}} = \frac{V / [a \ln(b/a)]}{V / [b \ln(b/a)]} = \frac{b}{a}$$ **Q.E.D.** ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Condition for Most Economical Conductor Size **Problem Statement:** For a single-core cable operating at a given maximum stress $E_{max}$, find the value of conductor radius $a$ that minimizes the overall volume (and cost) of insulation. **Step 1: Express Sheath Radius in Terms of Conductor Radius** The maximum stress condition is: $$E_{max} = \frac{V}{a \ln(b/a)} = \text{constant}$$ For a given $V$ and $E_{max}$: $$a \ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) = \frac{V}{E_{max}} = K \text{ (constant)}$$ **Step 2: Express Volume of Insulation per Unit Length** The cross-sectional area of the dielectric (between $r = a$ and $r = b$) is: $$A_{ins} = \pi(b^2 - a^2)$$ **Step 3: Find the Optimum Using Differentiation** We want to minimize $A_{ins}$ subject to the constraint. From the constraint: $$b = a \cdot e^{K/a}$$ Differentiating $A_{ins}$ with respect to $a$ and setting $dA_{ins}/da = 0$: $$\frac{d}{da}\left[a^2(e^{2K/a} - 1)\right] = 0$$ After algebraic simplification (using the chain rule and the derivative of $e^{2K/a}$): $$2a(e^{2K/a} - 1) + a^2 \cdot e^{2K/a} \cdot \left(-\frac{2K}{a^2}\right) = 0$$ $$2a(e^{2K/a} - 1) - 2K \cdot e^{2K/a} = 0$$ $$a(e^{2K/a} - 1) = K \cdot e^{2K/a}$$ Using $K = a \ln(b/a)$: $$a\left(\frac{b^2}{a^2} - 1\right) = a \ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) \cdot \frac{b^2}{a^2}$$ Multiplying through by $a^2$: $$b^2 - a^2 = a \ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) \cdot b^2 / a$$ After manipulation, the cleanest result emerges: $$\boxed{\frac{b}{a} = e = 2.718 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{b}{e}}$$ This means the **most economical conductor radius is $b/e$**, i.e., the insulation thickness $(b - a)$ is approximately **63.2%** of the conductor radius. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Capacitance Grading — Optimum Permittivity **Problem Statement:** For a two-layer dielectric cable (Layer 1: $a \le r \le c$, permittivity $\varepsilon_1$; Layer 2: $c \le r \le b$, permittivity $\varepsilon_2$), find the relationship between $\varepsilon_1$, $\varepsilon_2$, $a$, $b$, and $c$ for uniform stress throughout. **Step 1: Field in Layer 1** The electric flux $D$ must be continuous at the interface (no free surface charges): $$D = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_1 E_1 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_2 E_2 = \text{constant}$$ **Step 2: Stress in Each Layer** $$E_1(r) = \frac{D}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_1} = \frac{K}{\varepsilon_1 \cdot r}, \quad a \le r \le c$$ $$E_2(r) = \frac{K}{\varepsilon_2 \cdot r}, \quad c \le r \le b$$ **Step 3: Total Voltage** $$V = \int_a^c E_1 \, dr + \int_c^b E_2 \, dr = \frac{K}{\varepsilon_1}\ln\left(\frac{c}{a}\right) + \frac{K}{\varepsilon_2}\ln\left(\frac{b}{c}\right)$$ **Step 4: Uniform Stress Condition** For uniform stress, $E$ must be independent of $r$. Since $E = K/(\varepsilon \cdot r)$ for any single layer, this is **impossible within a layer** unless the layer is infinitesimally thin. In practice, capacitance grading produces a **stepwise-uniform** stress profile. The condition to make the **maximum stresses in the two layers equal** is: $$E_{1,max} = E_{2,max} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{K}{\varepsilon_1 \cdot a} = \frac{K}{\varepsilon_2 \cdot b}$$ $$\boxed{\varepsilon_1 \cdot a = \varepsilon_2 \cdot b}$$ By extension, for $n$ layers: $$\varepsilon_1 \cdot a = \varepsilon_2 \cdot r_1 = \varepsilon_3 \cdot r_2 = \dots = \varepsilon_n \cdot r_{n-1}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Two Grading Methods | Parameter | Capacitance Grading | Intersheath Grading | |---|---|---| | **Mechanism** | Varying dielectric permittivity | Inserting metallic intersheaths at fixed potentials | | **Dielectric type** | Non-homogeneous | Homogeneous | | **Number of regions** | Multiple dielectric layers | $(n+1)$ regions for $n$ intersheaths | | **Construction complexity** | High (multiple dielectrics) | Moderate (extra screens) | | **Maintenance** | Difficult (delamination risk) | Easier (single dielectric) | | **Cost** | Higher | Slightly lower | | **Stress profile** | Stepwise uniform | Piecewise uniform | | **Used at** | Moderate voltages (up to 66 kV) | EHV cables (>66 kV) | | **KTU weightage** | Frequently asked (10 marks) | Sometimes asked (5 marks) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Belted Cable ```mermaid flowchart TB C1["CONDUCTOR CORE 1 - Copper or Aluminum"] I1["INSULATION LAYER 1 - Impregnated Paper"] C2["CONDUCTOR CORE 2"] I2["INSULATION LAYER 2"] C3["CONDUCTOR CORE 3"] I3["INSULATION LAYER 3"] B["BELTED INSULATION - Common Sheath Paper Wrapping"] BL["BEDDING - jute or compounded paper"] S["METALLIC SHEATH - Lead or Aluminum"] ARM["ARMOURING - Steel Wire or Tape"] OVT["OUTER COVERING - jute and bitumen"] C1 --> I1 C2 --> I2 C3 --> I3 I1 --> B I2 --> B I3 --> B B --> BL BL --> S S --> ARM ARM --> OVT ``` **Reading the Diagram (Top-to-Bottom Hierarchy):** 1. Three sector-shaped conductors carry the three phases. 2. Each conductor is wrapped with paper insulation. 3. All three insulated cores are **belted together** in a common insulation layer. 4. The belted assembly is bedded, sheathed in lead, armoured, and given an outer protective cover. > [!NOTE] > **Limitation of Belted Construction:** The dielectric in the triangular region between the three cores (the "void" or "filling" region) experiences **tangential stresses**, leading to non-uniform stress distribution and eventual breakdown at higher voltages. Hence, belted cables are limited to **11 kV**. ## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic — H-Type (Hochstadter) Cable ```mermaid flowchart TB A["CONDUCTOR"] B["CONDUCTOR INSULATION - Paper"] C["METALLIC SCREEN PER CORE - Copper or Aluminum Foil"] D["FILLER - jute or paper"] E["COMMON METALLIC SHEATH - Lead Alloy"] F["BEDDING AND ARMOURING"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Visual Identification Trick:** In the H-type cable, **each core is individually screened** (Hochstadter screen) before belting. This forces the electric field to be **purely radial** within each core's insulation, eliminating tangential stress. The screening foils of all three cores are in contact and grounded. ## 4.3 Functional Flow — Cable Stress Analysis Methodology ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["Step 1: Identify cable type and geometry a, b, c"] S2["Step 2: Determine operating voltage V"] S3["Step 3: Compute capacitance C using formula"] S4["Step 4: Compute electric field E at conductor surface"] S5["Step 5: Compare E with dielectric strength"] S6["Step 6: Check if E exceeds allowable limit"] S7["Step 7a: If NO - cable is safe at this voltage"] S8["Step 7b: If YES - apply grading or increase insulation"] S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S5 --> S6 S6 --> S7 S6 --> S8 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — Intersheath Grading ```mermaid flowchart TB CD["CENTRAL CONDUCTOR Radius a"] D1["DIELECTRIC REGION 1 a to r1"] IS1["INTERSHEATH 1 at potential V1"] D2["DIELECTRIC REGION 2 r1 to r2"] IS2["INTERSHEATH 2 at potential V2"] D3["DIELECTRIC REGION 3 r2 to b"] MS["MAIN SHEATH grounded"] CD --> D1 D1 --> IS1 IS1 --> D2 D2 --> IS2 IS2 --> D3 D3 --> MS ``` > [!NOTE] > In intersheath grading, the intersheaths are NOT grounded. They are maintained at **specific intermediate potentials** via static screening transformers or auxiliary windings. The key is that each intersheath "resets" the stress distribution in the next dielectric region, creating a staircase pattern of stresses that are all below the dielectric breakdown limit. ## 4.5 Block Diagram — Cable Manufacturing and Testing Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TB RD["RAW MATERIALS - Copper, Aluminum, Paper, Lead"] DW["DRAWING AND STRANDING of conductor"] AW["APPLICATION OF CONDUCTOR SCREENS"] PI["PAPER INSULATION LAPPISTON"] IMP["IMPREGNATION with insulating compound"] SL["SHEATHING - Lead extrusion"] AR["ARMOURING - Steel wires or tapes"] OC["OUTER COVERING - Bitumen and jute"] HV["HIGH VOLTAGE TESTING in factory"] RT["ROUTINE TESTS - Conductor resistance, capacitance"] ST["TYPE TESTS - Bending, load cycle, impulse"] DS["DISPATCH to installation site"] RD --> DW DW --> AW AW --> PI PI --> IMP IMP --> SL SL --> AR AR --> OC OC --> HV HV --> RT RT --> ST ST --> DS ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 2 Examiner Note:** While manufacturing flow is rarely asked directly, understanding the **testing sequence** (especially why high-voltage testing is done BEFORE routine tests) is a high-yield 2-mark concept.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2023] **"Define the term 'grading' as applied to underground cables. Why is it necessary?"** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1 Mark):** Grading is the technique of obtaining a more uniform distribution of electric stress in the dielectric of a cable, either by varying the permittivity of the dielectric (capacitance grading) or by introducing metallic intersheaths at suitable positions (intersheath grading). > > **Necessity (2 Marks):** In a single-core cable, the electric stress varies inversely with radius ($E \propto 1/r$). Hence, the stress at the conductor surface ($E_{max} = V/[a \ln(b/a)]$) is much higher than at the sheath. For typical $b/a$ ratios, $E_{max}$ is 3 to 5 times $E_{min}$. This non-uniformity leads to overstressing of the dielectric near the conductor, causing breakdown. Grading equalizes the stress, allowing better utilization of insulation and a thinner (cheaper) cable for the same voltage rating. ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **"State the condition for the most economical size of a conductor in a single-core cable."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > The most economical conductor radius $a$ for a single-core cable operating at a given maximum allowable stress $E_{max}$ is obtained when: > $$\boxed{\frac{b}{a} = e = 2.718}$$ > i.e., $a = b/e \approx 0.368 \, b$, which means the conductor radius is approximately **36.8%** of the inner sheath radius. > > At this optimum ratio, the **insulation thickness** $(b - a)$ is approximately **63.2%** of the conductor radius, and the overall volume (and cost) of dielectric material required is minimized for a given operating voltage and maximum stress. > > **Derivation Reference:** This comes from minimizing $A_{ins} = \pi(b^2 - a^2)$ subject to the constraint $E_{max} = V/[a \ln(b/a)] = $ constant. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A: [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question] **"Derive the expression for the electric field intensity at any point in the dielectric of a single-core cable. Hence, obtain the condition for the most economical diameter of the conductor. [14 Marks]"** #### Part (a) — Derivation of Electric Field Intensity (7 Marks) **Step 1:** Consider a single-core cable of length $L$ with inner conductor of radius $a$ and outer sheath of inner radius $b$. Let the linear charge density on the conductor be $Q$ C/m. **Step 2:** Apply Gauss's law to a coaxial cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $r$ ($a \le r \le b$): $$E(r) \cdot (2\pi r L) = \frac{Q L}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$$ $$\Rightarrow E(r) = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot r}$$ **Step 3:** The voltage $V$ between conductor and sheath is: $$V = \int_a^b E(r) \, dr = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} \ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $Q$: $$Q = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot V}{\ln(b/a)}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute back: $$\boxed{E(r) = \frac{V}{r \ln(b/a)}}$$ At $r = a$ (conductor surface): $E_{max} = \dfrac{V}{a \ln(b/a)}$ At $r = b$ (sheath surface): $E_{min} = \dfrac{V}{b \ln(b/a)}$ **Mark Allocation (7 Marks):** - Gauss's law application: **2 Marks** - Voltage integration: **2 Marks** - Final E(r) expression: **1 Mark** - $E_{max}$ and $E_{min}$: **2 Marks** #### Part (b) — Most Economical Conductor Size (7 Marks) **Step 1:** Maximum stress condition: $$E_{max} = \frac{V}{a \ln(b/a)} = \text{constant (given operating condition)}$$ **Step 2:** Volume of insulation per unit length: $$A_{ins} = \pi(b^2 - a^2)$$ **Step 3:** For minimum volume, $\frac{dA_{ins}}{da} = 0$ subject to the constraint $a \ln(b/a) = K$. **Step 4:** After differentiation and simplification (as shown in Section 3.2): $$\frac{b}{a} = e = 2.718$$ $$\boxed{a = \frac{b}{e}}$$ **Step 5:** Conclude that the **most economical conductor diameter** is $d = 2b/e$, and at this optimum, the volume of dielectric is minimized, saving material cost. **Mark Allocation (7 Marks):** - Setup of $E_{max}$ constraint: **1 Mark** - Insulation volume expression: **1 Mark** - Differentiation step: **2 Marks** - Algebraic simplification: **2 Marks** - Final result and physical interpretation: **1 Mark** --- ### Question B: [Alternative — KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question] **"Explain with neat sketches the construction of (i) H-type cable and (ii) S.L. type cable. Compare their relative merits. [14 Marks]"** #### Part (a) — H-Type Cable Construction and Working (7 Marks) **Construction (4 Marks):** - The H-type cable was developed by **Hochstadter**. - Each of the three conductors is **individually wrapped with paper insulation**. - A **metallic screen** (copper or aluminum foil, often perforated) is applied over each insulated core. - The three screened cores are then **belted together** with additional paper insulation and fillers. - A **common lead sheath** is extruded over the entire assembly, followed by bedding and armouring. **Working Principle (3 Marks):** - The metallic screens of all three cores are in **electrical contact** with each other and **grounded**. - The screens force the electric flux lines to remain **perpendicular (radial) to the conductor surface**. - **No tangential stresses** develop in the dielectric, eliminating the inter-core breakdown problem of belted cables. - The cable can therefore be used at higher voltages (11 kV – 33 kV) than the belted type. **Mark Allocation (7 Marks):** - Sketch (neatly labelled, all parts): **3 Marks** - Constructional details: **2 Marks** - Working principle with stress distribution: **2 Marks** #### Part (b) — S.L. Type Cable and Comparison (7 Marks) **SL-Type Construction (3 Marks):** - "SL" stands for **Separate Lead**. - Each of the three cores has its own **insulation** AND its own **individual lead sheath**. - The three individually sheathed cores are then **belted together** with bedding, common armouring, and outer covering. **Comparison Table (4 Marks):** | Feature | H-Type Cable | S.L. Type Cable | |---|---|---| | Screening | Metallic foil per core | Lead sheath per core | | Sheath | Common to all three | Separate per core | | Mechanical strength | Moderate | Excellent (rigid individual sheaths) | | Moisture ingress | Possible in common belt | Each core fully sealed | | Voltage range | Up to 33 kV | Up to 66 kV | | Cost | Lower | Higher | | Failure mode | Belt insulation breakdown | Single core can be replaced | | Use of dielectric | Efficient | Slightly less efficient | **Mark Allocation (7 Marks):** - SL construction details + sketch: **3 Marks** - Comparison table: **3 Marks** - Merits/demerits conclusion: **1 Mark** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in High-Voltage Cable Questions:** > 1. **Confusing $E_{max}$ location:** Many students write $E_{max}$ at the sheath.
