ALWAYS at the **conductor surface** ($r = a$). > 2. **Forgetting units in $E(r)$:** The result $E(r) = V/[r \ln(b/a)]$ gives V/m only if $V$ is in volts and $r$ in meters. Examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for missing units. > 3. **Most economical ratio:** The answer is $b/a = e$, NOT $b/a = 2$ or $b/a = 2.5$. Memorize this exact value. > 4. **Grading confusion:** Intersheath grading uses a **homogeneous** dielectric; capacitance grading uses a **non-homogeneous** dielectric. Mixing these up costs **2 marks minimum**. > 5. **Belted cable limit:** Always state "limited to **11 kV**" — not "up to 33 kV" — for belted cables. > 6. **Skipping the sketch:** In 7-mark sub-parts, a labelled diagram is worth **at least 2–3 marks**. Skipping it can halve your score. > 7. **Capacitance formula:** The factor is $2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$, not $4\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ (that is the formula for an isolated sphere). Examiners specifically check for this. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Voltage Cables** ### Core Equations to Memorize - ✅ $E(r) = \dfrac{V}{r \ln(b/a)}$ — electric field at radius $r$ in a single-core cable - ✅ $E_{max} = \dfrac{V}{a \ln(b/a)}$ — maximum stress at conductor - ✅ $E_{min} = \dfrac{V}{b \ln(b/a)}$ — minimum stress at sheath - ✅ $E_{max}/E_{min} = b/a$ — stress ratio - ✅ $C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)}$ — capacitance per unit length (F/m) - ✅ $W_d = 2\pi f C V^2 \tan\delta$ — dielectric loss per unit length (W/m) - ✅ $b/a = e = 2.718$ — most economical conductor ratio - ✅ $\varepsilon_1 \cdot a = \varepsilon_2 \cdot b$ — capacitance grading uniform-stress condition ### Cable Types — Quick Identification - ✅ **Belted**: Common belt insulation, sector-shaped cores, **< 11 kV**, has tangential stresses - ✅ **H-Type**: Individual metallic screens, common lead sheath, **11–33 kV**, purely radial stress - ✅ **S.L. Type**: Each core has its own lead sheath, **11–66 kV**, individually sealed - ✅ **H.S.L. Type**: H-type screening + S.L. sheathing, **> 66 kV**, premium construction ### Grading Methods — Key Distinctions - ✅ **Capacitance Grading**: Non-homogeneous dielectric, varying $\varepsilon$, uniform stepwise stress - ✅ **Intersheath Grading**: Homogeneous dielectric, fixed-potential metallic layers, multiple uniform regions - ✅ **Purpose**: Equalize stress, reduce insulation thickness, lower cost, prevent breakdown ### Practical / Numerical Problem Tips - ✅ Always convert voltage to **phase voltage** (line voltage / $\sqrt{3}$) for single-phase capacitance formulas. - ✅ Use $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m — do NOT approximate as $9 \times 10^{-12}$ in derivations. - ✅ Working stress in design = **1/3 to 1/2** of dielectric strength (safety factor of 2 to 3). - ✅ In numerical problems, calculate stress in **kV/mm** or **MV/m** (multiply V/m by $10^{-6}$). ### Mnemonic for Cable Types (B-H-S-HS): > **"Boys Have Strong Hearts"** — **B**elted → **H**-type → **S.L.** → **H.S.L.** (in order of increasing voltage capacity) ### Last-Minute Reminders Before Exam - ✅ Draw the **H-type cable sketch** neatly; examiners reward clean diagrams. - ✅ State the **"why"** behind every formula — KTU 2024 emphasizes conceptual understanding. - ✅ Always mention **"stress at conductor is maximum"** explicitly — it's worth a mark every time. - ✅ Remember: HV cables are **cylindrical** (not spherical) capacitors; do not mix formulas. - ✅ Dielectric loss $\propto V^2$ — so doubling voltage **quadruples** the loss.

topics.title: High-Voltage Gas-Insulated Substations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** High-Voltage Gas-Insulated Substations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gas-Insulated Substation (GIS)** > A **Gas-Insulated Substation (GIS)** is a high-voltage substation in which the major current-carrying and switching components (busbars, disconnectors, circuit breakers, instrument transformers, earthing switches, and surge arresters) are housed inside a grounded metallic enclosure filled with a high dielectric strength insulating gas — most commonly **Sulphur Hexafluoride (SF₆)** at a typical pressure of **0.4–0.5 MPa (4–5 bar) gauge** — instead of being exposed to atmospheric air as in conventional Air-Insulated Substations (AIS). The driving philosophy of GIS is **compactness without compromising reliability**. By replacing atmospheric air (dielectric strength ≈ 3 kV/mm at standard conditions) with pressurized SF₆ (dielectric strength ≈ 8–10 kV/mm at 0.4 MPa), the creepage and clearance distances can be reduced by a factor of roughly **10× to 15×** for the same voltage class. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 2)** > This topic falls under *High-Voltage Substation Design Concepts* and connects directly to the previous module's study of gaseous dielectrics (Paschen's Law, Townsend breakdown, streamer theory) and the subsequent module's discussion of lightning/switching overvoltages. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hermetically Sealed Pressure Cooker for Electricity" Imagine a conventional open-air substation as a **kitchen with open pots** — heat, steam, and pressure dissipate freely into the atmosphere, and you need a large open area to keep things safe from each other. Now imagine putting all those pots inside a **sealed pressure cooker** with a high-pressure insulating fluid inside. The walls are metal (grounded), the lid is bolted down, and the gas inside prevents any spark from jumping between the conductors. That is exactly what a GIS does: - **The conductors** = the live electrical busbars (the "inner wire" of the pressure cooker). - **The metallic enclosure** = the grounded outer sheath of each "module" (the cooker's wall). - **The SF₆ gas** = the pressurized dielectric medium (replacing air). - **The insulators (spacers)** = the small porcelain/composite supports that hold the conductor centered inside the enclosure. > [!NOTE] > A typical 400 kV GIS bay occupies only **~10% of the footprint** of a comparable AIS bay — this is the central engineering motivation for GIS adoption in space-constrained urban substations, offshore platforms, and hydroelectric powerhouses. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Dielectric strength of SF₆ at 0.4 MPa | $E_{br}$ | 8–10 | kV/mm | | Dielectric strength of air at STP | $E_{air}$ | 3.0 | kV/mm | | Molecular weight of SF₆ | $M$ | 146.06 | g/mol | | Global Warming Potential of SF₆ (100-yr) | GWP | **23,500** | × CO₂ | | Boiling point of SF₆ at 1 atm | $T_b$ | -63.8 | °C | | Gas density at 0.5 MPa, 20 °C | $\rho$ | 31.3 | kg/m³ | | Sound velocity in SF₆ | $c$ | 136 | m/s | > [!WARNING] > **Environmental Note for KTU Examiners' Perspective:** SF₆ is the most potent greenhouse gas regulated under the **Kyoto Protocol**. KTU 2024 scheme explicitly flags this as a key evaluation point in Part A (3-mark) questions. Always remember: although SF₆ is being phased down under EU F-Gas Regulation 2024, **no alternative gas has yet matched its dielectric and arc-quenching performance** in the 145 kV–800 kV class. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic The operational integrity of a GIS rests on **three pillars**: (i) electric field control, (ii) gas pressure management, and (iii) particle contamination mitigation. The breakdown chain logic is: 1. **Establish a coaxial geometry** — concentric inner conductor (high voltage) and outer enclosure (grounded). The coaxial configuration inherently produces a *non-uniform* field that is strongest at the surface of the inner conductor. 2. **Pressurize SF₆** — at 0.4–0.5 MPa, the gas density triples compared to atmospheric, which **scales the breakdown strength almost linearly** with density (above a threshold governed by Paschen's Law). 3. **Eliminate particle initiators** — any free metal particle inside the enclosure will lift, bounce, and cause micro-discharges that lead to flashover. 4. **Provide solid insulators (spacers)** — epoxy resin castings (filled with alumina trihydrate, Al₂O₃·3H₂O) hold the conductor and divide the gas volume into compartments. 5. **Monitor continuously** — *gas density monitors* and *partial discharge (PD) sensors* alert operators to pressure loss or insulation degradation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Coaxial Geometry is Dominant in GIS** > In a coaxial cylinder, the electric field is **maximum at the inner conductor** and decreases with radius. This is the *opposite* of needing to handle peak stress; therefore the inner conductor surface is **always** the most highly polished, often using aluminum with a mirror finish (Ra < 1.6 µm). Any scratch, protrusion, or contamination at this surface is the #1 cause of in-service failures. ## 2.2 Critical Engineering Equations ### 2.2.1 Electric Field in a Coaxial Cylinder For an inner conductor of radius $a$ and outer grounded enclosure of radius $b$, separated by a dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$, the field at any radius $r$ (where $a \le r \le b$) carrying voltage $V$ between the conductors is: $$E(r) \;=\; \frac{V}{r \,\ln(b/a)}$$ The **maximum field at the inner conductor surface** is: $$E_{max} \;=\; \frac{V}{a\,\ln(b/a)}$$ The ratio $b/a$ that minimizes $E_{max}$ for a given outer radius $b$ is found by calculus: $$\frac{d}{da}\!\left(\frac{1}{a\,\ln(b/a)}\right) \;=\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; b/a \;=\; e \;\approx\; 2.718$$ So the **optimal ratio is $b/a = e$** (i.e., the inner conductor radius ≈ 37% of the enclosure inner radius). In practical GIS designs, $b/a$ ranges from 2.0 to 3.5 for mechanical and thermal reasons. ### 2.2.2 Paschen's Law for SF₆ The DC breakdown voltage of a uniform-field gap in SF₆ is: $$V_b \;=\; B \cdot p \cdot d \, / \, \ln\!\bigl(A \cdot p \cdot d\bigr) \; - \; \ln\!\bigl(\ln(1 + 1/\gamma_{se})\bigr)$$ where: - $p$ = gas pressure (Pa) - $d$ = gap distance (m) - $A$, $B$ = gas-specific Townsend constants - $\gamma_{se}$ = secondary emission coefficient at the cathode For SF₆: $A \approx 3.5 \;\text{Pa}^{-1}\text{m}^{-1}$, $B \approx 7.5 \times 10^{4}\;\text{V Pa}^{-1}\text{m}^{-1}$. ### 2.2.3 Streamer / Leader Breakdown Criterion for SF₆ For non-uniform fields in SF₆, the **critical field criterion** (Pedersen) for streamer inception is: $$E_{cr} \;=\; 24.2\,p\,\bigl(1 + 0.74/(\,p d_{eff}\,)^{0.5}\bigr) \quad \text{[kV/mm]}$$ where $d_{eff}$ is in meters and $p$ in MPa. The design rule is: $$E_{max} \;\le\; 0.9 \cdot E_{cr}$$ > [!NOTE] > The factor **0.9** is the engineering safety margin. This is the line that separates a *working* GIS from a *failed* one on a university exam numerical. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Unit Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E(r) = \dfrac{V}{r\,\ln(b/a)}$ | Field in coaxial gap at radius $r$ | $V$ in V, $r$ in m, $E$ in V/m | | 2 | $E_{max} = \dfrac{V}{a\,\ln(b/a)}$ | Max field at inner conductor | Same as above | | 3 | $b/a = e \approx 2.718$ | Optimal ratio for minimum $E_{max}$ | Dimensionless | | 4 | $E_{cr} = 24.2\,p\!\left(1 + \dfrac{0.74}{\sqrt{p\,d_{eff}}}\right)$ | Streamer inception field in SF₆ | $p$ in MPa, $d_{eff}$ in m, $E$ in kV/mm | | 5 | $V_b$ via Paschen's Law | DC breakdown in uniform gap | Standard SI | | 6 | $\rho_{SF_6} = 6.14\,p\,(T_{ref}/T)\;\text{kg/m}^3$ | Gas density in enclosure | $p$ in MPa, $T$ in K | | 7 | $\Delta V = V \cdot (1 - 1/K)$ | Voltage distribution error on a spacer surface | $K$ = field enhancement factor | | 8 | $E_{p} = 5.14 \times 10^{-6} \cdot d \cdot (p/p_{0})$ | Particle-lift-off field (classical) | $d$ in m (particle diameter), $E$ in V/m | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse the **enclosure inner radius $b$** with the **outer steel wall radius** — the wall thickness (typically 5–10 mm of aluminum or stainless steel) carries eddy currents and contributes to enclosure heating but is **not** part of the dielectric gap. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Urban substations** (e.g., 220 kV Gas-Insulated substations in metro cities like Mumbai, Singapore, Tokyo) — where land cost is prohibitive. - **Offshore wind platforms** — salt-spray contamination makes AIS impossible. - **Hydroelectric caverns** — GIS tolerates the high humidity of underground powerhouses. - **Mobile substations** on rail wagons or skids for emergency grid restoration. - **EHV/UHV transmission hubs** (420 kV, 550 kV, 800 kV, 1100 kV) — the 1100 kV GIS at the Changji–Guquan UHVDC link in China is the world's highest-voltage GIS. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Field Expression for Coaxial Cylindrical GIS Duct We begin with **Gauss's Law** in integral form for a Gaussian cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$ coaxial with the inner conductor: $$\oint \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{A} \;=\; Q_{enc}$$ For SF₆, $\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \vec{E}$, with $\varepsilon_r \approx 1.0084$ (very close to 1). The enclosed charge is the line charge $\lambda L$ on the inner conductor. $$\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, E(r) \, (2\pi r L) \;=\; \lambda L$$ $$E(r) \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, r}$$ The potential difference between the inner conductor ($r=a$) and outer enclosure ($r=b$) is found by integrating the field along a radial path: $$V \;=\; -\int_b^a E(r)\, dr \;=\; \int_a^b E(r)\, dr \;=\; \int_a^b \frac{\lambda\, dr}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, r}$$ $$V \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$ Solving for $\lambda$: $$\lambda \;=\; \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, V}{\ln(b/a)}$$ Substituting back into the field expression: $$E(r) \;=\; \frac{V}{r \, \ln(b/a)} \qquad \blacksquare$$ ### 3.1.1 Optimization: Finding the Conductor Radius that Minimizes $E_{max}$ For a **fixed outer radius $b$** and **fixed voltage $V$**, we minimize $E_{max} = V / [a\,\ln(b/a)]$ with respect to $a$. Equivalently, maximize the denominator $f(a) = a\,\ln(b/a)$: $$f(a) \;=\; a \bigl[\ln b - \ln a\bigr]$$ $$\frac{df}{da} \;=\; \ln b - \ln a \; - \; 1 \;=\; \ln(b/a) \; - \; 1$$ Setting $df/da = 0$: $$\ln(b/a) \;=\; 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; b/a \;=\; e$$ Substituting back, the **minimum possible maximum field** is: $$E_{min} \;=\; \frac{V}{a \cdot 1} \;=\; \frac{V}{a} \quad \text{where} \quad a = b/e \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; E_{min} \;=\; \frac{e \cdot V}{b}$$ This elegant result tells us: for any given enclosure size, the smallest peak field we can ever achieve is $eV/b$ — a hard physical limit on how compact a GIS can be. ## 3.2 Numerical Example — KTU Problem Style > **Problem (KTU 2024 Style):** > A 245 kV single-phase GIS duct has an inner conductor radius $a = 45\;\text{mm}$ and enclosure inner radius $b = 180\;\text{mm}$. The enclosure is filled with SF₆ at 0.4 MPa (gauge). Determine: > (a) The maximum electric field on the conductor surface. > (b) Whether the design satisfies the safety margin criterion. > (c) The optimal inner radius if you were redesigning the system for the same enclosure size. **Given:** - $V = 245\,\text{kV} = 245 \times 10^3\,\text{V}$ (peak line-to-ground voltage) - $a = 0.045\,\text{m}$ - $b = 0.180\,\text{m}$ - $p = 0.4\,\text{MPa}$ (gauge) ### Part (a): Maximum Field $$\ln(b/a) \;=\; \ln(180/45) \;=\; \ln(4) \;=\; 1.3863$$ $$E_{max} \;=\; \frac{V}{a\,\ln(b/a)} \;=\; \frac{245 \times 10^3}{0.045 \times 1.3863}$$ $$E_{max} \;=\; \frac{245{,}000}{0.06238} \;=\; 3.927 \times 10^{6}\;\text{V/m}$$ $$\boxed{E_{max} \;\approx\; 3.93\;\text{kV/mm}}$$ ### Part (b): Streamer Criterion Check Compute the critical field for $p = 0.4\;\text{MPa}$ and an effective gap $d_{eff} = b - a = 0.135\;\text{m}$: $$E_{cr} \;=\; 24.2 \times 0.4 \times \left(1 + \frac{0.74}{\sqrt{0.4 \times 0.135}}\right)$$ The denominator term: $$\sqrt{0.4 \times 0.135} \;=\; \sqrt{0.054} \;=\; 0.2324$$ $$\frac{0.74}{0.2324} \;=\; 3.184$$ $$E_{cr} \;=\; 9.68 \times (1 + 3.184) \;=\; 9.68 \times 4.184$$ $$E_{cr} \;\approx\; 40.50\;\text{kV/mm}$$ The safety limit is $0.9 \times E_{cr} = 0.9 \times 40.50 = 36.45\;\text{kV/mm}$. Since $E_{max} = 3.93\;\text{kV/mm} \ll 36.45\;\text{kV/mm}$: $$\boxed{\text{Design is SAFE — passes criterion with a safety factor of }\approx 9.3}$$ ### Part (c): Optimal Inner Radius for the Same Enclosure For minimum $E_{max}$ at fixed $b = 180\;\text{mm}$: $$a_{opt} \;=\; \frac{b}{e} \;=\; \frac{180}{2.718} \;=\; 66.2\;\text{mm}$$ $$E_{min} \;=\; \frac{eV}{b} \;=\; \frac{2.718 \times 245{,}000}{0.180} \;=\; 3.70 \times 10^{6}\;\text{V/m}$$ $$\boxed{E_{min} \;\approx\; 3.70\;\text{kV/mm} \quad \text{at} \quad a_{opt} = 66.2\;\text{mm}}$$ The redesign would reduce the peak field by only ~6% — confirming that the **current design is already close to optimal** (b/a = 4 is close to e ≈ 2.718). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Failure Modes vs. Design Countermeasures | Failure Mode | Physical Cause | Engineering Countermeasure | |---|---|---| | Particle-induced flashover | Free metal chips lift, bounce, and trigger micro-discharges | **Particle traps** (small cavities near the enclosure bottom) | | Spacer flashover | Tangential field at the triple junction (spacer–SF₆–conductor) | **Stress-grading coatings** (semi-conductive ZnO or SiC layer at the spacer–conductor interface) | | Enclosure corrosion | Moisture ingress → HF acid formation | **Molecular sieves** (alumina + zeolite) in gas compartments; dew point < -40 °C | | Pressure loss | Slow leak at flange gaskets | **Gas density switches** with alarm at -10% nominal pressure | | Contact erosion in CB | Arc erosion of arcing contacts | Use **Puffer-type** SF₆ breakers with PTFE nozzle | | Overheating of enclosure | Eddy current losses in single-phase enclosures | Use **3-phase-in-one-enclosure** (triple-pole GIS) above 145 kV to cancel fluxes | | SF₆ decomposition | Switching arcs → HF, SOF₂, SO₂F₂ | **Activated alumina filters** + periodic gas analysis by gas chromatography | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: A Single GIS Bay ```mermaid flowchart TB HVIn["HV Incoming Line
420 kV"] --> DS1["Disconnector 1
Busbar Side"] DS1 --> CB["SF6 Circuit Breaker
Puffer Type 63 kA"] CB --> DS2["Disconnector 2
Line Side"] DS2 --> ES["Earthing Switch
Fast Acting"] DS2 --> CT["Current Transformer
Single Phase CT"] CT --> VT["Voltage Transformer
SF6 / Inductive"] VT --> SA["Surge Arrester
ZnO Block"] SA --> SA2["Lightning Arrester
Outlet"] CB --> SA3["Busbar Earthing Switch"] HVIn --> BB["Busbar Enclosure
Coaxial SF6 Duct"] BB --> BB2["Bay Extension
To Next Bay"] BB --> PM["Gas Pressure Monitor
Density Switch 0.42 MPa"] PM --> CTRL["Bay Control Unit
BCU + SCADA"] CTRL --> SAS["Substation Automation
IEC 61850 Protocol"] ES --> CTRL DS1 --> CTRL DS2 --> CTRL CB --> CTRL classDef primary fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef protection fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef monitor fill:#14532d,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef control fill:#581c87,stroke:#581c87,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff class HVIn,DS1,CB,DS2,BB primary class ES,SA,CT,VT protection class PM,SA3 monitor class CTRL,SAS,BB2 control ``` ## 4.2 Internal Cross-Section of a GIS Duct Module (Functional) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A["Single GIS Duct Module"] A1["Inner Conductor
Al Tube 45-100 mm"] --- A2["SF6 Gas
0.4-0.5 MPa"] A2 --- A3["Spacer Epoxy Disc
Al2O3 Filled"] A3 --- A4["Enclosure
Al Alloy 5-8 mm"] A4 --- A5["Heater Band
Avoid Condensation"] A5 --- A6["Particle Trap
Bottom Cavity"] end subgraph B["Auxiliary Systems"] B1["Gas Density Monitor"] --- B2["Pressure Relief
Disc 0.6 MPa"] B2 --- B3["Gas Filling Valve
DN8 Self Sealing"] B3 --- B4["LCC Manometer
Density Calibrated"] end A --- B classDef gasPath fill:#0369a1,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff classDef mechanical fill:#b45309,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff classDef auxiliary fill:#4d7c0f,stroke:#4d7c0f,color:#ffffff class A1,A2,A3,A4 gasPath class A5,A6 mechanical class B1,B2,B3,B4 auxiliary ``` ## 4.3 Decision Logic for AIS vs. GIS Selection (Engineering Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD S0["Start New Substation Project"] --> Q1{"Voltage Level?"} Q1 -->|"<= 72.5 kV"| AIS["Prefer AIS
MV Switchgear"] Q1 -->|"> 72.5 kV"| Q2{"Land Area Available?"} Q2 -->|"> 10,000 sq m"| Q3{"Pollution / Coastal?"} Q2 -->|"<= 10,000 sq m
or Urban"| GIS["Select GIS"] Q3 -->|"No"| Q4{"Seismic Zone?"} Q3 -->|"Yes
Heavy salt or industrial"| GIS Q4 -->|"Low / Moderate"| Q5{"Budget?"} Q4 -->|"High
Zone 4 or 5"| GIS Q5 -->|"Constrained"| Q6{"Aesthetic / Heritage?"} Q5 -->|"Adequate"| AIS Q6 -->|"Yes"| GIS Q6 -->|"No"| AIS classDef decision fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#7e22ce,color:#ffffff classDef choice fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff classDef start fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff class S0 start class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4,Q5,Q6 decision class AIS,GIS choice ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **List any three advantages of Gas-Insulated Substations (GIS) over conventional Air-Insulated Substations (AIS).** **Model Answer (3 marks, ~70 words):** 1. **Compactness:** A GIS bay occupies only ~10% of the footprint of an equivalent AIS, since SF₆ at 0.4 MPa has roughly 3× the dielectric strength of air. 2. **Environmental immunity:** Being hermetically sealed, GIS is unaffected by humidity, salt fog, dust, and industrial pollution — ideal for coastal and high-altitude installations. 3. **Higher reliability & safety:** Lower failure rate (≈ 0.05 failures/bay-year vs. 0.5 for AIS), no exposed live parts, and lower electromagnetic field at the boundary. *(Examiner's note: Students who write "more reliable" without numbers lose 0.5 mark. Always quantify.)* ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Why is SF₆ gas preferred as the insulating medium in GIS?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** SF₆ is preferred because of its **exceptional combination of physical and electrical properties**: 1. **High dielectric strength:** ~2.5–3× that of air at the same pressure, and approximately 8–10 kV/mm at 0.4 MPa — enabling compact designs. 2. **Excellent arc-quenching capability:** Its electronegative nature (high electron affinity due to six fluorine atoms) rapidly captures free electrons after arc interruption, giving it a dielectric recovery strength ~100× faster than air. 3. **Chemical inertness and thermal stability:** Non-flammable, non-toxic in pure form, and remains gaseous down to –63.8 °C at 1 atm (suitable for cold-climate substations). 4. **High molecular weight (146.06 g/mol)** — gives a low sonic velocity that aids arc cooling. ## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A** — *(14 Marks)* > **[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]** > > **(a)** With a neat block schematic, explain the major components of a typical 245 kV GIS bay and the function of each. State the typical gas pressure used. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** A 420 kV GIS has a coaxial conductor of inner radius 60 mm and outer enclosure inner radius 200 mm, filled with SF₆ at 0.5 MPa. Compute the maximum electric field, the critical streamer field, and verify the design safety margin using the Pedersen criterion. Take $V = 420/\sqrt{3}\;\text{kV}$ (line-to-ground peak). *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution to Q-A(a): The major components of a 245 kV GIS bay (block schematic shown in Section 4.1 of these notes) are: | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Busbar duct** | Carries current between bays inside a common SF₆-filled enclosure | | **Disconnectors (DS)** | Provides visible isolation when opened; operates under no-load | | **Circuit Breaker (CB)** | Interrupts fault and load currents using SF₆ puff or self-blast arc-quenching | | **Earthing Switch (ES)** | Safely grounds a circuit section for maintenance; can be fast or slow type | | **Current Transformer (CT)** | Steps down current for metering and protection (usually toroidal, single-turn primary) | | **Voltage Transformer (VT)** | Steps down voltage; inductive or capacitive divider type | | **Surge Arrester (SA)** | ZnO blocks limit lightning and switching overvoltages | | **Gas Density Monitor** | Pressure-compensated switch that alarms on SF₆ leakage | | **Spacers** | Epoxy insulators that support the conductor and compartmentalize gas zones | | **Particle Traps** | Cavities that capture free conducting particles | **Typical gas pressure:** SF₆ filled at **0.4–0.5 MPa (gauge)** ≈ 4–5 bar above atmospheric. Alarm pressure ≈ 0.35 MPa; lockout pressure ≈ 0.30 MPa. #### Model Solution to Q-A(b): **Step 1: Identify the parameters.** $$V_{lg} = \frac{420}{\sqrt{3}} = 242.5\;\text{kV}$$ $$a = 0.060\;\text{m}, \quad b = 0.200\;\text{m}, \quad p = 0.5\;\text{MPa}$$ **Step 2: Maximum field at the inner conductor.** $$\ln(b/a) = \ln(200/60) = \ln(3.333) = 1.2040$$ $$E_{max} = \frac{V}{a\,\ln(b/a)} = \frac{242.5 \times 10^3}{0.060 \times 1.2040} = \frac{242{,}500}{0.07224}$$ $$E_{max} = 3.357 \times 10^6\;\text{V/m} \approx 3.36\;\text{kV/mm}$$ **Step 3: Critical streamer field (Pedersen).** $$d_{eff} = b - a = 0.140\;\text{m}$$ $$E_{cr} = 24.2 \times 0.5 \times \left(1 + \frac{0.74}{\sqrt{0.5 \times 0.140}}\right)$$ $$\sqrt{0.5 \times 0.140} = \sqrt{0.070} = 0.2646$$ $$\frac{0.74}{0.2646} = 2.797$$ $$E_{cr} = 12.10 \times (1 + 2.797) = 12.10 \times 3.797 = 45.94\;\text{kV/mm}$$ **Step 4: Safety check.** $$E_{allow} = 0.9 \times E_{cr} = 0.9 \times 45.94 = 41.35\;\text{kV/mm}$$ Since $E_{max} = 3.36\;\text{kV/mm} \ll 41.35\;\text{kV/mm}$: $$\boxed{\text{Design is SAFE; safety factor} = 41.35/3.36 \approx 12.3}$$ --- ### **Question B** — *(14 Marks — Alternative Choice)* > **[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]** > > **(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **particle-induced breakdown** in GIS. With a sketch, describe how a particle trap works to mitigate it. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** Discuss the key **environmental and safety considerations** when handling SF₆ in a GIS, including the Kyoto Protocol context and the recent alternative gases (e.g., g³, AirPlus, Fluoronitrile mixtures). *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution to Q-B(a): **Particle-induced breakdown phenomenon:** Free metallic particles (typically aluminum chips of 0.5–5 mm from manufacturing) inside the GIS enclosure can acquire charge from the conductor, lift off the enclosure bottom, and bounce toward the inner conductor under the alternating electric field. This movement initiates **micro-discharges** that distort the local field, eventually leading to full flashover. **Mechanism of particle lift-off:** A spherical particle of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_p$ lifts off when the electrostatic force exceeds gravity: $$q \cdot E \;\ge\; \tfrac{1}{6}\pi d^3 \rho_p g$$ This gives the classical **lift-off field**: $$E_{LO} \;\approx\; 5.14 \times 10^{-6} \cdot d \cdot (p/p_0)\;\text{[V/m]}$$ **Particle trap operation:** A particle trap is a small rectangular or cylindrical cavity machined into the **bottom of the enclosure** at a region of low electric field. The local field inside the trap is below the lift-off threshold, so once a particle falls in, it cannot escape. The trap's opening is sized so that a particle entering is guided by gravity into a dead-zone away from the high-field region. ```mermaid flowchart TB P1["Aluminum Particle on Enclosure Bottom"] --> P2["Charges by Contact with Enclosure"] P2 --> P3["Lifts Off Under AC Field 1.5 E_0"] P3 --> P4{"Particle Trap Region?"} P4 -->|"No"| P5["Bounces Toward Conductor
Causes Micro-discharge"] P4 -->|"Yes"| P6["Enters Low-Field Cavity
Trapped Permanently"] P5 --> P7["Flashover Risk"] P6 --> P8["Safe - No Further Movement"] classDef danger fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff classDef safe fill:#15803d,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff classDef process fill:#1e40af,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff class P1,P2,P3,P5,P7 danger class P4 process class P6,P8 safe ``` #### Model Solution to Q-B(b): **Environmental and safety considerations for SF₆:** 1. **Global Warming Potential (GWP):** SF₆ has a 100-year GWP of **23,500** — i.e., 1 kg of SF₆ traps as much heat as 23,5 tonnes of CO₂. Listed under the **Kyoto Protocol (1997)** as a controlled gas; the EU F-Gas Regulation (517/2014, revised 2024) mandates reporting, leak-checking, and recycling. 2. **Decomposition products under arcing:** Under high-temperature switching arcs, SF₆ decomposes into toxic and corrosive species: **HF, SOF₂, SO₂F₂, CF₄, S₂F₁₀**. These attack spacer surfaces and pose inhalation hazards to maintenance staff. 3. **Occupational safety:** - **Asphyxiation risk:** SF₆ is 5× denser than air; leakage into low-lying cable trenches can displace oxygen below 19.5% (TLV limit). - **Cryogenic burns:** Liquefied SF₆ (used for cylinder topping-up in cold climates) can cause skin damage at –63.8 °C. - **Personal monitors:** Operators must use HF detectors and O₂ meters before entering the substation pit. 4. **Alternative gases (KTU 2024 Hot Topic):** - **g³ (green gas for grid)** — a 3M/Novec-engineered CO₂/O₂/C₄F₇N (fluoronitrile) mixture with GWP reduced by **98%**; field-deployed by GE in 145 kV class. - **AirPlus™ (ABB)** — CO₂/O₂/C₅F₁₀O (fluoroketone) mixture; GWP < 1; commercial in 72.5–245 kV. - **Vacuum interrupters** — favored for ≤ 36 kV; not yet viable for EHV. - **Dry air / N₂** — viable up to 145 kV with larger enclosures. 5. **End-of-life handling:** Decommissioned SF₆ must be recovered at **>99% efficiency** using purpose-built reclaimers (e.g., Dilo, SF₆-Recovery-by-Enervac); it is then filtered, liquefied, and sent to SF₆ producers (e.g., Solvay, Showa Denko) for purification and re-use. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > - **Do not** confuse "switchgear" and "substation": The substation includes civil works, earthing, control building, and busbars; switchgear is only the switching/breaking equipment. > - **Do not** use bare "dielectric strength" without specifying pressure and gas — examiners expect you to state: "8–10 kV/mm at 0.4 MPa" or lose 0.5 mark. > - **Do not** write "SF₆ is harmful" — be specific: name at least one decomposition product and one regulation. > - **Do not** skip the $b/a = e$ derivation in Part B derivations — the optimal conductor radius derivation is a favourite 2-mark sub-question. > - **Always** state units in every line of a numerical — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit on a numerical answer. ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **GIS Definition:** A substation in which all major HV equipment is enclosed in a **grounded metal sheath filled with pressurized SF₆** (typically 0.4–0.5 MPa gauge). - **Dielectric strength of SF₆ at 0.4 MPa:** ≈ 8–10 kV/mm (≈ 3× air). - **Footprint reduction:** ~10% of equivalent AIS. - **Coaxial field equation (must memorize):** $E(r) = V / [r \ln(b/a)]$; **max at inner conductor** $E_{max} = V / [a \ln(b/a)]$. - **Optimal $b/a = e \approx 2.718$** for minimum peak field; minimum achievable $E_{max} = eV/b$ for fixed $b$. - **Streamer criterion (Pedersen):** $E_{cr} = 24.2\,p(1 + 0.74/\sqrt{p\,d_{eff}})$ kV/mm; design rule $E_{max} \le 0.9\,E_{cr}$. - **Key GIS components:** Busbar, DS, CB, ES, CT, VT, SA, spacer, gas-density monitor, particle trap. - **SF₆ properties:** Electronegative (high electron affinity), non-flammable, arc-quenching, GWP = 23,500, decomposition products include HF, SOF₂. - **Three pillars of GIS reliability:** (1) Field control via coaxial geometry, (2) gas pressure management, (3) particle contamination control. - **Failure modes to remember:** Particle flashover, spacer flashover at triple junction, enclosure corrosion, contact erosion in CB. - **Alternatives to SF₆:** g³, AirPlus, fluoronitriles, fluoroketones, dry air — all with dramatically lower GWP but lower dielectric strength (require larger enclosures or higher pressures). - **Regulatory:** Kyoto Protocol 1997, EU F-Gas Regulation 517/2014 + 2024 revision, IEC 62271-203 (HV GIS standard). - **Where to use GIS:** Urban substations, coastal/offshore, hydroelectric caverns, mobile substations, EHV/UHV hubs (up to 1100 kV operational in China). - **Where to prefer AIS:** Voltages ≤ 72.5 kV, large land available, low pollution, non-seismic zones, tight budget, where SF₆ handling infrastructure is unavailable.

topics.title: Risk of Failure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Risk of Failure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk of Failure (R)** in high voltage insulation engineering is defined as the **statistical probability** that the dielectric strength (withstand capability) of an insulation system will be exceeded by the electrical stress (overvoltage) imposed upon it during its operational lifetime.

In the language of probability theory applied to **insulation coordination**:

$$R = P(\text{Stress} > \text{Strength}) = P(S > C)$$

Where:
- $S$ = Random variable representing the **electrical stress** (overvoltage magnitude)
- $C$ = Random variable representing the **dielectric withstand strength** of the insulation
- Both $S$ and $C$ are **stochastic (random) variables** governed by probability density functions

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bridge and Truck" Analogy

Imagine a bridge that must support trucks of varying weights:

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Think of insulation as a **bridge** and overvoltages as **trucks** crossing it. The bridge's *strength* is not a single fixed number — different bridges of the same design will have slightly different load capacities (due to material variations, workmanship). Similarly, the *truck weights* (overvoltages) are not constant — they vary with switching events, lightning strokes, and fault conditions. **Risk of failure** is the probability that an unusually heavy truck crosses an unusually weak bridge. The bridge does not "know" which truck is coming, so the engineer must design for a *statistically acceptable* risk level (typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ per year per equipment).

This statistical perspective is the cornerstone of **modern insulation coordination**, replacing the older, wasteful deterministic approach where engineers used very large safety factors (often 2× to 3×) leading to over-engineered and expensive systems.

### 1.3 Why "Risk" Exists — The Two Sources of Statistical Scatter

| Source of Uncertainty | Description | Typical Spread |
|---|---|---|
| **Stress Variability** | Lightning amplitude, switching surge magnitude, fault levels | $\sigma_S \approx 20\% - 50\%$ of mean |
| **Strength Variability** | Manufacturing defects, aging, contamination, temperature | $\sigma_C \approx 5\% - 10\%$ of mean |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Because **both** the threat (overvoltage) and the protection (insulation strength) are statistical in nature, the failure of insulation is **inevitable** in the limit — only the *probability* can be managed. The art of insulation coordination is to push this probability to an economically and socially acceptable minimum.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Conventional acceptable risk level:** $R = 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ per year per substation component
- **IEC 60071 standard risk target:** Approximately $0.001\%$ to $0.01\%$ per year for EHV equipment
- **Reference impulse waveshapes:** $1.2/50 \ \mu s$ (lightning impulse), $250/2500 \ \mu s$ (switching impulse)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Statistical Foundation of Insulation Failure

The risk of failure arises from the **overlap** of two probability distributions plotted on a common voltage axis. This is the single most important concept in statistical insulation coordination.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Overlap Principle:** When the stress distribution $f_S(s)$ and the strength distribution $f_C(c)$ are drawn on the same voltage axis, the **area of overlap** between them represents the **risk of failure**. Wider distributions = greater overlap = higher risk for the same nominal safety margin.

### 2.2 The Two Fundamental Statistical Distribution Models

#### Model A: Normal (Gaussian) Distribution

Used when the failure mechanism is dominated by **multiple small random factors** (cumulative aging, manufacturing tolerances).

$$f_X(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma_X \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(x - X_{50})^2}{2\sigma_X^2}\right]$$

The **cumulative distribution function (CDF)** gives the probability of failure at or below voltage $x$:

$$F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(u) \, du = \Phi\!\left(\frac{x - X_{50}}{\sigma_X}\right)$$

Where $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the standard normal cumulative function.

#### Model B: Weibull Distribution (Most Common for Dielectric Breakdown)

Used when the failure mechanism follows a **weakest-link** model — typical for electrical breakdown where failure initiates at the most vulnerable point.

$$F_C(c) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{c - c_0}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right] \quad \text{for } c > c_0$$

$$f_C(c) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{c - c_0}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{c - c_0}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

Where:
- $c_0$ = Location parameter (minimum breakdown voltage, often $\approx 0$ for solid dielectrics)
- $\eta$ = Scale parameter (related to the 63.2% failure voltage)
- $\beta$ = Shape parameter (typically $5$ to $15$ for transformer oil, $10$ to $20$ for $\text{SF}_6$)

### 2.3 General Risk of Failure Formula (Distribution-Independent)

For **any** two statistical distributions, the risk of failure is computed as:

$$R = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f_S(s) \cdot F_C(s) \, ds$$

**Interpretation of the integrand:**
- $f_S(s) \, ds$ = probability that the stress lies in the infinitesimal range $[s, s+ds]$
- $F_C(s)$ = probability that the insulation fails at or below stress level $s$
- Their **product** = joint probability that stress occurs at level $s$ **AND** insulation cannot withstand it
- **Integration** over all $s$ = total probability of failure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative but mathematically equivalent form:**
> $$R = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f_C(c) \cdot \left[1 - F_S(c)\right] dc$$
> This is the same integral re-expressed by conditioning on the strength variable. It is useful when the strength distribution is known but stress CDF is easier to evaluate.

### 2.4 The Critical Safety Margin Equation

For the **normal distribution model**, the risk of failure depends on a single normalized quantity called the **Statistical Safety Margin** (or sometimes the *critical distance*):

$$m = \frac{C_{50} - S_{50}}{\sqrt{\sigma_S^2 + \sigma_C^2}}$$

Where:
- $C_{50}$ = 50% withstand voltage of insulation (mean dielectric strength)
- $S_{50}$ = 50% statistical overvoltage (mean expected stress)
- $\sigma_C$ = Standard deviation of insulation strength
- $\sigma_S$ = Standard deviation of overvoltage stress

The risk of failure then becomes:

$$\boxed{R = \Phi(-m) = 1 - \Phi(m)}$$

Where $\Phi$ is the **standard normal cumulative distribution function**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk of Failure (General) | $R = P(S > C) = \int f_S(s) F_C(s) \, ds$ | Any distribution pair |
| 2 | Normal PDF | $f_X(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$ | Gaussian stress/strength |
| 3 | Normal CDF | $F_X(x) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | Probability of failure $\leq x$ |
| 4 | Weibull CDF | $F_C(c) = 1 - \exp[-(c-c_0)/\eta]^{\beta}$ | Weakest-link breakdown |
| 5 | Weibull PDF | $f_C(c) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}[(c-c_0)/\eta]^{\beta-1} \exp[-(c-c_0)/\eta]^{\beta}$ | Dielectric strength scatter |
| 6 | Statistical Safety Margin | $m = (C_{50}-S_{50})/\sqrt{\sigma_S^2+\sigma_C^2}$ | Normalized design margin |
| 7 | Risk via Normal Margin | $R = \Phi(-m)$ | Quick design reference |
| 8 | Conventional Withstand Voltage | $V_{CW} = C_{50} - k \cdot \sigma_C$ | $k = 3$ gives $0.135\%$ failure |
| 9 | Statistical Withstand Voltage | $V_{SW}$ at $90\%$ or $97\%$ confidence | Insulation level definition |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of This Framework

The risk-of-failure concept is the **engine** behind the following real-world engineering decisions:

1. **Selection of Basic Insulation Level (BIL/Basic Switching Level, BSL)** for transformers, bushings, and surge arresters.
2. **Coordination of surge arrester ratings** with protected equipment withstand — the arrester's *protective level* must sit well below the equipment's *statistical withstand* in the overlap region.
3. **Economic optimization** of insulation thickness — a thicker insulation costs more in materials and cooling but lowers risk; an optimal thickness minimizes **(cost of insulation + cost of expected failures)**.
4. **Setting insulation test voltages** in the laboratory — the $90\%$ or $97\%$ withstand voltage becomes the type-test level.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the General Risk of Failure Integral

**Starting Point:** Define risk as the probability that the stress exceeds the strength.

**Step 1 — Write the joint probability space.**
The two random variables $S$ (stress) and $C$ (strength) form a 2D probability space. The event "failure" corresponds to the region $S > C$ in this space.

$$R = P(S > C) = \iint\limits_{s>c} f_{S,C}(s,c) \, ds \, dc$$

**Step 2 — Assume statistical independence.**
In insulation coordination, the cause of stress (lightning, switching) is **independent** of the manufacturing/aging factors that determine strength. Therefore:

$$f_{S,C}(s,c) = f_S(s) \cdot f_C(c)$$

**Step 3 — Restructure the integration limits.**
The region $s > c$ can be integrated by fixing $s$ and allowing $c$ to range from $-\infty$ to $s$:

$$R = \int_{s=-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{c=-\infty}^{s} f_S(s) \cdot f_C(c) \, dc \, ds$$

**Step 4 — Separate the integrals and recognize the inner CDF.**
The inner integral is the definition of the CDF of $C$ evaluated at $s$:

$$\int_{c=-\infty}^{s} f_C(c) \, dc = F_C(s)$$

**Step 5 — Write the final single-integral form.**

$$\boxed{R = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f_S(s) \cdot F_C(s) \, ds}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the alternative form (conditioning on C).**
Starting from the same joint PDF, we fix $c$ and let $s$ range from $c$ to $+\infty$:

$$R = \int_{c=-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{s=c}^{+\infty} f_S(s) f_C(c) \, ds \, dc = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f_C(c) \cdot [1 - F_S(c)] \, dc$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two forms?** Both are mathematically identical but differ in **computational ease**. Form A is preferred when $F_C(s)$ is known in closed form (typical for Weibull). Form B is preferred when the stress CDF is given as an empirical curve from field measurements.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Normal-Distribution Risk Formula

Assume both stress and strength follow **independent normal distributions**:

$$S \sim \mathcal{N}(S_{50}, \sigma_S^2), \qquad C \sim \mathcal{N}(C_{50}, \sigma_C^2)$$

**Step 1 — Define the difference random variable.**
Failure occurs when $S > C$, equivalently when $D = C - S < 0$. Since the difference of two independent normal variables is also normal:

$$D = C - S \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_D, \sigma_D^2)$$

$$\mu_D = C_{50} - S_{50}, \qquad \sigma_D^2 = \sigma_C^2 + \sigma_S^2$$

**Step 2 — Standardize the difference.**
Convert to the standard normal variable $Z$:

$$Z = \frac{D - \mu_D}{\sigma_D} = \frac{(C-S) - (C_{50}-S_{50})}{\sqrt{\sigma_C^2 + \sigma_S^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Express risk in terms of $Z$.**
Failure requires $D < 0$, so:

$$R = P(D < 0) = P\!\left(Z < \frac{0 - (C_{50} - S_{50})}{\sqrt{\sigma_C^2 + \sigma_S^2}}\right) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{S_{50} - C_{50}}{\sqrt{\sigma_S^2 + \sigma_C^2}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Define the statistical safety margin.**
Recognize the *negation* of the argument as the safety margin:

$$m = \frac{C_{50} - S_{50}}{\sqrt{\sigma_S^2 + \sigma_C^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Final compact result.**

$$\boxed{R = \Phi(-m) = 1 - \Phi(m)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reading:** A margin of $m = 3.72$ corresponds to $R = 10^{-4}$, while $m = 4.75$ corresponds to $R = 10^{-6}$. These are the typical design targets in IEC 60071.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (For Exam Practice)

**Problem:** A 400 kV transformer has a 50% lightning impulse withstand of $C_{50} = 1300$ kV with $\sigma_C = 65$ kV. The expected statistical overvoltage at its terminals has $S_{50} = 950$ kV with $\sigma_S = 95$ kV. Both distributions are normal. Find the risk of failure.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the safety margin.**

$$m = \frac{C_{50} - S_{50}}{\sqrt{\sigma_C^2 + \sigma_S^2}} = \frac{1300 - 950}{\sqrt{65^2 + 95^2}}$$

$$m = \frac{350}{\sqrt{4225 + 9025}} = \frac{350}{\sqrt{13250}} = \frac{350}{115.10}$$

$$m = 3.041$$

**Step 2 — Look up the standard normal CDF.**

$$R = \Phi(-3.041) = 1 - \Phi(3.041) \approx 1 - 0.99883 = 0.00117$$

**Step 3 — Express as percentage.**

$$R \approx 0.117\% \quad \text{or} \quad 1.17 \times 10^{-3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This design carries a risk of approximately **1.17 failures per 1000 years of operation** for this single insulation element. If the system has 10 such elements in parallel, the system-level risk is roughly $10 \times 1.17 \times 10^{-3} = 1.17\%$, which is unacceptable — the engineer would need to either increase $C_{50}$ or reduce $S_{50}$ (e.g., install a better surge arrester).

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — How to Reduce Risk

| Lever to Reduce $R$ | Mechanism | Typical Cost Impact | Risk Reduction Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase insulation thickness | Raises $C_{50}$ proportionally | High (material, size, weight) | Moderate |
| Improve manufacturing QC | Reduces $\sigma_C$ | Medium | High (non-linear) |
| Install surge arresters | Reduces $S_{50}$ and $\sigma_S$ | Low to Medium | Very High |
| Shield overhead lines | Reduces direct lightning stress | Medium | High |
| Use $\text{SF}_6$ / oil-paper better dielectrics | Raises $C_{50}$, tightens $\beta$ | High | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Risk-of-Failure Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Overvoltage Events: Lightning, Switching, Fault] --> B[Statistical Stress Distribution f_S s]
    C[Insulation Manufacturing + Aging + Environment] --> D[Statistical Strength Distribution f_C c]
    B --> E[Overlap Region Computation]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Integration of f_S s times F_C s ds]
    F --> G[Numerical Risk Value R]
    G --> H{R greater than R_target?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Design Iteration: Increase C_50 or Reduce S_50]
    I --> B
    I --> D
    H -- No --> J[Design Acceptable: Lock in BIL, BSL, Arrester Rating]
    J --> K[Type Tests at 90 percent or 97 percent Withstand Level]
```

### 4.2 Distribution Overlap Schematic (Mermaid Block Mapping)

The following block diagram shows the **functional flow** when the two statistical curves are numerically convolved to produce the risk curve. *(This replaces a hand-drawn bell-curve overlap diagram.)*

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputDistributions
        S1[Stress PDF: f_S s, mean S_50, std sigma_S]
        C1[Strength PDF: f_C c, mean C_50, std sigma_C]
    end
    subgraph OverlapZone
        O1[Identify Crossing Region: s_1 to s_2]
        O2[Compute Joint Probability: f_S s times F_C s]
    end
    subgraph Output
        R1[Integrate Overlap: R = integral f_S F_C ds]
        R2[Statistical Safety Margin: m = C_50 minus S_50 over sqrt sigma_C^2 + sigma_S^2]
        R3[Risk Lookup: R = Phi of minus m]
    end
    S1 --> O1
    C1 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> R1
    S1 --> R2
    C1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R1 --> R3
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Setting Insulation Level

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Define System Voltage and Exposure] --> Q1{Determine Statistical Overvoltage: S_50 and sigma_S}
    Q1 --> Q2{Choose Acceptable Risk: 10^-3 to 10^-6}
    Q2 --> Q3[Compute Required Safety Margin: m from Phi^-1]
    Q3 --> Q4[Compute Required C_50: C_50 = S_50 + m times sqrt sigma_C^2 + sigma_S^2]
    Q4 --> Q5[Select Insulator with C_50 greater than Required]
    Q5 --> Q6[Verify via Type Tests: 90 percent or 97 percent Withstand]
    Q6 --> End[Insulation Level Locked: BIL and BSL Finalized]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q:** Define the term *Risk of Failure* in the context of high voltage insulation coordination. Why is it a statistical concept rather than a deterministic one?

**Model Answer:**
Risk of failure $R$ is defined as the probability that the electrical stress (overvoltage) acting on an insulation system exceeds its dielectric withstand strength during service: $R = P(S > C)$. It is inherently statistical because:
1. Overvoltages (lightning, switching) occur with random magnitudes described by a probability distribution $f_S(s)$.
2. Insulation strength varies between identical units due to manufacturing tolerances, material inhomogeneities, aging, temperature, and contamination, described by $f_C(c)$.
3. The overlap area of these two distributions determines the failure probability. A deterministic approach ignores this overlap and therefore either over-designs (wasteful) or under-designs (unsafe).

#### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q:** What is the *statistical safety margin*? Write its expression for normal distributions and state its significance.

**Model Answer:**
The statistical safety margin is a normalized measure of separation between the mean strength and mean stress, scaled by the combined standard deviation:

$$m = \frac{C_{50} - S_{50}}{\sqrt{\sigma_S^2 + \sigma_C^2}}$$

**Significance:** It directly determines the risk of failure via $R = \Phi(-m)$. A larger $m$ corresponds to lower risk. Typical design targets: $m \approx 3.7$ for $R = 10^{-4}$ and $m \approx 4.75$ for $R = 10^{-6}$. It allows engineers to express safety in a single dimensionless number that is independent of voltage level.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the general expression for the risk of failure in terms of the stress PDF $f_S(s)$ and the strength CDF $F_C(c)$, clearly stating the assumption of statistical independence. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a 220 kV class equipment, the insulation has $C_{50} = 700$ kV and $\sigma_C = 35$ kV. The expected overvoltage has $S_{50} = 520$ kV and $\sigma_S = 60$ kV. Assuming both normal and independent, compute the risk of failure. Comment on whether this design is acceptable by IEC 60071 standards. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Define the failure event in probability space.

$$R = P(S > C)$$

**Step 2:** Express using the joint PDF of $S$ and $C$. Since the system is two-dimensional, integrate over the region where $s > c$:

$$R = \iint\limits_{s > c} f_{S,C}(s,c) \, ds \, dc$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **statistical independence** assumption. Overvoltage events (driven by atmospheric/grid conditions) are independent of insulation manufacturing/aging conditions:

$$f_{S,C}(s,c) = f_S(s) \cdot f_C(c)$$

**Step 4:** Restructure as an iterated integral by fixing $s$ first:

$$R = \int_{s=-\infty}^{+\infty} f_S(s) \left[ \int_{c=-\infty}^{s} f_C(c) \, dc \right] ds$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the inner integral as the CDF of strength evaluated at $s$:

$$F_C(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{s} f_C(c) \, dc$$

**Step 6:** Final compact result:

$$\boxed{R = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f_S(s) \cdot F_C(s) \, ds}$$

**Physical meaning of each term:**
- $f_S(s) \, ds$: probability that overvoltage falls in $[s, s+ds]$
- $F_C(s)$: probability that insulation cannot survive stress level $s$
- Product: joint probability of "that stress occurs" AND "insulation cannot take it"
- Integral: total failure probability over all possible stress levels

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the statistical safety margin.

$$m = \frac{C_{50} - S_{50}}{\sqrt{\sigma_C^2 + \sigma_S^2}} = \frac{700 - 520}{\sqrt{35^2 + 60^2}}$$

$$m = \frac{180}{\sqrt{1225 + 3600}} = \frac{180}{\sqrt{4825}} = \frac{180}{69.46} = 2.592$$

**Step 2:** Look up the standard normal CDF.

$$R = \Phi(-2.592) = 1 - \Phi(2.592)$$

From standard normal tables, $\Phi(2.59) \approx 0.9952$, so:

$$R \approx 1 - 0.9952 = 0.0048 = 4.8 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3:** Comparison with IEC 60071.

IEC 60071 typically targets a risk level in the range of $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ for EHV equipment. The calculated $R \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-3}$ is **about 5 to 50 times higher** than the recommended target.

**Step 4:** Engineering recommendation.
- **Option 1:** Install a surge arrester to reduce $S_{50}$ from 520 kV to about 450 kV and tighten $\sigma_S$ to 30 kV.
- **Option 2:** Improve insulation quality to raise $C_{50}$ to 780 kV and reduce $\sigma_C$ to 25 kV.
- **Verification (Option 1):**

$$m_{\text{new}} = \frac{700 - 450}{\sqrt{35^2 + 30^2}} = \frac{250}{\sqrt{1225 + 900}} = \frac{250}{46.10} = 5.42$$

$$R_{\text{new}} = \Phi(-5.42) \approx 3 \times 10^{-8} \quad \text{(Excellent — well within IEC limits)}$$

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the aid of suitable expressions, explain how the statistical nature of overvoltages and dielectric strength leads to a finite risk of failure. Define statistical safety margin. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A surge arrester is rated such that the statistical overvoltage at the protected equipment has $S_{50} = 800$ kV with $\sigma_S = 100$ kV. The equipment insulation has $C_{50} = 1200$ kV with $\sigma_C = 50$ kV. Both are normal. (i) Calculate the statistical safety margin. (ii) Determine the risk of failure. (iii) Find the *conventional withstand voltage* (3-sigma withstand). *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Concept Explanation:**

In real power systems, two statistical phenomena coexist:

1. **Overvoltages** due to lightning, switching operations, and faults occur with widely varying magnitudes. Their distribution $f_S(s)$ is typically right-skewed and may be modeled as normal or log-normal.

2. **Dielectric strength** of nominally identical insulation samples shows scatter due to microscopic defects, aging, temperature, and contamination. Their distribution $f_C(c)$ is often modeled as normal or Weibull.

When these two distributions are plotted on a common voltage axis, they **overlap** in a finite region. Within this overlap region, the stress can exceed the strength — causing insulation failure. The area under the product $f_S(s) \cdot F_C(s)$ integrated over all $s$ is the **risk of failure**, a non-zero number that quantifies the overlap.

The **statistical safety margin** $m$ is the normalized distance between the means of the two distributions, scaled by the combined standard deviation:

$$m = \frac{C_{50} - S_{50}}{\sqrt{\sigma_S^2 + \sigma_C^2}}$$

A larger $m$ means a smaller overlap and therefore a lower risk. For normal distributions, $R = \Phi(-m)$ is read directly from standard normal tables.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**(i) Statistical safety margin:**

$$m = \frac{C_{50} - S_{50}}{\sqrt{\sigma_C^2 + \sigma_S^2}} = \frac{1200 - 800}{\sqrt{50^2 + 100^2}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{2500 + 10000}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{12500}} = \frac{400}{111.80} = 3.578$$

**(ii) Risk of failure:**

$$R = \Phi(-3.578) = 1 - \Phi(3.578)$$

From tables, $\Phi(3.58) \approx 0.99983$, so:

$$R \approx 1 - 0.99983 = 1.7 \times 10^{-4}$$

This corresponds to a risk of **1.7 failures per 10,000 years** — a very good design, well within IEC 60071 recommendations for EHV equipment.

**(iii) Conventional withstand voltage:**

By IEC convention, the *conventional withstand voltage* is the insulation strength corresponding to a $0.135\%$ failure probability, which is the 3-sigma point below the mean:

$$V_{CW} = C_{50} - 3 \cdot \sigma_C = 1200 - 3(50) = 1200 - 150 = 1050 \text{ kV}$$

This is the voltage level that would be applied in a standard impulse type test.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Losing Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the square root** in the denominator of $m$ — students often write $\sigma_S + \sigma_C$ instead of $\sqrt{\sigma_S^2 + \sigma_C^2}$. This gives the wrong normalized margin and an incorrect $R$.
> 2. **Inverting the CDF direction** — confusing $\Phi(m)$ with $\Phi(-m)$ swaps failure and survival probabilities. Always remember: failure = $\Phi(-m)$.
> 3. **Assuming independence without stating it** — in Part (a), full marks require explicitly stating that $f_{S,C}(s,c) = f_S(s) \cdot f_C(c)$ because $S$ and $C$ are independent.
> 4. **Unit errors in the numerical part** — kV cancels in the safety margin (dimensionless), but stress and strength values must be in **the same units** (do not mix kV peak with kV rms).
> 5. **Forgetting the 3-sigma factor** in the conventional withstand — it is exactly $3\sigma$ below the mean, not $2\sigma$ or $4\sigma$.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk of Failure — Rapid Revision Bullets**

- **Definition:** $R = P(S > C)$ — probability that overvoltage stress exceeds insulation strength.
- **Why statistical:** Both $S$ (stress) and $C$ (strength) are random variables; their distributions overlap.
- **General integral:** $R = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f_S(s) \cdot F_C(s) \, ds$ — assumes $S$ and $C$ are **independent**.
- **Alternative form:** $R = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f_C(c) \cdot [1 - F_S(c)] \, dc$.
- **Normal distribution** for stress and strength leads to a single-parameter risk formula.
- **Statistical safety margin:** $m = (C_{50} - S_{50}) / \sqrt{\sigma_S^2 + \sigma_C^2}$ — **always use the square root of sum of squares**, never the sum of standard deviations.
- **Risk via normal margin:** $R = \Phi(-m)$, where $\Phi$ is the standard normal CDF.
- **Typical targets:** $m \approx 3.7 \Rightarrow R = 10^{-4}$; $m \approx 4.75 \Rightarrow R = 10^{-6}$.
- **Weibull distribution:** Used for weakest-link dielectric failure; characterized by shape $\beta$, scale $\eta$, and location $c_0$.
- **Conventional withstand voltage:** $V_{CW} = C_{50} - 3\sigma_C$ — corresponds to $0.135\%$ intrinsic failure probability.
- **Statistical withstand voltage:** The voltage at which insulation has a defined survival probability (commonly $90\%$ or $97\%$).
- **Design levers to lower risk:** (1) raise $C_{50}$ (better insulation), (2) reduce $\sigma_C$ (tighter QC), (3) reduce $S_{50}$ and $\sigma_S$ (arresters, shielding), (4) increase redundancy.
- **IEC 60071 standard** specifies acceptable risk levels of order $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ per year for EHV insulation.
- **Common exam trap:** Always check whether stress and strength are in **peak** or **rms** units and convert consistently.
- **Always state the independence assumption** explicitly when writing the joint PDF factorization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Withstand Voltage** of an electrical equipment or insulation is defined as the **maximum voltage** that the equipment can be subjected to without undergoing dielectric breakdown, flashover, or any form of insulation failure, under specified test conditions and for a defined duration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Definition (Use Verbatim):**
> *"The required withstand voltage is the test voltage level (expressed in peak kV or p.u. of the system's maximum operating voltage) which an insulation structure must be able to endure reliably throughout its operational life, considering the statistical distribution of both the overvoltages appearing in the network and the dielectric strength of the insulation."*

The **Determination of Required Withstand Voltage** is the systematic engineering procedure used by insulation coordination engineers to:

1. Quantify the **amplitude, waveshape, and frequency** of overvoltages (lightning, switching, temporary, very fast transient).
2. Characterize the **statistical dielectric strength** of the insulation (self-restoring and non-self-restoring types).
3. Apply **deterministic safety factors** OR **statistical probability methods** to fix the test voltage.
4. Apply **atmospheric and altitude correction factors** as per **IEC 60060-1** and **IS 2071**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> This topic sits at the heart of **Insulation Coordination** — the engineering discipline that ensures the cost of protection is balanced against the cost of insulation, while keeping the **risk of failure** (R) acceptably low (typically **R ≤ 0.001 to 0.0001**, i.e., 1 in 1000 to 1 in 10000 operations).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are designing a **bridge** in Kerala that must withstand the heaviest monsoon flooding in a 100-year period. The process goes like this:

| Engineering Step | Bridge Analogy | High-Voltage Engineering Equivalent |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Measure the maximum expected water level (flood data) | Gauge river levels over 50 years | Measure **Maximum System Voltage (Vsm)** and **overvoltage factor (K)** |
| Define "design flood" + safety margin | Add 1.5 m freeboard above highest flood | Apply **safety margin / statistical margin** over the overvoltage peak |
| Test the bridge model in a hydraulic lab | Subject scaled model to water flow | Apply **1.2/50 µs Lightning Impulse** or **250/2500 µs Switching Impulse** in the HV lab |
| Quality check the actual bridge | Inspect concrete, steel, joints | Apply **routine / type tests** at the manufactured equipment |

> [!TIP]
> **The Core Insight:** Just as a bridge is not designed for the *highest conceivable flood in infinity* (which would be infinitely expensive), insulation is not designed for the *worst conceivable overvoltage* — it is designed for a value where the **probability of failure × cost of failure = acceptable risk**. This is the philosophical foundation of statistical insulation coordination.

**The "Determination of Required Withstand Voltage" is therefore the engineering calculation that converts abstract overvoltage data into a concrete, testable kV rating for a transformer, breaker, insulator string, or bushing.**

---

## 1.3 The Two Philosophies of Determination

There are two fundamentally different approaches used in KTU-level problems:

### A. Conventional (Deterministic) Method
- Uses **fixed multiplication factors** over the maximum system voltage.
- **No statistical input** is used.
- Equations are of the form: **V_withstand = K × V_max × correction factor**.
- K is typically **3 to 5** for lightning impulse and **2 to 2.5** for switching impulse.
- Used in older systems and for **non-self-restoring insulation** (transformer windings, generator coils, cable dielectrics) where statistical testing would destroy the equipment.

### B. Statistical Method
- Treats both **overvoltages** and **insulation strength** as **random variables** with normal (Gaussian) distributions.
- Computes the **probability of failure (P_f)** or **risk of failure (R)**.
- Used for **self-restoring insulation** (air insulation, insulator strings, transmission line insulation, SF₆ gaps) where repeated flashover tests are possible.
- The number of standard deviations (σ) used below the 50% flashover voltage is decided by the **acceptable risk**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse the **"Withstand Voltage" (V_w)** with the **"50% Flashover Voltage (V₅₀)"**. They are **NOT the same**. The withstand voltage is the voltage at which the **probability of flashover is acceptably low (say, 2% or 10%)**, while V₅₀ is the voltage at which **50% of impulses cause flashover**. For a Gaussian distribution, **V_w = V₅₀ − n·σ** where **n = 2 to 3** typically.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Must Memorize)

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value | Source / Standard |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Standard atmospheric pressure | p₀ | **101.325 kPa** | IEC 60060-1 |
| Standard temperature | t₀ | **20 °C** | IEC 60060-1 |
| Standard absolute humidity | h₀ | **11 g/m³** | IEC 60060-1 |
| Altitude correction factor (per 1000 m above 1000 m) | m | **≈ 1.0 to 1.1** | IS 2071 |
| Standard lightning impulse waveshape | — | **1.2/50 µs** | IEC 60060-1 |
| Standard switching impulse waveshape | — | **250/2500 µs** | IEC 60060-1 |
| Statistical safety margin (self-restoring) | n | **2σ to 3σ** below V₅₀ | IEC 60071 |
| Statistical safety margin (non-self-restoring) | n | **0σ (single-shot test)** | IEC 60071 |
| Conventional lightning impulse safety factor (K) | K | **≈ 1.1 × p.u. of LIOV** | IS 2165 |
| Acceptable risk of failure (typical) | R | **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁴** | IEEE Std 1313 |

---

## 1.5 Block-Level Overview of the Determination Procedure

The complete process can be summarized as:

> **Step 1:** Identify the **highest system voltage (U_s)** and **temporary overvoltage (TOV)** level.
> **Step 2:** Determine the **lightning overvoltage (LIOV)** and **switching overvoltage (SIOV)** using system studies or field data.
> **Step 3:** Decide on the **type of insulation** (self-restoring or non-self-restoring).
> **Step 4:** Apply the **statistical formula** (for self-restoring) or **conventional factor** (for non-self-restoring).
> **Step 5:** Apply **atmospheric correction factor (K_t)** and **altitude correction factor (K_a)**.
> **Step 6:** Round off to the **standard BIL / BSL** from IEC 60071-1 tables.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Concept Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Step 1 — Establishing the Reference Voltage

The foundation of the entire calculation is the **Maximum System Voltage (U_s)**. This is NOT the nominal voltage; it is the **upper limit of the operating voltage range**, including sustained overvoltages due to load rejection, Ferranti effect, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> For a 220 kV nominal system, **U_s = 245 kV** (per IS / IEC). For a 400 kV system, **U_s = 420 kV**.

The **Basic Impulse Insulation Level (BIL)** and **Basic Switching Impulse Level (BSL)** are expressed in **kV peak**.

---

### 2.1.2 Step 2 — Classification of Overvoltages

The IEC 60071-1 classifies overvoltages into four categories:

| Class | Type | Origin | Duration | Frequency of Occurrence |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **A** | Lightning Impulse | Direct/indirect lightning stroke | < 1 ms | Rare |
| **B** | Switching Impulse | Line energization, fault clearing, load rejection | 0.1 to 10 ms | Occasional |
| **C** | Temporary (TOV) | Load rejection, resonance, ground fault | 0.1 s to 1 hr | Frequent |
| **D** | Very Fast Transient (VFTO) | GIS disconnector operation | < 1 µs | Frequent in GIS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The withstand voltage must be determined for **each class separately**, because the dielectric strength of an insulation depends strongly on the **waveshape** of the applied voltage.

---

### 2.1.3 Step 3 — Choice of Method: Statistical vs Conventional

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **statistical approach**. The decision rule is:

- **Self-restoring insulation** (air gaps, porcelain/glass insulators, SF₆ gas, transmission line insulation) → **Statistical Method** is preferred because repeated flashover tests do not damage the insulation.

- **Non-self-restoring insulation** (oil-paper, XLPE cables, transformer windings, generator coils, capacitor dielectrics) → **Conventional Method** is mandatory because a flashover test destroys the insulation. A single shot test is performed at the **Withstand Level (V_w)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Idea of Statistical Method:**
> The **insulation strength V_i** is a random variable with mean μ_i and standard deviation σ_i.
> The **overvoltage V_o** is a random variable with mean μ_o and standard deviation σ_o.
> The insulation will fail whenever **V_o > V_i**.
> The **Risk of Failure (R)** is therefore:
>
> $$ R = P(V_o > V_i) = P(\text{overvoltage exceeds insulation strength}) $$

This integral, when both distributions are Gaussian, reduces to a **closed-form expression** using the standard normal distribution function.

---

### 2.1.4 Step 4 — The Normal Distribution Model

For a normal (Gaussian) random variable **X** with mean **μ** and standard deviation **σ**, the probability density function is:

$$ f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) $$

In the standard normal form (Z = (x − μ)/σ):

$$ \phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{z^2}{2}\right) $$

The cumulative distribution function (probability that X ≤ x):

$$ \Phi(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{z} \phi(t)\,dt = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{z}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] $$

---

### 2.1.5 Step 5 — The Risk of Failure Formula (The Heart of the Topic)

Let:
- **f_o(V)** = PDF of overvoltage, with mean μ_o and standard deviation σ_o.
- **f_i(V)** = PDF of insulation strength, with mean μ_i (≈ V₅₀) and standard deviation σ_i.

The **risk of failure (R)** per operation is:

$$ R = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_o(V) \left[\int_{-\infty}^{V} f_i(u)\,du\right] dV $$

For two independent Gaussian distributions, this evaluates to:

$$ R = \Phi\left(\frac{\mu_o - \mu_i}{\sqrt{\sigma_o^2 + \sigma_i^2}}\right) = \Phi(-K) $$

where **K** is the **Statistical Safety Factor** (also called the **critical factor**):

$$ K = \frac{\mu_i - \mu_o}{\sqrt{\sigma_o^2 + \sigma_i^2}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Jargon:** The quantity **K** is also written as **"δ"** in some textbooks and is called the **"statistical safety margin in standard deviation units."**
> The risk of failure **R = Φ(−K)** decreases rapidly as K increases:
> - K = 0 → R = 0.5 (50% failure)
> - K = 2 → R ≈ 0.0228 (2.28% failure)
> - K = 3 → R ≈ 0.00135 (0.135% failure)
> - K = 4 → R ≈ 0.0000317 (very low)
> - K = 5 → R ≈ 0.0000003 (negligible)

---

### 2.1.6 Step 6 — The Withstand Voltage from the Statistical View

The **Statistical Withstand Voltage (V_w)** is defined as the voltage at which the probability of flashover is acceptably low. For self-restoring insulation:

$$ V_w = \mu_i - n \cdot \sigma_i = V_{50} - n \cdot \sigma_i $$

Typical values of **n** (KTU table to memorize):

| Risk R Required | n (no. of σ below V₅₀) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 10⁻¹ (10%) | 1.28 |
| 10⁻² (1%) | 2.33 |
| 10⁻³ (0.1%) | 3.09 |
| 10⁻⁴ (0.01%) | 3.72 |
| 10⁻⁵ (0.001%) | 4.27 |
| 10⁻⁶ (1 ppm) | 4.75 |

For **non-self-restoring insulation**, the single-shot test requires **V_w = μ_i − 0·σ_i** (or sometimes μ_i − 1.3σ_i if some safety margin is desired), because a single flashover is considered a total failure.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the table below. Every numerical problem in KTU Module 3 of HVE will be solved using one or more of these formulas.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $U_s$ = Max system voltage | Reference voltage for design | kV rms |
| 2 | $V_w = K \cdot U_s \cdot \sqrt{2}/\sqrt{3}$ | Conventional impulse level (rms → peak conversion) | kV peak |
| 3 | $V_w = \mu_i - n\sigma_i$ | Statistical withstand voltage | kV peak |
| 4 | $K = \dfrac{\mu_i - \mu_o}{\sqrt{\sigma_o^2 + \sigma_i^2}}$ | Statistical safety factor (dimensionless) | — |
| 5 | $R = \Phi(-K) = 1 - \Phi(K)$ | Risk of failure per impulse | dimensionless |
| 6 | $K_t = \delta^k$ | Atmospheric correction factor (IEC 60060-1) | — |
| 7 | $K_a = e^{m \cdot H/8150}$ | Altitude correction factor (H in metres) | — |
| 8 | $V_{test} = V_w \cdot K_t \cdot K_a$ | Final test voltage after correction | kV peak |
| 9 | $\sigma_i = 0.05\,V_{50}$ (typical) | Empirical σ for air insulation | kV |
| 10 | $\sigma_o = 0.10\,V_{peak}$ (typical) | Empirical σ for switching overvoltages | kV |
| 11 | $K = 1.65 - 1.78$ (LIOV) | Conventional lightning impulse factor | — |
| 12 | $K = 1.5 - 1.9$ (SIOV) | Conventional switching impulse factor | — |

> **Notation note:** In the table, `\vert` and `\mid` are used in place of the `|` character to keep Markdown table syntax intact.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Concept

The withstand voltage determined by these procedures directly drives:

1. **Transformer BIL rating stamping** on the nameplate (e.g., "BIL 900 kV").
2. **Selection of surge arresters** in substations.
3. **Insulator string design** for transmission lines (number of discs).
4. **Test specification** in HV labs (routine test voltage, type test voltage, special test voltage).
5. **Acceptance/rejection** of newly manufactured equipment in factory tests.
6. **Coordination between upstream and downstream** protection devices (insulation coordination map).

> [!TIP]
> Without this determination procedure, power systems would either be **over-engineered** (waste of copper, steel, oil) or **dangerously under-insulated** (catastrophic blackouts). It is the **economic-engineering balance** of the entire power industry.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Risk of Failure for Two Normal Distributions

### Derivation 3.1.1: Setting Up the Probability Integral

> **Physical Setup:** Let **V_o** = overvoltage random variable and **V_i** = insulation strength random variable.
> The insulation **fails** if and only if the overvoltage at some instant **exceeds** the insulation strength.
> We assume both are statistically independent, which is a standard assumption in insulation coordination.

**Step 1:** The event "failure" can be written as:

$$ \text{Failure} \iff \{V_o > V_i\} $$

**Step 2:** The probability of this joint event, by the law of total probability, is:

$$ R = P(V_o > V_i) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P(V_i < v \mid V_o = v) \cdot f_o(v)\,dv $$

**Step 3:** Since V_i and V_o are independent, the conditional probability becomes the marginal CDF:

$$ P(V_i < v \mid V_o = v) = P(V_i < v) = F_i(v) = \int_{-\infty}^{v} f_i(u)\,du $$

**Step 4:** Substituting back:

$$ R = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_o(v) \left[\int_{-\infty}^{v} f_i(u)\,du\right] dv $$

This is the **fundamental risk integral** in insulation coordination.

---

### Derivation 3.1.2: Evaluation for Gaussian Distributions

**Step 1:** Insert the Gaussian PDF for both V_o and V_i:

$$ f_o(v) = \frac{1}{\sigma_o\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(v-\mu_o)^2}{2\sigma_o^2}\right) $$

$$ f_i(u) = \frac{1}{\sigma_i\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(u-\mu_i)^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right) $$

**Step 2:** Substitute into R:

$$ R = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma_o\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(v-\mu_o)^2/(2\sigma_o^2)} \left[\int_{-\infty}^{v} \frac{1}{\sigma_i\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(u-\mu_i)^2/(2\sigma_i^2)} du\right] dv $$

**Step 3:** Use the change of variables to standard normal form for the inner integral. Let:

$$ z = \frac{u - \mu_i}{\sigma_i} \implies du = \sigma_i\,dz $$

The inner CDF becomes:

$$ F_i(v) = \int_{-\infty}^{(v-\mu_i)/\sigma_i} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2} dz = \Phi\!\left(\frac{v-\mu_i}{\sigma_i}\right) $$

**Step 4:** Now perform the outer integral. Substitute the variable for v:

$$ w = \frac{v - \mu_o}{\sigma_o} \implies dv = \sigma_o\,dw $$

The outer integral becomes:

$$ R = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \phi(w) \cdot \Phi\!\left(\frac{\sigma_o w + \mu_o - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}\right) dw $$

**Step 5:** Define the **statistical safety factor** (also called the **"criticial factor"** or **"δ"**):

$$ K = \frac{\mu_i - \mu_o}{\sqrt{\sigma_o^2 + \sigma_i^2}} $$

By a classical result in probability theory (sometimes called the **"bivariate normal integration identity"**), this integral reduces to:

$$ R = \Phi(-K) = 1 - \Phi(K) $$

where **Φ** is the standard normal cumulative distribution function.

**Step 6 (final, elegant form):**

$$ \boxed{\; R = \Phi\!\left(-\frac{\mu_i - \mu_o}{\sqrt{\sigma_o^2 + \sigma_i^2}}\right) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{\mu_i - \mu_o}{\sqrt{\sigma_o^2 + \sigma_i^2}}\right) \;} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Hint:** The minus sign inside the argument is the reason why **higher K → lower R**. The argument becomes large and negative, and Φ of a large negative number is very small (tends to 0).

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Atmospheric and Altitude Correction

### Derivation 3.2.1: Atmospheric Correction Factor (K_t)

Per **IEC 60060-1**, the withstand voltage depends on the air density (δ) and humidity (h). The correction factor is:

$$ K_t = \delta^m $$

where **m** is a function of the **type of voltage** and the **polarity**, given by IEC tables. For a **positive lightning impulse**, m ≈ 1.0. For **switching impulse**, m ≈ 0.75 to 0.9.

The air density factor δ is:

$$ \delta = \frac{p}{p_0} \cdot \frac{273 + t_0}{273 + t} = \frac{p}{p_0} \cdot \frac{293}{273 + t} $$

with:
- p = ambient pressure (kPa)
- t = ambient temperature (°C)
- p₀ = 101.325 kPa
- t₀ = 20 °C

### Derivation 3.2.2: Altitude Correction Factor (K_a)

For altitudes above 1000 m, the withstand voltage is **increased** to compensate for the reduced air density:

$$ K_a = e^{m \cdot H/8150} $$

where **H** is the altitude above sea level in metres, and **m** is the same exponent used in K_t.

**For Kerala (H ≈ 0 to 2700 m — Munnar, Wayanad):** K_a ranges from **1.00 (coastal)** to **≈ 1.28 (Munnar plateau)**.

The **final test voltage** is therefore:

$$ V_{test} = V_w \cdot K_t \cdot K_a $$

> [!NOTE]
> This **increases** the test voltage for low-density air (K_a > 1), so the insulation that is installed at high altitude is **tested at a higher kV** than its nameplate rating, ensuring it can survive the local lower-density environment.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Statistical Determination of Withstand Voltage

### Problem Statement (KTU-style)

A 400 kV system has a switching overvoltage distribution that is normal with mean **μ_o = 2.6 p.u.** and standard deviation **σ_o = 0.25 p.u.** The self-restoring insulation (air gap) has a 50% flashover voltage **V₅₀ = 3.5 p.u.** and a standard deviation **σ_i = 0.07 p.u.** Determine:

1. The statistical safety factor K.
2. The risk of failure R.
3. The statistical withstand voltage if the acceptable risk is **10⁻³**.
4. The required test voltage at an altitude of 1500 m, given that the standard temperature/pressure correction is **K_t = 1.05** and **m = 0.75** for switching impulse.

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute the statistical safety factor K**

Using the derived formula:

$$ K = \frac{\mu_i - \mu_o}{\sqrt{\sigma_o^2 + \sigma_i^2}} = \frac{3.5 - 2.6}{\sqrt{(0.25)^2 + (0.07)^2}} $$

$$ \sqrt{(0.25)^2 + (0.07)^2} = \sqrt{0.0625 + 0.0049} = \sqrt{0.0674} = 0.2596 $$

$$ K = \frac{0.9}{0.2596} = 3.466 $$

**Step 2: Compute the risk of failure R**

$$ R = \Phi(-K) = \Phi(-3.466) $$

From standard normal tables:

$$ \Phi(-3.47) \approx 0.00026 = 2.6 \times 10^{-4} $$

So the **risk of failure is 0.026%** ≈ 1 in 3800 operations. This is already an excellent design.

**Step 3: Compute the statistical withstand voltage for R = 10⁻³**
