$$0 = L \frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + R \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{i}{C}$$

This is a **second-order linear ordinary differential equation** whose solution depends on the damping ratio $\zeta$.

Define:

$$\alpha = \frac{R}{2L} \quad \text{(Neper frequency, damping coefficient)}$$

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \quad \text{(undamped natural frequency)}$$

$$\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2} \quad \text{(damped natural frequency)}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{\alpha}{\omega_0} = \frac{R}{2}\sqrt{\frac{C}{L}} \quad \text{(damping ratio)}$$

### 2.4 The Three Damping Regimes

| Regime | Condition | Waveform Shape | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Underdamped** ($\zeta < 1$) | $R^2 < 4L/C$ | Overshoot + oscillations | Rare for 8/20 µs; used for oscillating impulse |
| **Critically damped** ($\zeta = 1$) | $R^2 = 4L/C$ | Fastest non-oscillatory rise | Ideal transient limit |
| **Overdamped** ($\zeta > 1$) | $R^2 > 4L/C$ | Slow, smooth pulse | **Standard 8/20 µs operation** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard 8/20 µs impulse current waveform is **heavily overdamped** (typical $\zeta \approx 0.7$ to $1.2$ in well-designed generators), so that oscillations on the tail are minimized and the decay is monotonic after the peak.

### 2.5 Solution for the Overdamped Case ($\zeta > 1$)

The general solution for an overdamped RLC discharge starting from initial voltage $V_0$ and zero initial current is:

$$i(t) = \frac{V_0}{L(\lambda_2 - \lambda_1)} \left(e^{\lambda_1 t} - e^{\lambda_2 t}\right)$$

where the two real, negative roots are:

$$\lambda_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2} = -\frac{R}{2L} \pm \sqrt{\frac{R^2}{4L^2} - \frac{1}{LC}}$$

### 2.6 Solution for the Underdamped Case ($\zeta < 1$)

If $R^2 < 4L/C$, the discriminant becomes negative and the solution becomes oscillatory:

$$i(t) = \frac{V_0}{\omega_d L} e^{-\alpha t} \sin(\omega_d t)$$

This is the form that arises when the test-object impedance is small (e.g., a heavy short-circuit), and it leads to oscillatory currents superimposed on the main pulse.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{\text{stored}} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_0^2$ | Energy stored in capacitor bank |
| 2 | $T_1 = 1.25 \times (t_{90} - t_{10})$ | Front-time definition (per IEC) |
| 3 | $I_p \approx V_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}} \cdot f(\zeta)$ | Approximate peak current (under-damped) |
| 4 | $i(t) = \dfrac{V_0}{\omega_d L} e^{-\alpha t} \sin(\omega_d t)$ | Underdamped impulse current |
| 5 | $i(t) = \dfrac{V_0}{L(\lambda_2 - \lambda_1)}(e^{\lambda_1 t} - e^{\lambda_2 t})$ | Overdamped impulse current |
| 6 | $\alpha = R / (2L)$ | Damping coefficient (Np/s) |
| 7 | $\omega_0 = 1 / \sqrt{LC}$ | Undamped natural frequency (rad/s) |
| 8 | $\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$ | Damped natural frequency (rad/s) |
| 9 | $\zeta = \dfrac{R}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}}$ | Damping ratio (dimensionless) |
| 10 | $\dfrac{di}{dt}\big)_{\max} = \dfrac{V_0}{L}$ | Maximum di/dt at $t = 0^+$ |
| 11 | $Z_{\text{surge}} = \sqrt{L/C}$ | Surge / characteristic impedance |
| 12 | $R_{\text{critical}} = 2\sqrt{L/C}$ | Critical damping resistance |
| 13 | $T_1 \approx 2.2 \dfrac{L}{R}$ (for $\zeta \to 1$) | Empirical front-time estimate |
| 14 | $T_2 \approx 0.7 RC$ (for overdamped) | Empirical tail-time estimate |

> [!CAUTION]
> In the formula table above, expressions like $\tfrac{C}{L}$ are written as inline fractions (not as $\vert C/L \vert$) to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax with vertical pipes.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Surge arrester qualification:** Every metal-oxide arrester leaving a factory is impulse-current tested at 8/20 µs at the discharge current class (e.g., 5 kA, 10 kA, 20 kA) to verify voltage-limiting capability and energy absorption.
- **Substation hardware design:** The electromagnetic force between parallel conductors during a 100 kA impulse is on the order of $F = \mu_0 I^2 / (2\pi d)$ per unit length — busbar supports are designed using these forces.
- **High-power laboratories:** Test laboratories such as CESI (Italy), KEMA (Netherlands), CPRI (India), and BHEL use impulse current generators rated up to **300 kA** for arrester, fuse, and switchgear qualification.
- **Aircraft and rocket protection:** Lightning strike simulation on composite aircraft skins uses 4/10 µs and 8/20 µs waveforms to certify electromagnetic compatibility.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Discharge Current Through an Under-Damped RLC Circuit

**Starting point:** A capacitor $C$ is pre-charged to $V_0$ and discharged into a series $L$–$R$–$Z_{TO}$ loop. We approximate $Z_{TO}$ as a small linear resistance that we lump into $R$ for analytical purposes.

**KVL around the closed loop at $t > 0$:**

$$L \frac{di}{dt} + R i + \frac{1}{C}\int_0^t i(\tau)\,d\tau = V_0$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate both sides once with respect to $t$ to remove the integral:**

$$L \frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + R \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{i}{C} = 0$$

This is the standard second-order homogeneous linear ODE.

**Step 2 — Write the characteristic equation by assuming $i(t) = K e^{st}$:**

$$L s^2 + R s + \frac{1}{C} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s^2 + \frac{R}{L}s + \frac{1}{LC} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $s$ using the quadratic formula:**

$$s_{1,2} = -\frac{R}{2L} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 - \frac{1}{LC}}$$

Define the damping coefficient $\alpha = \dfrac{R}{2L}$ and the undamped natural frequency $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$. Then:

$$s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$$

**Step 4 — Underdamped case ($\alpha^2 < \omega_0^2$):** the square root is imaginary, write it as $j\omega_d$ where $\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$. The two roots are complex conjugates: $s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm j\omega_d$.

**Step 5 — General solution (underdamped):**

$$i(t) = e^{-\alpha t}\left(A_1 \cos\omega_d t + A_2 \sin\omega_d t\right)$$

**Step 6 — Apply initial conditions.** At $t = 0^+$, the inductor current is zero (inductor opposes sudden change in current):

$$i(0^+) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_1 = 0$$

The capacitor voltage at $t = 0^+$ is still $V_0$, and at the first instant the entire voltage is dropped across $L$ (since $i = 0$ means $iR = 0$ and the capacitor integral is zero). So:

$$L \frac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0^+} = V_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0^+} = \frac{V_0}{L}$$

Computing the derivative of the general solution and setting $t = 0$:

$$\frac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0} = -\alpha A_1 + \omega_d A_2 = \omega_d A_2 = \frac{V_0}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_2 = \frac{V_0}{\omega_d L}$$

**Step 7 — Final underdamped solution:**

$$\boxed{\,i(t) = \frac{V_0}{\omega_d L} e^{-\alpha t} \sin(\omega_d t)\,}$$

**Step 8 — Time of peak current $t_p$:** differentiate $i(t)$ and set $di/dt = 0$.

$$\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{V_0}{\omega_d L} e^{-\alpha t}\left(\omega_d \cos\omega_d t - \alpha \sin\omega_d t\right) = 0$$

This requires $\omega_d \cos\omega_d t_p = \alpha \sin\omega_d t_p$, hence:

$$\tan(\omega_d t_p) = \frac{\omega_d}{\alpha} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_p = \frac{1}{\omega_d}\arctan\frac{\omega_d}{\alpha}$$

**Step 9 — Peak current value (substitute $t_p$ back into $i(t)$):**

$$I_p = \frac{V_0}{\omega_d L} e^{-\alpha t_p} \sin(\omega_d t_p)$$

Using $\sin(\arctan(\omega_d/\alpha)) = \omega_d / \sqrt{\alpha^2 + \omega_d^2} = \omega_d / \omega_0$ and $\omega_d t_p = \arctan(\omega_d/\alpha)$:

$$I_p = \frac{V_0}{\omega_d L} \cdot e^{-\alpha t_p} \cdot \frac{\omega_d}{\omega_0} = \frac{V_0}{\omega_0 L} e^{-\alpha t_p} = V_0 \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}} \, e^{-\alpha t_p}$$

Substituting $t_p$:

$$\boxed{\,I_p = V_0 \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}} \exp\!\left[-\frac{\alpha}{\omega_d}\arctan\frac{\omega_d}{\alpha}\right]\,}$$

For the lightly damped case ($\alpha \ll \omega_d$), this simplifies to the often-quoted result:

$$I_p \approx V_0 \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}} \quad \text{when } \zeta \to 0$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Discharge Current Through an Overdamped RLC Circuit (Standard 8/20 µs Case)

**Starting point:** Same KVL equation, but now $R^2 > 4L/C$, so $\alpha^2 > \omega_0^2$. The two roots $s_1, s_2 = \lambda_1, \lambda_2$ are real and negative.

Define:

$$\lambda_1 = -\alpha + \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}, \quad \lambda_2 = -\alpha - \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$$

Note that $\lambda_2 < \lambda_1 < 0$, and $\lambda_2 - \lambda_1 = -2\sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2} < 0$.

**Step 1 — General solution (overdamped):**

$$i(t) = A_1 e^{\lambda_1 t} + A_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}$$

**Step 2 — Apply $i(0^+) = 0$:**

$$A_1 + A_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_2 = -A_1$$

**Step 3 — Apply $L\,di/dt(0^+) = V_0$:**

$$L(A_1 \lambda_1 + A_2 \lambda_2) = V_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad L A_1(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2) = V_0$$

Hence:

$$A_1 = \frac{V_0}{L(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2)} = -\frac{V_0}{L(\lambda_2 - \lambda_1)}$$

**Step 4 — Final overdamped solution:**

$$\boxed{\,i(t) = \frac{V_0}{L(\lambda_2 - \lambda_1)} \left(e^{\lambda_1 t} - e^{\lambda_2 t}\right)\,}$$

**Step 5 — Useful design simplifications (KTU-favorite).** For a heavily overdamped 8/20 µs wave, designers often approximate:

$$T_1 \approx 2.2 \frac{L}{R_{\text{eff}}}, \qquad T_2 \approx 0.7 R_{\text{eff}} C_{\text{eff}}$$

where $R_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective series resistance and $C_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective capacitance of the bank including the test object's stray capacitance.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Rate of Rise of Current (di/dt)_max

Differentiate the underdamped current expression:

$$\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{V_0}{\omega_d L} e^{-\alpha t}\left(\omega_d \cos\omega_d t - \alpha \sin\omega_d t\right)$$

At $t = 0^+$ (where $\sin 0 = 0$ and $\cos 0 = 1$):

$$\frac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0^+} = \frac{V_0}{\omega_d L} \cdot \omega_d = \frac{V_0}{L}$$

This is the **maximum possible di/dt** for a given charging voltage and inductance. For very high lightning currents (e.g., 200 kA in 4 µs), the required $L$ must be very small:

$$L_{\min} = \frac{V_0}{(di/dt)_{\max}}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> This worked example is a typical KTU Part B–style calculation. Read every step.

**Problem:** A 30 µF capacitor bank is charged to $V_0 = 50$ kV. It is discharged through a wave-shaping inductance $L = 50$ µH and a series resistance $R = 4$ Ω into a low-impedance test object. Compute the peak current $I_p$, time of peak $t_p$, and verify the 8/20 µs compliance.

**Step 1 — Compute the natural frequencies:**

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 30 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.5 \times 10^{-9}}} = \frac{1}{3.873 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\omega_0 = 2.582 \times 10^{4} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the damping coefficient:**

$$\alpha = \frac{R}{2L} = \frac{4}{2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{4}{10^{-4}} = 4.0 \times 10^{4} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Check the damping regime:**

$$\alpha^2 = 1.6 \times 10^{9} \text{ s}^{-2}, \qquad \omega_0^2 = 6.667 \times 10^{8} \text{ s}^{-2}$$

Since $\alpha^2 > \omega_0^2$, the circuit is **overdamped** — this is the regime used for 8/20 µs waves.

**Step 4 — Compute the real roots:**

$$\sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2} = \sqrt{1.6 \times 10^{9} - 6.667 \times 10^{8}} = \sqrt{9.333 \times 10^{8}} = 3.055 \times 10^{4} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\lambda_1 = -\alpha + \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2} = -4.0 \times 10^{4} + 3.055 \times 10^{4} = -9.45 \times 10^{3} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\lambda_2 = -\alpha - \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2} = -4.0 \times 10^{4} - 3.055 \times 10^{4} = -7.055 \times 10^{4} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Time of peak current (overdamped):**

$$t_p = \frac{1}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}\ln\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} = \frac{1}{6.11 \times 10^{4}}\ln\frac{-9.45 \times 10^{3}}{-7.055 \times 10^{4}}$$

$$t_p = \frac{1}{6.11 \times 10^{4}}\ln(0.1339) = \frac{-2.011}{6.11 \times 10^{4}} = 3.29 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 32.9 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 6 — Peak current:**

$$I_p = \frac{V_0}{L(\lambda_2 - \lambda_1)}\left(e^{\lambda_1 t_p} - e^{\lambda_2 t_p}\right)$$

The exponential terms:

$$e^{\lambda_1 t_p} = e^{-9.45 \times 10^3 \times 3.29 \times 10^{-5}} = e^{-0.311} = 0.733$$

$$e^{\lambda_2 t_p} = e^{-7.055 \times 10^4 \times 3.29 \times 10^{-5}} = e^{-2.321} = 0.0982$$

Difference: $0.733 - 0.0982 = 0.635$

Denominator: $L(\lambda_2 - \lambda_1) = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times (-6.11 \times 10^{4}) = -3.055$ (magnitude $3.055$)

$$I_p = \frac{50 \times 10^3 \times 0.635}{3.055} = 1.04 \times 10^4 \text{ A} \approx 10.4 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 7 — Check 8/20 µs compliance (this is a critical KTU concept).** To qualify as an 8/20 µs waveform, the front time $T_1$ and time to half value $T_2$ must fall within ±10 % of the standard values. The time to half value is found by solving $i(t) = I_p/2$ on the tail. A first-order estimate for the overdamped tail is:

$$T_2 \approx \frac{1}{|\lambda_1|}\ln(2) = \frac{0.693}{9.45 \times 10^{3}} = 7.33 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 73.3 \text{ µs}$$

This **does not** meet the 20 µs requirement, indicating that the chosen $C$, $L$, $R$ values are too heavily damped. The designer must reduce $R$ (and possibly $L$) to obtain a shorter tail. This iterative design process is the heart of the impulse-current-generator engineering exercise.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design to Obtain 8/20 µs Waveform

**Step 1 — Target 8/20 µs implies:** $T_1 \approx 8$ µs, $T_2 \approx 20$ µs. The ratio $T_2/T_1 \approx 2.5$ is characteristic of an overdamped but not over-damped wave.

**Step 2 — Empirical design relations:**

$$T_1 \approx 2.2 \frac{L}{R}, \qquad T_2 \approx 0.7 RC$$

**Step 3 — Invert for L and R given a chosen C:**

$$R = \frac{T_2}{0.7\,C}, \qquad L = R \frac{T_1}{2.2} = \frac{T_1\,T_2}{1.54\,C}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:** Take $C = 30$ µF, $T_1 = 8$ µs, $T_2 = 20$ µs.

$$R = \frac{20 \times 10^{-6}}{0.7 \times 30 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{20}{21} = 0.952 \text{ Ω}$$

$$L = 0.952 \times \frac{8 \times 10^{-6}}{2.2} = 3.46 \text{ µH}$$

**Step 5 — Peak current check:**

$$I_p \approx V_0 \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}} = 50 \times 10^{3} \sqrt{\frac{30 \times 10^{-6}}{3.46 \times 10^{-6}}} = 50 \times 10^{3} \times \sqrt{8.67} = 50 \times 10^{3} \times 2.94$$

$$I_p \approx 147 \text{ kA}$$

This is a typical peak for a high-power surge-arrester test.

### 3.6 Derivation 4 — Energy Delivered to the Test Object

The energy delivered by the capacitor bank to the test object up to time $t$ is:

$$E_{TO}(t) = \int_0^t i^2(\tau) R_{TO}(\tau)\,d\tau$$

For a non-linear test object (e.g., a ZnO varistor) with voltage $V(I)$ across it:

$$E_{TO} = \int_0^t i(\tau)\,V(i)\,d\tau$$

In practice, the laboratory uses the measured voltage across and current through the test object to compute this integral from oscillogram data.

**KTU note:** A common KTU question asks for the **maximum energy** an arrester can absorb in a single impulse. The relevant design equation is:

$$E_{\max} = \int_0^{T_2} i^2 R_{TO}\,dt \quad \text{(resistive, linear case)}$$

For a ZnO arrester, the manufacturer specifies a **thermal energy rating** in **kJ/kV** (or kJ) of rated voltage, and the test waveform energy must not exceed this value.

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Impulse Current Generator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[DC HV Rectifier] -->|Charges| node2[Capacitor Bank C]
    node2 -->|Stored at V0| node3[Triggered Spark Gap S]
    node3 -->|Closes on command| node4[Wave Shaping Inductor L]
    node4 --> node5[Damping Resistor R]
    node5 --> node6[Test Object ZTO]
    node6 -->|Return path| node2
    node7[Control and Trigger Unit] -->|Firing pulse| node3
    node8[Current Shunt / Rogowski Coil] -.-> node6
    node9[Digitizing Oscilloscope] -.-> node8
    node10[Voltage Divider] -.-> node6
    node9 -.-> node10
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **rectifier** charges the **capacitor bank** to the required DC voltage.
- The **triggered spark gap** is fired by the control unit to initiate the discharge.
- The **L and R network** shapes the discharge into the 8/20 µs or 4/10 µs waveform.
- The **test object** receives the impulse current.
- The **current shunt / Rogowski coil** measures the current, while a **voltage divider** measures the test-object voltage. Both signals are recorded on a **high-bandwidth digitizing oscilloscope**.

### 4.2 State Diagram of the Generator Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state1[Idle: Capacitor Discharged] --> state2[Charging: Rectifier raises VC to V0]
    state2 --> state3[Ready: Switch S open, VC = V0]
    state3 --> state4[Trigger Pulse Issued by Control Unit]
    state4 --> state5[Switch S Closes, Capacitor Discharges into L R ZTO]
    state5 --> state6[Impulse Current Flows, Waveform 8 20 mu s]
    state6 --> state7[Test Object Stressed, Measurements Recorded]
    state7 --> state8[Recharge Cycle Initiated for Next Pulse]
    state8 --> state2
    state7 --> state1
```

### 4.3 Test Setup Schematic — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Impulse Current Generator] -->|HV bus| nodeB[Test Object Chamber]
    nodeB -->|Safety Earthing| nodeC[Earth Electrode]
    nodeD[Triggering Console] --> nodeA
    nodeE[Measurement Console] --> nodeB
    nodeF[Safety Interlocks] --> nodeA
    nodeF --> nodeB
    nodeG[Data Acquisition System] --> nodeE
    nodeH[Operator Station] --> nodeD
    nodeH --> nodeE
    nodeH --> nodeG
```

### 4.4 Waveform Sketch — 8/20 µs Impulse Current

The visual structure of the impulse current is best understood as a **fast-rising front** that decays as an **exponential tail**, with no oscillation. Key points marked on the curve:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[T0 Virtual Origin] --> nodeB[T1 Front Time 8 mu s]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Peak Current Ip]
    nodeC --> nodeD[T2 Time to Half Value 20 mu s]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Tail Decay to Zero]
    nodeA -. 10 percent of Ip .-> nodeF[t10]
    nodeA -. 30 percent of Ip .-> nodeG[t30]
    nodeA -. 90 percent of Ip .-> nodeH[t90]
    nodeG -. Straight Line .-> nodeH
```

> [!NOTE]
> The straight line connecting the **30 %** and **90 %** peak points, when extended, defines the **virtual origin $O_1$**. This is the same convention used in impulse voltage testing — a high-yield KTU fact.

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Test Object Current] -->|Passes through| node2[Coaxial Shunt]
    node1 -->|Magnetic field couples| node3[Rogowski Coil]
    node2 -->|Voltage drop| node4[Integrator / Buffer Amplifier]
    node3 -->|EMF| node4
    node4 --> node5[High Speed ADC]
    node5 --> node6[Digitizing Oscilloscope]
    node6 --> node7[Waveform Analysis Software]
    node7 -->|T1, T2, Ip reported| node8[Test Report]
```

### 4.6 Comparison Matrix — 8/20 µs vs. 4/10 µs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Standard 8 20 mu s] -->|Test object type| nodeA[Surge Arresters, ZnO Blocks, Current Dividers]
    node1 -->|Typical peak| nodeB[1 to 100 kA]
    node1 -->|Test purpose| nodeC[Discharge capability, Thermal stability]
    node2[Standard 4 10 mu s] -->|Test object type| nodeD[High Steepness Components, Aircraft Skin, Down Conductors]
    node2 -->|Typical peak| nodeE[10 to 200 kA]
    node2 -->|Test purpose| nodeF[Direct Lightning Strike Simulation, High di dt Effects]
```

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define the impulse current waveform 8/20 µs as per IEC 60060-1. List the standard tolerances on its parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **8/20 µs** standard impulse current waveform is defined by:

- A **virtual front time $T_1 = 8$ µs**, measured as 1.25 times the time between the 10 % and 90 % peak-current points on the rising front (using the 30 %–90 % extrapolation method to determine the virtual origin).
- A **time to half value $T_2 = 20$ µs**, measured from the virtual origin to the instant when the current has decayed to 50 % of its peak value on the tail.
- A **peak impulse current $I_p$**, the maximum instantaneous value reached.

**Standard tolerances (per IEC 60060-1):**

| Parameter | Tolerance |
|---|---|
| Peak current $I_p$ | **±10 %** |
| Front time $T_1$ | **±10 %** |
| Time to half value $T_2$ | **±10 %** |

Any deviation beyond these tolerances invalidates the test as a standard 8/20 µs impulse.

> *Examiner's note (1 mark): The student must explicitly mention the ±10 % tolerance. Without it, 1 mark is deducted.*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2. Differentiate between impulse current tests and impulse voltage tests in three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Impulse Voltage Test | Impulse Current Test |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Applies a **high voltage** (kV to MV) to the test object. | Applies a **high current** (kA to 200 kA) to the test object. |
| 2 | Standard waveform **1.2/50 µs**. | Standard waveforms **8/20 µs** and **4/10 µs**. |
| 3 | Tests **insulation strength** of equipment. | Tests **current-handling capacity** of non-linear/arrester devices. |
| 4 | Uses **Marx generator** (capacitor charging in parallel, discharging in series). | Uses **capacitor bank discharged through L and R** (single discharge path). |
| 5 | Test object is generally **linear / capacitive** (insulation). | Test object is generally **non-linear** (e.g., ZnO varistor). |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024 — Model Paper, 14 Marks]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working of an impulse current generator used for testing surge arresters. Identify the role of each component. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An impulse current generator has $C = 20$ µF charged to $V_0 = 60$ kV. It discharges through $L = 40$ µH and $R = 2$ Ω into a low-impedance test object. Determine:
- (i) Whether the discharge is overdamped, critically damped, or underdamped. **[2 marks]**
- (ii) The peak impulse current $I_p$ and the time at which it occurs. **[3 marks]**
- (iii) Whether the resulting waveform complies with the 8/20 µs standard. **[2 marks]**

---

#### **Question A — Model Solution**

**(a) Working of the impulse current generator (7 marks):**

The impulse current generator consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **DC high-voltage source (rectifier + filter):** Charges the storage capacitor bank to the required DC voltage $V_0$. Typically a multi-stage Cockcroft-Walton or controlled HV rectifier rated 50–200 kV DC. *(1 mark)*

2. **Energy-storage capacitor bank $C$:** Stores the energy $\tfrac{1}{2}CV_0^2$ required for the impulse. Multiple parallel capacitors are switched in or out to obtain the desired $C$ value. The bank is charged through a charging resistor $R_c$ that limits the charging current. *(1 mark)*

3. **Triggered switch (spark gap $S$):** Holds off the DC voltage on the capacitor until a control pulse is applied. Common types are three-electrode triggered gaps, trigatrons, and mercury-ignitrons. The switch must have very low jitter (≤ 0.5 µs) and very low inductance (< 1 µH). *(1 mark)*

4. **Wave-shaping inductor $L$:** Determines the rate of rise of current. For a high peak current, $L$ must be small; for a slow front, $L$ must be large. *(1 mark)*

5. **Damping resistance $R$ (sometimes called front/tail resistor):** Controls the decay rate of the tail and the damping of oscillations. *(1 mark)*

6. **Test object (e.g., surge arrester) $Z_{TO}$:** Receives the impulse current. Its non-linear V–I characteristic influences the actual waveform. *(1 mark)*

7. **Measurement chain (shunt, divider, oscilloscope):** Records the actual current and voltage waveforms. The current shunt is a low-inductance coaxial resistor; the divider is a high-impedance resistive or damped capacitive divider. *(1 mark)*

**Sequence of operation:**

- **Charging phase:** The DC source charges $C$ to $V_0$ over a few seconds, with the spark gap open.
- **Triggering phase:** A control pulse ionizes the gap, and the gap breaks down, effectively short-circuiting the charged capacitor into the $L$–$R$–$Z_{TO}$ discharge loop.
- **Discharge phase:** The capacitor discharges through $L$, $R$, and $Z_{TO}$ in a transient that lasts typically 20–100 µs. The waveform is the impulse current.
- **Measurement and reset:** The current/voltage traces are captured on the oscilloscope, the spark gap de-ionizes, and the cycle repeats after a short interval.

---

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $C = 20$ µF, $V_0 = 60$ kV, $L = 40$ µH, $R = 2$ Ω.

**Step (i) — Damping regime (2 marks):**

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{40 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{8 \times 10^{-10}}} = 3.536 \times 10^4 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{R}{2L} = \frac{2}{2 \times 40 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.5 \times 10^4 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\alpha^2 = 6.25 \times 10^8, \quad \omega_0^2 = 1.25 \times 10^9$$

Since $\alpha^2 < \omega_0^2$ (or equivalently $R^2 = 4 < 4L/C = 8$), the circuit is **underdamped**. The waveform will show a sinusoidal ringing superimposed on the main pulse.

**Step (ii) — Peak current and time of peak (3 marks):**

$$\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2} = \sqrt{1.25 \times 10^9 - 6.25 \times 10^8} = \sqrt{6.25 \times 10^8} = 2.5 \times 10^4 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$t_p = \frac{1}{\omega_d}\arctan\frac{\omega_d}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^4}\arctan\frac{2.5 \times 10^4}{2.5 \times 10^4} = \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^4} \times \frac{\pi}{4} = 3.14 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 31.4 \text{ µs}$$

$$I_p = \frac{V_0}{\omega_d L} e^{-\alpha t_p} \sin(\omega_d t_p) = \frac{60 \times 10^3}{2.5 \times 10^4 \times 40 \times 10^{-6}} e^{-2.5 \times 10^4 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-5}} \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

$$I_p = \frac{60 \times 10^3}{1.0} \times e^{-0.785} \times 0.707 = 60 \times 10^3 \times 0.456 \times 0.707 = 1.93 \times 10^4 \text{ A} \approx 19.3 \text{ kA}$$

**Step (iii) — Compliance with 8/20 µs (2 marks):**

Since the discharge is **underdamped**, the waveform will exhibit a current reversal on the tail. The standard 8/20 µs waveform must be **monotonically decaying** after the peak — no current reversal is permitted. Therefore, the resulting waveform **does not** comply with the 8/20 µs standard. To comply, the designer must **increase $R$** until $R^2 > 4L/C$, pushing the system into the overdamped regime.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the four standard parameters of an impulse current waveform (peak value, front time, time to half value, and virtual origin) as defined by IEC 60060-1. Describe how the front time $T_1$ is measured using the 30 %–90 % points on the front. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 50 µF capacitor bank is to be used in an impulse current generator to deliver a peak current of 100 kA into a low-inductance test load. The available charging voltage is 50 kV DC. Estimate the values of $L$ and $R$ required to obtain an 8/20 µs waveform. Verify your design by computing the approximate front time and time to half value. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Question B — Model Solution**

**(a) The four parameters (7 marks):**

1. **Peak impulse current $I_p$ (1 mark):** The maximum instantaneous value of the impulse current. It is read directly from the recorded oscillogram. For a 10 kA arrester, $I_p = 10$ kA with a tolerance of ±10 %.

2. **Virtual origin $O_1$ (2 marks):** A fictitious time reference defined as the intersection of the time axis with a straight line drawn through the **30 %** and **90 %** peak-current points on the rising front of the impulse. The virtual origin is necessary because the actual start of the current is rounded and difficult to identify precisely. Using the 30 %–90 % line gives a reproducible reference.

3. **Front time $T_1$ (2 marks):** Defined as **1.25 times** the time interval between the instants when the current reaches **10 %** and **90 %** of its peak value on the rising front. The factor 1.25 converts the 10 %–90 % rise time to the conventional 0 %–100 % rise time of an ideal ramp. Mathematically, $T_1 = 1.25 \times (t_{90} - t_{10})$.

4. **Time to half value $T_2$ (2 marks):** The time interval between the virtual origin and the instant when the current has decayed to **50 %** of its peak value on the **tail** of the waveform. For an 8/20 µs wave, $T_2 = 20$ µs.

> *Examiner's note: Students often confuse the *front time* with the *rise time*. The correct relationship is $T_1 = 1.25 \times (t_{90} - t_{10})$; simply writing $T_1 = t_{90} - t_{10}$ loses 1 mark.*

---

**(b) Design calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Estimate $L$ from peak-current requirement (2 marks):**

For a lightly damped approximation, the peak current is:

$$I_p \approx V_0 \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}$$

Solving for $L$:

$$L \approx C \left(\frac{V_0}{I_p}\right)^2 = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times \left(\frac{50 \times 10^3}{100 \times 10^3}\right)^2 = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.25 = 12.5 \text{ µH}$$

**Step 2 — Estimate $R$ from the time-to-half-value requirement (2 marks):**

For an overdamped 8/20 µs wave, the empirical relation is:

$$T_2 \approx 0.7 R C$$

Solving for $R$:

$$R \approx \frac{T_2}{0.7\,C} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-6}}{0.7 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{20}{35} = 0.571 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 3 — Check the front time (1 mark):**

$$T_1 \approx 2.2 \frac{L}{R} = 2.2 \times \frac{12.5 \times 10^{-6}}{0.571} = 2.2 \times 21.9 \times 10^{-6} = 48.2 \text{ µs}$$

This is **much longer than 8 µs** — the design is over-damped. The designer must reduce $L$ significantly.

**Step 4 — Iterate using the coupled relations (2 marks):**

$$T_1 \approx 2.2 \frac{L}{R} = 8 \text{ µs}, \quad T_2 \approx 0.7 R C = 20 \text{ µs}$$

From the second equation: $R = T_2 / (0.7\,C) = 20 \times 10^{-6} / (0.7 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}) = 0.571$ Ω.

From the first equation: $L = R \times T_1 / 2.2 = 0.571 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} / 2.2 = 2.08$ µH.

**Step 5 — Verify peak current (1 mark — included in iteration step):**

$$I_p \approx V_0 \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}} = 50 \times 10^3 \sqrt{\frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{2.08 \times 10^{-6}}} = 50 \times 10^3 \sqrt{24.04} = 50 \times 10^3 \times 4.90 = 245 \text{ kA}$$

This exceeds the design requirement of 100 kA. The designer can therefore either:
- Reduce $V_0$ to $V_0 = 100 \text{ kA} / 4.90 = 20.4$ kV, **or**
- Add an additional series resistance to lower the peak, **or**
- Operate the capacitor bank with fewer modules switched in.

**Final design recommendation:**

$$L = 2.08 \text{ µH}, \quad R = 0.571 \text{ Ω}, \quad V_0 = 20.4 \text{ kV}$$

This achieves $I_p \approx 100$ kA, $T_1 \approx 8$ µs, $T_2 \approx 20$ µs — the standard 8/20 µs waveform.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse impulse current with impulse voltage.** The 8/20 µs is for *current*; the 1.2/50 µs is for *voltage*. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Always state the tolerance** (±10 %) when defining a standard waveform.
> 3. **The factor 1.25 in $T_1$** is the difference between the *actual measured 10 %–90 % rise time* and the *defined front time*. Students who write $T_1 = t_{90} - t_{10}$ (without the 1.25) lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Damping regime** must be checked using the $R^2$ vs. $4L/C$ comparison. Forgetting to check it means losing 1–2 marks in numerical problems.
> 5. **Units:** Always convert µF, µH, and µs to base SI units (F, H, s) before substituting into equations. Mixing micro-units with base units is the #1 source of numerical errors.
> 6. **Standard reference:** Always cite IEC 60060-1 or IEC 60099-4 when discussing standard waveforms — a 1-mark "good practice" credit is often awarded.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Two standard waveforms:** 8/20 µs (general high-current) and 4/10 µs (steep-fronted lightning). Both are defined per **IEC 60060-1**.
- [ ] **Tolerances** on all three parameters ($I_p$, $T_1$, $T_2$) are **±10 %**.
- [ ] **Virtual origin $O_1$** is found by extending a straight line through the **30 %** and **90 %** peak points down to the time axis.
- [ ] **Front time $T_1 = 1.25 \times (t_{90} - t_{10})$** — the 1.25 factor is *not optional*.
- [ ] **Time to half value $T_2$** is measured from the virtual origin to the 50 % point on the **tail** (not the front).
- [ ] **Three damping regimes:** overdamped, critically damped, underdamped — distinguished by whether $R^2$ is greater than, equal to, or less than $4L/C$.
- [ ] **Standard 8/20 µs waves are overdamped** — no current reversal on the tail is permitted.
- [ ] **Peak current formula** (underdamped limit): $I_p \approx V_0 \sqrt{C/L}$. The exact overdamped form involves $e^{-\alpha t_p}$.
- [ ] **Energy stored** in the capacitor bank: $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_0^2$. Typical laboratory values: **10 kJ to 1 MJ**.
- [ ] **Maximum di/dt** at switch closure: $(di/dt)_{\max} = V_0 / L$. Determines the steepness of the front.
- [ ] **Empirical design relations:** $T_1 \approx 2.2 L / R$ and $T_2 \approx 0.7 RC$ — frequently used in exam problems.
- [ ] **Critical resistance** $R_{\text{crit}} = 2\sqrt{L/C}$ — boundary between overdamped and underdamped.
- [ ] **Major applications:** surge arrester duty-cycle tests, lightning simulation, current-shunt calibration, fuse testing, aircraft lightning certification.
- [ ] **Test object is typically non-linear** (ZnO varistor) — its V–I characteristic affects the actual waveform; empirical $R$ and $L$ values are tuned by laboratory iteration.
- [ ] **Measurement devices:** Coaxial current shunts (low inductance, high bandwidth), Rogowski coils (non-intrusive), and wide-band digitizing oscilloscopes (≥ 100 MHz, ≥ 1 GS/s).
- [ ] **Safety:** Capacitor banks store lethal energy; the test area must have safety interlocks, dump circuits to discharge $C$ in case of fault, and proper earthing of all enclosures.
- [ ] **KTU Board focus:** numerical design problems (given $C$, $L$, $R$, find $I_p$ and verify compliance), waveform parameter definitions, and damping-regime classification appear every semester.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Residual Voltages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Residual Voltages # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Residual Voltage** in a High Voltage power system is defined as the **trapped or bound electric potential that remains on a de-energized transmission line, cable, substation bus, or apparatus after the primary source of energization has been disconnected or removed**. According to the KTU 2024 HVE syllabus (Module 3: Overvoltages, Switching, and Lightning Transients), residual voltage is a critical parameter because it directly governs the **Transient Recovery Voltage (TRV)**, the **rate of rise of recovery voltage (RRRV)**, and the **success of three-phase auto-reclosing** operations. Formally, if $V_s(t)$ is the source voltage and $V_L(t)$ is the line voltage after opening of a circuit breaker, then the **residual voltage** $V_R$ is: $$V_R(t) = V_L(t)\Big\vert_{t \,>\, t_{open}}$$ The two principal physical origins of residual voltage in HV systems are: 1. **Capacitive (Electrostatic) Coupling Residual Voltage** – induced from parallel energized circuits. 2. **Trapped Charge (Bound Charge) Residual Voltage** – the actual line capacitance holds an isolated charge after the breaker opens. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Definition (Verbatim Style):** > *"The voltage existing on an effectively open-circuited transmission line or cable after the isolating device has been opened, arising from either trapped electrostatic charge or capacitive coupling to adjacent live phases, is termed the residual voltage. It is expressed as a percentage of the system's peak phase-to-earth voltage."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Think of a long water pipe (the transmission line) connected to a pump (the source). When you suddenly close the valve (open the circuit breaker), the water inside the pipe does **not instantly stop moving** – it sloshes back and forth as a pressure wave. Similarly, when an HV line is disconnected: - The **distributed inductance and capacitance** of the line behave like a long spring-mass system. - The energy stored in the line capacitance ($E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$) and line inductance ($E = \frac{1}{2}LI^2$) cannot vanish instantly. - The result is an **oscillatory decay** of voltage on the line, which is the **residual voltage**. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Charged Water Tank:** > Imagine a large overhead water tank filled to the brim. Even after the inlet pump is switched OFF, the tank still holds water (analogous to **trapped charge** in a cable). If a neighbor's tank is full, some water may slowly seep sideways through the shared wall (analogous to **capacitive coupling**). The water in the first tank is the "residual" — it is unwanted, persistent, and must be drained (de-energized through a discharge resistor) before safe maintenance. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value (KTU Data Book) | |---|---|---| | Lightning Impulse Wavefront Time | $T_1$ | **1.2 μs** (tolerance ± 30 %) | | Lightning Impulse Wave Tail Time | $T_2$ | **50 μs** (tolerance ± 20 %) | | Switching Impulse Front Time | $T_p$ | **250 μs** | | Switching Impulse Time to Half Value | $T_2$ | **2500 μs** | | Residual Voltage Time Constant (typical line) | $\tau$ | **0.5 s to 5 s** | | Auto-Reclosing Dead Time (single) | $t_d$ | **0.3 s to 1 s** | | Auto-Reclosing Dead Time (three-phase) | $t_{3\phi}$ | **0.4 s to 1.5 s** | | Ground Flashover Density (Kerala) | $N_g$ | **3 to 7 flashes/km²/yr** | | Velocity of Light (travelling wave) | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | | Permittivity of Free Space | $\varepsilon_0$ | **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** | | Permeability of Free Space | $\mu_0$ | **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** | > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU Board examinations frequently test the numerical values of standard impulse waveforms (1.2/50 μs and 250/2500 μs). These are drawn from **IS 2071 / IEC 60060-1**. ## 1.4 Classification of Residual Voltages Residual voltages are broadly classified into the following four families (as per the KTU HVE Module 3 syllabus): 1. **DC Residual Voltage (Trapped Charge)** – $V_{R,DC}$ 2. **AC Residual Voltage (Capacitive Coupling)** – $V_{R,AC}$ 3. **Impulse Residual Voltage (Lightning/Switching Induced)** – $V_{R,imp}$ 4. **Dynamic Recovery Voltage** – $V_{TRV}(t)$ (Rate of Rise governed by line surge impedance) > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid — "D-I-A-C" = "Drum Is A Container":** > **D**C, **I**mpulse, **A**C, **C**oupled — these are the four categories examiners will test. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step ### Step 1 — Normal Operating State The line is energized at system voltage $V_s = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$, and carries steady-state load current $I_L$. ### Step 2 — Breaker Pole Opening At time $t = t_0$, the contacts of the circuit breaker separate. An **arc** initially bridges the contacts and conducts the line current. The arc extinguishes at the next **current zero** (for AC) or is forcibly interrupted (for DC). ### Step 3 — Trapped Charge Phenomenon At the instant of arc extinction, the line capacitance $C_L$ is charged to a voltage $V_C(t_{ext})$. Because the breaker is now an open circuit, **no discharge path exists**, and the charge remains "trapped": $$Q_{trapped} = C_L \cdot V_C(t_{ext})$$ ### Step 4 — Slow Decay via Leakage The trapped charge leaks away through the **corona losses**, **insulator surface leakage**, and any **discharge resistors** (e.g., 10 kΩ to 100 MΩ) connected across the breaker. The decay follows: $$V_R(t) = V_R(0) \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{leak}}$$ where $\tau_{leak} = R_{leak} \cdot C_L$. > [!IMPORTANT] > For an **unguarded** line (no discharge resistor), $\tau_{leak}$ can be **hundreds of seconds**, so the residual voltage remains nearly **constant** during the entire reclosing dead-time of 0.3 s – 1.0 s. ### Step 5 — Capacitive Coupling (Parallel Circuit) A **parallel energized circuit** (e.g., a healthy double-circuit line) injects voltage into the de-energized phase through mutual capacitance $C_m$. The induced residual voltage is: $$V_{R,AC} = \frac{C_m}{C_m + C_g} \cdot V_s$$ where $C_g$ is the capacitance to ground of the de-energized phase. > [!NOTE] > Typical values: $C_m \approx 5$ pF/m, $C_g \approx 10$ pF/m ⇒ $V_{R,AC} \approx 33\%$ of $V_s$. ### Step 6 — Reclosing Transient When the breaker recloses onto a line still holding $V_R$, the system sees a **surge** equal to: $$\Delta V = V_s - (-V_R) = V_s + V_R$$ For $V_R = V_s$ (worst case, opposite polarity), $\Delta V = 2 V_s$ — the dreaded **"2 p.u. switching surge"**. ## 2.2 Detailed Sources of Residual Voltage | Source | Physical Mechanism | Time Scale | Magnitude (typical) | |---|---|---|---| | **Trapped charge on cables** | E-field stored in $C_{cable}$ | Seconds to minutes | 50 % to 100 % of $V_s$ | | **Electrostatic induction** | Mutual capacitance to live phase | 0.1 s to 1 s | 15 % to 50 % of $V_s$ | | **Electromagnetic induction** | Mutual inductance, current in parallel | 0.1 s to 0.5 s | 5 % to 20 % of $V_s$ | | **Lightning back-flash** | Strike to shield wire/ tower | 1 μs to 100 μs | 2 p.u. to 5 p.u. | | **Inductive load current chopping** | $L \cdot di/dt$ at current zero | μs | 1 p.u. to 3 p.u. | | **Saturation transient (transformer)** | DC offset core saturation | 0.1 s to 1 s | 0.5 p.u. to 2 p.u. | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following is the **high-yield formula set** the KTU board examiner expects a 2024-scheme student to reproduce without reference. | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_R(t) = V_R(0) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | RC discharge of trapped charge | V, s | | 2 | $\tau = R_{leak} \cdot C_L$ | Leakage time constant | s | | 3 | $C_L = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(D/r)}$ | Single-phase line capacitance to neutral | F/m | | 4 | $V_{R,AC} = \dfrac{C_m}{C_m+C_g} \cdot V_s$ | Induced AC residual voltage | V | | 5 | $E_C = \dfrac{1}{2} C_L V_R^2$ | Stored electrostatic energy | J | | 6 | $v = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | Surge propagation velocity on line | m/s | | 7 | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Surge impedance of overhead line (~ 400 Ω) | Ω | | 8 | $\Delta V = V_s + V_R$ | Reclosing surge magnitude | V | | 9 | $V_{RRRV} = \dfrac{dV_{TRV}}{dt}\Big\vert_{max}$ | Rate of rise of recovery voltage | V/μs | | 10 | $V_{TRV}(t) = V_\infty \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{TRV}}\right)$ | Transient recovery voltage (single freq) | V | | 11 | $E_{arc} = \int_0^{t_{arc}} v_{arc}(t)\cdot i(t)\, dt$ | Arc energy in breaker | J | | 12 | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Natural frequency of LC oscillation | Hz | | 13 | $V_{res} = \dfrac{V_s}{1 + \omega R_{d} C_L}$ | Steady-state residual under leakage | V | | 14 | $t_{half} = \tau \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \tau$ | Time to halve the residual voltage | s | | 15 | $V_{2pu} = 2 V_m \sin\phi$ | Reclosing 2 p.u. stress (worst case) | V | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Pipe-Escaped Symbols:** > Use `\vert` (not $\vert$) for absolute value when writing inside tables. E.g., write $V_R(t) = \vert V_R(0)\vert \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ → render in LaTeX as $V_R(t) = \lvert V_R(0)\rvert \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Field | Application of Residual Voltage Concept | |---|---| | **Transmission Line Design** | Selection of insulator strings, surge arresters, line clearances | | **EHV Cable Systems** | Sheath bonding, cross-bonding, residual charge drain resistors | | **GIS Substations** | Very Fast Transient (VFT) management, disconnector resistor sizing | | **Auto-Reclosure Schemes** | Single-phase vs three-phase reclose, dead-time selection | | **Insulation Coordination** | Creepage distance, BIL vs BSL choice | | **Maintenance Safety** | Earth switches, "live-line permit-to-work" procedures | | **Lightning Protection** | Coupling between shield wire and phase conductor | | **Wind/Solar Integration** | Inverter ride-through, anti-islanding residual sensing | > [!TIP] > KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the **SCADA + numerical relay** integration of residual voltage sensing in **digital substations** under **IEC 61850**. Examiners may ask: *"How does a numerical relay detect residual voltage on a feeder?"* — Answer: using the **zero-sequence voltage channel (3U₀)** derived from the three phase VT secondaries. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — RC Decay of Trapped Charge on an Overhead Line **Problem Setup:** A 220 kV, three-phase, 50 Hz transmission line of length $\ell = 100$ km has a positive-sequence capacitance to neutral $C_1 = 11$ nF/km. After the breaker opens at the peak of the system voltage, a discharge resistor of $R_d = 50$ kΩ is connected across the open breaker contacts. Compute: (a) Total line capacitance (b) Time constant (c) Time taken for the residual voltage to fall to 5 % of the initial value (d) Whether single-pole auto-reclosing (dead time = 1.0 s) is safe. ### Step 1 — Total Line Capacitance The positive-sequence capacitance per phase to neutral is $C_1 = 11$ nF/km. For a single phase of 100 km line: $$C_{phase} = C_1 \cdot \ell = 11 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{F/km} \cdot 100 \, \text{km} = 1.1 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{F}$$ $$C_{phase} = 1.1 \, \mu\text{F}$$ ### Step 2 — Time Constant The discharge path consists of $R_d$ in parallel with the surface leakage of the insulators. The leakage is usually much higher and may be neglected, so the effective resistance is $R_d = 50$ kΩ. The time constant of the RC discharge is: $$\tau = R_d \cdot C_{phase} = 50 \times 10^{3} \times 1.1 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\tau = 55 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s} = 55 \, \text{ms}$$ ### Step 3 — Voltage as a Function of Time The trapped charge follows the classical exponential decay law: $$V_R(t) = V_R(0) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$ We need $V_R(t) = 0.05 \, V_R(0)$: $$0.05 = e^{-t/\tau}$$ Take the natural logarithm of both sides: $$\ln(0.05) = -\dfrac{t}{\tau}$$ $$-2.996 = -\dfrac{t}{\tau}$$ $$t = 2.996 \cdot \tau = 2.996 \cdot 55 \, \text{ms}$$ $$t = 164.76 \, \text{ms} \approx 0.165 \, \text{s}$$ ### Step 4 — Safety Evaluation for 1.0 s Reclosing Voltage remaining at $t = 1.0$ s: $$V_R(1.0) = V_R(0) \cdot e^{-1.0 / 0.055} = V_R(0) \cdot e^{-18.18}$$ $$V_R(1.0) = V_R(0) \cdot 1.30 \times 10^{-8} \approx 0$$ So the residual is essentially **completely drained** within 1 second. The auto-reclosing operation is **safe**. > [!IMPORTANT] > Without the discharge resistor ($R_d = \infty$), $\tau \to \infty$, and the line holds the trapped charge essentially unchanged. This is exactly why **EHV lines always have closing resistors or pre-insertion resistors** in modern breakers. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capacitive Coupling Voltage (Two Parallel Circuits) **Problem Setup:** A 400 kV double-circuit line has a per-phase mutual capacitance $C_m = 5$ pF/m and a self-capacitance to ground $C_g = 10$ pF/m. The healthy circuit is energized at the rated voltage. Compute the AC residual voltage induced on the de-energized circuit's phase A. ### Step 1 — Equivalent Circuit The de-energized phase A is floating. The healthy phase A' injects voltage through $C_m$, and the leakage to ground flows through $C_g$. The equivalent circuit is a simple **capacitive divider**. ### Step 2 — Capacitive Divider Equation The induced voltage on the floating phase is: $$V_{R,AC} = \dfrac{C_m}{C_m + C_g} \cdot V_s$$ ### Step 3 — Numerical Substitution Substitute $C_m = 5$ pF/m, $C_g = 10$ pF/m, $V_s = 400$ kV (line-to-line peak phase voltage = $\dfrac{400\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} = 326.6$ kV): $$V_{R,AC} = \dfrac{5}{5 + 10} \cdot 326.6 \, \text{kV}$$ $$V_{R,AC} = \dfrac{5}{15} \cdot 326.6 = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 326.6$$ $$V_{R,AC} = 108.87 \, \text{kV} \approx 109 \, \text{kV}$$ ### Step 4 — Interpretation The de-energized line sits at **109 kV** even though the breaker is open. This is **33 % of rated voltage** — enough to deliver a **lethal shock** to a maintenance worker standing under the line. > [!WARNING] > This is exactly why **Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) / KSEBL** safety codes (per CEA Regulations 2010) mandate **application of portable earth chains** before any line is certified "dead". The 109 kV is purely inductive/capacitive coupling — it can be drained only by **earthing**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reclosing Surge Magnitude **Problem Setup:** A 220 kV line is tripped with residual voltage $V_R$ at the opposite polarity to the source. Derive the maximum surge voltage and find the value of $V_R$ for which the surge equals 2 p.u. ### Step 1 — Pre-Close Voltage on Line Let source voltage be $V_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. The trapped residual voltage is DC (slowly decaying), so we treat it as constant $V_R$ during the reclose transient. ### Step 2 — Closing at Source Peak If the breaker closes when the source is at $+V_m$ and the line has $-V_R$, the **reflected wave** doubles the difference (lossless line assumption): $$V_{surge} = V_m - (-V_R) = V_m + V_R$$ ### Step 3 — Worst-Case Condition The maximum occurs when $V_R = V_m$ (full trapped charge, opposite polarity). Then: $$V_{surge,max} = V_m + V_m = 2 V_m = 2 \, \text{p.u.}$$ ### Step 4 — General Expression For arbitrary trapped charge magnitude $\alpha = \dfrac{V_R}{V_m}$ (where $\alpha \in [0, 1]$) and arbitrary phase angle $\phi$ of reclosing: $$V_{surge} = V_m \sin\phi + \alpha V_m = V_m (\sin\phi + \alpha)$$ Maximum when $\phi = 90°$ and $\alpha = 1$: $$\boxed{V_{surge,max} = 2 V_m}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This 2 p.u. switching surge is the **design basis** for EHV insulation. Beyond 400 kV, the switching impulse withstand level (SIWL) is **2.7 p.u. to 3.0 p.u.** to account for statistical overvoltages and trapped charge effects. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Discharge Resistor Sizing for a Cable **Problem Setup:** A 110 kV, 5 km underground cable has $C_{cable} = 0.4 \, \mu\text{F/km}$. To enable safe maintenance within 5 minutes, the residual voltage must decay below 50 V. Select the discharge resistor. ### Step 1 — Total Cable Capacitance $$C = 0.4 \times 5 = 2.0 \, \mu\text{F}$$ ### Step 2 — Required Time Constant We need $V_R(300) = 50$ V. Initial value is peak phase-to-ground voltage: $$V_R(0) = \dfrac{110 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} = 89.8 \, \text{kV}$$ We need: $$\dfrac{50}{89800} = e^{-300/\tau}$$ Take the natural logarithm: $$\ln(1.797 \times 10^{-3}) = -\dfrac{300}{\tau}$$ $$-6.32 = -\dfrac{300}{\tau}$$ $$\tau = \dfrac{300}{6.32} = 47.5 \, \text{s}$$ ### Step 3 — Required Resistance $$R = \dfrac{\tau}{C} = \dfrac{47.5}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.375 \times 10^{7} \, \Omega$$ $$R = 23.75 \, \text{M}\Omega$$ ### Step 4 — Standard Selection Standard practice: choose the **next-lower standard value** for **faster** discharge, e.g., **$R = 10$ MΩ**, giving $\tau = 20$ s, well within the safety window. > [!TIP] > The empirical IEC 60900 / IEEE 80 rule of thumb: choose $R$ such that $\tau \le 1$ minute for HV cables and $\tau \le 15$ s for EHV cables. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Trapped Charge vs. Coupled Voltage | Property | Trapped Charge (DC) | Capacitive Coupling (AC) | |---|---|---| | Origin | Line capacitance storage | Mutual capacitance to live line | | Time behaviour | Exponential decay | 50 Hz sinusoidal (after transient) | | Polarity | Fixed (sign of last arc) | Same as live phase | | Magnitude | Up to 100 % of $V_m$ | 15 % – 50 % of $V_m$ | | Mitigation | Discharge resistor | Disconnect all parallel circuits | | Safety priority | **Highest** (line lethal even after trip) | **High** (still induces 33 % rated) | | Standard discharge time | 5 min (110 kV) to 1 min (400 kV) | Vanishes when parallel line is also tripped | > [!NOTE] > This comparative matrix is a **favorite KTU 2024 14-mark question**: *"Compare and contrast the mechanisms of trapped charge residual voltage and capacitively coupled residual voltage, with reference to an auto-reclosing scheme on a 220 kV double-circuit line."* --- ## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Reclosing Surge via Bewley Lattice Diagram For a single-phase line of length $\ell$ with surge impedance $Z_0$, surge velocity $v$, and trapped voltage $V_R$ at the open end, when the source $V_s$ is suddenly applied, the **successive reflections** produce a lattice diagram. The voltage at any point $x$ and time $t$ is the sum of all forward ($F$) and backward ($B$) travelling waves: $$V(x,t) = \sum_k \left[ F_k\left(t - \dfrac{x}{v} \right) + B_k\left(t + \dfrac{x}{v} \right) \right]$$ For lossless line with source resistance $Z_s = 0$ and load open (infinite $Z_L$): $$\rho_L = +1 \quad (\text{load reflection coefficient})$$ $$\rho_S = -1 \quad (\text{source reflection coefficient, short-circuit source})$$ The first forward wave from the source is: $$F_1 = V_s - V_R = V_s - V_R$$ It arrives at the open end and reflects fully (since $\rho_L = +1$): $$B_1 = F_1 = V_s - V_R$$ So the **first voltage overshoot** at the open end is: $$V_{peak} = V_R + B_1 = V_R + (V_s - V_R) = V_s$$ But if the line had stored energy and the open end reflects, the **doubling** effect arises when the trapped voltage has opposite polarity: $$V_{peak,max} = 2 V_m \quad \text{(when } V_R = -V_m\text{)}$$ > [!TIP] > KTU 2024 expects students to draw a **Bewley Lattice Diagram** with at least 4 reflected waves for full marks on a 14-mark problem. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System Block Diagram — Residual Voltage Generation & Mitigation ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeSRC[Source
220 kV 50 Hz Bus] --> nodeCB1[Main Circuit Breaker
SF6 Puffer Type] nodeCB1 --> nodeLINE[Transmission Line
100 km 400 ohm Surge Z] nodeLINE --> nodeRECV[Receiving End
Substation Bus] nodeLINE -.-> nodeCSTRAP[Trapped Charge
C = 1.1 uF
Stored Energy E = 0.5 C V2] nodeLINE -.-> nodeCACP[Capacitive Coupling
Cm = 5 pF per m
Induces 33 percent V_s] nodeCSTRAP --> nodeVRES[Residual Voltage
V_R decays exp] nodeCACP --> nodeVRES nodeVRES --> nodeMON[Voltage Monitoring
3U0 Channel
IEC 61850 Sampled Values] nodeVRES --> nodeDRES[Discharge Resistor
R = 10 to 100 Mohm
Drains in 1 to 5 min] nodeVRES --> nodeAREC[Auto Reclosure Logic
Checks V_R less than 0.1 p.u.
Then Reclose] nodeMON --> nodeAREC nodeDRES --> nodeAREC nodeAREC --> nodeCB1 nodeDRES -.-> nodeEARTH[Earthing Switch
Maintenance Lockout] ``` > [!NOTE] > The above flowchart is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** showing how trapped charge and capacitive coupling feed into a single residual voltage node, which is then discharged, monitored, and managed. Replace the components with a real diagram of the substation if the topic is asked in a **drawing question**. ## 4.2 Mermaid — Lattice Diagram of Reclosing Surge ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[t = 0
Source applies V_s] --> B1[t = T
Wave at receiving end] B1 --> C1[t = 2T
Reflected wave at source] C1 --> D1[t = 3T
Second forward wave] D1 --> E1[t = 4T
Second reflection] E1 --> F1[t = 5T
Steady state reached] A1 -.Forward F1 = Vs - VR.-> B1 B1 -.Reflected B1 = F1.-> C1 C1 -.Reflected F2 = minus F1.-> D1 D1 -.Reflected B2 = F2.-> E1 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid — Decision Logic for Safe Auto-Reclosing ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Breaker Trips] --> CHK1{Is line
de-energized?} CHK1 -->|No| ABORT1[Hold Trip
Investigate] CHK1 -->|Yes| T1[Start Dead Timer
t = 0] T1 --> T2[Wait 0.3 s for
primary arc extinction] T2 --> SENSE[Measure V_R via 3U0 PT] SENSE --> CHK2{V_R less than
0.10 p.u.?} CHK2 -->|No| HOLD[Hold and apply
discharge resistor] HOLD --> SENSE CHK2 -->|Yes| CHK3{Is parallel
circuit live?} CHK3 -->|Yes| EARTH[Apply temporary
earth to phase] EARTH --> SENSE CHK3 -->|No| CLOSE[Issue Close Command
to Breaker] CLOSE --> END[Auto-Reclosing
Successful] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The above decision tree reflects the **modern numerical relay logic** as per **IEC 62271-200 (metal-enclosed switchgear)** and **IS 14658**. KTU 2024 14-mark problems often ask students to write this as a **flowchart or state diagram**. ## 4.4 Mermaid — Block Diagram of a Discharge Resistor Unit ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Line Conductor
HV Terminal] --> nodeB[Resistor R
10 Mohm Wire Wound] nodeB --> nodeC[Earth Bus] nodeB -.-> nodeD[Thermal Fuse
Fails Open if Overheated] nodeB -.-> nodeE[Series Air Gap
Sparkover at 5 kV] nodeA -.-> nodeF[Voltage Divider
R1 100 Mohm and R2 100 kohm] nodeF -.-> nodeG[Relay Aux Contact
Verifies Drainage] ``` ## 4.5 Conceptual Comparison Chart — DC vs AC Residual ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeDC[DC Residual
Trapped Charge] --> nodeDC1[Polarity fixed] nodeDC --> nodeDC2[Decay exp with tau] nodeDC --> nodeDC3[Up to 100 percent V_s] nodeAC[AC Residual
Capacitive Coupling] --> nodeAC1[Polarity tracks live phase] nodeAC --> nodeAC2[Frequency 50 Hz] nodeAC --> nodeAC3[15 to 50 percent V_s] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!TIP] > In the KTU 2024 scheme, **Part A** has 5 questions of 3 marks each, all **compulsory**. You will be tested on direct definitions and short derivations. ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define residual voltage. Mention any two sources of residual voltage in a transmission system.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Residual voltage is the voltage that remains on a transmission line, cable, or substation bus after the circuit breaker has opened and the line is supposedly de-energized. It is expressed as a percentage of the rated peak phase-to-earth voltage. **Sources (1.5 marks):** 1. **Trapped Charge (DC residual):** The line-to-ground capacitance $C_L$ stores electrostatic energy $E = \frac{1}{2} C_L V^2$ at the moment of arc extinction, and this charge remains trapped because the open breaker presents an infinite impedance. 2. **Capacitive Coupling (AC residual):** A parallel energized circuit induces a 50 Hz voltage on the de-energized phase through the mutual phase-to-phase capacitance $C_m$, governed by the relation $V_{R,AC} = \dfrac{C_m}{C_m+C_g} \cdot V_s$. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **A 220 kV line has a per-phase capacitance to neutral of 11 nF/km and length 80 km. Compute the total stored energy in the line at the instant of opening, if the residual voltage is 0.8 p.u. of peak phase voltage.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Step 1 — Phase voltage peak (0.5 mark):** $$V_m = \dfrac{220 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} = 179.6 \, \text{kV}$$ **Step 2 — Residual voltage (0.5 mark):** $$V_R = 0.8 \times 179.6 = 143.7 \, \text{kV}$$ **Step 3 — Total line capacitance (0.5 mark):** $$C_L = 11 \times 10^{-9} \times 80 = 0.88 \, \mu\text{F}$$ **Step 4 — Stored energy (1.5 marks):** $$E = \dfrac{1}{2} C_L V_R^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.88 \times 10^{-6} \times (143.7 \times 10^3)^2$$ $$E = 0.5 \times 0.88 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.065 \times 10^{10}$$ $$E = 9.09 \times 10^{3} \, \text{J} = 9.09 \, \text{kJ}$$ **Result:** $E = 9.09$ kJ per phase, or **27.3 kJ total** for a three-phase line. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 scheme mandates that every Part B question carry an **internal choice** between two alternatives of the **same module and same CO**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) Derive the expression for the transient recovery voltage appearing across the contacts of a circuit breaker after current interruption, with reference to the equivalent RLC circuit of the line. (7 marks)** **(b) A 400 kV, 200 km transmission line has surge impedance $Z_0 = 400 \, \Omega$, surge velocity $v = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, and is suddenly reclosed on a residual voltage $V_R = 0.6$ p.u. of opposite polarity. Determine: (i) the first reclosing surge magnitude, (ii) the time taken for the surge to reach the far end, and (iii) the steady-state voltage. (7 marks)** --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks) **Step 1 — Single-Frequency LCR Equivalent (2 marks):** After current zero in the breaker, the system can be modelled as a single-frequency LCR circuit with the source inductance $L_s$, line capacitance $C_L$, and breaker resistance $R_{arc}$. The TRV across the breaker contacts satisfies: $$L_s \dfrac{d^2 v}{dt^2} + R_{arc} \dfrac{dv}{dt} + \dfrac{v}{C_L} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Characteristic Equation (1.5 marks):** Assuming solution of the form $v(t) = e^{st}$: $$L_s s^2 + R_{arc} s + \dfrac{1}{C_L} = 0$$ $$s = \dfrac{-R_{arc} \pm \sqrt{R_{arc}^2 - 4L_s/C_L}}{2L_s}$$ **Step 3 — Three Regimes (2 marks):** - **Underdamped** (small $R_{arc}$): $s = -\alpha \pm j\omega_d$, giving oscillatory TRV - **Critically damped**: $R_{arc} = 2\sqrt{L_s/C_L}$ - **Overdamped**: $R_{arc} > 2\sqrt{L_s/C_L}$, non-oscillatory rise **Step 4 — Time Domain Expression (1.5 marks):** For the underdamped case, the TRV is: $$v_{TRV}(t) = V_\infty \left[1 - e^{-\alpha t} \left(\cos\omega_d t + \dfrac{\alpha}{\omega_d}\sin\omega_d t \right)\right]$$ with $\alpha = R_{arc}/(2L_s)$, $\omega_d = \sqrt{1/(L_s C_L) - \alpha^2}$, and $V_\infty$ being the source-side driving voltage. **Conclusion:** The TRV is the **time-domain manifestation of the residual voltage** recovering towards the source drive voltage after the breaker has interrupted the line current. --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks) **(i) First reclosing surge (3 marks):** The first voltage step launched down the line is the difference between the source voltage at the closing instant and the trapped voltage on the line: $$\Delta V = V_s - V_R$$ With $V_s = +1.0$ p.u. and $V_R = -0.6$ p.u. (opposite polarity): $$\Delta V = 1.0 - (-0.6) = 1.6 \, \text{p.u.}$$ This is the **first incident surge magnitude**. **(ii) Travel time to far end (2 marks):** $$T = \dfrac{\ell}{v} = \dfrac{200 \times 10^3}{3 \times 10^8} = 6.67 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{s} = 667 \, \mu\text{s}$$ **(iii) Steady-state voltage (2 marks):** After all reflections die out (in a lossless system, after infinite time), the line voltage equals the source voltage. Therefore: $$V_{steady} = V_s = 1.0 \, \text{p.u.} = \dfrac{400\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} = 326.6 \, \text{kV (peak)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget the **reflection at the far end (load)**. Because the load is open (no load connected during reclose), the reflection coefficient $\rho_L = +1$, so the voltage at the far end **doubles** the incident surge during the first transit. The maximum voltage actually observed at the far end is $2 \times \Delta V = 3.2$ p.u. (briefly). Full marks require this observation. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) Explain the four types of residual voltages in HV systems with neat sketches. (7 marks)** **(b) An 11 kV underground cable of length 4 km has capacitance $0.35 \, \mu\text{F/km}$. A discharge resistor of $R_d = 20$ MΩ is connected across the cable ends after switching off. Calculate: (i) total cable capacitance, (ii) discharge time constant, (iii) time for the residual voltage to fall to a safe value of 50 V from an initial peak of 14.4 kV, and (iv) the discharge resistor value required to achieve the safe voltage in exactly 30 seconds. (7 marks)** --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks) The four types of residual voltages are: 1. **DC Trapped Charge Residual Voltage (2 marks):** - Originates from the actual line/cable capacitance $C_L$ retaining the voltage $V$ at the moment of arc interruption. - Decays exponentially with time constant $\tau = R_{leak} C_L$. - Polarity is fixed (sign of the last arc). - Magnitude: up to 100 % of $V_m$. - *Sketch:* Capacitor $C_L$ in parallel with high-resistance leakage. 2. **AC Capacitive Coupling Residual Voltage (2 marks):** - Induced on a de-energized phase from a parallel energized circuit via mutual capacitance $C_m$. - Polarity tracks the live phase, frequency 50 Hz. - Magnitude: $V_{R,AC} = \dfrac{C_m}{C_m + C_g} V_s$, typically 15 % – 50 %. - *Sketch:* Two parallel bus bars with mutual capacitance. 3. **Impulse (Switching/Lightning) Residual Voltage (1.5 marks):** - Originating from a high-frequency transient (e.g., 1.2/50 μs lightning wave) that excites the natural frequencies of the line. - Decays in microseconds. - *Sketch:* Damped sinusoid envelope. 4. **Dynamic Recovery Voltage (TRV) (1.5 marks):** - The voltage that builds up across the breaker contacts immediately following current interruption, before the line capacitance has time to recharge to the source voltage. - Initial Rate of Rise of Recovery Voltage (RRRV) is governed by $di/dt$ at current zero and the source-side natural frequency. --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks) **(i) Total Cable Capacitance (1.5 marks):** $$C = 0.35 \times 4 = 1.4 \, \mu\text{F}$$ **(ii) Discharge Time Constant (1.5 marks):** $$\tau = R_d \cdot C = 20 \times 10^6 \times 1.4 \times 10^{-6} = 28 \, \text{s}$$ **(iii) Time for $V_R = 50$ V (2.5 marks):** We need: $$\dfrac{50}{14400} = e^{-t/\tau}$$ $$3.472 \times 10^{-3} = e^{-t/28}$$ $$\ln(3.472 \times 10^{-3}) = -t/28$$ $$-5.664 = -t/28$$ $$t = 28 \times 5.664 = 158.6 \, \text{s} \approx 2 \, \text{min} \, 38.6 \, \text{s}$$ **(iv) Resistor Value for 30 s Discharge (1.5 marks):** We need $\tau_{new}$ such that $t_{safe} = 30$ s with $V_R(30) = 50$ V: $$\tau_{new} = \dfrac{30}{5.664} = 5.297 \, \text{s}$$ $$R_{new} = \dfrac{\tau_{new}}{C} = \dfrac{5.297}{1.4 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.78 \times 10^6 \, \Omega = 3.78 \, \text{M}\Omega$$ > [!TIP] > **Standard Selection:** Choose the next-lower standard value, i.e., **$R = 3.3$ MΩ**, to ensure safety margin. This is a common EHV cable discharge resistor rating. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Student Errors):** > 1. **Forgetting to convert** $V_R(0)$ to **peak phase-to-ground voltage**. The 11 kV is the line-to-line **RMS** rating; you must multiply by $\sqrt{2}/\sqrt{3}$ to get peak phase voltage (8.16 kV → 11.55 kV is **wrong**, 14.4 kV is **wrong** for 11 kV). The correct value: $V_m = 11 \times \sqrt{2} / \sqrt{3} = 8.98$ kV. The 14.4 kV would correspond to a 22 kV cable. > 2. Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\ln$ when computing discharge time. > 3. Confusing **line-to-line** capacitance with **line-to-neutral** capacitance. > 4. **Not accounting** for the fact that the cable has **three cores** — multiply by 3 if computing total stored energy. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision)** - **Definition:** Residual voltage = voltage remaining on a de-energized line/cable after the breaker opens. - **Two Main Sources:** Trapped DC charge (line capacitance) + AC capacitive coupling (mutual capacitance to live phase). - **Trapped charge formula:** $Q = C_L V_R$, stored energy $E = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_R^2$. - **RC discharge law:** $V_R(t) = V_R(0) e^{-t/\tau}$, where $\tau = R_d C_L$. - **Time to half:** $t_{0.5} = \tau \ln 2 = 0.693 \tau$. - **Time to 5 %:** $t_{0.05} = 3.0 \tau$ (≈ 3 time constants). - **Coupled voltage:** $V_{R,AC} = \dfrac{C_m}{C_m+C_g} V_s \approx 0.15$–$0.5 \, V_s$. - **Reclosing surge worst case:** $V_{surge} = 2 V_m$ when $V_R = -V_m$ and source is at $+V_m$. - **Surge propagation velocity:** $v = 1/\sqrt{LC} \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s for overhead lines, $v \approx 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s for cables. - **Surge impedance:** $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C} \approx 400 \, \Omega$ (overhead), $30$–$50 \, \Omega$ (cable). - **Reflection coefficient at open end:** $\rho_L = +1$ (full doubling). - **Reflection coefficient at short-circuited end:** $\rho_L = -1$ (full cancellation). - **Reclose time constants:** Single-pole dead time 0.3–1.0 s; three-pole 0.4–1.5 s. - **Standard impulse waveforms:** Lightning 1.2/50 μs; Switching 250/2500 μs (per IS 2071). - **Discharge resistor practice:** EHV cable: 10–100 MΩ, must drain within 1 min. - **Numerical detection:** Zero-sequence voltage $3U_0$ sensed via three-phase VT residual winding. - **Safety code (CEA / KSEB):** Apply earth chains before issuing "permit-to-work". - **Bewley Lattice:** Method to track all reflections on a transmission line. - **Damping:** Resistance of ~ 400 Ω (matched to surge impedance) eliminates reflections. - **Auto-reclose scheme:** Check $V_R < 0.1$ p.u. before reclosing; otherwise apply discharge. - **Insulation coordination:** SIWL ≈ 2.7 p.u. for 400 kV to cover trapped-charge reclosing surge. - **GIS VFT:** Very fast transients (~ 5 ns rise) due to disconnector operation; mitigated by $Z_0$ matching. - **Numerical Relay Standard:** IEC 61850-9-2 SV stream samples $V_R$ at 4.8 kHz. - **Standard reference:** IS 2071 (Impulse Testing), IS 2165 (HV Switchgear), IEC 60060-1, IEC 62271-100. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonics:** > - **"D-R-A-F-T"** → **D**ischarge Resistor, **R**C decay, **A**C coupling, **F**lashover, **T**rapped charge > - **"2-3-5 Rule"** → 2 p.u. surge, 3 time constants to 5 %, 5–50 MΩ discharge resistor > - **"C-L-R-V"** → **C**apacitance, **L**ine length, **R**esistor, **V**oltage — the four pillars of any residual voltage problem

topics.title: Arrester Durability Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Arrester Durability Requirements**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ Arrester Durability Requirements — Core Technical Foundation

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Surge Arrester** (also called a *lightning arrester* or *metal-oxide varistor (MOV) arrester*) is a protective device used in power systems to limit overvoltages on equipment by diverting surge current and then restoring service conditions. The **Arrester Durability Requirements** define the comprehensive set of mechanical, electrical, thermal, and environmental performance criteria that a surge arrester must satisfy to ensure reliable, maintenance-free operation over its entire design service life (typically **20 to 30 years**) under field stresses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**  
> *"Arrester durability is the demonstrated ability of a surge protective device to withstand, without loss of protective function or physical integrity, all specified electrical, mechanical, thermal, and environmental stresses that occur during its installed service life, including the cumulative effects of multiple surge operations, temporary overvoltages, and pollution."*

Durability is therefore **not a single parameter** but a *constellation* of interacting requirements derived from international standards — primarily **IEC 60099-4** and **IEEE Std C62.11™**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tired Lifeguard"

Imagine a **lifeguard** posted at a beach for 25 years:

- **Daily duty** → Continuous line-to-ground voltage (rated voltage, $U_r$).
- **Sudden rescue (drowning swimmer)** → Lightning surge / switching surge — the lifeguard must act instantly without getting "injured."
- **Back-to-back rescues (a school of swimmers in distress)** → Multiple successive surges (duty cycle) — the lifeguard must not collapse from exhaustion (thermal runaway).
- **Stormy day with rough sea but no drowning** → Temporary Overvoltage (TOV) — the lifeguard must stand firm without quitting.
- **Salt spray, sandstorms, harsh sun** → Pollution, UV, humidity — corrosion over decades.
- **A 90 kg drunk man grabbing the lifeguard** → Short-circuit current / pressure relief scenario — the lifeguard must not explode!

**Arrester durability = the lifeguard's stamina, immunity, and resilience over decades.**

---

## 1.3 The Five Pillars of Arrester Durability

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 1 — Electrical Durability**  
> Withstand continuous operating voltage, temporary overvoltages, lightning impulses, and switching impulses **repeatedly** without degradation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 2 — Thermal Durability**  
> Absorb surge energy without the metal-oxide varistor (MOV) discs entering **thermal runaway** (where heating > cooling).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 3 — Mechanical Durability**  
> Withstand **seismic events** (ground acceleration), **wind load**, **cantilever load on terminals**, and **clamp pressure** without fracture or seal failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 4 — Environmental Durability**  
> Resist **UV radiation**, **humidity**, **temperature cycling** ($-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+45^{\circ}\text{C}$ standard), **salt fog**, and **industrial pollution**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 5 — Long-Term Operational Durability**  
> Maintain protective characteristics (clamping voltage, reference voltage) within **±5%** over a **30-year** design life under continuous AC stress.

---

## 1.4 Key Numerical Benchmarks (KTU High-Yield Constants)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Design Service Life** | **20–30 years** | IEEE C62.11 |
| **High Current Impulse** | **100 kA, 4/10 µs** | IEC 60099-4 |
| **Operating Temperature Range** | **$-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+45^{\circ}\text{C}$** | IEC 60099-4 |
| **Seismic withstand** | **0.5 g horizontal, 0.4 g vertical** | IEEE 693 |
| **Creepage distance** | **$\geq 31 \text{ mm/kV}$** (heavy pollution) | IEC 60815 |
| **Power frequency withstand (wet)** | **$2.5 \times U_r$ for 1 min** | IEC 60099-4 |

---

## 1.5 Role of the Gapless Metal-Oxide Arrester (GMA)

Modern gapless ZnO arresters dominate KTU curriculum discussions because **durability is now bounded by the MOV disc's long-term stability** rather than by series gap reignition problems seen in older SiC arresters. Hence the syllabus emphasises **MOV ageing** mechanisms:

- **Electrochemical migration** of zinc ions
- **Depletion of bismuth oxide** at grain boundaries
- **Increased leakage current** with time → accelerated thermal runaway risk

This is why **Accelerated Ageing Tests** (e.g., $115^{\circ}\text{C}$, 1000 h at $U_c$) are mandatory for type certification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Arresters by Durability Class

Per **IEC 60099-4**, arresters are grouped into **five line discharge classes** based on their ability to dissipate long-duration switching surge energy:

$$
\text{Class} \; 1 < 2 < 3 < 4 < 5
$$

| Class | Typical Application | Line Discharge Energy | Repetitive Charge Transfer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Station Class** | EHV substations (400 kV+) | $\geq 10 \text{ kJ/kV}$ | $\geq 1.6 \text{ C}$ |
| **Intermediate Class** | 66–220 kV systems | $5\text{–}10 \text{ kJ/kV}$ | $\geq 0.8 \text{ C}$ |
| **Distribution Class** | 11–33 kV feeders | $1\text{–}5 \text{ kJ/kV}$ | $\geq 0.4 \text{ C}$ |

> **KTU Memory Hook:** *"Station = Strong, Intermediate = In-between, Distribution = Domestic."*

---

## 2.2 The Four Operational Stressors Acting on an Arrester

The arrester, once installed, faces four concurrent stressors. Durability = ability to survive **all four simultaneously**.

### Stressor A — Continuous Operating Voltage ($U_c$)
The maximum permissible **continuous RMS power-frequency voltage** that may be applied indefinitely. The arrester's MOV must operate **below the knee point** of its V–I characteristic continuously.

$$
U_c \leq U_r \leq \frac{U_{TOV,\max}}{k_{safety}}
$$

### Stressor B — Temporary Overvoltage (TOV)
Power-frequency overvoltages of duration **0.1 s to 100 s** caused by:
- Load rejection
- Single-phase-to-ground faults
- Ferro-resonance
- Sudden loss of generation

The arrester must absorb the corresponding **TOV energy** $W_{TOV}$ without thermal runaway:

$$
W_{TOV} = U_{TOV} \cdot I_{TOV} \cdot t_{TOV} \quad [\text{joules}]
$$

### Stressor C — Surge Current (Lightning & Switching)
- **Lightning:** $8/20 \; \mu\text{s}$ waveform, $I_n$ = nominal discharge current (5 kA or 10 kA), $I_{max}$ = high current (up to 100 kA, $4/10 \; \mu\text{s}$).
- **Switching:** $30/60 \; \mu\text{s}$ to $500/2000 \; \mu\text{s}$ waveform, $0.5\text{–}2 \text{ kA}$ peak.

### Stressor D — Environmental & Mechanical
- Pollution creepage
- Wind, ice, seismic
- Rodent/bird damage
- Thermal cycling ($-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+60^{\circ}\text{C}$ at noon in India)

---

## 2.3 Thermal Stability Criterion — The Heart of Durability

The **single most important durability equation** in the KTU syllabus:

> **An arrester is thermally stable if, at the end of any duty cycle, the rate of heat generation $P_{gen}$ is less than the rate of heat dissipation $P_{diss}$:**

$$
P_{gen}(T) < P_{diss}(T)
$$

For a single ZnO disc, after a surge of energy $W$:

$$
T_{final} = T_{amb} + \frac{W}{m \cdot c_p}
$$

where $m$ = mass, $c_p$ = specific heat. The cooling follows:

$$
T(t) = T_{amb} + (T_{peak} - T_{amb}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}
$$

with thermal time constant:

$$
\tau = \frac{m \cdot c_p}{h \cdot A}
$$

where $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient, $A$ = surface area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** If $T_{final} > T_{critical}$ (typically **$150^{\circ}\text{C}$** for epoxy-housed MOVs), the V–I characteristic shifts leftward, leakage current **increases exponentially**, leading to **thermal runaway** — and the arrester fails within seconds.

---

## 2.4 Complete KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U_c = 0.8 \cdot U_r$ | Continuous operating voltage is 80% of rated | kV |
| 2 | $U_{res} = k \cdot I^{\beta}$ | Residual voltage (V–I of MOV); $k$, $\beta$ are constants | kV |
| 3 | $W = \int_0^{t_s} u(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt$ | Surge energy absorbed | J or kJ |
| 4 | $W_{TOV} = U_{TOV} \cdot I_{leak,TOV} \cdot t$ | TOV energy absorbed | J |
| 5 | $T(t) = T_{amb} + (T_p - T_{amb})e^{-t/\tau}$ | Arrester cooling curve | °C |
| 6 | $\tau = mc_p / (hA)$ | Thermal time constant | s |
| 7 | $I_{leak} = I_0 \cdot e^{\alpha(V/V_{ref})}$ | Leakage current vs. voltage | A |
| 8 | $E_{2kA} \leq U_{res,5kA}$ | Protection level check (switching < lightning residual) | kV |
| 9 | $U_{m,co-ord} \geq 1.4 \cdot U_{p,1.2/50}$ | Insulation co-ordination margin | kV |
| 10 | $L = k_p \cdot U_m \cdot \lambda$ | Creepage distance ($k_p$ = pollution factor, $\lambda$ = specific creepage) | mm |

---

## 2.5 Why Durability Requirements Matter in Real Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility — A Field Failure Costs 100× the Arrester Price:**
> - **Substation outage:** ₹10–50 lakh/day revenue loss
> - **Transformer damage:** ₹2–10 crore replacement
> - **Cascading grid failure:** Blackout of a state (e.g., 2012 India grid failure affected **620 million** people)
> - **Safety:** Arrester rupture can throw porcelain fragments at 100 m/s — **lethal**

Hence, durability is not an academic luxury — it is a **public safety mandate**.
