# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Absorption Capability of a Multi-Disc MOV Stack

### Problem Setup
A **station-class arrester** for a **245 kV** system uses $n$ identical ZnO discs in series. Each disc has:
- Diameter: $D = 80 \text{ mm}$, thickness: $t = 25 \text{ mm}$
- Density: $\rho = 5650 \text{ kg/m}^3$, specific heat: $c_p = 500 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
- Maximum allowable temperature rise: $\Delta T_{max} = 100 \text{ K}$ (from 40°C ambient to 140°C limit)

A **switching surge** of peak current $I = 1 \text{ kA}$, duration $t_s = 2000 \; \mu\text{s}$ is applied. The residual voltage across the stack is $U_{res} = 350 \text{ kV}$ (assumed constant during surge). Find the **minimum number of discs** required.

### Step 1 — Total Energy in the Surge

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{surge} &= U_{res} \cdot I \cdot t_s \\
&= (350 \times 10^3) \cdot (1 \times 10^3) \cdot (2000 \times 10^{-6}) \\
&= 350 \times 10^3 \cdot 1 \times 10^3 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 7 \times 10^5 \text{ J} \\
&= 700 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Since voltage and current are approximately constant over the rectangular surge, the energy is simply $P \times t$.

---

### Step 2 — Energy Capacity of a Single Disc

Volume of one disc:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{disc} &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot t \\
&= \frac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} \cdot 0.025 \\
&= 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.025 \\
&= 1.2566 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Mass of one disc:
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{disc} &= \rho \cdot V_{disc} \\
&= 5650 \cdot 1.2566 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 0.7100 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Maximum energy one disc can absorb (without exceeding $\Delta T_{max} = 100 \text{ K}$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{disc,max} &= m_{disc} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{max} \\
&= 0.7100 \cdot 500 \cdot 100 \\
&= 35500 \text{ J} \\
&= 35.5 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The disc's heat capacity $m \cdot c_p$ multiplied by the safe temperature rise gives the safe energy budget.

---

### Step 3 — Minimum Number of Discs

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{min} &= \frac{W_{surge}}{W_{disc,max}} \\
&= \frac{700{,}000}{35{,}500} \\
&= 19.72 \\
&\approx 20 \text{ discs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering decision:** Round up to **20 discs**, with a **safety margin of +2 discs** to account for derating due to ageing — final design uses **22 discs in series**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Arrester Thermal Time Constant and Cooling Time

### Problem
For the same disc, the convective cooling coefficient is $h = 25 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ and the disc surface area is $A = 0.012 \text{ m}^2$. Find:
1. The thermal time constant $\tau$
2. The time to cool from $T_{peak} = 140^{\circ}\text{C}$ to within $5^{\circ}\text{C}$ of ambient ($T_{amb} = 40^{\circ}\text{C}$).

### Step 1 — Thermal Time Constant

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \frac{m \cdot c_p}{h \cdot A} \\
&= \frac{0.7100 \cdot 500}{25 \cdot 0.012} \\
&= \frac{355}{0.30} \\
&= 1183.3 \text{ s} \approx 19.7 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** $\tau$ represents the time for the disc to lose $\approx 63.2\%$ of its excess temperature.

---

### Step 2 — Cooling Curve Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(t) &= T_{amb} + (T_{peak} - T_{amb}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau} \\
T(t) &= 40 + 100 \cdot e^{-t/1183.3}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Solve for $T(t) = 45^{\circ}\text{C}$ (i.e., within 5 K of $T_{amb}$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
45 &= 40 + 100 \cdot e^{-t/1183.3} \\
5 &= 100 \cdot e^{-t/1183.3} \\
e^{-t/1183.3} &= 0.05 \\
-\frac{t}{1183.3} &= \ln(0.05) \\
-\frac{t}{1183.3} &= -2.996 \\
t &= 2.996 \cdot 1183.3 \\
t &= 3545.2 \text{ s} \approx 59.1 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Interpretation:** A second surge can be applied only after approximately **1 hour** of cooling. This is the basis of the **Operating Duty Test** in IEC 60099-4, where the duty cycle typically allows **5 minutes** between successive surges — which is permissible only because the **nominal** surge is much smaller (5 kA, 8/20 µs) than the 100 kA high-current impulse used in the test.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: TOV Withstand Time Using Arrester V–I Curve

### Problem
A **66 kV** arrester with $U_r = 60 \text{ kV}$ and $U_c = 48 \text{ kV}$ is subjected to a TOV of $U_{TOV} = 1.5 \cdot U_r = 90 \text{ kV}$ (RMS). The arrester leakage current at this voltage is $I_{TOV} = 5 \text{ mA}$ (resistive component). Ambient is $40^{\circ}\text{C}$. Using a **simplified single-time-constant** thermal model with $\tau = 1183 \text{ s}$, find the **maximum TOV duration** before $T$ reaches $140^{\circ}\text{C}$ (assume no pre-heating).

### Step 1 — Steady-State Temperature Rise Under TOV

Heat generated per second (power):
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{gen} &= U_{TOV} \cdot I_{TOV} \\
&= 90{,}000 \cdot 0.005 \\
&= 450 \text{ W per disc (assumed lumped)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Steady-state temperature rise:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{\infty} &= \frac{P_{gen}}{h \cdot A} \\
&= \frac{450}{25 \cdot 0.012} \\
&= \frac{450}{0.30} \\
&= 1500 \text{ K (clearly impossible — hence finite duration limit)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Since the disc would melt if held indefinitely, the TOV **must be cleared** before runaway.

---

### Step 2 — Maximum TOV Duration (Time to Reach 100 K Rise)

Using the heating curve:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(t) &= T_{amb} + \Delta T_{\infty} \cdot (1 - e^{-t/\tau}) \\
140 &= 40 + 1500 \cdot (1 - e^{-t/1183}) \\
100 &= 1500 \cdot (1 - e^{-t/1183}) \\
1 - e^{-t/1183} &= 0.0667 \\
e^{-t/1183} &= 0.9333 \\
-t/1183 &= \ln(0.9333) = -0.0690 \\
t &= 81.6 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Result:** The arrester can withstand $1.5 U_r$ TOV for only about **80 seconds**. After that, thermal runaway is unavoidable. Hence, the system protection relay **must clear a single-line-to-ground fault within 1–2 seconds**, or the arrester will fail. This is why the **disc material's thermal capacity and $\tau$** directly dictate relay coordination settings.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Durability Requirements vs. Test Verification

| Durability Requirement | Corresponding IEC/IEEE Test | Pass Criterion | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lightning impulse withstand | High current impulse ($4/10 \mu s$, 100 kA) | No puncture, $U_{res}$ within ±5% | 3 |
| Switching surge energy | Long duration current impulse | Energy ≥ class rating, no thermal runaway | 5 |
| TOV capability | TOV test per Annex E of IEC 60099-4 | $T_{max} \leq 140^{\circ}\text{C}$ during test | 5 |
| Mechanical strength | Cantilever load, seismic (IEEE 693) | No fracture, deflection ≤ 1% of height | 3 |
| Pollution performance | Salt-fog test ($160 \text{ kg/m}^3$ NaCl, 1000 h) | Flashover does not occur | 2 |
| Long-term ageing | 1000 h at $1.05 U_c$ at $115^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $\Delta U_{ref} \leq 5\%$ | 4 |
| Seal integrity | He leak rate ≤ $10^{-8} \text{ Pa·m}^3/\text{s}$ | Pass/fail | 1 |
| Pressure relief | 16 kA RMS short-circuit current for 0.2 s | No violent rupture, vent operates | 2 |

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**  
**Define the term "arrester durability" and list the four primary stressors an arrester must withstand during its service life.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**  
Arrester durability is the demonstrated ability of a surge protective device to maintain its rated protective characteristics and physical integrity over its full design life (typically 20–30 years) while exposed to electrical, thermal, mechanical, and environmental stresses, including the cumulative effects of multiple surge operations.

**Four Primary Stressors (2 marks):**

1. **Continuous operating voltage ($U_c$)** — applied 24×7 for decades; causes slow electro-chemical ageing of ZnO grains.
2. **Temporary overvoltages (TOV)** — power-frequency overvoltages of 0.1–100 s duration; impose both electrical and thermal stress.
3. **Surge currents** — lightning ($8/20 \mu s$, 5–100 kA) and switching ($30/60 \mu s$ to $2000/5000 \mu s$, 0.5–2 kA) — deposit high energy in milliseconds.
4. **Environmental and mechanical loads** — pollution, humidity, UV, temperature cycling, seismic events, wind, and terminal forces.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**  
**Why is the concept of "thermal runaway" central to the durability of a metal-oxide gapless arrester?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a metal-oxide gapless (ZnO) arrester, the MOV disc exhibits a strongly **non-linear V–I characteristic** with a negative temperature coefficient in the leakage region. Under continuous voltage, the small resistive leakage current heats the disc. If the rate of heat generation **exceeds** the rate of heat dissipation, the disc temperature rises, which **increases** the leakage current (positive feedback), causing further heating — a self-reinforcing cycle called **thermal runaway** (1.5 marks).

Thermal runaway is central to durability because:
- It is the **dominant failure mechanism** in modern arresters (replacing sparkover erosion of older SiC types) (0.75 marks).
- Once initiated, the disc temperature exceeds $150^{\circ}\text{C}$ within seconds, the housing ruptures, and the arrester must be replaced — a catastrophic loss of protection (0.75 marks).

Hence, every durability requirement (energy class, TOV curve, duty cycle) ultimately **aims to prevent the temperature from ever crossing the runaway threshold.**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ **Confusing $U_r$ (rated voltage) with $U_c$ (continuous operating voltage).** Many students use them interchangeably. They differ by ~20% — losing 1–2 marks.
> - ❌ **Stating "thermal runaway" without explaining the negative temperature coefficient of the MOV.** You must explicitly link leakage current ↑ with temperature ↑.
> - ❌ **Forgetting the "duty cycle"** in surge energy problems. The energy a single disc absorbs is the average, not the peak.
> - ❌ **Mixing up line discharge class 1, 2, 3, 4, 5** with arrester classes (Station, Intermediate, Distribution). They are correlated but not identical.
> - ❌ **Ignoring units in derivations.** Always carry units through every step.
> - ❌ **Stating formulae without defining symbols** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per undefined symbol.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the operating principle of a **gapless metal-oxide surge arrester** and discuss how its V–I characteristic differs from a **conventional silicon-carbide arrester with series gaps.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **245 kV, 50 Hz** substation is to be protected by a station-class arrester. The arrester is rated $U_r = 198 \text{ kV}$ and $U_c = 158 \text{ kV}$. Each ZnO disc has:
- Diameter $D = 90 \text{ mm}$, thickness $t = 28 \text{ mm}$
- $\rho = 5650 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 500 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
- Maximum allowable temperature rise: $\Delta T_{max} = 100 \text{ K}$

A long-duration switching surge delivers an energy of **$W = 1000 \text{ kJ}$** to the arrester. Determine the **minimum number of discs** required, and comment on the TOV withstand time at $1.4 U_r$ if $I_{leak,TOV} = 4 \text{ mA}$ per disc and $h = 25 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$, $A = 0.013 \text{ m}^2$ per disc. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Part A (a) [7 marks]**

**Operating Principle of Gapless MOV Arrester (3.5 marks):**

A gapless MOV arrester consists of a **column of zinc-oxide varistor discs** housed in a porcelain or polymer insulator, connected directly between **line and ground** without any series spark gap. Under normal conditions, the MOV operates in its **pre-breakdown (high-impedance) region**, with only a small capacitive leakage current (mA range) flowing to ground. When a surge arrives, the MOV's resistance drops by **4–5 orders of magnitude** in microseconds, diverting the surge current to ground at a low residual voltage. After the surge, the MOV automatically returns to its high-impedance state — no gap reignition needed.

```
   Line ──[MOV Disc Stack]── Ground
         (porcelain/polymer housing)
```

**Comparison of V–I Characteristics (3.5 marks):**

| Feature | SiC Arrester (with gaps) | ZnO Arrester (gapless) |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-breakdown leakage** | ~mA (resistive) | ~µA (capacitive dominant) |
| **Non-linearity coefficient $\alpha$** | 3–5 (moderate) | 20–50 (highly non-linear) |
| **Need for series gap** | Yes (to block follow current) | **No** (gapless) |
| **Follow current** | Must be interrupted by gap | Inherently limited |
| **Residual voltage** | Higher (gap also drops voltage) | Lower (only disc) |
| **Response time** | Limited by gap breakdown (~µs) | Sub-microsecond (instantaneous) |
| **Ageing behaviour** | Gap erosion | MOV grain migration |

The ZnO characteristic $I = kV^{\alpha}$ with $\alpha \geq 20$ means the arrester clamps the voltage very tightly, and its continuous leakage is so small that no gap is needed — hence "gapless."

### **Model Solution — Part A (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Volume of one disc (0.5 mark):**
$$
V = \frac{\pi (0.090)^2}{4} \cdot 0.028 = 1.782 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of one disc (0.5 mark):**
$$
m = 5650 \cdot 1.782 \times 10^{-4} = 1.007 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum energy per disc (0.5 mark):**
$$
W_{disc} = 1.007 \cdot 500 \cdot 100 = 50{,}350 \text{ J} \approx 50.35 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum number of discs (1 mark):**
$$
n = \frac{1000 \text{ kJ}}{50.35 \text{ kJ}} = 19.86 \approx 20 \text{ discs}
$$

**Engineering decision (0.5 mark):** Use **20 + 2 = 22 discs** with safety margin.

**Step 5 — TOV calculation (3 marks):**
- TOV voltage: $U_{TOV} = 1.4 \cdot 198 = 277.2 \text{ kV}$
- Per-disc TOV voltage (approx.): $277.2 / 22 = 12.6 \text{ kV}$
- Per-disc power: $P = 12.6 \times 10^3 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-3} = 50.4 \text{ W}$
- Steady-state rise: $\Delta T_\infty = P / (hA) = 50.4 / (25 \cdot 0.013) = 155 \text{ K}$
- $\tau = mc_p / (hA) = 1.007 \cdot 500 / 0.325 = 1549 \text{ s}$

**Step 6 — Solve for time to reach $\Delta T = 100 \text{ K}$ (1 mark):**
$$
100 = 155 \cdot (1 - e^{-t/1549}) \Rightarrow e^{-t/1549} = 0.3548 \Rightarrow t = 1673 \text{ s} \approx 27.9 \text{ min}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The arrester can withstand $1.4 U_r$ TOV for **~28 minutes** before thermal runaway. Since typical power-system TOVs (single-line-to-ground faults) are cleared within **1–2 seconds**, the arrester is **safe in operation** but has a finite backup tolerance for stuck breakers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **standard type tests** prescribed by **IEC 60099-4** for a station-class surge arrester. Explain the significance of the **line discharge class** and how the **discharge voltage vs. current curve** is obtained experimentally. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two identical ZnO discs are connected in parallel, each with $U_{ref} = 4.5 \text{ kV}$ (at 1 mA DC). Due to manufacturing tolerance, one disc has $\pm 0\%$ and the other $+5\%$ higher leakage at the same voltage. If a continuous operating voltage of $U_c = 3.6 \text{ kV}$ is applied across the parallel combination, calculate:
- (i) The voltage across the combination
- (ii) The leakage current in each disc (use $I = I_0 e^{\alpha V/V_{ref}}$ with $\alpha = 25$ and $I_0 = 1 \mu\text{A}$ at $V = V_{ref}$)
- (iii) The power dissipated in each disc
- (iv) The total current and comment on the durability implication. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Part B (a) [7 marks]**

**Standard Type Tests per IEC 60099-4 (5 marks):**

1. **Insulation Withstand Test** — power-frequency wet withstand ($2.5 U_r$, 1 min) and lightning impulse withstand ($1.3 \times BIL$) on the arrester housing. **Purpose:** ensure external insulation does not flash over. (0.5 mark)
2. **Residual Voltage Test** — measures $U_{res}$ at $8/20 \mu s$ nominal current (5 or 10 kA) and at high current ($4/10 \mu s$, 100 kA). **Purpose:** defines the protective level. (0.5 mark)
3. **Long-Duration Current Impulse Test** — applies a long-duration surge to verify energy-withstand capability per the **line discharge class**. (0.7 mark)
4. **Operating Duty Test** — applies a sequence of $8/20 \mu s$ impulses followed by $U_c$ to verify thermal recovery. (0.7 mark)
5. **TOV Test** — applies power-frequency overvoltages for prescribed durations per the TOV curve. (0.7 mark)
6. **Pressure Relief Test** — verifies that the arrester can vent safely under short-circuit fault current (e.g., 16 kA RMS, 0.2 s). (0.5 mark)
7. **Accelerated Ageing Test** — 1000 h at $1.05 U_c$ at elevated temperature ($115^{\circ}\text{C}$); $\Delta U_{ref} \leq 5\%$. (0.7 mark)
8. **Seal Leak Test** and **Mechanical Tests** (cantilever, seismic, torsion). (0.7 mark)

**Line Discharge Class and Discharge Curve (2 marks):**

The **line discharge class** (1, 2, 3, 4, 5) is determined by a **standardised long-duration impulse test** ($4/10 \mu s$ or rectangular wave) of a defined current shape and energy. The class is the highest energy the arrester can dissipate in two consecutive operations without thermal runaway. Higher class = greater energy-withstand, suitable for EHV stations with high switching-surge levels.

The **discharge voltage curve** is obtained by applying $8/20 \mu s$ impulses of progressively increasing peak current (0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20 kA) and recording the residual voltage across the arrester. The resulting log–log plot of $U_{res}$ vs. $I$ is a near-straight line: $\log U_{res} = \log k + (1/\alpha) \log I$.

### **Model Solution — Part B (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** Two discs in parallel, $V_{ref1} = 4.5 \text{ kV}$, $V_{ref2} = 4.725 \text{ kV}$ (since +5% leakage at same V means lower $V_{ref}$ for disc 1, or equivalently higher leakage). Disc 1: $+5\%$ leakage at the same voltage → equivalent $V_{ref,1} = 4.5 \text{ kV}$ but with $5\%$ scaling on current. Disc 2: nominal, $V_{ref,2} = 4.5 \text{ kV}$.

For simplicity, treat the difference as Disc 1 having effective pre-factor $1.05 I_0$:

**Step 1 — Formulate current equations (1 mark):**
$$
I_1 = 1.05 I_0 e^{25 \cdot V/4.5} \quad \text{(higher leakage disc)}
$$
$$
I_2 = I_0 e^{25 \cdot V/4.5} \quad \text{(nominal disc)}
$$
where $V$ is in kV, $I_0 = 1 \mu\text{A}$.

**Step 2 — Total current constraint and apply KCL (1 mark):**

For a parallel combination fed by a current source, $V$ is self-adjusted. But here $U_c = 3.6 \text{ kV}$ is applied directly across the parallel combination (since both discs share the same terminals). So $V = 3.6 \text{ kV}$.

**Step 3 — Compute individual currents (1.5 marks):**
$$
I_1 = 1.05 \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot e^{25 \cdot 3.6/4.5} = 1.05 \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot e^{20} = 1.05 \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot 4.85 \times 10^{8} = 509.2 \text{ A}
$$

Wait — this is unrealistically high for leakage current. The model $I = I_0 e^{\alpha V/V_{ref}}$ is for the **breakdown region**, not the leakage region. In the leakage region, a more appropriate model is $I = I_0 e^{\beta (V/V_{ref})}$ with much smaller exponent. The numbers here are illustrative; in practice $\alpha_{leakage} \approx 5$–$10$ in the pre-breakdown region. **For the KTU exam, follow the formula as given, even if the answer is large — note the conceptual point.**

Let us **recompute with the given values** as the examiner intends:

$$
I_1 = 1.05 \times 10^{-6} \times e^{25 \times 3.6 / 4.5} = 1.05 \times 10^{-6} \times e^{20} = 1.05 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.85 \times 10^{8} = 509.2 \text{ A}
$$
$$
I_2 = 1 \times 10^{-6} \times e^{20} = 485.2 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 4 — Power dissipated (1.5 marks):**
$$
P_1 = V \cdot I_1 = 3600 \cdot 509.2 = 1.833 \text{ MW}
$$
$$
P_2 = V \cdot I_2 = 3600 \cdot 485.2 = 1.747 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 5 — Total current and durability comment (2 marks):**
$$
I_{total} = I_1 + I_2 = 994.4 \text{ A}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Durability Implication (Conceptual, 1 mark):** In practice, even a small difference in disc characteristics causes the **"weaker" disc (Disc 1) to carry 4.9% more current** and dissipate 4.9% more heat. Over thousands of hours, this **positive feedback** — more heating → more leakage → more heating — drives Disc 1 to **age faster**, then to **fail first**, which forces the entire current into Disc 2, which then cascades into failure. This is why **parallel disc matching** is a strict manufacturing requirement (typically $\Delta V_{ref} \leq 2\%$) and why **disc grading** (selecting discs with matched $V_{ref}$ for parallel connection) is a key **durability engineering practice**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on numerical realism:** The $\alpha = 25$ value represents the **highly non-linear breakdown region**, not the pre-breakdown leakage. In a real exam, if the question intends pre-breakdown, use $\alpha = 5$–$10$; if it intends near breakdown, use $\alpha = 25$ and expect large currents. **Always state your assumption explicitly to gain method marks.**

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Arrester durability = Electrical + Thermal + Mechanical + Environmental + Long-term ageing**
- ✅ **Service life target:** 20–30 years, with **±5%** drift in $U_{ref}$ allowed
- ✅ **Standard:** IEC 60099-4 (international) and IEEE C62.11 (American) — both widely cited
- ✅ **Five Line Discharge Classes** (1 < 2 < 3 < 4 < 5) — Station class = highest energy
- ✅ **Critical equation:** $P_{gen} < P_{diss}$ — defines thermal stability
- ✅ **Cooling curve:** $T(t) = T_{amb} + (T_p - T_{amb}) e^{-t/\tau}$, with $\tau = mc_p / (hA)$
- ✅ **Thermal runaway:** Positive feedback loop — leakage current ↑ → heat ↑ → leakage current ↑ ↑
- ✅ **TOV curve:** Defines max TOV duration (seconds to minutes) before runaway — critical for relay coordination
- ✅ **High current impulse:** 100 kA, $4/10 \mu s$ — once in lifetime
- ✅ **Nominal discharge current:** 5 kA (Station), 10 kA (some classes), $8/20 \mu s$ — multiple times
- ✅ **Creepage distance:** $\geq 31 \text{ mm/kV}$ for heavy pollution (Kerala coastal areas!)
- ✅ **Operating duty test:** Two $8/20 \mu s$ surges with $U_c$ in between — must recover thermally
- ✅ **Accelerated ageing test:** 1000 h at $1.05 U_c$ at $115^{\circ}\text{C}$ — $\Delta U_{ref} \leq 5\%$
- ✅ **Disc grading:** Matched $V_{ref}$ within **2%** for parallel connection — prevents cascading failure
- ✅ **Pressure relief:** Arresters **must vent** safely under 16 kA short-circuit current — never implode
- ✅ **Seismic withstand:** 0.5 g horizontal per IEEE 693 — critical for Kerala seismic zone III
- ✅ **Protection margin:** BIL of protected equipment $\geq 1.4 \times U_{res,8/20,5kA}$ — KTU-favourite numerical
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** **"D-E-M-E-L"** — **D**urability = **D**uty + **E**nergy + **M**echanical + **E**nvironmental + **L**ife

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked a numerical, **always start with the standard rating formula** ($U_c = 0.8 U_r$), **then check protection margin** ($U_{res,5kA} \leq BIL/1.4$), **then estimate energy class**, and **finally check TOV with relay time**. This four-step drill is the examiner's expected **thinking sequence** — and following it explicitly earns full method marks even if your arithmetic is slightly off.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Voltage Testing of Insulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** High Voltage Testing of Insulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# High Voltage Testing of Insulators

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *High Voltage Testing of Insulators* is the systematic experimental procedure employed to verify the dielectric integrity, mechanical strength, and long-term operational reliability of insulating components used in overhead transmission lines, substations, and switchgear under simulated and accelerated electrical, environmental, and mechanical stresses. The tests are governed by Indian Standards (IS 2071, IS 2544, IS 731) and International Electrotechnical Commission standards (IEC 60060-1, IEC 60815).

The objective of these tests is to ensure that insulators neither **flashover** (surface breakdown along the insulator surface), **puncture** (dielectric failure through the body of the insulator), nor suffer **mechanical collapse** under combined electrical and environmental stresses during their service life.

### Types of Insulators Tested in KTU Curriculum

| Insulator Type | Typical Voltage Class | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Pin Insulator** | Up to **33 kV** | Distribution lines (low voltage) |
| **Disc / Cap-and-Pin Insulator** | **33 kV to 765 kV** | Suspension strings on EHV lines |
| **Long-Rod Insulator** | **11 kV to 400 kV** | Transmission and traction |
| **Strain / Dead-End Insulator** | **11 kV onwards** | Terminal towers, angle towers |
| **Post Insulator** | **11 kV to 1200 kV** | Substations, busbar supports |
| **Composite (Polymer) Insulator** | **11 kV to 1100 kV** | Modern EHV/UHV lines |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Firefighter's Staircase Test"**
> Imagine a multi-storey building's fire escape staircase. Before allowing people to use it, the fire department performs three tests:
> 1. **Load Test** — They pile weights on each step to ensure it can hold a crowd of people (this is the *Mechanical Test*).
> 2. **Heat Test** — They expose the staircase to a controlled fire blast to ensure it doesn't catch fire and crumble (this is the *Power-Frequency Withstand Test* — simulates sustained overvoltages).
> 3. **Shockwave Test** — They simulate a sudden explosion's pressure wave hitting the staircase to check if it can survive an instant, violent shock (this is the *Impulse Voltage Test* — simulates lightning).
>
> An **insulator** in a transmission line is exactly like that staircase — it must hold the conductor's weight, survive continuous AC voltage, and withstand sudden lightning impulses, all while being battered by rain, pollution, and pollution. **High Voltage Testing is the "fire department inspection" of insulators.**

### Why Insulators Need High Voltage Testing

- **Safety of Life and Equipment:** A single insulator failure can cause fatal line-to-ground faults, fires, and widespread blackouts.
- **Quality Assurance at Manufacturing:** Statistical variation in porcelain firing, cementing, and polymer compounding mandates batch-wise testing.
- **Acceptance Testing:** Utilities must verify each insulator before stringing on the line.
- **R&D Validation:** New insulator designs (composite, silicone-rubber) must prove superiority over conventional porcelain.
- **Compliance with Grid Code and Standards:** Regulatory bodies mandate certification under IS/IEC standards.

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used in KTU 2024 Examinations:**
> - **Power Frequency Test Voltage:** **1 kV/s** rate of rise (standard)
> - **Standard Lightning Impulse:** **1.2/50 μs** waveform (front time / tail time)
> - **Standard Switching Impulse:** **250/2500 μs** waveform
> - **Wet Test Water Resistivity:** **100 Ω·m ± 10%** at **20°C**
> - **Wet Test Precipitation Rate:** **1 mm/min to 2 mm/min**
> - **Wet Test Water Temperature:** Ambient ± **15°C**
> - **Standard Reference Atmospheric Conditions:** Temperature **20°C**, Pressure **101.3 kPa**, Absolute Humidity **11 g/m³**
> - **Number of Impulses in Withstand Test:** **15 impulses** of standard wave
> - **Number of Impulses in 50% Flashover Test:** Minimum **20 valid impulses** per level

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of High Voltage Tests on Insulators

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes tests into the following hierarchy:

### A. Type Tests (Design Verification — Done Once on a Prototype)
> [!NOTE]
> Type tests prove that the **design** of the insulator is sound. They are exhaustive, destructive in nature, and performed on a few samples from a new design.

1. **Power Frequency Voltage Withstand Test (Dry and Wet)**
2. **Power Frequency Flashover Test (50% Flashover Voltage Determination)**
3. **Lightning Impulse Voltage Withstand Test (Dry and Wet)**
4. **Switching Impulse Voltage Withstand Test (for ≥ 300 kV class)**
5. **Puncture Voltage Test** (for porcelain pin-type insulators only)
6. **Mechanical Strength Test (Ultimate Tensile, Cantilever, Torsional)**
7. **Thermal-Mechanical Performance Test (Combined Stress)**
8. **Steep Front Impulse Test (for cap-and-pin discs)**
9. **Electromechanical Failing Load Test**

### B. Routine Tests (Quality Control — Done on Every Insulator)
- Visual and Dimensional Inspection
- Electrical Routine Test (Power frequency spark-over test / puncture test)
- Mechanical Routine Test (Proof load test)

### C. Sample Tests (Statistical Quality — Done on Random Samples)
- Corrosion resistance test
- Thermal shock test
- Porosity test (for porcelain)
- Galvanizing test
- Power frequency voltage withstand on a few samples

---

## 2.2 Power Frequency Voltage Withstand Test — Detailed Procedure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Purpose:** Verify that the insulator can withstand the specified **one-minute power-frequency test voltage** without flashover or puncture.

### Test Arrangement
- The insulator is mounted in its **normal service orientation** on a grounded metal support structure.
- A **smooth cylindrical rod** or **conductor equivalent** is attached to the top groove/cap.
- High voltage is applied between the conductor and the grounded support.

### Procedure
1. Initial application of voltage should not exceed **1/3** of the test voltage.
2. Voltage is raised gradually at a uniform rate of approximately **1 kV/s** until the specified test value is reached.
3. The specified test voltage is held for **1 minute (60 seconds)**.
4. The voltage is then reduced gradually at the same rate to zero.
5. **Pass Criterion:** No flashover, no puncture, no surface tracking.

### Dry vs Wet Power Frequency Test
- **Dry Test:** Conducted under standard atmospheric conditions (Temperature 20°C, Pressure 101.3 kPa, Humidity 11 g/m³).
- **Wet Test:** Conducted while the insulator is sprayed with water of **100 Ω·m resistivity** at **1–2 mm/min** precipitation rate, at an angle of **45°** to the vertical.

### Standard Test Voltages for Different Voltage Classes
> [!NOTE]
> These values appear frequently in KTU numerical problems.

| System Voltage (kV rms) | Standard Withstand Voltage (kV rms, dry & wet) |
|---|---|
| **11 kV** | **28 kV (dry) / 28 kV (wet)** |
| **22 kV** | **50 kV (dry) / 50 kV (wet)** |
| **33 kV** | **70 kV (dry) / 70 kV (wet)** |
| **66 kV** | **140 kV** |
| **110 kV** | **230 kV** |
| **132 kV** | **275 kV** |
| **220 kV** | **460 kV** |
| **400 kV** | **680 kV / 630 kV** |
| **765 kV** | **830 kV** |

---

## 2.3 Lightning Impulse Voltage Withstand Test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Purpose:** Verify that the insulator can withstand the **1.2/50 μs standard lightning impulse** without flashover.

### Standard Lightning Impulse Parameters
- **Front Time (T₁) = 1.2 μs** (Virtual front duration between 30% and 90% points multiplied by 1.67)
- **Tail Time (T₂) = 50 μs** (Time from virtual origin to 50% of peak on the tail)
- **Tolerance:** ± 30% on T₁, ± 20% on T₂

### Test Procedure
1. Apply **15 consecutive impulses** of the specified peak voltage with **positive polarity**.
2. Wait sufficient time between impulses (typically **1 minute**) to allow de-ionization.
3. **Pass Criterion:** Not more than **2 flashovers** out of 15 impulses (statistical allowance for dispersion).
4. If test fails, repeat with 15 new impulses — must have **zero flashovers** in the second set.
5. **For wet impulse test:** Water spray conditions as in wet power frequency test.

### 50% Flashover Voltage (V₅₀) Determination Method
- The simplest statistical method is the **Up-and-Down Method** (IEC 60060-1).
- Apply impulses, starting at an estimated V₅₀.
- If flashover occurs, reduce the next impulse voltage by ΔV.
- If no flashover, increase the next impulse voltage by ΔV.
- **V₅₀ = Mean of the applied voltages (flashovers and withstands)**
- **Standard Deviation σ ≤ 3%** is required for a valid test.

---

## 2.4 Puncture Voltage Test (Porcelain Pin Insulators)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Purpose:** Verify that the porcelain body of the insulator can withstand voltage **through its thickness** without internal puncture, even if external surface flashover occurs.

### Why Puncture Test is Critical
- A flashover is a recoverable event (the arc travels externally).
- A **puncture** is an internal, irreversible dielectric failure — the insulator becomes permanently shorted.
- The safety principle: **Puncture Voltage > Flashover Voltage**, so that even under the worst overvoltage, the insulator safely flashes over externally rather than failing internally.

### Test Procedure
1. The pin insulator is immersed in an **insulating oil bath** to prevent external surface flashover.
2. AC voltage is raised gradually until puncture occurs.
3. **Pass Criterion:** Puncture voltage must be at least **1.3 times** the dry power-frequency flashover voltage (per IS 731).

---

## 2.5 Mechanical Strength Tests

> [!NOTE]
> Insulators must also carry **combined electro-mechanical loads** in service. Common tests include:
>
> - **Electromechanical Failing Load Test:** Apply 75% of rated mechanical load, then increase voltage until electrical failure. The insulator must hold the load throughout.
> - **Mechanical Failing Load Test:** Apply tensile load (for suspension discs) or cantilever load (for pin insulators) until mechanical failure. The breaking load must exceed the rated value with a safety factor.
> - **Thermal-Mechanical Test (for composite insulators):** Apply 50% of rated mechanical load and subject to **+50°C / -30°C** thermal cycling for **96 hours** to test seal integrity.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024 Examinations**

| Sl. No. | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{test} = 1.1 \text{ to } 1.15 \times V_{LL}$ | Withstand test voltage as multiple of line-to-line voltage | kV |
| 2 | $V_{test} = 1.5 \times V_{LL} + 30$ | Alternate empirical rule for system voltage (Clarke & Recknagel) | kV |
| 3 | $\text{Creepage Distance } (L_c) = k_c \times U_m$ | Minimum creepage distance for insulator | mm |
| 4 | $k_c = \text{Specific Creepage Distance}$ | Depends on pollution severity (16 to 31 mm/kV) | mm/kV |
| 5 | $\text{Unified Specific Creepage Distance (USCD)} = L_c / U_m$ | Pollution performance indicator | mm/kV |
| 6 | $V_{50\%} = \text{Mean of up-and-down voltages}$ | 50% flashover voltage | kV |
| 7 | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (V_i - V_{50})^2}{n-1}}$ | Standard deviation of flashover voltages | kV |
| 8 | $\text{Safety Factor} = \dfrac{V_{puncture}}{V_{flashover}}$ | Must be $\geq 1.3$ for porcelain insulators | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Disruptive Discharge Voltage (DDV)}$ | Voltage causing breakdown across insulation | kV |
| 10 | $\text{Withstand Voltage} = 0.8 \times V_{50\%}$ | Conservative design withstand value | kV |
| 11 | $T_1 = 1.67 \times t_{30\% \to 90\%}$ | Virtual front time of impulse | μs |
| 12 | $T_2 = t_{50\% \text{ on tail}}$ | Virtual tail time of impulse | μs |
| 13 | $V_{50\% \text{ corrected}} = \dfrac{V_{50\% \text{ measured}}}{\delta^m \times k^t}$ | Atmospheric correction factor | kV |
| 14 | $\delta = \dfrac{b}{b_0} \times \dfrac{(273 + t_0)}{(273 + t)}$ | Air density factor (temperature t in °C, pressure b in kPa) | Dimensionless |
| 15 | $k = h / \delta^8 \times e^{(C/\delta) \times (1/h - 1)}$ | Humidity correction factor (simplified) | Dimensionless |

### Atmospheric Correction Factor (Detailed)

For correcting the measured test voltage to standard atmospheric conditions, IEC 60060-1 uses:

$$V_{corrected} = \dfrac{V_{measured}}{K_t \times K_h}$$

where $K_t$ is the **air density correction factor** and $K_h$ is the **humidity correction factor**.

For insulators (which flashover in air, not oil), the **g factor** (gap factor) determines the correction exponents $m$ and $w$:

$$K_t = \delta^m, \quad K_h = h^w$$

Here:
- $\delta$ = relative air density (RATIO of actual density to standard)
- $h$ = absolute humidity in g/m³
- $m \approx 1.0$ and $w \approx 1.0$ for typical insulator configurations (rod-plane gap-like behavior)

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Knowledge Matters in Industry:**
> - **Power Transmission Utilities (KSEB, NTPC, PGCIL, Adani Power):** Use these tests to qualify insulator batches before stringing on 765 kV and 400 kV lines.
> - **Insulator Manufacturers (BHEL, Aditya Birla Insulators, NGK, Siemens):** Must perform type tests at NABL-accredited labs (CPRI, ERDA) for product certification.
> - **Polymer Insulator Industry (Deccan Enterprises, Teknomega):** Need to validate silicone rubber housing under UV, salt-fog, and acid-rain conditions.
> - **Railway Electrification:** Traction insulators on Indian Railways undergo severe pollution testing using salt-fog chambers.
> - **Substation Equipment Testing:** Transformer bushings, CT/PT insulators, and lightning arresters housing must pass similar test protocols.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Atmospheric Correction Factor (Complete)

Given a **measured 50% flashover voltage** at non-standard atmospheric conditions, derive the corrected value for standard conditions.

### Given Data
- Measured 50% flashover voltage: $V_{50,m} = 142 \text{ kV}$
- Ambient temperature: $t = 35°C$
- Atmospheric pressure: $b = 96 \text{ kPa}$
- Absolute humidity: $h_a = 18 \text{ g/m}^3$
- Standard temperature: $t_0 = 20°C$
- Standard pressure: $b_0 = 101.3 \text{ kPa}$
- Standard humidity: $h_0 = 11 \text{ g/m}^3$

### Step 1: Calculate Relative Air Density $\delta$

The relative air density is defined as the ratio of the actual air density to the standard air density. Using the ideal gas law:

$$\delta = \dfrac{b \cdot (273 + t_0)}{b_0 \cdot (273 + t)}$$

Substituting values:

$$\delta = \dfrac{96 \times (273 + 20)}{101.3 \times (273 + 35)}$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{96 \times 293}{101.3 \times 308}$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{28128}{31200.4}$$

$$\delta = 0.9015$$

### Step 2: Calculate Air Density Correction Factor $K_t$

For a typical insulator gap (similar to a rod-plane), $m = 1.0$:

$$K_t = \delta^m = 0.9015^1 = 0.9015$$

### Step 3: Calculate Humidity Ratio $h/h_0$

The humidity ratio is the absolute humidity divided by the standard value:

$$h_{ratio} = \dfrac{h_a}{h_0} = \dfrac{18}{11} = 1.636$$

### Step 4: Calculate Humidity Correction Factor $K_h$

For typical insulator gaps, $w = 1.0$:

$$K_h = h_{ratio}^w = 1.636^1 = 1.636$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Corrected 50% Flashover Voltage

The corrected voltage is:

$$V_{50,corrected} = \dfrac{V_{50,measured}}{K_t \times K_h}$$

$$V_{50,corrected} = \dfrac{142}{0.9015 \times 1.636}$$

$$V_{50,corrected} = \dfrac{142}{1.4749}$$

$$\boxed{V_{50,corrected} = 96.27 \text{ kV}}$$

### Interpretation
Since the test was conducted at **lower air density** (hot day) and **higher humidity**, the measured flashover voltage is **higher** than the standard value. After correction, the insulator would flash over at **96.27 kV** under standard conditions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Specific Creepage Distance Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 220 kV transmission line passes through an area with **heavy industrial pollution**. The pollution severity class is **IV (Very Heavy)**. Calculate the minimum creepage distance required for the suspension insulator string.

### Given Data
- Maximum system voltage (phase-to-phase): $U_m = 245 \text{ kV}$ (245 kV class for 220 kV system)
- Pollution severity class: **IV — Very Heavy**
- From IS 60815 / IEC 60815: Specific creepage distance for Class IV: $k_c = 31 \text{ mm/kV}$ (phase-to-phase)

### Step 1: Apply the Creepage Distance Formula

$$L_c = k_c \times U_m$$

where $U_m$ is the **maximum system voltage between phases (rms)**.

### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$L_c = 31 \times 245$$

$$\boxed{L_c = 7595 \text{ mm} = 7.595 \text{ m}}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Number of Disc Insulators

Each standard **disc insulator** (120 kN class, 146 mm creepage) provides approximately **320 mm** of creepage distance (typical 250 mm × 1.3 pollution boost).

$$N = \dfrac{L_c}{L_{disc}} = \dfrac{7595}{320} \approx 23.7$$

Rounding up: **24 discs** are required for a 220 kV line in Class IV pollution.

### Comparison Table

| Pollution Class | $k_c$ (mm/kV) | Creepage for 220 kV | Approx. Discs |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I (Light)** | 16 | 3920 mm | 13 |
| **II (Medium)** | 20 | 4900 mm | 16 |
| **III (Heavy)** | 25 | 6125 mm | 20 |
| **IV (Very Heavy)** | 31 | 7595 mm | 24 |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Safety Factor Verification for Pin Insulator

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 33 kV pin insulator has a measured **dry power-frequency flashover voltage of 95 kV** and a **puncture voltage of 140 kV** (measured in oil bath). Verify if the insulator meets IS 731 safety requirements.

### Step 1: Recall the IS 731 Criterion

The puncture voltage must be at least **1.3 times** the dry flashover voltage:

$$V_{puncture, required} \geq 1.3 \times V_{flashover, dry}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Required Puncture Voltage

$$V_{puncture, required} = 1.3 \times 95 = 123.5 \text{ kV}$$

### Step 3: Compare with Measured Value

$$V_{puncture, measured} = 140 \text{ kV} \geq 123.5 \text{ kV}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Actual Safety Factor

$$\text{SF}_{actual} = \dfrac{V_{puncture, measured}}{V_{flashover, dry}} = \dfrac{140}{95} = 1.474$$

### Conclusion

$$\boxed{\text{SF}_{actual} = 1.474 > 1.3 \text{ (IS 731 requirement) → INSULATOR QUALIFIES}}$$

The insulator has a 47.4% safety margin over the minimum requirement, ensuring that the porcelain body will not puncture even if the surface flashes over.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Impulse Voltage Withstand from V₅₀

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 110 kV suspension insulator string has a measured V₅₀ (positive polarity, dry) of **320 kV**. Calculate the **impulse withstand voltage** of the string.

### Step 1: Apply the IEC Design Rule

The standard withstand voltage is taken as:

$$V_{withstand} = 0.8 \times V_{50\%}$$

This 20% safety margin accounts for:
- Polarity reversal effects
- Wave shape variation
- Aging degradation

### Step 2: Substitute

$$V_{withstand} = 0.8 \times 320 = 256 \text{ kV}$$

### Step 3: Compare with Required Standard

For 110 kV system, the standard impulse withstand is **550 kV** (BIL). The string is **insufficient** and would need more discs.

$$N_{required} = 550 / 256 \approx 2.15 \implies 3 \text{ discs minimum}$$

$$\boxed{V_{withstand} = 256 \text{ kV per disc; needs 3 discs for BIL compliance}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — KTU 2024 Numerical Problem Patterns

| Problem Type | Key Data Given | Key Formula | Typical Marks in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric correction | $V_{50,m}, t, b, h_a$ | $V_{50,c} = V_{50,m} / (\delta^m \cdot h^w)$ | **7 marks** |
| Creepage distance | $U_m$, Pollution class | $L_c = k_c \times U_m$ | **3–7 marks** |
| Number of discs | $L_c$, $L_{disc}$ | $N = L_c / L_{disc}$ | **3 marks** |
| Safety factor | $V_{flashover}, V_{puncture}$ | $SF = V_{puncture} / V_{flashover}$ | **3 marks** |
| Withstand voltage | $V_{50}$ | $V_w = 0.8 \times V_{50}$ | **3 marks** |
| Test voltage from system voltage | $V_{LL}$ | $V_t = 1.5 V_{LL} + 30$ | **3–7 marks** |
| Time constant of impulse generator | $R_1, C_1, R_2, C_2$ | $T_1 = 3 R_1 C_1 / 0.9$ (approx.) | **7 marks** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Insulator Test Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High Voltage Tests on Insulators] --> B[Type Tests]
    A --> C[Routine Tests]
    A --> D[Sample Tests]

    B --> B1[Power Freq Withstand - Dry]
    B --> B2[Power Freq Withstand - Wet]
    B --> B3[Lightning Impulse Withstand - 1.2/50 us]
    B --> B4[Switching Impulse Withstand - 250/2500 us]
    B --> B5[Puncture Voltage Test]
    B --> B6[Mechanical Strength Tests]
    B --> B7[Steep Front Impulse Test]

    C --> C1[Visual and Dimensional Check]
    C --> C2[Power Freq Sparkover Routine Test]
    C --> C3[Mechanical Proof Load Test]

    D --> D1[Thermal Shock Test]
    D --> D2[Porosity Test for Porcelain]
    D --> D3[Galvanizing Test]
    D --> D4[Corrosion Resistance Test]
```

---

## 4.2 Power Frequency Test Setup — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HV Transformer 220V to 100kV+ Input] --> B[Regulating Variac]
    B --> C[HV Step Up Transformer]
    C --> D[HV Test Object - Insulator]
    D --> E[Ground Plane and Support]
    C --> F[Resistive Voltage Divider]
    F --> G[Peak Voltmeter Display]
    D --> H[Sphere Gap for Calibration]

    I[Water Spray System 100 ohm meter 1 to 2 mm per min] --> D
```

---

## 4.3 Impulse Voltage Test Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Test] --> Step1[Charge Impulse Generator Capacitor]
    Step1 --> Step2[Trigger Sphere Gap - Apply Impulse]
    Step2 --> Step3[Impulse Travels to Test Object]
    Step3 --> Decision{Flashover Occurred?}

    Decision -- Yes --> Record1[Record Flashover Voltage]
    Decision -- No --> Record2[Record Withstand Voltage]

    Record1 --> Adjust1[Reduce Voltage by delta V]
    Record2 --> Adjust2[Increase Voltage by delta V]

    Adjust1 --> Step1
    Adjust2 --> Step1

    Step1 --> Counter[Increment Impulse Count]
    Counter --> Check{15 to 20 Impulses Completed?}
    Check -- No --> Step1
    Check -- Yes --> Calc[Calculate V50 and Standard Deviation]
    Calc --> Verify{sigma less than 3 percent of V50?}
    Verify -- Yes --> Pass[Test Valid - Report V50]
    Verify -- No --> Repeat[Repeat Test with Fresh Sample]
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Logic: Does the Insulator Pass or Fail?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Test[Completed High Voltage Tests] --> Q1{Puncture Voltage > 1.3 times Flashover Voltage?}
    Q1 -- No --> FAIL1[FAIL - Internal Puncture Risk]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Withstands 1 minute Power Freq Test?}
    Q2 -- No --> FAIL2[FAIL - Surface Flashover]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Withstands 15 Lightning Impulses with max 2 Flashovers?}
    Q3 -- No --> FAIL3[FAIL - Impulse Weakness]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Mechanical Load exceeds Rated by safety factor?}
    Q4 -- No --> FAIL4[FAIL - Mechanical Weakness]
    Q4 -- Yes --> PASS[QUALIFIED - Issue Test Certificate]
```

---

## 4.5 Composite Insulator Pollution Performance Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pollution Severity Assessment] --> B[Site Survey - ESDD NSDD]
    B --> C{Equivalent Salt Deposit Density}

    C -- Less than 0.03 mg per sq cm --> Class1[Class I - Light]
    C -- 0.03 to 0.06 --> Class2[Class II - Medium]
    C -- 0.06 to 0.10 --> Class3[Class III - Heavy]
    C -- Greater than 0.10 --> Class4[Class IV - Very Heavy]

    Class1 --> R1[Required Creepage 16 mm per kV]
    Class2 --> R2[Required Creepage 20 mm per kV]
    Class3 --> R3[Required Creepage 25 mm per kV]
    Class4 --> R4[Required Creepage 31 mm per kV]
```

---

## 4.6 Test Voltage Sequence Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Insulator Under Test] --> B{Test Type}

    B --> C[Power Freq Test]
    B --> D[Lightning Impulse Test]
    B --> E[Switching Impulse Test]

    C --> C1[Rise 1 kV per s]
    C1 --> C2[Hold 60 seconds]
    C2 --> C3{Reduction 1 kV per s}

    D --> D1[Apply 1.2 by 50 us Wave]
    D1 --> D2[15 Impulses Total]
    D2 --> D3{Flashover count less than 2?}

    E --> E1[Apply 250 by 2500 us Wave]
    E1 --> E2[15 Impulses Total]
    E2 --> E3{Flashover count less than 2?}
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Define creepage distance. Why is it important in the design of insulators used in polluted environments?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Creepage distance is the **shortest distance measured along the surface of an insulating material** between two conductive parts at different potentials. For an insulator, it is the distance along the outer surface from the top metal fitting (cap) to the bottom metal fitting (pin).

**Importance in Polluted Environments (1.5 marks):** In coastal, industrial, or desert environments, the insulator surface gets coated with conductive pollutants like salt, soot, and chemicals. When wetted by dew or rain, this forms a **conductive film** that allows **leakage current** to flow along the surface. The creepage distance must be long enough to prevent this leakage current from developing into a **flashover**. Hence, insulators used in polluted areas have **longer creepage distances**, achieved through extra **weather sheds (petticoats)**.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **What is the significance of the 1.2/50 μs standard lightning impulse waveform in insulator testing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Waveform Definition (1.5 marks):** The 1.2/50 μs waveform is the **standardized lightning impulse** that represents the voltage stress on an insulator due to a typical lightning strike. It has a **virtual front time of 1.2 μs** (fast rise) and a **virtual tail time of 50 μs** (decay to 50% of peak).

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
1. **Reproducibility:** It provides a uniform test wave globally accepted by IS and IEC standards, allowing comparison of test results from different labs.
2. **Realism:** The fast front (1.2 μs) emulates the steep rise of a lightning strike, while the 50 μs tail represents the discharge duration.
3. **Quality Benchmark:** It is used to define the **Basic Insulation Level (BIL)** of the insulator — e.g., 110 kV insulators must withstand 550 kV of this waveform.
4. **Statistical Validation:** Using 15 impulses, the impulse withstand voltage is statistically verified, ensuring the insulator is robust against natural lightning variability.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Internal Choice Option 1)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram, the **procedure for conducting the power frequency voltage withstand test** (dry and wet) on a **pin-type insulator** as per IS 731. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A 66 kV class pin insulator has the following test data:
> - Dry flashover voltage = 140 kV
> - Wet flashover voltage = 110 kV
> - Puncture voltage = 195 kV
> Determine the **safety factor** and comment on whether the insulator qualifies for use. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A(a) — **7 Marks**

**1. Test Purpose (1 mark):** The test verifies that the insulator can withstand the **specified 1-minute power-frequency test voltage** without flashover or puncture under both dry and wet conditions.

**2. Test Arrangement (2 marks):**
- The pin insulator is mounted on a **grounded metal cross-arm** or support, simulating service orientation.
- A **conductor equivalent** (smooth metal rod of standard diameter) is fixed in the top groove.
- For the **wet test**, a **water spray system** is arranged to deliver water at:
  - Resistivity: **100 Ω·m ± 10%** at 20°C
  - Precipitation rate: **1–2 mm/min**
  - Spray angle: **45°** to the vertical
  - Water temperature: Ambient ± 15°C

**3. Test Procedure (3 marks):**
| Step | Action | Specification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial voltage application | Not more than **1/3** of test voltage |
| 2 | Voltage rise rate | **1 kV/s** (uniform) |
| 3 | Hold at test voltage | **1 minute (60 s)** |
| 4 | Voltage reduction | Uniform, **1 kV/s** to zero |
| 5 | Observation | No flashover, no puncture, no damage |
| 6 | Repeat for wet test | Same procedure with spray ON |

**4. Pass Criterion (1 mark):** No flashover, no puncture during the 1-minute hold. The insulator is declared qualified for the voltage class.

---

#### Solution to Question A(b) — **7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- Dry flashover voltage, $V_{f,dry} = 140 \text{ kV}$
- Wet flashover voltage, $V_{f,wet} = 110 \text{ kV}$
- Puncture voltage, $V_{puncture} = 195 \text{ kV}$

**Step 1: Calculate the Safety Factor (3 marks)**

The safety factor is defined as the ratio of puncture voltage to dry flashover voltage:

$$SF = \dfrac{V_{puncture}}{V_{f,dry}}$$

$$SF = \dfrac{195}{140}$$

$$\boxed{SF = 1.393}$$

**Step 2: Compare with IS 731 Criterion (2 marks)**

IS 731 requires:

$$SF_{required} \geq 1.3$$

$$1.393 \geq 1.3 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Comment (2 marks)**

The insulator has a safety factor of **1.393**, which is **9.3% above the IS 731 minimum requirement of 1.3**. This means the puncture voltage is comfortably higher than the flashover voltage, ensuring that the insulator will safely flash over externally rather than fail internally under any overvoltage. **The insulator QUALIFIES for use** in 66 kV class applications. However, designers may consider using an insulator with a higher safety margin (≥ 1.5) for critical installations or highly polluted environments.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Option 2)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the **Up-and-Down method** for determining the **50% flashover voltage (V₅₀)** of a suspension insulator string under standard lightning impulse. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A **220 kV disc insulator string** is to be designed for a **Class III (Heavy) pollution** area. The system maximum voltage is **245 kV**. Each disc has a creepage of **320 mm**. Calculate:
> - (i) The **total creepage distance** required.
> - (ii) The **number of discs** required.
> - (iii) The **specific creepage distance (USCD)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(a) — **7 Marks**

**1. Concept of V₅₀ (1 mark):** The 50% flashover voltage is the peak value of the standard impulse that causes flashover in **50% of the applications**. It is the most statistically reliable measure of the impulse strength of an insulator.

**2. Why Up-and-Down Method (1 mark):** The Up-and-Down method is preferred over the Multi-Level Method because it **concentrates the test voltages around V₅₀**, reducing the number of impulses required (typically 20–30) and providing a **good statistical estimate** with **minimum stress on the insulator**.

**3. Block Diagram of the Method (3 marks):**

```
START
   |
   v
Estimate Initial Voltage V0 ≈ V50 (from past data)
   |
   v
Apply First Impulse at V0
   |
   v
+-----------------+
| Flashover?      |
+-----------------+
   | YES          | NO
   v              v
Reduce next      Increase next
voltage by ΔV    voltage by ΔV
   |              |
   +------+-------+
          v
   Apply next impulse
          |
          v
   Repeat for at least 20 valid impulses
          |
          v
   Calculate V50 = Mean of all applied voltages
   Calculate σ = Standard deviation
          |
          v
   Validate: σ ≤ 3% of V50 (per IEC 60060-1)
          |
          v
   Report V50
```

**4. Step ΔV Selection (1 mark):** The step voltage ΔV should be approximately **3% to 5% of the estimated V₅₀**. Too small ΔV wastes time; too large ΔV reduces accuracy.

**5. Pass Criterion (1 mark):** Per IS/IEC, **at least 20 valid impulses** must be applied, and the **standard deviation must be ≤ 3%** of V₅₀. If σ > 3%, the test must be repeated on a fresh sample.

---

#### Solution to Question B(b) — **7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- Maximum system voltage (line-to-line rms): $U_m = 245 \text{ kV}$
- Pollution class: **III (Heavy)**
- Creepage per disc: $L_{disc} = 320 \text{ mm}$
- Specific creepage for Class III: $k_c = 25 \text{ mm/kV}$ (from IS 60815 / IEC 60815)

---

**Step (i): Total Creepage Distance (2.5 marks)**

The minimum creepage distance required is:

$$L_c = k_c \times U_m$$

$$L_c = 25 \times 245$$

$$\boxed{L_c = 6125 \text{ mm} = 6.125 \text{ m}}$$

**Step (ii): Number of Discs (2.5 marks)**

The number of discs required is:

$$N = \dfrac{L_c}{L_{disc}} = \dfrac{6125}{320} = 19.14$$

Rounding up to the next integer:

$$\boxed{N = 20 \text{ discs}}$$

**Step (iii): Specific Creepage Distance USCD (2 marks)**

The Unified Specific Creepage Distance is the **achieved** value after design:

$$USCD = \dfrac{N \times L_{disc}}{U_m} = \dfrac{20 \times 320}{245}$$

$$\boxed{USCD = 26.12 \text{ mm/kV}}$$

**Verification:** USCD (26.12) > Required (25.0) for Class III. **Design is compliant** with a small safety margin.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget to use $U_m$ (maximum system voltage, e.g., 245 kV for 220 kV line) and mistakenly use the **nominal** system voltage (220 kV). This gives **incorrect creepage** and **fewer discs**, which is a serious design error in practice.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to **round UP** the number of discs. Never use 19 discs if 19.14 is calculated — always round up to the next integer (20) for safety.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing **dry flashover voltage** with **wet flashover voltage** in the safety factor formula. IS 731 specifies the **dry** flashover as the reference.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not mentioning the **1 kV/s** rise rate in the power frequency test. Examiners specifically look for this standard rate.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** For the Up-and-Down method, students often confuse **"flashover"** with **"puncture"** — these are different phenomena. Flashover is a surface event; puncture is an internal failure.
> - **Common Mistake #6:** Not applying atmospheric correction in V₅₀ calculations. Always check if the test was conducted under standard conditions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam:**

- [ ] **Definition:** High voltage testing of insulators verifies dielectric, mechanical, and environmental performance per IS 2071, IS 2544, IS 731, IEC 60060-1, and IEC 60815.
- [ ] **Test Categories:** **Type tests** (design), **Routine tests** (every unit), **Sample tests** (statistical).
- [ ] **Power Freq Withstand Test:** Voltage rise at **1 kV/s**, hold for **1 minute**, no flashover/puncture allowed.
- [ ] **Standard Lightning Impulse:** **1.2/50 μs** waveform; 15 impulses; max **2 flashovers** allowed.
- [ ] **Standard Switching Impulse:** **250/2500 μs** waveform; used for systems **≥ 300 kV**.
- [ ] **Wet Test Conditions:** Water resistivity **100 Ω·m**, precipitation **1–2 mm/min**, angle **45°**.
- [ ] **Standard Reference Atmosphere:** **20°C, 101.3 kPa, 11 g/m³ humidity**.
- [ ] **Puncture Safety Factor:** $SF = V_{puncture} / V_{flashover} \geq 1.3$ (IS 731).
- [ ] **50% Flashover Voltage (V₅₀):** Determined by **Up-and-Down method**; ≥ 20 impulses; **σ ≤ 3%** of V₅₀.
- [ ] **Withstand Voltage:** $V_w = 0.8 \times V_{50\%}$ (20% safety margin).
- [ ] **Creepage Distance Formula:** $L_c = k_c \times U_m$ where $k_c$ depends on pollution class (16 to 31 mm/kV).
- [ ] **Pollution Classes:** Light (16), Medium (20), Heavy (25), Very Heavy (31) mm/kV.
- [ ] **Atmospheric Correction:** $V_{corrected} = V_{measured} / (\delta^m \times h^w)$ with $m \approx 1.0$, $w \approx 1.0$ for insulators.
- [ ] **Creepage per disc:** Typically **280–320 mm** for standard 120 kN/160 kN/210 kN discs.
- [ ] **Composite Insulators:** Need additional **thermal-mechanical** and **UV ageing** tests per IEC 61109.
- [ ] **Always use $U_m$** (maximum system voltage), not nominal voltage, in creepage calculations.
- [ ] **Always round UP** the number of discs to the next integer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bushings
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bushings

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Bushing** is a carefully engineered insulating device employed to safely carry an energized high-voltage conductor through a grounded metallic barrier — such as the wall of a transformer tank, a circuit-breaker tank, the steel tank of an oil-filled reactor, or through a concrete wall of a high-voltage substation — without permitting any flashover, puncture, or surface discharge between the live conductor and the grounded enclosure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A bushing is an *insulating structure* that provides a *controlled leakage path* and a *predetermined potential gradient* between the central conductor and the outer flange, simultaneously offering mechanical support to the conductor and a sealed barrier against moisture, oil, and atmospheric contamination.

In other words, a bushing is essentially an **insulated "sleeve"** that solves a fundamental engineering problem: how to bring high voltage safely *inside* a grounded enclosure without compromising either the conductor's electrical isolation or the structural integrity of the enclosure.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine trying to pass a high-pressure water pipe through the wall of a sealed metal tank. If you simply drilled a hole and stuck the pipe through, water would spray everywhere and the joint would leak. To handle this, engineers use a **flanged fitting with rubber gaskets** that gradually seals the pipe to the wall while distributing pressure evenly around the joint.

A **bushing performs the same job for electricity**:
- The **central conductor** is like the water pipe (carrying the high-voltage current).
- The **insulating body** (oil, paper, porcelain, resin) is like the rubber gasket (preventing electrical leakage).
- The **flange** is the metal ring bolted to the tank wall (anchoring the bushing and providing the seal).
- The **sheds/weather sheds** on the outdoor surface are like the ridges on a waterproof coat — they force any creeping water droplet to take a longer, non-conductive path along the surface.

So the bushing accomplishes **three simultaneous jobs**:
1. **Electrical Insulation** — prevents breakdown between the HV conductor and the grounded tank.
2. **Mechanical Support** — bears the weight, vibration, and electromagnetic forces on the conductor.
3. **Environmental Sealing** — prevents ingress of moisture, dust, oil, and contaminants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** The bushing is *not* a passive pipe — it is an active electrical component whose internal design (especially the capacitive grading layers) directly controls the **voltage distribution along its length**. This is why KTU frequently tests the *theory of capacitance-graded condenser bushings*.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_{0}$ | **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** | Fundamental field constant |
| Relative permittivity (oil-impregnated paper) | $\varepsilon_{r}$ | **3.0 to 4.5** | Dielectric property of OIP |
| Relative permittivity (porcelain) | $\varepsilon_{r}$ | **5.0 to 7.0** | Used for outdoor housings |
| Relative permittivity (resin) | $\varepsilon_{r}$ | **3.5 to 5.0** | Used in RBP / RIP bushings |
| Creepage distance | $L_{c}$ | **25–50 mm/kV** (line-to-ground) | Length of leakage path |
| Specific creepage for heavily polluted zones | — | **up to 60 mm/kV** | IEC 60815 standard |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Primary Functions of a Bushing

A high-voltage bushing must simultaneously satisfy four engineering requirements:

1. **Withstand the highest system voltage continuously** without internal partial discharge.
2. **Withstand transient overvoltages** (lightning impulse and switching impulse) without puncture.
3. **Provide a long, controlled surface leakage path** to prevent external flashover under rain, fog, or pollution.
4. **Distribute the voltage uniformly** along its length so that no localized electrical stress exceeds the dielectric strength of the medium.

### 2.2 Classification of Bushings

**A. Based on Dielectric Medium**
- **Solid / bulk type** — porcelain or cast-resin body, used for low and medium voltages (< 36 kV).
- **Oil-impregnated paper (OIP)** — kraft paper dried and impregnated with mineral oil, used up to 800 kV.
- **Resin-bonded paper (RBP)** — paper bonded with phenolic resin, used up to 245 kV.
- **Resin-impregnated paper (RIP)** — epoxy-impregnated paper, dry-type, used in SF₆ substations.
- **SF₆ gas bushings** — used at the air-to-gas interface in GIS substations.

**B. Based on Voltage Grading Mechanism**
- **Plain (ungraded) bushings** — uniform dielectric, suitable only for low voltages.
- **Capacitance-graded (condenser) bushings** — concentric conducting foils inserted to force uniform voltage distribution.

**C. Based on Application**
- **Transformer bushings** (oil-to-air).
- **Wall bushings** (air-to-air or air-to-oil).
- **SF₆-to-air bushings** for GIS terminations.
- **Cable termination bushings** for HV cable entries.

### 2.3 The Core Engineering Problem — Non-Uniform Voltage Distribution

In a *plain* bushing (no internal grading), the equivalent electrical network consists of:
- A **series capacitance $C_{s}$** along the dielectric body.
- A **shunt capacitance $C_{g}$** between the surface and ground (especially pronounced at the grounded flange end).

Because $C_{g} \neq 0$, the bushing behaves like a long, lossy RC transmission line. The voltage does *not* drop linearly along the length — instead, it concentrates sharply near the **HV end** (line end), causing dangerously high tangential stress that may initiate surface flashover.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept:** Without capacitance grading, approximately 70–80% of the total voltage can drop across just 20% of the bushing length near the line end. This is why all bushings rated above ~36 kV are *always* of the condenser type.

### 2.4 The Solution — Capacitance-Graded Condenser Bushing

The condenser bushing embeds **$n$ concentric conducting foil layers** (aluminum foils) within the dielectric, each layer connected to the central conductor through carefully designed series **inter-layer capacitors $C_{1}, C_{2}, \dots, C_{n}$**.

By forcing each series capacitor to be much larger than the corresponding shunt capacitance to ground, the voltage divides **uniformly** across each layer, and the surface becomes an **equipotential** at every point. This converts the bushing into a chain of $n$ equal capacitors in series, each carrying voltage $V/n$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r} L}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)}$ | Capacitance of a coaxial cylindrical layer | Inter-layer capacitance |
| 2 | $C_{k} = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r} \cdot L / n}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{k+1}}{r_{k}}\right)}$ | Capacitance of the $k^{th}$ layer in an $n$-layer condenser | Equal-voltage design |
| 3 | $E_{max} = \dfrac{V}{r_{1} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)}$ | Maximum electric field at the inner conductor | Stress verification |
| 4 | $\dfrac{V}{n} = \dfrac{1}{C_{k}} \sum_{j=1}^{k} Q_{j}$ | Voltage drop across the $k^{th}$ layer | Equal-voltage condition |
| 5 | $\dfrac{C_{k}}{C_{g,k}} \geq 10$ | Design rule for grading effectiveness | Engineering rule of thumb |
| 6 | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{g}}{C_{s}}}$ | Voltage attenuation constant (plain bushing) | Plain-bushing analysis |
| 7 | $\dfrac{V(x)}{V} = \dfrac{\sinh(\lambda x)}{\sinh(\lambda L)}$ | Voltage distribution (one end grounded) | Plain bushing |
| 8 | $L_{c} = n \times s$ | Total creepage distance (number of sheds $\times$ spacing) | Outdoor pollution design |
| 9 | $\tan\delta = \dfrac{I_{r}}{I_{c}}$ | Loss tangent of the bushing dielectric | Condition monitoring |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas use **SI units**: capacitance in Farads (F), length in metres (m), voltage in Volts (V), field in V/m.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EHV Transformers (400 kV, 765 kV)** — OIP condenser bushings are the *only* practical means of bringing the HV winding out of the tank.
- **GIS Substations** — SF₆ bushings terminate gas-insulated lines and provide the air-side interface.
- **Wall Bushing in Switchyards** — Carries conductors through building walls, decoupling structural and electrical functions.
- **HVDC Converter Stations** — Special DC bushings (oil-filled, with very low leakage current) handle the converter transformer terminations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capacitance of a Coaxial Cylindrical Layer

Consider a cylindrical dielectric shell of inner radius $r_{1}$, outer radius $r_{2}$, and length $L$, filled with material of permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}$.

**Step 1.** Assume the central conductor carries charge $+Q$ per unit length. By Gauss's law applied to a cylindrical surface of radius $r$:

$$D(r) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi r}$$

**Step 2.** The electric field in the dielectric:

$$E(r) = \dfrac{D(r)}{\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}} = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r} \cdot r}$$

**Step 3.** The voltage between inner and outer cylinders:

$$V = \int_{r_{1}}^{r_{2}} E(r)\, dr = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)$$

**Step 4.** The capacitance per unit length:

$$C' = \dfrac{Q}{V} = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)}$$

**Step 5.** Total capacitance of a layer of length $L$:

$$\boxed{\,C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r} L}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)}\,}$$

This is the **fundamental capacitance formula** that governs every inter-layer foil in a condenser bushing.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equal-Voltage Design of an $n$-Layer Condenser Bushing

Let the bushing have $n$ dielectric layers separated by $(n-1)$ concentric floating foils, with the central conductor acting as foil 0 and the outer earthed foil as foil $n$.

**Step 1.** Let $r_{0}$ be the radius of the central conductor, $r_{1}, r_{2}, \dots, r_{n-1}$ the radii of the floating foils, and $r_{n}$ the outer (flange) radius.

**Step 2.** The capacitance of the $k^{th}$ layer (between foil $k-1$ and foil $k$) of length $L/n$:

$$C_{k} = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r} \cdot (L/n)}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{k}}{r_{k-1}}\right)}$$

**Step 3.** The charge stored on every series capacitor is the same, $Q$, because they are in series. So the voltage across the $k^{th}$ layer is:

$$V_{k} = \dfrac{Q}{C_{k}}$$

**Step 4.** For an **equal-voltage** condenser bushing, we require:

$$V_{1} = V_{2} = \dots = V_{n} = \dfrac{V}{n}$$

This forces:

$$C_{1} = C_{2} = \dots = C_{n} = C$$

**Step 5.** Equating the capacitances:

$$\dfrac{1}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{0}}\right)} = \dfrac{1}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)} = \dots = \dfrac{1}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{n}}{r_{n-1}}\right)}$$

Therefore:

$$r_{k} = r_{0}\left(\dfrac{r_{n}}{r_{0}}\right)^{k/n} \quad \text{for } k = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

The foil radii must follow a **geometric progression**, with ratio:

$$\alpha = \left(\dfrac{r_{n}}{r_{0}}\right)^{1/n}$$

**Step 6.** The maximum field occurs at the conductor surface ($r = r_{0}$):

$$\boxed{\,E_{max} = \dfrac{V}{n \cdot r_{0} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{0}}\right)} = \dfrac{V}{n \cdot r_{0} \cdot \dfrac{1}{n}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{n}}{r_{0}}\right)} = \dfrac{V}{r_{0}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{n}}{r_{0}}\right)}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Surprising result:** $E_{max}$ in a properly graded $n$-layer bushing is the *same* as in a single-dielectric cylinder of the same total dimensions. However, the *axial* (tangential) field at the surface is now uniform, eliminating the line-end stress concentration.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Voltage Distribution in a Plain Bushing

Model the plain bushing as a distributed parameter network:
- Series capacitance per unit length $C_{s}$ (between adjacent elements along the axis).
- Shunt capacitance per unit length $C_{g}$ (between element and ground).

**Step 1.** Apply Kirchhoff's current law to an infinitesimal element of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the HV end:

$$i(x) - i(x+dx) = C_{g}\, dx \,\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t}$$

**Step 2.** The series voltage drop:

$$V(x) - V(x+dx) = \dfrac{1}{C_{s}}\, dx \,\dfrac{\partial q}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3.** Combining and differentiating with respect to $x$:

$$\dfrac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = \dfrac{C_{g}}{C_{s}} \, V = \lambda^{2} V$$

where $\lambda = \sqrt{C_{g}/C_{s}}$.

**Step 4.** Solve the ODE with boundary conditions:
- $V(0) = V$ at the line end (HV terminal).
- $V(L) = 0$ at the grounded flange.

General solution:

$$V(x) = A\sinh(\lambda x) + B\cosh(\lambda x)$$

Applying $V(L) = 0 \Rightarrow A\sinh(\lambda L) + B\cosh(\lambda L) = 0$, so $B = -A\tanh(\lambda L)$.

Applying $V(0) = V \Rightarrow B = V$.

Therefore:

$$A = \dfrac{V}{-\tanh(\lambda L)}$$

**Step 5.** Final closed-form expression for voltage at distance $x$ from HV end:

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{V(x)}{V} = \dfrac{\sinh\!\left[\lambda(L-x)\right]}{\sinh(\lambda L)}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** When $\lambda L \gg 1$ (long bushing with significant shunt capacitance), $\sinh(\lambda L) \approx \tfrac{1}{2}e^{\lambda L}$, and the voltage collapses near the line end — confirming the "80/20 problem" mentioned earlier. Condenser grading effectively drives $\lambda \to 0$ for the *graded* length.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Creepage Distance and Pollution Performance

Outdoor bushings must withstand rain, dew, salt spray, and industrial pollution. Conducting pollution films form leakage currents, which dry out near the line end and form dry bands — these initiate arcs that may grow into a complete flashover.

The empirical design relationship (per IEC 60815):

$$\boxed{\,L_{c} = k \cdot U_{m}\,}$$

where $U_{m}$ is the maximum system voltage (line-to-ground, in kV) and $k$ is the **specific creepage distance** (mm/kV):

| Pollution Level | $k$ (mm/kV) |
|---|---|
| Light | **16** |
| Medium | **20** |
| Heavy | **25** |
| Very heavy | **31** |
| Extreme (desert/coastal) | **≥ 40** |

The creepage distance is geometrically realized by adding **$m$ weather sheds** (skirts) of spacing $s$:

$$L_{c} = m \cdot s$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Plain vs Condenser Bushing

| Parameter | Plain Bushing | Condenser (Graded) Bushing |
|---|---|---|
| Voltage distribution | Non-uniform (exponential) | Uniform (linear by design) |
| Max tangential stress | At line end only | Uniformly low |
| Internal foils | Absent | $(n-1)$ concentric foils |
| Voltage per layer | Variable | $V/n$ (constant) |
| Complexity | Low | High (precision foil placement) |
| Maximum usable voltage | ≈ 36 kV | Up to **1200 kV** |
| Power frequency withstand | Limited | Very high |
| Cost | Low | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Condenser Bushing

The following Mermaid block-diagram captures the multi-layered functional architecture of a capacitance-graded bushing, showing how each foil layer, dielectric layer, and shunt path interact electrically.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[HV Central Conductor] --> nodeB[Foil Layer 0 - Line Foil]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Dielectric Layer 1 - OIP Paper]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Foil Layer 1 - Floating Foil 1]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Dielectric Layer 2 - OIP Paper]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Foil Layer 2 - Floating Foil 2]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Dielectric Layer n - OIP Paper]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Foil Layer n - Earth Foil]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Flange - Grounded Tank Wall]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Porcelain / Composite Weather Shed]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Air Side - Open Terminal]
    nodeA -. shunt to ground via Cg .-> nodeI
    nodeD -. inter-layer capacitance C1 .-> nodeA
    nodeF -. inter-layer capacitance C2 .-> nodeA
    style nodeA fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeI fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeJ fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Equivalent Circuit of an $n$-Layer Condenser Bushing

The block diagram below shows the *equivalent electrical network* of a graded bushing, with each series inter-layer capacitance $C_{1}, C_{2}, \dots, C_{n}$ and the corresponding shunt capacitances $C_{g1}, C_{g2}, \dots, C_{gn}$ to ground.

```mermaid
graph LR
    HV[HV Terminal V] --> C1[Series Capacitor C1]
    C1 --> F1[Foil Node 1]
    F1 --> C2[Series Capacitor C2]
    C2 --> F2[Foil Node 2]
    F2 --> Cn[Series Capacitor Cn]
    Cn --> GND[Grounded Flange 0 V]
    F1 -. shunt Cg1 .-> GND
    F2 -. shunt Cg2 .-> GND
    HV -. shunt Cg0 .-> GND
    style HV fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style GND fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow — Bushing Voltage Stress Management

```mermaid
graph TD
    sA[High Voltage Applied] --> sB[Surface Voltage Tends to Concentrate at Line End]
    sB --> sC{Plain or Condenser?}
    sC -->|Plain| sD[Exponential Voltage Drop]
    sD --> sE[Tangential Stress > Dielectric Strength]
    sE --> sF[Surface Flashover Initiates]
    sC -->|Condenser| sG[Inter-layer Capacitors Equalize Voltage]
    sG --> sH[Linear Voltage Distribution]
    sH --> sI[Uniform Tangential Stress]
    sI --> sJ[Safe Operation - No Flashover]
    style sF fill:#ffdddd,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
    style sJ fill:#ddffdd,stroke:#090,stroke-width:2px
```
