## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B, Q2(a)]**
*Define a bushing. List the essential functions of a high-voltage bushing used in a transformer.*

**Model Answer:**

A bushing is an insulating device that allows a high-voltage conductor to pass safely through a grounded metallic barrier (such as a transformer tank wall), providing controlled electrical insulation, mechanical support, and environmental sealing.

The essential functions are:
1. **Electrical insulation** — to isolate the HV conductor from the grounded tank.
2. **Mechanical support** — to bear the conductor's weight and short-circuit electromagnetic forces.
3. **Controlled surface leakage path** — long creepage distance prevents external flashover.
4. **Sealing** — prevents ingress of moisture, oil, and contaminants.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the **controlled potential grading** function. Always state that the bushing *controls the voltage distribution* — this is the third keyword the examiner is listening for, worth 1 mark on its own.

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A, Q2(b)]**
*Why are capacitance-graded bushings preferred over plain bushings for voltages above 36 kV?*

**Model Answer:**

In a plain bushing, the voltage distribution is non-uniform due to the shunt capacitance between the bushing surface and ground. Most of the voltage drops near the line end, producing a very high tangential electrical stress that initiates surface flashover well below the rated voltage.

In a **capacitance-graded (condenser) bushing**, concentric floating conducting foils are inserted inside the dielectric. These foils, together with carefully sized inter-layer capacitors, force each layer to carry an equal fraction $V/n$ of the total voltage. The surface becomes nearly equipotential, the tangential stress becomes uniform, and the bushing can be made compact even for 400 kV, 765 kV, or 1200 kV systems.

Therefore, for any voltage above ~36 kV, only condenser bushings are practically usable.

---

### Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Provided — Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Set A, Q4(a) Module 4, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **capacitance-graded condenser bushing**. Derive the condition for equal voltage distribution across each dielectric layer of an $n$-layer condenser bushing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 kV, single-layer oil-impregnated paper bushing has an inner conductor radius of 25 mm and an outer flange radius of 200 mm. The dielectric has $\varepsilon_{r} = 4.0$. The bushing length is 2.5 m.
- (i) Calculate the capacitance of the bushing. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Calculate the maximum electric field at the conductor surface. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) If the bushing is replaced by an 8-layer condenser bushing of the same overall dimensions, calculate the radius of each successive foil. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Construction:** A condenser bushing consists of:
- A central HV conductor (usually copper or aluminum).
- Multiple layers of oil-impregnated kraft paper wound on the conductor.
- Between paper layers, concentric aluminum foils are inserted at predetermined radii.
- The outermost foil is bonded to the metallic flange which is grounded.
- The lower portion (oil-end) is immersed in the transformer oil; the upper portion (air-end) is enclosed in a porcelain or composite weather-shed housing.

**Working principle:** The foils and the conductor form a series of capacitors $C_{1}, C_{2}, \dots, C_{n}$. Because the bushing is otherwise a parallel-plate/coaxial structure with significant shunt capacitance to ground, inserting the foils makes each series capacitance $C_{k}$ much larger than the shunt capacitance to ground at that point. As a result, the voltage divides equally across each layer:

$$V_{k} = \dfrac{V}{n}$$

The surface leakage path becomes nearly equipotential, eliminating flashover.

**Derivation of equal-voltage condition:**

Let $r_{0}$ = conductor radius, $r_{k}$ = radius of the $k^{th}$ foil, $r_{n}$ = outer (flange) radius.

Each layer has length $L/n$. The capacitance of the $k^{th}$ layer:

$$C_{k} = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r} (L/n)}{\ln(r_{k}/r_{k-1})}$$

Because the layers are in series, charge is constant: $Q_{k} = Q$ for all $k$. Hence:

$$V_{k} = \dfrac{Q}{C_{k}} = \dfrac{Q \cdot \ln(r_{k}/r_{k-1})}{2\pi \varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r} (L/n)}$$

For equal voltages $V_{1} = V_{2} = \dots = V_{n} = V/n$, we need:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{0}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right) = \dots = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{n}}{r_{n-1}}\right) = \dfrac{1}{n}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{n}}{r_{0}}\right)$$

Therefore the foil radii form a geometric progression:

$$\boxed{\,r_{k} = r_{0}\,\alpha^{k}\quad \text{where} \quad \alpha = \left(\dfrac{r_{n}}{r_{0}}\right)^{1/n}\,}$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $V = 220\text{ kV} = 220 \times 10^{3}\text{ V}$
- $r_{1} = 25\text{ mm} = 0.025\text{ m}$ (conductor)
- $r_{2} = 200\text{ mm} = 0.200\text{ m}$ (outer flange)
- $\varepsilon_{r} = 4.0$
- $L = 2.5\text{ m}$
- $n = 8$ for part (iii)

**(i) Capacitance:**

$$C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r} L}{\ln(r_{2}/r_{1})}$$

$$C = \dfrac{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4.0 \times 2.5}{\ln(0.200/0.025)}$$

$$\ln(0.200/0.025) = \ln(8) = 2.0794$$

$$C = \dfrac{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4.0 \times 2.5}{2.0794}$$

$$C = \dfrac{5.564 \times 10^{-10}}{2.0794} = 2.676 \times 10^{-10}\text{ F}$$

$$\boxed{\,C \approx 267.6\text{ pF}\,}$$

**(ii) Maximum electric field at the conductor surface:**

$$E_{max} = \dfrac{V}{r_{1} \ln(r_{2}/r_{1})} = \dfrac{220 \times 10^{3}}{0.025 \times 2.0794}$$

$$E_{max} = \dfrac{220{,}000}{0.051985} = 4.232 \times 10^{6}\text{ V/m}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{max} \approx 4.23\text{ MV/m (or kV/mm)}\,}$$

**(iii) Radii of successive foils for an 8-layer bushing:**

$$\alpha = \left(\dfrac{r_{n}}{r_{0}}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\dfrac{200}{25}\right)^{1/8} = 8^{1/8}$$

$$\alpha = e^{\ln(8)/8} = e^{2.0794/8} = e^{0.2599} = 1.2968$$

Calculating each $r_{k}$:

| $k$ | $r_{k} = 25 \times \alpha^{k}$ (mm) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 25.00 |
| 1 | 32.42 |
| 2 | 42.04 |
| 3 | 54.52 |
| 4 | 70.69 |
| 5 | 91.66 |
| 6 | 118.86 |
| 7 | 154.13 |
| 8 | 200.00 |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Set B, Q5(a) Module 4, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a schematic diagram, explain the **equivalent circuit of a plain bushing** as a distributed parameter network. Derive the expression for the voltage distribution along the bushing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The series capacitance per unit length of a 1.5 m long plain bushing is $C_{s} = 100\text{ pF/m}$, and the shunt capacitance per unit length to ground is $C_{g} = 1.0\text{ pF/m}$. If a voltage of 132 kV is applied at the line end, calculate:
- (i) The attenuation constant $\lambda$. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The voltage at a point 0.3 m from the line end. **(3 marks)**
- (iii) Comment on whether the bushing will flashover if the surface dielectric strength is 4 kV/cm. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Equivalent Circuit:** The plain bushing can be modelled as a distributed RC network:
- $C_{s}$ per unit length represents the capacitance along the bushing axis (between adjacent infinitesimal elements).
- $C_{g}$ per unit length represents the capacitance from each element to the grounded tank.

Applying Kirchhoff's current and voltage laws to an element of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the line end:

$$\dfrac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = \dfrac{C_{g}}{C_{s}} V = \lambda^{2} V$$

General solution:

$$V(x) = A\sinh(\lambda x) + B\cosh(\lambda x)$$

Boundary conditions: $V(0) = V_{0}$ (HV terminal) and $V(L) = 0$ (grounded flange).

Applying these:

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{V(x)}{V_{0}} = \dfrac{\sinh\!\left[\lambda(L-x)\right]}{\sinh(\lambda L)}\,}$$

This shows that the voltage drops non-linearly along the bushing, with the steepest gradient near the line end.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $L = 1.5\text{ m}$
- $C_{s} = 100\text{ pF/m} = 100 \times 10^{-12}\text{ F/m}$
- $C_{g} = 1.0\text{ pF/m} = 1.0 \times 10^{-12}\text{ F/m}$
- $V_{0} = 132\text{ kV}$
- $x = 0.3\text{ m}$ from line end

**(i) Attenuation constant:**

$$\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{g}}{C_{s}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-12}}{100 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{0.01} = 0.1\text{ m}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Voltage at $x = 0.3$ m:**

Compute $\lambda L$ and $\lambda(L-x)$:

$$\lambda L = 0.1 \times 1.5 = 0.15$$

$$\lambda(L-x) = 0.1 \times (1.5 - 0.3) = 0.1 \times 1.2 = 0.12$$

Evaluating:

$$\sinh(0.15) = \dfrac{e^{0.15} - e^{-0.15}}{2} = \dfrac{1.1618 - 0.8607}{2} = 0.1506$$

$$\sinh(0.12) = \dfrac{e^{0.12} - e^{-0.12}}{2} = \dfrac{1.1275 - 0.8869}{2} = 0.1203$$

$$V(0.3) = V_{0} \times \dfrac{\sinh(0.12)}{\sinh(0.15)} = 132 \times \dfrac{0.1203}{0.1506}$$

$$V(0.3) = 132 \times 0.7988 \approx 105.4\text{ kV}$$

So in just 0.3 m (20% of the bushing length), the voltage has dropped by ~26.6 kV, meaning the average gradient in this region is:

$$\dfrac{dV}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0.3} \approx \dfrac{V_{0} \lambda \cosh[\lambda(L-x)]}{\sinh(\lambda L)} = \dfrac{132{,}000 \times 0.1 \times \cosh(0.12)}{0.1506}$$

$$\cosh(0.12) \approx 1.0072$$

$$\dfrac{dV}{dx} \approx \dfrac{132{,}000 \times 0.1 \times 1.0072}{0.1506} \approx 88{,}266\text{ V/m} = 0.883\text{ kV/cm}$$

**(iii) Flashover comment:**

At $x = 0.3$ m, the gradient is 0.883 kV/cm, which is well below the dielectric strength of 4 kV/cm, so the bushing will *not* flashover at this point.

However, the gradient is **maximum at the line end** ($x = 0$):

$$\dfrac{dV}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = \dfrac{V_{0} \lambda \cosh(\lambda L)}{\sinh(\lambda L)} = \dfrac{132{,}000 \times 0.1 \times \cosh(0.15)}{\sinh(0.15)}$$

$$\cosh(0.15) \approx 1.0113$$

$$\dfrac{dV}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} \approx \dfrac{132{,}000 \times 0.1 \times 1.0113}{0.1506} \approx 88{,}640\text{ V/m} = 0.886\text{ kV/cm}$$

Even at the line end, the gradient (0.886 kV/cm) is below 4 kV/cm. Hence, for *this specific bushing*, flashover does not occur.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students calculate the *average* gradient ($V/L$) and compare it to dielectric strength. This is *wrong* — the gradient is highly non-uniform. Always compute the **maximum gradient** at $x = 0$ (line end) and compare *that* to the dielectric strength. Loss of 1–2 marks is common here.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bushing definition:** Insulating device for safely passing a HV conductor through a grounded barrier.
- **Three functions:** Insulation, mechanical support, controlled leakage path.
- **Plain bushing limitation:** Non-uniform exponential voltage distribution → flashover at the line end → usable only below ~36 kV.
- **Condenser bushing solution:** Insert $(n-1)$ concentric foils so each layer carries $V/n$.
- **Equal-voltage condition:** $C_{1} = C_{2} = \dots = C_{n}$, which geometrically forces the foil radii to follow a geometric progression with common ratio $\alpha = (r_{n}/r_{0})^{1/n}$.
- **Coaxial capacitance formula:** $C = 2\pi\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}L / \ln(r_{2}/r_{1})$.
- **Maximum field:** $E_{max} = V / [r_{0} \ln(r_{n}/r_{0})]$, occurs at the central conductor.
- **Attenuation constant (plain):** $\lambda = \sqrt{C_{g}/C_{s}}$; voltage profile is $\sinh[\lambda(L-x)] / \sinh(\lambda L)$.
- **Creepage distance:** $L_{c} = k \cdot U_{m}$, where $k$ ranges from 16 mm/kV (clean) to ≥ 40 mm/kV (extreme pollution).
- **Types by dielectric:** Solid, OIP, RBP, RIP, SF₆.
- **Typical usage:** OIP up to 800 kV transformer bushings; RIP for GIS dry-type; SF₆ bushings at GIS air interfaces.
- **Tests performed:** Power-frequency withstand, lightning impulse, switching impulse, $\tan\delta$ measurement, partial discharge measurement, thermal stability, oil-leakage test.
- **KTU favourite trap:** When asked about voltage distribution, students must distinguish *plain* (non-uniform, exponential) from *condenser* (uniform by design, linear in $V/n$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Isolator** (also called a **disconnector** or **disconnecting switch**) is a **mechanical switching device** used in high-voltage (HV) and extra-high-voltage (EHV) substations that provides a **visible, verifiable, and assured physical gap** in the electrical circuit when opened. It is operated *only* under **no-load** or *negligibly small* current conditions to isolate a section of the network, equipment, or busbar for safe maintenance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> An isolator is a switchgear device designed to open a circuit under **no-current conditions**. It is not intended to break load current or short-circuit current. Its primary function is to provide **galvanic isolation** and a **visible break** between the source and the disconnected section, thereby guaranteeing the safety of personnel and equipment during maintenance.

### Key Identifying Properties of an Isolator

| Property | Specification |
| :--- | :--- |
| Operating State | **No-load** only (after circuit breaker is open) |
| Break Type | **Visible** physical air gap |
| Current Interruption | **Zero** making/breaking capacity |
| Position Indication | Mechanical, often with **interlocks** |
| Standard Reference | **IS 1818 / IEC 62271-102** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Domestic Water-Tank Valve**
> Think of the **main water-supply valve** at the inlet of your house. When a plumber needs to repair a leaking pipe inside the house, you first turn off the valve, then you open a nearby tap to **vent the residual pressure** (this venting is what a circuit breaker does in a substation), and only then does the plumber safely cut the pipe. The valve itself is **not designed to stop a high-pressure jet** — you would never try to stop a flowing stream with it. The isolator plays exactly this role in a substation: it gives a **physical, visible guarantee** that no voltage can reach the work area.

A second useful analogy is the **drawbridge** of a fortification:
- The bridge is raised (isolator opened) **only after** the gates are sealed and soldiers have cleared (circuit breaker has opened the current).
- It provides a *visible, mechanical proof* that nothing can pass through.
- The bridge cannot be raised while troops are crossing (isolator cannot open under load).

---

## 1.3 Why Isolators Are Necessary in a Substation

A circuit breaker, though it extinguishes the arc and stops the current, leaves the busbar/line on the source side **still energized at full system voltage**. If a technician opens the breaker for maintenance and accidentally recloses it (or a back-feed occurs), the result is fatal. The isolator provides a **second, physically verifiable, and mechanically lockable** layer of safety.

### Functional Hierarchy in a Substation Bay

1. **Upstream Circuit Breaker** → Interrupts load/fault current
2. **Isolator (Line Side)** → Provides visible isolation from the line
3. **Earthing Switch** → Connects the isolated section to earth
4. **Maintenance Zone** → Now safe for human access

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook — "I-B-E" Sequence:**
> **I**solator opens → **B**reaker already OFF → **E**arthing switch closes. Reverse this order during energization: Close Earthing Switch (open) → Close Isolator → Close Breaker.

---

## 1.4 Basic Operating Principle

When the isolator's contacts are **closed**, current flows through a set of silver-plated copper or aluminium jaws that grip a moving blade. The contacts are housed on **porcelain / composite post insulators** which provide the necessary creepage distance and dielectric strength. When actuated (manually by a handle, or motor-operated), the blade rotates about a pivot, physically separating from the fixed contact, thereby creating an **air gap** whose dielectric strength must withstand the **impulse voltage level** of the system plus a safety margin.

The minimum required air gap length is governed by the **rod-rod / rod-plane breakdown voltage** of air, typically:

$$d_{min} \approx \frac{V_{BIL}}{500 \text{ kV/m}}$$

where **V_BIL** is the **Basic Insulation Level** in kV peak.

---

## 1.5 Standard Voltage Ratings Encountered (KTU Context)

For Indian/Kerala transmission systems, isolators are commonly designed for:

| System Voltage (rms) | Impulse Withstand (1.2/50 µs) | Typical Isolator Class |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 11 kV | 75 kV peak | Light-duty, single-break |
| 33 kV | 170 kV peak | Double-break |
| 110 kV | 450 kV peak | Double-break, post type |
| 220 kV | 950 kV peak | Double-break, rotating pantograph |
| 400 kV | 1550 kV peak | Pantograph type, motorised |
| 765 kV | 2400 kV peak | Semi-pantograph, heavy-duty |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never assume the isolator can break load current.** Most isolator failures in KTU-region substations have historically been traced to operators opening them under transformer magnetizing inrush or line-charging current, which causes **arcs of 10–20 m** that can re-strike and trigger three-phase short circuits.

---

## 1.6 Section-1 Quick Recap

- An isolator is a **no-load, mechanical, visible-break** switchgear device.
- It does **not interrupt current**; the **circuit breaker** must open first.
- It provides **personnel safety** through a **lockable, verifiable open gap**.
- It operates under the strict sequence: **Breaker OFF → Isolator OPEN → Earthing Switch CLOSE**.
- It must withstand the system's full **BIL** and **switching impulse** levels.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Constructional Anatomy

A typical HV isolator consists of the following sub-assemblies, each engineered to perform a precise function:

### 2.1.1 The Base Frame Assembly
- A **galvanised steel** channel frame, hot-dip zinc coated (≈ **85 µm** thickness) to resist Kerala's coastal saline corrosion.
- Houses the **main rotating shaft**, **operating mechanism linkage**, and the **auxiliary switch** cam assembly.
- Designed to be **earthed** through two distinct bolted earth-pads (one for the base, one for the rotating frame).

### 2.1.2 The Post Insulator Stack
- **Material**: Brown-glazed **porcelain** (alumina-rich C-130 grade) or modern **silicone-rubber composite** with fibre-glass core.
- **Creepage Distance**: Determined by pollution level. For Kerala's coastal sites (Very Heavy pollution, **Class IV**), the standard is **31 mm/kV** of system voltage (phase-to-phase).
- The insulators carry both the **mechanical cantilever load** of the moving blade and the **dielectric stress**.

### 2.1.3 The Contact System (the heart of the isolator)
Three sub-contacts exist in parallel to optimise current carrying and arc resistance:

| Contact Type | Function | Material |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Main Current Contact** | Carries **rated normal current** continuously | Silver-plated copper |
| **Arcing Contact** | Takes the brief arc during opening/closing | Copper-tungsten (Cu-W) alloy |
| **Finger / Jaw Contact** | Provides the clamping force and low contact resistance | Phosphor-bronze with silver inlay |

### 2.1.4 The Operating Mechanism
- **Manual type**: Bevel-geared handle requiring **2–3 rotations** of the handle for full 90° blade rotation.
- **Motor-operated type**: 3-phase squirrel-cage motor (typically **0.37 kW** or **0.55 kW**) with limit switches and torque-limiters.
- Operating time: **8–12 seconds** for full close-open cycle.

---

## 2.2 Why and How of Each Functional Step

### Step 1 — The Closing Operation
> The motor (or human effort) drives the main shaft through a **scotch-yoke** or **crank-lever** linkage. The blade rotates, lifts, and enters the **fixed-contact jaw**. Spring-loaded fingers inside the jaw deflect and create a uniform contact pressure of approximately **30–60 N per finger**, ensuring contact resistance below **30 µΩ**.

### Step 2 — Current Carrying
> The full load current passes through the silver-plated contacts. **I²R losses** must be kept below **0.005% of rated VA** to prevent local hot-spots that would anneal the silver and accelerate wear.

### Step 3 — The Opening Operation
> The mechanism rotates the blade out of the jaw. The **arcing contact** parts first (because it is slightly longer), bearing the brief micro-arc. The arc is a **cold-cathode arc** in air, extinguished within a few cycles because the **striking distance d** is engineered such that:

$$V_{system} < V_{breakdown}(d)$$

> As the blade continues to travel, d increases, the available voltage becomes incapable of re-striking the arc, and the current is finally interrupted (or rather, the arc is **stretched to extinction**).

### Step 4 — Lock-out and Indication
> A mechanical **padlock provision** plus an **auxiliary switch (a-contact / b-contact)** feeds back the position to the SCADA. Interlocking prevents:
- Opening the isolator while the breaker is closed (**mechanical interlock**)
- Closing the earthing switch while the isolator is closed (**key interlock**)

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** a KTU student must memorise for problems on isolators, contact design, and insulator coordination.

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $R_c = \dfrac{\rho}{2 a} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi H}{P}}$ | $R_c$ = contact resistance (Ω); $\rho$ = resistivity (Ω·m); $a$ = contact radius (m); $H$ = hardness (Pa); $P$ = contact force (N) | Contact design — derived from **Holm's formula** |
| 2 | $I_{sc} = I_{th} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{t_{th}}{t_{sc}}}$ | $I_{sc}$ = short-time current (kA); $I_{th}$ = thermal current (kA); $t$ = duration (s) | Rating of short-time current carrying capacity |
| 3 | $d_{min} = \dfrac{k \cdot V_{system}}{\ln\left(\dfrac{A}{B}\right)}$ | $d_{min}$ = minimum open-gap (m); $V_{system}$ (kV); $k, A, B$ = empirical air-breakdown constants | Gap design for visible break |
| 4 | $L_{creepage} = k_{pol} \cdot V_{LL}$ | $L$ in mm; $V_{LL}$ = line-to-line voltage (kV); $k_{pol}$ = pollution factor (mm/kV) | Post-insulator creepage length |
| 5 | $V_{BIL} = 1.45 \cdot V_{peak} + 10\%$ margin | $V_{BIL}$ = Basic Insulation Level (kV peak) | Withstand requirement |
| 6 | $\eta_{string} = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{n \cdot V_{disc}} \times 100\%$ | $\eta$ = string efficiency (%); $n$ = number of discs; $V_{disc}$ = disc rating | Insulator string effectiveness |
| 7 | $T_{mech} = F_{wind} \cdot h_{blade} + M_{ice} \cdot g$ | $T$ = torque on insulator (N·m); $F_{wind}$ (N); $h$ = height (m); $M_{ice}$ (kg) | Mechanical design of post |
| 8 | $E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{line} V^2$ | $E$ in J; $C$ in F; $V$ in V | Stored line energy to be discharged by earthing switch |
| 9 | $t_{oper} = \dfrac{J \cdot \omega}{\eta_m \cdot T_m - T_L}$ | $t$ in s; $J$ = moment of inertia (kg·m²); $\omega$ = angular vel. (rad/s); $\eta_m$ = motor efficiency; $T_m, T_L$ = motor & load torques (N·m) | Motor-driven operation time |
| 10 | $V_{rod-rod} \approx 5.0 \cdot d \text{ (kV, rms, uniform field)}$ | $d$ in cm | Air-gap withstand voltage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In the markdown tables above, the absolute-value and dividing bars have been written as `\vert` or `\mid` (or written out as fractions) to **protect the markdown table** from breaking. KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for ambiguous notation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Isolators are indispensable in:

- **EHV Transmission Substations** (110 kV – 1200 kV) — bay isolation.
- **Power Transformers** — primary and secondary side isolation.
- **Reactor & Capacitor Banks** — series/parallel switching.
- **Bus Coupler Bays** — sectionalising double-bus systems.
- **Generator Circuit Breaker (GCB) bays** — generator-transformer unit isolation.
- **Railway Traction** (25 kV OHE) — sectioning for maintenance.

In **Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB)** substations, the most common isolator types encountered are:
- **110 kV Double-Break Centre-Rotating** at substations like Areacode, Kalamassery.
- **220 kV Pantograph-type** at Madakkathanam, Pallipuram.
- **400 kV Semi-Pantograph** at the Trivandrum and Kochi HVDC-adjacent stations.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Open-Gap Distance of an Isolator

**Problem Context:** Design the minimum air-gap between the moving blade and the fixed contact of an isolator rated for 220 kV, 50 Hz, with a Basic Insulation Level (BIL) of 950 kV peak.

### Given Data
- System voltage: $V_{LL} = 220 \text{ kV (rms)}$
- Frequency: $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$
- BIL (peak impulse): $V_{BIL} = 950 \text{ kV peak}$
- Atmospheric conditions: Standard (T = 20 °C, p = 101.3 kPa)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Convert system rms voltage to peak phase-to-neutral voltage.

$$V_{peak,phase} = \frac{V_{LL} \cdot \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$V_{peak,phase} = \frac{220 \cdot \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the numerator and denominator.

$$V_{peak,phase} = \frac{220 \times 1.4142}{1.7321}$$

$$V_{peak,phase} = \frac{311.13}{1.7321}$$

**Step 4:** Final numerical result for peak phase voltage.

$$V_{peak,phase} \approx 179.63 \text{ kV peak}$$

**Step 5:** Apply a safety factor of **1.25** (standard for isolator gap design) to account for pollution, humidity (Kerala's monsoon reaches **95% RH**), and rod-rod non-uniformity.

$$V_{design} = 1.25 \cdot V_{BIL}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $V_{BIL} = 950 \text{ kV}$.

$$V_{design} = 1.25 \times 950 \text{ kV peak}$$

**Step 7:** Numerical result.

$$V_{design} = 1187.5 \text{ kV peak}$$

**Step 8:** Use the **uniform-field air-breakdown** approximation: $V_{breakdown} \approx 5.0 \text{ kV (rms) per cm}$ for air at standard conditions (becomes ≈ **3.5 kV (rms)/cm** for a rod-rod geometry due to field non-uniformity).

$$d_{min} = \frac{V_{design,rms}}{E_{breakdown,rod-rod}}$$

**Step 9:** Convert $V_{design}$ to rms by dividing by $\sqrt{2}$.

$$V_{design,rms} = \frac{1187.5}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1187.5}{1.4142}$$

$$V_{design,rms} \approx 839.7 \text{ kV (rms)}$$

**Step 10:** Substitute to get the gap length.

$$d_{min} = \frac{839.7 \text{ kV}}{3.5 \text{ kV/cm}}$$

**Step 11:** Final gap.

$$\boxed{d_{min} \approx 240 \text{ cm} = 2.40 \text{ m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** For a real 220 kV isolator, the *open* gap is typically **1.4 – 1.8 m**. The extra dielectric strength is provided by the **post insulators** at the contact terminations, which act as barrier electrodes and reduce the field non-uniformity considerably.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Contact Resistance of the Main Blade (Holm's Formula)

**Problem Context:** Calculate the contact resistance of a silver-plated copper blade-jaw contact pair in a 110 kV isolator, where the contact is a hemisphere-on-flat geometry.

### Given Data
- Resistivity of silver: $\rho = 1.59 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega \cdot m$
- Vickers hardness of silver: $H \approx 3.0 \times 10^{8} \, N/m^2$
- Contact radius: $a = 5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \, m$
- Contact force per finger: $P = 50 \, N$
- Number of fingers in parallel: $N_f = 4$

### Derivation

**Step 1:** State Holm's equation for spherical-on-flat contact.

$$R_c = \frac{\rho}{2 a} \sqrt{\frac{\pi H}{P}}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the term $\dfrac{\pi H}{P}$.

$$\frac{\pi H}{P} = \frac{3.1416 \times 3.0 \times 10^{8}}{50}$$

$$\frac{\pi H}{P} = \frac{9.4248 \times 10^{8}}{50}$$

$$\frac{\pi H}{P} = 1.885 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the square root.

$$\sqrt{1.885 \times 10^{7}} = 4.342 \times 10^{3}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into $R_c$.

$$R_c = \frac{1.59 \times 10^{-8}}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} \times 4.342 \times 10^{3}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the denominator $2a$.

$$2a = 2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.0 \times 10^{-2} \, m$$

**Step 6:** Divide $\rho$ by $2a$.

$$\frac{\rho}{2a} = \frac{1.59 \times 10^{-8}}{1.0 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.59 \times 10^{-6} \, \Omega$$

**Step 7:** Multiply.

$$R_c = 1.59 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.342 \times 10^{3}$$

$$R_c = 6.904 \times 10^{-3} \, \Omega$$

**Step 8:** This is resistance of **one contact spot**. With **4 fingers in parallel**:

$$R_{c,total} = \frac{R_c}{N_f} = \frac{6.904 \times 10^{-3}}{4}$$

**Step 9:** Final result.

$$\boxed{R_{c,total} \approx 1.726 \times 10^{-3} \, \Omega \approx 1.73 \, m\Omega}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A 1.73 mΩ contact resistance for a 110 kV / 2000 A isolator produces $I^2 R = (2000)^2 \times 1.73 \times 10^{-3} \approx 6.92 \text{ kW}$ of heat. This is dissipated through the contact body and is acceptable; however, if contact force drops below **30 N** due to wear, $R_c$ rises sharply and may cause **thermal runaway**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Short-Time Current Rating of the Earthing Switch

**Problem Context:** A 220 kV isolator's integral earthing switch is rated for a thermal current of $I_{th} = 31.5 \text{ kA}$ for 3 seconds. Find the equivalent short-time current for a fault duration of 0.5 seconds (typical for modern high-speed breakers).

### Derivation

**Step 1:** State the heating equivalence (adiabatic assumption).

$$I_{th}^2 \cdot t_{th} = I_{sc}^2 \cdot t_{sc}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $I_{sc}$.

$$I_{sc} = I_{th} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{t_{th}}{t_{sc}}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values.

$$I_{sc} = 31.5 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{3.0}{0.5}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the ratio.

$$\frac{3.0}{0.5} = 6.0$$

**Step 5:** Take the square root.

$$\sqrt{6.0} = 2.449$$

**Step 6:** Multiply.

$$I_{sc} = 31.5 \times 2.449$$

**Step 7:** Final result.

$$\boxed{I_{sc} \approx 77.2 \text{ kA}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This shows the **dynamic short-time rating can be 2.45× the 3-second rating**. This is why isolator manufacturers publish a **time-current characteristic curve**; the rating is *not* a single number but a *function of fault clearance time*.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Number of Discs in a Suspension Insulator String Used as an Isolator's Support

**Problem Context:** A 110 kV transmission line is to be sectionalised using a double-break isolator mounted on a 110 kV disc-insulator string. Each disc has a wet flashover voltage of 35 kV rms, and string efficiency is **80%**. How many discs are needed?

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Determine the maximum phase-to-earth voltage (rms).

$$V_{phase} = \frac{V_{LL}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{110}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the numerical value.

$$V_{phase} = \frac{110}{1.7321} \approx 63.51 \text{ kV}$$

**Step 3:** Write the string efficiency formula.

$$\eta = \frac{V_{actual}}{n \cdot V_{disc}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $n$.

$$n = \frac{V_{actual}}{\eta \cdot V_{disc}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5:** Substitute values.

$$n = \frac{63.51}{0.80 \times 35}$$

**Step 6:** Compute denominator.

$$0.80 \times 35 = 28.0$$

**Step 7:** Divide.

$$n = \frac{63.51}{28.0}$$

**Step 8:** Final result.

$$\boxed{n \approx 2.27 \rightarrow \text{round up to } 3 \text{ discs}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Installation:** A standard 110 kV string in KSEB uses **7 discs** — the extra discs are for *mechanical strength* (cantilever load of the isolator blade) and *safety margin* for pollution, not just electrical withstand. **Always oversize mechanically before oversizing electrically.**

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-offs in Isolator Selection

| Design Parameter | Increase Consequence | Decrease Consequence | Typical KTU/Industry Choice |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Open gap length | Higher BIL, larger substation footprint | Compact, but risk of re-strike | **1.4 m for 220 kV** |
| Number of post insulators | More pollution margin, higher cost | Cheaper, but flashover risk in monsoon | **3 stacks for 220 kV** |
| Contact material hardness | Wear-resistant, but higher $R_c$ | Lower $R_c$, but short life | **Silver-plated Cu** |
| Operating motor torque | Faster operation, mechanical stress | Slower, smoother | **0.55 kW, 12 s** |
| Earthing switch rating | Handles higher fault levels | Cost, weight | **40 kA / 1 s** |

## 4.4 Sequence-of-Operation Block Diagram (Closing Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Idle Isolator Open] --> S1[Operator Command: Close]
    S1 --> S2{Is Breaker Open?}
    S2 -- No --> S2a[Command Rejected Interlock]
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Energise Motor]
    S3 --> S4[Rotate Main Shaft 90 deg]
    S4 --> S5[Blade Enters Fixed Jaw]
    S5 --> S6[Arcing Contact Engages First]
    S6 --> S7[Main Current Contact Engages]
    S7 --> S8[Limit Switch LSC Trips Motor]
    S8 --> S9[Position: CLOSED and Locked]
    S9 --> S10[SCADA Updated: a contact Closed]
```

> [!TIP]
> The **limit switch (LSC)** at step S8 is the single most important safety device in the motor circuit. It is a *positive-feedback* device that *physically* cuts motor power when the closed position is reached, regardless of any SCADA command. **Always test the LSC during maintenance** — KTU examiners love to ask about it.

## 4.5 Insulation Coordination Block — Why the Isolator Needs a BIL Rating

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lightning Strike on Line] --> B[Travelling Wave Vpeak]
    B --> C[Line Surge Impedance Z = 400 to 500 ohm]
    C --> D[Wave reaches Substation]
    D --> E[Isolator Open Position]
    E --> F[Reflection Coefficient plus 1]
    F --> G[Doubled Voltage at Open Contact]
    G --> H[BIL of Isolator must exceed 2x Vpeak]
```
