# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
*Define an isolator. Why is it called a "no-load" switching device, and how is it different from a circuit breaker?*

#### Model Answer (3 marks)
- **Definition (1 mark):** An isolator is a mechanical switchgear device that provides a *visible and verifiable* open gap in a circuit for the purpose of *isolating* a section of the system from the source during maintenance.
- **No-load operation (1 mark):** It is designed to be operated *only* when the current through it is essentially zero. Opening an isolator under load causes an unsupported arc, which can re-strike, leading to phase-to-phase or phase-to-earth faults.
- **Difference from circuit breaker (1 mark):** A circuit breaker is capable of *making*, *carrying*, and *breaking* load as well as short-circuit currents, with arc-quenching media (oil, SF₆, vacuum). An isolator has *no arc-quenching* provision and *no making/breaking capacity*; it merely isolates an already dead circuit.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*List any three types of HV isolators and state the typical voltage range for which each is used.*

#### Model Answer (3 marks)
1. **Single-break isolator (1 mark):** Used up to **33 kV**. Has one contact pair that opens to give the full gap in one motion.
2. **Double-break (centre-rotating) isolator (1 mark):** Used from **66 kV to 245 kV**. Two contact pairs open in series, halving the gap per break and improving reliability.
3. **Pantograph-type isolator (1 mark):** Used from **245 kV to 550 kV**. The moving blade is a scissor-type pantograph that drops vertically onto a fixed upper contact; saves horizontal space.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **double-break centre-rotating isolator** for 110 kV applications. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 220 kV substation uses an isolator with a BIL of 950 kV peak. Design the **minimum open-air gap** required between the contacts, using rod-rod air breakdown data. Discuss the role of the **post insulators** in enhancing the dielectric strength of the gap. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks)

**Construction (4 marks):**
A double-break centre-rotating isolator consists of the following key sub-assemblies:

1. **Base Frame (0.5 mark):** Galvanised steel channel frame bolted to the substation gantry. Houses the rotating shaft, auxiliary switch, and operating-mechanism linkages. Two earthing pads (distinctly marked) are provided.

2. **Centre Post Insulator (0.5 mark):** A single rotating post insulator of brown-glazed porcelain (or silicone composite) is mounted centrally. It carries the rotating blade and provides the drive-shaft mechanical support while insulating the HV side from earth.

3. **Fixed Contact Assemblies (0.5 mark each, 1 mark total):** Two identical assemblies are mounted on the two outer post insulators. Each contains:
   - A silver-plated copper **jaw contact** with spring-loaded fingers.
   - An **arcing contact** (copper-tungsten) that parts last during opening.
   - A **terminal pad** for connecting the HV conductor.

4. **Moving Blade (1 mark):** A high-conductivity copper (or aluminium) blade rigidly mounted on the central insulator. The blade has *T-shaped* ends that engage the two fixed jaws. As the central insulator rotates by 90°, both ends of the blade disengage simultaneously, creating **two gaps in series**.

5. **Operating Mechanism (1 mark):** A motor (or manual handle) drives the shaft through a worm-gear reducer, providing a 90° rotation in 8–12 seconds. Limit switches, auxiliary contacts, and mechanical interlocks ensure safe operation and SCADA feedback.

**Working (3 marks):**

- **Closing:** Motor energised → worm gear rotates the central insulator → blade rotates 90° → both ends enter the fixed jaws → arcing contacts engage first → main contacts engage → limit switch LSC stops the motor.
- **Current Carrying:** Load current flows from the line conductor → left fixed jaw → left blade-end → through the blade → right blade-end → right fixed jaw → outgoing line. Path resistance is the sum of two contact resistances.
- **Opening:** Motor reversed (or manual handle turned) → blade rotates out of jaws → arcing contacts part first (any micro-arc is sustained on the Cu-W tips) → main contacts part → blade clears both jaws → fully isolated → limit switch LSO stops motor.

**Why Double-Break (1 mark, integration):** The 90° rotation creates **two gaps in series**, each of half the system peak voltage. This allows a *physically shorter* blade travel and *lower* drive torque, while the dual gap improves reliability — even if one gap fails, the other still isolates.

### Part (b) — Minimum Open-Air Gap Design (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Phase-to-earth peak voltage (1 mark):**

$$V_{peak,phase} = \frac{V_{LL} \cdot \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{220 \times 1.4142}{1.7321} \approx 179.63 \text{ kV peak}$$

**Step 2 — Design voltage with safety factor (1.25) (1 mark):**

$$V_{design} = 1.25 \times V_{BIL} = 1.25 \times 950 = 1187.5 \text{ kV peak}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to rms (1 mark):**

$$V_{design,rms} = \frac{1187.5}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 839.7 \text{ kV (rms)}$$

**Step 4 — Use rod-rod air breakdown strength (1 mark):**

$$E_{breakdown,rod-rod} \approx 3.5 \text{ kV(rms)/cm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the gap (1 mark):**

$$d_{min} = \frac{839.7}{3.5} \approx 240 \text{ cm} = 2.40 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Practical design value (1 mark):**
In a real 220 kV double-break isolator, each break is designed for ≈ **1.4 m** (so the total open distance is ≈ 2.8 m). The post insulators act as **barrier electrodes** that grade the field and prevent the high non-uniformity typical of rod-rod geometry.

**Step 7 — Role of post insulators (1 mark):**
The brown-glazed porcelain posts at the contact terminations:
- Provide a **creepage path of length** $L_{creepage} = k_{pol} \cdot V_{LL}$ = 31 mm/kV × 220 kV = **6820 mm = 6.82 m** for Very Heavy pollution.
- Act as **field-grading barriers** that reduce the local field at the contact tip.
- Mechanically support the cantilever load of the blade and terminal pad.
- Resist **UV, rain, salt-fog** (typical of Kerala's coastal substations).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**(a)** Explain the **classification of HV isolators** based on contact action, with suitable sketches. Compare **double-break** and **pantograph** types in terms of substation footprint, contact reliability, and cost. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **contact resistance** of a hemispherical contact using Holm's formula. Given $\rho = 1.7 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega \cdot m$, $H = 3.5 \times 10^8 \, N/m^2$, $a = 4 \text{ mm}$, and $P = 40 \text{ N}$, calculate the contact resistance. Also discuss the effect of *oxidation* and *contact force* on the resistance. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Classification and Comparison (7 marks)

**Classification (4 marks):**
| Type | Description | Voltage Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Single-break** | Blade pivots at one end, opens one gap | ≤ 33 kV |
| **Double-break (centre-rotating)** | Centre blade creates two gaps in series | 66 – 245 kV |
| **Pantograph** | Scissor-type blade drops vertically onto fixed upper contact | 245 – 550 kV |
| **Semi-pantograph** | Hybrid with horizontal reach and vertical drop | 420 – 800 kV |
| **Knee-type** | Blade folds in a knee action for compact GIS bays | 145 – 245 kV GIS |

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**
| Parameter | Double-Break | Pantograph |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Substation Footprint** | Wider (needs horizontal space) | Compact (vertical drop) |
| **Contact Reliability** | Two parallel contact paths (redundancy) | Single contact, but high contact force |
| **Cost** | Moderate (rotating insulator is expensive) | High (precision pantograph mechanism) |
| **Maintenance** | Easy (single rotating shaft) | Complex (many scissor joints) |
| **EHV Suitability** | Excellent up to 245 kV | Standard for ≥ 400 kV |
| **Ice / Wind Loading** | Better mechanical balance | Sensitive to asymmetric ice |

### Part (b) — Holm's Contact Resistance Derivation (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Consider a hemispherical contact spot of radius $a$ pressed against a flat surface with force $P$. The lines of current flow constrict through the contact, leading to a "spreading resistance" given by:

**Step 1:** The constriction area is the small circular region of radius $a$.

**Step 2:** The current spreads radially outward in a hemispherical pattern, so the area at radius $r$ is $2\pi r^2$.

**Step 3:** The resistance of a thin hemispherical shell of thickness $dr$ at radius $r$ is:

$$dR = \frac{\rho \, dr}{2\pi r^2}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate from $r = a$ to $r = \infty$:

$$R_c = \int_{a}^{\infty} \frac{\rho \, dr}{2\pi r^2} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi a}$$

**Step 5:** Now relate $a$ to the contact force $P$ using the hardness $H$:

$$P = H \cdot \pi a^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \sqrt{\frac{P}{\pi H}}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back:

$$R_c = \frac{\rho}{2\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\pi H}{P}} = \frac{\rho}{2 a} \sqrt{\frac{\pi H}{P}}$$

This is **Holm's classical contact resistance formula** (sometimes called the "spreading resistance" formula).

**Numerical Calculation (1.5 marks):**

**Step 1:** $R_c = \dfrac{\rho}{2 a} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi H}{P}}$

**Step 2:** $\dfrac{\pi H}{P} = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 3.5 \times 10^8}{40} = \dfrac{1.0996 \times 10^9}{40} = 2.749 \times 10^7$

**Step 3:** $\sqrt{2.749 \times 10^7} = 5.243 \times 10^3$

**Step 4:** $\dfrac{\rho}{2a} = \dfrac{1.7 \times 10^{-8}}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{1.7 \times 10^{-8}}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.125 \times 10^{-6} \, \Omega$

**Step 5:** $R_c = 2.125 \times 10^{-6} \times 5.243 \times 10^3$

$$\boxed{R_c \approx 1.114 \times 10^{-2} \, \Omega \approx 11.14 \text{ m}\Omega}$$

**Effect of Oxidation and Contact Force (1.5 marks):**

- **Oxidation Effect (0.75 mark):** Silver oxide (Ag₂O) and copper oxide (Cu₂O) are *semiconductors*, not insulators. They increase $\rho$ in the contact region, raising $R_c$ by 2× to 10× over time. This is why isolator contacts are **silver-plated** — silver oxide is still reasonably conductive.
- **Contact Force Effect (0.75 mark):** $R_c \propto 1/\sqrt{P}$. Halving the contact force *increases* $R_c$ by $\sqrt{2} \approx 41\%$. Mechanical wear that reduces spring tension is therefore a *critical* failure mode — the spring must be inspected every 1000 operations (typical KSEB maintenance schedule).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Drawing the sketch but not labelling the parts (–1 mark):** A neat labelled sketch is worth at least 1.5 marks. Always label: *base frame, post insulators, fixed jaw, arcing contact, main blade, operating mechanism, earthing switch, HV terminal.*
> 2. **Confusing isolator with circuit breaker (–2 marks):** A common error is writing "isolator breaks load current". This is *fatal* for your score. Always state: *"operated only under no-load, after the breaker is open."*
> 3. **Forgetting the safety factor (–0.5 mark):** When designing the open gap, always apply the 1.25× safety factor.
> 4. **Mixing rms and peak (–1 mark):** $V_{BIL}$ is in **kV peak**, $V_{LL}$ in **kV rms**. Convert explicitly.
> 5. **Skipping the unit check (–0.5 mark):** Always show units. $1.73 \, m\Omega$ is acceptable; $1.73 \times 10^{-3}$ without units loses the mark.
> 6. **Using wrong Holm constant (–0.5 mark):** $\sqrt{\pi H / P}$ has the units of $m^{-1}$. Forgetting $H$ in Pa and $P$ in N gives a dimensional error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [x] An **isolator** is a *no-load, visible-break* mechanical switch.
- [x] It **cannot** break load or fault current — the *circuit breaker must open first*.
- [x] Operating sequence: **Breaker OFF → Isolator OPEN → Earthing Switch ON** (for maintenance).
- [x] Reverse sequence for energising: **Earthing OFF → Isolator CLOSE → Breaker CLOSE**.
- [x] Types: **Single-break (≤33 kV), Double-break (66–245 kV), Pantograph (245–550 kV), Semi-pantograph (420–800 kV)**.
- [x] Three parallel contacts: **main (Ag-plated Cu), arcing (Cu-W), fingers (phosphor-bronze)**.
- [x] Post insulators use **porcelain (C-130) or silicone composite**, creepage = 31 mm/kV for coastal Kerala.
- [x] **Holm's formula:** $R_c = \dfrac{\rho}{2a}\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi H}{P}}$.
- [x] **Open gap design:** $d_{min} = \dfrac{1.25 \times V_{BIL,peak}}{\sqrt{2} \cdot E_{br,rod-rod}}$.
- [x] **Thermal equivalence:** $I_{sc} = I_{th}\sqrt{t_{th}/t_{sc}}$ — gives the dynamic short-time rating.
- [x] **String efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{n \cdot V_{disc}} \times 100\%$.
- [x] **Mechanical interlocks** prevent: opening isolator under load, closing earthing switch on live bus.
- [x] **Auxiliary contacts** (a-contact for close, b-contact for open) feed SCADA.
- [x] **Limit switch LSC** physically cuts motor power at the closed position — *test every maintenance*.
- [x] **Operating time** of motor-driven isolator: typically **8 to 12 seconds**.
- [x] **Standard reference:** IS 1818 / IEC 62271-102.
- [x] **Motor rating:** typically **0.37 kW or 0.55 kW**, 3-phase squirrel cage.
- [x] **Earthing switch** must withstand full **short-time current** (e.g., 40 kA for 1 s) and *make* onto a faulted line.
- [x] **Failure mode #1 in KSEB history:** opening under line-charging current → restrike → 3-phase fault.
- [x] **Failure mode #2:** contact wear → reduced spring force → $R_c$ rises → thermal runaway.
- [x] **Always oversize mechanically before oversizing electrically** in KTU design problems.
- [x] **Common KTU exam trap:** "Why two gaps in series?" — Answer: *voltage division* and *redundancy*.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute 60-Second Recall (KTU Exam Hall Mnemonic):**
> **"I S C A M E"**
> **I** — Isolator: no-load, visible break.
> **S** — Sequence: Breaker OFF first, then Isolator.
> **C** — Contacts: Main + Arcing + Fingers.
> **A** — Air gap: $d_{min} = V_{design,rms} / E_{br,rod-rod}$.
> **M** — Motor: 0.55 kW, 12 s, with LSC limit switch.
> **E** — Earthing switch: short-time rating 40 kA / 1 s.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circuit Breakers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Circuit Breakers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Circuit Breaker?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **Circuit Breaker (CB)** is an automatically operated electrical switch designed to protect an electrical circuit from damage caused by overcurrent/overload or short-circuit conditions. Unlike a fuse, which operates once and must be replaced, a circuit breaker can be reset (either manually or automatically) to resume normal operation. In High Voltage Engineering, the CB must forcefully **interrupt fault currents (typically 10 kA – 80 kA)** at transmission voltages (11 kV – 765 kV), extinguishing the resulting **power arc** within milliseconds.

---

## 1.2 The Fundamental Problem: The Stubborn Arc 🤯

When two current-carrying contacts of a breaker are pulled apart under high voltage, the air (or oil/SF₆) between them gets **ionised**. Free electrons and ions form a conducting plasma channel — the dreaded **arc**. 

If this arc is not extinguished, the system cannot be considered "open" and the fault persists, leading to catastrophic equipment damage, fires, and grid collapse.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Job of a High Voltage Circuit Breaker:**
> 1. **Detect** the fault (via relays).
> 2. **Separate** the contacts.
> 3. **Extinguish** the resulting arc as the current passes through natural zero.
> 4. **Withstand** the transient Recovery Voltage without the arc restriking.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Firefighter Hose 💧

Imagine a high-pressure water hose (the current) spraying through a small gap. If you suddenly clamp the hose shut, water pressure builds up dangerously and a spray bursts through anyway — analogous to **arc restrike**.

- **Without resistance switching:** A violent burst (high RRRV, arc reignites).
- **With a "flow restrictor" (arc resistance / SF₆ gas / vacuum):** The flow is choked gradually, allowing the system pressure (recovery voltage) to rise gently without bursting the seal.

---

## 1.4 Essential Terminology (KTU Board Favourites) 🎯

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Arc Voltage ($V_{arc}$)** | Voltage drop across the arc column during interruption. |
| **Restriking Voltage ($v_r$)** | The high-frequency transient voltage that appears across the breaker contacts immediately after current zero. |
| **Recovery Voltage ($v_{re}$)** | The power-frequency RMS voltage that appears across the contacts after the transient oscillations die out. |
| **RRRV** | Rate of Rise of Restriking Voltage (kV/µs) — measures severity. |
| **Current Chopping** | Forced interruption of current before natural zero (a problem in vacuum/SF₆ breakers). |
| **Arc Length** | Physical stretch of the ionised plasma column. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 mandates the study of *arc interruption theories*, *RRRV*, *current chopping*, *resistance switching*, and *testing of circuit breakers*. All are covered in this sheet.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Arc Interruption (Step-by-Step)

When breaker contacts separate under load:

1. **Contact separation:** The contact area shrinks, current density skyrockets → intense heat → **metal vapour arc**.
2. **Arc elongation:** The arc is stretched (either physically by moving contacts or magnetically by the **arc chute / gas blast**).
3. **Cooling & De-ionisation:** Cold SF₆ / oil / air absorbs energy, reducing ionisation.
4. **Current zero (CZ):** Every AC wave passes through zero 100 times/second (50 Hz). At CZ, the energy input to the arc momentarily vanishes.
5. **Race against time:** The arc has two competing parameters:
   - **Dielectric Strength (DBS)** — rate at which the gap insulation recovers.
   - **Restriking Voltage** — rate at which system voltage tries to re-ignite the gap.
6. **Success condition:** If **DBS > Restriking Voltage at every instant** post-CZ, the arc is extinguished permanently. Otherwise, it restrikes.

---

## 2.2 Arc Interruption Theories — The "Big Three" Theorems

### 🔵 Theory 1: Slepian's Theory (Energy Balance)
- **Hypothesis:** Interruption is governed by **energy** in the arc column.
- **Logic:** If the rate of energy removal (by cooling) > rate of energy input (from the circuit), the arc extinguishes.
- **Best for:** Resistive (low inductance) circuits.

### 🟢 Theory 2: Cassie's Theory (Dynamic Equilibrium)
- **Assumption:** Arc is a **variable resistance** with constant temperature.
- **Equation Basis:** The arc resistance adjusts instantaneously to circuit conditions.
- **Interrupt Condition:** The source can no longer sustain the arc voltage when the arc area becomes too small at current zero.
- **Best for:** **High-current** interruption (short-circuit faults).

### 🟠 Theory 3: Mayr's Theory (Thermal Re-ignition)
- **Assumption:** Arc is a **variable resistance** with **constant power loss per unit length**.
- **Equation Basis:** Resistance is an exponential function of energy.
- **Interrupt Condition:** Post-CZ, if the **dielectric strength recovers faster than the restriking voltage** builds up, the arc is quenched thermally.
- **Best for:** **Low-current** interruption (small inductive loads).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📜

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** Use `\vert` instead of `|` in all formulas below to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_r(t) = V_m \left[ 1 - e^{-\alpha t} \left( \cos\beta t + \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}\sin\beta t \right) \right]$ | Restriking voltage transient (general solution of LC double-frequency circuit) | Volts |
| 2 | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | Natural frequency of the LC transient | rad/s |
| 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{R}{2L}$ | Damping coefficient (R, L, C = breaker parameters) | 1/s |
| 4 | $\beta = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$ | Damped natural frequency | rad/s |
| 5 | $\text{RRRV} = \dfrac{d v_r}{dt}\bigg\vert_{t=0} = V_m \cdot \omega_0^2 \cdot \dfrac{L}{C}$ | Rate of Rise of Restriking Voltage | V/s |
| 6 | $\text{RRRV}_{max} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{LC}} = V_m \cdot \omega_0$ | Peak RRRV (when R = 0, undamped) | V/s |
| 7 | $\text{Active Recovery Voltage} = V_m \sin\phi$ | Component of recovery voltage driving arc | Volts |
| 8 | $P_{loss} = k \cdot \dfrac{d(g)}{dt}$ | Mayr's thermal equation (g = arc conductance) | Watts |
| 9 | $E_{arc} \cdot v_{arc} = P_{input}$ | Cassie's energy equilibrium | Watts |
| 10 | $t_{arc} = \dfrac{\text{Arc length}}{\text{velocity of separation}}$ | Arc duration approximation | seconds |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SF₆ Circuit Breakers:** Used in **GIS (Gas-Insulated Switchgear)** for 220 kV–1200 kV. SF₆ has 100× the dielectric strength of air and excellent arc-quenching properties.
- **Vacuum Interrupters:** Preferred for **11 kV–33 kV** distribution and **traction** (Indian Railways) because of zero maintenance, no gas handling, and 20,000+ mechanical operations.
- **Air Blast Breakers:** Legacy systems for arc furnace switching and EHV testing labs (still used in KTU laboratories for synthetic testing).
- **Testing Stations (CPRI, India):** KTU graduates often work in CB testing — short-circuit test duties like **Duties 1–5** are critical benchmarks per **IEC 62271-100**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** Modern "**DC Circuit Breakers**" for HVDC (±800 kV lines like Raigarh–Pugalur) use hybrid mechanical-solid-state interruption — a hot research area aligned with renewable grid integration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: RRRV of a Pure Capacitive / Inductive Circuit

Consider a simplified breaker connected to a fault: an **L–C–R** lumped-parameter network where `L` = source inductance, `C` = stray capacitance across the breaker, and `R` = arc resistance (assumed small).

**Step 1 — Write the KVL (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law) around the loop:**

$$v_r(t) + L\dfrac{di}{dt} + R \cdot i = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

**Step 2 — Relate current to voltage via capacitor:**

$$i = C \dfrac{d v_r}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into KVL:**

$$L \cdot C \cdot \dfrac{d^2 v_r}{dt^2} + R \cdot C \cdot \dfrac{d v_r}{dt} + v_r = 0$$

**Step 4 — Identify the characteristic equation:**

$$\text{Let } \omega_0^2 = \dfrac{1}{LC}, \quad 2\alpha = \dfrac{R}{L}$$

Then:

$$\dfrac{d^2 v_r}{dt^2} + 2\alpha \dfrac{d v_r}{dt} + \omega_0^2 v_r = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve the 2nd-order ODE (for the under-damped case, typical of EHV systems):**

$$v_r(t) = e^{-\alpha t} \left( A \cos\beta t + B \sin\beta t \right)$$

**Step 6 — Apply boundary conditions:**
- At $t = 0$: $v_r(0) = 0 \Rightarrow A = 0$
- Differentiate and apply $\dfrac{dv_r}{dt}\bigg\vert_{t=0} = \dfrac{i(0)}{C} = \dfrac{I_0}{C}$:

$$B = \dfrac{I_0}{\beta C}$$

**Step 7 — Final form (using $I_0 = V_m \omega_0^2 / \omega_0$ etc.):**

$$v_r(t) = V_m \left[ 1 - e^{-\alpha t} \left( \cos\beta t + \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}\sin\beta t \right) \right]$$

**Step 8 — Differentiate to find RRRV:**

$$\dfrac{dv_r}{dt} = V_m e^{-\alpha t} \left( \alpha^2 + \beta^2 \right) \sin\beta t \cdot \dfrac{1}{\beta}$$

At $t = 0$, $\sin\beta t = \beta t$ (small-angle):

$$\text{RRRV} = V_m \cdot \left( \alpha^2 + \beta^2 \right) = V_m \cdot \omega_0^2$$

**Step 9 — Substitute $\omega_0^2 = 1/LC$:**

$$\boxed{\text{RRRV} = \dfrac{V_m}{LC}}$$

This is the **single most important formula in CB design** — it tells engineers that adding even 1 µH of stray inductance or 1 nF of stray capacitance *drastically* spikes the RRRV.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mayr's Equation (Thermal Re-ignition Criterion)

**Step 1 — Energy balance per unit arc length:**

$$P_{input} - P_{loss} = \dfrac{dW}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Mayr's postulate:** Loss is proportional to arc conductance $g$:

$$P_{loss} = K \cdot g \quad \text{and} \quad P_{input} = v_{arc}^2 \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Arc conductance dynamics:**

$$\dfrac{1}{g} \dfrac{dg}{dt} = \dfrac{P_{input} - P_{loss}}{Q_0} = \dfrac{v_{arc}^2 \cdot g - K \cdot g}{Q_0 \cdot g}$$

**Step 4 — Mayr's Differential Equation:**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{g} \dfrac{dg}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{\theta} \left( \dfrac{v_{arc}^2}{P_{loss}} - 1 \right)}$$

where $\theta = Q_0 / P_{loss}$ is the **arc time constant** (typically 1–10 µs).

**Step 5 — Interpretation for KTU exams:**

| Condition | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\dfrac{v_{arc}^2}{P_{loss}} > 1$ | Energy input > Loss → Arc **sustained** |
| $\dfrac{v_{arc}^2}{P_{loss}} < 1$ | Loss > Input → Arc **extinguished** |
| $\dfrac{v_{arc}^2}{P_{loss}} = 1$ | Critical **thermal equilibrium** |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Theories

| Parameter | Slepian | Cassie | Mayr |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arc Resistance | Variable | Variable | Variable |
| Arc Temperature | Variable | **Constant** | **Decreasing** |
| Current Range | Any | **High current** | **Low current** |
| Post-CZ Behaviour | Energy decay | Area shrink | Thermal recovery |
| Key Equation | $E_{in} = E_{out}$ | $R_{arc} = f(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{g}\dfrac{dg}{dt} = f(v)$ |
| Use Case | Conceptual | Bulk Oil, SF₆ | Vacuum, Air Blast |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Arc Interruption Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Normal Operation - Contacts Closed] --> B{Fault Detected?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Relay Trips - Trip Command Sent]
    C --> D[Operating Mechanism Releases]
    D --> E[Contacts Begin to Separate]
    E --> F[Arc Initiated - Ionised Plasma]
    F --> G[Arc Elongation via Mechanism/Gas]
    G --> H[Cooling - Energy Removal]
    H --> I{Current Zero Crossing?}
    I -- No --> G
    I -- Yes --> J{Is DBS > Restriking Voltage?}
    J -- No --> K[Arc Restrikes - Loop Back]
    K --> G
    J -- Yes --> L[Arc Successfully Extinguished]
    L --> M[Recovery Voltage Appears]
    M --> N[Breaker Fully Open - Fault Isolated]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Circuit Breaker Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sensing[Sensing and Trip Unit]
        CT[Current Transformer] --> Relay[Protection Relay]
        Relay --> TripCoil[Trip Coil]
    end
    subgraph Operating[Operating Mechanism]
        TripCoil --> Latch[Mechanical Latch]
        Latch --> Spring[Stored Energy Spring]
        Spring --> MovingContact[Moving Contact]
    end
    subgraph Interruption[Arc Interruption Chamber]
        MovingContact --> ArcZone[Arc Plasma Zone]
        ArcZone --> GasBlast[SF6 Blast or Oil Jet]
        ArcZone --> ArcChute[Arc Chute with Splitter Plates]
    end
    subgraph Recovery[Post-Arc Dielectric Recovery]
        ArcChute --> DielectricGap[Dielectric Gap Recovers]
        GasBlast --> DielectricGap
        DielectricGap --> Reclose[Reclose or Lockout Logic]
    end
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Restriking Voltage Transient Waveform

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Current Zero T0] --> B[Oscillatory LC Transient Begins]
    B --> C[Peak Restriking Voltage Vp]
    C --> D[Oscillations Damp Out]
    D --> E[Recovery Voltage Reaches RMS Value]
    E --> F[Steady State Power Frequency]
    style A fill:#ff9999
    style C fill:#ffcc00
    style E fill:#99ccff
    style F fill:#99ff99
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define Restriking Voltage and RRRV. Why is RRRV critical in circuit breaker design?**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Restriking Voltage:** The transient high-frequency voltage that appears across the circuit breaker contacts immediately after the current passes through zero, attempting to reignite the arc.
>
> **RRRV (Rate of Rise of Restriking Voltage):** The rate at which the restriking voltage rises with respect to time, measured in **kV/µs**.
>
> **Importance:** If the RRRV exceeds the rate of recovery of dielectric strength (DBS) of the contact gap, the arc **restrikes**, leading to breaker failure. Hence, lower RRRV = more reliable interruption. Methods to reduce RRRV include **resistance switching** and **shunt capacitors** across the breaker.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is current chopping? In which type of circuit breaker is it most prominent?**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Current Chopping** is the phenomenon where the circuit breaker interrupts the current **abruptly before it reaches its natural zero crossing**, forcing the current to fall to zero instantaneously.
>
> This is most prominent in **Vacuum Circuit Breakers (VCBs)** and **SF₆ breakers** because the dielectric strength of the medium recovers so rapidly that the arc is extinguished prematurely.
>
> **Consequence:** A high $di/dt$ produces a high overvoltage ($\dfrac{L \cdot di}{dt}$) which can damage insulation. It is mitigated using **surge arresters** and **RC snubbers**.

---

## 📕 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **restriking voltage** with **recovery voltage** — remember: restriking is a *transient* (oscillatory, µs timescale), recovery is *steady-state* (power frequency RMS).
> - Always state **assumptions** in derivation questions (e.g., "Assuming R = 0 for worst-case RRRV…").
> - In Mayr vs Cassie questions, mention the **current magnitude range** explicitly.
> - Draw waveforms with **labelled axes** (time vs voltage) — easy 2 marks.
> - Unit conversions (kA, kV, µs) are often checked.

---

### ❓ **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**a)** Derive an expression for the **Rate of Rise of Restriking Voltage (RRRV)** in a circuit breaker connected to a system with stray inductance `L`, stray capacitance `C`, and negligible arc resistance. (7 marks)

**b)** A 132 kV, 50 Hz, three-phase system has a circuit breaker interrupting a short-circuit current of 25 kA (RMS). The natural frequency of the restriking voltage transient is 12 kHz. Calculate:
   1. The peak restriking voltage.
   2. The maximum RRRV.
   3. The time to reach peak restriking voltage. (7 marks)

---

#### 📌 Model Solution

### Part (a) — Derivation of RRRV

**Step 1:** Consider the equivalent circuit of the breaker with `L` (source inductance), `C` (stray capacitance), and `R` (arc resistance, neglected here for worst-case).

**Step 2:** Applying KVL after current zero:

$$L \cdot C \cdot \dfrac{d^2 v_r}{dt^2} + R \cdot C \cdot \dfrac{d v_r}{dt} + v_r = 0$$

**Step 3:** With $R = 0$, this simplifies to an undamped harmonic oscillator:

$$\dfrac{d^2 v_r}{dt^2} + \omega_0^2 v_r = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 4:** The solution is sinusoidal (no damping):

$$v_r(t) = V_m (1 - \cos\omega_0 t)$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate to find RRRV:

$$\text{RRRV} = \dfrac{dv_r}{dt}\bigg\vert_{t=0} = V_m \cdot \omega_0 \cdot \sin\omega_0 t \bigg\vert_{t=0} = V_m \cdot \omega_0$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $\omega_0$:

$$\boxed{\text{RRRV} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{LC}}}$$

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $V_{LL} = 132$ kV (line-to-line)
- $f = 50$ Hz
- $I_{sc} = 25$ kA (RMS)
- $f_n = 12$ kHz (natural frequency)

**Step 1:** Convert to phase voltage (peak):

$$V_m = \dfrac{V_{LL} \cdot \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{132{,}000 \cdot 1.414}{1.732} = 107{,}775 \text{ V} \approx 107.8 \text{ kV}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $\omega_0$:

$$\omega_0 = 2\pi f_n = 2\pi \times 12{,}000 = 75{,}398 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(i) Peak Restriking Voltage:**

$$V_{peak} = 2 V_m = 2 \times 107{,}775 = 215{,}550 \text{ V} \approx 215.5 \text{ kV}$$

**(ii) Maximum RRRV:**

$$\text{RRRV} = V_m \cdot \omega_0 = 107{,}775 \times 75{,}398 = 8.13 \times 10^9 \text{ V/s} = 8.13 \text{ kV/µs}$$

**(iii) Time to Reach Peak:**

$$T/2 = \dfrac{1}{2 f_n} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 12{,}000} = 41.67 \text{ µs}$$

**Final Answers:** $V_{peak} = 215.5$ kV, RRRV = $8.13$ kV/µs, $t_{peak} = 41.67$ µs.

---

### ❓ **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternative]**

**a)** Explain **Cassie's** and **Mayr's theories of arc interruption** with their governing equations. Compare their applicability to high-current and low-current interruption. (7 marks)

**b)** Discuss the **testing of circuit breakers** as per IEC 62271-100. List the various **short-circuit test duties** (Duty 1, 2, 3, 4, 5) and explain the significance of the **short-circuit test plant (Kurzschlussprüfung)**. (7 marks)

---

#### 📌 Model Solution

### Part (a) — Arc Interruption Theories

**Cassie's Theory:**
- Assumes arc is a cylindrical column of **constant temperature**.
- Cross-sectional area varies with current; resistance $R_{arc} \propto 1/A$.
- **Governing equation:**

$$\dfrac{1}{r} \cdot \dfrac{dr}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{\theta_C} \left( \dfrac{v^2}{V_0^2} - 1 \right)$$

where $r$ = arc radius, $V_0$ = constant arc voltage, $\theta_C$ = Cassie time constant.
- **Applies to:** High-current interruption (bulk oil, SF₆ breakers near peak short-circuit currents).

**Mayr's Theory:**
- Assumes arc has **constant power loss per unit length** and decreasing temperature.
- Conductance follows an exponential decay.
- **Governing equation:**

$$\dfrac{1}{g} \cdot \dfrac{dg}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{\theta_M} \left( \dfrac{v^2}{P_{loss}} - 1 \right)$$

where $g$ = arc conductance, $\theta_M$ = Mayr time constant.
- **Applies to:** Low-current interruption (vacuum breakers, air-blast breakers at small inductive currents).

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Cassie | Mayr |
|---|---|---|
| Current range | High (kA) | Low (A) |
| Arc temperature | Constant | Decreasing |
| Energy loss | Convection-dominated | Conduction-dominated |
| Time constant | Large (ms) | Small (µs) |

---

### Part (b) — Testing of Circuit Breakers

**IEC 62271-100 Standard** specifies the following short-circuit test duties:

| Duty | Description | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Duty 1** | O – t – CO – t' – CO (break–make–break sequence) | Verify break–make capability under short-circuit |
| **Duty 2** | O – t – CO | Make–break test for TRV (Transient Recovery Voltage) |
| **Duty 3** | Single Break Test | Rated short-circuit breaking capacity |
| **Duty 4** | Single Make Test | Rated short-circuit making capacity (peak withstand) |
| **Duty 5** | Load Current Switching | Verify capability at normal operating current |

**Significance of Short-Circuit Test Plant (Kurzschlussprüfung):**

- A high-power test lab (e.g., **CPRI Bhopal, KEMA Netherlands, CESI Italy**) that can deliver **hundreds of kA at full EHV voltage** (up to 800 kV, 80 kA) for direct testing.
- Components: **Short-circuit generator** (specially designed 2-pole machine with very low reactance), **make switches**, **master breaker**, **test bay**, and **synthetic testing circuits** (Weil-Dobke for indirect EHV testing).
- For voltages beyond lab capacity, **synthetic testing** is used: actual current from one source + synthetic TRV from a separate voltage source (parallel or series injection).
- **Why it matters:** Real-world faults generate TRVs that breakers must withstand — laboratory testing ensures grid safety. A failure in test means redesign.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Arc interruption is a race** between **Dielectric Strength (DBS)** rising and **Restriking Voltage** rising after current zero. **Winner takes the system.**
- ✅ **RRRV** = $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{LC}}$ for the worst-case undamped case. **Minimise L, maximise C** to tame it.
- ✅ **Cassie** = high current, constant T. **Mayr** = low current, decaying T. **Slepian** = pure energy balance.
- ✅ **Current chopping** is a *problem* in vacuum and SF₆ breakers — causes $L \cdot di/dt$ overvoltages.
- ✅ **Resistance switching** (shunting a resistor across contacts) damps RRRV and limits overvoltages.
- ✅ **IEC 62271-100** defines 5 standard test duties — know at least Duty 1 and Duty 2.
- ✅ **SF₆** is the gold standard for EHV; **Vacuum** for MV distribution; **Air-blast** mostly historical.
- ✅ Always mention **system voltage, frequency, current magnitude, and natural frequency** in numericals.
- ✅ Two constants to memorise: **$\sqrt{2} = 1.414$** and **$\sqrt{3} = 1.732$** for RMS-to-peak and line-to-phase conversions.
- ✅ Modern frontiers: **HVDC breakers** (hybrid mech-electronic), **SF₆-free** alternatives (CO₂, vacuum interrupters at 145 kV).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transformers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **High Voltage Engineering (HVE)**, *Transformers* refer to specialized electromagnetic energy-conversion devices engineered to step-up low-voltage alternating inputs (typically **50 Hz / 60 Hz mains**) to extremely high alternating output voltages (ranging from hundreds of kilovolts to several megavolts) required for **high-voltage testing of insulation systems, components, and equipment**. Unlike conventional power distribution transformers, HV testing transformers are characterized by their **high voltage ratio, low power rating (typically kVA to few MVA), short-time intermittent duty cycle, stringent insulation requirements, and low partial discharge levels**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (Module 4), this topic specifically encompasses:

- **Cascaded Transformers** for very high AC voltage generation
- **Resonant Transformers** (Series and Parallel)
- **Tesla Coil** (Electromagnetic Resonant Transformer)
- **Design Requirements** specific to HV testing transformers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Learning Outcome**: The student must be able to *describe, analyze, and compare* the various transformer configurations used for generating high alternating voltages in HV laboratories, including their equivalent circuits, voltage multiplication principles, and operational limitations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy**: Imagine a *water pump system*. A small, gentle pump (low-voltage mains supply) pushes water into a narrow pipe. The narrow pipe's high velocity is then converted into *pressure* through a narrow nozzle. Similarly, a small electrical "push" (low voltage at high current from the mains) is converted through a transformer into a *large electrical push* (high voltage at low current) — without violating energy conservation. The transformer's job in HV labs is essentially to "compress" voltage into a usable high-voltage test source, much like a nozzle compresses water pressure.

Another helpful analogy: Think of the transformer as a **voltage "lever"** — analogous to a mechanical lever that trades distance for force. The transformer trades current for voltage: a small current at the primary becomes a large voltage at the secondary, while the *product* (VA rating) remains approximately constant.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard ratings are central to HV transformer analysis:

- **Mains frequency**: $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ (India standard) or $f = 60 \text{ Hz}$ (US standard)
- **Permeability of free space**: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$
- **Permittivity of free space**: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
- **Velocity of light**: $c \approx 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
- **Typical HV testing voltages**: $100 \text{ kV}$ to $2000 \text{ kV}$
- **Typical duty cycle**: short-time (5 minutes ON, 15 minutes OFF for repeated testing)
- **Standard short-circuit impedance**: $Z_{sc} = 6\% \text{ to } 12\%$ (for HV testing transformers)
- **Standard no-load current**: $I_0 = 1\% \text{ to } 5\%$ of full-load current

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are HV testing transformers different from power transformers?**
> 1. They operate under **highly capacitive loads** (the test object is often a cable or capacitor).
> 2. They need **extremely low partial discharge (PD)** levels (< 5 pC).
> 3. They have **specially graded insulation** to manage non-uniform voltage distribution.
> 4. They are designed for **intermittent duty** rather than continuous full-load operation.

### 1.4 Classification of HV Transformers (KTU Module 4 Scope)

The KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus groups HV transformers into the following four major categories:

1. **Single-Stage HV Testing Transformer** (up to ~500 kV, single unit)
2. **Cascaded HV Testing Transformers** (for voltages > 500 kV, using 2 or more stages in series)
3. **Series Resonant Transformer** (using LC resonance for sinusoidal, low-current testing)
4. **Tesla Coil** (air-core resonant transformer for very high frequency, very high voltage impulse-like AC)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — Step by Step

#### 2.1.1 The Fundamental Transformer Principle (Electromagnetic Induction)

A transformer operates on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, which states that a time-varying magnetic flux linking a coil induces an EMF proportional to the rate of change of flux and the number of turns:

$$e = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

where:
- $e$ = induced EMF (volts)
- $N$ = number of turns in the coil
- $\phi$ = magnetic flux (webers)

For a sinusoidal flux $\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$, the RMS induced EMF becomes:

$$E = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \phi_m \cdot k_w$$

where:
- $f$ = frequency (Hz)
- $\phi_m$ = maximum flux (Wb)
- $k_w$ = winding factor (≈ 1 for HV transformers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Transformer EMF Equation (4.44 f N φ_m)** is the **single most important equation** for HV transformer design. It links output voltage directly to flux density, frequency, and core cross-section.

#### 2.1.2 Voltage and Turns Ratio

For an ideal transformer:

$$\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = a \quad \text{(turns ratio)}$$

For HV transformers, $a \ll 1$ (since $V_2 \gg V_1$). The HV secondary may have tens of thousands of turns.

#### 2.1.3 The Equivalent Circuit of a HV Testing Transformer

The **T-equivalent circuit** referred to the primary side consists of:

- $R_1$ = primary winding resistance
- $R_2'$ = secondary winding resistance referred to primary
- $X_1$ = primary leakage reactance
- $X_2'$ = secondary leakage reactance referred to primary
- $R_c$ = core (iron) loss component (representing hysteresis + eddy current losses)
- $X_m$ = magnetizing reactance
- $V_1$ = applied primary voltage
- $E_1$ = induced EMF in primary
- $I_0$ = no-load current (vector sum of core-loss current $I_c$ and magnetizing current $I_m$)

**Approximate voltage drop on full load:**

$$\text{Regulation (\%)} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility**: For HV testing, *voltage regulation* under capacitive load is critical. A test object (like a long cable) acts as a capacitor, causing the transformer's output voltage to *rise* above the open-circuit value (Ferranti-like effect). This must be accounted for during testing.

#### 2.1.4 Cascaded HV Testing Transformers

When a single transformer cannot economically or technically produce voltages above ~500 kV (due to transport limits, insulation difficulties, and manufacturing constraints), a **cascade** arrangement is used.

**Working Principle:**
- Two or more transformers are connected such that the **secondary of stage 1 supplies the primary of stage 2** through an *excitation winding* on the stage-1 transformer.
- The **secondaries are in series**, so voltages add up.
- For **n** cascaded stages, each stage is rated for $V/n$, and the final output is **n × V**.

**Voltage distribution in a 3-stage cascade:**

$$V_{output} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 = 3V_{stage}$$

where each stage produces $V_{stage}$ when its primary is supplied at rated LV voltage.

**Excitation winding concept:**
- The LV primary of each stage is called the *excitation winding*.
- Each stage's secondary is connected in series with the next.
- All primaries are supplied from the LV mains, but stage 2 and 3 primaries are fed from auxiliary windings on the previous stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cascade Advantages**: (i) Modular construction, (ii) Each stage is transportable, (iii) Voltage grading is naturally achieved.
> **Cascade Disadvantages**: (i) Lower short-circuit impedance ⇒ harder to limit short-circuit currents, (ii) Higher reactive power demand, (iii) Increased cost.

#### 2.1.5 Resonant Transformers (Series Resonant Circuit)

When testing **highly capacitive loads** (e.g., long cables, large capacitor banks), the load capacitance can be **tuned** with an adjustable inductor to form a **series LC resonant circuit** with the transformer's secondary. At resonance:

$$X_L = X_C \implies \omega L = \frac{1}{\omega C}$$

The resonant frequency becomes:

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

At resonance, the current is purely resistive and limited only by the small circuit resistance. The voltage across the capacitor (or inductor) becomes **Q times** the source voltage, where Q is the quality factor:

$$Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{\omega_r L}{R}$$

**Voltage magnification at resonance:**

$$V_C = Q \cdot V_s \quad \text{(voltage across the test object capacitor)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility**: Series resonant HV test sets are extensively used for **onsite cable testing** and **GIS (Gas Insulated Switchgear) testing** because they require much less input power than a conventional transformer to charge the capacitive load. The transformer only needs to supply the resistive losses.

#### 2.1.6 Tesla Coil — Air-Core Resonant Transformer

The **Tesla coil** is an *air-core* (or partially air-core) double-resonant transformer invented by Nikola Tesla (1891). It consists of:

- **Primary coil** (L₁): few turns of heavy conductor, often a flat spiral
- **Secondary coil** (L₂): thousands of turns of fine wire wound on a tall cylindrical form
- **Primary capacitor** (C₁): high-voltage capacitor (Leyden jar or modern equivalent)
- **Secondary capacitor** (C₂): top-load sphere or toroid
- **Spark gap** (SG): for oscillatory discharge
- **HV step-up transformer**: charges C₁ to breakdown voltage of SG

**Working Principle (in 4 phases):**

1. **Charging phase**: HV transformer charges primary capacitor C₁.
2. **Breakdown phase**: When V across C₁ exceeds SG breakdown voltage, spark gap fires.
3. **Primary oscillation**: C₁ discharges through L₁, producing damped oscillation at $f_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_1 C_1}}$.
4. **Secondary resonance**: The oscillating current in L₁ magnetically couples to L₂. If $f_1 \approx f_2 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_2 C_2}}$, **resonance** builds up the secondary voltage to extremely high values (MV range).

**Output voltage of Tesla Coil:**

$$V_2(t) \approx V_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{L_1}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C_1}{C_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tesla Coil in KTU context**: Although primarily a high-frequency device, the Tesla coil is included in Module 4 because it represents the *extreme limit* of transformer-based HV generation, demonstrating the principle of *resonant voltage multiplication* without iron cores.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This cheat sheet contains the formulas most frequently tested in KTU 2024 ESE Module 4 questions on Transformers.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Application | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = 4.44 f N \phi_m k_w$ | Transformer EMF equation (RMS) — used for HV winding design | V, Hz, Wb |
| 2 | $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = a$ | Turns ratio of ideal transformer | dimensionless |
| 3 | $V_{cascade} = n \cdot V_{stage}$ | Output voltage of n-stage cascaded transformer | V |
| 4 | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Resonant frequency (Series & Parallel) | Hz |
| 5 | $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \dfrac{\omega_r L}{R}$ | Quality factor of resonant circuit | dimensionless |
| 6 | $V_C = Q \cdot V_s$ | Voltage magnification across capacitor at resonance | V |
| 7 | $X_L = \omega L$ | Inductive reactance | Ω |
| 8 | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$ | Capacitive reactance | Ω |
| 9 | $V_2(t) \approx V_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{L_2}{L_1}}\sqrt{\dfrac{C_1}{C_2}}$ | Tesla coil output voltage (approximate) | V |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Transformer efficiency | % |
| 11 | $\% \text{Reg} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$ | Voltage regulation under load | % |
| 12 | $Z_{pu} = \dfrac{Z_{actual}}{Z_{base}}$ | Per-unit impedance (for short-circuit analysis) | p.u. |
| 13 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency | rad/s |
| 14 | $B_m = \dfrac{\phi_m}{A_c}$ | Maximum flux density in core | T (Tesla) |
| 15 | $E_p = \dfrac{V_p}{N_p} = \dfrac{V_s}{N_s}$ | Volts per turn | V/turn |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications of HV Transformers:**
>
> 1. **Cable Manufacturing Industry**: Every XLPE cable reel must pass a *voltage withstand test* before dispatch. Cascaded transformers (typically 100 kV–500 kV) or resonant transformers generate the required test voltage.
> 2. **Power Transformer Testing**: HV bushings, windings, and tap changers are tested using HV testing transformers up to 1000 kV.
> 3. **Research Laboratories (BHEL, CPRI, NABL labs in India)**: Use cascaded transformers for dielectric testing of insulators, surge arresters, and insulators.
> 4. **Medical Equipment (X-ray generators)**: Tesla coil-derived circuits are used to generate high voltages for X-ray tubes.
> 5. **Particle Accelerators (CERN, TIFR)**: Use cascaded and resonant transformer topologies for initial beam acceleration stages.
> 6. **Electrostatic Precipitators**: HV AC or pulsating DC sources based on Tesla coil principles are used for industrial air filtration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Transformer EMF Equation from Faraday's Law

**Starting Point — Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction:**

$$e = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

**Step 1:** Assume sinusoidal alternating flux in the core:

$$\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to time:

$$\frac{d\phi}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[\phi_m \sin(\omega t)\right] = \omega \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back into Faraday's law:

$$e = -N \cdot \omega \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$

The negative sign simply indicates the direction (Lenz's law); we drop it for magnitude calculation.

**Step 4:** Express angular frequency in terms of frequency:

$$\omega = 2\pi f$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$ into the expression:

$$e = N \cdot 2\pi f \cdot \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 6:** The peak (maximum) value of the EMF occurs when $\cos(\omega t) = 1$:

$$E_{max} = 2\pi f N \phi_m$$

**Step 7:** Convert peak value to RMS value (RMS = Peak / √2):

$$E_{rms} = \frac{E_{max}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\pi f N \phi_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 8:** Simplify the constant:

$$\frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\pi}{1.4142} \approx 4.4429$$

**Step 9:** Include the winding factor $k_w$ (accounts for non-sinusoidal flux and winding distribution; typically $k_w \approx 1$ for HV testing transformers with concentrated windings):

$$\boxed{E_{rms} = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \phi_m \cdot k_w}$$

**Numerical Check:** For a 230 V, 50 Hz transformer with $N_1 = 100$ turns and $k_w = 1$:

$$\phi_m = \frac{V_1}{4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_1} = \frac{230}{4.44 \cdot 50 \cdot 100} = \frac{230}{22200} \approx 0.01036 \text{ Wb}$$

For a 100 kV secondary with the same flux, the turns required:

$$N_2 = \frac{V_2}{4.44 \cdot f \cdot \phi_m} = \frac{100000}{4.44 \cdot 50 \cdot 0.01036} = \frac{100000}{2.300} \approx 43{,}478 \text{ turns}$$

This shows why HV secondary windings have tens of thousands of turns.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Output Voltage of n-Stage Cascaded Transformer

**Setup:** Consider n identical HV testing transformer stages, each with secondary rated voltage $V_{stage}$, cascaded in series.

**Step 1:** Each stage receives its primary (LV) excitation from either the LV mains or from the LV winding (excitation winding) of the previous stage.

**Step 2:** The secondary windings of all n stages are connected in **series aiding** (positive terminals chained).

**Step 3:** By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL), the total output voltage is the sum of individual secondary voltages (assuming all stages are in phase and properly polarized):

$$V_{out} = V_{sec,1} + V_{sec,2} + V_{sec,3} + \dots + V_{sec,n}$$

**Step 4:** For identical stages, $V_{sec,1} = V_{sec,2} = \dots = V_{sec,n} = V_{stage}$:

$$V_{out} = V_{stage} + V_{stage} + \dots + V_{stage} \quad (n \text{ times})$$

$$V_{out} = n \cdot V_{stage}$$

$$\boxed{V_{cascade} = n \cdot V_{stage}}$$

**Step 5 (Verification with numerical example):** Consider a 3-stage cascade, each stage rated 250 kV.

$$V_{out} = 3 \times 250 = 750 \text{ kV}$$

This is the typical rating for **750 kV class HV test sets** used by power utilities for testing 400 kV-class equipment.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Voltage Magnification in Series Resonant HV Test Set

**Setup:** An HV testing transformer with secondary voltage $V_s$ and internal impedance $R_s + jX_s$ is connected to a test object (capacitive load $C_{load}$). A series tuning inductor $L$ is inserted to create resonance.

**Step 1:** The total loop impedance is:

$$Z_{total} = R_s + R_L + j(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C_{load}})$$

where $R_L$ is the resistance of the inductor.

**Step 2:** At **series resonance**, the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel:

$$\omega L = \frac{1}{\omega C_{load}}$$

So the imaginary part becomes zero, and the total impedance is purely resistive:

$$Z_{total}\big|_{res} = R_s + R_L = R$$

**Step 3:** The circuit current at resonance is:

$$I_{res} = \frac{V_s}{R}$$

**Step 4:** The voltage across the test object (capacitor) is:

$$V_C = I_{res} \cdot X_C = \frac{V_s}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{\omega C_{load}}$$

**Step 5:** Define the quality factor Q:

$$Q = \frac{X_L}{R} = \frac{\omega L}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega C_{load} R}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute Q into the expression for $V_C$:

$$V_C = \frac{V_s}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{\omega C_{load}} = V_s \cdot \frac{1}{\omega C_{load} R}$$

$$V_C = V_s \cdot Q$$

$$\boxed{V_C = Q \cdot V_s}$$

**Step 7 (Numerical Verification):** Suppose $V_s = 50 \text{ kV}$ (transformer secondary), $C_{load} = 10 \text{ nF}$, $L = 10 \text{ H}$, $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$, $R = 50 \Omega$.

- $\omega = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$
- $X_L = \omega L = 314.16 \times 10 = 3141.6 \Omega$
- $Q = X_L / R = 3141.6 / 50 = 62.83$
- $V_C = Q \cdot V_s = 62.83 \times 50 \text{ kV} = 3141.5 \text{ kV} \approx 3.14 \text{ MV}$

This demonstrates the *enormous* voltage magnification achievable with resonance.

---

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation 4: Tesla Coil Output Voltage (Energy Conservation Argument)

**Setup:** A Tesla coil has primary (L₁, C₁) and secondary (L₂, C₂) tank circuits, both tuned to approximately the same resonant frequency $f_r$.

**Step 1 (Energy Conservation):** Assuming lossless coupling, the energy initially stored in the primary capacitor $C_1$ at voltage $V_1$ is:

$$W_1 = \frac{1}{2} C_1 V_1^2$$

**Step 2:** This energy is transferred to the secondary circuit. The maximum voltage on the secondary capacitor (top load) $C_2$ occurs when all this energy is stored in $C_2$:

$$W_2 = \frac{1}{2} C_2 V_{2,max}^2$$

**Step 3 (Equating energies for ideal case):**

$$\frac{1}{2} C_1 V_1^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_2 V_{2,max}^2$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $V_{2,max}$:

$$V_{2,max} = V_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C_1}{C_2}}$$

**Step 5 (Inductance-based voltage gain from turns ratio):** Mutual inductance coupling gives an additional factor:

$$V_{2,max} = V_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{L_1}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C_1}{C_2}}$$

**Step 6:** Combining inductors and capacitors gives the well-known Tesla coil formula:

$$\boxed{V_{2,max} = V_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{L_1} \cdot \frac{C_1}{C_2}}}$$

**Step 7 (Numerical Example):** Let $V_1 = 20 \text{ kV}$, $L_1 = 10 \mu\text{H}$, $L_2 = 100 \text{ mH}$, $C_1 = 1 \mu\text{F}$, $C_2 = 100 \text{ pF}$.

- $\sqrt{L_2/L_1} = \sqrt{100 \times 10^{-3} / 10 \times 10^{-6}} = \sqrt{10^4} = 100$
- $\sqrt{C_1/C_2} = \sqrt{10^{-6} / 10^{-10}} = \sqrt{10^4} = 100$
- $V_{2,max} = 20 \text{ kV} \times 100 \times 100 = 200{,}000 \text{ kV} = 200 \text{ MV}$

In practice, the value is much lower due to losses, spark-gap losses, and incomplete coupling, but the theoretical principle is sound.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — HV Transformer Types

| Parameter | Single-Stage HV Transformer | Cascaded Transformer | Series Resonant Transformer | Tesla Coil |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Max Voltage** | up to ~500 kV | 500 kV to 5 MV | 100 kV to 800 kV | 100 kV to several MV |
| **Frequency** | 50/60 Hz | 50/60 Hz | 50/60 Hz (tunable) | 50 kHz – 1 MHz |
| **Core Type** | Iron | Iron | Iron | Air (no iron) |
| **Waveform** | Pure sine | Pure sine | Pure sine | Damped oscillation |
| **Load Type** | Resistive/capacitive | Resistive/capacitive | Purely capacitive | Spark / corona load |
| **Power Required** | Full kVA rating | Full kVA rating | Only resistive loss | Very low average power |
| **Efficiency** | 90–98% | 85–95% | 95–99% | < 50% |
| **PD Level** | < 5 pC | < 5 pC | < 5 pC | Very high (noisy) |
| **Cost** | Moderate | High | Moderate-High | Low |
| **Application** | General HV testing | Ultra HV testing | Cable/GIS testing | Research, demo, medical |
| **Voltage Multiplication** | Direct turns ratio | Series addition of stages | Q-factor magnification | Resonant magnification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight**: For *steady-state high-voltage testing*, resonant and cascaded transformers dominate. For *impulse-like* or *very high frequency* applications, the Tesla coil principle is preferred. Modern impulse generators (Marx generators) supplement these AC techniques.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Cascaded HV Transformer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LV1["LV Mains Supply (11 kV / 415 V)"]
    EX1["Stage 1 Excitation Winding (Primary)"]
    SEC1["Stage 1 Secondary (250 kV)"]
    EX2["Stage 2 Excitation Winding (Primary)"]
    SEC2["Stage 2 Secondary (250 kV)"]
    EX3["Stage 3 Excitation Winding (Primary)"]
    SEC3["Stage 3 Secondary (250 kV)"]
    OUT["Cascade Output (750 kV)"]
    LOAD["HV Test Object (Capacitive)"]

    LV1 --> EX1
    EX1 --> SEC1
    SEC1 -.feeds.-> EX2
    EX2 --> SEC2
    SEC2 -.feeds.-> EX3
    EX3 --> SEC3
    SEC1 --- SERIES1{{Series Connection}}
    SEC2 --- SERIES1
    SEC3 --- SERIES1
    SERIES1 --> OUT
    OUT --> LOAD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The dashed arrows represent the *excitation* power transfer from one stage to the next. The solid horizontal connection between stages (SERIES1) represents the *series addition* of secondary voltages. The final output is the sum of all three stage outputs.

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Series Resonant HV Test Set

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MAINS["LV Mains (50 Hz)"]
    REG["Variable Voltage Regulator"]
    XFMR["HV Step-Up Transformer (T)"]
    L["Tuning Inductor (L)"]
    CLOAD["Test Object Capacitance (C_load)"]
    DIV["Capacitive Voltage Divider (Measurement)"]
    MEAS["Peak Voltmeter / HV Probe"]

    MAINS --> REG
    REG --> XFMR
    XFMR -- "V_s (HV output)" --> L
    L -- "Resonant loop" --> CLOAD
    CLOAD -- "Return path" --> XFMR
    CLOAD -.sample voltage.-> DIV
    DIV --> MEAS

    subgraph RESONANT_BLOCK ["Series Resonant Circuit (Tuned)"]
        L
        CLOAD
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight**: The variable voltage regulator allows the *output voltage* to be gradually raised. The tuning inductor is selected such that $X_L \approx X_C$ at the operating frequency. The capacitive divider provides a *safe, low-voltage replica* of the HV output for measurement.

---

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Tesla Coil System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AC["LV AC Mains (230 V, 50 Hz)"]
    HVX["HV Step-Up Transformer (Neon Sign Type, 10-20 kV)"]
    RECT["Rectifier (Optional, for DC charging)"]
    C1["Primary Capacitor C1 (Leyden Jar / MMC)"]
    SG["Spark Gap (SG)"]
    L1["Primary Coil L1 (Few Turns, Heavy Copper)"]
    L2["Secondary Coil L2 (Thousand Turns, Fine Wire)"]
    C2["Top Load Capacitor C2 (Sphere / Toroid)"]
    DISCHG["HV Discharge / Spark to Ground"]

    AC --> HVX
    HVX --> RECT
    RECT --> C1
    C1 -.charges until breakdown.-> SG
    SG -- "fires" --> L1
    L1 -.magnetic coupling.-> L2
    L2 --> C2
    C2 --> DISCHG

    subgraph PRIM_TANK ["Primary Tank Circuit"]
        C1
        SG
        L1
    end

    subgraph SEC_TANK ["Secondary Tank Circuit"]
        L2
        C2
    end
```

---

### 4.4 Comparative Architecture Flow — Choosing the Right HV Transformer

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Q1{"Voltage level?"}
    Q2{"Load type?"}

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -- "Up to 500 kV" --> Q2
    Q1 -- "Above 500 kV" --> CASC

    LOW -.use for.-> LOW_APP["Insulator testing, Bushing tests"]
    CASC -.use for.-> CASC_APP["EHV equipment, 765 kV class"]
    RES -.use for.-> RES_APP["Cable HV withstand, GIS"]
    TES -.use for.-> TES_APP["Research, X-ray, demo"]
```

---

### 4.5 Voltage Multiplication Principle — Visual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STAGE_A ["Stage 1 (250 kV)"]
        A1["LV Primary"]
        A2["HV Secondary 250 kV"]
        A1 --> A2
    end

    subgraph STAGE_B ["Stage 2 (250 kV)"]
        B1["LV Primary (Excited from Stage 1)"]
        B2["HV Secondary 250 kV"]
        B1 --> B2
    end

    OUTPUT["Total Output: 750 kV"]

    A2 --> SUM
    B2 --> SUM
    C2 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUTPUT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway**: In cascade design, *each stage is identical* and rated for only $V_{total}/n$. This makes the construction, transport, and maintenance of ultra-HV test sets feasible.
