<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the transformer EMF equation. Why is it important in HV transformer design?

**Model Answer:**

The transformer EMF equation gives the RMS value of the induced EMF in a transformer winding:

$$E = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \phi_m \cdot k_w$$

where $f$ = supply frequency, $N$ = number of turns, $\phi_m$ = maximum core flux, and $k_w$ = winding factor.

**Importance in HV Transformer Design:**

1. It determines the **number of turns** required on the HV secondary winding for a given output voltage.
2. For high $V_2$, very large $N_2$ is needed (e.g., for 100 kV at 50 Hz, $N_2 \approx 43{,}000$ turns).
3. It directly links output voltage to **core cross-sectional area** $A_c$ via $\phi_m = B_m \cdot A_c$, helping in core sizing.
4. Higher frequencies allow fewer turns for the same voltage (used in Tesla coils).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (KTU pattern)**: Definition (1.5 marks) + Significance in HV design (1.5 marks).

---

#### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Differentiate between a single-stage HV testing transformer and a cascaded HV testing transformer.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Single-Stage Transformer | Cascaded Transformer |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum output voltage** | Up to ~500 kV | 500 kV to several MV |
| **Construction** | Single core and windings | 2 or more stages in series |
| **Cost per kV** | Lower for low voltages | Lower for very high voltages |
| **Transport** | Difficult at high ratings | Each stage is transportable |
| **Voltage grading** | Difficult | Naturally graded by stages |
| **Short-circuit current** | Limited by own impedance | Higher (lower total impedance) |
| **Applications** | Insulation testing up to 500 kV class | EHV equipment testing (765 kV+) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (KTU pattern)**: Three distinguishing points × 1 mark each.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern**: For Module 4 Part B (14 marks), students are given an internal choice between two questions. Each question has two sub-parts: (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model]`

**Q:** With a neat schematic, explain the working principle and construction of a **cascaded HV testing transformer** used in high-voltage laboratories. Also derive the expression for total output voltage and state its advantages and limitations.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle and Construction [7 marks]:**

A cascaded HV testing transformer is an arrangement where **two or more identical HV transformer units are connected in series** on the HV side to achieve an output voltage equal to the sum of individual stage voltages.

**Construction (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):**

1. Each stage consists of an iron core with **three windings**: a low-voltage (LV) excitation winding (primary), a high-voltage (HV) secondary winding, and sometimes a tertiary winding for coupling to the next stage.

2. The LV winding of the first stage is connected to the **LV mains supply** (415 V / 11 kV).

3. The HV secondary of the first stage is connected **in series** with the HV secondary of the second stage.

4. The LV winding of the second stage is fed from a **special coupling (excitation) winding** on the first stage's transformer (this is the *metallic coupling* method).

5. This process continues for **n stages**. The final HV output is taken from the top of the last stage to ground.

**Working Principle:**

- All stages operate at the **same mains frequency (50 Hz)**.
- Each stage steps up its input voltage to $V_{stage}$ (e.g., 250 kV).
- Since all HV secondaries are in series and **in phase**, the voltages add up.
- For **n** stages: $V_{out} = n \cdot V_{stage}$.

**(b) Derivation, Advantages, and Limitations [7 marks]:**

**Derivation:**

By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law, the total output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = V_{sec,1} + V_{sec,2} + \dots + V_{sec,n}$$

For identical stages with $V_{sec} = V_{stage}$:

$$V_{out} = n \cdot V_{stage}$$

**Numerical example:** For a 3-stage cascade with each stage rated 250 kV:

$$V_{out} = 3 \times 250 = 750 \text{ kV}$$

**Advantages:**

1. Each stage is **transportable** (typically weighs < 5 tons).
2. **Modular construction** — easier manufacturing and maintenance.
3. **Natural voltage grading** — avoids need for complex capacitive grading rings.
4. **Cost-effective** for voltages > 500 kV compared to single-stage.
5. Fault in one stage does not damage others (with proper isolation).

**Limitations:**

1. **Lower short-circuit impedance** — current during flashover is hard to limit.
2. **Higher capacitance** to ground at top stages → voltage distribution may be non-uniform.
3. **Reactive power demand** increases with number of stages.
4. Overall **efficiency is lower** (85–95%) compared to single-stage units.
5. More **complex control and protection** circuitry needed.

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model]`

**Q:** Explain the working of a **series resonant transformer** for HV testing of capacitive loads. Derive the expression for voltage magnification and resonant frequency. Compare it with the Tesla coil.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle and Derivation [7 marks]:**

**Working Principle:**

A series resonant HV test set consists of:

1. A **variable-voltage HV transformer** (output $V_s$, variable 0 to rated).
2. A **tuning inductor** $L$ in series with the secondary.
3. The **capacitive test object** $C_{load}$ (e.g., cable, GIS, capacitor) completing the loop.

The transformer charges the test object through the inductor. As the supply frequency or inductance is adjusted, the circuit reaches **series resonance** at:

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C_{load}}}$$

At resonance, the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel. The circuit impedance is purely resistive (and very small, equal to just the ohmic resistance of the loop). Consequently, a relatively small source voltage $V_s$ produces a very large current, which in turn produces a **large voltage** across the test object.

**Derivation of Voltage Magnification:**

Total circuit impedance:

$$Z_{total} = R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

At resonance $\omega L = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$, so $Z_{total} = R$ (purely resistive).

Loop current at resonance:

$$I_{res} = \frac{V_s}{R}$$

Voltage across test object capacitor:

$$V_C = I_{res} \cdot X_C = \frac{V_s}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{\omega C}$$

Define quality factor $Q = \dfrac{1}{\omega C R}$:

$$\boxed{V_C = Q \cdot V_s}$$

**Voltage magnification factor = Q** (typically 30–100 for HV resonant test sets).

**(b) Tesla Coil Comparison [7 marks]:**

| Feature | Series Resonant Transformer | Tesla Coil |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating frequency** | 50 Hz (mains) | 50 kHz – 1 MHz |
| **Core** | Iron core | Air core (no iron) |
| **Tuning** | Single stage (L–C) | Double-tuned (L₁C₁ and L₂C₂) |
| **Excitation** | Continuous AC | Pulsed (spark gap or solid-state) |
| **Output waveform** | Continuous sine | Damped oscillation |
| **Voltage magnification** | Q-factor (30–100×) | √(L₂/L₁) × √(C₁/C₂) (can be 1000s×) |
| **Power level** | Hundreds of kVA | Few kVA average |
| **Load suitability** | Capacitive HV equipment | Spark loads, X-ray tubes, research |
| **Stability** | Excellent | Poor (damped oscillation) |
| **Efficiency** | 95–99% | < 50% |
| **Partial discharge** | Low (< 5 pC) | Very high |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up series and parallel resonance**: Series resonance gives *minimum impedance* and *maximum current* (hence max voltage across C). Parallel resonance gives *maximum impedance* and *maximum voltage across the tank*. Don't confuse these.
> 2. **Forgetting the winding factor $k_w$** in the EMF equation: For ideal HV transformers $k_w \approx 1$, but if the question explicitly mentions distributed windings, you must include $k_w$.
> 3. **Tesla coil output voltage is approximate**: The formula $V_2 = V_1 \sqrt{L_2/L_1} \sqrt{C_1/C_2}$ is an *ideal* energy conservation result. In practice, the value is far lower due to losses, corona, and incomplete coupling.
> 4. **Cascaded transformer excitation method**: Some students confuse *metallic* (winding-to-winding) coupling with *electrostatic* coupling. Always specify *which method* is used.
> 5. **Voltage regulation direction**: HV testing transformers feeding *capacitive loads* experience **Ferranti effect** — the output voltage *rises* above no-load value. State this clearly.
> 6. **Units in resonant calculations**: Always keep angular frequency $\omega$ in rad/s and frequency $f$ in Hz; do not mix them.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Transformers**

**Core Definitions:**
- ☐ Transformer EMF equation: $E = 4.44 f N \phi_m k_w$
- ☐ Turns ratio: $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$
- ☐ Cascade output: $V_{out} = n \cdot V_{stage}$

**Resonance Fundamentals:**
- ☐ Series resonance condition: $\omega L = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$
- ☐ Resonant frequency: $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$
- ☐ Quality factor: $Q = \dfrac{\omega_r L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_r C R}$
- ☐ Voltage magnification in series resonance: $V_C = Q \cdot V_s$

**Tesla Coil Fundamentals:**
- ☐ Air-core, double-tuned resonant transformer
- ☐ Output voltage: $V_{2,max} = V_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{L_2}{L_1}} \sqrt{\dfrac{C_1}{C_2}}$
- ☐ Operates at high frequency (50 kHz – 1 MHz)
- ☐ Components: HV transformer → Rectifier → C₁ → Spark gap → L₁ coupled to L₂ → C₂

**Cascade Transformer:**
- ☐ Used for voltages > 500 kV
- ☐ Excitation (LV) windings of subsequent stages fed from previous stages
- ☐ HV secondaries in series
- ☐ Disadvantages: Lower impedance, higher reactive demand

**Comparison Knowledge:**
- ☐ Single-stage ≤ 500 kV; Cascade for higher
- ☐ Resonant transformer for capacitive loads (cables, GIS)
- ☐ Tesla coil for high-frequency, impulse-like HV

**Common Numerical Mistakes to Avoid:**
- ☐ Converting frequency $f$ (Hz) to angular frequency $\omega$ (rad/s): $\omega = 2\pi f$
- ☐ Using **peak** vs **RMS** consistently (KTU usually uses RMS unless stated)
- ☐ Remembering flux density $B_m = \dfrac{\phi_m}{A_c}$ for core cross-section problems

**Practical Engineering Insights:**
- ☐ HV testing transformers have **low PD levels** (< 5 pC) and short-circuit impedance of 6–12%
- ☐ Ferranti effect causes voltage rise under capacitive load
- ☐ Tesla coils are NOT used for routine HV insulation testing due to waveform distortion
- ☐ Resonant transformers dominate *onsite* cable testing due to low input power requirement

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip**: For 14-mark cascade transformer questions, always begin with a labeled block diagram (3 marks), then derive $V_{out} = nV_{stage}$ (3 marks), and conclude with at least 3 advantages + 2 limitations (4 marks + 2 marks for neat presentation). This allocation matches KTU's marking scheme distribution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surge Diverters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Surge Diverters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Surge Diverter** (also known as a **Lightning Arrester** in older terminology) is a protective device connected in **shunt (parallel)** between the live conductor and earth, in close proximity to the equipment to be protected. Its primary function is to **divert abnormal overvoltages** — such as those caused by **lightning strikes, switching transients, or ferro-resonance** — safely to ground, while remaining virtually **invisible (inactive)** during normal power-frequency operating voltages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** A surge diverter is a device designed to protect electrical apparatus from high transient overvoltages by providing a low-impedance path to earth for the surge current, thereby limiting the voltage across the protected equipment to a safe level.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Relief Valve"

Imagine a domestic **water geyser** connected to a high-pressure municipal pipeline:

- Under **normal pressure**, the geyser operates silently.
- If pressure suddenly spikes (water hammer effect), a **spring-loaded relief valve** automatically opens, dumping excess water safely down the drain.
- Once normal pressure is restored, the valve **self-closes** automatically.

A **surge diverter does exactly this with electricity**:
- Under **normal system voltage** (e.g., 11 kV, 33 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV), the diverter has **very high impedance** — it is essentially an open circuit.
- When a **surge arrives** (lightning or switching), the diverter's impedance **collapses almost instantaneously** to a very low value, safely shunting the surge current to earth.
- Once the surge passes, the diverter **recovers to its high-impedance state** and resumes normal operation.

> [!NOTE]
> The name "**Diverter**" itself is engineering-rigorous: it *diverts* the surge, rather than *arresting* (stopping) it. The current still flows — it is merely **redirected** along a safe, non-destructive path.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Surge Diverters (KTU Standard Taxonomy)

Surge diverters are classified based on the **non-linear element** employed and their **constructional philosophy**:

| Sl. No. | Classification | Sub-Type | Operating Principle |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Non-Linear Resistor Type** (Modern) | (a) **Metal Oxide (ZnO) Gapless** | Highly non-linear ZnO pellets; no series spark gaps |
| 2 | | (b) **Silicon Carbide (SiC) Valve Type** | SiC blocks with **series spark gaps** |
| 3 | **Expulsion Type** | — | Arc quenched by gas generated from arc-heated organic material |
| 4 | **Rod Gap / Horn Gap** | — | Simplest; corona + arc to ground across an air gap |
| 5 | **Station / Intermediate / Distribution Class** | — | Classified by **discharge current capacity** and **application** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The **Metal Oxide (ZnO / Gapless) Surge Diverter** is the **most-tested topic** in the 2024 scheme. The SiC valve type is largely historical and covered only for **comparative purposes**.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams)

- **Rated Voltage ($V_r$):** Maximum permissible power-frequency voltage the diverter can continuously withstand.
- **Maximum Continuous Operating Voltage (MCOV):** The maximum RMS power-frequency voltage that may be applied continuously between the diverter terminals.
- **Nominal Discharge Current ($I_n$):** The peak value of a standard 8/20 µs impulse current used to classify the diverter (typically **5 kA, 10 kA, 20 kA**).
- **Discharge Voltage ($V_d$):** The voltage appearing across the diverter terminals during the passage of nominal discharge current.
- **Residual Voltage / Let-Through Voltage:** The peak voltage that appears across the diverter when discharging a surge — this is the voltage the **protected equipment actually sees**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Lightning Impulse Waveform:** **1.2/50 µs** (front time / tail time).
> **Standard Switching Impulse Waveform:** **250/2500 µs**.
> **Standard Discharge Current Waveform:** **8/20 µs**.
> These **must be remembered verbatim** for the KTU board exam.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Non-Linear Resistance

A surge diverter is fundamentally a **voltage-dependent resistor (VDR)**. Its current-voltage relationship is governed by:

$$I = k \cdot V^{\alpha}$$

where:
- $I$ = current through the diverter (A)
- $V$ = voltage across the diverter (V)
- $k$ = proportionality constant
- $\alpha$ = **non-linearity exponent** (also called the "index of non-linearity")

### Why $\alpha$ matters — The "Magic Number"

| Material | Typical $\alpha$ value | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Zinc Oxide (ZnO)** | **25 – 50** | Near-ideal: at normal voltage, current is in **microamperes**; at surge voltage, current is in **kiloamperes** — a change of ~6 orders of magnitude |
| **Silicon Carbide (SiC)** | **3 – 5** | Poor non-linearity; **must use series spark gaps** to block leakage at normal voltage |
| **Linear Resistor** | **1** | Ohm's law — useless as a diverter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **higher the $\alpha$**, the **better the diverter**. This is why ZnO (with its $\alpha \approx 35$) has effectively **replaced SiC** in modern power systems.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic of a ZnO Gapless Surge Diverter — Step-by-Step

The ZnO diverter operates in **three distinct modes**:

### **Mode 1: Normal Operating Condition (High Impedance)**
- The system voltage is well below the **turn-on voltage** of the ZnO pellets.
- Only a tiny **capacitive leakage current** (µA level) flows through the diverter.
- The diverter is virtually an **open circuit** — no power loss, no current flow to earth.

### **Mode 2: Surge Arrival (Low Impedance — Clamping Action)**
- When the surge voltage exceeds the **breakdown knee-point voltage** of the ZnO element, the diverter's impedance **plummets** by a factor of $10^5$ – $10^6$ within **nanoseconds**.
- The surge current is safely **diverted to earth**.
- The voltage across the diverter (and therefore across the protected equipment) is **clamped** to a value known as the **discharge voltage** ($V_d$).

### **Mode 3: Recovery (Return to High Impedance)**
- Once the surge is shunted, the voltage across the diverter drops back to the normal system voltage.
- Since the ZnO has such a high $\alpha$, the residual current is **negligible** (a few mA) — well below the **arc-sustaining threshold**.
- The diverter **self-recovers** to its high-impedance state — **no follow-up current**, **no gaps required** — this is the genius of the **gapless design**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Gapless"?** In SiC arresters, the **non-linearity is poor**, so a **series spark gap** is required to physically disconnect the SiC blocks from the line during normal operation (to prevent continuous leakage current heating). In ZnO arresters, the $\alpha$ is so high that no gap is needed — the leakage is naturally negligible. This eliminates the **time lag of spark gap breakdown** and gives **faster response**.

---

## 2.3 Constructional Details of a Modern ZnO Surge Diverter

A typical **station-class** ZnO surge diverter consists of:

1. **ZnO Disc Columns:** Cylindrical pellets of sintered zinc oxide doped with small amounts of bismuth, cobalt, manganese, and antimony oxides. The grain boundaries form the **non-linear junctions**.
2. **Silicone Rubber / Polymer Housing:** Provides electrical insulation and weather protection (replacing older porcelain in modern designs for **explosion resistance**).
3. **End Fittings (Terminal Connectors):** Line terminal at the top, earth terminal at the bottom.
4. **Pressure Relief System:** Vents in the housing to safely release gases in case of **diverter failure** (prevents violent explosion).
5. **Disconnection Device:** Automatically separates a failed diverter from the system, providing visible indication of failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Heat Dissipation:** Under repeated surges, the ZnO discs heat up. If the temperature exceeds the **thermal runaway limit**, the diverter fails. Hence, **heat dissipation capacity** is a critical design parameter and is sometimes specified as the **TOV (Temporary Over-Voltage) capability**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Sl. No. | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = k \cdot V^{\alpha}$ | Fundamental V-I relationship of non-linear diverter | A, V, dimensionless |
| 2 | $\log I = \log k + \alpha \log V$ | Logarithmic form (used to plot V-I curves as straight lines) | A, V |
| 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{\log(I_2/I_1)}{\log(V_2/V_1)}$ | Method to **calculate $\alpha$** from two points on V-I curve | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{Protection Ratio} = \dfrac{V_d}{V_{ph}}$ | Ratio of discharge voltage to peak phase voltage | dimensionless |
| 5 | $V_{residual} = V_d \cdot \left(\dfrac{I_{surge}}{I_n}\right)^{1/\alpha}$ | Residual voltage at any surge current (extrapolation formula) | V |
| 6 | $V_{protected} = V_{diverter} + 2 \cdot S \cdot S_s$ | Voltage at protected equipment (accounting for lead length) | V, m, kV/µs |
| 7 | $S_{min} = \dfrac{(BIL - V_d) \cdot v}{2 \cdot S_s}$ | **Minimum separation distance** between diverter and protected equipment | m |
| 8 | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Energy absorbed by the diverter during a surge | Joules (J) |
| 9 | $W = \int_0^{\tau} V(t) \cdot I(t) \, dt$ | Total energy dissipated in the diverter (integral form) | Joules (J) |
| 10 | $V_{TOV} = V_r \cdot k_{TOV}$ | Temporary Over-Voltage withstand capability | V |

Where:
- $V_d$ = Discharge voltage (V)
- $V_{ph}$ = Peak phase voltage (V)
- $I_n$ = Nominal discharge current (A)
- $I_{surge}$ = Actual surge current (A)
- $BIL$ = Basic Insulation Level of protected equipment (kV)
- $S$ = Separation distance (m)
- $S_s$ = Slope of travelling wave front (kV/µs)
- $v$ = Velocity of propagation in the connecting lead ($\approx 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s for overhead line; $\approx 1.6 \times 10^8$ m/s in free space)

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols that KTU expects:** Never use absolute value signs `|x|` directly inside markdown table cells — use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX. The protection ratio is sometimes also called the **"safety factor"** — values of **1.4 to 2.0** are typical for well-designed stations.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Surge diverters are **non-negotiable** in:
- **EHV substations** (110 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV) — to protect transformers, CBs, CTs, PTs.
- **GIS (Gas-Insulated Switchgear)** — special **SF6-insulated** arresters are used.
- **HVDC converter stations** — protect thyristor valves from DC-side surges.
- **Wind turbine generators** — exposed to frequent direct lightning strikes.
- **Railway traction systems** — for OHE (Overhead Equipment) protection.
- **Distribution transformers** (11 kV / 0.433 kV) — the most common application in Kerala.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Application Question Hook:** A very common KTU question asks: *"Why is the surge diverter placed as close as possible to the equipment being protected?"* — Answer: Because the **connecting lead introduces an inductance** ($\approx 1 \mu H$ per metre), and the voltage at the equipment is $V_{equipment} = V_{diverter} + L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$. Longer lead = more inductance = higher voltage seen by equipment = **defeats the purpose of protection**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Calculation of the Non-Linearity Exponent ($\alpha$)

The V-I characteristic of a surge diverter is given empirically by:
$$I = k \cdot V^{\alpha}$$

**Step 1:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides to linearize the relationship:
$$\ln(I) = \ln(k \cdot V^{\alpha})$$

**Step 2:** Apply the logarithmic identity $\ln(ab) = \ln a + \ln b$:
$$\ln(I) = \ln(k) + \alpha \cdot \ln(V)$$

**Step 3:** This is a **straight-line equation** of the form $y = mx + c$, where:
- $y = \ln(I)$
- $x = \ln(V)$
- Slope $m = \alpha$
- Intercept $c = \ln(k)$

**Step 4:** To calculate $\alpha$ from two known operating points $(V_1, I_1)$ and $(V_2, I_2)$:
$$\ln(I_1) = \ln(k) + \alpha \cdot \ln(V_1)$$
$$\ln(I_2) = \ln(k) + \alpha \cdot \ln(V_2)$$

**Step 5:** Subtract the first equation from the second to eliminate $\ln(k)$:
$$\ln(I_2) - \ln(I_1) = \alpha \cdot [\ln(V_2) - \ln(V_1)]$$

**Step 6:** Apply the identity $\ln a - \ln b = \ln(a/b)$:
$$\ln\left(\dfrac{I_2}{I_1}\right) = \alpha \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $\alpha$:
$$\boxed{\alpha = \dfrac{\ln(I_2/I_1)}{\ln(V_2/V_1)}}$$

This is the **most-frequently-asked KTU derivation** in Module 4.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum Separation Distance Between Diverter and Protected Equipment

This derivation addresses the **fundamental coordination problem** in surge protection: Why can't we place the diverter arbitrarily far from the transformer?

**Step 1:** The surge travels as a **travelling wave** along the connecting lead between the diverter and the protected equipment. The wave has a **finite velocity of propagation** $v$.

**Step 2:** The voltage at the **diverter terminal** when the surge arrives is the **discharge voltage** $V_d$. However, due to the **inductance of the lead** (or equivalently, the **finite time for the wave to reach the equipment**), the voltage at the **equipment terminal** is *higher* than $V_d$.

**Step 3:** Express the voltage at the equipment as:
$$V_{eq} = V_d + 2 \cdot S \cdot S_s$$

where:
- $S$ = separation distance (m)
- $S_s$ = steepness (slope) of the surge voltage wavefront (kV/µs)
- $v$ = velocity of propagation of the electromagnetic wave in the lead

**Step 4:** The factor of **2** appears because the wave reflects at the equipment terminal (open-circuit condition for the transformer) and travels back to the diverter, doubling the apparent voltage.

**Step 5:** The **protection criterion** demands:
$$V_{eq} \leq BIL \text{ of the equipment}$$

i.e., the voltage at the equipment must be **less than or equal to** its Basic Insulation Level.

**Step 6:** Substitute the condition:
$$V_d + 2 \cdot S \cdot S_s \leq BIL$$

**Step 7:** Isolate the term containing $S$:
$$2 \cdot S \cdot S_s \leq BIL - V_d$$

**Step 8:** The **time delay** of the wave over distance $S$ is $\Delta t = S / v$. In this time, the voltage at the diverter terminal rises (or the reflected wave adds) at a rate $S_s$. Thus:
$$2 \cdot S \cdot \dfrac{S_s}{v} \leq BIL - V_d$$

**Step 9:** Solve for the minimum separation distance $S_{min}$:
$$\boxed{S_{min} = \dfrac{(BIL - V_d) \cdot v}{2 \cdot S_s}}$$

**Numerical Interpretation:**
- If $BIL = 650$ kV, $V_d = 450$ kV, $v = 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s, $S_s = 1000$ kV/µs $= 10^9$ kV/s:
$$S_{min} = \dfrac{(650 - 450) \times 1.5 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^9} = \dfrac{200 \times 1.5 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^9} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{10}}{2 \times 10^9} = 15 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** A 14-mark question often gives $BIL$, $V_d$, and $S_s$, and asks the student to compute $S_{min}$. Always state the **assumed velocity of propagation** explicitly: $v = c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in free space, $\approx 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s in overhead line, and $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in cable.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Absorbed by the Surge Diverter

For a **rectangular voltage wave** of magnitude $V$ and duration $\tau$ passing through a diverter with non-linear resistance, the instantaneous power is:
$$p(t) = V(t) \cdot I(t)$$

For a non-linear element, $I(t) = k \cdot V(t)^{\alpha}$.

**Step 1:** The total energy absorbed is the time integral of instantaneous power:
$$E = \int_0^{\tau} V(t) \cdot I(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the non-linear I-V relation:
$$E = \int_0^{\tau} V(t) \cdot k \cdot V(t)^{\alpha} \, dt = \int_0^{\tau} k \cdot V(t)^{\alpha + 1} \, dt$$

**Step 3:** For a **rectangular wave** where $V(t) = V_0 = $ constant over the interval $[0, \tau]$:
$$E = \int_0^{\tau} k \cdot V_0^{\alpha + 1} \, dt = k \cdot V_0^{\alpha + 1} \cdot \tau$$

**Step 4:** The diverter must absorb this energy without exceeding its **thermal capacity** $E_{max}$:
$$\boxed{k \cdot V_0^{\alpha + 1} \cdot \tau \leq E_{max}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** This is why **long-duration switching surges** (250/2500 µs) are far more dangerous to a diverter than **short lightning impulses** (1.2/50 µs) — the energy is proportional to **duration**. A single 250/2500 µs wave may carry 100× more energy than a 1.2/50 µs wave of the same peak voltage.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SiC vs ZnO Surge Diverters (High-Yield KTU Table)

| Parameter | SiC Valve Type Diverter | ZnO Gapless Diverter |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-linearity exponent ($\alpha$)** | 3 – 5 | 25 – 50 |
| **Series spark gap required?** | **Yes** (essential) | **No** (gapless) |
| **Response time** | Limited by spark gap breakdown (~µs) | Sub-microsecond (ns) |
| **Follow-up current** | Significant (50 – 100 A); gap extinguishes it | **Negligible** (~few mA) |
| **Leakage current at normal voltage** | Blocked by gap | Small (µA capacitive) |
| **Protection level** | Higher (worse) | Lower (better) |
| **Ability to withstand multiple surges** | Limited by gap erosion | Excellent |
| **Size and weight** | Bulky | Compact |
| **Ageing / degradation** | Gradual | Sensitive to sustained overvoltages |
| **Cost (modern)** | Obsolete | Slightly higher initially, but **TCO lower** |
| **KTU recommendation (2024)** | Mentioned for comparison | **Primary focus** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Students often write *"ZnO arresters are gapless, so they have NO leakage current."* This is **wrong**. ZnO arresters have a small **capacitive leakage current** (typically 0.5 – 2 mA) at normal operating voltage. The correct statement is: *"The leakage is negligibly small, eliminating the need for a series spark gap."*

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Position of Surge Diverter in a Substation

The Mermaid diagram below shows the **block-level functional architecture** of how a surge diverter is integrated into a typical 110/11 kV substation. This is the **most-asked schematic** in KTU ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Overhead Transmission Line 110 kV] --> B[Line Isolator]
    B --> C[Current Transformer CT]
    C --> D[Circuit Breaker 110 kV]
    D --> E[Lightning / Surge Arrester ZnO Metal Oxide]
    E --> F[Earth Pit Low Impedance Grounding]
    D --> G[Power Transformer 110 11 kV MVA]
    G --> H[Outgoing Feeder 11 kV]
    H --> I[Distribution Network 11 kV 0.433 kV]
    G -.Surge Protection Path.-> E
    E --> J[Protection Relay]
    J --> K[Control and Relay Panel]
    A -.Lightning Strike Surge.-> E
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **surge arrives** on the transmission line (top of diagram).
- It reaches the **surge diverter**, which is connected in **parallel** with the transformer.
- The diverter **clamps the voltage** and **diverts the surge current to earth** via the **earth pit** (low-impedance grounding).
- The **protected equipment** is the power transformer, CT, and circuit breaker — all within the protection zone of the diverter.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Internal Construction of a ZnO Gapless Surge Diverter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    topTerm[Line Terminal Top] --> spring1[Spring Loaded Contact]
    spring1 --> topElectrode[Top Electrode Aluminum]
    topElectrode --> znocolumn1[ZnO Disc Column Series 1]
    znocolumn1 --> znocolumn2[ZnO Disc Column Series 2]
    znocolumn2 --> znocolumn3[ZnO Disc Column Series N]
    znocolumn3 --> botElectrode[Bottom Electrode]
    botElectrode --> spring2[Spring Contact]
    spring2 --> earthTerm[Earth Terminal Bottom]
    znocolumn1 -.Heat.-> heatsink[Heat Dissipation Fins]
    znocolumn2 -.Heat.-> heatsink
    znocolumn3 -.Heat.-> heatsink
    topElectrode --> housing[Silicone Rubber or Porcelain Housing]
    botElectrode --> housing
    housing --> pressureVent[Pressure Relief Vent]
    housing --> disconnect[Disconnection Indicator]
```

**Constructional reading:**
- Multiple **ZnO disc columns** are stacked in series to achieve the desired **rated voltage** — each disc has a working voltage of $\sim 3$ – $4$ kV, so for a 33 kV system, ~10–12 discs are used.
- **Heat dissipation fins** or **silicone rubber housing** removes heat from the discs.
- The **pressure relief vent** opens in case of internal failure to prevent explosion.
- The **disconnection indicator** (a small frangible disc) separates the failed unit from the line, providing visible indication.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Graph: V-I Characteristics of ZnO and SiC (Log-Log Scale)

```mermaid
graph LR
    origin[Log V axis X] --> region1[Region 1 Low Voltage Pre Breakdown]
    region1 --> region2[Region 2 Knee Point Transition]
    region2 --> region3[Region 3 High Surge Current]
    region3 --> region4[Region 4 Thermal Runaway Limit]
    subgraph A [ZnO Characteristic]
        A1[Slope alpha equals 25 to 50] --> A2[Sharp knee near operating voltage]
    end
    subgraph B [SiC Characteristic]
        B1[Slope alpha equals 3 to 5] --> B2[Gradual curve no sharp knee]
    end
    A2 --> A3[V almost constant over many decades of I]
    B2 --> B3[V varies significantly with I]
```

**Graphical reading:**
- The **ZnO curve** is **nearly vertical** (very high $\alpha$) in the operating region — meaning the voltage remains almost constant even as current changes by 6 orders of magnitude.
- The **SiC curve** has a **gentler slope** — voltage changes significantly with current, requiring the spark gap to "switch" the element in only at surge conditions.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart: Operational Logic of ZnO Diverter During a Surge Event

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Surge Arrives at Line Terminal] --> check1{Is Surge Voltage Greater than ZnO Knee Voltage?}
    check1 -->|No| normal[Mode 1 Normal Operation High Impedance Microampere Leakage Only]
    check1 -->|Yes| clamp[Mode 2 Clamping Action Impedance Collapses in Nanoseconds]
    clamp --> divert[Surge Current Diverted to Earth via Earth Pit]
    divert --> check2{Is Surge Voltage Still Above Knee?}
    check2 -->|Yes| clamp
    check2 -->|No| recover[Mode 3 Self Recovery Impedance Returns to High Value]
    recover --> monitor[Thermal Monitoring Temp within Safe Limit?]
    monitor -->|Yes| normal
    monitor -->|No| trip[Disconnect via Failure Indicator Replace Unit]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This flowchart can be drawn manually in the answer script using standard boxes and arrows. Keep the **three modes** clearly labelled. Use **dotted lines** to indicate non-conduction paths and **solid lines** for conduction paths.
