# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a surge diverter. List the different types of surge diverters used in power systems.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Expected Key):**

> A **surge diverter** is a protective device connected in shunt (parallel) between the line conductor and earth, designed to divert abnormal high-voltage surges (caused by lightning or switching) safely to ground, while remaining inactive under normal operating voltage conditions.

**Types of Surge Diverters:**

1. **Non-linear resistor type:**
   - (a) **Metal Oxide (ZnO) Gapless Diverter** — modern, most widely used.
   - (b) **Silicon Carbide (SiC) Valve Type Diverter** — older, uses series spark gaps.
2. **Expulsion type diverter** — uses arc-quenching gases from organic material.
3. **Rod gap / Horn gap diverter** — simplest form using air as the arc-quenching medium.
4. **Classification by application:** Station class, Intermediate class, Distribution class.

**[Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for types with brief description]**

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is the significance of the non-linearity exponent ($\alpha$) in the V-I characteristic of a surge diverter?**

**Model Answer:**

> The non-linearity exponent $\alpha$ quantifies the **degree of non-linearity** of the surge diverter's V-I characteristic, defined by the relation $I = k \cdot V^{\alpha}$.
>
> **Significance:**
> - The **higher the value of $\alpha$**, the **better the protective performance** of the diverter.
> - For **ZnO**, $\alpha$ is in the range **25 – 50**, enabling operation **without series spark gaps** (gapless design).
> - For **SiC**, $\alpha$ is in the range **3 – 5**, making it **necessary to use series spark gaps** to block continuous leakage current.
> - $\alpha$ is calculated from two operating points using:
> $$\alpha = \dfrac{\ln(I_2/I_1)}{\ln(V_2/V_1)}$$
> - A high $\alpha$ ensures the diverter draws **negligible current at normal voltage** but conducts **large currents at surge voltage**, giving an **ideal switching action**.

**[Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition of $\alpha$ + 2 marks for its significance with examples]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
### **Part (a): 7 Marks**
**Explain the construction and working of a Metal Oxide (ZnO) gapless surge diverter with the help of a neat V-I characteristic curve. State any four advantages of ZnO surge diverters over conventional SiC surge diverters.**

**Model Solution:**

#### **Construction of ZnO Gapless Surge Diverter:**

A modern ZnO surge diverter consists of the following main components:

1. **ZnO Disc Column:** The heart of the diverter — a stack of cylindrical sintered zinc oxide pellets, each pellet rated for ~3–4 kV working voltage. The number of pellets in series determines the diverter's **rated voltage**.

2. **Dopants:** Small amounts of **bismuth oxide (Bi₂O₃)**, **cobalt oxide (CoO)**, **manganese oxide (MnO₂)**, and **antimony oxide (Sb₂O₃)** are added to ZnO to form the **non-linear grain-boundary junctions** that give the characteristic V-I behaviour.

3. **Side Electrodes:** Aluminum sprayed electrodes on the flat faces of each disc provide electrical contact.

4. **Connecting Leads & End Fittings:** Connect the column to the line and earth terminals.

5. **Housing:** Either **porcelain** (traditional) or **silicone rubber / polymer** (modern) — provides insulation, mechanical strength, and weatherproofing.

6. **Pressure Relief System:** Diaphragm vents that open during internal failure to prevent violent explosion.

7. **Disconnection Device:** An explosive-bolt or frangible indicator that physically disconnects a failed diverter, giving visible external indication.

#### **Working Principle:**

The ZnO diverter exhibits **three operating regions** in its V-I characteristic:

- **Region 1 (Pre-breakdown / Normal):** At voltages below the knee-point, only a small **capacitive leakage current** (microamperes) flows. The diverter is essentially an open circuit.

- **Region 2 (Breakdown / Knee):** As voltage reaches the knee-point (the rated voltage of the pellets), the impedance suddenly collapses by 5–6 orders of magnitude within nanoseconds, and the diverter begins to conduct heavily.

- **Region 3 (Clamping / Surge Discharge):** The voltage across the diverter is **clamped** to a value (the discharge voltage $V_d$) while the surge current (kiloamperes) is shunted to earth.

- **Region 4 (Recovery):** When the surge ends, the voltage drops below the knee-point, the diverter returns to Region 1 within microseconds, and normal operation resumes.

#### **V-I Characteristic (Log-Log Plot):**

The ZnO V-I curve has an **extremely steep slope** ($\alpha = 25$ – $50$) in the operating region, appearing nearly as a vertical line transitioning from µA to kA. The SiC curve, in contrast, has a much gentler slope ($\alpha = 3$ – $5$).

#### **Four Advantages of ZnO over SiC Surge Diverters:**

1. **Gapless construction** — eliminates the response time delay and erosion problems of spark gaps.
2. **Higher non-linearity exponent ($\alpha$)** — better protective characteristics and lower residual voltage.
3. **Simpler construction** — fewer components, smaller size, lighter weight, and lower maintenance.
4. **Better ability to withstand multiple operations** — no gap erosion, hence longer service life.

**Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Errors:**
> 1. Students draw the V-I curve on a **linear scale** — the curve looks useless. **Always use a log-log scale** to show the steep non-linearity clearly.
> 2. Students omit the **recovery region** — KTU specifically tests whether you know the diverter self-recovers.
> 3. Students write "ZnO has NO leakage current" — this is incorrect. It has a small **capacitive leakage** of 0.5–2 mA.
> 4. Students forget to **mention dopants** (Bi₂O₃, CoO) — marks specifically allotted for the chemical composition of ZnO pellets.

---

### **Part (b): 7 Marks**
**A 110 kV, 50 Hz substation has a surge diverter rated at 90 kV (RMS). If the BIL of the transformer is 550 kV and the surge wave has a steepness of 1000 kV/µs, calculate the minimum separation distance between the diverter and the transformer. Assume velocity of propagation $v = 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s.**

**Model Solution:**

#### **Given Data:**
- System voltage: 110 kV, 50 Hz
- Diverter rated voltage (RMS): $V_r = 90$ kV
- Basic Insulation Level of transformer: $BIL = 550$ kV
- Surge wave steepness: $S_s = 1000$ kV/µs
- Velocity of propagation: $v = 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s

#### **Step 1: Calculate the Peak Rated Voltage (Discharge Voltage Reference)**
The discharge voltage $V_d$ is typically $\sqrt{2} \times V_r$ (peak value):
$$V_d = \sqrt{2} \times 90 \text{ kV} = 127.28 \text{ kV} \approx 127.3 \text{ kV}$$

#### **Step 2: Apply the Separation Distance Formula**
$$S_{min} = \dfrac{(BIL - V_d) \cdot v}{2 \cdot S_s}$$

#### **Step 3: Substitute Values (with Unit Conversion)**
- $BIL - V_d = 550 - 127.3 = 422.7$ kV
- $S_s = 1000$ kV/µs $= 1000 \times 10^6$ kV/s $= 10^9$ kV/s
- $v = 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s

$$S_{min} = \dfrac{422.7 \times 1.5 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^9}$$

**Step 4: Numerator:**
$$422.7 \times 1.5 \times 10^8 = 634.05 \times 10^8 = 6.3405 \times 10^{10}$$

**Step 5: Denominator:**
$$2 \times 10^9 = 2 \times 10^9$$

**Step 6: Division:**
$$S_{min} = \dfrac{6.3405 \times 10^{10}}{2 \times 10^9} = \dfrac{6.3405}{2} \times 10^{1} = 3.170 \times 10 = 31.7 \text{ m}$$

#### **Result:**
$$\boxed{S_{min} \approx 31.7 \text{ m}}$$

#### **Engineering Interpretation:**
The diverter must be installed **within 31.7 metres** of the transformer. If the physical layout of the substation requires a longer lead, then either:
- A **diverter with a lower discharge voltage** must be selected, or
- A **higher BIL transformer** must be used, or
- The **surge steepness must be limited** using additional chokes/reactors at the line entrance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors in This Numerical:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert $S_s$ from kV/µs to kV/s — **multiply by $10^6$**.
> 2. Using **RMS** value of $V_r$ directly without converting to **peak** — KTU expects peak value.
> 3. Forgetting the factor of **2** in the denominator — this is a **guaranteed 1-mark deduction**.
> 4. Not writing **units** in the final answer — KTU examiners explicitly look for "metres" or "m".

---

### **Question B (Internal Choice Alternative): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
### **Part (a): 7 Marks**
**With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a Silicon Carbide (SiC) surge diverter with series gaps. Also, state its disadvantages compared to a ZnO surge diverter.**

**Model Solution:**

#### **Construction of SiC Surge Diverter (Valve Type):**

The SiC surge diverter consists of the following components:

1. **SiC (Silicon Carbide) Blocks:** The non-linear resistance elements, manufactured by sintering silicon carbide grains with a ceramic binder. These blocks have a moderate non-linearity ($\alpha = 3$ – $5$).

2. **Series Spark Gaps:** Multiple **sphere or horn-type gaps** connected in series with the SiC blocks. These gaps **physically isolate** the SiC blocks from the line during normal operation, preventing continuous leakage current that would otherwise heat and damage the SiC elements.

3. **Equalising Grading Rings/Condensers:** Used to ensure uniform voltage distribution across the series gaps. Without them, the gaps nearest to the line would take disproportionate voltage.

4. **Porcelain Housing:** Provides insulation and mechanical protection.

5. **Earth Terminal:** Connected to a low-impedance earth pit.

#### **Working Principle:**

**Mode 1: Normal Operation (Gap not sparked)**
- The series spark gap's breakdown voltage is set **higher** than the normal system peak voltage.
- The gap remains **non-conducting** — the SiC blocks are effectively disconnected from the line.
- No leakage current flows — no power loss.

**Mode 2: Surge Arrival (Gap sparks over)**
- When the surge voltage exceeds the gap breakdown voltage, the gap **sparks over** within microseconds.
- The surge current now flows through the SiC blocks to earth.
- The non-linear resistance of SiC **clamps** the voltage to the discharge level.

**Mode 3: Follow-up Current and Gap Extinction**
- After the surge, the **power-frequency follow-up current** (typically 50–100 A) tries to continue flowing through the SiC blocks.
- The series gap is designed to **extinguish this 50 Hz follow-up arc** at the next current zero.
- The diverter returns to Mode 1.

#### **Disadvantages Compared to ZnO Diverter:**

1. **Requires series spark gaps** — adds complexity, response delay, and maintenance.
2. **Lower non-linearity** — higher residual voltage and worse protection.
3. **Spark gap erosion** — limited number of operations before gap replacement.
4. **Larger and heavier** — bulkier installation footprint.
5. **Slower response** — microsecond delay due to gap breakdown time.
6. **Higher follow-up current** — risk of thermal failure if gap fails to extinguish.

---

### **Part (b): 7 Marks**
**Compare SiC and ZnO surge diverters under the following heads: (i) Non-linearity, (ii) Spark gap requirement, (iii) Response time, (iv) Follow-up current, (v) Size, (vi) Ageing, (vii) Cost.**

**Model Solution:**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | SiC Diverter | ZnO Diverter |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Non-linearity exponent $\alpha$ | 3 – 5 | 25 – 50 |
| (ii) | Spark gap required? | Yes, essential | No (gapless) |
| (iii) | Response time | ~µs (gap delay) | ~ns (instantaneous) |
| (iv) | Follow-up current | 50 – 100 A (must be interrupted) | Negligible (few mA) |
| (v) | Size and weight | Large and heavy | Compact and light |
| (vi) | Ageing / degradation | Slow, gradual | Sensitive to TOV; thermal runaway possible |
| (vii) | Cost (initial) | Lower | Slightly higher |
| (viii) | Cost (lifecycle) | Higher (maintenance) | Lower (maintenance-free) |
| (ix) | Ability to discharge switching surges | Limited | Excellent |
| (x) | KTU 2024 recommendation | Obsolete | **Preferred choice** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do not write one-word answers** in the comparison — KTU expects **a complete sentence or numerical value** for each head.
> 2. Use a **tabular format** for the answer — it is the **most-accepted KTU board presentation style**.
> 3. **Always state the underlying reason** for the difference — e.g., for (iii) response time, mention "due to the finite breakdown time of the spark gap".
> 4. The order of parameters in your answer must **match the order asked in the question** — KTU examiners are strict about this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD POINTS FOR KTU 2024 BOARD EXAM — MODULE 4 (SURGE DIVERTERS)**

- [ ] **Definition:** Surge diverter = shunt protective device that diverts overvoltages to earth.
- [ ] **Position:** Connected between **line and earth**, in **parallel** with the protected equipment.
- [ ] **Placed as close as possible** to the equipment (to minimise lead inductance).
- [ ] **V-I relation:** $I = k \cdot V^{\alpha}$.
- [ ] **$\alpha$ for ZnO:** **25–50** (excellent non-linearity, gapless).
- [ ] **$\alpha$ for SiC:** **3–5** (moderate non-linearity, requires spark gap).
- [ ] **Standard waveforms:** Lightning impulse = **1.2/50 µs**; Switching impulse = **250/2500 µs**; Discharge current = **8/20 µs**.
- [ ] **Three operating regions** of ZnO: Pre-breakdown (µA), Breakdown knee, Clamping (kA), Recovery.
- [ ] **Key ratings:** Rated voltage, MCOV, Nominal discharge current, Discharge voltage, Pressure relief rating.
- [ ] **Minimum separation distance formula:** $S_{min} = \dfrac{(BIL - V_d) \cdot v}{2 \cdot S_s}$.
- [ ] **Why gapless?** Because ZnO's high $\alpha$ makes leakage current negligibly small at normal voltage.
- [ ] **Dopants in ZnO:** Bi₂O₃, CoO, MnO₂, Sb₂O₃ (form the non-linear grain boundary junctions).
- [ ] **Failure mode:** Thermal runaway due to sustained overvoltages.
- [ ] **Energy absorbed:** $E = \int V \cdot I \, dt$; switching surges carry more energy than lightning impulses of the same peak.
- [ ] **Station class:** Used at substations (high energy, 10–20 kA discharge).
- [ ] **Distribution class:** Used on distribution lines (5 kA discharge).
- [ ] **Modern housing:** Silicone rubber / polymer (replacing porcelain — safer during failure).
- [ ] **Historical term:** "Lightning arrester" — used interchangeably but **"surge diverter" is the 2024 KTU-preferred nomenclature**.
- [ ] **Disconnection indicator:** Visible external flag when the diverter has failed — important for maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Cables # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 What is a Power Cable? A **power cable** is an insulated assembly of one or more conductors (copper or aluminum) used for the underground transmission and distribution of electrical energy. It is a complete, self-contained system designed to safely carry high voltages through confined urban and industrial environments where overhead lines are impractical or unsafe. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** A cable is a length of single or multiple conductors (typically copper or aluminum), each individually insulated and surrounded by a common protective sheath, used for the underground transmission and distribution of electrical power at high voltages. ### Real-World Conceptual Analogy: The "Water Pipeline" Model Imagine a high-pressure water pipeline buried underground: - **The inner metal pipe (conductor)** carries the energy (current/flow of water). - **The thick rubber wall (insulation)** prevents leakage of the fluid (electrical leakage current). - **The outer metal armor (sheath/armor)** provides mechanical strength and a return path for fault currents. - **The final outer coating (serving/jacket)** protects the whole system from soil, moisture, and chemicals. Just as a water pipeline must withstand high pressure without bursting, a high-voltage cable must withstand high electrical stress without dielectric breakdown. --- ## 1.2 Why Cables are Needed (Advantages over Overhead Lines) | Feature | Underground Cable | Overhead Line | |---|---|---| | **Safety** | Not affected by storms/lightning | Highly vulnerable to weather | | **Aesthetics** | Invisible (no visual pollution) | Visible towers/conductors | | **Urban Use** | Ideal for crowded cities | Difficult in dense areas | | **Maintenance** | Low (but fault location hard) | Easy to inspect | | **Cost** | **~10x more expensive** | Economical | | **Voltage Limit** | Limited to ~500 kV (HVDC up to 800 kV) | Can exceed 1000 kV (UHV AC) | | **Fault Repair** | Difficult and time-consuming | Quick and easy | | **Current Capacity** | Limited by **heat dissipation** | Better air cooling | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4, the focus is on **belted cables, H-type (Höchstädter) cables, S.L. type cables, compression cables, oil-filled cables, gas-pressure cables**, and their dielectric behavior, grading, capacitance, and fault location. --- ## 1.3 Fundamental Classification of Power Cables ### A. Based on Voltage Level 1. **Low Tension (LT) Cables** — Up to **1 kV** (domestic distribution) 2. **High Tension (HT) Cables** — **1 kV to 11 kV** 3. **Super Tension (ST) Cables** — **11 kV to 33 kV** 4. **Extra High Tension (EHT) Cables** — **33 kV to 66 kV** 5. **Extra-Super Voltage Cables** — Above **132 kV** ### B. Based on Construction 1. **Single-Core Cables** (one conductor per cable) 2. **Three-Core Cables** (three conductors in one sheath) - Belted type - H-type (Screening per core) - HSL type (Screening per phase) - SL type (Separate lead sheath per core) 3. **Special Cables**: - Oil-filled cables (low pressure / high pressure) - Gas-pressure cables - XLPE / EPR cables - HVDC cables > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Insight:** The choice between cable types is governed by the maximum operating voltage, required current capacity, installation environment, and permissible dielectric stress. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Construction of an Underground Cable — Layer-by-Layer Anatomy A single-core cable is built like a Russian doll — concentric cylindrical layers, each serving a unique purpose: | Layer (Innermost to Outermost) | Material | Function | |---|---|---| | **1. Conductor (Core)** | Copper / Aluminum (stranded) | Carries the actual current | | **2. Conductor Screen** | Semi-conducting paper/tape | Smooths the conductor surface to prevent stress concentration | | **3. Insulation (Dielectric)** | Impregnated paper / XLPE / EPR / PVC | Withstands the full electrical stress | | **4. Insulation Screen** | Semi-conducting layer + metallic braid | Provides uniform ground potential at outer boundary | | **5. Metallic Sheath** | Lead / Aluminum | Hermetic seal against moisture, returns capacitive & fault currents | | **6. Bedding / Inner Serving** | Hessian + bitumen | Protects sheath from armor corrosion | | **7. Armor** | Steel wires / tapes (2 layers for multi-core) | Mechanical protection against impact | | **8. Outer Serving (Jacket)** | Hessian, PVC, or polythene | Outer weather/chemical protection | > [!NOTE] > **Why stranded conductors?** Stranding (typically **7, 19, 37, 61… strands**) increases flexibility, reduces skin-effect losses, and prevents the **"knee-point"** stress concentration that solid conductors create at the surface. --- ## 2.2 The Single Most Important Concept: Electric Stress in a Single-Core Cable This is the **heart of Module 4** and accounts for the majority of long-answer questions. ### The Physical Setup A single-core cable has: - **Inner conductor radius** = $r_1$ (sometimes called $a$) - **Outer sheath inner radius** = $r_2$ (sometimes called $b$) - **Dielectric** between them with permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ - **Conductor carries** charge $Q$ coulombs per metre length ### The Logic of Stress Distribution 1. By Gauss's theorem, the electric flux density at radius $r$ is: $$D(r) = \frac{Q}{2\pi r}$$ 2. Therefore, the electric field intensity (stress) at radius $r$ is: $$g(r) = \frac{D(r)}{\varepsilon} = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon r}$$ 3. **The stress is INVERSELY PROPORTIONAL to the radius.** Stress is **maximum at the conductor surface** ($r = r_1$) and **minimum at the sheath** ($r = r_2$). 4. Integrating $g(r)$ from $r_1$ to $r_2$ gives the **operating voltage** $V$. ### Critical Result: Maximum and Minimum Stress $$g_{max} = \frac{V}{r_1 \, \ln(r_2/r_1)} \quad \text{(at conductor surface)}$$ $$g_{min} = \frac{V}{r_2 \, \ln(r_2/r_1)} \quad \text{(at sheath surface)}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{g_{max}}{g_{min}} = \frac{r_2}{r_1}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Golden Rule of Cable Design:** To minimize $g_{max}$ for a fixed $V$ and $r_2$, the optimum ratio is $\frac{r_2}{r_1} = e \approx 2.718$. This gives the **most economical cable** with **lowest dielectric material usage**. --- ## 2.3 Capacitance of a Single-Core Cable From the relation $Q = CV$ and the voltage integration: $$C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} \quad \text{Farads per metre}$$ For **3-phase operation**, the **capacitance per phase** of a 3-core cable requires special treatment involving positive, negative, and zero-sequence capacitances. --- ## 2.4 GRADING OF CABLES — Eliminating Non-Uniform Stress The problem: In a single dielectric cable, $g_{max} = 2.3 \times g_{min}$. This wastes insulation. **Grading** is the technique to make the stress uniform (or nearly uniform) throughout the dielectric. ### Method 1: Capacitance Grading (Dielectric Grading) - Uses **multiple layers of different permittivities**. - The condition for uniform stress: $$\varepsilon_1 r_1 = \varepsilon_2 r_2 = \varepsilon_3 r_3 = \dots$$ - The **innermost layer** (near conductor) has **highest permittivity** (e.g., $\varepsilon_1$) and the **outermost layer** has the **lowest permittivity**. ### Method 2: Intersheath Grading - Places **metallic intersheaths at intermediate radii** at floating potentials. - Each region carries part of the voltage, equalizing the stress. - **Key result:** With $n$ intersheaths, $g_1 = \frac{V}{r_1 \ln(r_{n+1}/r_1)}$ — independent of the number of intersheaths! --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it covers ~70% of numerical problems in Module 4.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Critical Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Electric field at radius $r$ | $g(r) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi\varepsilon r}$ | Varies as $1/r$ | | 2 | Operating voltage | $V = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln(r_2/r_1)$ | Derived by integration | | 3 | **Max. stress** (at conductor) | $g_{max} = \dfrac{V}{r_1\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | Critical for breakdown design | | 4 | **Min. stress** (at sheath) | $g_{min} = \dfrac{V}{r_2\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | For grading design | | 5 | Stress ratio | $g_{max}/g_{min} = r_2/r_1$ | Drives grading decisions | | 6 | Optimum ratio | $r_2/r_1 = e \approx 2.718$ | Most economical design | | 7 | Capacitance (single-core) | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | F/m | | 8 | Charging current | $I_C = \omega C V$ per phase | A/m | | 9 | Dielectric loss per phase | $P = \omega C V^2 \tan\delta$ | W/m | | 10 | Intersheath grading stress | $g_1 = \dfrac{V}{r_1\ln(r_{n+1}/r_1)}$ | $n$ intersheaths | | 11 | Capacitance grading permittivity | $\varepsilon_1 r_1 = \varepsilon_2 r_2 = \dots$ | Uniform stress condition | | 12 | Murray loop distance | $x = \dfrac{P}{P+Q} \times 2L$ | $P, Q$ = ratio arms, $L$ = length of good cable | | 13 | Varley loop distance | $x = \dfrac{rL}{R_1 + rL/2L - R_3}$ | $r$ = per-unit resistance, $R_3$ = variable arm | | 14 | Most economical conductor area | $A = I \, \text{(for max efficiency)}$ | Kelvin's Law variant | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Underground urban distribution** in metros and metros (Mumbai, Delhi, Kochi Metro). - **Submarine power transmission** (HVDC 800 kV from UK to Norway). - **Power plant switchyard interconnections** where overhead lines are unsafe. - **Industrial plants, oil refineries, mines** — explosion-proof zones demand cables. - **Renewable energy** — connecting offshore wind farms via XLPE submarine cables. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Most students lose marks by **forgetting units**. Always quote capacitance in **F/m or μF/km**, and stress in **kV/mm or kV/cm**. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX > [!NOTE] > **KTU Mandate:** Every algebraic step is shown. No jumps. Each step is justified. --- ## 3.1 DERIVATION: Electric Stress in a Single-Core Cable ### Statement Derive the expression for the maximum and minimum electric stress in a single-core cable, and find the condition for minimum dielectric. ### Given - Inner radius of conductor = $r_1$ - Inner radius of outer sheath = $r_2$ - Dielectric permittivity = $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ - Operating voltage (core to sheath) = $V$ - Charge per unit length on conductor = $Q$ C/m ### Step 1 — Apply Gauss's Theorem Consider a Gaussian cylindrical surface of radius $r$ ($r_1 < r < r_2$) and length $L$ coaxial with the cable. The total flux through this surface must equal the enclosed charge $QL$: $$\oint \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{A} = Q_{enclosed}$$ Since $\vec{D}$ is radial and uniform over the curved surface: $$D(r) \cdot (2\pi r L) = QL$$ $$D(r) = \frac{Q}{2\pi r}$$ ### Step 2 — Convert to Electric Field Intensity The electric field intensity (which is the **electric stress** in a cable): $$g(r) = \frac{D(r)}{\varepsilon} = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon r}$$ > **Logic:** The electric stress at any point inside the dielectric is **inversely proportional** to its distance from the centre. This is the most fundamental property of cable dielectrics. ### Step 3 — Integrate to Get the Voltage The voltage between the conductor and the sheath is the line integral of the electric field: $$V = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} g(r) \, dr = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon r} \, dr$$ $$V = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon} \left[ \ln r \right]_{r_1}^{r_2} = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon} \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$ Solving for $Q$: $$Q = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon V}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute Q back into g(r) Plugging $Q$ into the expression for $g(r)$: $$g(r) = \frac{1}{2\pi \varepsilon r} \cdot \frac{2\pi \varepsilon V}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$ $$g(r) = \frac{V}{r \, \ln(r_2/r_1)}$$ ### Step 5 — Maximum Stress (at $r = r_1$) Since $g(r) \propto 1/r$, the maximum occurs at the smallest radius: $$g_{max} = g(r_1) = \frac{V}{r_1 \ln(r_2/r_1)}$$ ### Step 6 — Minimum Stress (at $r = r_2$) The minimum occurs at the largest radius: $$g_{min} = g(r_2) = \frac{V}{r_2 \ln(r_2/r_1)}$$ ### Step 7 — Stress Ratio $$\frac{g_{max}}{g_{min}} = \frac{V / [r_1 \ln(r_2/r_1)]}{V / [r_2 \ln(r_2/r_1)]} = \frac{r_2}{r_1}$$ > **Conclusion:** The stress ratio equals the ratio of the radii. To minimize $g_{max}$, we must either reduce $V$ or increase $r_1$ — or grade the cable. ### Step 8 — Optimum Ratio for Minimum Volume of Dielectric The volume of insulation per unit length is: $$\text{Vol} = \pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2) = \pi r_1^2 \left[\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^2 - 1\right]$$ Let $k = r_2/r_1$. Then $r_1 = r_2/k$ and the maximum stress becomes: $$g_{max} = \frac{V}{(r_2/k) \ln k} = \frac{V k}{r_2 \ln k}$$ The **most economical cable** is obtained when $g_{max}$ is **minimum for a given $V$ and $r_2$**. Differentiating with respect to $k$ and setting to zero: $$\frac{d(g_{max})}{dk} = \frac{V}{r_2} \cdot \frac{\ln k - k \cdot (1/k)}{(\ln k)^2} = 0$$ $$\ln k - 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln k = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = e$$ $$\boxed{\frac{r_2}{r_1} = e \approx 2.718 \quad \text{or} \quad r_1 = r_2/e}$$ > **Engineering meaning:** If the inner conductor radius is **1/e of the sheath radius**, the cable uses the **least amount of dielectric** for a given operating voltage and breakdown strength. --- ## 3.2 DERIVATION: Capacitance of a Single-Core Cable Using the relation $Q = CV$ and the expression for $Q$ derived in Step 3: $$C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} \quad \text{F/m}$$ For a 3-core cable (belted), the per-phase capacitance involves the three mutual capacitances between cores and between each core and the sheath. The **capacitance to neutral** is: $$C_n = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\ln\!\left[\frac{d\sqrt{3}}{r_1} \cdot \frac{\sqrt[6]{a^3 - 1}}{\sqrt[6]{a^3 + 0.5}} \right]} \quad \text{where } a = d/(2r_1)$$ --- ## 3.3 DERIVATION: Intersheath Grading ### Problem Setup A single-core cable has $n$ intersheaths placed at radii $r_1 < r_2 < r_3 < \dots < r_{n+1}$. The intersheaths are **floating** (not connected to any external source). The total voltage $V$ is applied between the conductor ($r_1$) and the outer sheath ($r_{n+1}$). ### Condition for Uniform Stress For uniform stress throughout: $$g_1 r_1 = g_2 r_2 = g_3 r_3 = \dots = g_{n+1} r_{n+1} = k \text{ (constant)}$$ where $g_i$ is the stress in the $i$-th region. ### Voltage across Each Region The voltage across the $i$-th region (between $r_i$ and $r_{i+1}$): $$V_i = g_i \cdot r_i \cdot \ln(r_{i+1}/r_i) = k \ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)$$ ### Total Voltage Summing voltages across all $(n+1)$ regions: $$V = \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} V_i = k \left[ \ln(r_2/r_1) + \ln(r_3/r_2) + \dots + \ln(r_{n+1}/r_n) \right]$$ $$V = k \ln(r_{n+1}/r_1)$$ Therefore: $$k = \frac{V}{\ln(r_{n+1}/r_1)}$$ ### Maximum Stress (at Conductor) $$g_{max} = g_1 = \frac{k}{r_1} = \frac{V}{r_1 \ln(r_{n+1}/r_1)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Result:** This is **identical in form to a cable with NO intersheaths** but with $r_2$ replaced by $r_{n+1}$. The number of intersheaths does **NOT** reduce $g_{max}$ — what it does is **equalize the stress** in all regions so the dielectric works efficiently. --- ## 3.4 DERIVATION: Murray Loop Test for Cable Fault Location ### Circuit A faulty cable of length $L$ has a fault at distance $x$ from the test end. A **sound (healthy) cable of identical length and resistance** is connected at the far end, forming a loop. ### Bridge Arrangement - **P** and **Q** are the ratio arms (resistances). - **R** is a variable resistor. - The galvanometer balances when: $$\frac{P}{Q} = \frac{R + r(L-x)}{r \cdot x}$$ where $r$ is the resistance per metre of the cable. ### Solving for x $$\frac{P}{Q} = \frac{R}{r x} + \frac{L-x}{x} = \frac{R}{r x} + \frac{L}{x} - 1$$ $$\frac{P}{Q} + 1 = \frac{R + r L}{r x}$$ $$x = \frac{R + r L}{r (1 + P/Q)} = \frac{R + r L}{r \cdot \frac{P+Q}{Q}} = \frac{Q(R + r L)}{r(P+Q)}$$ Since $rL = R_{cable}$ is the total loop resistance: $$x = \frac{Q(R + rL)}{r(P+Q)} = \frac{R + rL}{r} \cdot \frac{Q}{P+Q}$$ The **simpler form** often quoted: $$\boxed{x = \frac{Q}{P+Q} \cdot 2L \quad \text{(when } R \text{ is set to zero, or after adjustment)}}$$ > **Real Use:** Murray loop test gives **distance to fault**, then the exact spot is pinpointed using acoustic or electromagnetic detectors walking along the cable route. --- ## 3.5 DERIVATION: Varley Loop Test The Varley loop test is a **modification of Murray's test** used when the faulty cable's total resistance is **unknown** (e.g., when no identical sound cable is available). ### Working Equation The bridge is balanced with a **known fixed resistance** $R_1$ in series with the loop: $$\frac{P}{Q} = \frac{R_1 + r(L-x) + r(L+x)}{R_3 + rx} = \frac{R_1 + 2rL - rx}{R_3 + rx}$$ Solving for $x$: $$P(R_3 + rx) = Q(R_1 + 2rL - rx)$$ $$PR_3 + Prx = QR_1 + 2QrL - Qrx$$ $$x(Pr + Qr) = QR_1 + 2QrL - PR_3$$ $$x = \frac{QR_1 + 2QrL - PR_3}{r(P+Q)}$$ In the **second (confirmatory) balance**, switch connections and balance with $R_2$: $$x = \frac{QR_2 - PR_3}{r(P+Q)} \quad \text{(second balance)}$$ > **Note:** The Varley test is performed **twice** — once with and once without polarity reversal — to confirm the result and remove lead-resistance errors. --- ## 3.6 DERIVATION: Dielectric Loss in a Cable Dielectric loss per phase per unit length: $$P = \omega C V^2 \tan\delta$$ where: - $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency - $C$ is the capacitance per phase - $V$ is the phase voltage - $\tan\delta$ is the dielectric loss tangent (e.g., **0.003 to 0.008** for XLPE) > **For 3-phase:** Total loss = $3 \omega C_n V_{phase}^2 \tan\delta$ # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Diagram 1: Cross-Sectional Architecture of a 3-Core Belted Cable ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Conductor - Copper or Aluminum
Stranded for Flexibility] --> B[Conductor Screen
Semiconducting Layer] B --> C[Insulation
Impregnated Paper or XLPE] C --> D[Insulation Screen
Semiconducting + Metallic Braid] D --> E[Metallic Sheath
Lead or Aluminum Alloy] E --> F[Bedding Layer
Hessian with Bitumen] F --> G[Armour Layer
Steel Wires or Tapes] G --> H[Outer Serving
PVC or Polythene Jacket] P1[Core 1 with Insulation] --> I[Belt Insulation
Common Paper Wrapping] P2[Core 2 with Insulation] --> I P3[Core 3 with Insulation] --> I I --> D ``` > [!NOTE] > **Belted cable** — All three cores are wrapped with a **common belt of insulation** before the sheath is applied. The **disadvantage** is that the electrical stresses in the belt insulation between cores are tangential and create void formation in service, leading to failure above **33 kV**. --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Electric Stress Distribution in a Single-Core Cable ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Stress_Distribution A[Conductor Surface
g_max occurs here] -->|1/r decay| B[Middle of Dielectric] B -->|continued decay| C[Sheath Inner Surface
g_min occurs here] end A -.->|Highest stress| D[Breakdown risk zone] C -.->|Lowest stress| E[Dielectric underutilized] style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff style B fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005 style C fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44 style D fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff style E fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44 ``` **Key Insight:** The stress at the conductor is $r_2/r_1$ times that at the sheath. The **insulation is wasted** near the sheath because it operates at less than half its breakdown strength. --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Capacitance Grading Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Conductor r1] --> B[Layer 1
High Permittivity eps1
Permittivity = eps1] B --> C[Layer 2
Medium Permittivity eps2
Permittivity = eps2] C --> D[Layer 3
Low Permittivity eps3
Permittivity = eps3] D --> E[Outer Sheath r_n] F[Condition: eps1 r1 = eps2 r2 = eps3 r3] -.-> A F -.-> B F -.-> C F -.-> D style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff style B fill:#ff922b,stroke:#d9480f,color:#fff style C fill:#fcc419,stroke:#f08c00 style D fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44 style E fill:#339af0,stroke:#1864ab,color:#fff ``` > **Working:** Each layer has $\varepsilon_i r_i = \text{constant}$, ensuring **uniform stress** across all layers. The high-$\varepsilon$ material is placed **near the conductor** where the field would otherwise be strongest. --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Intersheath Grading Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Conductor at Potential V
radius r1] -->|Region 1 stress g1| B[Intersheath 1
Floating Potential V1
radius r2] B -->|Region 2 stress g2| C[Intersheath 2
Floating Potential V2
radius r3] C -->|Region 3 stress g3| D[Outer Sheath at 0 V
radius r4] E[Condition: g1 r1 = g2 r2 = g3 r3] -.-> A E -.-> B E -.-> C style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff style B fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005 style C fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005 style D fill:#339af0,stroke:#1864ab,color:#fff ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note:** Intersheaths are **NOT externally connected** — they "float" at self-induced potentials. Adding more intersheaths **equalizes** stress but does **not reduce** $g_{max}$ — the maximum stress depends only on $r_1$ and $r_{n+1}$. --- ## 4.5 Diagram 5: Murray Loop Test Circuit ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Murray_Bridge V[Battery] --> R1[Resistor P] V --> R2[Resistor Q] R1 --> J[Galvanometer Junction] R2 --> J J --> R3[Variable Resistor R] R3 --> G[Galvanometer] G --> M[Mid-point] end subgraph Cable_Loop X1[Faulty Cable
Length L
Fault at distance x] --> X2 X2 --> Y1[Healthy Return Cable
Length L] Y1 --> M end M --> X1 R1 --> X1 R2 --> Y1 ``` --- ## 4.6 Diagram 6: Varley Loop Test Circuit ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Varley_Bridge V[Battery] --> P1[Resistor P] V --> P2[Resistor Q] P1 --> J1[Junction A] P2 --> J2[Junction B] J1 --> R1[Known Resistance R1] J2 --> R3[Variable Resistance R3] R1 --> G[Galvanometer] R3 --> G end subgraph Faulty_Cable G --> FC[Faulty Cable of Unknown Total Resistance
Fault at distance x from test end] end style V fill:#339af0,stroke:#1864ab,color:#fff style FC fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff ``` > **Varley vs Murray:** Varley is used when **no identical healthy cable** is available, and uses a **known resistor** $R_1$ instead. The test is run **twice** (with and without polarity) to eliminate lead resistance errors. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **Define grading of cables. Why is it necessary?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Grading of cables is the technique of achieving **uniform electric stress distribution** throughout the dielectric of a cable, thereby maximizing the use of insulation and increasing the safe working voltage of the cable. It is **necessary** because in a single-core cable with uniform dielectric, the electric stress is **inversely proportional to the radius** ($g \propto 1/r$), resulting in $g_{max}$ at the conductor surface (where $r$ is minimum) and $g_{min}$ at the sheath. The stress ratio $g_{max}/g_{min} = r_2/r_1$ can be as high as **3 to 4**, which means a large portion of the dielectric is understressed. Grading eliminates this inefficiency through **(i) capacitance (dielectric) grading** and **(ii) intersheath grading**. > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **What is the significance of the ratio $r_2/r_1 = e$ in cable design?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The ratio $r_2/r_1 = e \approx 2.718$ corresponds to the **most economical cable design**, where the volume of dielectric material used is **minimum for a given operating voltage and breakdown strength**. At this ratio, the maximum electric stress $g_{max}$ is at its minimum possible value: $$g_{max,min} = \frac{V e}{r_2}$$ Any deviation from this ratio — either larger or smaller $r_2/r_1$ — increases the dielectric volume required, raising cable cost and weight. This principle is used in practice to **optimize insulation thickness** in HV cable manufacture. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question has an **internal choice**. You must answer EITHER **(a) OR (b)**, not both. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the electric stress at a point in the dielectric of a single-core cable. Hence obtain the ratio of maximum to minimum stress. **[7 Marks]** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Statement of the problem:** Consider a single-core cable with inner conductor radius $r_1$, outer sheath inner radius $r_2$, and a uniform dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$. The conductor carries charge $Q$ coulombs per metre length. **Step 2 — Apply Gauss's theorem at radius $r$ ($r_1 \le r \le r_2$):** For a cylindrical Gaussian surface of length $L$: $$\oint \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{A} = Q \cdot L \Rightarrow D(r) \cdot 2\pi r L = QL$$ $$D(r) = \frac{Q}{2\pi r}$$ **Step 3 — Electric field intensity (stress):** $$g(r) = \frac{D(r)}{\varepsilon} = \frac{Q}{2\pi\varepsilon r} \quad \text{V/m} \quad \cdots (i)$$ **Step 4 — Integrate to obtain operating voltage:** $$V = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} g(r) dr = \frac{Q}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) \quad \cdots (ii)$$ **Step 5 — Eliminate $Q$ from (i) and (ii):** $$Q = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon V}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$ $$g(r) = \frac{V}{r\ln(r_2/r_1)} \quad \cdots (iii)$$ **Step 6 — Maximum stress** (at $r = r_1$): $$g_{max} = \frac{V}{r_1\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$ **Step 7 — Minimum stress** (at $r = r_2$): $$g_{min} = \frac{V}{r_2\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$ **Step 8 — Ratio:** $$\frac{g_{max}}{g_{min}} = \frac{r_2}{r_1}$$ > **Conclusion:** The stress ratio equals the radius ratio, which for a typical cable is between 3 and 5. This non-uniformity motivates the use of **grading** techniques. **\[7 marks\]** --- **(b)** A single-core cable has a conductor of diameter **2 cm** and a sheath of inner diameter **5.3 cm**. The dielectric has a relative permittivity of **3.6**. If the cable operates at **11 kV, 50 Hz**, find: 1. The maximum and minimum electric stress. **[4 Marks]** 2. The capacitance of the cable per km. **[2 Marks]** 3. The charging current per km. **[1 Mark]** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Given data:** - $r_1 = 1\,\text{cm} = 0.01\,\text{m}$ - $r_2 = 5.3/2 = 2.65\,\text{cm} = 0.0265\,\text{m}$ - $\varepsilon_r = 3.6$ - $V = 11\,\text{kV} = 11{,}000\,\text{V}$ - $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$, $\omega = 2\pi \cdot 50 = 314.16\,\text{rad/s}$ **Step 1 — Compute $\ln(r_2/r_1)$:** $$\ln(r_2/r_1) = \ln(2.65/1.0) = \ln(2.65) = 0.9746$$ **Step 2 — Maximum stress:** $$g_{max} = \frac{11{,}000}{0.01 \cdot 0.9746} = \frac{11{,}000}{0.009746} \approx 11.29 \times 10^5\,\text{V/m}$$ $$\boxed{g_{max} \approx 11.29\,\text{kV/mm} = 112.9\,\text{kV/cm}}$$ **Step 3 — Minimum stress:** $$g_{min} = \frac{11{,}000}{0.0265 \cdot 0.9746} = \frac{11{,}000}{0.02583} \approx 4.26 \times 10^5\,\text{V/m}$$ $$\boxed{g_{min} \approx 4.26\,\text{kV/mm} = 42.6\,\text{kV/cm}}$$ **Step 4 — Capacitance per km:** $$C = \frac{2\pi \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 3.6}{0.9746} \times 1000\,\text{m}$$ $$C = \frac{2\pi \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 3.6}{0.9746} \cdot 1000$$ $$C = \frac{2.0026 \times 10^{-10}}{0.9746} \cdot 1000 \approx 2.055 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{F/km}$$ $$\boxed{C \approx 0.2055\,\mu\text{F/km}}$$ **Step 5 — Charging current per km:** $$I_C = \omega C V = 314.16 \cdot 0.2055 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 11{,}000$$ $$\boxed{I_C \approx 0.710\,\text{A per km}}$$ > **\[Total 7 marks\]** --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (Internal Choice Alternative) **(a)** Explain the construction of an H-type (Höchstädter) cable with a neat sketch. How does it differ from a belted cable? **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **H-type cable construction (Höchstädter cable, 1914):** The H-type cable is a special construction for voltages above **22 kV** (typically **33 kV to 66 kV**). It is built as follows: 1. Each core is **individually screened** with a metallized paper (usually **perforated copper tape**) at earth potential. 2. The three screened cores are then laid together and a **common metallic sheath** (lead) is applied. 3. **No common "belt" insulation** is used — each core has its own complete insulation system. **Differences from belted cable:** | Feature | Belted Cable | H-Type Cable | |---|---|---| | Belt insulation | Common belt over all 3 cores | **No common belt** | | Voltage limit | **Up to 11 kV** (max 22 kV) | **33 to 66 kV** | | Electric stress | Tangential stress in belt (void formation) | **Radial stress only** — no tangential | | Core screening | **None** | **Each core individually screened** | | Reliability at HV | Low (belt insulation fails) | High | | Cost | Lower | Higher | **Why H-type is better:** In belted cables above 22 kV, the **tangential electrical stress** in the belt insulation causes **void formation** and eventual breakdown. The H-type eliminates tangential stress by **individually screening** each core, so all stress is **radial** — aligned with the insulation's strongest direction. > **\[7 marks\]** --- **(b)** A **3-core belted cable** has conductors of diameter **2.5 cm** spaced at **5.5 cm centres**. The dielectric has $\varepsilon_r = 3$ and a thickness of **1.2 cm** between conductors and sheath. Find: 1. The capacitance of each conductor to neutral. **[4 Marks]** 2. The charging kVAR per km per phase. **[3 Marks]** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Given data:** - Conductor radius $r_1 = 1.25\,\text{cm}$ - Spacing $d = 5.5\,\text{cm}$ - Belt thickness (conductor to sheath) $R - r_1 = 1.2\,\text{cm}$, so $R = 2.45\,\text{cm}$ - $\varepsilon_r = 3$, $V$ not given — assume **11 kV, 50 Hz** **Step 1 — Use the standard empirical formula for 3-core belted cable:** For a 3-core belted cable, the capacitance to neutral is: $$C_n = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln\!\left[\frac{d^3 - 0.75 d \, r_1^2 - 0.25 r_1^3 \cdot 0.77}{r_1^2 \cdot d}\right]} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{geometric correction}}$$ Using the **simplified empirical formula** commonly used in KTU problems: $$C_n = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{d}{r_1} \cdot \dfrac{R^6 - d^3 r_1^3}{R^6 + d^3 r_1^3}\right]^{1/3}}$$ Hmm, this is getting complex. Let me use the **Simon-type formula** most often used in KTU textbooks: $$C_n = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln\!\left[\frac{d}{r_1} \cdot \frac{\sqrt[3]{(R^2 - d^2/3)^3 + (R^2 \cdot 0.5d^2)^{1.5}}}{\sqrt[3]{(R^2 - d^2/3)^3 - (R^2 \cdot 0.5d^2)^{1.5}}}\right]}$$ For exam purposes, a **simpler Russell formula** is acceptable: $$C_n \approx \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln\!\left[\frac{d \sqrt{3}}{r_1} \right]} \quad \text{(approximate, neglecting proximity)}$$ Plugging in: $$\ln\!\left[\frac{5.5 \cdot 1.732}{1.25}\right] = \ln\!\left[\frac{9.526}{1.25}\right] = \ln(7.621) = 2.030$$ $$C_n = \frac{2\pi \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 3}{2.030} = \frac{1.668 \times 10^{-10}}{2.030}$$ $$C_n = 8.216 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{F/m} = 0.0822\,\mu\text{F/km}$$ **Step 2 — Charging kVAR per phase per km:** Phase voltage: $V_{ph} = 11{,}000/\sqrt{3} = 6350\,\text{V}$ $$Q_{c} = \omega C_n V_{ph}^2 = 314.16 \cdot 0.0822 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (6350)^2$$ $$Q_c = 314.16 \cdot 0.0822 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 4.032 \times 10^7$$ $$Q_c \approx 1041\,\text{VAR/km/phase} \approx 1.041\,\text{kVAR/km/phase}$$ $$\boxed{C_n \approx 0.082\,\mu\text{F/km} \quad \text{and} \quad Q_c \approx 1.04\,\text{kVAR/km/phase}}$$ > **\[Total 7 marks\]** --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Examiner's Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Students often plug cm into formulas that need metres. **Always convert to SI units first.** > 2. **Mixing peak and RMS voltages:** Use RMS values for cable ratings. $V_{peak} = \sqrt{2} V_{rms}$ is for breakdown, not operating. > 3. **Assuming $g_{min} = 0$ in Murray loop test:** The minimum stress is **not zero** — it occurs at the sheath, with a finite value $V / [r_2 \ln(r_2/r_1)]$. > 4. **Incorrect intersheath stress formula:** Many students write $g = V/(nr_1 \ln(r_2/r_1))$ — this is **wrong**. The correct form is $g_1 = V/[r_1 \ln(r_{n+1}/r_1)]$. > 5. **Skipping steps in derivations:** KTU evaluators award marks for **every step** shown. A single jump loses 1–2 marks. > 6. **Confusing capacitance per phase with total 3-phase capacitance.** Always clarify: **per phase** is the standard. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this checklist and revise one hour before the exam.** - [x] **Cable definition:** Insulated underground power conductor with concentric layers. - [x] **Construction order:** Conductor → Conductor screen → Insulation → Insulation screen → Sheath → Bedding → Armour → Serving. - [x] **Electric stress at radius $r$:** $g(r) = V / [r \ln(r_2/r_1)]$. - [x] **Maximum stress location:** Conductor surface, $r = r_1$. - [x] **Minimum stress location:** Sheath inner surface, $r = r_2$. - [x] **Stress ratio:** $g_{max}/g_{min} = r_2/r_1$. - [x] **Optimum ratio:** $r_2/r_1 = e \approx 2.718$. - [x] **Capacitance (single-core):** $C = 2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r / \ln(r_2/r_1)$ in F/m. - [x] **Capacitance grading condition:** $\varepsilon_1 r_1 = \varepsilon_2 r_2 = \varepsilon_3 r_3 = \dots$ - [x] **Intersheath grading stress:** $g_1 = V / [r_1 \ln(r_{n+1}/r_1)]$ — independent of number of intersheaths. - [x] **Belted cable:** Voltage limit **≤ 22 kV**, common belt, has tangential stress issues. - [x] **H-type cable:** Individual screening, **up to 66 kV**, only radial stress. - [x] **SL-type cable:** Separate lead sheath per core, **up to 132 kV**, eliminates all inter-core stress. - [x] **Murray loop test:** Uses identical healthy cable, gives $x = [Q/(P+Q)] \cdot 2L$. - [x] **Varley loop test:** Uses known resistor $R_1$, run twice with reversed polarity. - [x] **Dielectric loss formula:** $P = \omega C V^2 \tan\delta$ W/phase. - [x] **Optimum $r_2/r_1$ for minimum dielectric:** $e = 2.718$. - [x] **Modern cables (XLPE, EPR):** Lower $\tan\delta$, higher current rating, no oil leaks. - [x] **HVDC cables:** Use **mass-impregnated** or **extruded DC** insulation, special design for space charges. - [x] **Submarine cables:** Double armour, water-blocking compound, factory joint only. > **Module 4 is complete. Proceed to Module 5: Overvoltages, Transients, and Insulation Coordination.**

topics.title: Insulation Systems for AC Voltages - Cables, Bushings and Transformers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Insulation Systems for AC Voltages - Cables, Bushings and Transformers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Insulation System for AC Voltages?

In high-voltage (HV) engineering, an **insulation system** is a carefully engineered combination of dielectric materials (solids, liquids, gases, or composites) that prevents the uncontrolled flow of electric current between conductors operating at different potentials, and from conductors to ground. For AC voltages, these systems must withstand not only the peak voltage but also the transient overvoltages (switching surges, lightning impulses), continuous AC stress, thermal aging, and environmental degradation over decades of service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> An *insulation system* for AC voltages is the coordinated arrangement of dielectric media — solid (paper, XLPE, epoxy, pressboard), liquid (mineral oil, ester fluids), and gaseous (SF₆, N₂, air) — used in HV equipment such as cables, bushings, and transformers, designed such that the **electric stress distribution remains within safe dielectric limits** under both steady-state and transient operating conditions.

The three principal equipment classes where this matters most are:

| Equipment | Primary Function | Insulation Challenge |
|---|---|---|
| **HV Power Cables** | Underground/submarine power transmission | Non-uniform field in cylindrical geometry; high stress at conductor screen |
| **Bushings** | Safe passage of conductor through grounded tank/wall | Combined radial + axial field; outdoor pollution creepage |
| **Power Transformers** | Step-up/step-down voltage | Oil–paper composite; thermal + electrical + mechanical stress |
| Operating voltage reference | **11 kV, 33 kV, 66 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV** (Indian grid classes) | — |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pressure Vessel & Tapering Pipe"

Imagine a **high-pressure water pipeline** carrying fluid from a small inlet pipe into a much larger outlet pipe.

- At the **narrow end (inlet)**, the fluid velocity and pressure on the pipe wall are enormous — it is the point most likely to fail.
- At the **wide end (outlet)**, the pressure is much lower and the wall is less stressed.

An HV cable behaves *exactly* the same way electrically:

- The **inner conductor (small radius r₁)** corresponds to the narrow inlet. **Electric stress (g) is maximum here** — the dielectric is most likely to break down right at the conductor surface.
- The **outer sheath (large radius r₂)** corresponds to the wide outlet. Stress here is much lower.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as a pipeline engineer would either (a) make the inlet wall thicker, or (b) gradually taper the pipe to smooth pressure variation, an HV insulation engineer uses **stress grading** techniques to flatten the electric stress profile. The two main techniques are **Capacitance Grading** and **Intersheath Grading**.

## 1.3 Why Insulation Coordination is Critical for AC

Unlike DC, AC insulation must handle:

1. **Peak voltage stress** = √2 × V_rms (continuous)
2. **Polarity reversals** every half-cycle → interfacial (tangential) stress at oil–paper boundaries
3. **Dielectric losses** (tan δ) causing internal heating
4. **Capacitive charging currents** in long cables
5. **Partial discharge (PD) inception** at voids, delaminations, or sharp edges
6. **Thermal cycling** causing differential expansion and bubble formation
7. **Transient overvoltages** — lightning impulse (1.2/50 μs) and switching impulse (250/2500 μs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Constant Reminders (Bold):**
> - Permittivity of free space: **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
> - Relative permittivity of XLPE: **εᵣ ≈ 2.3**
> - Relative permittivity of oil-impregnated paper: **εᵣ ≈ 3.5**
> - Relative permittivity of transformer mineral oil: **εᵣ ≈ 2.2**
> - Dielectric strength of transformer oil (1 mm gap): **≈ 30–40 kV (rms)**
> - Dielectric strength of XLPE: **≈ 30–50 kV/mm**
> - Dielectric strength of SF₆ at 0.4 MPa: **≈ 8–10 kV/mm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electric Stress Distribution in a Single-Core Cable

For a coaxial cylindrical cable with inner radius **r₁** and outer sheath radius **r₂**, carrying voltage **V** between conductor and sheath, the field is purely radial. By applying Gauss's law to a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius **r** and length **L**:

$$D = \frac{Q}{2\pi r L}, \quad E(r) = \frac{D}{\varepsilon} = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon L \cdot r}$$

The potential difference between conductor and sheath is obtained by integration:

$$V = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} E(r)\, dr = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon L} \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$

Hence the **electric stress (field intensity) at any radius r** is:

$$g(r) = \frac{V}{r \cdot \ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The stress is **inversely proportional to r** — hence maximum at the conductor surface (r = r₁) and minimum at the sheath (r = r₂).

### 2.1.1 Maximum and Minimum Stress

**Maximum stress** (at conductor surface, r = r₁):

$$g_{max} = \frac{V}{r_1 \cdot \ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

**Minimum stress** (at sheath, r = r₂):

$$g_{min} = \frac{V}{r_2 \cdot \ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

**Stress ratio:**

$$\frac{g_{max}}{g_{min}} = \frac{r_2}{r_1}$$

This ratio is the **insulation utilization factor inverse** — the larger this ratio, the more non-uniform the stress, and the more insulation material is wasted.

## 2.2 Methods of Stress Control in Cables

### 2.2.1 Capacitance Grading (Homogeneous Dielectric with Variable ε)

In a single dielectric, the stress cannot be flattened (it always follows 1/r). To flatten it, **the dielectric constant is varied radially** so that the permittivity at each radius r is:

$$\varepsilon(r) = \frac{k}{r}$$

where k is a constant chosen so that the integrated voltage gives the required V. The result is that **stress becomes constant** throughout:

$$g = \frac{V}{r_2 - r_1}$$

In practice, ε(r) = k/r is approximated using **N concentric layers**, each with a constant permittivity **εₙ**. The radius of the n-th interface is:

$$r_n = r_1 \exp\!\left[\frac{n}{N} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Drawback of capacitance grading:** It is difficult to obtain a continuum of dielectric constants; it requires many thin layers with carefully graded ε — economically expensive. Therefore it is used in **bushings (condenser bushings)**, not commonly in cables.

### 2.2.2 Intersheath Grading (Multiple Conductive Screens)

The cable is divided into **N sections** by inserting (N − 1) thin metallic **intersheaths** between the conductor and the outer sheath. Each intersheath is held at a specific intermediate potential, designed so that the stress in each section is uniform and equal to g_max.

For **N intersheaths**, the total system is divided into **N + 1** sub-cables. The radius of the n-th intersheath (0-indexed, with r₀ = r₁) is:

$$r_n = r_1 \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{n}{N+1} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)\right]$$

The maximum stress (same in every sub-section) is:

$$g_{max} = \frac{V_{section}}{(r_n - r_{n-1})\cdot \text{(geometric term)}}$$

A more compact working expression: if voltage is V and there are **N intersheaths** (so N+1 layers in series, each carrying V/(N+1)):

$$g_{max} = \frac{V}{(N+1)\, r_1 \cdot \ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison with no intersheath (homogeneous cable):** Adding N intersheaths reduces g_max by a factor of (N+1). However, it does NOT change g_min by the same amount, so the **stress uniformity improves dramatically**.

## 2.3 Bushing Insulation Systems

A **bushing** is a specialized insulator that allows a HV conductor to pass safely through a grounded barrier (transformer tank, wall, circuit-breaker enclosure). It must handle:

- **Radial stress** (between conductor and flange/flange ground)
- **Axial (tangential) stress** along the creepage surface (especially in outdoor bushings)
- **Pollution performance** (creepage distance)

### 2.3.1 Types of Bushings

| Type | Construction | Voltage Class |
|---|---|---|
| **Porcelain / Resin-bonded paper (RBP)** | Solid insulation, no internal grading | ≤ 36 kV |
| **Oil-impregnated paper (OIP)** | Paper layers in oil, with conductive foils | 36–245 kV |
| **Resin-impregnated paper (RIP)** | Epoxy-impregnated crepe paper, dry type | 36–245 kV |
| **Resin-impregnated synthetics (RIS)** | Fiberglass + resin, dry, explosion-proof | 245–800 kV |
| **SF₆ / Gas bushings** | For GIS applications | 72.5–800 kV |
| **Capacitance-graded (Condenser) bushing** | OIP/RIP with inserted metal foils at calculated radii | 36–800 kV |

### 2.3.2 Condenser (Capacitance-Graded) Bushing

A **condenser bushing** has (N − 1) concentric cylindrical metal foils embedded in the insulation, each at a calculated radius:

$$r_n = r_1 \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{n}{N-1} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)\right]$$

Each foil is sized to act as a **cylindrical capacitor plate**, and the foils in series form a capacitive voltage divider. The result is that:

- The **radial stress** is forced to be equal in every layer.
- The **axial (tangential) stress** is also controlled by the foil lengths (the foils are progressively shortened toward the flange end).

> [!NOTE]
> The **specific capacitance** of the bushing is the **series equivalent capacitance** per unit length: C = 2π ε₀ εᵣ / ln(r₂/r₁), and this is matched at every foil step for equal voltage sharing.

### 2.3.3 Creepage Distance

For outdoor bushings, the **specific creepage distance** (mm/kV of phase-to-earth voltage) is chosen based on pollution level:

| Pollution Level | Min. Specific Creepage (mm/kV) |
|---|---|
| Light | 16 |
| Medium | 20 |
| Heavy | 25 |
| Very Heavy | 31 |

## 2.4 Transformer Insulation Systems

A power transformer's insulation is a **composite oil–paper (or oil–pressboard) system**. The insulation is conventionally divided into:

1. **Major insulation** — between windings, and between windings and core/tank (oil ducts + pressboard barriers).
2. **Minor insulation** — between turns, between turns and discs, within a single winding (paper covering on conductors, oil-impregnated paper, enamel).

### 2.4.1 Oil–Paper Dielectric

- **Transformer mineral oil** provides insulation, cooling, and arc-quenching. Typical breakdown strength (1 mm gap, IEC 60156): **≥ 30 kV (rms), 50 Hz**.
- **Kraft paper / pressboard** in oil forms a **composite dielectric** with very high dielectric strength (typical: **≈ 50–60 kV/mm for paper, ≈ 35–40 kV/mm for pressboard**).
- The interface forms **interfacial polarization** → important under impulse voltages.

### 2.4.2 Creepage and Clearance in Transformers

- **Phase-to-phase clearance** in air (inside tank, above oil): typically **25–50 mm** for 11 kV class.
- **Creepage distance** along pressboard surfaces: defined as **mm/kV** of highest system voltage, with minimum values (e.g., **25 mm/kV for heavily polluted outdoor**).

### 2.4.3 Electrostatic Shield (Stress Grading Ring)

At the **line-end of HV windings**, the first few turns experience very high impulse stress. To control this:

- A **static shield (stress grading ring)** — a small metal ring lined with pressboard — is placed at the line end.
- It redistributes the impulse stress so that the **initial voltage distribution** is close to the **final (resistive) distribution**, preventing inter-turn breakdown.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without the stress ring, the impulse voltage concentrates in the first 5–10% of the winding turns, leading to insulation failure. This is a classic KTU 12-mark question topic.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for the Module 4 part of the KTU ESE. Commit it to memory.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stress in single-core cable | $g(r) = \dfrac{V}{r\,\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | V/m | Inversely proportional to r |
| 2 | Max stress (at r₁) | $g_{max} = \dfrac{V}{r_1\,\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | V/m | At conductor surface |
| 3 | Min stress (at r₂) | $g_{min} = \dfrac{V}{r_2\,\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | V/m | At outer sheath |
| 4 | Stress ratio | $g_{max}/g_{min} = r_2/r_1$ | — | Indicates non-uniformity |
| 5 | Capacitance per unit length (cable) | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | F/m | Geometric capacitance |
| 6 | Capacitance grading radius (n-th layer) | $r_n = r_1\exp\!\left[\dfrac{n}{N}\ln\!\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right]$ | m | For N layers, n = 0…N |
| 7 | Intersheath radius (n-th sheath) | $r_n = r_1\exp\!\left[\dfrac{n}{N+1}\ln\!\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right]$ | m | N intersheaths → N+1 sections |
| 8 | g_max with N intersheaths | $g_{max} = \dfrac{V}{(N+1)\,r_1\,\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | V/m | Reduced by factor (N+1) |
| 9 | Condenser bushing foil radius | $r_n = r_1\exp\!\left[\dfrac{n}{N-1}\ln\!\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right]$ | m | N-1 foils |
| 10 | Creepage distance | $L_{creep} = k \cdot V_{LL}/\sqrt{3}$ | mm | k in mm/kV from pollution table |
| 11 | Breakdown strength (oil, 1 mm) | E_bd ≈ 30–40 kV (rms) | — | IEC 60156 standard gap |
| 12 | Dielectric loss (per unit volume) | $p = \omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r\tan\delta \cdot E^2$ | W/m³ | Determines heating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility (real-world):** These formulas are used daily in cable design (Prysmian, Nexans), bushing design (ABB, Siemens, GE), and transformer design (BHEL, Hitachi Energy, Schneider). They directly determine the **thickness, number of layers, and material choice** for a given voltage class.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stress Distribution in a Single-Core Cable

**Statement to prove:** Show that for a single-core cable of inner radius r₁ and outer radius r₂ carrying voltage V between conductor and sheath, the electric stress at any radius r is g(r) = V / [r · ln(r₂/r₁)].

**Setup:** Assume a coaxial geometry. The conductor is at potential V (relative to sheath at 0 V). The dielectric has permittivity ε = ε₀εᵣ. The field is purely radial and depends only on r (cylindrical symmetry).

**Step 1 — Apply Gauss's Law to a cylindrical surface of radius r and length L:**

The enclosed charge per unit length is λ = Q/L. By Gauss's law for a cylinder:

$$\oint \vec{D}\cdot d\vec{A} = Q_{enc}$$

Since D is radial and uniform over the cylindrical surface:

$$D \cdot (2\pi r L) = Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \frac{Q}{2\pi r L}$$

**Step 2 — Relate D to electric field E:**

$$E(r) = \frac{D}{\varepsilon} = \frac{Q}{2\pi\varepsilon L r}$$

**Step 3 — Express the potential V as a line integral of the field from r₁ to r₂:**

The potential difference between the inner conductor (higher potential) and the outer sheath (lower potential) is:

$$V = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} E(r)\, dr$$

**Step 4 — Substitute E(r) and integrate:**

$$V = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{Q}{2\pi\varepsilon L r}\, dr = \frac{Q}{2\pi\varepsilon L} \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r}$$

$$V = \frac{Q}{2\pi\varepsilon L} \Big[\ln r\Big]_{r_1}^{r_2} = \frac{Q}{2\pi\varepsilon L}\ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the constant Q/(2πεL):**

$$\frac{Q}{2\pi\varepsilon L} = \frac{V}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into the expression for E(r) to get the field at any radius:**

$$E(r) = \frac{V}{r\,\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

Since the electric stress g(r) is defined as the magnitude of E(r), the result is:

$$g(r) = \frac{V}{r\,\ln(r_2/r_1)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate at the boundaries:**

- At r = r₁ (conductor surface): $g_{max} = \dfrac{V}{r_1 \ln(r_2/r_1)}$
- At r = r₂ (sheath): $g_{min} = \dfrac{V}{r_2 \ln(r_2/r_1)}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** Because of the 1/r dependence, the conductor–insulation interface is the **electrically weakest point** in a single-dielectric cable.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Capacitance-Graded Bushing Radius Formula

**Statement to prove:** For a condenser bushing with N − 1 metal foils inserted between inner radius r₁ and outer radius r₂, show that the n-th foil must be placed at:

$$r_n = r_1 \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{n}{N-1}\ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)\right]$$

**Setup:** A condenser bushing is a coaxial cylinder with N − 1 concentric cylindrical foils, creating **N − 1 insulating layers** of different thicknesses but equal radial capacitance per unit axial length.

**Step 1 — Capacitance per unit axial length of a coaxial layer:**

For a cylindrical capacitor with inner radius a, outer radius b, and permittivity ε:

$$C = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$$

**Step 2 — Equal capacitance condition:**

For the bushing to have **equal radial stress** in every layer, every layer must have the **same capacitance per unit length**:

$$C_1 = C_2 = \cdots = C_N \equiv C_0$$

This means for the n-th layer between r_{n−1} and r_n:

$$\frac{\ln(r_n/r_{n-1})}{\varepsilon_n} = \text{constant}$$

For homogeneous insulation (ε_n = ε for all n), the geometric ratio must be the same:

$$\frac{r_1}{r_0} = \frac{r_2}{r_1} = \cdots = \frac{r_N}{r_{N-1}} = k$$

where r₀ = r₁ (inner conductor) and r_N = r₂ (outer sheath). So each layer has the same **geometric ratio** k = (r₂/r₁)^{1/N}.

**Step 3 — Inductive expression for the n-th foil radius:**

Since each successive radius is k times the previous one:

$$r_n = r_1 \cdot k^n = r_1 \cdot \left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^{n/N}$$

Rewriting using the property a^b = exp(b ln a):

$$r_n = r_1 \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{n}{N}\ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)\right] \qquad \blacksquare$$

(Note: with N foils = N+1 interfaces including inner and outer; for **N − 1 foils** creating **N layers**, substitute N → N − 1.)

**Step 4 — Voltage across each layer:**

Because each layer has the same capacitance C₀, and the foils are connected in series, the voltage divides equally. With applied voltage V across N layers, each layer gets V/N, and the radial stress in each layer is:

$$g_n = \frac{V/N}{r_{n+1} - r_n} \approx \frac{V/N}{r_1(k-1)} \quad \text{(equal for all } n\text{)}$$

This confirms **uniform radial stress** — the design goal of capacitance grading.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Intersheath Grading — Reduced Maximum Stress

**Statement to prove:** For a cable with N intersheaths (creating N + 1 sub-cables in series), show that the maximum stress is reduced by a factor of (N + 1) compared to the same cable without intersheaths.

**Setup:** A single-core cable (r₁, r₂, V) without intersheath has g_max,0 = V / [r₁ ln(r₂/r₁)].

Now insert N concentric thin metallic cylindrical screens (intersheaths) at carefully chosen radii such that **each sub-section is identical in field geometry**, but operates at a fraction of the total voltage.

**Step 1 — Choose intersheath radii so that the geometric ratio r_{n+1}/r_n is the same for every sub-section:**

For (N + 1) sub-sections of equal geometric ratio k:

$$k = \left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^{1/(N+1)}$$

The n-th intersheath radius:

$$r_n = r_1 \cdot k^n = r_1 \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{n}{N+1}\ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Voltage per sub-section:**

By symmetry, each sub-section carries an equal fraction V/(N + 1) of the total voltage.

**Step 3 — Maximum stress in sub-section n (occurs at its inner radius r_{n-1}):**

$$g_{max,n} = \frac{V/(N+1)}{r_{n-1}\,\ln(r_n/r_{n-1})} = \frac{V/(N+1)}{r_{n-1}\,\ln k}$$

Since r_{n-1} ≤ r₂ but ln k is small, the worst case is the innermost sub-section, r₀ = r₁:

$$g_{max} = \frac{V/(N+1)}{r_1\,\ln k} = \frac{V/(N+1)}{r_1 \cdot \frac{1}{N+1}\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

**Step 4 — Simplification:**

$$g_{max} = \frac{V}{r_1\,\ln(r_2/r_1)} \cdot \frac{1}{N+1} = \frac{g_{max,0}}{N+1} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical implication:** A 33 kV cable with r₂/r₁ = 3.0 might have g_max,0 ≈ 12 kV/mm without grading. Adding 3 intersheaths (N = 3) reduces g_max to 12/4 = 3 kV/mm — a four-fold reduction. This is why intersheath grading was historically used in **EHV cables (66 kV and above)**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Which Grading Method Wins?

| Criterion | Capacitance Grading | Intersheath Grading | No Grading (Plain) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Radial stress uniformity | Excellent (theoretically constant) | Good (stepwise constant) | Poor (1/r variation) |
| g_max / g_min ratio | ≈ 1 | ≈ 1 (per section) | r₂/r₁ (large) |
| Manufacturing difficulty | Very hard (graded ε needed) | Moderate (thin foils at calculated radii) | Trivial |
| Cost | Highest | Moderate | Lowest |
| Application | Condenser bushings | EHV cables (historic) | LV / MV cables |
| Reliability | High (if graded) | Foil corrosion risk in oil | High |
| KTU exam frequency | Very high | High | Low (basic) |

## 3.5 Sample Numerical — Intersheath Cable Design

**Problem:** A single-core cable of inner radius 10 mm and outer radius 25 mm operates at 66 kV (phase voltage). Design an intersheath grading system with N = 2 intersheaths. Compute (a) intersheath radii, (b) maximum stress, (c) compare with ungraded cable.

**Given:** r₁ = 0.010 m, r₂ = 0.025 m, V = 66 × 10³ / √3 = 38,105 V (phase), N = 2.

**Step (a) — Intersheath radii:**

Geometric ratio: k = (r₂/r₁)^{1/(N+1)} = (25/10)^{1/3} = 2.5^{1/3} = 1.3572

- r₀ = 10.0 mm (inner conductor)
- r₁ = 10.0 × 1.3572 = 13.57 mm (first intersheath)
- r₂ = 13.57 × 1.3572 = 18.43 mm (second intersheath)
- r₃ = 18.43 × 1.3572 = 25.0 mm (outer sheath) ✓

**Step (b) — Maximum stress (graded):**

$$g_{max} = \frac{V}{(N+1)\,r_1\,\ln(r_2/r_1)} = \frac{38105}{3 \times 0.010 \times \ln(2.5)}$$

$$g_{max} = \frac{38105}{0.030 \times 0.9163} = \frac{38105}{0.02749} = 1.386 \times 10^6 \,\text{V/m} = 1.39 \,\text{kV/mm}$$

**Step (c) — Without grading:**

$$g_{max,0} = 3 \times 1.39 = 4.17 \,\text{kV/mm}$$

**Conclusion:** A two-intersheath design reduces the maximum stress by a factor of 3, allowing a thinner dielectric or a higher operating voltage.
