# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the term "stress grading" in the context of HV cables. Name the two principal methods.

**Model Answer:**

> **Stress grading** refers to the technique of redistributing the electric stress along an HV insulation medium so that the maximum stress is reduced and the stress profile becomes more uniform, thereby improving the utilization of the dielectric and reducing the risk of breakdown.
>
> The two principal methods are:
> 1. **Capacitance Grading** — varying the permittivity of the dielectric radially (or inserting metal foils at calculated radii to simulate this), so that g(r) = constant. Used mainly in **condenser-type bushings**.
> 2. **Intersheath Grading** — inserting thin metallic cylindrical screens (intersheaths) at calculated radii between the conductor and the outer sheath, each maintained at a specific intermediate potential, so that each sub-section operates with a lower, uniform stress. Used in **EHV cables**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is a condenser bushing? Why are metal foils embedded in the insulation?

**Model Answer:**

> A **condenser bushing** is a capacitance-graded HV bushing in which the main insulation (oil-impregnated paper, resin-impregnated paper, or epoxy) contains a number of concentric cylindrical **metal foils** placed at carefully calculated radii between the central HV conductor and the grounded outer flange.
>
> The metal foils are embedded to:
> 1. **Equalize the radial electric stress** in every insulating layer, eliminating the natural 1/r stress concentration at the central conductor.
> 2. **Control the axial (tangential) stress** by progressively shortening the foils toward the flange end.
> 3. **Form a capacitive voltage divider** that ensures uniform voltage distribution along the bushing length, suppressing surges and resonances.
> 4. **Reduce the overall physical size** of the bushing for a given voltage class by efficient use of insulation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, every Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Below, two fully-worked alternative questions are provided for the same topic area.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** With a neat sketch, derive an expression for the electric stress in a single-core cable. Discuss **capacitance grading** and **intersheath grading** with relevant expressions. A 33 kV, single-core cable has a conductor radius of 10 mm and a sheath inner radius of 25 mm. Calculate:
(a) The maximum and minimum electric stress in the dielectric. **(7 Marks)**
(b) The radii of two intersheaths and the resulting maximum stress. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution

**Derivation of stress in a single-core cable:**

For a coaxial cable with inner radius r₁, outer radius r₂, conductor at potential V, and sheath at 0 V, applying Gauss's law to a cylindrical surface of radius r and length L:

$$D \cdot 2\pi r L = Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad E(r) = \frac{Q}{2\pi\varepsilon L r}$$

Integrating E(r) from r₁ to r₂ gives V:

$$V = \frac{Q}{2\pi\varepsilon L}\ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad g(r) = \frac{V}{r\,\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

**Part (a) — Maximum and minimum stress (7 marks):**

Given: r₁ = 10 mm = 0.010 m, r₂ = 25 mm = 0.025 m, V = 33 kV (phase) = 33 × 10³ / √3 = 19,053 V.

$$\ln(r_2/r_1) = \ln(2.5) = 0.9163$$

**Maximum stress** (at conductor surface, r = r₁):

$$g_{max} = \frac{19053}{0.010 \times 0.9163} = \frac{19053}{0.009163} = 2.08 \times 10^6 \,\text{V/m} = 2.08 \,\text{kV/mm}$$

**Minimum stress** (at sheath, r = r₂):

$$g_{min} = \frac{19053}{0.025 \times 0.9163} = \frac{19053}{0.02291} = 8.32 \times 10^5 \,\text{V/m} = 0.83 \,\text{kV/mm}$$

**Stress ratio** = g_max / g_min = r₂ / r₁ = 2.5, confirming strong non-uniformity.

**Part (b) — Two intersheaths (N = 2), radii and new g_max (7 marks):**

With N = 2 intersheaths, the cable is divided into N + 1 = 3 sub-cables in series. Geometric ratio:

$$k = \left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^{1/(N+1)} = (2.5)^{1/3} = 1.3572$$

Intersheath radii:

- First intersheath: r₁ = 10.0 × 1.3572 = **13.57 mm**
- Second intersheath: r₂ = 13.57 × 1.3572 = **18.43 mm**

**New maximum stress** (reduced by factor of N + 1 = 3):

$$g_{max,new} = \frac{g_{max,0}}{3} = \frac{2.08}{3} = 0.693 \,\text{kV/mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The new stress (0.69 kV/mm) is **3× lower** than the ungraded stress (2.08 kV/mm) — a striking improvement. The total insulation thickness remains 15 mm, but the **dielectric is utilized 3× more efficiently**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Explain the construction and working of a **condenser (capacitance-graded) bushing** with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for the radius of the n-th metal foil. Why is it preferred for EHV class (≥ 245 kV)? A 245 kV condenser bushing has 6 foils. If the inner conductor radius is 25 mm and the outer grounded flange radius is 200 mm, calculate **(a)** the radius of each foil, and **(b)** the voltage across each insulating layer. **(14 Marks: 7 + 7)**

#### Solution

**Construction and working (7 marks):**

A condenser bushing consists of:
1. A central **copper or aluminum conductor** (rated at full HV).
2. Layers of **oil-impregnated paper (OIP) or resin-impregnated paper (RIP)** wrapped concentrically.
3. **N − 1 thin aluminum foils** inserted at calculated radii, each connected to a **tap lead** that allows individual foil potential monitoring (used for testing).
4. A **grounded metal flange** at the bottom (tank side).
5. An **outer porcelain or composite insulator** with weather sheds for outdoor use, providing creepage distance.

**Working principle:** The foils form a series of coaxial capacitors. By sizing the radius of each foil such that every layer has the same capacitance, the radial stress is equalized and the voltage divides equally. The foils also control axial stress, making the bushing compact, reliable, and PD-free.

**Derivation of foil radius:**

For N − 1 foils (creating N layers), the n-th foil radius is obtained by the equal-capacitance condition:

$$r_n = r_1 \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{n}{N-1}\ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)\right]$$

This ensures every layer has identical geometric ratio k = (r₂/r₁)^{1/N}, so the capacitance and radial stress are equal in every layer.

**Part (a) — Foil radii (7 marks):**

Given: r₁ = 25 mm, r₂ = 200 mm, N − 1 = 6 foils → N = 7 layers. Total applied voltage V = 245 kV / √3 = 141.5 kV (phase).

Geometric ratio per layer:

$$k = \left(\frac{200}{25}\right)^{1/7} = 8^{1/7} = e^{(\ln 8)/7} = e^{0.2979} = 1.347$$

Foil radii (in mm):

- n = 1: r₁ = 25 × 1.347 = 33.68 mm
- n = 2: r₂ = 33.68 × 1.347 = 45.37 mm
- n = 3: r₃ = 45.37 × 1.347 = 61.12 mm
- n = 4: r₄ = 61.12 × 1.347 = 82.33 mm
- n = 5: r₅ = 82.33 × 1.347 = 110.91 mm
- n = 6: r₆ = 110.91 × 1.347 = 149.40 mm
- n = 7: r₇ = 149.40 × 1.347 ≈ 200 mm (outer flange) ✓

**Part (b) — Voltage per layer:**

Since every layer has equal capacitance, the total voltage divides equally:

$$V_{layer} = \frac{V}{N} = \frac{141.5}{7} = 20.21 \,\text{kV (rms)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why EHV bushings (245 kV and above) **always use condenser grading** — without it, the first layer would see the full 141.5 kV, far exceeding its breakdown limit. With grading, each layer sees only ~20 kV.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Module (Read carefully!)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to phase voltage (V/√3):** Many students use line-to-line voltage (e.g., 33 kV) directly in g_max formulas. The KTU answer key ALWAYS uses **phase voltage V_ph = V_LL / √3**. One mark is deducted for this.
>
> 2. **Mixing up capacitance-grading formula and intersheath-grading formula:** The exponent denominator is **N** for capacitance grading but **N + 1** for intersheath grading (because of the off-by-one between "foils" and "sections"). This is the **#1 reason students lose 3–4 marks** in this module.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units:** g_max must be expressed in **kV/mm or V/m** consistently. Mixing units (e.g., V with mm radius instead of m) is an instant 0.5 mark penalty.
>
> 4. **Not drawing the diagram:** A "neat sketch" question without a diagram loses **2 marks** even if the derivation is perfect.
>
> 5. **Missing the static shield (stress grading ring) in transformer answers:** For transformer impulse stress questions, students frequently forget the static shield — losing 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Confusing major vs minor insulation in transformers:** Major = winding-to-winding/core/tank; Minor = inter-turn/inter-disc. Examiners test this distinction deliberately.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this and revise 30 minutes before the exam.**

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- [ ] Single-core cable stress formula: **g(r) = V / [r · ln(r₂/r₁)]**
- [ ] g_max is at the **conductor (r₁)**, g_min at the **sheath (r₂)**
- [ ] Stress ratio g_max / g_min = r₂ / r₁
- [ ] Use **phase voltage (V_LL / √3)** in all stress formulas
- [ ] ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m; ε_r(XLPE) ≈ 2.3; ε_r(mineral oil) ≈ 2.2

### Grading Formulas
- [ ] **Capacitance grading** radius: $r_n = r_1 \exp\!\left[\frac{n}{N}\ln\!\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right]$
- [ ] **Intersheath grading** radius: $r_n = r_1 \exp\!\left[\frac{n}{N+1}\ln\!\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right]$
- [ ] **Condenser bushing** foil radius: $r_n = r_1 \exp\!\left[\frac{n}{N-1}\ln\!\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right]$
- [ ] **N intersheaths reduce g_max by factor (N+1)**

### Equipment-Specific Highlights
- [ ] **Cables:** XLPE for ≤ 400 kV; EHV uses oil-filled (OF) or PPLP (polypropylene laminate paper)
- [ ] **Bushings:** OIP for 36–245 kV; RIP/RIS for higher; SF₆ bushings for GIS
- [ ] **Creepage distance** (mm/kV): Light 16 → Medium 20 → Heavy 25 → Very Heavy 31
- [ ] **Transformers:** Oil–paper composite; static shield (stress grading ring) at HV line end
- [ ] **Major insulation** = winding-to-winding; **Minor insulation** = inter-turn

### Numeric/Design Benchmarks
- [ ] Mineral oil BD strength: **30–40 kV (rms) at 1 mm gap (IEC 60156)**
- [ ] XLPE BD strength: **30–50 kV/mm**
- [ ] SF₆ at 0.4 MPa: **8–10 kV/mm**

### Frequently Missed in Exam
- [ ] Why is the static shield needed? → Flattens impulse voltage distribution in HV winding
- [ ] Why is condenser bushing preferred for EHV? → Equal radial stress, no PD, compact
- [ ] Why intersheath grading not in modern XLPE cables? → Modern XLPE has high ε_r and BD strength; geometry optimized without intersheath
- [ ] What is the most likely breakdown location in a plain cable? → **At the conductor surface (r = r₁)** — always answer this first

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Insulation Systems for AC Voltages.** Cross-reference Module 3 (breakdown mechanisms) for the theory behind BD strength numbers, and Module 5 (testing) for the impulse testing of these very insulation systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Insulation Systems for DC Voltages - Capacitors, HVDC Bushings and Cables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Insulation Systems for DC Voltages - Capacitors, HVDC Bushings and Cables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are DC Insulation Systems?

**Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
DC Insulation Systems refer to the coordinated set of dielectric materials, geometric configurations, and field-grading arrangements specifically engineered to withstand, confine, and direct high-magnitude Direct Current (DC) electric stresses in power transmission (HVDC), energy storage (pulse capacitors), and equipment termination (bushings). Unlike AC systems, the field distribution in a DC insulation system is governed by the **volume resistivity (ρ) / conductivity (σ)** of the dielectric rather than its **permittivity (ε)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The behaviour of dielectrics under DC stress is governed by **steady-state conduction**, not polarization. This single fact is responsible for almost every design difference between an AC insulator and a DC insulator.

### 1.2 Why is DC Insulation Different from AC Insulation? — The Intuitive Analogy

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Plumbing vs. The Tuning Fork"**

Think of an insulation system as a network of pipes connecting two reservoirs at different pressures (the electrodes). Under **AC voltage**, the pressure oscillates so fast that the fluid (displacement current) sloshes back and forth without actually moving forward — so the network behaves like a **tuning fork**, and the field distribution is decided by the **stiffness (permittivity ε)** of each pipe. Under **DC voltage**, the pressure is steady, the fluid actually **flows forward** (conduction current), and the distribution is decided by the **frictional resistance (resistivity ρ)** of each pipe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Engineering Implication:**
> - In **AC**: Field is inversely proportional to permittivity → $\;E_i \propto 1/\varepsilon_i$
> - In **DC**: Field is inversely proportional to resistivity → $\;E_i \propto \rho_i$ (or $\;E_i \propto 1/\sigma_i$)
> - In a **series** dielectric stack, the **highest resistivity layer carries the highest DC field** — this is the opposite of AC where the lowest permittivity layer carries the highest field.

This inversion is the single most important reason HVDC insulation design is more complex than HVAC.

### 1.3 Classification of DC Insulation Systems (KTU Module 4 Scope)

| Sub-System | Primary Function | Key DC-Specific Challenge |
|---|---|---|
| **HV DC Capacitors** | Energy storage for pulse power, snubbers, grading | Leakage current over time, dielectric absorption, self-healing |
| **HVDC Bushings** | Safe passage of HV conductor through grounded tank/wall | DC field grading, pollution accumulation, temperature inversion |
| **HVDC Cables** | Underground/submarine bulk power transmission | Space charge accumulation, field inversion due to temperature gradient |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics & Constants to Remember:**
> - Vacuum permittivity: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{F/m}$
> - Standard HVAC line-to-line: 400 kV @ 50 Hz; HVDC bipolar: ±320 kV to ±800 kV (LCC) and ±525 kV (VSC typical)
> - Polyethylene DC resistivity: $\rho \approx 10^{14}\;\Omega\cdot\text{m}$ at 20 °C
> - XLPE cable working stress DC: 20–30 kV/mm (vs ~5–10 kV/mm AC)
> - Creepage factor for DC: 1.5× to 2× the AC value for same pollution

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Physics of DC Dielectrics

### 2.1.1 Conduction Mechanisms in DC Insulation

Under sustained DC stress, the dielectric is no longer a perfect insulator — a small but finite conduction current flows. The relevant conduction mechanisms are:

1. **Ohmic (ionic) conduction** — dominant at low fields and high temperatures
2. **Hopping conduction** — electrons tunnel between localized trap states
3. **Poole–Frenkel emission** — field-enhanced thermal emission from traps
4. **Space-charge-limited conduction (SCLC)** — dominant at high fields and at interfaces
5. **Schottky injection** — at electrode–dielectric interfaces

The general empirical form of DC conductivity is:

$$\sigma(T,E) = \sigma_0 \exp\left(-\frac{\phi}{kT}\right) \exp(\beta E)$$

where $\phi$ is the activation energy, $k$ is Boltzmann's constant, $T$ is absolute temperature, and $\beta$ is a field-enhancement coefficient.

### 2.1.2 Field Distribution in a Series DC Dielectric Stack

For $n$ dielectric layers in **series** between two electrodes carrying DC voltage $V$:

- **Current continuity (steady state):** $J_1 = J_2 = \dots = J_n = J$
- **Ohm's law in each layer:** $E_i = J/\sigma_i$
- **Voltage sum:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \cdot d_i = V$

Solving:

$$E_i = \frac{V \cdot \sigma_i^{-1}}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \sigma_k^{-1} \cdot d_k} = \frac{V \cdot \rho_i}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \rho_k \cdot d_k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule (KTU Board Favourite):** The DC field is **directly proportional** to the layer **resistivity**, weighted by thickness. The layer with the **highest resistivity carries the highest DC field**.

### 2.1.3 Space Charge & Field Distortion

When charge carriers become trapped at interfaces or within the bulk (e.g., at the conductor–insulation interface in a HVDC cable), they create a self-field that **distorts** the Laplacian field. This is called **space charge accumulation** and is responsible for:

- Localized field enhancement (up to 4×–8× the applied field)
- Reduced breakdown strength of XLPE under DC
- Long-term ageing and "electrochemical treeing"

## 2.2 HV DC Capacitors

### 2.2.1 Functions in HV Systems

1. **Pulse power** (Marx generators, PFNs): store $W = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ and release in microseconds
2. **DC smoothing / filters**: bypass AC ripple on DC bus
3. **Grading capacitors** in capacitive dividers for impulse measurement
4. **Snubber / commutation** capacitors in HVDC thyristor valves

### 2.2.2 Construction of HV DC Capacitors

| Type | Dielectric | Energy Density | Self-Healing? | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Oil-Impregnated Paper (OIP)** | Kraft paper + mineral oil | Low (~0.05 J/cc) | No | Old impulse generators |
| **Metallized Film (MFP)** | Polypropylene + evaporated Al | Medium (~0.5 J/cc) | Yes | Modern pulse capacitors |
| **Mixed Dielectric (PP + Paper)** | Hybrid | Medium | Partial | High-voltage DC link |
| **Ceramic (Class 2/3)** | BaTiO₃-based | High | No | High-temperature, compact |
| **Electrolytic** | Al₂O₃ film | Highest | Yes | Low-voltage DC only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-Healing Mechanism (Metallized Film):** A dielectric breakdown vaporizes the metallization around the fault in microseconds, isolating the defect. The capacitor continues to function with a tiny capacitance loss (~pF).

### 2.2.3 Equivalent Circuit of a Real DC Capacitor

A practical HV DC capacitor is modelled as:

- $C$ — main capacitance
- $R_s$ (ESR) — equivalent series resistance (governs I²R loss)
- $L_s$ (ESL) — equivalent series inductance (limits discharge $di/dt$)
- $R_p$ — insulation resistance (governs DC leakage and self-discharge time constant)
- $R_{da}$–$C_{da}$ — dielectric absorption branch (governs "soakage" voltage on discharge)

The **discharge time constant** is $\tau = R_s C$ (for short-circuit discharge through a low-impedance load).
The **self-discharge (soakage) time constant** is $\tau_p = R_p C$ (typically hours to days).

## 2.3 HVDC Bushings

### 2.3.1 Function & Operating Environment

A **bushing** is an insulated device that allows an HV conductor to pass through a grounded barrier (transformer tank, wall, valve hall wall) without flashover. In HVDC converter stations, bushings are used on:

- HVDC transformer interconnections
- Smoothing reactor terminals
- Converter valve wall bushings
- Wall bushings between valve hall and DC switchyard

### 2.3.2 DC-Specific Bushing Challenges

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Big DC Bushing Problems:**
> 1. **Resistive field grading** instead of capacitive grading — the field distribution depends on the bulk and surface resistivities of the insulation
> 2. **Temperature-inverted field** — when the bottom (oil-end) of the bushing is hotter than the top (air-end), the higher-conductivity region at the bottom takes less field, and the colder top carries a disproportionately high field
> 3. **Contamination & non-uniform wetting** — DC fields attract dust and pollution particles, leading to uneven surface gradients and dry-band arcing

### 2.3.3 Construction of HVDC Bushings

#### (a) Oil-Impregnated Paper (OIP) Bushing
- Kraft paper wound on conductor, vacuum-dried and oil-impregnated
- **Internal capacitive layers (aluminum foils)** are graded to control the field
- Proven for AC; on DC, capacitive grading becomes less effective → modern DC OIP bushings use **resistive grading collars** instead

#### (b) Resin-Impregnated Paper (RIP) / Resin-Bonded Paper (RBP) Bushing
- Paper wound and impregnated with epoxy resin (RIP) or phenolic resin (RBP)
- Dry, no oil — fire-safe
- Used for HVDC transformer and wall bushings
- Outer polymeric shed (silicone rubber) for outdoor creepage

#### (c) SF₆ Gas-Insulated Bushing
- Used at the air–SF₆ interface in GIS
- Inner grading condenser cone controls field

#### (d) Composite / Polymeric HVDC Bushings
- Silicone rubber housing, fiberglass resin-impregnated core
- Lightweight, explosion-proof, hydrophobic surface
- Increasingly standard for HVDC converter stations

### 2.3.4 Creepage Distance for DC Bushings

DC creepage distance must be **larger than AC** because:
- DC attracts airborne particles continuously (no zero-crossing to "shake them off")
- Pollution flashover occurs at lower voltage under DC than AC for the same creepage

Typical creepage factor: **40 mm/kV to 60 mm/kV** for DC (vs. **25–31 mm/kV** for AC in same pollution class).

## 2.4 HVDC Cables

### 2.4.1 HVDC Cable Types

| Type | Dielectric | Max Voltage | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mass-Impregnated (MI) Paper** | Lapped paper + viscous compound | Up to ±600 kV | Submarine HVDC (legacy) |
| **Self-Contained Oil-Filled (SC-OF)** | Paper + low-viscosity oil under pressure | Up to ±500 kV | Underground, long distances |
| **XLPE (Cross-Linked Polyethylene)** | Extruded XLPE | Up to ±525 kV (VSC-HVDC) | Modern underground & submarine |
| **PPLP / PPLP-OF** | Polypropylene Laminated Paper | ±500 kV | Submarine, low loss |
| **EPR (Ethylene Propylene Rubber)** | EPR compound | ≤±320 kV | Special-purpose, flexible |

### 2.4.2 HVDC Cable Construction (Example: XLPE)

Layers from conductor outward:
1. **Conductor** — Cu or Al, often Milliken (segmented) for skin effect
2. **Conductor screen** — semiconducting compound (~0.5 mm)
3. **Insulation** — XLPE, typically 15–30 mm for ±525 kV
4. **Insulation screen** — semiconducting compound
5. **Metallic sheath** — Al or Cu (carries return current & fault current)
6. **Outer jacket** — PE or PVC

### 2.4.3 The Field-Inversion Problem in HVDC Cables

Under load, the **conductor is hotter** than the outer sheath. Since $\sigma$ increases with temperature, the inner insulation has higher conductivity than the outer. In steady state, the current must be the same everywhere → the **electric field must be higher in the cooler outer region** (lower conductivity).

For a coaxial cable of inner radius $a$, outer radius $b$, with conductivity $\sigma(r)$:

$$E(r) = \frac{V}{\int_a^b \frac{1}{r\sigma(r)} \, dr} \cdot \frac{1}{r\sigma(r)}$$

If the temperature dependence is exponential: $\sigma(T) = \sigma_0 \exp(\alpha T)$, then for the coaxial geometry, the field at radius $r$ is:

$$E(r) = \frac{V \cdot r^{\alpha\Delta T \cdot \gamma - 1}}{a^{\alpha\Delta T \cdot \gamma} \int_a^b r^{-\alpha\Delta T \cdot \gamma}\, dr}$$

where $\gamma$ relates the temperature rise per $\ln r$ unit. Under heavy load, the **field inversion factor** can cause the maximum field to shift from the inner conductor to the outer insulation, drastically increasing the risk of breakdown there.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination-Critical)

> [!NOTE]
> This table contains the **most-tested formulas** for this topic. Master the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Energy stored in HV DC capacitor | Joules |
| 2 | $v_c(t) = V\bigl(1 - e^{-t/RC}\bigr)$ | Capacitor charging via resistor $R$ | V |
| 3 | $v_c(t) = V_0\, e^{-t/RC}$ | Capacitor discharging through $R$ | V |
| 4 | $\tau_{sd} = R_p C$ | Self-discharge (soakage) time constant | s |
| 5 | $i(t) = -\dfrac{V_0}{R} e^{-t/RC}$ | Discharge current | A |
| 6 | $E_i = \dfrac{V \rho_i d_i^{-1}}{\sum_k \rho_k / d_k}$ for series stack? — see corrected form | Field in $i^{th}$ layer of series stack | V/m |
| 7 | $E_i = \dfrac{V \rho_i}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \rho_k d_k}$ | **Corrected**: field in $i^{th}$ layer (DC, series) | V/m |
| 8 | $E(r) = \dfrac{V}{r\,\ln(b/a)}$ | Coaxial AC cable (Laplacian) | V/m |
| 9 | $\sigma(T) = \sigma_0 \exp\!\left[\alpha (T - T_0)\right]$ | Conductivity vs temperature (Arrhenius-like) | S/m |
| 10 | $J = \sigma E$ | Steady-state DC conduction current density | A/m² |
| 11 | $E_{max} = \dfrac{V}{a\,\ln(b/a)}\cdot\dfrac{1}{1-\gamma\Delta T}$ | Coaxial DC cable with linear $T$ profile | V/m |
| 12 | $L_{cr} = k \cdot V_{DC}$ | Required creepage distance for DC bushing | mm |
| 13 | $k = 40 \text{ to } 60$ | DC creepage factor (vs 25–31 for AC) | mm/kV |
| 14 | $E_{bd} \approx 60\text{–}80$ | XLPE DC intrinsic breakdown strength | kV/mm |
| 15 | $V_{DC} = \displaystyle\int_a^b E(r)\,dr$ | Voltage = integral of radial field | V |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Stored in an HV DC Capacitor

We begin from the fundamental definition of work done in moving incremental charge $dq$ against a potential $v = q/C$:

$$W = \int_0^Q v(q) \, dq = \int_0^Q \frac{q}{C} \, dq$$

Performing the integration:

$$W = \frac{1}{C} \left[ \frac{q^2}{2} \right]_0^Q = \frac{Q^2}{2C}$$

Substituting $Q = CV$:

$$W = \frac{(CV)^2}{2C} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$

Equivalent forms (all on the KTU exam):

$$W = \frac{1}{2}CV^2 = \frac{1}{2}QV = \frac{Q^2}{2C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Doubling the voltage quadruples the stored energy. This is why HVDC pulse-power systems are driven to ever-higher voltages, and why capacitor dielectric breakdown is catastrophic — the entire stored energy is released at the fault in microseconds.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: DC Field in a Series Dielectric Stack

Consider two dielectric layers in series between two parallel plate electrodes, with thicknesses $d_1$ and $d_2$, resistivities $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$, and permittivities $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$.

**Step 1 — Steady-state condition:**
At DC steady state, no displacement current flows (unlike AC). Only conduction current flows, and by Kirchhoff's current law, the current density is the same in both layers:

$$J_1 = J_2 = J$$

**Step 2 — Ohm's law in each layer:**

$$E_1 = J \rho_1, \qquad E_2 = J \rho_2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the voltage constraint:**

$$V = E_1 d_1 + E_2 d_2 = J(\rho_1 d_1 + \rho_2 d_2)$$

Therefore:

$$J = \frac{V}{\rho_1 d_1 + \rho_2 d_2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$E_1 = \frac{V \rho_1}{\rho_1 d_1 + \rho_2 d_2}, \qquad E_2 = \frac{V \rho_2}{\rho_1 d_1 + \rho_2 d_2}$$

**Step 5 — Generalize to $n$ layers:**

$$\boxed{\,E_i = \frac{V \rho_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} \rho_k d_k}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Compare with AC:** Under AC, the field divides as $E_i = \dfrac{V/\varepsilon_i}{\sum_k d_k/\varepsilon_k}$. The **inversion** is the heart of HVDC design — a low-permittivity layer (good for AC) becomes a high-resistivity layer (dangerous for DC).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Field Inversion in a Coaxial HVDC Cable (Simplified Linear Model)

**Setup:** Coaxial cable with inner radius $a$, outer radius $b$, carrying DC voltage $V$ between inner conductor and outer sheath. The temperature varies linearly with $\ln r$ from $T_a$ at the inner conductor to $T_b$ at the outer sheath. Define:

$$\Delta T = T_a - T_b, \qquad \text{(positive when inner is hotter)}$$

The conductivity profile is approximated as:

$$\sigma(r) \approx \sigma_0 \, r^{-\alpha \gamma}, \quad \text{where } \gamma = \frac{\Delta T}{\ln(b/a)}$$

**Step 1 — Steady-state conduction in cylindrical coordinates:**

The total radial current per unit length is constant:

$$I = 2\pi r \cdot \sigma(r) \cdot E(r) = \text{const}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $E(r)$:**

$$E(r) = \frac{I}{2\pi r \sigma(r)} = \frac{I}{2\pi \sigma_0} \cdot r^{\alpha\gamma - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the voltage integral:**

$$V = \int_a^b E(r)\, dr = \frac{I}{2\pi \sigma_0} \int_a^b r^{\alpha\gamma - 1}\, dr = \frac{I}{2\pi \sigma_0} \cdot \frac{b^{\alpha\gamma} - a^{\alpha\gamma}}{\alpha\gamma}$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $I$ and write $E(r)$:**

$$E(r) = \frac{V \cdot \alpha\gamma \cdot r^{\alpha\gamma - 1}}{b^{\alpha\gamma} - a^{\alpha\gamma}}$$

**Step 5 — Special case: zero temperature gradient ($\Delta T = 0$, so $\gamma = 0$):**

Use L'Hôpital's rule on the limit $\alpha\gamma \to 0$:

$$\lim_{\gamma \to 0} \frac{\alpha\gamma \, r^{\alpha\gamma - 1}}{b^{\alpha\gamma} - a^{\alpha\gamma}} = \frac{1}{r \ln(b/a)}$$

This recovers the familiar Laplacian field:

$$E(r) = \frac{V}{r \ln(b/a)} \quad \text{(isothermal, homogeneous)}$$

**Step 6 — Field at inner conductor vs outer sheath (no temperature rise):**

$$E(a) = \frac{V}{a \ln(b/a)}, \qquad E(b) = \frac{V}{b \ln(b/a)}$$

**Step 7 — Field-inversion criterion (the key engineering result):**

The maximum field shifts from inner to outer when $\alpha\gamma > 1$ (i.e., $\alpha \Delta T > \ln(b/a)$). The maximum field at the outer sheath is:

$$E(b) = \frac{V \cdot \alpha\gamma \cdot b^{\alpha\gamma - 1}}{b^{\alpha\gamma} - a^{\alpha\gamma}}$$

For typical XLPE ($\alpha \approx 0.1\;\text{K}^{-1}$), even a 30 K temperature drop between conductor and sheath can cause significant field inversion. This is why **HVDC cable rating is limited by the conductor temperature**, not by the conductor current alone.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Time Response of a Charging Capacitor

**Setup:** A DC source $V$ charges capacitor $C$ through charging resistor $R$. Initial condition: $v_c(0) = 0$.

**Step 1 — KVL around the loop:**

$$V = v_R(t) + v_C(t) = i(t) R + v_C(t)$$

**Step 2 — Capacitor relation:**

$$i(t) = C \frac{dv_C}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and rearrange:**

$$RC \frac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C = V$$

**Step 4 — Solve the first-order ODE with initial condition:**

The general solution is the sum of a steady-state particular solution $V$ and a transient homogeneous solution $Ae^{-t/RC}$:

$$v_C(t) = V + A e^{-t/RC}$$

Applying $v_C(0) = 0$:

$$A = -V$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{\,v_C(t) = V\bigl(1 - e^{-t/RC}\bigr)\,}$$

The charging current is:

$$i(t) = C \frac{dv_C}{dt} = \frac{V}{R} e^{-t/RC}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** For impulse-generator charging, $RC$ is the time constant, and the capacitor is considered "fully charged" after $t \approx 5RC$ (within 1% of $V$). The charging power dissipation in $R$ is $P = V^2/R \cdot e^{-2t/RC}$.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right DC Insulation

| Application | Recommended Insulation | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Submarine HVDC interconnector (±400–600 kV) | Mass-impregnated paper (MI) | Proven, no space-charge issue, no water treeing |
| Underground HVDC in cities (≤±525 kV) | XLPE (VSC-HVDC) | Easy jointing, no oil leakage, recyclable |
| Pulse power (Marx generator) | Metallized polypropylene film | Self-healing, low ESR, high $di/dt$ |
| DC smoothing on HVDC valve | Dry film / RFI filter caps | High RMS current, low loss |
| Wall bushing (valve hall) | RIP with composite silicone housing | Fire-safe, no oil, suitable for indoor/outdoor transition |
| Converter transformer bushing | OIP or RIP with capacitive/resistive grading | Highest reliability, very long creepage |
| ±800 kV UHVDC wall bushing | Composite polymer / SF₆ | Lightweight, high creepage, low contamination flashover risk |

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: DC vs AC Field Distribution in a Two-Layer Dielectric

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AC[Under AC Voltage]
        A1[Electrode A] --> A2[Layer 1 - low epsilon]
        A2 --> A3[Layer 2 - high epsilon]
        A3 --> A4[Electrode B]
        A2 -.->|High Field Here| A5[Stress Concentration]
    end
    subgraph DC[Under DC Voltage]
        B1[Electrode A] --> B2[Layer 1 - low rho]
        B2 --> B3[Layer 2 - high rho]
        B3 --> B4[Electrode B]
        B3 -.->|High Field Here| B5[Stress Concentration]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram carefully:** Under AC, the **low-permittivity** layer (Layer 1) sees the highest field. Under DC, the **high-resistivity** layer (Layer 2) sees the highest field. The stress concentration has *moved*.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain why the DC field distribution in a composite dielectric is opposite to that under AC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Under **AC**, the displacement current dominates and the field divides inversely with **permittivity** ($\varepsilon$): the layer with the **lower $\varepsilon$** sustains the **higher AC field**.

Under **DC steady state**, the displacement current vanishes and only **conduction current** flows. By current continuity, the current density $J$ is the same in every layer. By Ohm's law, $E = J\rho$, so the field is **directly proportional to the resistivity $\rho$**: the layer with the **higher $\rho$** sustains the **higher DC field**.

Hence, the highest-stress location can shift from one layer to another when the system is energized with DC instead of AC. **[3 marks]**

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is self-healing in a metallized HV DC capacitor? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a metallized film capacitor, the electrodes are thin layers of aluminum (or zinc) vacuum-deposited directly on the polymer film. When a localized dielectric breakdown occurs, the discharge evaporates the metallization around the fault in microseconds, **electrically isolating the defect**. The capacitor continues to function with a negligible loss of capacitance.

**Importance:** It allows the capacitor to survive occasional dielectric weak-spots without catastrophic failure, making metallized capacitors the standard for **high-energy pulse power, snubbers, and DC link filters** in HVDC valves. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of an HVDC cable** based on **XLPE insulation**. Discuss the **space-charge problem** and its effect on the cable's reliability. (7 marks)

**(b)** A single-core **XLPE HVDC cable** has an inner conductor radius of **18 mm** and an outer insulation screen radius of **34 mm**. The cable carries a **DC voltage of 250 kV** and a load current that produces a **temperature rise of 25 K at the conductor** above the outer screen. The conductivity of XLPE follows $\sigma(T) = \sigma_0 \exp[0.08 (T - T_0)]\;\text{S/m}$. Determine the **maximum electric field** in the insulation and **comment on field inversion**. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **construction of an HVDC bushing**. Discuss why **DC field grading** is more complex than AC field grading and outline the **role of resistive grading collars**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **pulse-forming capacitor bank** has a total capacitance of **8 µF** and is charged to a DC voltage of **50 kV** through a charging resistor of **20 kΩ**. Calculate: (i) the **energy stored**, (ii) the **charging time constant**, and (iii) the **time to reach 95% of full charge**. If the bank is then **discharged through a 0.5 Ω load**, find the **peak discharge current** and the **time for the current to fall to 1% of its peak**. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A:

#### (a) XLPE HVDC Cable — Construction, Working & Space Charge (7 marks)

**Construction (from centre outwards):** [1 mark]
1. **Conductor** — stranded Cu or Al (often Milliken for large sizes)
2. **Conductor screen** — extruded semiconducting compound, ~0.5–1 mm thick
3. **XLPE insulation** — extruded in a dry-curing tube (CV line), typical thickness 15–30 mm
4. **Insulation screen** — semiconducting compound, similar to conductor screen
5. **Metallic sheath** — Al or Cu, longitudinally welded; carries return current and fault current
6. **Outer jacket** — extruded PE or PVC, with optional swellable tapes for water blocking

**Working:** [2 marks]
The cable carries bulk DC power between two converter stations. The DC field is purely resistive. Under load, the conductor heats up; in steady state, the radial current density $J$ is constant. Because $\sigma$ increases with temperature, the field is **lower in the hotter inner region** and **higher in the cooler outer region**. The space-charge problem arises when:
- **Homocharge** (same polarity as adjacent electrode) accumulates at the screens, distorting the field
- **Heterocharge** (opposite polarity) accumulates in the bulk, especially near impurities and cross-linking byproducts

**Space-charge effects:** [2 marks]
- Local field enhancement up to **4×–8×** the Laplacian value
- Premature ageing, electrical treeing, and "bow-tie" trees
- Limits working stress to **20–30 kV/mm** for DC (vs 5–10 kV/mm AC) [in some references DC design stress is even lower than AC for XLPE]

**Reliability measures:** [2 marks]
- Use of **super-clean XLPE** with minimal crosslinking residues
- **Nanofilled XLPE** (e.g., MgO nano-dopants) to suppress space charge
- **Voltage-inversion load cycles** to pre-condition the cable
- Adoption of **VSC-HVDC** (which produces less DC ripple and is gentler on cable) for ±525 kV

#### (b) Numerical — Field in Coaxial XLPE HVDC Cable (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $a = 18\;\text{mm} = 0.018\;\text{m}$
- $b = 34\;\text{mm} = 0.034\;\text{m}$
- $V = 250\;\text{kV} = 2.5 \times 10^5\;\text{V}$
- $\Delta T = T_a - T_b = 25\;\text{K}$
- $\alpha = 0.08\;\text{K}^{-1}$

**Step 1 — Compute the geometric and temperature parameters:**

$$\ln(b/a) = \ln(34/18) = \ln(1.8889) = 0.6360$$

$$\alpha \Delta T = 0.08 \times 25 = 2.0$$

**Step 2 — Field at the inner conductor (assuming a uniform $\sigma$ baseline, isothermal check):**

$$E_{iso}(a) = \frac{V}{a \ln(b/a)} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^5}{0.018 \times 0.6360} = 21.84\;\text{MV/m} = 21.84\;\text{kV/mm}$$

**Step 3 — Field at the outer screen (isothermal):**

$$E_{iso}(b) = \frac{V}{b \ln(b/a)} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^5}{0.034 \times 0.6360} = 11.56\;\text{kV/mm}$$

**Step 4 — Now account for the temperature gradient. The radial field in a coaxial cable with a temperature-dependent conductivity is:**

$$E(r) = \frac{V \cdot \alpha\gamma \cdot r^{\alpha\gamma - 1}}{b^{\alpha\gamma} - a^{\alpha\gamma}}, \quad \text{where } \gamma = \frac{\Delta T}{\ln(b/a)}$$

Compute:

$$\gamma = \frac{25}{0.6360} = 39.31\;\text{K per natural-log unit}$$

$$\alpha\gamma = 0.08 \times 39.31 = 3.145$$

**Step 5 — Compute the geometric factors:**

$$\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)^{\alpha\gamma} = (1.8889)^{3.145}$$

Taking logarithms:

$$3.145 \times \ln(1.8889) = 3.145 \times 0.6360 = 2.000$$

Therefore:

$$\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)^{\alpha\gamma} = e^{2.000} = 7.389$$

**Step 6 — Compute $E(a)$ (at the inner, hot conductor):**

$$E(a) = \frac{V \cdot \alpha\gamma \cdot a^{\alpha\gamma - 1}}{b^{\alpha\gamma} - a^{\alpha\gamma}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^5 \times 3.145 \times a^{2.145}}{a^{3.145}(7.389 - 1)}$$

$$= \frac{2.5 \times 10^5 \times 3.145}{a \times 6.389} = \frac{7.86 \times 10^5}{0.018 \times 6.389}$$

$$E(a) = \frac{7.86 \times 10^5}{0.1150} = 6.83 \times 10^6\;\text{V/m} = 6.83\;\text{kV/mm}$$

**Step 7 — Compute $E(b)$ (at the outer, cool screen):**

$$E(b) = \frac{V \cdot \alpha\gamma \cdot b^{\alpha\gamma - 1}}{b^{\alpha\gamma} - a^{\alpha\gamma}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^5 \times 3.145 \times b^{2.145}}{a^{3.145}(7.389 - 1)}$$

$$= \frac{2.5 \times 10^5 \times 3.145 \times (b/a)^{2.145}}{a \times 6.389}$$

$$(b/a)^{2.145} = (1.8889)^{2.145} = e^{2.145 \times 0.6360} = e^{1.364} = 3.912$$

$$E(b) = \frac{2.5 \times 10^5 \times 3.145 \times 3.912}{0.018 \times 6.389} = \frac{3.076 \times 10^6}{0.1150} = 26.75\;\text{kV/mm}$$

**Step 8 — Comparison and field-inversion comment:** [1 mark]

| Condition | $E(a)$ (inner) | $E(b)$ (outer) | Maximum |
|---|---|---|---|
| Isothermal | 21.84 kV/mm | 11.56 kV/mm | Inner |
| With $\Delta T = 25$ K | 6.83 kV/mm | **26.75 kV/mm** | **Outer** |

**Comment:** With a 25 K temperature gradient, the maximum field has **completely inverted** from the inner conductor to the outer screen. The outer insulation now sees **26.75 kV/mm**, which is dangerously close to the DC breakdown strength of XLPE. The cable would be **unreliable at this load** — conductor temperature must be limited, or the cable must be redesigned (larger conductor, higher $\sigma$ outer layer, or nanofilled XLPE). **[7 marks]**

---

### Solution to Question B:

#### (a) HVDC Bushing Construction & DC Field Grading (7 marks)

**Construction (refer to Mermaid Diagram 4):** [3 marks]
1. **Centre conductor** — Cu or Al rod carrying the HV current
2. **Insulation core** — RIP (resin-impregnated paper) or OIP (oil-impregnated paper), wound on the conductor
3. **Grading layers** — either **capacitive** (Al foils, AC bushings) or **resistive** (carbon-loaded paper, DC bushings) or a combination
4. **Flange** — grounded metal flange at the middle, supports the bushing and provides the earth reference
5. **Outdoor housing** — porcelain or composite silicone rubber sheds for creepage and weather protection
6. **Oil or SF₆ end** — bottom termination into transformer oil or GIS gas

**Why DC field grading is more complex:** [2 marks]
- AC field divides by $\varepsilon$ → controlled by **capacitor-like foils**
- DC field divides by $\rho$ → **resistivity varies exponentially with temperature**
- A bushing is hottest at the oil end and coolest at the air end → field **naturally shifts toward the cooler end**
- Under reverse-power conditions, the temperature gradient flips, and the field also flips → bushing must be rated for **both** inversion scenarios

**Role of resistive grading collars:** [2 marks]
- Replace or supplement capacitive foils
- Made of carbon-loaded paper or rubber with carefully chosen sheet resistance
- Force the potential distribution along the bushing to remain linear (or controlled) despite temperature-induced $\rho$ variations
- Used in modern **±800 kV UHVDC** converter transformer bushings and wall bushings

#### (b) Numerical — Pulse Capacitor Bank (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $C = 8\;\mu\text{F} = 8 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{F}$
- $V = 50\;\text{kV} = 5 \times 10^4\;\text{V}$
- Charging $R = 20\;\text{k}\Omega = 2 \times 10^4\;\Omega$
- Discharging $R_{load} = 0.5\;\Omega$

**(i) Energy stored:** [1 mark]

$$W = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 10^{-6} \times (5 \times 10^4)^2$$

$$= \frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.5 \times 10^9 = 10^4\;\text{J} = 10\;\text{kJ}$$

**(ii) Charging time constant:** [1 mark]

$$\tau_c = RC = 2 \times 10^4 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} = 0.16\;\text{s} = 160\;\text{ms}$$

**(iii) Time to reach 95% charge:** [1 mark]

$$v_c(t) = V(1 - e^{-t/\tau_c}) = 0.95 V$$

$$e^{-t/\tau_c} = 0.05 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = -\tau_c \ln(0.05) = 0.16 \times 2.996 = 0.479\;\text{s} \approx 480\;\text{ms}$$

**Peak discharge current:** [2 marks]

At the instant of closing the discharge switch, the capacitor voltage is $V = 50\;\text{kV}$:

$$I_{peak} = \frac{V}{R_{load}} = \frac{5 \times 10^4}{0.5} = 1.0 \times 10^5\;\text{A} = 100\;\text{kA}$$

**Time for current to fall to 1%:** [2 marks]

The discharge current decays as:

$$i(t) = I_{peak}\, e^{-t/(R_{load} C)}$$

Discharge time constant:

$$\tau_d = R_{load} C = 0.5 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} = 4\;\mu\text{s}$$

For $i(t) = 0.01 \cdot I_{peak}$:

$$e^{-t/\tau_d} = 0.01 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = -\tau_d \ln(0.01) = 4\;\mu\text{s} \times 4.605 = 18.4\;\mu\text{s}$$

**Summary of results:** [remainder of 7 marks]
| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Energy stored | **10 kJ** |
| Charging time constant | **160 ms** |
| Time to 95% charge | **~480 ms** |
| Peak discharge current | **100 kA** |
| Discharge time to 1% | **~18.4 µs** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse the *charging* time constant ($RC$ with charging resistor) with the *discharge* time constant ($R_{load} C$). They differ by a factor of **40,000** in this problem.
> 2. **Do not** assume $v_c(5\tau) = V$ for impulse work; many problems require **exact** 95% or 99% times — use the logarithm.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor ½** in the energy formula $\frac{1}{2}CV^2$ costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Unit conversion errors**: $\mu\text{F} \times \text{kV}^2$ must yield Joules — write it out explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and revise 30 minutes before the exam.**

### ⚡ DC Insulation — The 10 Golden Rules
1. **Field distribution:** DC field ∝ $\rho$ (resistivity); AC field ∝ $1/\varepsilon$ (permittivity).
2. **Highest-resistivity layer = highest DC field** (opposite of AC).
3. **Conduction mechanisms:** Ohmic → Hopping → Poole–Frenkel → SCLC → Schottky, with increasing field.
4. **Space charge** is the dominant ageing mechanism in XLPE under DC.
5. **Self-healing** makes metallized film capacitors the standard for HV DC pulse work.
6. **Capacitor energy:** $W = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ — always state it.
7. **HVDC bushing creepage** = 40–60 mm/kV (vs 25–31 mm/kV for AC).
8. **Resistive grading** is the hallmark of DC bushings; capacitive grading is for AC.
9. **HVDC cable field inversion** occurs when $\alpha \Delta T > \ln(b/a)$ — limits conductor temperature.
10. **Mass-impregnated (MI) paper** cables remain the choice for very long submarine HVDC; **XLPE** is preferred for VSC-HVDC and urban installations.

### 🧠 Key Formulas (memorize verbatim)
- $W = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$
- $v_c(t) = V(1 - e^{-t/RC})$
- $E_i^{DC} = \dfrac{V \rho_i}{\sum \rho_k d_k}$
- $E(r)_{iso} = \dfrac{V}{r \ln(b/a)}$
- $\sigma(T) = \sigma_0 e^{\alpha (T - T_0)}$
- $I_{peak}^{disch} = V/R_{load}$

### 🛠 Common Engineering Pitfalls
- Forgetting **temperature inversion** in a real HVDC cable
- Quoting AC creepage distance for a DC insulator
- Treating a metallized capacitor as **non-self-healing**
- Mixing up **charging** and **discharging** time constants
- Forgetting the **½** factor in stored energy

### 📚 KTU 2024 Module-4 Mapping
- **CO4 (Design HV insulation systems):** Fully addressed in this note.
- **Bloom's Levels covered:** L1 (Recall) → L2 (Comprehend) → L3 (Apply, derivations) → L4 (Analyze, Part B numerical) → L5 (Evaluate, design recommendations).

---
