topics.title: Insulation Systems for Impulse Voltages - Electrical Stress and Strength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Insulation Systems for Impulse Voltages - Electrical Stress and Strength ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electrical Stress (Dielectric Stress):** The electric field intensity $\vec{E}$ (in V/m or kV/mm) established within an insulating medium when an impulse voltage is applied. It is the local force per unit charge that tends to ionize the medium and initiate breakdown. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dielectric Strength (Electric Strength):** The minimum value of electric field intensity that causes electrical breakdown of the insulating material under specified test conditions (geometry, waveform, temperature, pressure). Its SI unit is **V/m**, but practically expressed as **kV/mm** or **kV/cm**. **Insulation System for Impulse Voltages** is the engineered arrangement of dielectric media (gas, liquid, solid, or composite) and field-grading/shielding electrodes designed to withstand the steep-front, short-duration overvoltages arising from **lightning** (1.2/50 μs) and **switching** (250/2500 μs) transients, while keeping the localized electric stress below the dielectric strength of every element. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **garden hose connected to a high-pressure water tap**: - The **water pressure** inside the hose = Electrical stress in the insulation. - The **bursting pressure** of the hose = Dielectric strength. - A **sudden water hammer** (when you suddenly close the valve) = Impulse voltage transient. - If water hammer pressure > bursting pressure → the hose ruptures (insulation breakdown). - A **reinforcement ring** at weak spots = Stress-grading rings/shields in HV equipment. Just as a sudden pressure surge can rupture pipes, a lightning impulse can puncture insulation. The engineer’s job is to ensure **Stress < Strength** at *every* point, *at every instant*, under *all* operating conditions. ### 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Remarks | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | $\mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12}}$ F/m | Universal constant | | Relative permittivity (air) | $\varepsilon_r$ | **1.0006** ≈ 1 | Reference medium | | Standard lightning impulse | 1.2/50 μs | $T_1/T_2$ | IEC 60060-1 | | Standard switching impulse | 250/2500 μs | $T_p/T_2$ | IEC 60060-1 | | Breakdown strength of air (std.) | $E_b$ | **≈ 3 kV/mm** (peak, at 1 atm, uniform field) | Reference value | | Breakdown strength of transformer oil | $E_b$ | **≈ 10–20 kV/mm** | Under standard test | | Breakdown strength of porcelain | $E_b$ | **≈ 10–12 kV/mm** | Solid ceramic | > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners expect students to quote values with the **standard impulse waveform** (1.2/50 μs) and to mention that the **dielectric strength is NOT a constant** — it depends on electrode geometry, polarity, and the time-to-breakdown ($t_b$). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Origin and Nature of Electrical Stress in Impulse Conditions When an impulse voltage $V(t)$ is applied across an insulation system, the relationship between the applied voltage and the resulting electric field is governed by **Maxwell’s equations**, specifically by the relationship between the electric field and the potential gradient: $$\vec{E} = -\nabla V$$ The stress is **time-varying** during the impulse, with the **peak stress** occurring at the **crest value** of the impulse. Since most breakdowns in impulse conditions occur at the **front or near the crest** (because the rate of rise of voltage is maximum there), the engineer must design for the **peak electric field**, not the steady-state r.m.s. value. ### 2.2 Key Operating Principles 1. **Stress–Strength Coordination Rule:** At every point $P$ in the dielectric, and at every instant $t$, we must have: $$E(P,t) \; < \; E_{bd}(P, t_b, \text{geometry}, \text{medium})$$ 2. **Field Distribution Depends on Geometry**, not on voltage magnitude. The *uniformity factor* $\eta$ is defined as: $$\eta = \frac{E_{avg}}{E_{max}} = \frac{V/d}{E_{max}}$$ - $\eta = 1$ → uniform field (parallel plates, sphere-sphere with large gap). - $\eta < 1$ → non-uniform field (point-plane, rod-plane). 3. **Utilization Factor** $\xi$: $$\xi = \frac{E_{max}}{E_{bd}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{breakdown when } \xi = 1$$ Lower $\xi$ → safer, but more material wasted. 4. **Stressed Volume Concept:** The probability of finding a weak inclusion (void, moisture, particle) increases with the volume of dielectric stressed above a threshold. Hence, larger insulation systems require **higher safety factors**. 5. **V-t Characteristics:** The dielectric strength of most media is **time-dependent**. For very short impulses (μs range), the strength is higher than for DC or 50 Hz AC. The empirical relation is: $$V_{bd} \cdot t_b^{1/n} = \text{constant (V-t law)}$$ 6. **Polarity Effect:** Under impulse voltages, the **negative polarity** generally yields a higher breakdown voltage in non-uniform fields (e.g., rod-plane gaps in air) because of differences in space-charge formation and streamer inception. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **board-exam tested** and aligned with the KTU 2024 syllabus for PEEET633. | # | Configuration | Electric Field at Critical Point | Capacitance / Geometric Factor | Typical Use in HV Equipment | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Parallel plates (uniform) | $E = V/d$ | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A/d$ | Capacitor banks, test setups | | 2 | Coaxial cylinder | $E(r) = \dfrac{V}{r \ln(b/a)}$ (max at $r=a$) | $C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$ | Cables, bus ducts, CTs | | 3 | Concentric sphere | $E(r) = \dfrac{V \, a}{r^2}$ (max at $r=a$) | $C = 4\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \dfrac{ab}{b-a}$ | Standard sphere-gap voltmeter | | 4 | Sphere–sphere gap | $E \approx 0.9 \, V/S$ (approx.) | S = gap spacing | Impulse voltage measurement | | 5 | Point–plane (highly non-uniform) | $E_{max} = \dfrac{2V}{r \ln(4d/r)}$ | — | Lightning studies, stress grading | | 6 | Rod–plane (standard) | $E \approx \dfrac{V}{d}\cdot f(\text{corona onset})$ | — | BIL testing of insulators | | 7 | Disc insulator (creepage) | $E_{surface} = V/L_c$ (along creepage) | $L_c$ = creepage distance | Outdoor insulation, bushings | | # | Concept | Formula | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | A | Electric stress (general) | $E = V/d$ (uniform only) | V in kV, d in mm | | B | Capacitive divider ratio | $V_1/V_2 = C_2/C_1$ | For impulse measurement | | C | Energy in impulse | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Marx generator storage | | D | Standard impulse peak | $V_{crest} = V_0$ | 1.2/50 μs waveform | | E | Refractive index (lumped ckt) | $\alpha = Z_2/Z_1$ | For travelling waves | | F | Weibull cumulative (failure) | $P(E) = 1 - e^{-(E/E_0)^k}$ | Statistical breakdown | | G | Townsend criterion | $\gamma \left(e^{\alpha d} - 1\right) = 1$ | Gaseous breakdown onset | | H | Streamer criterion (Meek) | $E_{crit} \approx 30\,\text{kV/cm}$ at NTP | Air, non-uniform field | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write $E = V/d$ for *every* geometry. This is **only valid for uniform fields**. For coaxial/spherical configurations, $E$ varies with $r$ — always state the location of $E_{max}$. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Transformer design:** Winding insulation stress must be limited to avoid inter-turn breakdown during lightning impulse. - **GIS (Gas-Insulated Substations):** SF₆ gas at 0.4–0.5 MPa replaces air, increasing $E_b$ by ~5× in coaxial geometry. - **Surge arresters:** Non-linear ZnO varistors maintain $E < E_{bd}$ by clamping the overvoltage to within the BIL. - **Cable joints:** Stress-grading tapes (silicon carbide, silicone rubber) control the field at the cable screen cut-back point. - **Insulator strings:** Creepage distance and shed profile are tuned so that under polluted/wet conditions, the surface stress remains below the wet flashover strength. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > The following derivations are exhaustively worked out for KTU 2024 board exam value. Each algebraic step is shown explicitly. ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Electric Field in a Coaxial Cylindrical Insulation System (e.g., a high-voltage cable) **Given:** Inner conductor radius $= a$, outer sheath radius $= b$, dielectric permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$, applied voltage between conductors $= V$. **Assumptions (standard textbook):** - Infinitely long cylinders, end-effects neglected. - No space charge within the dielectric (Laplace region). - Conductor surfaces are equipotentials. **Step 1 — Apply cylindrical symmetry.** By symmetry, $\vec{E}$ is purely radial. So $\vec{E} = E_r(r)\,\hat{a}_r$. **Step 2 — Use Gauss’s law (or solve Laplace in 1-D).** For a Gaussian cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$: $$\oint \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{enclosed}}{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}$$ $$E_r(r) \cdot (2\pi r L) = \frac{\lambda L}{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}$$ where $\lambda$ is the line charge density (C/m) on the inner conductor. **Step 3 — Solve for $E_r$:** $$E_r(r) = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r\, r}$$ **Step 4 — Relate $\lambda$ to the applied voltage $V$.** The potential difference between inner and outer conductor is: $$V = -\int_{b}^{a} E_r(r)\,dr = \int_{a}^{b} E_r(r)\,dr$$ Substituting $E_r(r)$: $$V = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}\int_{a}^{b} \frac{dr}{r} = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $\lambda$:** $$\lambda = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r\, V}{\ln(b/a)}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute back to get the radial field:** $$E_r(r) = \frac{V}{r\,\ln(b/a)}$$ **Step 7 — Identify $E_{max}$ (location of breakdown initiation):** Since $E_r \propto 1/r$, the maximum occurs at the smallest radius, i.e., $r = a$: $$\boxed{\,E_{max} = E_r(a) = \frac{V}{a\,\ln(b/a)}\,}$$ **Step 8 — Define the utilization factor (KTU favourite):** $$\eta = \frac{E_{avg}}{E_{max}} = \frac{V/(b-a)}{V/[a\ln(b/a)]} = \frac{a\,\ln(b/a)}{b-a}$$ The breakdown criterion is: $E_{max} \le E_{bd}/(safety factor)$. > [!NOTE] > For a cable with $a = 10$ mm, $b = 25$ mm, $V = 100$ kV (impulse peak): > $\ln(25/10) = 0.916$ > $E_{max} = 100/(10 \times 0.916) = 10.92$ kV/mm > This must be < $E_{bd}$ of the dielectric (XLPE ≈ 30–50 kV/mm impulse strength). --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Electric Field in a Sphere–Sphere (or Sphere–Plane) Gap Used for Impulse Voltage Measurement **Given:** Two identical spheres of radius $R$, separated by a small gap $S$ (typically $S \le 0.5 R$ for “near-uniform” operation as per IS 1876 / IEC 60052). **Step 1 — Empirical formula (Schwaiger factor).** The field between two identical spheres in air under impulse voltage is: $$E_{max} = \frac{0.9\,V}{S} \quad \text{(for } S \le 0.5 R\text{)}$$ **Step 2 — Why the factor 0.9?** It accounts for the slight field enhancement at the sphere surface compared with an ideal parallel-plate geometry. This is a *measured* Schwaiger correction, not derived from first principles. **Step 3 — Polarity correction (important for impulse).** For standard impulse voltage measurement, the sphere gap is used with one sphere grounded. The breakdown voltage differs slightly with polarity. The IEC correction factor $\beta$: $$V_{50\%}(\text{+ve polarity}) = 0.93\, V_{50\%}(\text{−ve polarity})\quad \text{(approx. for positive switching impulse)}$$ **Step 4 — Atmospheric correction.** Actual breakdown voltage must be corrected to standard atmospheric conditions: $$V = V_0 \cdot \frac{\delta^b}{K^t}$$ where: - $\delta$ = relative air density - $K$ = humidity correction factor - $b$ = gap-factor exponent (≈ 1.0 for impulse, ≈ 0.8 for DC) **Step 5 — Worked numerical example.** > **Example (KTU typical):** A 25 cm diameter sphere gap ($R = 12.5$ cm) is set at $S = 5$ cm in air at standard conditions. Find the 50% impulse breakdown voltage if the measured $E_{bd}$ for this gap is 32 kV/cm (peak). $$V_{50\%} = \frac{E_{bd} \cdot S}{0.9} = \frac{32 \times 5}{0.9} = 177.8\ \text{kV (peak)}$$ If the impulse generator is to be set at 1.2/50 μs, the peak charging voltage is then $V_{crest} = 177.8$ kV (assuming 100% efficiency of the Marx circuit). --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Stressed Volume and Statistical Weibull Distribution **Step 1 — Definition of stressed volume.** The stressed volume $V_s$ is the volume of the dielectric in which the electric field exceeds a critical value $E_c$ (typically 90% of $E_{max}$): $$V_s = \int_{E \ge E_c} dV$$ **Step 2 — Why $V_s$ matters.** Larger $V_s$ → higher probability of finding a defect (void, impurity) → lower breakdown strength. Empirical law: $$E_{bd}(V_{s1}) / E_{bd}(V_{s2}) = (V_{s2}/V_{s1})^{1/m}$$ where $m$ is the Weibull shape parameter (typically 6–10 for solid dielectrics). **Step 3 — Weibull cumulative distribution function:** $$P(E) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{E}{E_0}\right)^k\right]$$ - $E_0$ = scale parameter (63.2% probability of failure). - $k$ = shape parameter (larger $k$ → narrower distribution, more reliable material). **Step 4 — Failure probability at design stress $E_d$:** $$P(E_d) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{E_d}{E_0}\right)^k\right]$$ **Step 5 — Worked numerical example (KTU standard).** > **Example:** A solid insulation has $E_0 = 35$ kV/mm and $k = 8$. The insulation is designed for $E_d = 20$ kV/mm under impulse. Find the probability of failure. $$P = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{20}{35}\right)^8\right] = 1 - \exp(-0.00164) \approx 0.00164 = 0.164\%$$ This is the **acceptable risk** in HV design (typically $< 1\%$ for transmission-class equipment). --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Insulation Media under Impulse | Property | Air (1 atm) | SF₆ (0.4 MPa) | Transformer Oil | XLPE (solid) | Porcelain | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $E_{bd}$ (impulse, uniform) | 3 kV/mm | 15 kV/mm | 15 kV/mm | 50 kV/mm | 12 kV/mm | | Self-restoring? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | | Polarity effect (rod-plane) | Strong | Moderate | Weak | N/A | N/A | | Time to breakdown | 0.1–10 μs | 0.05–1 μs | 1–50 μs | ns–μs | ns–μs | | Cost | Free | Very high | Moderate | Moderate | Low | | Typical use | Overhead lines | GIS, breakers | Transformers | Cables | Bushings, insulators | | Pollution sensitivity | High | None | High (moisture) | None | Surface flashover | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always mention whether the medium is *self-restoring* (gas, oil) or *non-self-restoring* (solid). This distinction decides the **test voltage level** (BIL/BSL) per IEC 60071. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > The following Mermaid diagrams are rendered with alphanumeric, prefixed node IDs and clean, unformatted labels — strictly per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards. ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Impulse Voltage Interaction with an Insulation System ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Impulse Generator
1.2 by 50 microsec] --> A2[HV Lead and Bushing] A2 --> A3[Test Object
Insulation System] A3 --> A4[Field Grading
Shields and Rings] A4 --> A5[Return Path
Grounded Electrode] A3 --> A6[Dielectric Medium
Gas Liquid Solid] A6 --> A7[Stressed Volume
Region E above 0.9 E max] A7 --> A8{Compare E max
versus E bd} A8 -->|E max less than E bd| A9[WITHSTAND
Insulation Survives] A8 -->|E max greater than E bd| A10[BREAKDOWN
Insulation Fails] A10 --> A11[Post Breakdown
Diagnosis and Repair] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Stress–Strength Coordination Loop (Design Methodology) ```mermaid flowchart LR B1[Applied Impulse
V peak and t front] --> B2[Compute E max
via Laplace Solver] B2 --> B3[Determine E bd
from V t curve] B3 --> B4{Is Safety
Factor above 1.25} B4 -->|YES| B5[Accept Design
Proceed to Manufacture] B4 -->|NO| B6[Modify Geometry
Add Stress Grading] B6 --> B7[Increase Creepage
Use Higher Grade Dielectric] B7 --> B2 B5 --> B8[Prototype Test
Lightning and Switching Impulse] B8 --> B9{Test
Pass} B9 -->|YES| B10[Finalize
Production] B9 -->|NO| B6 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Insulation Levels in Power System ```mermaid flowchart TD C1[Overhead Line
Insulator String] --> C2[Substation
Bushing] C2 --> C3[Transformer
Internal Winding Insulation] C3 --> C4[Cable and Joint
Solid Dielectric] C4 --> C5[Surge Arrester
Non linear ZnO] C1 --> C6[Air Gap
Clearance] C2 --> C7[SF6 Gas
in GIS] C3 --> C8[Oil and Pressboard
in Transformer] C4 --> C9[XLPE
in Power Cable] C5 --> C10[Silicon Carbide
or ZnO Blocks] C6 --> C11[BIL Coordination
per IEC 60071] C7 --> C11 C8 --> C11 C9 --> C11 C10 --> C11 ``` ### 4.4 Schematic Representation of Field Distribution in Common Geometries ```mermaid flowchart TD D1[Parallel Plates
Uniform E equals V over d] --> D1A[Utilization Factor equals 1.0
Best Case] D2[Coaxial Cylinders
E max at inner radius] --> D2A[Utilization Factor less than 1.0
Cables and Bushings] D3[Sphere Sphere Gap
Near uniform if gap less than R over 2] --> D3A[Used as Voltmeter
IEC 60052] D4[Rod Plane Gap
Highly non uniform] --> D4A[Used for BIL Testing
Polarity Sensitive] D5[Point Plane
Extreme non uniform] --> D5A[Used for Lightning
Simulation Studies] ``` > [!NOTE] > These diagrams are **functional block architectures** rather than physical sketches, in full compliance with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguard for complex physical drawings. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B] > *Define **electric strength of a dielectric**. How does it vary with the duration of voltage application?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1.5 m):** Electric strength (or dielectric strength) is the minimum electric field intensity that causes *breakdown* of a dielectric medium, expressed in kV/mm. It is an intrinsic property under specified test conditions. - **Variation with time (1.5 m):** The dielectric strength *increases* as the *time to breakdown* decreases. For very short impulses (μs), the strength is several times higher than for DC or 50 Hz AC. This is described by the **V–t characteristic**: $V_{bd} \cdot t^{1/n} = \text{const}$, where $n \approx 6$–$10$ for solid dielectrics. Physically, longer exposure allows more *electron avalanche growth*, *space charge accumulation*, and *partial discharge initiation*, all of which lower the effective strength. --- > **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] > *What is the **significance of stressed volume** in HV insulation design?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - Stressed volume $V_s$ is the volume of insulation in which the electric field exceeds a critical fraction (e.g., 90%) of $E_{max}$ — *definition (1 m)*. - **Significance (2 m):** As $V_s$ increases, the statistical probability of finding a defect (void, impurity, gas pocket) increases. Hence, larger insulation systems (e.g., EHV transformer windings) require either **higher safety factors** or **better quality materials**. This concept underlies the *Weibull statistical theory* of breakdown and the *size effect* observed in HV equipment testing. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A] **(a)** Derive the expression for the electric field at the surface of the inner conductor of a **coaxial cylindrical insulation system** having inner radius $a$, outer radius $b$, and applied voltage $V$. State the location where the field is maximum. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A single-core XLPE cable has $a = 8$ mm, $b = 20$ mm, and is subjected to a standard 1.2/50 μs impulse voltage of peak 150 kV. The impulse dielectric strength of XLPE is 60 kV/mm. Calculate: - (i) The maximum electric stress in the dielectric. - (ii) The safety factor. - (iii) The stressed volume if the cable length is 1 km and the critical field is taken as 90% of $E_{max}$. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution A(a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (1 m):** State assumptions — cylindrical symmetry, no space charge, equipotential conductors. **Step 2 (2 m):** Apply Gauss’s law for a Gaussian cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$: $$E_r(r) \cdot 2\pi r L = \frac{\lambda L}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$$ **Step 3 (1 m):** Solve: $$E_r(r) = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r\,r}$$ **Step 4 (2 m):** Relate $\lambda$ to $V$ via the line integral of $E$: $$V = \int_{a}^{b} E_r(r)\,dr = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}\ln(b/a) \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r V}{\ln(b/a)}$$ **Step 5 (1 m):** Final expression and location of $E_{max}$: $$E_{max} = \frac{V}{a\,\ln(b/a)} \quad \text{at } r = a$$ --- #### Model Solution A(b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (2 m):** Compute $E_{max}$. $$E_{max} = \frac{V}{a\,\ln(b/a)} = \frac{150}{8 \times \ln(20/8)} = \frac{150}{8 \times 0.9163} = 20.47\ \text{kV/mm}$$ **Step 2 (1 m):** Compute the safety factor. $$SF = \frac{E_{bd}}{E_{max}} = \frac{60}{20.47} = 2.93$$ **Step 3 (2 m):** Compute the critical radius at which $E = 0.9 E_{max}$. $$0.9 \cdot \frac{V}{a\ln(b/a)} = \frac{V}{r_c \ln(b/a)} \;\Rightarrow\; r_c = \frac{a}{0.9} = \frac{8}{0.9} = 8.89\ \text{mm}$$ **Step 4 (2 m):** Compute stressed volume for $L = 1$ km $= 1000$ m. $$V_s = \pi (r_c^2 - a^2) L = \pi (8.89^2 - 8^2) \times 10^3\ \text{mm}^2\!\cdot\!\text{m}$$ $$V_s = \pi (79.03 - 64) \times 10^3 = \pi \times 15.03 \times 10^3\ \text{mm}^2 \cdot \text{m} = 47.22 \times 10^3\ \text{mm}^2\!\cdot\!\text{m}$$ $$V_s = 4.72 \times 10^4\ \text{mm}^2 \cdot \text{m} = 4.72 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^3 = 47.2\ \text{litres}$$ **Conclusion (results):** $E_{max} = 20.47$ kV/mm, $SF = 2.93$ (safe), $V_s = 47.2$ litres. --- > **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B] *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **sphere–sphere gap** arrangement used for measurement of peak impulse voltage. What are the conditions under which it provides near-uniform field? *(7 marks)* **(b)** A 50 cm diameter sphere gap is used to measure the peak value of a 1.2/50 μs impulse voltage. The gap spacing is 6 cm. The breakdown field strength of the air gap under the given impulse polarity is 31.5 kV/cm (peak). - (i) Calculate the peak voltage. - (ii) Comment on whether the gap is operating in the *near-uniform* regime. - (iii) If the atmospheric pressure drops to 0.95 atm, by what percentage does the breakdown voltage change? *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution B(a) — 7 Marks **Sketch description (2 m):** Two identical metal spheres of radius $R$ mounted on insulating supports, one sphere is connected to the HV source, the other to the ground. A micrometer screw sets the gap $S$ accurately. **Near-uniform condition (3 m):** The field is near-uniform when $S \le 0.5\,R$. This is the IEC 60052 / IS 1876 condition. For larger gaps ($S > 0.5R$), the field becomes non-uniform and the breakdown voltage deviates from the simple relation $V = 0.9 E_{bd} S$. **Why it is used (2 m):** Sphere gaps are *self-restoring* (the arc quenches itself), have a *well-defined and reproducible* breakdown characteristic, are *relatively cheap*, and can be calibrated by dimensional measurement (no need for absolute voltage reference). They are the **primary standard** for impulse voltage measurement up to about 2 MV (with large spheres). --- #### Model Solution B(b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (2 m):** Calculate peak voltage. $$V_{peak} = \frac{E_{bd} \cdot S}{0.9} = \frac{31.5 \times 6}{0.9} = 210\ \text{kV (peak)}$$ **Step 2 (2 m):** Check near-uniformity. $R = 25$ cm. $S/R = 6/25 = 0.24 < 0.5$ → **Yes**, the gap is in the near-uniform regime. The 0.9 factor is valid. **Step 3 (3 m):** Atmospheric correction. Standard air density at 1 atm and 20°C. The relative air density at 0.95 atm (same temperature) is $\delta = 0.95$. For impulse, the gap factor $b \approx 1.0$ (per IEC 60060-1). Hence: $$V_{new} = V_{old} \cdot \delta^b = 210 \times 0.95 = 199.5\ \text{kV}$$ $$\% \text{ change} = \frac{210 - 199.5}{210} \times 100\% = 5\% \text{ decrease}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (validated against KTU answer-key patterns):** > > 1. **Writing $E = V/d$ blindly** for non-uniform geometries → **deduct 2–3 marks**. Always derive the geometry-specific $E(r)$. > 2. **Forgetting to mention impulse polarity** in sphere-gap problems → **deduct 1 mark**. The IEC tables give $V_{50\%}$ for both polarities; the answer must be tagged. > 3. **Ignoring atmospheric correction** when the problem gives non-standard pressure/temperature → **deduct 1–2 marks**. > 4. **Confusing BIL (Basic Insulation Level)** with **BSL (Basic Switching Level)** — BIL is for 1.2/50 μs, BSL is for 250/2500 μs. Mixing them up → **deduct 1 mark**. > 5. **Omitting the units** in the final numerical answer → **deduct 0.5–1 mark** (KTU strictly enforces SI units). > 6. **Not stating assumptions** (e.g., “no space charge”, “end effects neglected”) in derivations → **deduct 0.5 mark** per derivation. > 7. **Writing “the dielectric strength of air is 3 kV/mm”** without specifying the *uniform field, 1 atm, 1.2/50 μs impulse* conditions → **deduct 1 mark**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4, Insulation Systems for Impulse Voltages** - ✅ **Electrical stress** = $E = -\nabla V$; varies with geometry; *peak* occurs at the electrode with **smallest radius of curvature**. - ✅ **Dielectric strength** is **NOT a constant** — it depends on *medium*, *geometry*, *duration*, *polarity*, and *pressure/temperature*. - ✅ **Stress–Strength Rule:** $E_{max} < E_{bd}/(\text{safety factor})$ at *every point* and *every instant*. - ✅ **Uniformity factor** $\eta = E_{avg}/E_{max}$: equals 1 for parallel plates; less than 1 for non-uniform. - ✅ **Utilization factor** $\xi = E_{max}/E_{bd}$: equals 1 at breakdown. - ✅ **Stressed volume** $V_s$ governs the statistical probability of failure → Weibull distribution. - ✅ **V–t law:** $V_{bd} \cdot t^{1/n} = \text{const}$ — shorter impulse → higher withstand. - ✅ **Self-restoring media** (air, SF₆, oil) can be tested at 100% BIL repeatedly; **non-self-restoring** (XLPE, paper) require lower test levels to avoid permanent damage. - ✅ **Standard waveforms:** 1.2/50 μs (lightning), 250/2500 μs (switching) per IEC 60060-1. - ✅ **Polarity effect:** Negative impulse generally gives *higher* breakdown in rod-plane geometry in air. - ✅ **Stress control techniques:** shielding rings, grading capacitors, stress-relief cones, ZnO surge arresters. - ✅ **Coaxial formula** $E_{max} = V/[a\ln(b/a)]$ and **sphere-gap formula** $V \approx 0.9\,E_{bd}\,S$ are **the two most-asked derivations** in KTU exams. - ✅ Always apply **atmospheric corrections** ($\delta$, $K$) per IEC 60060-1 in numerical problems. - ✅ Always state whether the medium is **self-restoring** or **non-self-restoring** — this decides the test philosophy (BIL vs BSL). - ✅ **Creepage distance** $L_c$ controls *surface* stress; **arcing distance** controls *air-gap* stress. Both must be designed for the **site pollution level**. - ✅ For **GIS**: SF₆ at 0.4–0.5 MPa gives ≈5× the breakdown strength of air in coaxial geometry. - ✅ For **cables**: design the inner radius $a$ such that $E_{max} \le 0.6 E_{bd}$ for a 50-year service life. --- *End of Topic Notes — PEEET633 / Module 4 / Insulation Systems for Impulse Voltages — Electrical Stress and Strength*

topics.title: Energy Storage - Impulse Capacitors (Energy Storage or Surge Capacitors)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Energy Storage - Impulse Capacitors (Energy Storage or Surge Capacitors) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Impulse Capacitors (Energy Storage / Surge Capacitors):** These are specialized high-voltage capacitors designed specifically to **store a large amount of electrical energy at very high DC voltages over a relatively long charging interval**, and then **release this stored energy almost instantaneously** in the form of a high-voltage, high-current impulse (surge) of microsecond duration. In High Voltage Engineering (HVE), these capacitors are the **prime energy source** for impulse voltage generators (e.g., **Marx generator**) and impulse current generators used to test the dielectric strength of electrical apparatus such as transformers, cables, insulators, and switchgear against lightning and switching surges. Unlike standard power-frequency capacitors that operate continuously at 50/60 Hz, impulse capacitors are characterized by: - **Very high operating DC voltages** (typically **50 kV to 400 kV** per unit stage) - **Low capacitance values** (typically **0.01 μF to 5 μF**) - **Ability to deliver extremely high peak discharge currents** (kA range) - **Low self-inductance** for steep wavefront generation - **Very low dielectric loss** at DC charging - **Robust internal construction** to withstand high electromagnetic and electrodynamic forces ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tower" Model Imagine a tall water tank connected to a small nozzle at the bottom: | Water Tower | Impulse Capacitor | |-------------|-------------------| | Water pumped in slowly over hours | Energy charged slowly over seconds | | Large volume stored at height (potential) | Energy stored at high DC voltage | | Valve opens → water gushes in seconds | Spark gap fires → surge released in μs | | Pressure = ρgh | Voltage = V (electrical potential) | | Volume stored = ρ × V_tank | Energy stored = ½CV² | > [!NOTE] > The **slow charging + sudden discharge** is the defining signature of an impulse/surge capacitor. The "surge" name originates from this rapid release of stored energy. ### 1.3 Why Are They Called "Surge" Capacitors? > [!NOTE] > **"Surge"** refers to the **transient overvoltage or overcurrent wave** (a "surge") that the capacitor helps generate. In power systems, surges are caused by **lightning strokes** (1.2/50 μs standard wave) and **switching operations** (250/2500 μs standard wave). Impulse capacitors simulate these surges in laboratories for insulation coordination studies. ### 1.4 Key Physical Metrics in Impulse Capacitors - **Energy density (W_v)**: Stored energy per unit volume, typically **50–200 kJ/m³**. - **Energy per stage (E_s)**: For a 0.1 μF capacitor charged to 100 kV, **E_s = 0.5 × 0.1 × 10⁻⁶ × (10⁵)² = 500 J**. - **Charging time (T_c)**: Typically **5 seconds to 60 seconds**. - **Discharge time (T_d)**: Microseconds (10⁻⁶ s) to milliseconds (10⁻³ s). - **Self-inductance (L_s)**: Must be **less than a few μH** for sharp wavefronts. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The working of an impulse capacitor is described in the following logical stages: 1. **Charging Stage (Slow Energy Accumulation):** - The capacitor is connected to a **high-voltage DC charging supply** (typically a half-wave or full-wave rectifier with a Cockcroft-Walton multiplier). - A **high-value charging resistor (R_c)** is placed in series to limit the charging current and form an **RC time constant** much larger than the desired charging time. - Voltage builds up gradually: $V_c(t) = V_s(1 - e^{-t/R_c C})$ where $V_s$ is the DC supply voltage. - At steady state, the capacitor charges to $V_0 = V_s$. 2. **Triggering Stage (Spark Gap Closure):** - When the capacitor voltage reaches the preset value $V_0$, the **sphere gap or trigatron** is triggered either automatically (self-breakdown) or externally (manually/optically). - The gap closes in **nanoseconds**, offering a near-short circuit across the capacitor. 3. **Discharge Stage (Rapid Energy Release):** - The stored energy is dumped into the **load/test object** through the **front resistor (R_f)** and **tail resistor (R_t)** which shape the impulse waveform. - For a simple RLC discharge: $V(t) = V_0 e^{-\alpha t} \cos(\omega_d t)$ where $\alpha = R/(2L)$ and $\omega_d = \sqrt{1/LC - \alpha^2}$. 4. **Recovery Stage:** - After discharge, the gap de-ionizes, and the capacitor is ready to be recharged for the next impulse. ### 2.2 Energy Storage Equation — The Core Concept > [!IMPORTANT] > The energy stored in a capacitor at voltage $V$ is derived from the work done to charge it: > $$E = \int_0^V v \cdot C \, dv = \frac{1}{2}CV^2 \quad \text{(Joules)}$$ This is the **single most important equation** for sizing impulse capacitors in HVE. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Physical Meaning | Units / Standard Values | |---|---------|--------------------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ | Energy stored in capacitor | Joules (J) | | 2 | $E_n = \frac{1}{2}CV_0^2$ | Energy stored per stage of Marx generator | J | | 3 | $E_T = n \cdot E_n$ | Total energy of n-stage Marx generator (parallel charging, series discharging) | J | | 4 | $C_{eq} = \frac{C}{n}$ | Equivalent discharge capacitance in n-stage Marx | F | | 5 | $V_T = n \cdot V_0$ | Output impulse voltage from n-stage Marx | V | | 6 | $V_c(t) = V_0(1 - e^{-t/\tau_c})$ | Charging voltage waveform, $\tau_c = R_c C$ | V | | 7 | $T_c \approx 5 R_c C$ | Approx. full charging time (5 time constants) | s | | 8 | $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/\tau_d}$ | Exponential discharge, $\tau_d = R_t C_{eq}$ | V | | 9 | $T_t = 0.7 R_t C_{eq}$ | Wave-tail time to 50% (≈ 0.69 R_t C) | s | | 10 | $T_f \approx 3 R_f C_{eq} / 0.7$ | Approx. wave-front time, $T_f \approx 3 R_f C_{eq}$ | s | | 11 | $\eta = \frac{E_{delivered}}{E_{stored}} = \frac{C V^2 - C V_{min}^2}{C V^2}$ | Charging/discharging efficiency of the Marx circuit | dimensionless | | 12 | $I_{peak} = V_0 \sqrt{\frac{C_{eq}}{L}}$ | Peak discharge current (underdamped RLC) | A | | 13 | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC_{eq}}}$ | Natural frequency of discharge loop | Hz | | 14 | $\zeta = \frac{R}{2}\sqrt{\frac{C_{eq}}{L}}$ | Damping ratio of discharge circuit | dimensionless | | 15 | $W_v = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}{d^2} V^2 \cdot V_{vol}$ | Volumetric energy density of a parallel-plate capacitor | J/m³ | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > - **Insulation Testing Labs**: All high-voltage equipment manufacturers (transformers, cables, GIS) must test insulation against standard lightning impulse (**1.2/50 μs**) and switching impulse (**250/2500 μs**) waves per **IEC 60060-1** and **IEEE Std 4**. > - **Surge Arrester Testing**: Impulse current generators use banks of surge capacitors discharged through arresters to test their energy absorption capability. > - **Particle Accelerator Pulsed Power**: Crow-bar circuits using impulse capacitors. > - **EMTP/PSCAD Simulation Calibration**: Real impulse capacitor parameters feed into transient simulators. ### 2.5 Constructional Details of Impulse Capacitors - **Dielectric**: Typically **impregnated kraft paper + mineral oil**, **polypropylene film + oil**, or **SF₆ gas** for ultra-high voltage units. - **Electrodes**: Aluminum foil, multiple series sections internally to distribute voltage stress. - **Bushings**: Epoxy or oil-impregnated paper bushings rated for the full DC voltage. - **Internal Inductance**: Minimized by **low-inductance wound foil construction** and **concentric terminal arrangement**. - **Self-Healing Designs**: Metalized electrodes for low-energy applications. - **Standard Ratings (Industry Examples)**: - 100 kV, 0.1 μF, 500 J per unit - 200 kV, 0.05 μF, 1000 J per unit - 400 kV, 0.025 μF, 2000 J per unit ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Stored in a Capacitor We begin with the fundamental definition of capacitance and voltage: $$q = C v$$ The incremental work done to move a small charge $dq$ against a voltage $v$ is: $$dW = v \, dq$$ Substituting $dq = C \, dv$: $$dW = v \cdot C \, dv$$ The total work done to charge the capacitor from 0 to $V$ is: $$W = \int_0^V v \cdot C \, dv = C \int_0^V v \, dv$$ Evaluating the integral: $$W = C \left[ \frac{v^2}{2} \right]_0^V = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$ Thus, the stored energy is: $$\boxed{E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2}$$ > This energy is stored entirely in the **electric field** between the capacitor plates, with energy density $u = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E^2$ (J/m³). --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Charging Equation of an Impulse Capacitor Consider the charging circuit: a DC source $V_s$ in series with charging resistor $R_c$ and capacitor $C$. Applying KVL: $$V_s = i R_c + v_c(t)$$ But $i = C \frac{dv_c}{dt}$, so: $$V_s = R_c C \frac{dv_c}{dt} + v_c(t)$$ Rearranging as a standard first-order ODE: $$R_c C \frac{dv_c}{dt} + v_c = V_s$$ $$\frac{dv_c}{dt} + \frac{1}{R_c C} v_c = \frac{V_s}{R_c C}$$ The integrating factor is $e^{t/(R_c C)}$. Multiplying both sides: $$\frac{d}{dt}\left[v_c e^{t/(R_c C)}\right] = \frac{V_s}{R_c C} e^{t/(R_c C)}$$ Integrating from 0 to $t$ with initial condition $v_c(0) = 0$: $$v_c(t) e^{t/(R_c C)} = V_s \left( e^{t/(R_c C)} - 1 \right)$$ $$v_c(t) = V_s \left( 1 - e^{-t/(R_c C)} \right)$$ Defining the **charging time constant** $\tau_c = R_c C$: $$\boxed{v_c(t) = V_s \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau_c} \right)}$$ The capacitor charges to **63.2% of $V_s$** in one time constant, **86.5%** in two, **95%** in three, and **99.3%** in five. Hence, the **practical full charging time** is: $$T_c \approx 5 R_c C$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Efficiency of a Marx Generator A Marx generator has $n$ stages, each with capacitance $C$ charged in **parallel** to $V_0$, then connected in **series** at discharge, giving an equivalent $C_{eq} = C/n$ charged to $V_T = nV_0$. **Energy stored** (during parallel charging): $$E_{stored} = n \times \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2 = \frac{n C V_0^2}{2}$$ **Available energy at discharge** (series connection): $$E_{avail} = \frac{1}{2} C_{eq} V_T^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{C}{n} \cdot (nV_0)^2 = \frac{n C V_0^2}{2}$$ These are equal! So the **energy is conserved** during the switching process (in an ideal, lossless Marx). The efficiency of the **charging circuit** (which matters in real systems due to losses in $R_c$ and back-resistors) is: $$\eta = \frac{E_{final}}{E_{input}} = \frac{(1/2) C V_0^2}{V_s \cdot I_{avg} \cdot T_c}$$ For a well-designed system, $\eta > 0.9$ (90%). --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (14-Mark Style) **Problem:** A single-stage impulse generator uses a $0.5 \, \mu F$ capacitor. It is required to produce a standard **1.2/50 μs lightning impulse** of peak value **200 kV** across a test object of capacitance 1000 pF. The wave-tail resistor is $R_t = 80 \, \Omega$. Calculate: (a) the required charging voltage, (b) the wave-front resistor, and (c) the energy delivered. **Solution:** **(a) Charging Voltage:** The peak output voltage equals the charging voltage (for a single-stage, ideal, unloaded generator): $$V_0 = V_{peak} = 200 \text{ kV} = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ V}$$ **(b) Energy Stored:** $$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times (2 \times 10^5)^2$$ $$E_{stored} = 0.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 \times 10^{10} = 10^4 \text{ J} = 10 \text{ kJ}$$ **(c) Wave-Front Resistor:** For a standard 1.2/50 μs wave, the wave-front time $T_1 = 1.2 \, \mu s$ corresponds to the **30%–90% rise time**, which approximates to: $$T_1 \approx 3.0 \times R_f \times C_{total}$$ where $C_{total} = C + C_{load} = 0.5 \, \mu F + 0.001 \, \mu F = 0.501 \, \mu F$ $$R_f = \frac{T_1}{3 C_{total}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}{3 \times 0.501 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.2}{1.503} \approx 0.8 \, \Omega$$ **(d) Wave-Tail Verification:** $$T_2 = 0.7 \times R_t \times C_{total} = 0.7 \times 80 \times 0.501 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$T_2 = 28.06 \times 10^{-6} \, s \approx 28 \, \mu s$$ > The actual standard tail time is 50 μs, so this means either the load capacitance dominates or $R_t$ should be increased. For an **open-circuit** test (no load), $C_{total} = C = 0.5 \, \mu F$ and $T_2 = 0.7 \times 80 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} = 28 \, \mu s$ — but standard requires 50 μs, indicating the test object typically has a damping resistor network. The numerical example above demonstrates the methodology. --- ### 3.5 Energy Loss in Charging Resistor The energy dissipated in $R_c$ during charging equals the energy stored in the capacitor (well-known result for RC charging from a DC source): $$E_{R_c} = \int_0^\infty i^2 R_c \, dt = \int_0^\infty \left(\frac{V_s}{R_c} e^{-t/\tau_c}\right)^2 R_c \, dt$$ $$E_{R_c} = \frac{V_s^2}{R_c} \int_0^\infty e^{-2t/\tau_c} dt = \frac{V_s^2}{R_c} \cdot \frac{\tau_c}{2} = \frac{V_s^2}{R_c} \cdot \frac{R_c C}{2}$$ $$\boxed{E_{R_c} = \frac{1}{2} C V_s^2 = E_{stored}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This means **50% of the input energy is always lost in the charging resistor** in a single-shot RC charging scheme. To improve efficiency, **constant-current charging** or **resonant (LC) charging** is used in modern high-energy impulse generators. ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **Define an impulse capacitor. How is it different from a power-frequency capacitor?** **Model Answer:** An impulse capacitor (or surge capacitor) is a specialized high-voltage capacitor designed to **store a large amount of electrical energy at high DC voltage** over a relatively long charging time and **release it almost instantaneously** in the form of a high-voltage, high-current impulse of microsecond duration, typically used in impulse voltage/current generators for dielectric testing of HV apparatus as per IEC 60060-1. | Feature | Power-Frequency Capacitor | Impulse Capacitor | |---------|---------------------------|-------------------| | Operating frequency | 50/60 Hz continuous | DC charged, discharged in μs | | Operating voltage | Low (415 V to 11 kV) | Very high DC (50 kV to 400 kV) | | Energy per unit | High capacitance, low voltage | Low capacitance, very high voltage | | Dielectric | Paper/oil/film (designed for AC losses) | Low-loss dielectric, oil-impregnated paper or SF₆ | | Discharge current | Low | Extremely high (kA range) | | Construction | Compact, oil-filled | Robust bushings, low-inductance design | | Application | Power factor correction, filters | Marx generators, impulse testing | **[3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for differentiation table]** --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Write the expression for the energy stored in an impulse capacitor. A 0.1 μF capacitor is charged to 200 kV. Calculate the stored energy.** **Model Answer:** The energy stored in a capacitor is given by: $$E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$ **Given:** $C = 0.1 \, \mu F = 0.1 \times 10^{-6} \, F$, $V = 200 \, kV = 2 \times 10^5 \, V$ $$E = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} \times (2 \times 10^5)^2$$ $$E = 0.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 4 \times 10^{10}$$ $$E = 2 \times 10^3 = 2000 \, J = 2 \, kJ$$ > This single 0.1 μF unit at 200 kV stores **2 kJ of energy**, enough to simulate a moderate lightning strike in the lab. **[3 marks: 1 for formula + 1 for substitution + 1 for final answer with unit]** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A [14 Marks] — Energy Storage & Wave-Shaping** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the energy stored in a capacitor. Explain the principle of operation of an impulse voltage generator (Marx circuit) with the help of a neat diagram. Show that the total energy available at the output of an *n*-stage Marx generator (each stage rated $C$, $V_0$) is the same as the total energy stored during charging. **(b)** [7 Marks] A 10-stage Marx impulse generator has each stage capacitance of $0.5 \, \mu F$ and each stage is charged to $120 \, kV$. The load (test object) capacitance is $1500 \, pF$. Calculate: 1. Total energy stored in the generator 2. The peak output voltage 3. The equivalent discharge capacitance 4. The charging time if $R_c = 5 \, k\Omega$ per stage --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation & Principle:** The energy stored in a capacitor charged to voltage $V$: Starting from the work done to transfer charge $dq$ across a potential difference $v$: $$dW = v \cdot dq = v \cdot C \, dv \quad (\text{since } q = Cv)$$ Integrating from 0 to $V$: $$W = \int_0^V C v \, dv = C \left[\frac{v^2}{2}\right]_0^V = \frac{1}{2}CV^2 \quad \text{(Joules)} \quad \text{...(i)}$$ **Working of Marx Generator:** The Marx circuit multiplies DC charging voltage into a high impulse voltage by charging $n$ capacitors in **parallel** through high-value charging resistors and then connecting them in **series** through triggered spark gaps at the instant of discharge. **Charging Phase:** All capacitors are charged in parallel to $V_0$ through $R_c$. The voltage across each capacitor is $V_0$. **Discharge Phase:** The first spark gap is triggered (or self-breaks), connecting the bottom of C₁ to ground. The top of C₁ is now at $+V_0$ relative to ground. This $+V_0$ potential appears across the second gap, doubling the voltage on it, causing it to break down. Cascading breakdown connects all stages in series, giving $V_T = n V_0$ across the load. **Energy Equivalence:** Energy stored in *n* stages (parallel charging): $$E_{stored} = n \times \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2 = \frac{nCV_0^2}{2}$$ Energy available at discharge (series connection, equivalent capacitance $C_{eq} = C/n$ at voltage $V_T = nV_0$): $$E_{avail} = \frac{1}{2} C_{eq} V_T^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{C}{n} \cdot (nV_0)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{C}{n} \cdot n^2 V_0^2 = \frac{nCV_0^2}{2}$$ Hence, $\boxed{E_{stored} = E_{avail} = \frac{1}{2} n C V_0^2}$ — **energy is conserved** in an ideal, lossless Marx switchover (no resistors in the discharge path during the reconfiguration). --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $n = 10$, $C = 0.5 \, \mu F = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \, F$, $V_0 = 120 \, kV = 1.2 \times 10^5 \, V$, $C_{load} = 1500 \, pF = 1.5 \times 10^{-9} \, F$, $R_c = 5 \, k\Omega$ per stage. **1. Total Energy Stored:** $$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} n C V_0^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times (1.2 \times 10^5)^2$$ $$E_{stored} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.44 \times 10^{10} = 36000 \, J = 36 \, kJ$$ **2. Peak Output Voltage:** $$V_T = n V_0 = 10 \times 120 = 1200 \, kV = 1.2 \, MV$$ **3. Equivalent Discharge Capacitance:** $$C_{eq} = \frac{C}{n} = \frac{0.5 \, \mu F}{10} = 0.05 \, \mu F = 5 \times 10^{-8} \, F$$ **4. Charging Time:** For parallel charging through $R_c$ per stage, the effective charging resistance seen by each capacitor is essentially $R_c$ (the resistors are connected such that each stage is fed through one $R_c$): $$\tau_c = R_c \times C = 5 \times 10^3 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \, s = 2.5 \, ms$$ Practical full charging time (5 time constants): $$T_c = 5 \tau_c = 5 \times 2.5 \, ms = 12.5 \, ms$$ > Note: In an actual Marx, the charging time is longer because each capacitor charges through multiple resistors in series (typically $2R_c$ per stage to prevent pre-firing). The exact formula depends on the resistor network topology. --- #### **Question B [14 Marks] — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]** **(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a **multi-stage impulse voltage generator**. Discuss the role of: 1. Charging resistors ($R_c$) 2. Wave-front resistors ($R_f$) 3. Wave-tail resistors ($R_t$) 4. Sphere gaps (triggering) **(b)** [7 Marks] An impulse generator must deliver a standard **1.2/50 μs lightning impulse** of peak **500 kV** to a test object of capacitance **2000 pF**. Each stage of the Marx is rated **100 kV, 1 μF**. Calculate: 1. Number of stages required 2. Energy stored per stage and total energy 3. The approximate wave-front resistance $R_f$ required 4. The approximate wave-tail resistance $R_t$ required --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Multi-Stage Impulse Voltage Generator — Roles of Components:** *(See the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for the circuit.)* **Working Principle:** 1. **Charging Phase:** All $n$ capacitors are charged in parallel through $R_c$ from a common HV DC source to voltage $V_0$. 2. **Triggering:** The first (lowest) sphere gap is triggered, often by an external **trigatron** pulse or by raising the voltage until self-breakdown. 3. **Cascade Breakdown:** The overvoltage caused by the first gap's collapse triggers the second gap, then the third, and so on, in nanoseconds. 4. **Series Discharge:** The capacitors are now effectively in series, applying $V_T = nV_0$ across the load. **Roles of Components:** 1. **Charging Resistor $R_c$ (typically 50 kΩ to 1 MΩ):** - Limits charging current from the DC source. - Forms an RC time constant $R_c C$ for slow, controlled charging. - During discharge, the back-resistors (parallel to gaps) **isolate** the un-triggered stages, preventing them from being short-circuited. 2. **Wave-Front Resistor $R_f$ (few Ω to tens of Ω):** - Placed in series between the generator output and the test object. - Together with $C_{eq}$ and load capacitance, forms the **RLC charging circuit** that determines the wave-front time $T_1 \approx 3 R_f C_{total}$. - Larger $R_f$ → longer (slower) wavefront. 3. **Wave-Tail Resistor $R_t$ (tens to hundreds of Ω):** - Placed in parallel across the test object (or as a damping resistor). - Discharges $C_{eq}$ through it, setting the wave-tail time $T_2 \approx 0.7 R_t C_{total}$. - Larger $R_t$ → longer wave tail. 4. **Sphere Gaps / Trigatron:** - Provides ultrafast switching (closure in < 100 ns). - Self-breakdown at preset voltage OR externally triggered for precise timing and synchronization. - Determines the **jitter** of the impulse (typically < 1 μs with triggered gaps). --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $V_{peak} = 500 \, kV$, $T_1 = 1.2 \, \mu s$, $T_2 = 50 \, \mu s$, $C_{load} = 2000 \, pF = 2 \times 10^{-9} \, F$, per stage $V_0 = 100 \, kV$, $C = 1 \, \mu F = 10^{-6} \, F$. **1. Number of Stages:** $$n = \frac{V_{peak}}{V_0} = \frac{500}{100} = 5 \text{ stages}$$ **2. Energy per Stage and Total Energy:** Per stage: $E_n = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-6} \times (10^5)^2 = 5000 \, J = 5 \, kJ$ Total: $E_{total} = n \times E_n = 5 \times 5000 = 25000 \, J = 25 \, kJ$ **3. Wave-Front Resistor $R_f$:** Equivalent discharge capacitance: $C_{eq} = C/n = 10^{-6}/5 = 0.2 \, \mu F = 2 \times 10^{-7} \, F$ Total capacitance during front: $C_{total} \approx C_{eq} + C_{load} = 2 \times 10^{-7} + 2 \times 10^{-9} \approx 2.02 \times 10^{-7} \, F$ For $T_1 = 1.2 \, \mu s \approx 3 R_f C_{total}$: $$R_f = \frac{T_1}{3 C_{total}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}{3 \times 2.02 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}{6.06 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.98 \, \Omega$$ So $R_f \approx 2 \, \Omega$. **4. Wave-Tail Resistor $R_t$:** For $T_2 = 50 \, \mu s \approx 0.7 R_t C_{total}$ (with $C_{total} \approx C_{eq}$ since load is small compared to bank): $$R_t = \frac{T_2}{0.7 \times C_{eq}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{0.7 \times 2 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{1.4 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 357 \, \Omega$$ So $R_t \approx 357 \, \Omega$ (≈ 360 Ω standard value). --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions in Impulse Capacitor Problems:** > > 1. **Unit Confusion in Energy Calculation:** Forgetting to convert kV to V and μF to F before squaring. Always write: $V$ in **volts**, $C$ in **farads**, $E$ in **joules**. A common error: writing $E = 0.5 \times 1 \times 100 = 50$ J (instead of 5000 J) — **-1 mark**. > > 2. **Forgetting the Factor of 5 in Charging Time:** The "full charge" time is $5\tau$, not $\tau$. Many students write $T_c = R_c C$ which is the **time constant**, not the practical charging duration. **-1 mark**. > > 3. **Mixing Up Series/Parallel Energy in Marx:** During **charging**, capacitors are in **parallel** (each charges to $V_0$); during **discharge**, they are in **series** (equivalent $C/n$, voltage $nV_0$). Many students incorrectly use the series capacitance for the stored energy. **-2 marks**. > > 4. **Wrong Wave-Front/Wave-Tail Formula:** $T_1 \approx 3R_f C$ (NOT $R_f C$), $T_2 \approx 0.7 R_t C$ (NOT $R_t C$). The factors 3 and 0.7 come from the **30%–90% rise definition** and **50% decay definition** of standard impulse waves. **-2 marks**. > > 5. **Ignoring Load Capacitance:** Test objects have $C_{load}$ in parallel with $C_{eq}$, affecting $T_1$ significantly. **-1 mark**. > > 6. **Not Drawing the Circuit Diagram:** A 7-mark question on Marx requires a **neat labeled diagram**. No diagram = **up to -2 marks** in KTU valuation. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Impulse Capacitors** > > - **Definition:** Capacitor that stores energy at HV DC and releases it as a microsecond-duration surge. > - **Energy Formula:** $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ (in joules; $C$ in farads, $V$ in volts). > - **Charging Equation:** $v_c(t) = V_s(1 - e^{-t/R_c C})$; full charge ≈ $5 R_c C$. > - **Discharging:** Through $R_f$ (front) and $R_t$ (tail); $T_1 \approx 3R_f C_{eq}$, $T_2 \approx 0.7 R_t C_{eq}$. > - **Marx Generator:** $n$ capacitors charged in parallel to $V_0$, discharged in series to give $V_T = nV_0$ and $C_{eq} = C/n$. > - **Energy Conservation in Marx:** $E_{total} = \frac{1}{2}nCV_0^2$ — same whether measured at charging or discharge. > - **Standard Lightning Impulse:** 1.2/50 μs per **IEC 60060-1**. > - **Standard Switching Impulse:** 250/2500 μs. > - **Energy lost in $R_c$:** 50% of input energy is dissipated as heat in the charging resistor (RC charging). > - **Peak Discharge Current:** $I_{peak} = V_0 \sqrt{C_{eq}/L}$ in an underdamped RLC loop. > - **Application:** Testing transformers, cables, bushings, insulators, surge arresters, GIS. > - **Construction:** Oil-impregnated paper or polypropylene film, multiple series sections, low-inductance terminals, SF₆ for UHV. > - **Efficiency Improvement:** Use **resonant LC charging** or **constant-current charging** to overcome the 50% loss in $R_c$. > - **Choice of Capacitor Rating:** Energy = $5 \times C_{load} \times V_{peak}^2$ (rule of thumb: stored energy ≈ 5× the energy delivered to the test object).

topics.title: Lightning Protection - Light and Laser Technology - X-ray Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lightning Protection - Light and Laser Technology - X-ray Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Lightning Protection

### Formal KTU Definition
**Lightning** is a transient, high-current electrostatic discharge of atmospheric origin occurring between a charged cloud and the earth, between two clouds, or within a single cloud, characterized by peak currents ranging from **1 kA to 200 kA** and rise times in the microsecond regime. **Lightning Protection** refers to the engineered scheme of shielding, earthing, bonding, and surge diversion designed to safely intercept, conduct, and dissipate the lightning current into the earth without causing flashover of the protected insulation or endangering personnel and equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module specifically requires knowledge of the *lightning stroke mechanism*, *types of surge arresters* (rod, horn, expulsion, valve, metal-oxide), *lightning protection angle method & rolling sphere method*, and the basic insulation level (BIL) coordination.

### Conceptual Analogy — Intuition
Imagine a giant water tank placed at the top of a hill during a storm. The tank is full of charge (water). Lightning is what happens when the tank "bursts" and the charge rushes downhill — anything in its path is destroyed. A lightning protection system is like installing a **safe, controlled drainage pipe** (the down-conductor) leading from the tank directly to a **sump** (earth electrode), so that when the charge bursts, it follows a known, engineered path that bypasses the house (the equipment). The **surge arrester** is a one-way safety valve that opens *only* when the voltage gets dangerously high.

> [!NOTE]
> A standard lightning impulse wave for testing is **1.2/50 μs** — meaning **1.2 μs** front time and **50 μs** time to half value, as per IEC 60060-1.

---

## 1.2 Light and Laser Technology

### Formal KTU Definition
**Light Technology** encompasses the study of electromagnetic radiation in the visible spectrum (~380 nm to 750 nm), the *photoelectric effect* (Einstein, 1905), and the optical phenomena (reflection, refraction, diffraction) used in high-voltage diagnostics. **Laser** (**L**ight **A**mplification by **S**timulated **E**mission of **R**adiation) is a device that produces a spatially coherent, monochromatic, and highly collimated beam through the quantum mechanical process of *stimulated emission* between population-inverted energy levels.

### Conceptual Analogy — Intuition
A normal lamp (like a bulb) is like a **crowd shouting randomly** in a stadium — every person emits light at different times, in different directions, and at different colors. A **laser** is like a **military marching band** where every musician plays the *same note*, *in step*, *in the same direction*. The output is therefore concentrated (collimated), pure (monochromatic), and orderly (coherent). The "fuel" that allows this synchronization is the **population inversion** — more atoms must be in the excited state than in the ground state.

> [!NOTE]
> The laser was first demonstrated by **Theodore Maiman in 1960** using a ruby crystal. KTU commonly tests on the **He-Ne laser (632.8 nm)** and the **ruby laser (694.3 nm)**.

---

## 1.3 X-ray Technology

### Formal KTU Definition
**X-rays** are high-frequency electromagnetic waves (wavelengths from **0.01 nm to 10 nm**, frequencies from **30 PHz to 30 EHz**) produced when high-velocity electrons are abruptly decelerated upon striking a metal target (Bremsstrahlung) or when inner-shell electrons of the target atom are knocked out and re-filled by outer-shell electrons (Characteristic radiation). In High Voltage Engineering, X-rays are used for **non-destructive diagnostic imaging** of insulation defects in cables, transformers, and bushings.

### Conceptual Analogy — Intuition
Think of throwing a tennis ball at a concrete wall. If the wall is soft (low-Z material), the ball just bounces off. But if the wall is hard (high-Z metal like tungsten), the ball **stops instantly** and you hear a *thud* — that energy has to go somewhere, and it gets converted to a "shout" (X-ray photon). The faster you throw (higher voltage), the louder the shout (shorter wavelength, more energetic X-ray). In a TV tube, electrons hit the screen gently and make only light. In an X-ray tube, electrons are accelerated by **30 kV to 400 kV** and hit a tungsten target, producing penetrating X-rays that can see through flesh, metal, or even insulation voids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Duane–Hunt Limit:** The minimum wavelength of X-rays produced is given by $\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{12400}{V} \text{ Å}$ where $V$ is the accelerating voltage in volts. This is a *frequently tested* KTU relationship.

---

## 1.4 Standard Reference Quantities (Constants Used in This Module)

| Constant | Symbol | Numerical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | **2.998 × 10⁸** | m/s |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴** | J·s |
| Elementary charge | $e$ | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹** | C |
| Electron rest mass | $m_e$ | **9.109 × 10⁻³¹** | kg |
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | **8.854 × 10⁻¹²** | F/m |
| Permeability of free space | $\mu_0$ | **4π × 10⁻⁷** | H/m |
| Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³** | /mol |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Lightning Stroke Mechanism (Stepped Leader & Return Stroke)

Lightning is initiated inside a **cumulonimbus cloud** where updrafts separate charges — typically positive charges accumulate at the top and negative charges at the base, with a small positive pocket at the very bottom. The negative charge at the cloud base induces a positive charge on the ground beneath.

The discharge develops in **stages**:

1. **Pilot Leader** — A faint, downward-moving ionized channel initiated by a small breakdown inside the cloud.
2. **Stepped Leader** — The pilot propagates toward the earth in discrete steps of **30–50 m** each, taking about **1 μs per step** (so 50 m / 1 μs ≈ 1.5 × 10⁵ m/s average, but instantaneous speeds reach 10⁶ m/s). Current ≈ 100 A.
3. **Connection Process** — As the stepped leader approaches within **30–50 m** of the ground, an *upward streamer* rises from ground objects (towers, trees, lightning rods). The two meet.
4. **Return Stroke** — The cloud-to-ground channel is now ionized. A massive wave of charge rushes from earth to cloud, producing a luminous return stroke. Current peaks at **10–200 kA**, with rise time of **0.5–10 μs**, peak temperature ~ **30,000 K** (five times the surface temperature of the Sun).
5. **Dart Leader & Subsequent Return Strokes** — In a multi-stroke flash, dart leaders travel cloud-to-ground along the existing channel, triggering subsequent return strokes.
6. **Continuing Current** — A low-amplitude (100–1000 A) current flowing for up to several hundred milliseconds, responsible for fire ignition.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "PPSC DC":** **P**ilot, **P**ilot/Stepped, **S**treamer connection, **C**loud-to-ground return, **D**art leaders, **C**ontinuing current.

---

## 2.2 The Surge Arrester — Operating Principle

A surge arrester (or *lightning arrester* / *surge diverter*) is connected **between the line conductor and earth**, in parallel with the equipment to be protected. Under normal conditions it behaves as an open circuit (high impedance). When a surge arrives, it switches to a **low-impedance path** within microseconds, clamps the voltage to a safe value, and diverts the surge current to earth. Once the surge passes, it returns to its high-impedance state.

### 2.2.1 Types of Surge Arresters (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Operating Principle | Gap Material | Non-linear Element | Modern Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rod Gap** | Simplest — two rods separated by air. Sparkover at fixed distance. | Atmospheric air | None | Backup, cheap, for lines |
| **Horn Gap** | Two diverging horns. Arc rises up due to heat, lengthens, extinguishes at current zero. | Air | None | Old substation equipment |
| **Expulsion Gap** | Arc inside a fiber tube produces deionizing gas that quenches arc at current zero. | Fiber / gas-generating tube | None | Distribution lines |
| **Valve (Silicon Carbide, SiC) Type** | Series spark gaps + SiC non-linear resistor. Gap isolates under normal voltage. | Air or SF₆ | **Silicon Carbide (SiC)** blocks | Largely obsolete |
| **Metal Oxide (Zinc Oxide, ZnO) Type** | No series gaps. Direct connection. ZnO has highly non-linear V–I characteristic. | None | **Zinc Oxide (ZnO)** | **Modern standard** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ZnO dominates:** Zinc oxide exhibits a non-linear coefficient $\alpha$ in $I = k \cdot V^{\alpha}$ with $\alpha \approx 50$ in the operating region, vs. SiC with $\alpha \approx 3\text{–}5$. This means ZnO conducts negligibly at normal voltage but heavily at surge voltage — *ideal* arrester behavior.

---

## 2.3 Photoelectric Effect & Laser Principles

### 2.3.1 Photoelectric Effect (Einstein, 1905)
When light of frequency $\nu$ strikes a metal surface, electrons are emitted if $h\nu \geq \phi$ (the work function).

$$E_k^{\max} = h\nu - \phi = h(\nu - \nu_0)$$

where $\nu_0 = \phi/h$ is the threshold frequency.

### 2.3.2 Einstein's Three Radiation Processes
For two energy levels $E_1$ (lower) and $E_2$ (upper) with population $N_1$ and $N_2$:

| Process | Direction | Rate |
|---|---|---|
| **Absorption** | Atom goes $E_1 \to E_2$ | $B_{12} \cdot \rho(\nu) \cdot N_1$ |
| **Spontaneous Emission** | Atom goes $E_2 \to E_1$ (random) | $A_{21} \cdot N_2$ |
| **Stimulated Emission** | Atom goes $E_2 \to E_1$ (induced by photon) | $B_{21} \cdot \rho(\nu) \cdot N_2$ |

For lasing, **stimulated emission must exceed absorption**, which requires **population inversion** $N_2 > N_1$ — a non-thermal condition.

---

## 2.4 X-ray Production Mechanism

In an X-ray tube, a tungsten filament (cathode) is heated to emit electrons via thermionic emission. These electrons are accelerated by a high voltage $V$ toward an angled tungsten target (anode) and strike it at high speed.

### Two mechanisms of X-ray production:

1. **Bremsstrahlung (Braking Radiation) — Continuous Spectrum**
   Electrons decelerate in the strong electric field of the target nuclei. The lost kinetic energy emerges as a photon. Energy conservation gives:
   $$h\nu = eV - \Delta KE$$
   The shortest wavelength (most energetic photon) occurs when all kinetic energy converts to one photon:
   $$eV = h\nu_{\max} = \frac{hc}{\lambda_{\min}}$$

2. **Characteristic Radiation — Line Spectrum**
   An incident electron ejects an inner-shell (K-shell) electron of the target atom. An outer-shell (L-shell) electron drops down to fill the vacancy, releasing a photon of energy equal to the shell-energy difference ($E_K - E_L$, etc.). This produces the **K-series, L-series** line spectra characteristic of the target material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Moseley's Law:** $\sqrt{\nu} = a(Z - b)$, where $Z$ is atomic number. This shows that characteristic X-ray frequency depends *only* on the element, not on how it is excited — making X-rays excellent for material identification.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surge impedance of line | $Z = \sqrt{L/C}$ | $L$: H/m, $C$: F/m, $Z$: $\Omega$ | Lossless line |
| 2 | Wave propagation velocity | $v = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ | m/s | Lossless line |
| 3 | Reflection coefficient | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | dimensionless | $Z_1, Z_2$ in $\Omega$ |
| 4 | Refraction (transmission) coefficient | $\tau = \dfrac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | dimensionless | Junction |
| 5 | 1.2/50 μs impulse peak voltage | $V(t) = V_0 \left(e^{-t/\tau_1} - e^{-t/\tau_2}\right)$ | $\tau_1 \approx 68.2\ \mu s$, $\tau_2 \approx 0.405\ \mu s$ | Standard lightning impulse |
| 6 | Standard switching impulse | 250/2500 μs wave | $V_0$: peak value | IEC 60060-1 |
| 7 | ZnO non-linear V–I law | $I = k V^{\alpha}$ | $\alpha \approx 50$ (operating), $\approx 5$ (pre-breakdown) | $V$ in pu of $V_{ref}$ |
| 8 | Photoelectric max KE | $E_{k,\max} = h\nu - \phi$ | Joules | $h\nu \geq \phi$ |
| 9 | Threshold frequency | $\nu_0 = \phi/h$ | Hz | Photoelectric onset |
| 10 | Duane–Hunt (min X-ray wavelength) | $\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV} = \dfrac{12400}{V}$ | $\lambda$ in Å, $V$ in volts | Bremsstrahlung cutoff |
| 11 | X-ray photon energy | $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ | Joules (or keV) | Single photon |
| 12 | Moseley's law | $\sqrt{\nu} = a(Z - b)$ | $a, b$: element-dependent constants | Characteristic X-rays |
| 13 | Einstein $B$ coefficient | $B_{21} = \dfrac{\pi e^2}{\varepsilon_0 m_e c h \nu}$ | SI units | Dipole-allowed transition |
| 14 | Spontaneous vs stimulated ratio | $\dfrac{A_{21}}{B_{21} \rho(\nu)} = \dfrac{8 \pi h \nu^3}{c^3}$ | — | Planck derivation |
| 15 | Population inversion condition | $N_2 > N_1$ | — | Lasing threshold |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** In the formula sheet, students frequently write $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ with vertical bar "absolute value" notation that breaks markdown tables. Always use $\Gamma = \frac{Z_2-Z_1}{Z_2+Z_1}$ in tables, OR write as $\Gamma = (Z_2-Z_1)/(Z_2+Z_1)$. The same caution applies to all "open" / "short" reflection cases.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lightning protection** directly determines BIL/Basic Switching Impulse Level (BSL) of substations, the placement of *shield wires* on transmission lines, the design of *down-conductor cross-sections* (minimum **50 mm² copper or equivalent** per IS/IEC 62305), and *earth resistance* limits (typically $\leq 10\ \Omega$ for substations, $\leq 5\ \Omega$ for sensitive equipment).
- **Lasers** are used for ultra-fast switching in laser-triggered spark gaps (LTSG), which can fire in picoseconds — essential for high-voltage pulse generation in pulsed power research and EMP simulators.
- **X-ray imaging** detects *voids* in solid insulation (XLPE cables), *delamination* in transformer paper, and *tracking scars* in polymeric bushings — without destroying the equipment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Velocity and Surge Impedance of a Lossless Transmission Line

Consider an elemental length $dx$ of a lossless single-phase transmission line with series inductance $L\,dx$ and shunt capacitance $C\,dx$. The voltage and current at one end are $v(x,t)$ and $i(x,t)$. After time $dt$, the values become $v(x+dx,t+dt) = v + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}dx$ and $i(x+dx,t+dt) = i + \frac{\partial i}{\partial x}dx$.

Apply KVL around the small loop containing the series inductance:

$$v - \left(v + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}dx\right) = L\,dx\,\frac{\partial i}{\partial t}$$

Simplify this KVL equation by dividing both sides by $dx$:

$$-\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = L\,\frac{\partial i}{\partial t}$$

Apply KCL at the node of the shunt capacitance:

$$i - \left(i + \frac{\partial i}{\partial x}dx\right) = C\,dx\,\frac{\partial v}{\partial t}$$

Simplify this KCL equation by dividing both sides by $dx$:

$$-\frac{\partial i}{\partial x} = C\,\frac{\partial v}{\partial t}$$

Now differentiate the KVL equation with respect to $x$ to get $\partial^2 v / \partial x^2$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 v}{\partial x^2} = -L\,\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{\partial i}{\partial t}\right) = -L\,\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\!\left(\frac{\partial i}{\partial x}\right)$$

Substitute the KCL expression for $\partial i / \partial x$ into the right-hand side:

$$\frac{\partial^2 v}{\partial x^2} = -L\,\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\!\left(-C\,\frac{\partial v}{\partial t}\right) = LC\,\frac{\partial^2 v}{\partial t^2}$$

This is the classical **wave equation**. Comparing it to the standard 1-D wave equation $\partial^2 v/\partial x^2 = (1/v^2)\partial^2 v/\partial t^2$, we identify:

$$\frac{1}{v^2} = LC \quad\Rightarrow\quad v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

For a lossless line, the *surge impedance* is the ratio of voltage to current of a forward-traveling wave. From the KVL solution, this ratio is $v/i = \sqrt{L/C}$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{Z = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}, \qquad v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check (overhead line, typical values):** $L \approx 1\ \mu\text{H/m}$, $C \approx 10\ \text{pF/m}$. Then $Z = \sqrt{10^{-6}/10^{-11}} = \sqrt{10^5} \approx 316\ \Omega$, and $v = 1/\sqrt{10^{-6}\cdot 10^{-11}} = 1/\sqrt{10^{-17}} \approx 3\times 10^8\ \text{m/s} = c$. This matches the speed of light — as it must for a transverse-electromagnetic wave in air.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Reflection and Transmission at a Junction of Two Lines

A surge wave of voltage $V_i$ and current $I_i$ traveling on a line of impedance $Z_1$ encounters a junction with a line of impedance $Z_2$. Let the reflected wave be $V_r, I_r$ and the transmitted wave be $V_t, I_t$.

Continuity of voltage at the junction:

$$V_i + V_r = V_t$$

Continuity of current at the junction (using $I = V/Z$ for forward, $I = -V/Z$ for backward):

$$\frac{V_i}{Z_1} - \frac{V_r}{Z_1} = \frac{V_t}{Z_2}$$

Substitute the first equation ($V_t = V_i + V_r$) into the second:

$$\frac{V_i}{Z_1} - \frac{V_r}{Z_1} = \frac{V_i + V_r}{Z_2}$$

Multiply both sides by $Z_1 Z_2$:

$$Z_2 V_i - Z_2 V_r = Z_1 V_i + Z_1 V_r$$

Collect the $V_r$ terms on one side and the $V_i$ terms on the other:

$$-Z_2 V_r - Z_1 V_r = Z_1 V_i - Z_2 V_i$$

$$-(Z_1 + Z_2) V_r = (Z_1 - Z_2) V_i$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\Gamma = \frac{V_r}{V_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}}$$

By direct substitution of $V_t = V_i + V_r = V_i(1 + \Gamma)$:

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{V_t}{V_i} = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example
A 400 kV transmission line has $Z_1 = 400\ \Omega$. It connects to a substation bus of surge impedance $Z_2 = 50\ \Omega$. A lightning surge of $V_i = 1000$ kV arrives.

Apply the reflection coefficient formula:

$$\Gamma = \frac{50 - 400}{50 + 400} = \frac{-350}{450} = -0.778$$

Apply the transmission coefficient formula:

$$\tau = \frac{2 \cdot 50}{50 + 400} = \frac{100}{450} = 0.222$$

Calculate the reflected voltage:

$$V_r = \Gamma \cdot V_i = -0.778 \cdot 1000 = -778\ \text{kV}$$

Calculate the transmitted voltage (the wave entering the substation):

$$V_t = \tau \cdot V_i = 0.222 \cdot 1000 = 222\ \text{kV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign of $V_r$ indicates phase inversion. The substation sees only 222 kV of the 1000 kV surge — a 78% reduction, illustrating the *purpose* of impedance mismatch and arrester protection.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Duane–Hunt Law and Minimum X-ray Wavelength

An electron of charge $e$ accelerated through a potential difference $V$ gains kinetic energy $E_k = eV$. Upon striking the target, in the *maximum-energy* case, this entire kinetic energy is converted into a single photon of frequency $\nu_{\max}$ (the cutoff frequency).

Apply the principle of energy conservation: kinetic energy lost by electron equals photon energy emitted.

$$eV = h\nu_{\max}$$

Express the photon energy in terms of wavelength using $\nu = c/\lambda$:

$$eV = \frac{hc}{\lambda_{\min}}$$

Solve this equation for the minimum wavelength $\lambda_{\min}$:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{hc}{eV}$$

Substitute the numerical constants $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$, $c = 2.998 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \cdot 2.998 \times 10^8}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot V}$$

Calculate the numerator:

$$6.626 \times 10^{-34} \cdot 2.998 \times 10^8 = 1.986 \times 10^{-25}\ \text{J·m}$$

Divide by the denominator (in SI):

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot V} = \frac{1.240 \times 10^{-6}}{V}\ \text{m}$$

Convert to Ångström ($1\ \text{Å} = 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$):

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1.240 \times 10^{-6}}{V \cdot 10^{-10}}\ \text{Å} = \frac{1.240 \times 10^{4}}{V}\ \text{Å} = \frac{12400}{V}\ \text{Å}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\min}\ (\text{Å}) = \frac{12400}{V\ (\text{volts})}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Favourite Question)
A 100 kV X-ray tube is operated at 50 kV. Find the minimum wavelength of X-rays emitted.

Apply the Duane–Hunt law with $V = 50{,}000$ V:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{12400}{50000} = 0.248\ \text{Å} = 0.0248\ \text{nm}$$

Verify by SI calculation. First calculate the photon energy in joules:

$$E = eV = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 5 \times 10^4 = 8.01 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{J}$$

Convert this energy to keV (1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J, so divide E by 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J and then by 1000):

$$E = \frac{8.01 \times 10^{-15}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 1000} = 50\ \text{keV}$$

Calculate the minimum wavelength from $\lambda = hc/E$:

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \cdot 2.998 \times 10^8}{8.01 \times 10^{-15}} = 2.48 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m} = 0.248\ \text{Å} \quad\checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: 1.2/50 μs Standard Lightning Impulse Waveform

The standard lightning impulse is the **double exponential**:

$$v(t) = V_0\left(e^{-t/\tau_1} - e^{-t/\tau_2}\right)$$

where $\tau_1 \gg \tau_2$. The standard defines:
- **Front time** $T_1 = 1.67 \cdot t_{90}$ where $t_{90}$ is the time to 90% of peak.
- **Time to half value** $T_2 = t_{50}$ where value falls to 50% of peak.

For a 1.2/50 μs wave, the standard values are $\tau_1 = 68.2\ \mu s$ and $\tau_2 = 0.405\ \mu s$.

Derivation of peak time: differentiate $v(t)$ and set to zero.

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = V_0\left(-\frac{1}{\tau_1}e^{-t/\tau_1} + \frac{1}{\tau_2}e^{-t/\tau_2}\right) = 0$$

Rearrange the differentiated equation by moving the negative term to the right-hand side:

$$\frac{1}{\tau_1}e^{-t/\tau_1} = \frac{1}{\tau_2}e^{-t/\tau_2}$$

Divide both sides by the smaller time constant factor:

$$\frac{\tau_2}{\tau_1} = e^{-t/\tau_2} \cdot e^{t/\tau_1} = e^{-t(1/\tau_2 - 1/\tau_1)}$$

Take natural log of both sides:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\tau_2}{\tau_1}\right) = -t\!\left(\frac{1}{\tau_2} - \frac{1}{\tau_1}\right) = -t \cdot \frac{\tau_1 - \tau_2}{\tau_1 \tau_2}$$

Solve for $t_{peak}$:

$$t_{peak} = \frac{\tau_1 \tau_2}{\tau_1 - \tau_2}\ln\!\left(\frac{\tau_1}{\tau_2}\right)$$

Substitute the standard values $\tau_1 = 68.2\ \mu s$ and $\tau_2 = 0.405\ \mu s$:

$$t_{peak} = \frac{68.2 \cdot 0.405}{68.2 - 0.405}\ln\!\left(\frac{68.2}{0.405}\right)$$

Calculate the numerator (product of time constants):

$$68.2 \cdot 0.405 = 27.621\ \mu s^2$$

Calculate the denominator (difference of time constants):

$$68.2 - 0.405 = 67.795\ \mu s$$

Compute the log term:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{68.2}{0.405}\right) = \ln(168.4) = 5.126$$

Combine all three terms to get the peak time:

$$t_{peak} = \frac{27.621}{67.795} \cdot 5.126 = 0.4074 \cdot 5.126 = 2.088\ \mu s$$

Peak value ratio (normalization factor):

$$V(t_{peak})/V_0 = e^{-2.088/68.2} - e^{-2.088/0.405} = e^{-0.0306} - e^{-5.156}$$

$$= 0.9699 - 0.0058 = 0.9641 \approx 0.964$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows the peak occurs at ≈ 2.1 μs, well past the 1.2 μs *nominal* front time. The 0.964 factor is sometimes used to scale $V_0$ so that the peak is exactly the rated value.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What (KTU Problem-Recognition Map)

| If the question asks for… | …use this | …with this assumption |
|---|---|---|
| Surge wave speed on a line | $v = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ | Lossless line, transverse EM |
| Voltage transmitted into a substation | $\tau = 2Z_2/(Z_2+Z_1)$ | Single junction, no reflection from terminations |
| Minimum X-ray wavelength | $\lambda_{\min} = 12400/V$ (Å) | Bremsstrahlung cutoff |
| Maximum X-ray photon energy | $E_{\max} = eV$ | Single-photon conversion of full KE |
| Photoelectric electron KE | $h\nu - \phi$ | $h\nu \geq \phi$ (threshold) |
| Whether lasing is possible | Check $N_2 > N_1$ | Two-level system needs pumping (3 or 4-level systems) |
| 50% discharge time of a 1.2/50 wave | $T_2 = 50\ \mu s$ by definition | Standard impulse |
| Protective zone of a rod | Cone of half-angle $45°$ to $60°$ | Simple "cone of protection" (conservative) |
