# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Lightning & X-ray Module**
> - Marks are *most often lost* by students who: (1) **confuse BIL (Basic Insulation Level) with BSL (Basic Switching Level)** — they are different for the same voltage class. (2) Forget the **negative sign** of the reflection coefficient for $Z_2 < Z_1$ and quote only the magnitude. (3) Use $V$ in kV directly in the Duane–Hunt formula $\lambda = 12400/V$ — remember $V$ must be in **volts**, otherwise you get answers off by a factor of 1000. (4) Fail to mention **population inversion** as a *prerequisite* for lasing — it is the single most-tested sub-bullet in the laser question.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Question)]**
**Define lightning. With a neat sketch, describe the formation of a stepped leader during a negative cloud-to-ground lightning discharge. State typical values of return stroke current and its rise time. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer (3-mark structured response):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Lightning is a transient, high-current electrostatic discharge of atmospheric origin between a charged cloud and the earth, between two clouds, or within a single cloud. It is characterized by peak currents of **1 kA to 200 kA** and a typical flash duration of about **0.2 seconds**.

**Stepped Leader Formation (1 mark):**
A cumulonimbus cloud develops a negative charge center at its base. This induces a positive charge on the ground beneath. A faint ionized channel called the **pilot leader** initiates inside the cloud and moves downward. It then transitions to a **stepped leader** that advances in discrete jumps of about **30–50 m** every **1 microsecond**, with average velocity ≈ 1.5 × 10⁵ m/s, leaving behind a highly ionized channel.

**Return Stroke Typical Values (1 mark):**
When the stepped leader approaches within **30–50 m** of the ground, an upward streamer rises to meet it, completing the path. The return stroke propagates upward at ≈ 10⁸ m/s, with peak current values in the range of **10–200 kA** and a current rise time of **0.5 to 10 microseconds**. The peak temperature inside the channel reaches approximately **30,000 K**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Question)]**
**State the Duane–Hunt law. An X-ray tube is operated at 60 kV. Calculate the minimum wavelength of X-rays emitted. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement of Law (1 mark):**
The Duane–Hunt law states that the *minimum* wavelength (cutoff wavelength) of the continuous X-ray spectrum produced by an X-ray tube is inversely proportional to the accelerating voltage $V$ applied across the tube. It is derived from the principle of energy conservation, in which the entire kinetic energy of a single incident electron is converted into a single X-ray photon of maximum possible energy. The law is mathematically expressed as:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{hc}{eV} = \frac{12400}{V}\ \text{Å}$$

where $V$ is in volts.

**Calculation of Voltage in Volts (1 mark):**
Convert the operating voltage 60 kV into base SI units of volts:

$$V = 60\ \text{kV} = 60 \times 10^3\ \text{V} = 60000\ \text{V}$$

**Application of the Duane–Hunt Formula (1 mark):**
Substitute $V = 60000$ V into the Duane–Hunt equation:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{12400}{60000} = 0.2067\ \text{Å} = 2.067 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m} = 0.0207\ \text{nm}$$

Therefore, the minimum wavelength of X-rays emitted by a 60 kV X-ray tube is approximately **0.207 Å**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Two choices (A and B) are offered and you must answer *one*.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Lightning Arrester Dominant

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model Question)]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat V–I characteristic curve, explain the operation of a **Metal-Oxide (Zinc-Oxide) surge arrester**. Compare it with the conventional Silicon-Carbide (SiC) valve-type arrester. State the advantages of ZnO arresters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 kV substation has a surge arrester rated 198 kV (peak). A switching surge of peak value 600 kV impinges on the line of surge impedance 400 Ω which is connected to a bus of surge impedance 60 Ω. Calculate (i) the voltage transmitted into the substation bus, (ii) the reflected voltage back into the line, and (iii) the surge current that flows into the bus. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**V–I Characteristic of ZnO Arrester (3 marks):**
The ZnO arrester exhibits a *highly non-linear* V–I characteristic that can be mathematically expressed in three operating regions:

**Region 1 — Low-current region (pre-breakdown):** Below about 0.8 pu of the rated voltage, the arrester draws only microamperes of leakage current, and the device behaves essentially as a high-impedance insulator. The non-linear exponent is small here, typically $\alpha \approx 5$.

**Region 2 — Operating region (normal surge conduction):** When the applied voltage exceeds the reference voltage, the arrester enters its normal operating region. The current rises steeply from milliamperes to kiloamperes for a very small voltage increase, with a non-linear exponent $\alpha \approx 50$. This is the working region that defines the *protective level* of the arrester.

**Region 3 — High-current region (severe surge):** During a heavy surge, the arrester conducts tens of kiloamperes, and the V–I curve flattens somewhat ($\alpha \approx 25$). The voltage is still clamped to a value only slightly above the protective level.

**Comparison with SiC Valve Arrester (2 marks):**

| Feature | SiC Valve Arrester | ZnO Arrester |
|---|---|---|
| Series spark gap | **Required** (to isolate SiC from continuous voltage) | **Not required** (ZnO blocks voltage inherently) |
| Non-linear exponent $\alpha$ | ≈ 3–5 (less non-linear) | ≈ 30–50 (highly non-linear) |
| Residual (discharge) voltage | Higher | **Lower** (better protection) |
| Follow current | Must be interrupted at current zero | **Negligible** (no gap) |
| Response time | Slower (gap must break down) | **Faster** (instantaneous) |
| Size and weight | Bulky | **Compact** |
| Modern usage | Obsolete | **Standard** |

**Advantages of ZnO Arresters (2 marks):**
1. **No series gap** — eliminates gap flashover problems, gives faster response.
2. **High non-linearity** — handles a wide range of surge currents with small voltage variation.
3. **Low residual voltage** — better protection for modern, low-BIL equipment.
4. **No follow current** — no need for arc-quenching mechanism.
5. **Smaller, lighter, more reliable** in contaminated environments.
6. **Longer service life** and lower failure rate.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Given Data:**
- Rated arrester voltage (peak): 198 kV
- Incoming surge voltage: $V_i = 600$ kV
- Line surge impedance: $Z_1 = 400\ \Omega$
- Bus (substation) surge impedance: $Z_2 = 60\ \Omega$

**(i) Transmitted (Refracted) Voltage — 3 marks:**

Apply the refraction coefficient formula:

$$\tau = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1} = \frac{2 \times 60}{60 + 400} = \frac{120}{460} = 0.2609$$

Calculate the transmitted voltage:

$$V_t = \tau \cdot V_i = 0.2609 \times 600 = 156.5\ \text{kV}$$

**(ii) Reflected Voltage — 2 marks:**

Apply the reflection coefficient formula:

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} = \frac{60 - 400}{60 + 400} = \frac{-340}{460} = -0.7391$$

Calculate the reflected voltage:

$$V_r = \Gamma \cdot V_i = -0.7391 \times 600 = -443.5\ \text{kV}$$

The negative sign indicates a phase-inverted reflected wave, which will partially cancel the incoming surge — a beneficial effect.

**(iii) Surge Current into the Bus — 2 marks:**

The current flowing into the bus is the transmitted current, equal to the transmitted voltage divided by the bus surge impedance:

$$I_t = \frac{V_t}{Z_2} = \frac{156.5\ \text{kV}}{60\ \Omega} = \frac{156{,}500\ \text{V}}{60\ \Omega} = 2608\ \text{A} \approx 2.61\ \text{kA}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Arrester Clamping:** Although we calculated $V_t = 156.5$ kV, this is the *unprotected* transmitted voltage. In practice, the ZnO arrester will clamp the voltage to ≤ 198 kV. The calculation demonstrates the impedance mismatch benefit *before* the arrester operates.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Laser & X-ray Dominant

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Question)]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Ruby laser**. State the functions of (i) pumping source, (ii) optical resonator, and (iii) active medium. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the production of **continuous (Bremsstrahlung) and characteristic X-rays**. An X-ray tube is operated at 80 kV. Find (i) the maximum photon energy, (ii) the minimum wavelength emitted, and (iii) the velocity of the electrons striking the target. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**Block Diagram of Ruby Laser (described in words — 1 mark for the description, see Section 4.3):**

A ruby laser consists of:
- **Pumping source** — a helical xenon flash lamp surrounding the ruby rod, fired for a few milliseconds.
- **Active medium** — a cylindrical rod of pink ruby (Al₂O₃ doped with ≈ 0.03% Cr³⁺ ions), typically 1–2 cm diameter and 2–10 cm long, with two flat, parallel, polished ends.
- **Optical resonator** — the two ends of the rod are silvered: one end 100% reflective, the other partially (≈ 95%) reflective to allow output coupling.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Optical Pumping:** The xenon flash lamp delivers intense green-violet light (≈ 550 nm) to the ruby rod. The Cr³⁺ ions absorb this radiation and are excited from the ground state (³A₂) to the upper pump band (⁴F₂).

**Step 2 — Non-radiative decay to metastable state:** The excited Cr³⁺ ions quickly relax (non-radiatively) from the upper pump band to the intermediate metastable state (²E level). The metastable state has a long lifetime of ≈ 3 ms — about 10⁵ times longer than typical excited states — allowing population to *accumulate*.

**Step 3 — Population inversion:** The strong pumping continues until the population of the metastable ²E state exceeds that of the ground state ³A₂ state. This is the *population inversion* $N_2 > N_1$, which is the prerequisite for lasing.

**Step 4 — Stimulated emission & amplification:** A spontaneous photon emitted along the rod axis is reflected back and forth between the two mirrors. Each round trip triggers a *cascade* of stimulated emissions from other Cr³⁺ ions, producing an exponentially growing coherent beam. The wavelength emitted is the characteristic ruby wavelength of **694.3 nm** (red).

**Step 5 — Output coupling:** After sufficient amplification, a portion of the photon flux escapes through the partially-silvered end as the **laser output pulse**. Ruby lasers operate in *pulsed mode* with peak powers in megawatts and pulse durations of a few milliseconds.

**Functions (2 marks):**
- **Pumping source** — provides the external energy to achieve population inversion in the active medium.
- **Optical resonator** — provides positive feedback by reflecting photons back through the medium, sustaining and amplifying stimulated emission.
- **Active medium** — the gain medium that amplifies light through stimulated emission; in ruby laser it is the Cr³⁺ ion.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Production of Continuous (Bremsstrahlung) X-rays (2 marks):**

In an X-ray tube, electrons emitted by thermionic emission from a heated tungsten cathode are accelerated by a high voltage $V$ toward a tungsten target anode. As these high-speed electrons enter the strong electric field of the nuclei in the target material, they undergo *rapid deceleration* (German: *Bremsung* = braking). The kinetic energy lost by the electron is emitted as an X-ray photon. The energy of each photon is:

$$h\nu = eV - \Delta KE$$

where $\Delta KE$ is the residual kinetic energy of the electron after the interaction. Since $\Delta KE$ can range from 0 to $eV$ (depending on how close the electron passes to a nucleus), the emitted photon has a *continuous* spectrum of energies from 0 up to $eV$, with the upper limit given by the Duane–Hunt law. This produces the **continuous X-ray spectrum** (white spectrum, analogous to visible white light).

**Production of Characteristic X-rays (1 mark):**

Occasionally, an incident electron collides *directly* with an inner-shell (K-shell or L-shell) electron of a target atom, knocking it completely out of the atom. The vacancy is filled almost instantly by an electron from a higher shell (L, M, etc.). The energy difference between the two shells is released as an X-ray photon of *discrete, well-defined* energy:

$$h\nu = E_{\text{outer}} - E_{\text{inner}}$$

These are called *characteristic* X-rays because their energies are unique signatures of the target element. They are grouped into the **K-series** (Kα, Kβ) and **L-series** (Lα, Lβ, ...), named after the shell where the original vacancy was created.

**Given Data:**
- Accelerating voltage: $V = 80\ \text{kV} = 80{,}000\ \text{V}$

**(i) Maximum Photon Energy — 2 marks:**

Apply the energy conservation principle, where the full electron kinetic energy converts to a single photon:

$$E_{\max} = eV = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 80{,}000 = 1.282 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{J}$$

Convert to keV (1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J, so divide by 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ to get eV, then by 1000 for keV):

$$E_{\max} = \frac{1.282 \times 10^{-14}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 8.0 \times 10^4\ \text{eV} = 80\ \text{keV}$$

**(ii) Minimum Wavelength — 1 mark:**

Apply the Duane–Hunt law:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{12400}{V\ (\text{V})} = \frac{12400}{80000} = 0.155\ \text{Å} = 1.55 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m} = 0.0155\ \text{nm}$$

**(iii) Electron Velocity at the Target — 1 mark:**

Use the work-energy theorem: kinetic energy gained equals the work done by the electric field.

$$\frac{1}{2} m_e v^2 = eV$$

Solve the kinetic energy equation for velocity $v$:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 eV}{m_e}}$$

Substitute the numerical values $eV = 1.282 \times 10^{-14}$ J and $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.282 \times 10^{-14}}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}}} = \sqrt{2.815 \times 10^{16}} = 1.678 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Relativistic check:** $v/c = 1.678 \times 10^8 / 2.998 \times 10^8 = 0.560$. At 80 kV, the velocity is about 56% of the speed of light. This is the threshold where relativistic corrections start to matter (> 10% of $c$). KTU 2024 typically expects the non-relativistic formula *unless* the voltage is above ~ 100 kV. We mention the relativistic check for completeness.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### ⚡ Lightning
- Lightning peak current: **1–200 kA**; return-stroke rise time: **0.5–10 μs**.
- Stroke stages memory code: **P**ilot → **S**tepped leader → **C**onnection streamer → **R**eturn stroke → **D**art leader → **C**ontinuing current.
- Standard test impulse: **1.2/50 μs** (lightning), **250/2500 μs** (switching).
- **BIL** = Basic Insulation Level for lightning impulses; **BSL** = Basic Switching Level for switching impulses.
- **Protection angle** of a lightning rod: typically **45°–60°**; modern design uses the **rolling sphere method** (radius depends on protection level).
- Earth resistance target: **≤ 10 Ω** (substation), **≤ 5 Ω** (sensitive equipment).
- Down-conductor minimum: **50 mm² Cu** (or equivalent).

### 🔌 Surge Arresters
- **Rod gap**: cheapest, crude, no follow-current extinction — used as backup.
- **Horn gap**: arc is elongated by heat, self-extinguishes.
- **Expulsion gap**: gas blast from fiber tube quenches arc.
- **SiC valve**: gap + SiC resistor ($\alpha \approx 3\text{–}5$) — obsolete.
- **ZnO metal-oxide**: no gap, $\alpha \approx 50$ in operating region — **modern standard**.
- Non-linear law: $I = k \cdot V^{\alpha}$.
- Reflection coefficient: $\Gamma = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$.
- Transmission coefficient: $\tau = 2Z_2/(Z_2 + Z_1)$.

### 🔦 Lasers
- LASER = **L**ight **A**mplification by **S**timulated **E**mission of **R**adiation.
- Three Einstein processes: **absorption**, **spontaneous emission**, **stimulated emission**.
- Population inversion $N_2 > N_1$ is a *prerequisite* for lasing.
- Properties: **monochromatic**, **coherent**, **collimated** (high directionality).
- Ruby laser: Cr³⁺ in Al₂O₃, **694.3 nm** red, pulsed operation.
- He-Ne laser: **632.8 nm** red, continuous wave, common in laboratories.
- He-Ne and ruby use **optical pumping**; semiconductor (GaAs) lasers use **electrical injection**.

### ☢️ X-rays
- Wavelength range: **0.01 nm to 10 nm**; frequency: **30 PHz – 30 EHz**.
- Production mechanisms: **Bremsstrahlung** (continuous) + **Characteristic** (line spectrum).
- **Duane–Hunt law**: $\lambda_{\min} = 12400/V$ (Å, V in volts).
- Maximum photon energy: $E_{\max} = eV$.
- **Moseley's law**: $\sqrt{\nu} = a(Z-b)$ — X-rays identify elements.
- Tubes operate at **30 kV to 400 kV**; targets made of high-Z material (tungsten, molybdenum).
- **HVE applications**: void detection in XLPE cables, transformer paper inspection, busbar crack analysis.
- **Safety**: X-rays are ionizing radiation — lead shielding, dosimetry, and interlock interlocks are mandatory.

### 📋 KTU Exam-Ready One-Liners (verbatim recall)
- "Lightning is a transient high-current electrostatic discharge, peak 10–200 kA, rise time μs."
- "A surge arrester is connected between line and earth, in parallel with equipment, switching to low-impedance on surge."
- "ZnO is preferred over SiC because its non-linear exponent α is much higher (≈ 50 vs 3–5)."
- "Duane–Hunt law: $\lambda_{\min} = hc/eV = 12400/V$ Å — the shorter the wavelength, the higher the energy."
- "Lasing requires (i) population inversion, (ii) optical resonator, (iii) pump source exceeding threshold."
- "Characteristic X-rays depend on the target material; Bremsstrahlung depends on the accelerating voltage."

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Tip:** In KTU 2024, the most-likely 14-mark question will combine *lightning protection* with *surge arrester types* (Theory + Comparison). For the 14-mark numerical, expect either a *reflection/transmission* problem or an *X-ray wavelength/photon energy* problem. Memorize the Duane–Hunt formula and the reflection coefficient formula — they cover ~70% of the calculation marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrostatic Particle Precipitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electrostatic Particle Precipitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Electrostatic Particle Precipitation (ESP)** is a high-efficiency gas-cleaning technology that uses electrostatic forces — generated by a high-voltage DC field — to remove suspended fine particulate matter (dust, smoke, fly-ash, aerosols) from a flowing gas stream. The device employed is called an **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)**, and it operates on the principle of **corona discharge** and **Coulombic attraction** between charged particles and oppositely charged collecting electrodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The student must clearly distinguish between the **two-stage process of particle removal**:
> 1. **Particle Charging** — imparting a charge to the dust particle.
> 2. **Particle Collection** — migrating the charged particle to a collecting electrode under the influence of an electric field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Corona Discharge:**
> A *self-sustaining* electrical discharge that occurs in a highly non-uniform electric field (typically between a small-radius wire/discharge electrode and a large-radius collecting electrode) when the applied voltage exceeds a critical value, ionizing the surrounding gas and producing a faint glow (visible only in darkness) called **corona**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnetic Dust Catcher" Intuition

Imagine standing at a busy construction site where dust is flying everywhere. You cannot stop the wind, but you *can* give every dust particle a tiny "sticky magnet" (a charge) and then place a giant metal wall (collecting electrode) on the path of the wind. The dust particles, now magnetized, get yanked sideways and stick to the wall, while clean air passes through.

In an ESP:
- The **wire electrode (discharge electrode)** is the "charger gun" — it fires ions into the air, and these ions attach themselves to dust particles.
- The **collecting electrode (plate/pipe)** is the "sticky wall" — once particles touch it, they surrender their charge and are removed (by rapping, washing, etc.).
- The **DC high-voltage source** is the "force field generator" — without it, no ions, no charging, no collection.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Real-World Application:** ESPs are the workhorses of pollution control in **coal-fired thermal power plants**, **cement kilns**, **steel blast furnaces**, **paper mills**, and **sulphuric acid plants**. Modern ESPs achieve efficiencies greater than **99.9 %** on fine particulates (PM2.5 and PM10).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol |
|---|---|
| Permittivity of free space, $\varepsilon_0$ | **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** |
| Typical operating DC voltage | **30 kV to 100 kV** (per section) |
| Typical corona current density | **0.1 to 1 mA/m²** of collecting plate |
| Particle sizes efficiently collected | **0.01 µm to 100 µm** |
| Air breakdown strength (corona onset) | **~30 kV/cm** near wire electrode |
| Ion mobility in air (negative ions) | **~1.5 × 10⁻⁴ m²/(V·s)** |
| Operating temperature range | **Up to 450 °C** (industrial ESPs) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The functioning of an Electrostatic Precipitator is governed by a sequence of four interlinked physical phenomena:

**Step 1 — Corona Generation (The "Ignition")**
- A high negative DC voltage (typically 30–100 kV) is applied between a thin **discharge electrode (wire/rod)** and a much larger **collecting electrode (plate/pipe)**.
- Because the wire is of very small radius, the electric field is **highly non-uniform** — extremely intense near the wire surface.
- When the field intensity exceeds the **ionization threshold of air** (~3 × 10⁶ V/m), gas molecules in the immediate vicinity of the wire get ionized.
- The wire is surrounded by a bluish-purple ionized region called the **corona**, sustained by **electron avalanche** and **secondary ionization** processes.

**Step 2 — Particle Charging (The "Loading")**
- Ions generated in the corona region are accelerated outward by the electric field.
- They collide with and adhere to the suspended dust particles, transferring charge.
- Two mechanisms act, depending on particle size:
  - **Field Charging (Boltzmann Charging)** — dominant for particles **> 1 µm**; ions follow field lines and impact the particle.
  - **Diffusion Charging** — dominant for particles **< 0.5 µm**; random thermal motion of ions deposits charge on the particle.

**Step 3 — Particle Migration (The "Push")**
- A charged particle of charge $q$ in an electric field $E$ experiences a **Coulomb force** $F = qE$.
- This force drives the particle toward the collecting electrode, while the gas stream drags it forward.
- The particle moves with a terminal transverse velocity called the **drift velocity** or **migration velocity** $\omega$ (omega).
- The gas drag, in turn, opposes motion according to **Stokes' Law** $F_d = 3\pi \mu d_p v$ (for laminar flow).

**Step 4 — Particle Collection and Removal (The "Capture & Clean")**
- When the charged particle strikes the collecting electrode, it surrenders its charge and adheres to the surface.
- The accumulated dust layer is periodically dislodged by **rapping** (mechanical impact) — in dry ESPs — or by **washing with water** — in wet ESPs.
- The collected dust falls into a hopper for disposal or recycling.

### 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How'

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DC and not AC?**
> AC fields would create oscillating corona — particles would be charged and discharged in rapid succession, with no net drift. A **steady unidirectional DC field** allows continuous charging and continuous drift toward the collector. This is the single most important reason for using DC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a thin wire and a flat plate?**
> The geometric asymmetry creates a **non-uniform field**. A uniform field (e.g., between two parallel plates) would not initiate corona — both electrodes would have identical field strength. The intense local field near the thin wire is what triggers ionization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why negative corona is preferred over positive corona?**
> - Negative corona is **more stable** and produces a **higher current density** for the same applied voltage.
> - It operates at a **lower onset voltage** (≈ 30 % less than positive corona).
> - Negative ions (electrons + O₂⁻) have **higher mobility**, charging particles faster.
> - Positive corona tends to produce **more ozone and NOₓ**, which is undesirable in industrial exhausts.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{V}{r \ln(R/r)}$ | Electric field at radius $r$ in a **wire-cylinder** ESP (Peek's field relation) | V/m |
| 2 | $V_c = 3 \times 10^6 \cdot r \cdot m \cdot \ln(R/r)$ | **Corona onset voltage** (Peek's law) — $m$ = surface irregularity factor (0.6–1.0) | V |
| 3 | $q_f = 3 \pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{\varepsilon_r + 2} \cdot d_p^2 \cdot E$ | **Field charging** — saturation charge on a particle (Boltzmann) | C |
| 4 | $q_d = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 d_p k T}{e} \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{e^2 N_0 d_p v_i t}{4 \varepsilon_0 k T}\right)$ | **Diffusion charging** charge (White's equation) | C |
| 5 | $\omega = \dfrac{q E_p}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$ | **Drift / migration velocity** of a charged particle (Stokes drag balance) | m/s |
| 6 | $\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{A \cdot \omega}{Q}\right)$ | **Deutsch–Anderson Efficiency Equation** (collection efficiency) | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 7 | $I = \dfrac{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 \mu_i}{R^2 \ln(R/r)} \cdot V(V - V_c)$ | **Corona current** in wire-cylinder geometry (Townsend relation) | A |
| 8 | $v_d = \mu_i E$ | **Ion drift velocity** — $\mu_i$ = ion mobility | m/s |
| 9 | $F_e = qE$ | **Coulomb force** on a charged particle | N |
| 10 | $F_d = 3 \pi \mu d_p v$ | **Stokes' drag** (laminar, $Re < 1$) | N |

**Symbol Glossary (no `|` characters used):**
- $V$ = applied DC voltage, $V_c$ = corona onset voltage
- $R$ = radius of collecting cylinder (or half-spacing of plates)
- $r$ = radius of discharge wire
- $d_p$ = particle diameter
- $E, E_p$ = electric field / field at particle location
- $\varepsilon_0$ = permittivity of free space, $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity
- $\mu$ = gas dynamic viscosity, $\mu_i$ = ion mobility
- $A$ = total collecting electrode area, $Q$ = gas volumetric flow rate
- $N_0$ = ion number density, $v_i$ = ion thermal velocity
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant, $T$ = absolute temperature, $e$ = electron charge

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Sector | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Thermal Power Plants** | Fly-ash removal from flue gas (> 99.9 % efficiency on fine PM) |
| **Cement Industry** | Kiln and clinker cooler dedusting |
| **Steel Plants** | Blast furnace gas cleaning, sinter plant dedusting |
| **Pulp & Paper** | Recovery boiler precipitators |
| **Non-Ferrous Metallurgy** | Roaster gas cleaning (Cu, Zn, Pb smelters) |
| **Incinerators** | Municipal solid waste (MSW) particulate control |
| **Acid Plants** | Sulphuric acid plant gas cleaning |

**Key Engineering Advantage:** ESPs have **very low pressure drop** (typically 0.5–1.5 cm of water column) compared to fabric filters or scrubbers, resulting in **lower fan power consumption** and significant **energy savings** in continuous industrial operation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Peek's Law for Corona Onset Voltage

The corona discharge threshold is a critical design parameter. Starting from the **Townsend breakdown criterion** for non-uniform fields:

**Step 1 — Field at the wire surface in a wire-cylinder geometry**

By Gauss's law, the electric field at a distance $r$ from the axis of a long wire of radius $r_w$ inside a coaxial cylinder of radius $R$ is:

$$E(r) = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}$$

where $\lambda$ is the linear charge density (C/m) on the wire. The potential difference between wire and cylinder is:

$$V = \int_{r_w}^{R} E(r) \, dr = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0} \ln\left(\dfrac{R}{r_w}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Eliminate $\lambda$ to get field at the wire surface**

Substituting $\lambda = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 V}{\ln(R/r_w)}$ back into the field expression and evaluating at $r = r_w$:

$$E_{r_w} = \dfrac{V}{r_w \ln(R / r_w)}$$

This shows that for a given $V$, a smaller $r_w$ produces a **larger surface field** — confirming why thin wires are used.

**Step 3 — Apply Peek's empirical correction**

Peek introduced a dimensionless surface irregularity factor $m$ (where $0.6 \le m \le 1.0$ for polished wires, lower for rough wires) and a **gas density factor** to account for atmospheric conditions. The corona onset condition is:

$$E_{\text{onset}} = 3 \times 10^6 \cdot m \cdot \delta \quad \text{[V/m]}$$

where $\delta$ is the **air density factor** (≈ 1 at STP, < 1 at altitude).

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for $V_c$**

Setting $E_{r_w} = E_{\text{onset}}$ and solving for the critical voltage:

$$\boxed{\,V_c = 3 \times 10^6 \cdot m \cdot r_w \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{r_w}\right)\,}$$

> This is **Peek's Law**, the central design equation for the inception of corona in wire-plate and wire-pipe ESPs.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Field Charging (Boltzmann) — Saturation Charge

Consider a spherical particle of diameter $d_p$ and relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ placed in a uniform external field $E_0$. Ions drift along field lines and deposit on the particle.

**Step 1 — Induced dipole and effective internal field**

The applied field polarizes the particle. The local field at the particle surface is enhanced near the "poles" of the particle (the points where field lines meet the surface tangentially).

**Step 2 — As charge accumulates, the local field decreases**

The deposited charge creates its own opposing field. Charging continues until the **local field at the particle surface is reduced to zero everywhere on the surface** — this is the **saturation condition**.

**Step 3 — Solve Laplace's equation with boundary conditions**

The solution (by the method of images, originally due to **Pauthenier and Moreau-Hanot**) yields the saturation charge:

$$\boxed{\,q_{f,\text{sat}} = 3 \pi \varepsilon_0 \, d_p^{2} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{\varepsilon_r + 2} \cdot E_0\,}$$

**Step 4 — Time to reach saturation**

The charging is exponential:

$$q_f(t) = q_{f,\text{sat}} \cdot \dfrac{t / \tau}{1 + t / \tau}, \quad \tau = \dfrac{4 \varepsilon_0}{N_0 e \mu_i}$$

**Significance for ESP design:**
- A typical fly-ash particle ($\varepsilon_r \approx 4$, $d_p = 10 \,\mu\text{m}$, $E_0 = 4 \times 10^5$ V/m) accumulates $q \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-14}$ C — enough to be easily drifted across an industrial gas duct.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Drift (Migration) Velocity from Force Balance

**Step 1 — Identify the forces on a charged particle in the ESP**

A charged particle moving transversely (perpendicular to the gas flow) experiences:
- **Coulomb driving force:** $F_e = qE_p$ (toward collecting electrode)
- **Stokes' drag force:** $F_d = 3\pi \mu d_p v$ (opposing motion)

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state condition (terminal velocity)**

At terminal velocity, the net force vanishes. For laminar flow ($Re < 1$, true for fine particles):

$$q E_p - 3 \pi \mu d_p \omega = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the drift velocity**

$$\boxed{\,\omega = \dfrac{q E_p}{3 \pi \mu d_p}\,}$$

> This is called the **migration velocity** or **drift velocity** $\omega$, and is the single most important design parameter in ESP sizing.

**Step 4 — Substituting the field-charging saturation charge**

For a field-charged particle, substitute $q = q_{f,\text{sat}}$:

$$\omega = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, E_p}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{\varepsilon_r + 2} \cdot E_0 \cdot d_p$$

This shows that drift velocity is **proportional to particle diameter** — larger particles are removed faster. This is one reason ESPs handle coarse dust very well.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Deutsch–Anderson Collection Efficiency

This is the **most heavily weighted derivation in KTU university examinations**.

**Step 1 — Set up the differential efficiency equation**

Consider an elementary strip of collecting plate at distance $x$ from the gas inlet. The gas flow carries a dust concentration $c(x)$ (kg/m³ or particles/m³). The drift velocity $\omega$ drives particles to the plate at a rate proportional to $\omega \cdot c(x) \cdot dA$, where $dA$ is the differential plate area.

**Step 2 — Mass balance over an infinitesimal section**

In a length $dx$ of an ESP, the plate area is $dA = w \, dx$, where $w$ is the plate width. The rate of dust removal in $dx$:

$$-Q \, dc = \omega \cdot c(x) \cdot w \, dx$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric gas flow rate (m³/s).

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate**

$$\dfrac{dc}{c} = -\dfrac{\omega \, w}{Q} \, dx$$

Integrating from inlet ($x = 0$, $c = c_0$) to outlet ($x = L$, $c = c_L$):

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{c_L}{c_0}\right) = -\dfrac{\omega \, w L}{Q}$$

Since the total collecting area is $A = w L$:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{c_L}{c_0}\right) = -\dfrac{\omega A}{Q}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for collection efficiency**

The collection efficiency $\eta$ is the fraction of dust removed:

$$\eta = \dfrac{c_0 - c_L}{c_0} = 1 - \dfrac{c_L}{c_0}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{\,\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{A \, \omega}{Q}\right)\,}$$

> This is the **Deutsch–Anderson Equation**, the cornerstone of ESP performance analysis.

**Step 5 — Interpretation and limitations**

- $\eta$ depends only on the ratio $A \omega / Q$, called the **specific collection area (SCA)**.
- For $\eta = 0.99$, we need $A \omega / Q \approx 4.6$.
- For $\eta = 0.999$, we need $A \omega / Q \approx 6.9$ — a **50 % increase in plate area** to gain just **0.9 %** in efficiency. This explains the **diminishing-returns behavior** of ESP design.
- **Limitation (KTU Pitfall):** The Deutsch equation assumes **plug flow** (uniform gas velocity, no back-mixing), **uniform particle charging**, and **re-entrainment-free** collection. Real ESPs deviate from these assumptions, leading to actual efficiencies lower than the theoretical value.

### 3.5 Numerical-Style Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A wire-plate ESP handles $Q = 30$ m³/s of flue gas. The total collecting area is $A = 5000$ m². The effective migration velocity is $\omega = 0.10$ m/s. Calculate the collection efficiency. If the emission standard requires $\eta = 0.995$, what additional plate area is needed?

**Solution:**

$$\eta_1 = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{5000 \times 0.10}{30}\right) = 1 - \exp(-16.67) \approx 1 - 5.89 \times 10^{-8} \approx 0.9999999$$

This is unrealistically high — a clear indication that in practice, **non-idealities** (back-mixing, re-entrainment, gas sneakage, uneven field) drastically reduce actual efficiency. Industrial practice uses **effective migration velocities** of 0.05–0.20 m/s **calibrated to actual plant data** to obtain realistic predictions.

For the second part:

$$0.995 = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{A \cdot 0.10}{30}\right) \Rightarrow A = \dfrac{30 \cdot \ln(1/0.005)}{0.10} = \dfrac{30 \times 5.30}{0.10} = 1590 \, \text{m}^2$$

So only ~1590 m² is required theoretically — the **5× safety factor** built into real designs accommodates the non-idealities.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an Electrostatic Precipitator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polluted Gas Inlet] --> B[Gas Distribution Plates]
    B --> C[High Voltage DC Field Zone]
    C --> D[Corona Discharge Region]
    D --> E[Particle Charging Stage]
    E --> F[Particle Migration Zone]
    F --> G[Collection Electrode Plates]
    G --> H[Rapper Mechanism]
    H --> I[Hopper Dust Collection]
    I --> J[Clean Gas Outlet to Stack]
    K[HV Transformer Rectifier Set] --> C
    L[Control Panel and Microprocessor] --> K
    L --> H
    M[Ash Disposal System] --> I
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Block Diagram (Wire-Plate ESP Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ESP1[Wire-Plate ESP Unit]
        A1[Discharge Wire -ve Electrode] --- B1[Collecting Plate +ve Grounded Electrode]
        A2[Discharge Wire -ve Electrode] --- B1
        A3[Discharge Wire -ve Electrode] --- B1
    end
    HVSRC[HV DC Source 30 to 100 kV] --> A1
    HVSRC --> A2
    HVSRC --> A3
    GAS[Polluted Gas Flow Direction] --> ESP1
    ESP1 --> CLEAN[Clean Gas to Stack]
    ESP1 --> DUST[Dust to Hopper]
```

### 4.3 Operational Sequence Flow (Process State Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST1[Stage 1 High Voltage Application] --> ST2[Stage 2 Corona Onset at Wire Surface]
    ST2 --> ST3[Stage 3 Ion Generation and Avalanche]
    ST3 --> ST4[Stage 4 Ion Drift Toward Collecting Plate]
    ST4 --> ST5[Stage 5 Ion Particle Collision and Charge Transfer]
    ST5 --> ST6[Stage 6 Charged Particle Drift Under Coulomb Force]
    ST6 --> ST7[Stage 7 Particle Deposition on Plate]
    ST7 --> ST8[Stage 8 Charge Neutralization at Plate]
    ST8 --> ST9[Stage 9 Rapping and Dust Removal to Hopper]
    ST9 --> ST10[Stage 10 Clean Gas Exit]
    ST10 --> ST1
```

### 4.4 Comparison Matrix of ESP Types (Schematic Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ESP[Electrostatic Precipitator]
    ESP --> T1[Dry Type]
    ESP --> T2[Wet Type]
    T1 --> T1A[Wire-Plate]
    T1 --> T1B[Wire-Pipe]
    T2 --> T2A[Water-Washed]
    T2 --> T2B[Steam-Conditioned]
    T1A --> APP1[Thermal Power Plants Cement Steel]
    T1B --> APP2[Small Scale Applications]
    T2A --> APP3[Acid Mists Tar and Oil]
    T2B --> APP4[High Temperature Exhaust Streams]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Interpretation:** The diagrams above intentionally use **clean uppercase alphanumeric labels** (no markdown formatting inside node labels) to ensure Mermaid compatibility and clarity. Each block represents a **functional unit** in the ESP system. Physical drawings (e.g., a real wire-plate assembly) are typically supplied in the standard textbooks; for the purpose of KTU board examinations, students are expected to sketch and label a simple **cross-sectional view** showing the discharge electrodes, collecting plates, HV input, gas flow direction, and dust hopper.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define corona discharge. Explain the conditions necessary for the formation of corona in an electrostatic precipitator.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Corona discharge is a self-sustaining, non-arcing electrical discharge that occurs in a highly non-uniform electric field when the local field intensity at a small-radius electrode exceeds the dielectric strength of the surrounding gas, causing localized ionization of gas molecules in the immediate vicinity of the electrode.

**Conditions for formation (2 marks):**
1. The electric field must be **highly non-uniform** — typically produced by a small-radius wire (discharge electrode) facing a much larger collecting electrode (plate or cylinder).
2. The applied voltage must exceed the **corona onset voltage** given by Peek's law: $V_c = 3 \times 10^6 \cdot m \cdot r_w \cdot \ln(R / r_w)$.
3. The gas pressure, temperature, and humidity must be such that **avalanche ionization** can be sustained by secondary processes at the cathode (for negative corona) or anode (for positive corona).
4. The current must be limited by external circuit resistance to prevent the transition from corona to **sparkover or arc discharge**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*List the advantages and disadvantages of electrostatic precipitators compared to other particulate control devices.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. **Very high collection efficiency** (> 99.9 %) on fine and ultra-fine particulates.
2. **Low pressure drop** (0.5–1.5 cm WC) → low fan power consumption.
3. Can handle **high-temperature gases** (up to 450 °C) and corrosive atmospheres.
4. **Low operating cost** in continuous-duty industrial applications.
5. Can collect **dry dust** (recoverable) or **wet slurry** (in wet ESPs).

**Disadvantages (1.5 marks):**
1. **High initial capital cost** — HV rectifier-transformer sets, control gear, and large physical footprint.
2. **Not suitable for explosive gas mixtures** (risk of spark-induced ignition).
3. Performance is **sensitive to dust resistivity** — high-resistivity dust (> 10¹¹ Ω·cm) causes back-corona, sharply reducing efficiency.
4. Cannot remove **gaseous pollutants** (SO₂, NOₓ) — only particulates.
5. Requires **large space** for adequate collecting plate area.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the **Deutsch–Anderson equation** for the collection efficiency of an electrostatic precipitator. State clearly the assumptions involved. (7 marks)

**(b)** A wire-plate ESP handles 25 m³/s of flue gas with an effective migration velocity of 0.12 m/s. If the required collection efficiency is 99.5 %, determine:
(i) The specific collection area (SCA) required.
(ii) The total collecting electrode area.
(iii) The new efficiency if the gas flow rate increases by 20 % while area remains unchanged. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Deutsch–Anderson Equation (7 marks):**

Consider an elementary strip of collecting plate at axial distance $x$ from the inlet. Let $c(x)$ be the dust concentration in the gas at that location, $w$ the plate width, and $Q$ the volumetric gas flow rate.

The rate at which dust is removed from the gas in an infinitesimal length $dx$ is:
$$-Q \, dc = \omega \cdot c(x) \cdot w \, dx$$

Separating variables:
$$\dfrac{dc}{c} = -\dfrac{\omega w}{Q} dx$$

Integrating from $x = 0$ (inlet, $c = c_0$) to $x = L$ (outlet, $c = c_L$):
$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{c_L}{c_0}\right) = -\dfrac{\omega \cdot wL}{Q} = -\dfrac{\omega A}{Q}$$

where $A = wL$ is the total collecting plate area.

Exponentiating and using the definition of efficiency $\eta = 1 - c_L/c_0$:

$$\boxed{\,\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\omega A}{Q}\right)\,}$$

**Assumptions (must be stated for full marks):**
1. **Plug flow** — uniform gas velocity profile; no axial mixing.
2. **Uniform charging** — all particles acquire the same charge in transit.
3. **Constant migration velocity** — independent of position and particle history.
4. **No re-entrainment** — once collected, particles remain on the plate.
5. **No gas sneakage** around the active ESP zone.
6. **Complete transverse mixing** — uniform dust concentration across any cross-section.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $Q = 25$ m³/s, $\omega = 0.12$ m/s, $\eta_{\text{required}} = 0.995$.

**(i) Specific Collection Area (SCA):**

From Deutsch–Anderson:
$$\eta = 1 - \exp(-\omega A / Q) \Rightarrow \omega A / Q = -\ln(1 - \eta)$$
$$\text{SCA} = \dfrac{A}{Q} = \dfrac{-\ln(1 - 0.995)}{0.12} = \dfrac{5.30}{0.12} = 44.17 \text{ s/m}$$

**Mark allocation: (2 marks)**

**(ii) Total Collecting Area:**
$$A = \text{SCA} \times Q = 44.17 \times 25 = 1104.3 \text{ m}^2$$

**Mark allocation: (2 marks)**

**(iii) New efficiency with $Q_{\text{new}} = 1.20 \times 25 = 30$ m³/s, $A$ unchanged:**

$$\eta_{\text{new}} = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{0.12 \times 1104.3}{30}\right) = 1 - \exp(-4.417)$$
$$\eta_{\text{new}} = 1 - 0.0121 = 0.9879 = 98.79 \, \%$$

The efficiency **drops from 99.5 % to 98.79 %** — a 0.71 % reduction due to the 20 % increase in gas flow, demonstrating the sensitivity of ESP performance to gas throughput.

**Mark allocation: (3 marks — including interpretation)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the two mechanisms by which dust particles acquire electric charge in an ESP. Compare their ranges of applicability. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **wire-pipe type ESP**. Why is negative corona preferred over positive corona in industrial ESPs? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Particle Charging Mechanisms (7 marks):**

**1. Field Charging (Boltzmann Charging) — Applicable for particles $d_p > 1 \,\mu\text{m}$ (4 marks)**

In this mechanism, ions generated in the corona region are accelerated by the applied electric field and travel along field lines. When an ion approaches a particle, it follows the distorted field line and collides with the particle's surface, transferring its charge.

- The charging rate decreases as the deposited charge increases, because the accumulated charge creates a repulsive field that opposes further ion deposition.
- Charging is **saturating** — after a finite time $\tau$, the particle reaches a **maximum (saturation) charge** given by:
$$q_{f,\text{sat}} = 3 \pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot d_p^2 \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{\varepsilon_r + 2} \cdot E_0$$
- The charging time constant is $\tau = 4\varepsilon_0 / (N_0 e \mu_i)$ — typically **0.01 to 1 second** in industrial ESPs.
- The mechanism is dominant for **coarse and medium particles** in the 1 µm to 100 µm range.

**2. Diffusion Charging — Applicable for particles $d_p < 0.5 \,\mu\text{m}$ (3 marks)**

In this mechanism, ions undergo **random Brownian motion** in the gas and collide with the particle surface by thermal agitation, independent of the applied field direction.

- There is **no saturation** — the charge keeps increasing with time (logarithmically).
- White's equation gives:
$$q_d(t) = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 d_p k T}{e} \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{e^2 N_0 d_p v_i t}{4 \varepsilon_0 k T}\right)$$
- Dominant for **sub-micron particles** (typically < 0.5 µm) and becomes increasingly important at higher operating temperatures.

**Comparison Table (within part a):**

| Parameter | Field Charging | Diffusion Charging |
|---|---|---|
| Particle size | $d_p > 1\,\mu\text{m}$ | $d_p < 0.5\,\mu\text{m}$ |
| Driving force | Electric field | Thermal motion |
| Saturation | Yes | No |
| Field dependence | Strong | Weak |
| Speed | Fast | Slow |
| Industrial relevance | Fly-ash, cement dust | Sub-micron fumes, smokes |

**(b) Wire-Pipe Type ESP (7 marks):**

**Construction (4 marks):**
- A **vertical or horizontal cylindrical pipe** (typically 0.15–0.4 m diameter) acts as the **collecting electrode** and is grounded.
- A thin **discharge wire** (typically 2–4 mm diameter) is suspended **axially along the center** of the pipe, supported at the top by an insulator bushing and tensioned at the bottom by a weight.
- The wire is connected to the **negative terminal** of a high-voltage DC rectifier (set 30–100 kV).
- The dirty gas enters at the bottom (or one end), flows **axially upward (or through)** the pipe, and exits clean at the top.
- A **rapping or washing system** removes collected dust from the inner pipe wall.

**Working (2 marks):**
1. High DC voltage applied → corona forms around the wire.
2. Ions generated, flow outward, charge dust particles.
3. Charged particles drift radially to the pipe wall.
4. Particles deposit on the wall; clean gas exits.
5. Periodic rapping dislodges dust into a bottom hopper.

**Why negative corona is preferred (1 mark — must be in full for top marks):**
- **Lower onset voltage** (≈ 30 % lower than positive corona).
- **Higher current density** for the same applied voltage → faster particle charging.
- **More stable discharge** — less prone to sparking.
- **Higher ion mobility** of negative ions → faster migration.
- **Reduced ozone and NOₓ generation** in industrial exhaust streams.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in ESP Questions — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the assumptions** of the Deutsch–Anderson equation — this is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction in any derivation question.
> 2. **Mixing up field charging and diffusion charging** ranges. Field charging dominates for **$d_p > 1 \,\mu\text{m}$**; diffusion charging for **$d_p < 0.5 \,\mu\text{m}$**. A common confusion is reversing these.
> 3. **Confusing corona onset voltage $V_c$ with breakdown voltage $V_b$.** Corona is a *partial* discharge; breakdown is a *complete* arc. The ESP operates **between** $V_c$ and $V_b$.
> 4. **Using $\omega$ without specifying its value range.** In board answers, always quote $\omega$ = 0.05 to 0.20 m/s as a typical industrial value and note that the Deutsch equation is **idealized**.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical problems.** KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 6. **Not drawing the diagram** in construction questions. A neatly labeled cross-section is worth **at least 2 marks** in part (b) of a 14-mark question.
> 7. **Conflating "back-corona" with "corona."** Back-corona is a *secondary* discharge from a high-resistivity dust layer on the collecting plate — opposite to the original corona — and is a **failure mode**, not a design feature.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ESP Module 4**
>
> - [ ] **ESP** = gas cleaning device using **corona + Coulomb attraction**.
> - [ ] **Two stages:** (1) Particle charging, (2) Particle collection.
> - [ ] **Corona** = self-sustained partial discharge in a **non-uniform field**.
> - [ ] **Wire** is the **discharge electrode (small radius)**, **plate/pipe** is the **collecting electrode (large radius, grounded)**.
> - [ ] **DC voltage** (30–100 kV) is essential — AC would not produce net migration.
> - [ ] **Negative corona** is preferred: more stable, lower onset voltage, higher current, less ozone.
> - [ ] **Field charging** (Boltzmann) — for $d_p > 1\,\mu\text{m}$ — **saturating** with $q_{\text{sat}} = 3\pi\varepsilon_0 d_p^2 \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{\varepsilon_r+2} E_0$.
> - [ ] **Diffusion charging** (White) — for $d_p < 0.5\,\mu\text{m}$ — **non-saturating**, logarithmic in time.
> - [ ] **Migration velocity** $\omega = qE_p / (3\pi\mu d_p)$ — from Stokes drag balance.
> - [ ] **Deutsch–Anderson equation:** $\eta = 1 - \exp(-A\omega / Q)$ — the master efficiency equation.
> - [ ] **SCA** = $A/Q$ is the key design parameter; for $\eta = 99.5\%$, SCA$\cdot\omega \approx 5.3$ m/s is needed.
> - [ ] **Peek's law** for corona onset: $V_c = 3 \times 10^6 \, m \, r_w \ln(R/r_w)$.
> - [ ] **Efficiency** depends only on $A\omega/Q$ — **doubling the area doubles the exponent**, not the efficiency.
> - [ ] **Back-corona** is a critical failure mode for high-resistivity dust (> 10¹¹ Ω·cm).
> - [ ] **Re-entrainment**, **sneakage**, **non-uniform gas flow** are major real-world efficiency reducers.
> - [ ] ESPs are **dry** (fly-ash, cement) or **wet** (acid mist, tar); dry ESPs use **rappers**, wet ESPs use **water wash**.
> - [ ] **Typical $\omega$ in industry:** 0.05 to 0.20 m/s.
> - [ ] **KTU favourite topics for 14-mark questions:** Deutsch equation derivation + numerical, wire-pipe vs wire-plate comparison, and corona physics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ionization - Spark Plugs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET633 - HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ionization - Spark Plugs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Ionization** in the context of High Voltage Engineering refers to the process by which a neutral gas atom or molecule acquires or loses an electron, thereby becoming a charged particle (ion). In gases subjected to a sufficiently strong electric field, free electrons gain kinetic energy from the field and, upon colliding with neutral gas molecules, liberate additional electrons. This cumulative process, termed **Townsend Avalanche Ionization**, is the fundamental mechanism that sustains electrical conduction in gases and ultimately leads to **electrical breakdown**.

A **Spark Plug** is an electro-mechanical device that utilizes this high-voltage ionization phenomenon to deliver an electrical spark (a transient, luminous arc discharge) from an ignition coil into the combustion chamber of an internal combustion engine (ICE). The spark ignites the compressed air-fuel mixture, initiating the combustion process that powers the engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is critical under Module 4 because it bridges *theoretical gas discharge physics* (Townsend mechanism, Paschen's law) with *practical industrial application* (automotive ignition systems). Expect questions linking the microscopic avalanche process to macroscopic spark plug parameters.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **marble at the top of a staircase**:
- Each step represents a gas molecule in the gap between two electrodes.
- The marble (electron) is pushed down by an invisible force (electric field).
- If the marble rolls gently, nothing happens (no ionization).
- If you push hard enough (high enough voltage), the marble gains enough kinetic energy to *knock another marble off the next step*. Now **two marbles** roll down together.
- Each of these two marbles, when pushed, can knock off two more, giving **four marbles**, then **eight**, then **sixteen**... This chain reaction is the **Townsend Avalanche**.
- Eventually, the staircase becomes a thundering cascade — this is the **SPARK**, visible as a bright flash.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Connection:** Every time you start a petrol scooter, car, or motorcycle, a spark plug creates roughly **20,000 to 30,000 volts** across a tiny air gap of just **0.6 mm to 1.1 mm**. The electric field intensity at this point is approximately **3 × 10⁷ V/m** — strong enough to ionize air and trigger a controlled lightning bolt inside the engine cylinder!

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dielectric strength of air at STP | E_bd | **≈ 30,000** (i.e. 3 × 10⁶) | V/cm |
| Dielectric strength of air at STP | E_bd | **≈ 3 × 10⁶** | V/m |
| Typical spark plug gap | d | **0.6 – 1.1** | mm |
| Typical breakdown voltage (spark plug) | V_bd | **8,000 – 25,000** | V |
| Required secondary voltage (ignition coil) | V_s | **20,000 – 40,000** | V |
| Spark current | I_spark | **30 – 1000** | mA |
| Spark duration | t_spark | **1 – 2** | ms |
| Spark energy (typical) | E_spark | **30 – 100** | mJ |
| Electron charge | e | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹** | C |
| Electron rest mass | m_e | **9.109 × 10⁻³¹** | kg |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Townsend Avalanche Mechanism — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The ionization process in a spark plug gap does not occur instantaneously. It evolves through a structured, four-stage sequence:

**Stage 1 — Seed Electron Generation**
- A "lucky" free electron must first appear in the gap. Sources include:
  - **Cosmic ray ionization** (always present in air)
  - **Thermionic emission** from the hot central electrode
  - **Field emission** from microscopic surface irregularities
- Without this initial electron, no avalanche can begin.

**Stage 2 — Primary Ionization (α-process)**
- The seed electron accelerates in the electric field **E = V/d** between the electrodes.
- When its kinetic energy exceeds the **first ionization potential** of the gas molecule (e.g., **15.6 eV** for air), it ejects an additional electron upon collision.
- This produces one new electron and one positive ion. The new electron repeats the process.
- The number of new electrons produced per electron per unit drift distance is defined as **Townsend's first ionization coefficient, α** (units: m⁻¹ or cm⁻¹).

**Stage 3 — Secondary Ionization (γ-process)**
- The positive ions, electrons, and photons generated in Stage 2 bombard the cathode, releasing **additional secondary electrons**.
- The number of secondary electrons produced per primary avalanche is defined as **Townsend's second ionization coefficient, γ** (dimensionless, typically 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻¹).

**Stage 4 — Avalanche Multiplication & Breakdown**
- The combined α and γ processes create a self-sustaining avalanche.
- When the current grows without bound, **electrical breakdown** occurs, and a luminous **spark channel** forms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a spark plug, the central electrode runs hot (600°C – 800°C), which dramatically enhances thermionic emission. This is why spark plugs are extremely reliable at cold engine starts — there is always a ready supply of seed electrons.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Law | Expression | Symbols & Units | Application in Spark Plugs |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Townsend Current Growth** | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{\alpha d}$ | I = avalanche current (A), I₀ = initial current (A), α = first Townsend coefficient (m⁻¹), d = gap (m) | Describes how current grows across the spark gap before breakdown |
| 2 | **Breakdown Criterion** | $\gamma \left( e^{\alpha d} - 1 \right) = 1$ | γ = second Townsend coefficient | Determines the critical gap at which spark fires |
| 3 | **Townsend α — Empirical** | $\dfrac{\alpha}{p} = A \cdot e^{-B p / E}$ | p = gas pressure (Pa), E = electric field (V/m), A & B = gas constants | Relates ionization to field strength |
| 4 | **Paschen's Law** | $V_b = \dfrac{B \cdot p \cdot d}{\ln(A \cdot p \cdot d) - \ln\!\left[ \ln\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{\gamma}\right) \right]}$ | V_b = breakdown voltage (V) | Predicts the minimum voltage required to spark at given pd |
| 5 | **Paschen's Law (Standard Form)** | $V_b = \dfrac{B \cdot p \cdot d}{\ln(A \cdot p \cdot d) - C}$ | $C = \ln\!\left[ \ln\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{\gamma}\right) \right]$ | Simplified engineering form |
| 6 | **Electric Field in Spark Gap** | $E = \dfrac{V}{d}$ | E (V/m), V (V), d (m) | Field strength in uniform-field spark plug gap |
| 7 | **Required Spark Voltage** | $V_b = E_{bd} \cdot d$ | E_bd = dielectric strength of gas (V/m) | Engineering shortcut using E_bd |
| 8 | **Spark Energy Stored** | $E_s = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | C = capacitance (F), V = charging voltage (V) | Energy delivered by ignition capacitor |
| 9 | **Mean Free Path of Electron** | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi r^2 n}$ | r = molecular radius, n = number density | Determines the gap distance over which an electron gains ionization energy |
| 10 | **Drift Velocity of Electron** | $v_d = \mu_e \cdot E$ | μ_e = electron mobility (m²/V·s) | Determines transit time across spark gap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE SYNTAX NOTE:** Inside the table above, mathematical absolute value bars and similar delimiters were deliberately typeset using `\left[ ... \right]` and `\ln\!\left(...\right)` to avoid breaking the markdown pipe `\|` table delimiter. Always follow this convention in your own notes.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of This System

The ionization process in spark plugs drives numerous engineering applications:

- **Automotive ignition systems** (petrol cars, motorcycles, scooters, chainsaws, lawnmowers)
- **Aircraft piston engines** (require dual magneto-spark systems for redundancy)
- **Industrial gas turbines** (jet engine igniters use a spark plug-like igniter for cold starts)
- **Rocket engine ignition** (pyrotechnic + spark ignition for liquid-fueled rockets)
- **Domestic gas burners** (piezoelectric spark igniters)
- **Flame rectification systems** in modern boilers
- **High-energy physics** (Geiger counters, spark chambers for particle detection)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Townsend Current Growth Equation

**Objective:** Derive how current grows across a uniform-field gas-filled gap as a function of distance.

**Step 1 — Setup the differential equation**

Consider a uniform field gap of length d. At distance x from the cathode, let n(x) be the number of electrons per unit cross-sectional area. As these electrons drift a further infinitesimal distance dx, each electron produces α·dx new ionizing collisions (by definition of α).

Therefore, the increase in electron density over dx is:
$$dn = n(x) \cdot \alpha \cdot dx$$

**Step 2 — Solve the differential equation**

Separating variables and integrating from x = 0 (cathode, electron count = n₀) to x = d (anode):

$$\int_{n_0}^{n_d} \dfrac{dn}{n} = \int_{0}^{d} \alpha \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration**

Since α is constant in a uniform field:

$$\ln\!\left( \dfrac{n_d}{n_0} \right) = \alpha d$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate to obtain the electron density growth**

$$n_d = n_0 \cdot e^{\alpha d}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to current**

Current is charge × number density × drift velocity. Since velocity is the same for all electrons:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{\alpha d}$$

$$\boxed{I = I_0 \cdot e^{\alpha d}}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** For every unit of initial current I₀, the avalanche amplifies it by a factor of $e^{\alpha d}$. In a typical spark plug, $\alpha d$ can reach values of **18–22**, meaning the current amplification factor $e^{\alpha d}$ ranges from **6.5 × 10⁷** to **3.6 × 10⁹**. This is why breakdown is so abrupt — current skyrockets in microseconds.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Townsend Breakdown Criterion

**Objective:** Derive the condition at which a gas gap becomes self-conducting (breakdown).

**Step 1 — Identify the source of secondary electrons**

At the cathode, three mechanisms liberate new electrons:
1. Positive ion bombardment (dominant mechanism, characterized by γ)
2. Photon emission from excited atoms hitting the cathode
3. Metastable atom bombardment

All three are lumped into a single coefficient γ (electrons released per positive ion).

**Step 2 — Write the recursive relation for one avalanche generation**

Let n₀ = initial electrons leaving the cathode. After traveling distance d:

$$n_0' = n_0 \cdot e^{\alpha d} \quad \text{(electrons reaching anode)}$$

The number of new positive ions produced per generation is:
$$n_0 \left( e^{\alpha d} - 1 \right)$$

(The subtraction of 1 excludes the original electron, leaving only the ion-generating events.)

**Step 3 — Compute secondary electrons from these ions**

Each ion liberates γ secondary electrons at the cathode. Therefore, the number of secondary electrons reaching the cathode is:

$$n_0'' = \gamma \cdot n_0 \left( e^{\alpha d} - 1 \right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the self-sustaining criterion**

For the discharge to sustain itself without external ionization source, the secondary electrons must equal or exceed the original number:

$$n_0'' \geq n_0$$

$$\gamma \cdot n_0 \left( e^{\alpha d} - 1 \right) \geq n_0$$

**Step 5 — Simplify**

Cancelling n₀ (non-zero):

$$\boxed{\gamma \left( e^{\alpha d} - 1 \right) \geq 1}$$

> **Critical Insight for KTU:** This equation is the *mathematical condition* that defines electrical breakdown. When this inequality is satisfied, the gap conducts without any external source — a self-sustaining avalanche.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Paschen's Law from First Principles

**Objective:** Show that the breakdown voltage V_b is a function only of the product p·d (gas pressure × gap distance), not of p or d independently.

**Step 1 — Combine the breakdown criterion with the empirical α expression**

Townsend's empirical formula for α is:
$$\dfrac{\alpha}{p} = A \cdot e^{-B p / E}$$

Since E = V/d (uniform field), we have $p/E = pd/V$. Thus:

$$\alpha = A p \cdot e^{-B p d / V}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute α into the breakdown criterion**

Let the breakdown voltage be V = V_b at the threshold (γ(e^{αd} - 1) = 1):

$$e^{\alpha d} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma}$$

Take natural logarithm on both sides:
$$\alpha d = \ln\!\left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the α expression**

$$A p d \cdot e^{-B p d / V_b} = \ln\!\left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the exponential**

$$e^{-B p d / V_b} = \dfrac{1}{A p d} \ln\!\left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Take natural logarithm of both sides**

$$-\dfrac{B \cdot p d}{V_b} = \ln\!\left[ \dfrac{1}{A p d} \ln\!\left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma} \right) \right]$$

**Step 6 — Solve for V_b**

$$V_b = -\dfrac{B \cdot p d}{\ln\!\left[ \dfrac{1}{A p d} \ln\!\left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma} \right) \right]}$$

This is algebraically equivalent to the standard Paschen form:

$$\boxed{V_b = \dfrac{B \cdot p d}{\ln(A \cdot p d) - \ln\!\left[ \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma}\right) \right]}}$$

> **Key Conclusion:** Since p and d appear only as the product p·d, this confirms **Paschen's Law**: $V_b = f(pd)$. This is why, for a given spark plug, increasing compression (which raises pressure p) raises the required breakdown voltage by exactly the same amount as proportionally increasing the gap d would.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Numerical Spark Energy Computation

**Problem:** A capacitor-discharge ignition (CDI) system has C = 2 μF, charged to V = 400 V. Compute the spark energy delivered to the cylinder.

**Step 1 — Identify the formula**

$$E_s = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$E_s = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (2 \times 10^{-6}) \times (400)^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the square**

$$400^2 = 160{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Multiply**

$$E_s = 0.5 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.6 \times 10^{5}$$

$$E_s = 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.6 \times 10^{5}$$

$$\boxed{E_s = 0.16 \text{ J} = 160 \text{ mJ}}$$

> **Engineering Judgment:** This 160 mJ is the typical energy of a CDI spark — sufficient to ignite a lean air-fuel mixture in modern motorcycles. Modern automotive coil-on-plug systems deliver **50–80 mJ** in the spark.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Parameter on Spark Plug Performance

| Parameter | Increase Effect on V_b (Breakdown Voltage) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Gas pressure (p)** ↑ | V_b increases | High-compression engines need stronger ignition coils |
| **Gap distance (d)** ↑ | V_b increases | Larger gaps need higher voltage |
| **Product (p·d)** ↑ | V_b increases (after Paschen minimum) | Critical for engine design |
| **Electrode work function** ↑ | V_b slightly decreases (better cathode emission) | Use low-work-function alloys (platinum, iridium) |
| **Temperature** ↑ | V_b decreases | Hot plugs fire more easily (thermionic help) |
| **Electrode shape (sharpness)** ↑ | Local field enhancement → V_b decreases | Sharp edges lower breakdown threshold |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Spark Plug Ignition System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Battery 12V DC] --> B[Ignition Switch]
    B --> C[Ignition Coil Primary Winding Np approx 200 turns]
    C --> D[Switching Device Breaker Points or IGBT Transistor]
    D --> E[Magnetic Flux Build Up in Iron Core]
    E --> F[Sudden Collapse of Current via Switching Off]
    F --> G[High EMF Induced in Secondary Winding Ns approx 20000 turns]
    G --> H[Voltage Multiplication Ns over Np approx 100x]
    H --> I[Spark Plug Central Electrode Gap d approx 0.8 mm]
    I --> J[High Electric Field E equals V over d approx 3e7 V per m]
    J --> K[Townsend Avalanche alpha process]
    K --> L[Secondary Emission gamma process]
    L --> M[Self Sustaining Criterion gamma exp alpha d minus 1 equals 1]
    M --> N[Spark Channel Formation]
    N --> O[Combustion Chamber Air Fuel Ignition]
    O --> P[Engine Power Stroke]
```

**How to read this diagram:** Energy flows from the **12V battery** (left) → through the **ignition coil** (electromagnetic transformer) → to the **spark plug gap** (where ionization happens) → to the **combustion chamber**. The critical step is the **voltage multiplication** (Np ≈ 200 turns, Ns ≈ 20,000 turns gives ~100× boost) that converts 12V DC into 25,000V AC across the spark gap.

---

### 4.2 Spark Plug Cross-Section — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CENTRAL_ASSEMBLY
        A1[Terminal Stud Brass Nickel Plated]
        A2[Sealant Glass Powder Ceramic Bond]
        A3[Central Electrode Copper Core Nickel Alloy Tip]
    end
    subgraph INSULATOR
        B1[Alumina Ceramic Al2O3 95 percent Purity]
        B2[Ribbed Outer Surface for Creepage Distance]
    end
    subgraph HOUSING
        C1[Steel Shell Hex Head for Wrench Grip]
        C2[Thread M14 x 1.25 Standard]
        C3[Copper Gasket Seal to Cylinder Head]
    end
    subgraph GAP_REGION
        D1[Ground Electrode Nickel Alloy Side Wire]
        D2[Spark Gap 0.6 to 1.1 mm]
        D3[Ionized Plasma Channel During Spark]
    end
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> D2
    B1 --> A2
    B1 --> B2
    C1 --> B1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    A3 --> D2
```

---

### 4.3 Process Flow — Townsend Avalanche to Spark Discharge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1 Seed Electron from cosmic ray or thermionic emission] --> P2[Stage 2 Primary Ionization alpha collisions with gas molecules]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3 Positive Ions Drift to Cathode gamma secondary emission]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 4 Self Sustaining Criterion Satisfied]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 5 Avalanche Multiplication current grows exponentially]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 6 Plasma Channel Formation luminous spark]
    P6 --> P7[Stage 7 Spark Produces Ignition Kernel in combustion chamber]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**
Define Townsend's first ionization coefficient (α) and Townsend's second ionization coefficient (γ). Explain their role in the formation of a spark in a spark plug.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Townsend's First Ionization Coefficient (α):** It is defined as the number of ionizing collisions made by one electron per unit drift distance in the direction of the electric field. Units: per metre (m⁻¹) or per cm (cm⁻¹). **Role:** It governs the multiplication of electrons in the avalanche across the spark gap.

- **Townsend's Second Ionization Coefficient (γ):** It is defined as the average number of secondary electrons emitted from the cathode per incident positive ion (or photon). It is dimensionless. **Role:** It determines whether the avalanche becomes self-sustaining, leading to spark formation.

- The breakdown criterion is $\gamma(e^{\alpha d} - 1) = 1$. When this is satisfied, the current grows exponentially and a spark is produced.

*(Allocation: 1 mark each for α, γ; 1 mark for breakdown criterion / role)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]**
State and explain Paschen's Law. How is it relevant to the design of spark plugs?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Paschen's Law:** The breakdown voltage $V_b$ of a gas gap is a function only of the product of gas pressure (p) and gap distance (d), i.e., $V_b = f(pd)$. It is expressed as:
$$V_b = \dfrac{B \cdot p d}{\ln(A \cdot p d) - \ln\!\left[\ln\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{\gamma}\right)\right]}$$

- The plot of $V_b$ vs. $p d$ is the **Paschen curve**, which has a U-shape with a minimum (e.g., for air: V_b,min ≈ 327 V at pd ≈ 0.7 torr·cm).

- **Relevance to Spark Plugs:**
  1. Increasing engine compression raises cylinder pressure (p), which increases the breakdown voltage required. Hence, high-performance engines require stronger ignition systems.
  2. The spark plug gap (d) must be carefully chosen — too large requires too much voltage; too small may cause misfire or quenching.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for statement; 1 mark for equation; 1 mark for spark plug relevance)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 / KTU Premium Pattern]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a spark plug used in internal combustion engines. State the materials used for each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive Townsend's breakdown criterion for a uniform-field gas gap. Using this, explain how the spark plug gap influences the breakdown voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks):**

**Construction (refer Section 4.2 diagram):**

1. **Terminal Stud (1 mark):** Brass/nickel-plated brass top terminal where the high-voltage cable from the ignition coil connects.

2. **Insulator (1 mark):** High-purity alumina ceramic (Al₂O₃, 95% purity) provides electrical insulation and thermal endurance. The ribbed outer surface lengthens creepage distance and prevents surface flashover.

3. **Central Electrode (1 mark):** A copper-cored nickel alloy (or platinum/iridium in premium plugs) conductor that carries the high voltage into the combustion chamber. Copper core aids heat dissipation.

4. **Steel Shell / Hex Head (1 mark):** Cold-rolled steel housing with hex head for wrench tightening and a threaded shank (typically M14 × 1.25) for screwing into the cylinder head.

5. **Ground Electrode (1 mark):** Nickel alloy side wire welded to the shell; forms the spark gap (typically 0.6–1.1 mm) with the central electrode.

6. **Gasket Seal:** Copper or stainless steel gasket ensures gas-tight seating on the cylinder head.

**Working (1 mark):**

When the ignition coil generates a high voltage (~25 kV), it is applied between the central electrode and the ground electrode. The electric field across the gap E = V/d is of the order of $3 \times 10^7$ V/m, which exceeds the dielectric strength of the gas mixture, ionizing it. A spark channel forms, igniting the air-fuel mixture.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — Derivation & Gap Influence (7 marks):**

**Derivation (5 marks — full step-by-step, see Section 3.2 for complete derivation):**

Final breakdown criterion:
$$\boxed{\gamma \left( e^{\alpha d} - 1 \right) = 1}$$

**Explanation with spark plug gap influence (2 marks):**

- For a fixed gap geometry, the parameter $\alpha d$ (called the *number of avalanche generations*) directly determines the breakdown voltage.
- A **larger gap d** requires a **higher V** to achieve the same $\alpha d$ (since E = V/d must remain high enough for ionization).
- Hence: $V_b \propto d$ approximately (linear relation in practical engineering range).
- **Engineering trade-off:** A larger gap improves spark kernel formation (better ignition of lean mixtures) but demands a more powerful ignition coil. A smaller gap is more reliable at low battery voltage but provides a smaller ignition kernel.
- **Standard recommendation:** 0.7–0.9 mm for modern motorcycles; 1.0–1.1 mm for high-performance cars.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 / KTU Premium Pattern]

**(a)** Derive Paschen's Law from Townsend's breakdown criterion and the empirical expression for α. Sketch the Paschen curve and explain the concept of the minimum breakdown voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spark plug has a gap of 1.0 mm. The dielectric strength of the air-fuel mixture under compression is $2 \times 10^6$ V/m. Calculate: (i) the breakdown voltage; (ii) the energy stored in the ignition capacitor of 2 μF charged to 400 V. Comment on whether this energy is sufficient for reliable ignition. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — Paschen's Law Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step-by-step derivation (5 marks) — see Section 3.3 for full derivation:**

Final result:
$$\boxed{V_b = \dfrac{B \cdot p d}{\ln(A \cdot p d) - \ln\!\left[\ln\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{\gamma}\right)\right]}}$$

**Paschen Curve Sketch (1 mark):**

```
   V_b (V)
    │       *  *  *
    │      *        *
    │     *          *
    │    *            *
    │   *              *
    │   *              *
    │___*_____*___*____*___ (pd)
        (min)   (increasing)
```

**Explanation (1 mark):** The U-shaped Paschen curve has a minimum at $pd_{min}$ (for air: ~0.7 torr·cm at V_b,min ≈ 327 V). For pd < (pd)_min, breakdown voltage rises because the gap is too short for electrons to gain sufficient ionization energy between collisions. For pd > (pd)_min, breakdown voltage rises because the gas becomes too dense, requiring more voltage to drive avalanches.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**(i) Breakdown voltage (3 marks):**

$$V_b = E_{bd} \times d$$
$$V_b = (2 \times 10^6 \text{ V/m}) \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m})$$
$$\boxed{V_b = 2000 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) Energy stored (3 marks):**

$$E_s = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$$
$$E_s = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}) \times (400 \text{ V})^2$$
$$E_s = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.6 \times 10^5$$
$$\boxed{E_s = 0.16 \text{ J} = 160 \text{ mJ}}$$

**Comment (1 mark):**

Yes, 160 mJ is comfortably above the **minimum ignition energy (~15–20 mJ)** required for a stoichiometric air-fuel mixture. However, in a real engine with lean or EGR-diluted mixtures, the required ignition energy can rise to **50–100 mJ**, so 160 mJ provides a healthy safety margin. The breakdown voltage of 2000 V is well within the capability of a standard ignition coil (which typically delivers 20–40 kV).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "−1" in the breakdown criterion:** A very common error is writing $e^{\alpha d} = 1/\gamma$ instead of $\gamma(e^{\alpha d} - 1) = 1$. The "−1" represents the *original* electron (excluding it from the new ion count). Loss: **1–2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing α (first coefficient) with γ (second coefficient):** α is per unit length (m⁻¹); γ is dimensionless. Examiners often test this distinction.
>
> 3. **Writing Paschen's Law with absolute values using the `\|` symbol:** This breaks markdown tables. Always use `\left\vert x \right\vert` or `\ln\!\left(...\right)` syntax in KTU answers.
>
> 4. **Unit errors in numerical problems:** mm vs. m confusion in $V = E \times d$. Always convert mm to m (multiply by $10^{-3}$).
>
> 5. **Skipping the seed electron explanation:** Examiners often give 1 mark specifically for mentioning the source of the *initial* electron (cosmic ray, thermionic emission, or field emission).
>
> 6. **Wrong formula for spark energy:** Writing $E = CV^2$ instead of $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Ionization & Spark Plugs**

- ✅ **Ionization** is the process of creating charged particles from neutral gas molecules. In spark plugs, it is driven by a high electric field.

- ✅ **Townsend's first coefficient α** = number of ionizing collisions per electron per unit drift distance. Units: m⁻¹.

- ✅ **Townsend's second coefficient γ** = number of secondary electrons per ion at the cathode. Dimensionless.

- ✅ **Current growth equation:** $I = I_0 \cdot e^{\alpha d}$ — exponential amplification across the gap.

- ✅ **Breakdown criterion (master equation):** $\gamma(e^{\alpha d} - 1) = 1$ — must be memorized in full.

- ✅ **Paschen's Law:** $V_b$ depends only on $pd$. U-shaped curve with a minimum at ~0.7 torr·cm for air.

- ✅ **Dielectric strength of air at STP** ≈ $3 \times 10^6$ V/m = 30 kV/cm.

- ✅ **Spark plug gap:** typically 0.6–1.1 mm; larger gap = higher V_b required.

- ✅ **Required ignition voltage:** 8–25 kV across the gap (typical); up to 40 kV for high-performance engines.

- ✅ **Spark energy:** $E_s = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ = ~30–100 mJ typical.

- ✅ **Spark plug insulator:** 95% alumina ceramic with ribbed outer surface for creepage.

- ✅ **Electrode materials:** Copper-cored nickel alloy (standard), platinum/iridium (premium) for longer life.

- ✅ **Hot plug vs cold plug:** Hot plugs have higher heat range (longer insulator nose) — used in low-power engines. Cold plugs have shorter nose — used in high-performance engines to dissipate heat.

- ✅ **Real-world link:** Every spark plug is essentially a controlled laboratory demonstration of Townsend's avalanche and Paschen's Law — they cannot be fully understood without these foundations.

- ✅ **Common KTU exam traps:** Sign errors in logarithms, missing "−1" in breakdown criterion, mm vs. m in numericals, confusing α and γ.
